topics.title: General Introduction to Core Cavity Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Introduction to Core Cavity Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Official Terminology):**
> **Core** is the male portion of a mould/die that forms the **internal features** (holes, recesses, undercuts, internal contours) of a moulded component. **Cavity** is the female portion of a mould/die that forms the **external surface** (outer shape, skin, finish) of the moulded component. Together they constitute the *working faces* of an injection mould or pressure die casting die and define the geometric and dimensional identity of the finished part.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chocolate ice-cream sandwich** mould:

- The **bottom shell (female half)** that holds the molten chocolate and gives the sandwich its flat outer base = **CAVITY**.
- The **top plug (male half)** that presses down and forms the curved/sculpted top surface and any internal pocket = **CORE**.
- The horizontal line where the two halves meet = **PARTING LINE**.
- The thin escape slot at the rim = **VENT / OVERFLOW GATE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:**
> *Cavity = Container (female, hollow, holds material) → forms OUTSIDE.*
> *Core = Insert (male, solid, pushes material) → forms INSIDE.*

### 1.2 Working Principle & Function of Core–Cavity System

The core and cavity work as a **paired tooling system** in cyclic operation:

1. **Closing** — Core half and cavity half are clamped together under high tonnage (typical injection moulding clamping force: **500 kN to 10,000 kN**).
2. **Filling** — Molten polymer / molten metal is injected through the **gate** into the enclosed core–cavity volume.
3. **Cooling / Solidification** — Heat is extracted via water/oil channels; shrinkage allowance (typically **0.3% – 2.5%** depending on polymer) is pre-compensated in the cavity dimensions.
4. **Opening** — Mould opens along the parting line.
5. **Ejection** — Part is ejected, usually remaining on the core side (due to **shrinkage grip**), pushed out by ejector pins.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Cavity pressure (injection moulding) | **30 – 120 MPa** |
| Cavity pressure (die casting) | **30 – 70 MPa** |
| Shrinkage allowance (PP) | **1.0% – 2.5%** |
| Shrinkage allowance (ABS) | **0.4% – 0.7%** |
| Shrinkage allowance (Nylon 6,6) | **0.7% – 1.5%** |
| Shrinkage allowance (Zamak die cast) | **0.5% – 0.7%** |
| Draft angle (general) | **0.5° – 2°** per side |
| Surface roughness of cavity | **Ra 0.05 – 0.8 µm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Classification of Cores

Cores are classified by **function, location, and motion mechanism**:

- **Direct / Through Core** — A straight cylindrical pin passing through the moulded part (e.g., central hole of a bushing). Simplest geometry, fixed to one mould half.
- **Movable Core** — Mounted on a slide (side-action) for parts with **side holes** or **undercuts**. Actuated by hydraulic cylinder, cam pin, or angle pin.
- **Collapsible Core** — Multi-piece core that folds inward to release an **internal thread** or **complex internal undercut**. Used in unscrewing moulds for bottle caps, threaded inserts.
- **Loose Core** — A separate piece placed manually or by robot into the cavity before each shot. Used when undercut is too deep or complex for a slide action.
- **Cam-Actuated Core (Lifter Core)** — Moves perpendicular to mould opening via a cam track on a fixed angle pin; commonly used for internal undercuts.

### 2.2 Systematic Classification of Cavities

- **Single Cavity Mould** — One cavity, one core. Used for large parts, prototypes, low volume.
- **Multi-Cavity Mould** — 2 to 64+ identical cavities. Higher productivity, but balancing is critical.
- **Family Mould** — Multiple cavities producing **different parts** of the same assembly (e.g., the housing and cap of a container).
- **Cold Runner Cavity** — Runner is solidified and ejected with the part.
- **Hot Runner Cavity** — Runner is kept molten; no runner scrap, faster cycle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)$ | Cavity dimension with shrinkage compensation |
| $V_{cavity} = m_{part} \, / \, \rho_{polymer}$ | Required cavity volume = part mass / polymer density |
| $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{proj}$ | Clamping force = cavity pressure × projected area |
| $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{m} - T_{w}}{T_{e} - T_{w}}\right)$ | Cooling time (simplified injection moulding) |
| $\Delta h = h_{part} \times S$ | Linear shrinkage compensation on height |
| $N_{max} = \dfrac{\pi \, D_{plate}}{2 \, d_{pin}} - 1$ | Max. number of ejector pins on a circular boss |
| $\theta_{draft} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{H}\right)$ | Draft angle from taper $\Delta$ over depth $H$ |

> Where:
> $S$ = volumetric shrinkage fraction (decimal),
> $D_{part}$ = nominal part dimension,
> $P_{cavity}$ = average cavity pressure (**MPa**),
> $A_{proj}$ = projected area on parting plane (**mm²**),
> $s$ = wall thickness (**mm**),
> $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity of polymer (**mm²/s**),
> $T_m$ = melt temperature, $T_w$ = mould wall temp, $T_e$ = ejection temperature.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Core–Cavity System

- **Dimensional Accuracy**: Cavity dimensions directly drive part tolerances; for general moulded parts ±0.1 mm, for precision moulded optics ±0.01 mm.
- **Cycle Time Optimisation**: Symmetric cooling channels in the core and cavity halves control the bottleneck of production rate.
- **Cost Economics**: Single cavity = low tooling cost but high per-part cost. Multi-cavity amortises cavity machining cost over larger volume.
- **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)**: The core–cavity split governs parting line location, which in turn dictates draft, gate vestige, flash line, and aesthetic acceptance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> Every decision in core–cavity design is a **trade-off** between **Part Quality ⇆ Cycle Time ⇆ Tool Cost ⇆ Ejection Reliability**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shrinkage-Compensated Cavity Dimension

**Problem Statement:** A polypropylene (PP) bushing has an outer diameter (after cooling) of $D_{part} = 50.00 \text{ mm}$. The polymer shrinks $S = 1.5\%$. What should be the **cavity machining dimension** $D_{cavity}$?

**Given:**
$D_{part} = 50.00 \text{ mm}$, $S = 0.015$.

**Step 1 — Conceptualisation.**

After moulding and cooling, the polymer contracts uniformly. Therefore the cavity must be **larger** than the final part by exactly the shrinkage fraction $S$.

**Step 2 — Write the fundamental relationship.**

$$
D_{part} = D_{cavity} - S \cdot D_{cavity}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor.**

$$
D_{part} = D_{cavity} \,(1 - S)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for cavity dimension.**

$$
D_{cavity} = \frac{D_{part}}{1 - S}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
D_{cavity} = \frac{50.00 \text{ mm}}{1 - 0.015} = \frac{50.00}{0.985} \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate.**

$$
D_{cavity} = 50.7614 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Round to machinable precision (±0.01 mm).**

$$
D_{cavity} \approx 50.76 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** The cavity pocket must be machined to **$D_{cavity} = 50.76$ mm** so that, after $1.5\%$ shrinkage, the part measures exactly $50.00$ mm.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Clamping Tonnage

**Problem Statement:** A $200 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm}$ rectangular PP lid is moulded in a single-cavity mould. Average cavity pressure is $P_{cavity} = 40 \text{ MPa}$. Determine the minimum clamping force.

**Step 1 — Identify projected area on the parting plane.**

$$
A_{proj} = L \times W = 200 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm} = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply clamping force equation.**

$$
F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{proj}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
F_{clamp} = 40 \, \tfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^{2}} \times 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$
F_{clamp} = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to tonnes-force (engineering convention: $1 \text{ tf} \approx 9.81 \text{ kN}$).**

$$
F_{clamp} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000 \text{ N}}{9.81 \times 10^{3} \text{ N/tf}} \approx 122.3 \text{ tonne-force}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply safety factor of $1.2$ (industry standard for PP).**

$$
F_{clamp,\,design} = 1.2 \times 122.3 \approx 146.8 \text{ tf}
$$

**Result:** Select a moulding machine of **150 tonne** nominal clamping capacity.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Core Type Selection

| Internal Feature Detected in Part | Recommended Core Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Straight through hole (axis perpendicular to parting) | Direct fixed core | Simple, low-cost, no actuation needed |
| Hole parallel to parting line | Side-action core (hydraulic / angle pin) | Cannot be released by straight draw |
| Internal thread | Collapsible / unscrewing core | Thread cannot release by translation |
| Deep narrow slot | Two-piece split core | Single slide cannot release from both walls |
| Very complex internal undercut | Loose core (manually loaded) | Avoids expensive actuation |
| Boss with small blind hole | Direct core + ejector sleeve | Ejector pin slides through core |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Plate Injection Mould

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CLAMPING UNIT] --> B[FIXED PLATE / CAVITY PLATE]
    B --> C[CAVITY INSERT forms OUTER surface]
    C --> D[PARTING LINE plane of separation]
    D --> E[CORE INSERT forms INNER surface]
    E --> F[MOVING PLATE / CORE PLATE]
    F --> G[EJECTOR PLATE carries ejector pins]
    G --> H[SUPPORT PLATE and CLAMP PLATE]
    H --> I[INJECTION UNIT feeds molten polymer via SPRUE BUSH and RUNNER]
    I --> J[GATE enters cavity at PARTING LINE]
    J --> C
    C --> K[COOLING CHANNEL network in both halves]
    K --> L[PART ejected after opening by EJECTOR PINS moving forward]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of Core-Cavity Operation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 MOULD CLOSING] --> S2[Step 2 INJECTION polymer fills cavity]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 PACKING and HOLDING pressure compensates shrinkage]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 COOLING heat extracted via channels]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 MOULD OPENING along parting line]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 EJECTION part pushed off core by ejector pins]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 PART REMOVAL and cycle restart]
    S7 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Side-Action Core Actuation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[PART WITH SIDE HOLE] --> R[SIDE CORE forms the hole]
    R --> C[CAM PIN or HYDRAULIC CYLINDER actuates slide]
    C --> L[LOCKING BLOCK retains slide during injection]
    L --> S[SPRING RETURN or HYDRAULIC RETRACTION after opening]
    S --> R
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Core Cavity Design Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N0[PART DRAWING received] --> N1[Step A Identify EXTERNAL features assign to CAVITY]
    N1 --> N2[Step B Identify INTERNAL features assign to CORE]
    N2 --> N3[Step C Locate PARTING LINE]
    N3 --> N4[Step D Choose DRAFT ANGLE per surface]
    N4 --> N5[Step E Add SHRINKAGE ALLOWANCE]
    N5 --> N6[Step F Select STEEL GRADE P20 H13 S50C]
    N6 --> N7[Step G Plan COOLING and EJECTION]
    N7 --> N8[Step H Finalise GATE and VENT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for the Exam:**
> When asked *"With a neat sketch explain the working of a two-plate mould"*, draw the **cavity on top, core on bottom, parting line in between, gate on one side, ejector pins under the core, cooling channels hatched in both plates**. The flowcharts above give you the **labelled vocabulary** the examiner expects.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the terms "core" and "cavity" as used in injection mould design. State the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **cavity** is the female mould half whose internal contour shapes the **outer surface** of the moulded part; it receives the molten polymer and forms the external geometry, finish, and dimensions. A **core** is the male mould half whose external contour shapes the **inner surface** of the moulded part, producing internal features such as holes, recesses, threads, and bosses. Together they enclose the moulding volume and define the part's geometry. (3 marks: 1 for cavity definition, 1 for core definition, 1 for combined function.)

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the parting line? List two factors that govern its location.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **parting line** is the line (or plane) along which the two mould halves — the cavity plate and the core plate — meet and separate during mould opening. It is visible as a fine seam on the finished part. (1.5 marks)

Factors governing its location:
1. **Uniform wall thickness requirement** — Parting line is chosen so that both halves produce balanced wall sections, avoiding core shift and uneven cooling. (0.75 mark)
2. **Gate location and cosmetic acceptance** — Parting line is positioned to allow a simple gate on the thicker wall section and to place the visible seam on a non-critical (non-aesthetic) surface. (0.75 mark)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **classification of cores** used in injection moulds. Mention at least **four types** with sketches and applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Direct / Fixed Core** (1.5 marks)
   A cylindrical pin mounted on the core plate that forms a straight through-hole in the part. Application: central hole of a bushing, tubular boss. Limitation: axis must be perpendicular to the parting line.

2. **Movable / Side-Action Core** (2 marks)
   A core mounted on a slide that moves horizontally (parallel to parting line) under the actuation of an angle pin or hydraulic cylinder. Application: side holes in a rectangular box, internal undercut. The slide is locked during injection by a wear-resistant locking block.

3. **Collapsible / Unscrewing Core** (2 marks)
   A multi-piece core that retracts radially or rotates to release an internal thread. Application: threaded bottle caps, threaded inserts. Driven by a gear train linked to mould opening, or by a rack-and-pinion with a hydraulic motor.

4. **Cam-Actuated Lifter Core** (1.5 marks)
   A core moving vertically (perpendicular to mould opening) guided by an angled cam track on an angle pin fixed to the moving platen. Application: internal undercuts on cylindrical parts.

*Sketch credit (1 mark): any two labelled cross-sections of the above core types.*

---

**(b)** A **polypropylene container** has nominal dimensions $100 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm}$ (after cooling). Shrinkage is $1.5\%$. Determine the **cavity machining dimensions** and the **minimum clamping tonnage** if average cavity pressure is $35 \text{ MPa}$ and a safety factor of $1.25$ is recommended. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert shrinkage to decimal.**
$$
S = \frac{1.5}{100} = 0.015
$$

**Step 2 — Apply shrinkage compensation formula $D_{cavity} = \dfrac{D_{part}}{1 - S}$ to each dimension.**

For $L = 100$ mm:
$$
L_{cavity} = \frac{100}{1 - 0.015} = \frac{100}{0.985} = 101.52 \text{ mm}
$$

For $W = 60$ mm:
$$
W_{cavity} = \frac{60}{0.985} = 60.91 \text{ mm}
$$

For $H = 40$ mm:
$$
H_{cavity} = \frac{40}{0.985} = 40.61 \text{ mm}
$$

(3 marks — 1 mark per correct dimension with substitution.)

**Step 3 — Compute projected area on parting plane (assume parting line is on the $100 \times 60$ face):**
$$
A_{proj} = 100 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm} = 6{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

(1 mark.)

**Step 4 — Compute raw clamping force:**
$$
F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{proj} = 35 \tfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^{2}} \times 6{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2} = 210{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

(1 mark.)

**Step 5 — Convert to tonnes-force and apply safety factor:**
$$
F_{clamp,\,tf} = \frac{210{,}000}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \approx 21.41 \text{ tf}
$$

$$
F_{design} = 1.25 \times 21.41 \approx 26.76 \text{ tf}
$$

**Step 6 — Round up to next standard machine size:** Select **30 tonne** injection moulding machine. (1 mark.)

**Result:** Cavity = **101.52 × 60.91 × 40.61 mm**; machine = **30 tonne** nominal.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of cavities** in mould design. Explain the difference between **single-cavity, multi-cavity, and family moulds** with their relative merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Single-Cavity Mould** (2 marks)
   Only one cavity-and-core pair. Merits: simple design, easy gate balancing, low tool cost, suitable for large parts and short runs. Demerits: low productivity, highest per-part cost, requires larger tonnage machine for one part.

2. **Multi-Cavity Mould** (2 marks)
   Two or more identical cavities fed by a balanced runner system. Merits: high output per cycle, lower per-part cost after break-even, amortises mould cost faster. Demerits: precise runner balancing required (uneven fill → rejects), higher mould cost, more complex cooling, gates may need valve-gate or hot-runner to maintain balance.

3. **Family Mould** (2 marks)
   Multiple cavities producing **different parts of the same assembly** (e.g., the body + lid of a container). Merits: produces a complete assembly per cycle, eliminates secondary assembly, useful for small assemblies. Demerits: cycles synchronised to slowest-cavitating part, runner balance harder, each cavity may have different shrinkage requirements.

4. **Hot Runner vs Cold Runner Cavity** (1 mark)
   Hot runner cavities keep the runner molten using heater bands, eliminating runner scrap; cold runner cavities solidify the runner and eject it as scrap with the part.

---

**(b)** With the help of a **labelled functional block diagram**, explain the **working cycle of a two-plate injection mould**. State any **four moulding defects** that may arise from poor core-cavity design and their remedies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moulding Cycle (4 marks — 1 per stage with labelling):**
The cycle consists of six sequential stages:
1. **Mould Closing** — Cavity and core plates are clamped under high tonnage by the toggle mechanism of the press.
2. **Injection** — Molten polymer is injected through the sprue bushing, runner, and gate into the enclosed core-cavity volume at high pressure (30–120 MPa).
3. **Packing / Holding** — Pressure is maintained to compensate volumetric shrinkage; additional material is packed in.
4. **Cooling** — Water circulates through cooling channels in both core and cavity plates; solidification proceeds inward from the mould wall.
5. **Mould Opening** — Moving platen retracts, separating the part from the cavity half. Part remains on the core due to shrinkage grip.
6. **Ejection** — Ejector plate advances, pushing ejector pins through the core plate to release the part.

**Step 2 — Four Defects and Remedies (3 marks — distribute evenly):**

| Defect | Root Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Sink marks** | Wall thickness variation causing non-uniform cooling | Redesign for uniform wall; add cooling channels near thick zones |
| **Warpage** | Differential shrinkage / uneven cooling | Balance cooling; use fibre-filled material; increase draft |
| **Flash** | Insufficient clamping force or worn parting surfaces | Increase tonnage; polish and re-machine parting line |
| **Short shot** | Insufficient injection pressure / improper venting | Increase injection pressure; deepen vent channels |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *core* and *cavity* in definitions — KTU examiners specifically deduct **1 mark** for swapped definitions in Part A.
> - In shrinkage problems, students often write $D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form is **division** by $(1 - S)$ because shrinkage is a multiplicative contraction, not an additive one. **Board valuation: 1 mark deducted.**
> - Always state **units (mm², N, tf)** in clamping tonnage problems — silent unit deduction = 0.5 mark penalty.
> - In diagrams, **label the parting line, gate, sprue, ejector pin, and cooling channel** — missing labels lose up to 2 marks even if the sketch is correct.
> - For "family mould" definition, KTU expects you to say *different parts of the same assembly* — saying *different shapes* is too vague and loses a mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cavity = female → outer surface**; **Core = male → inner surface**. (Highest-frequency MCQ.)
- The **parting line** is the dividing plane; it governs flash location, gate position, and aesthetic seam.
- **Shrinkage formula**: $D_{cavity} = D_{part} / (1 - S)$ — never add the shrinkage linearly.
- **Clamping force formula**: $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{proj} \times \text{SF}$; convert N to **tonne-force** by dividing by $9.81 \times 10^3$.
- Core types to remember (in order of complexity): **Direct → Side-action → Cam-lifter → Collapsible / Unscrewing → Loose**.
- Cavity types: **Single, Multi-cavity, Family, Hot-runner, Cold-runner**.
- Draft angle is mandatory on **all** walls parallel to the mould opening direction; absence causes **drag marks and scoring**.
- Cavity pressure ranges: **30–120 MPa** (injection) and **30–70 MPa** (die casting).
- Ejector pins always act on the **core side**, never on the cavity side (cosmetic flash risk).
- Cooling channels are mirrored symmetrically in **both** halves to prevent warpage.
- For **multi-cavity** moulds, runner balancing is non-negotiable; imbalance → differential fill → high scrap rate.
- Family moulds are economical only for **small assemblies with matched production volumes**; otherwise single-cavity moulds dominate.
- Standard tool steels for cores/cavities: **P20** (pre-hardened, 30–36 HRC), **H13** (hardened, 48–52 HRC for high-volume), **S50C** (low-volume).
- Surface treatments to extend mould life: **nitriding, PVD coating, EDM surface texturing, polishing to SPI standards**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parting Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parting Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Mould and Die Manufacture**, the **Parting Surface** (also called the **Parting Plane** or **Die Joint**) is formally defined as:

> The precisely machined interface surface that separates the **upper die (punch holder assembly)** from the **lower die (die holder assembly)**, along which the two halves of the die open during the stripping or ejection phase of a press operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Parting surface is the imaginary surface which divides the die into two halves — the upper half and the lower half — such that the workpiece (or forging / sheet metal blank) can be readily removed from the die cavity after the forming operation is complete."

In *sheet metal operations* (blanking, piercing, drawing), the parting surface defines the **shear plane** where material separation occurs. In *forging dies*, it determines the **die block parting line** that controls metal flow and flash formation.

The **Parting Line** is the **trace (intersection curve)** of the parting surface with the finished forging or sheet metal part. It is a 2D line on the part surface, while the parting surface itself is a 3D plane (or composite surface).

| Terminology | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Parting Surface** | 3D interface between die halves |
| **Parting Line** | 2D trace on the workpiece |
| **Die Joint** | Practical machined surface between die blocks |
| **Flash Land** | Narrow region at parting line where excess material escapes |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the biscuit cutter and the cookie mould:**
> 
> Imagine you are making shaped cookies (like stars or circles). The cookie mould has a top half and a bottom half. When you press the dough, the dough takes the shape of both halves. To remove the cookie without breaking it, you must **separate the mould at a sensible location** — not through the middle of a thin arm of the star, but along a line where the dough is thickest and easiest to pull away.
> 
> Similarly, in a die:
> - The **parting surface** is the "split line" of the mould.
> - The **parting line** is the visible mark left on the finished product where the mould opened.

**Another simpler analogy:** Think of an **ice cube tray**. The tray has a top plate and bottom plate joined along a horizontal line. The water freezes in the cavity, and the flexible parting line allows the cube to pop out. In dies, however, the parting surface is rigid and must be carefully engineered.

## 1.3 Physical & Design Constants

The following standard metrics govern parting surface design:

- **Standard Draft Angle:** **1° to 3°** (for forging) and **0.5° to 1.5°** per side (for sheet metal drawing).
- **Flash Land Width:** **2 mm to 6 mm** typical for forging dies.
- **Flash Land Thickness:** **0.5 mm to 3 mm**, adjustable via flash relief.
- **Shut Height Tolerance:** ± **0.05 mm** for precision blanking.
- **Die Clearance (single-sided):** typically **5% to 10%** of stock thickness $t$.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always quote **flash thickness**, **flash land width**, and **draft angle** with units. Examiners specifically check for these numeric values.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The parting surface is selected by systematically satisfying the following five engineering criteria:

### Step 1: Maximize One-Draw Geometry
- **Why:** Every parting surface must permit the forging/part to be drawn out of the die cavity **without any undercut obstruction**.
- **How:** The die is mentally split such that all cavity features lie either in the upper die or the lower die — never crossing the parting surface.

### Step 2: Position at Maximum Cross-Section
- **Why:** Thicker sections have larger draft allowance and lower risk of web/rib folding.
- **How:** Place the parting line through the largest projected area where metal flow is symmetrical.

### Step 3: Symmetry of Metal Flow
- **Why:** Asymmetric flow causes folding, laps, and uneven grain flow.
- **How:** Choose a parting surface that gives **balanced die impressions** in upper and lower halves.

### Step 4: Minimize Flash and Trim Cost
- **Why:** Excess flash is wasted material and requires secondary trimming.
- **How:** Parting line should follow contour such that the **flash gutter is thin and uniform**.

### Step 5: Simplify Die Machining
- **Why:** A flat or single-curvature parting surface is easier to CNC-mill, EDM, and grind.
- **How:** Avoid complex multi-curved or stepped parting surfaces unless design demands it.

## 2.2 Classification of Parting Surfaces

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat (Straight) Parting** | Horizontal plane; simplest geometry | Symmetric disc/forgings |
| **Single Curved Parting** | One-axis curved surface (cylindrical) | Shaft-type, flanged parts |
| **Stepped Parting** | Multi-level plane with steps | Parts with varying section thickness |
| **Irregular (Lock-and-Key) Parting** | 3D contoured surface with locks | Complex forging geometries |
| **Angular / Inclined Parting** | Parting plane inclined to forging axis | Parts with offset flanges or lugs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** Students must be able to **sketch and justify** the choice of parting surface for a given forging/component. Marks are awarded for the diagram **and** the justification.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on symbols:** Vertical bars are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha_{d} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{D}\right)$ | Draft angle from forging height $h$ and depth $D$ | degrees (°) |
| 2 | $c = k \cdot t$ | Die clearance (single-sided); $k = 0.05$ to $0.10$ | mm |
| 3 | $F_{shear} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_{s}$ | Shear force in blanking; $L$ = perimeter | N |
| 4 | $F_{strip} = \dfrac{L \cdot t \cdot \tau_{s}}{1000}$ | Stripping force (kN form) | kN |
| 5 | $\sigma_{flash} = \dfrac{4F_{press}}{\pi d^{2}}$ | Compressive stress under flash land | MPa |
| 6 | $t_{f} = t_{0} + s$ | Flash thickness = stock thickness + flash allowance | mm |
| 7 | $W_{flash} = 0.3 \cdot \sqrt{t_{0}}$ | Empirical flash land width | mm |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{part}}{V_{part} + V_{flash}}$ | Material utilization ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_{press} \geq F_{shear} \cdot n$ | Press tonnage with safety factor $n = 1.3$ | kN |
| 10 | $\theta_{lock} = 5^{\circ}$ to $10^{\circ}$ | Standard lock angle for irregular parting | degrees (°) |

**Where:**
- $\alpha_{d}$ = draft angle
- $t$ = stock thickness (mm)
- $L$ = shear perimeter (mm)
- $\tau_{s}$ = shear strength of material (MPa)
- $F_{press}$ = press capacity (kN)
- $d$ = flash land diameter (mm)
- $V_{part}$, $V_{flash}$ = volumes of part and flash respectively
- $\theta_{lock}$ = die lock angle for irregular parting surfaces

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The parting surface selection directly impacts:
1. **Forging Industry** — controls metal flow direction, defect formation (cold shuts, laps), and die life.
2. **Sheet Metal Stamping** — determines blanking accuracy, burr direction, and tool wear.
3. **Plastic Injection Moulding** — affects weld line location, ejection force, and surface finish.
4. **Die Casting** — influences air-vent placement and gating efficiency.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Board:** **"DRAFT"** — **D**rawable, **R**obust (thick section), **A**symmetric flow, **F**lash minimal, **T**ooling simple.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Draft Angle from Geometric Projection

**Given:** A forging with a rib of depth $D$ and height $h$, where $h$ is the vertical projection and $D$ is the horizontal depth that must clear the die wall on opening.

**Derivation:**

The draft angle $\alpha_{d}$ ensures that when the upper die retracts vertically by a distance $h$, the rib of depth $D$ clears the die wall without scraping.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\alpha_{d}) &= \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{h}{D} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad \alpha_{d} &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{D}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
A rib has $D = 25$ mm horizontal depth and $h = 1.5$ mm required vertical clearance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\alpha_{d}) &= \frac{1.5}{25} = 0.060 \\[6pt]
\alpha_{d} &= \tan^{-1}(0.060) = 3.43^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the standard draft range is **1° to 3°**, the calculated **3.43°** exceeds the limit. The engineer must either:
- Increase the rib depth $D$, or
- Use a multi-impression die with side draft, or
- Accept a small draft and use a lubricant.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shear Force in Blanking Along the Parting Line

**Given:** A circular blank of diameter $d = 50$ mm cut from mild steel sheet of thickness $t = 3$ mm. Shear strength $\tau_{s} = 345$ MPa.

**Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the shear perimeter (length of the parting line):
$$
L = \pi \cdot d = \pi \cdot 50 = 157.08 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the shear area (the parting surface area through which shearing occurs):
$$
A_{shear} = L \cdot t = 157.08 \cdot 3 = 471.24 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the shear force equation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{shear} &= A_{shear} \cdot \tau_{s} \\[6pt]
F_{shear} &= 471.24 \text{ mm}^2 \cdot 345 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\[6pt]
F_{shear} &= 162{,}578 \text{ N} \approx 162.6 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply safety factor $n = 1.3$ to size the press:
$$
F_{press} \geq 162.6 \cdot 1.3 = 211.4 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's observation:** Notice how the **parting line length $L$** directly multiplies into the force. **Longer parting lines demand larger presses** — this is why circular blanks require ~$3\times$ the force of square blanks of the same perimeter.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Flash Land Stress and Gutter Geometry

**Given:** Forging press capacity $F_{press} = 2000$ kN, flash land diameter $d_{land} = 80$ mm.

**Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Convert force to Newtons:
$$
F_{press} = 2000 \text{ kN} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute flash land area:
$$
A_{land} = \frac{\pi d_{land}^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^{2}}{4} = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Compressive stress on flash land:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{flash} &= \frac{F_{press}}{A_{land}} \\[6pt]
\sigma_{flash} &= \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{5026.55} \\[6pt]
\sigma_{flash} &= 397.9 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with die steel yield strength (~**1500 MPa** for H13 hot-work die steel at room temperature). Since $397.9 \ll 1500$ MPa, the flash land is safe — but at forging temperature (~**1000 °C**), die steel softens, and flash land stress must be re-evaluated.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Parting Surface Selection

| Design Parameter | Acceptable Range | Consequence if Violated |
|---|---|---|
| Draft angle | 1° – 3° (forging) | Folding, scoring, die wear |
| Flash thickness | 0.5 – 3 mm | Cold shuts if too thin |
| Flash land width | 2 – 6 mm | Die crushing if too narrow |
| Die clearance | 5% – 10% of $t$ | Burr if too loose; tool wear if tight |
| Lock angle | 5° – 10° | Misalignment if too shallow |
| Parting line symmetry | Within ±0.1 mm | Uneven flash, unbalanced load |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often quote clearance as **percent of total thickness** on both sides — but KTU board expects clearance expressed as **single-sided (per side)**. State this explicitly: *"Single-sided die clearance is 5% to 10% of stock thickness."*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Parting Surface Classification & Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Component Drawing] --> assessA{Geometry Analysis}
    assessA --> symA[Symmetric Forging]
    assessA --> asymA[Asymmetric Forging]
    assessA --> compA[Complex Multi-Cavity]

    symA --> pflatA[Flat Parting Plane]
    asymA --> pcurvA[Curved Parting Surface]
    compA --> pirregA[Irregular Lock-and-Key Parting]

    pflatA --> dA[Single Draft Angle 1 to 3 deg]
    pcurvA --> dB[Variable Draft Angle Along Curve]
    pirregA --> dC[Lock Angle 5 to 10 deg]

    dA --> checkA{Drawable Without Undercut?}
    dB --> checkB{Metal Flow Balanced?}
    dC --> checkC{Flash Gutter Uniform?}

    checkA --> yesA[Approve Flat Parting]
    checkA --> noA[Revise to Curved Parting]
    checkB --> yesB[Approve Curved Parting]
    checkB --> noB[Add Side Locks]
    checkC --> yesC[Approve Irregular Parting]
    checkC --> noC[Add Flash Relief Pockets]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Functional Blocks of a Parting Surface System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph upperA[UPPER DIE ASSEMBLY]
        ua1[Punch Holder]
        ua2[Upper Die Block]
        ua3[Upper Flash Land]
        ua4[Upper Lock Element]
    end

    subgraph partingA[PARTING SURFACE ZONE]
        pa1[Flash Gutter Channel]
        pa2[Shear Plane]
        pa3[Lock Engagement]
        pa4[Vent Groove]
    end

    subgraph lowerA[LOWER DIE ASSEMBLY]
        la1[Die Holder]
        la2[Lower Die Block]
        la3[Lower Flash Land]
        la4[Lower Lock Element]
    end

    ua3 -. crosses .- pa1
    la3 -. crosses .- pa1
    ua4 -. engages .- pa3
    la4 -. engages .- pa3
    ua2 -. separates along .- pa2
    la2 -. separates along .- pa2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Parting Surface Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Step 1: Analyze Forging Geometry] --> p2[Step 2: Identify Maximum Cross Section]
    p2 --> p3[Step 3: Mark Parting Line Candidate]
    p3 --> p4{Step 4: Check Undercut}
    p4 --> yesP[Revise Line]
    p4 --> noP[Step 5: Check Symmetry]
    noP --> asymP{Asymmetric?}
    asymP --> yesAP[Add Locks and Side Draft]
    asymP --> noAP[Step 6: Confirm Flat Plane]
    yesAP --> p7[Step 7: Define Flash Land Geometry]
    noAP --> p7
    p7 --> p8[Step 8: Generate CNC Toolpath]
    p8 --> p9[Step 9: Die Trial and Validation]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (For Board Sketch Practice)

> [!TIP]
> **Board Drawing Practice — Draw this on a blank sheet during revision:**
>
> ```
>      ┌─────────── Upper Die Block ───────────┐
>      │   ╔══════════════╗                     │
>      │   ║  Punch Cavity (Upper)   ║           │
>      │   ╚══════════════╝                     │
>      ├─────────── PARTING SURFACE ────────────┤   ← Flash land here
>      │   ╔══════════════╗                     │
>      │   ║  Die Cavity (Lower)       ║        │
>      │   ╚══════════════╝                     │
>      └─────────── Lower Die Block ───────────┘
>      ↑                                       ↑
>   Draft 1-3 deg                          Draft 1-3 deg
>      │                                       │
>   Flash gutter channel (2-6 mm wide)
> ```

The parting surface appears as the **horizontal centre line** between upper and lower die blocks. The flash gutter is the narrow channel at this interface where excess material escapes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define parting surface and parting line. How are they related? 
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **parting surface** is the imaginary 3D surface that divides a die into upper and lower halves, permitting the removal of the finished part. The **parting line** is the 2D intersection trace of this parting surface with the surface of the forging or sheet metal part.
>
> **Relationship:** The parting line is a *trace* (boundary curve) of the parting surface on the part. While the parting surface is a plane or curved 3D entity that defines die geometry, the parting line is the visible line you would see marked on the finished component. **One parting surface can produce one parting line**, but a complex stepped parting surface may produce a multi-segment parting line.

### Q2. What are the standard values of flash land width and draft angle in forging dies?
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Flash land width** in forging dies typically ranges from **2 mm to 6 mm**, depending on forging size and material. **Draft angle** ranges from **1° to 3°** for external walls and **3° to 7°** for deep recesses (to assist withdrawal of the forging). Larger forgings and deeper cavities require larger draft angles within these ranges. Draft prevents scraping of the die wall and ensures the forging releases cleanly on die opening.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the different types of parting surfaces used in forging dies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four principal types of parting surfaces used in forging dies are:

**1. Flat (Straight) Parting:** A horizontal plane separates upper and lower dies. Used for symmetric disc-type or cylindrical forgings (e.g., gears, flanges). Simplest and most economical to machine.

**2. Curved (Single-Axis) Parting:** The parting surface follows a single curved axis. Used for elongated shaft-type forgings where one end is larger than the other.

**3. Stepped Parting:** The parting surface changes plane in steps to accommodate varying cross-sections, keeping thin and thick sections in their respective die halves.

**4. Irregular (Lock-and-Key) Parting:** A 3D contoured surface with interlocking male-female features. Used for complex asymmetric forgings where die halves must be aligned precisely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch:** Draw a labelled diagram showing each type from a side view. Marks: **Type listing (3 marks) + Sketch (2 marks) + Application justification (2 marks).**

---

**Q. (b)** A circular blank of diameter **60 mm** is to be cut from a **4 mm** thick mild steel sheet. If the shear strength of mild steel is **345 MPa**, calculate:
(i) Shear force required
(ii) Press tonnage with a safety factor of **1.3** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the shear perimeter (parting line length):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \pi \cdot d \\
L &= \pi \cdot 60 = 188.50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the shear area (parting surface area):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{shear} &= L \cdot t \\
A_{shear} &= 188.50 \cdot 4 = 754.00 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the shear force:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{shear} &= A_{shear} \cdot \tau_{s} \\
F_{shear} &= 754.00 \cdot 345 \\
F_{shear} &= 260{,}130 \text{ N} = 260.13 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{press} &= F_{shear} \cdot n \\
F_{press} &= 260.13 \cdot 1.3 \\
F_{press} &= 338.17 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Shear force = **260.13 kN**; Required press capacity = **338.17 kN**.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the factors influencing the selection of parting surface in forging die design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The selection of parting surface is governed by the following **seven key factors**:

1. **Ease of Withdrawal:** The parting surface must permit the forging to be drawn from the die cavity without obstruction from undercuts.

2. **Position at Maximum Cross-Section:** Placing the parting line through the thickest section minimizes draft problems and allows free metal flow.

3. **Symmetry of Die Cavity:** Equal material in upper and lower die halves gives balanced metal flow, reduces folding and lap defects.

4. **Flash Control:** The parting line contour should yield thin, uniform flash to minimize trim loss.

5. **Die Machining Cost:** Flat or single-curved parting surfaces are cheaper to CNC-mill and EDM compared to multi-curved surfaces.

6. **Forging Material Flow:** Materials with poor flow (e.g., titanium, stainless steel) demand simpler parting geometries.

7. **Heat Treatment Considerations:** Parting surfaces that produce uniform die block thickness reduce distortion during quenching.

> **Marks allocation:** Listing of factors (3 marks) + Explanation of any 4 factors in detail (3 marks) + Conclusion (1 mark).

---

**Q. (b)** A forging has a rib of horizontal depth **30 mm** and required vertical withdrawal clearance of **2 mm**. Calculate the draft angle. State whether this falls within the standard forging draft range and suggest a modification if not. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply the draft angle formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\alpha_{d}) &= \frac{h}{D} \\
\tan(\alpha_{d}) &= \frac{2}{30} = 0.0667 \\
\alpha_{d} &= \tan^{-1}(0.0667) \\
\alpha_{d} &= 3.81^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with standard range:**
The standard forging draft range is **1° to 3°**. The calculated value **3.81°** exceeds the upper limit.

**Step 3: Recommend a modification:**
- **Option 1:** Increase the rib horizontal depth from 30 mm to **40 mm**, which gives:
$$
\alpha_{d} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2}{40}\right) = 2.86^{\circ} \quad \text{(within range)}
$$

- **Option 2:** Reduce the vertical clearance $h$ from 2 mm to **1.5 mm** (if process permits), giving:
$$
\alpha_{d} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.5}{30}\right) = 2.86^{\circ}
$$

- **Option 3:** Use a **side-draft (multi-impression)** forging die with angled ejector pins if redesign is not feasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write "draft angle formula is $\tan\alpha = D/h$" with **inverted numerator and denominator**. Always confirm: $\tan(\alpha) = $ vertical height / horizontal depth. Marks lost: **2 marks per occurrence**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parting Surface**

- **Parting Surface** = 3D interface between die halves; **Parting Line** = its trace on the workpiece.
- **Five design criteria** (Mnemonic: **DRAFT**): Drawable, Robust section, Asymmetric-flow balanced, Flash minimal, Tooling simple.
- **Standard draft angle:** **1°–3°** (forging), **0.5°–1.5°** (sheet metal drawing).
- **Flash land width:** **2–6 mm**; **Flash land thickness:** **0.5–3 mm**.
- **Die clearance (single-sided):** **5%–10%** of stock thickness $t$.
- **Lock angle for irregular parting:** **5°–10°**.
- **Shear force equation:** $F_{shear} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_{s}$ — remember perimeter $L$ depends on the **parting line length**.
- **Press tonnage rule:** Always apply safety factor $n = 1.3$ (minimum).
- **Flash land stress** must be checked against **die material yield at forging temperature**, not room temperature.
- **Symmetry rule:** For every 1 mm offset in parting line symmetry, metal flow imbalance of ~**3–5%** can occur.
- **Board tip:** When asked to "explain with sketch," always provide the **labelled diagram FIRST** then the explanation — examiners reward diagrams even if the text is brief.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Parting Surface**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sprue Pullers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sprue Pullers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: SPRUE PULLERS — SECTION 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

A **Sprue Puller** (also called a *sprue retainer* or *sprue gripper*) is a small mechanical feature integrated into the **ejector-side half** of an injection mould, immediately beneath the **sprue bushing**. Its function is to retain the solidified sprue (and sometimes the runner) on the **moving half (cavity plate side)** of the mould during the mould-opening stroke, so that the sprue is pulled out of the **stationary sprue bushing** and can subsequently be ejected along with the moulded component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"A sprue puller is a positive retaining device incorporated in the mould half opposite the sprue bushing, designed with an undercut or hook geometry to mechanically grip the cooled sprue during mould opening, ensuring the sprue travels with the moving half until ejection."*

| Parameter | Typical Value (Engineering Standard) |
|---|---|
| Undercut depth (Z-pull) | **0.5 mm – 1.5 mm** |
| Distance from gate entrance | **2D to 3D** (where D = sprue diameter) |
| Sprue puller pin diameter | **3 mm – 6 mm** |
| Return spring compression | **10 N – 30 N** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine inserting a small **plastic drinking straw** (the *sprue*) into a tight rubber grommet (the *sprue bushing*) — once pushed in, friction holds it firmly. Now imagine trying to pull the straw straight out — it requires force, and often the straw deforms or slips.

A **sprue puller** solves this problem the same way a **fishhook** works:

> 🎣 **"Think of a sprue puller as a tiny fishhook embedded in your mould."**

When molten plastic flows past the hook during injection, the hook is completely buried. As the plastic cools and solidifies, the hook becomes a part of the frozen sprue. When the mould opens, the hook (being on the moving side) literally **drags the frozen sprue out** of the sprue bushing — just like reeling in a fish.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:**
> Without a sprue puller, the sprue stays lodged in the stationary half and either (a) drops into the mould cavity during the next shot (causing defects), or (b) requires manual removal (slowing cycle time). Hence, the sprue puller is a **cycle-time optimization** and **defect-prevention** feature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theory

A sprue puller operates through **four sequential mechanical phases** tied directly to the injection moulding cycle:

### **Phase 1 — Filling (Injection Phase)**
- The mould closes; the nozzle seats against the sprue bushing.
- Molten polymer is injected under pressure (typically **70 MPa – 140 MPa**).
- Plastic flows through the sprue, past the **submerged** sprue puller (whose undercut is fully encapsulated by melt).
- No mechanical interference occurs because the polymer is fluid.

### **Phase 2 — Cooling & Solidification**
- The cavity and sprue cool below the polymer's **heat deflection temperature**.
- The polymer shrinks onto the sprue puller undercut — this shrinkage creates a **mechanical interlock** (similar to a snap-fit).
- Simultaneously, the sprue may *also* shrink onto the stationary sprue bushing wall — this is the **adhesion force** the sprue puller must overcome.

### **Phase 3 — Mould Opening & Pull-Out**
- The mould opens; the stationary half (containing the sprue bushing) retracts away.
- The sprue puller, attached to the moving half via the ejector plate, remains in place.
- The frozen sprue is **mechanically gripped** by the undercut and travels with the moving half.
- The **holding force** of the undercut must exceed the **static friction force** between the sprue and the sprue bushing wall.

### **Phase 4 — Ejection & Reset**
- The ejector system advances; the sprue is pushed out of the mould.
- A **return spring** (or return pin) pushes the sprue puller back to its original position as the ejector plate retracts.
- Cycle repeats.

## 2.2 Governing Physics — The Retention Condition

For the sprue puller to function correctly, the **undercut holding force** must be **greater** than the **static friction force** in the sprue bushing:

$$F_{\text{hold}} \ \geq \ F_{\text{friction}} \ + \ F_{\text{safety}}$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{friction}} &= \mu_{\text{s}} \cdot N_{\text{bushing}} \\
N_{\text{bushing}} &= P_{\text{cavity}} \cdot A_{\text{contact}} \\
F_{\text{hold}} &= \sigma_{\text{shrink}} \cdot A_{\text{undercut}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{\text{hold}}$ | Retention force from undercut interlock | **N** |
| $F_{\text{friction}}$ | Frictional resistance of sprue in bushing | **N** |
| $\mu_{\text{s}}$ | Static coefficient of friction (polymer vs steel) | **0.15 – 0.35** |
| $N_{\text{bushing}}$ | Normal force from cavity pressure on bushing wall | **N** |
| $P_{\text{cavity}}$ | Residual cavity pressure at mould opening | **MPa** |
| $A_{\text{contact}}$ | Contact area of sprue in bushing | **mm²** |
| $\sigma_{\text{shrink}}$ | Shrinkage-induced clamping stress on undercut | **MPa** |
| $A_{\text{undercut}}$ | Effective undercut cross-section | **mm²** |
| $F_{\text{safety}}$ | Engineering safety margin (typically 1.5–2.0×) | **N** |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where Sprue Pullers Are Used

- **Three-plate moulds**: Almost mandatory, because runners stay with the moving half.
- **Two-plate moulds with cold runners**: Recommended when the sprue tends to stay in the stationary half.
- **Hot-runner moulds**: Generally **not required** because the sprue does not solidify.
- **Engineering plastics (PA, POM, PC)**: Highly recommended — these polymers exhibit strong adhesion to polished steel.
- **Soft polymers (LDPE, PP)**: Optional — low adhesion often allows sprue to fall out naturally.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** If the exam states *"three-plate mould"* or *"runner sticks in stationary half"*, the answer must mention a sprue puller as the corrective design feature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Undercut Depth for a Z-Type Sprue Puller

**Given:**
- Sprue diameter: $D = 6 \text{ mm}$
- Cavity pressure at opening: $P_c = 35 \text{ MPa}$
- Coefficient of static friction (PP on tool steel): $\mu_s = 0.25$
- Shrinkage stress on undercut (semi-crystalline polymer): $\sigma_{\text{sh}} = 4 \text{ MPa}$
- Safety factor: $n = 2.0$

**Step 1 — Compute contact area in sprue bushing**

The sprue has a hemispherical/conical seat; approximate contact area as the lateral surface of the bushing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{contact}} &= \pi \cdot D \cdot L_{\text{bushing}} \\
L_{\text{bushing}} &\approx 1.5 \cdot D = 1.5 \cdot 6 = 9 \text{ mm} \\
A_{\text{contact}} &= \pi \cdot 6 \cdot 9 \\
A_{\text{contact}} &= 169.65 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute friction force opposing pull-out**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{friction}} &= \mu_s \cdot P_c \cdot A_{\text{contact}} \\
F_{\text{friction}} &= 0.25 \cdot 35 \text{ N/mm}^2 \cdot 169.65 \text{ mm}^2 \\
F_{\text{friction}} &= 1484.4 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety margin to determine required holding force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{hold,req}} &= n \cdot F_{\text{friction}} \\
F_{\text{hold,req}} &= 2.0 \cdot 1484.4 \\
F_{\text{hold,req}} &= 2968.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for required undercut cross-sectional area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{undercut}} &= \frac{F_{\text{hold,req}}}{\sigma_{\text{sh}}} \\
A_{\text{undercut}} &= \frac{2968.8}{4} \\
A_{\text{undercut}} &= 742.2 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to undercut depth (for a circular puller pin of diameter d)**

The undercut is a circumferential groove. Approximating it as a thin radial lip of depth $h$ and width equal to pin diameter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{undercut}} &\approx \pi \cdot d \cdot h \\
h &= \frac{A_{\text{undercut}}}{\pi \cdot d} = \frac{742.2}{\pi \cdot 6} \\
h &= 39.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Reality check & refinement**

A depth of **39.4 mm** is impractically large. This indicates the **shrinking-stress model** underestimates actual retention. Real sprue pullers work because:

- The sprue **deforms around the hook**, increasing mechanical lock.
- Cooling creates a **vacuum effect** aiding pull-out.
- A depth of **0.5 mm – 1.5 mm** is empirically sufficient because the **mechanical interlock** (not friction) is dominant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often calculate undercut depth using only friction equations and arrive at unrealistic values. The correct approach is to recognize that the **undercut acts as a positive mechanical lock** (like a snap-fit), not a friction device.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Return Spring Force Calculation

**Given:**
- Sprue puller pin mass: $m = 0.05 \text{ kg}$
- Ejector stroke: $s = 25 \text{ mm}$
- Required pin reset reliability (no jamming): target acceleration $a = 2g = 19.62 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Force needed to accelerate the pin**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{inertia}} &= m \cdot a \\
F_{\text{inertia}} &= 0.05 \cdot 19.62 \\
F_{\text{inertia}} &= 0.981 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Force needed to overcome sprue weight (gravity drop during return)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{gravity}} &= m \cdot g = 0.05 \cdot 9.81 = 0.4905 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total return spring force at full compression**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{spring,min}} &= F_{\text{inertia}} + F_{\text{gravity}} + F_{\text{safety}} \\
F_{\text{spring,min}} &= 0.981 + 0.4905 + 2.0 \\
F_{\text{spring,min}} &\approx 3.47 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Spring constant for a 25 mm compression stroke**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{F_{\text{spring,min}}}{s} = \frac{3.47}{25} \\
k &= 0.139 \text{ N/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Derived Quantity | Value | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Required holding force | **2968.8 N** | Mechanical interlock must exceed this |
| Theoretical undercut depth | **39.4 mm** | Impractical — proves interlock dominates |
| Practical undercut depth | **0.5 – 1.5 mm** | Industry-standard empirical range |
| Return spring force | **≥ 3.47 N** | Ensures positive reset of puller pin |
| Spring rate | **0.139 N/mm** | Soft spring — prevents pin binding |

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Type

| Selection Criterion | Z-Pull (Undercut) | Hook Type | Reverse Taper | No Sprue Puller |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Three-plate mould | **Highly Recommended** | Optional | Suitable | Not advised |
| Two-plate mould | **Recommended** | Suitable | Optional | Acceptable |
| Sticky engineering polymers (PA, PC) | **Best choice** | Good | Marginal | Fails often |
| Soft polyolefins (LDPE, PP) | Optional | Optional | Optional | Often works |
| Hot-runner system | **Not needed** | Not needed | Not needed | **Standard** |
| High-speed automated cycle | **Required** | Required | Required | Causes downtime |
| Cosmetic critical parts | **Preferred** | Avoid (marks) | Avoid | Preferred |
| Cycle time impact | None | None | None | Increases (manual) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Sprue Puller Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cycle Start: Mold Closes] --> B[Nozzle Seats on Sprue Bushing]
    B --> C[Molten Polymer Injected under 70-140 MPa]
    C --> D[Plastic Flows Past Sprue Puller Pin]
    D --> E[Cooling Phase Begins]
    E --> F[Polymer Shrinks onto Sprue Puller Undercut]
    F --> G[Mechanical Interlock Forms - Shrink Fit]
    G --> H[Mold Opens: Stationary Half Retracts]
    H --> I{Sprue Puller Holding Force > Bushing Friction?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Sprue Travels with Moving Half]
    I -->|No| K[FAILURE: Sprue Stays in Stationary Half]
    K --> L[Manual Intervention or Defect]
    J --> M[Ejector Plate Advances]
    M --> N[Part and Sprue Ejected Together]
    N --> O[Return Spring Pushes Sprue Puller Back]
    O --> P[Reset Complete - Cycle Repeats]
    P --> A
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StationaryHalf[STATIONARY HALF - Fixed to Machine Platen]
        SB1[Sprue Bushing]
        SB2[Locating Ring]
        SB3[Clamp Plate]
    end

    subgraph MovingHalf[MOVING HALF - Fixed to Moving Platen]
        MP1[Cavity Plate]
        MP2[Core Plate]
        MP3[Support Plate]
    end

    subgraph EjectorSystem[EJECTOR SYSTEM - Retracts and Advances]
        EJ1[Ejector Plate]
        EJ2[Sprue Puller Pin]
        EJ3[Return Spring]
        EJ4[Return Pin]
        EJ5[Ejector Pin for Part]
    end

    SB1 -. molten plastic flows through .-> EJ2
    EJ2 --> EJ1
    EJ3 --> EJ1
    EJ1 --> EJ4
    EJ2 --> EJ5
    MP2 --> EJ5
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Z-Type Sprue Puller Geometry

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MoldClosed
    MoldClosed --> Injection: Molten polymer enters
    Injection --> PackingAndCooling: Pressure held, then cooled
    PackingAndCooling --> MoldOpening: Polymer solidifies on undercut
    MoldOpening --> SprueRetained: Undercut drags frozen sprue from bushing
    SprueRetained --> EjectionForward: Ejector plate advances
    EjectionForward --> SprueReleased: Sprue ejected with part
    SprueReleased --> EjectionReturn: Return spring retracts puller pin
    EjectionReturn --> MoldClosed: Pin returns to original position
    MoldClosed --> [*]: Cycle complete

    note right of SprueRetained
        KEY MECHANICAL EVENT:
        Undercut depth h = 0.5 to 1.5 mm
        creates positive mechanical lock
    end note
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagram distinguishes the *stationary half* (where the sprue bushing lives) from the *moving half* (where the cavity is machined). The sprue puller is mounted on the ejector plate and reaches up toward the sprue bushing when the mould is closed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define a sprue puller. State its location in an injection mould.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A sprue puller is a positive retaining device fitted on the ejector plate of an injection mould, positioned directly beneath the sprue bushing on the moving half. *(1 mark)* Its purpose is to mechanically grip the solidified sprue via an undercut or hook geometry and pull it out of the stationary sprue bushing during mould opening. *(1 mark)* It is typically located at a distance of 2D to 3D (where D = sprue diameter) below the gate entrance to ensure good polymer flow past the feature. *(1 mark)*

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three types of sprue pullers used in injection moulds.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Z-type (undercut-type) sprue puller** — A pin with a circumferential Z-shaped undercut, most common in three-plate moulds.
2. **Hook-type sprue puller** — A bent pin resembling a hook; used where space constraints prevent an undercut.
3. **Reverse-taper sprue puller** — A pin with a tapered head wider than its shank; uses shrinkage to grip the sprue.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a Z-type sprue puller. Why is it preferred over other types?**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
The Z-type sprue puller consists of a hardened steel pin (HRC 58–62) with a precisely machined **circumferential Z-shaped undercut** at its tip. *(0.5 mark)*

1. **During injection:** Molten polymer flows past the submerged pin; the Z-undercut is fully encapsulated and offers no flow obstruction. *(1 mark)*
2. **During cooling:** As the polymer solidifies, it shrinks onto the undercut, creating a **positive mechanical interlock** analogous to a snap-fit joint. *(1 mark)*
3. **During mould opening:** The stationary sprue bushing retracts; the undercut drags the frozen sprue out of the bushing wall. *(1 mark)*
4. **During ejection:** The ejector plate advances; the sprue is released from the undercut and ejected along with the part. *(1 mark)*
5. **Reset:** A return spring pushes the sprue puller pin back to its starting position. *(0.5 mark)*

**Why Preferred (2 marks):**
- Provides **positive mechanical grip** independent of friction. *(1 mark)*
- Self-releasing during ejection — no manual intervention needed. *(1 mark)*
- Compatible with **high-speed automated cycles**.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A sprue puller is to be designed for a 6 mm diameter sprue in a three-plate mould moulding Nylon-6. Calculate the minimum undercut depth if the holding force must exceed 2.5 kN and the shrinkage stress is 5 MPa. Comment on the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write retention condition** *(1 mark)*

$$
F_{\text{hold}} = \sigma_{\text{sh}} \cdot A_{\text{undercut}}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate required undercut area** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{undercut}} &= \frac{F_{\text{hold}}}{\sigma_{\text{sh}}} \\
A_{\text{undercut}} &= \frac{2500 \text{ N}}{5 \text{ N/mm}^2} \\
A_{\text{undercut}} &= 500 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to undercut depth** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{A_{\text{undercut}}}{\pi \cdot D} \\
h &= \frac{500}{\pi \cdot 6} \\
h &= 26.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering comment** *(2 marks)*

The calculated depth of **26.5 mm** is impractical for a 6 mm sprue. This confirms that the **mechanical interlock mechanism** (snap-fit action), not pure shrinkage stress, is the dominant retention principle. *In practice, an undercut depth of 0.8 mm to 1.2 mm* is sufficient because the polymer deforms plastically around the undercut, multiplying the effective holding force by 5×–10×.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Compare hot-runner and cold-runner systems. Under what mould conditions is a sprue puller mandatory?**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Hot Runner | Cold Runner |
|---|---|---|
| Sprue solidifies? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Material waste | Zero | 10–30 % |
| Cycle time | Shorter | Longer |
| Mould cost | High | Low |
| Sprue puller needed? | **No** | **Yes** (often) |
| Colour changeover | Difficult | Easy |

**Conditions where sprue puller is mandatory (3 marks):**
1. **Three-plate moulds** with cold runners — runners must be retained on the moving half. *(1 mark)*
2. **Sticky polymers** such as PA (Nylon), PC, POM — high adhesion to steel. *(1 mark)*
3. **High-speed automated cycles** where manual sprue removal is impractical. *(1 mark)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a block diagram, explain the sequence of events during mould opening when a Z-type sprue puller is used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```
Mould Closed → Plastic Solidified on Sprue Puller
     ↓
Mould Opens → Stationary Half Retracts
     ↓
Sprue Puller (on Moving Half) Drags Sprue out of Bushing
     ↓
Ejector Plate Advances → Part and Sprue Ejected
     ↓
Return Spring Resets Sprue Puller Pin
     ↓
Cycle Repeats
```

**Explanation (3 marks — 1 mark each key point):**
1. **Mould opening initiation:** Stationary platen retracts; sprue is gripped by undercut.
2. **Sprue travel:** Sprue is pulled clear of the bushing along the moving half.
3. **Ejection:** Ejector plate advances; sprue is pushed off the undercut pin and ejected with the part.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Sprue Puller Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing location:** Writing that the sprue puller is on the *stationary* half — it is on the **moving/ejector half**. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Drawing the undercut on the wrong side:** The Z-undercut opens *toward the gate*, not away from it. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Forgetting the return spring:** A sprue puller without a return mechanism will jam during the next cycle. *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Stating "sprue puller holds by friction":** This is incorrect — the dominant mechanism is **mechanical interlock (snap-fit)**, not friction. *(−2 marks)*
> 5. **Omitting distance-from-gate rule (2D–3D):** Examiners frequently test this empirical design rule. *(−1 mark)*
> 6. **Not specifying in three-plate moulds:** Many students wrongly assume sprue pullers are only for two-plate moulds. *(−2 marks)*

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

| # | Key Concept | Must-Remember Value / Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sprue puller location | **Moving half**, mounted on **ejector plate** |
| 2 | Position from gate | **2D to 3D** below gate entrance |
| 3 | Undercut depth | **0.5 mm – 1.5 mm** |
| 4 | Pin diameter | **3 mm – 6 mm** |
| 5 | Pin material hardness | **HRC 58–62** |
| 6 | Retention mechanism | **Mechanical interlock** (not friction) |
| 7 | Most common type | **Z-type sprue puller** |
| 8 | Mandatory in | **Three-plate cold-runner moulds** |
| 9 | Not needed in | **Hot-runner moulds** |
| 10 | Return mechanism | **Return spring** (10–30 N) |
| 11 | Cycle impact | **Zero added cycle time** |
| 12 | Critical for polymers | **PA, PC, POM** (high adhesion) |
| 13 | Release event | During **ejector forward stroke** |
| 14 | Failure mode | Sprue stays in stationary half → defect |
| 15 | KTU exam weightage | **High** — frequently asked in Part A & Part B |

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** Always pair your sprue puller answer with a labelled **Z-shaped undercut sketch** showing the undercut *opening toward the gate*. This single visual cue can earn you the full 14 marks in a Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mould Cooling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mould Cooling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mould Cooling** is the controlled removal of heat from the mould cavity (and consequently from the molten plastic/polymer/metal charge) through a network of precisely machined internal channels through which a circulating heat-transfer fluid (typically water, oil, or glycol-water mixtures) flows at a regulated temperature, flow rate, and pressure. It is one of the most critical operations in **Injection Mould Design** because it directly governs **cycle time**, **part dimensional accuracy**, **warpage**, **residual stresses**, **surface finish**, and overall **production economics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mould cooling accounts for nearly **70–80%** of the total cycle time in a typical injection moulding operation. The remaining time is split between injection (≈5%), holding/cooling (≈75%), and ejection/handling (≈20%). Therefore, cooling channel design is the single biggest lever to reduce cycle time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of mould cooling like the **radiator system in a car engine**:

- The **engine block** is your mould (it gets very hot).
- The **coolant pipes running through the engine** are your cooling channels.
- The **water/antifreeze mixture** is your circulating cooling medium.
- The **radiator fan and pump** are your mould's circulation pump.
- The **thermostat** is your temperature control unit (TCU).

If the pipes are too far apart, too small, or clogged, hot spots appear (just like an overheating engine). The result? Warped parts, burn marks, long cycle times, and expensive scrap. The job of a mould designer is to lay out these "pipes" so that heat is extracted **uniformly and efficiently** from every square millimeter of the cavity surface.

### 1.3 Key Terminology in Mould Cooling

| Term | Standard Symbol | Definition |
|------|----------------|------------|
| Cooling Time | $t_c$ | Time required to eject the part at a safe temperature |
| Heat Transfer Coefficient | $h$ | Rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit $\Delta T$ |
| Reynolds Number | $Re$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces in the coolant |
| Coolant Temperature | $T_{in}, T_{out}$ | Inlet and outlet temperature of the cooling fluid |
| Cooling Channel Diameter | $D$ | Inner diameter of the cooling channel |
| Channel-to-Cavity Distance | $L_d$ | Distance between channel centerline and cavity surface |
| Mould Surface Temperature | $T_{mould}$ | Steady-state temperature of cavity wall |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental principle:** Heat always flows from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature. In mould cooling, heat flows from the **hot plastic** → through the **mould steel** → into the **coolant fluid**. The cooling channel design determines how fast this journey happens.

### 1.4 Why Mould Cooling Matters in Industry

- **Automotive:** Dashboard panels, bumpers, and interior trims require uniform cooling to prevent sink marks and warpage.
- **Medical Devices:** Syringes, vials, and diagnostic cartridges demand precise dimensional control.
- **Electronics:** Thin-wall connectors and housings need rapid, even cooling to maintain tight tolerances.
- **Packaging:** Bottle caps and thin containers need short cycles (often < 2 seconds) to be economically viable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Heat-Transfer Mechanisms in a Mould

The heat from the molten plastic does NOT jump directly into the coolant. It travels in **three distinct stages**:

1. **Conduction through the solid plastic** (from core to cavity wall).
2. **Conduction through the mould steel** (from cavity surface to the cooling channel wall).
3. **Convection from the channel wall to the coolant fluid** (and the fluid carries it away).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 2** is usually the **rate-limiting (bottleneck)** step because mould steel is a solid conductor. That is why **channel placement (i.e., the distance $L_d$ from cavity)** matters more than the coolant flow rate.

### 2.2 Heat Removal Rate (Q-dot) — The Most Important Equation

The total rate of heat that must be removed by the cooling system equals the heat introduced by the injected material:

$$Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{eject}) = \dot{m}_{coolant} \cdot C_{p,coolant} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Total heat to be removed per cycle, in **Watts (W)**
- $m$ = Mass of one shot, in **kg**
- $C_p$ = Specific heat capacity of plastic, in **J/(kg·K)**
- $T_{melt}$ = Melt temperature entering cavity, in **°C**
- $T_{eject}$ = Part ejection temperature, in **°C**
- $\dot{m}_{coolant}$ = Mass flow rate of coolant, in **kg/s**
- $C_{p,coolant}$ = Specific heat capacity of coolant, in **J/(kg·K)**
- $T_{out}, T_{in}$ = Coolant outlet and inlet temperature, in **°C**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $Q = m C_p (T_m - T_e)$ | Total heat to remove per cycle | Energy balance problem (3-mark direct) |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T_c$ | Coolant heat absorption | Cooling fluid selection problem |
| 3 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho C_p \Delta T_c}$ | Required coolant volumetric flow rate | Channel sizing |
| 4 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D \mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | Turbulent vs laminar check |
| 5 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter correlation (turbulent) | Heat transfer coefficient |
| 6 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | Cooling rate analysis |
| 7 | $t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln \left[ \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w} \right]$ | Cooling time (1D transient conduction) | **Most asked 14-mark question** |
| 8 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop across a channel | Pump selection |
| 9 | $f = 0.079 \cdot Re^{-0.25}$ | Blasius friction factor (turbulent) | For $Re < 10^5$ |
| 10 | $T_{mould} = T_{in} + \dfrac{Q}{2 \dot{m} C_{p,coolant}}$ | Average mould surface temperature | Setting TCU |
| 11 | $L_d \leq 3D$ | Recommended channel-to-cavity distance | Design rule of thumb |
| 12 | Channel pitch $P \approx 3D$ to $5D$ | Cooling channel spacing | Uniform cooling design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Rule (KTU-favorite):** For **uniform cooling**, the distance from the cavity surface to the centerline of the cooling channel ($L_d$) should be **at least equal to the channel diameter ($D$) but no more than $3D$**, and the **pitch (center-to-center distance between channels)** should be **3D to 5D**. This gives a temperature variation across the cavity surface of only **±2 °C**, which is the industry benchmark for "good cooling."

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Design Heuristics

- **Why $L_d \leq 3D$?** If the channel is too deep inside the steel, the conduction path becomes long, the local mould surface overheats, and you get hot spots that cause warpage. If it is too close ($< 1D$), the channel wall is thin and may burst under pressure.
- **Why pitch = 3D–5D?** If channels are too close, you waste machining and risk intersecting them. If they are too far, the cooling is non-uniform and you get differential shrinkage.
- **Why turbulent flow?** At $Re > 10{,}000$, the flow is turbulent. Turbulent flow has a much higher heat transfer coefficient ($h$ increases roughly with $Re^{0.8}$), meaning the coolant absorbs heat much faster.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **medical device industry**, a syringe mould may have **24+ cooling channels** in a small plate to maintain ±0.02 mm tolerance. In **packaging**, a 48-cavity PET preform mould uses **baffles and bubblers** to deliver coolant into the core pin. In **die-casting of aluminum**, cooling channels must handle enormous instantaneous heat fluxes (often **5–10 MW/m²**) and use **heat-treated H13 tool steel** to resist thermal fatigue cracking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cooling Time $t_c$ for a Plastic Part

The **cooling time** is the time required for the centerline of the plastic part to cool from the melt temperature $T_m$ down to the ejection temperature $T_e$. The plastic part is approximated as a **semi-infinite slab** of thickness $s$, with mould surface at constant temperature $T_w$.

The governing equation is the **1-D transient heat conduction equation** (Fourier's law):

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 1 — Define non-dimensional temperature:**
$$\theta = \dfrac{T - T_w}{T_m - T_w}$$

**Step 2 — Apply initial and boundary conditions:**
- At $t = 0$: $\theta = 1$ for $0 \leq x \leq s$
- At $x = 0$ and $x = s$: $\theta = 0$ (mould walls at $T_w$)

**Step 3 — Apply separation of variables:**
The general solution for a slab of thickness $s$ with both surfaces held at $T_w$ is:

$$\theta(x,t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n \sin\left(\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi x}{s}\right) \exp\left[-\alpha \left(\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi}{s}\right)^2 t\right]$$

**Step 4 — Determine coefficients from the initial condition:**
Using orthogonality of sine functions, the coefficients become:

$$A_n = \dfrac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} \quad \text{(for the first term, n = 0)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at the centerline $x = s/2$:**
The mid-plane is the slowest-cooling point (this is the **design location** for ejection):

$$\theta_{center} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left[-\alpha \left(\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi}{s}\right)^2 t\right]$$

**Step 6 — For long cooling times, the first term dominates:**
When $t$ is large enough, all higher-order terms decay to near zero. Keeping only the $n=0$ term:

$$\dfrac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \approx \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left[-\alpha \left(\dfrac{\pi}{s}\right)^2 t_c\right]$$

**Step 7 — Solve for cooling time $t_c$:**
Rearranging:

$$\exp\left[-\alpha \left(\dfrac{\pi}{s}\right)^2 t_c\right] = \dfrac{\pi^2}{8} \cdot \dfrac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w}$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$-\alpha \left(\dfrac{\pi}{s}\right)^2 t_c = \ln\left[ \dfrac{\pi^2}{8} \cdot \dfrac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \right]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left[ \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w} \right]\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
