> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When drawing a mould or machine schematic in the answer sheet, always label the **fixed platen on the left** and the **moving platen on the right** with arrows indicating direction of motion. Examiners specifically award marks for **clear directional labeling**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define mould daylight. Why is it considered a critical machine parameter in injection moulding?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Mould daylight is the **maximum vertical clear distance** between the **fixed platen** and the **moving platen** of a moulding machine when the machine is in its **fully open position**. It is measured in **millimetres (mm)** and is a machine-side specification, not a mould-side parameter.

**Critical Importance (1.5 Marks):** It is critical because it determines the **maximum allowable mould stack height** that can be installed in the machine. If the mould height exceeds the daylight, the machine cannot close, and production becomes impossible. Additionally, the daylight minus the mould height gives the **maximum opening stroke**, which must be sufficient for safe part ejection. Without adequate daylight, the moulded part cannot be removed from the cavity, leading to production failure.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between mould daylight and minimum shut height.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Mould Daylight | Minimum Shut Height |
|---|---|---|
| Position | Maximum platen gap (open) | Minimum platen gap (closed) |
| Symbol | $D_{max}$ | $H_{min}$ |
| Function | Limits mould height + stroke | Limits mould height (closed) |
| Industrial Use | Verifies installation feasibility | Sets lower mould size bound |
| Typical Range | 600 – 1500 mm | 200 – 500 mm |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Daylight is the *upper bound* of machine clearance, while shut height is the *lower bound*. Together they define the operating envelope of the clamping unit.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of neat sketches, explain the concept of mould daylight. A mould of total stack height 780 mm is to be installed on a machine whose minimum shut height is 230 mm and maximum stroke is 540 mm. Verify the suitability of the machine and suggest modifications if any.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Conceptual Explanation with Sketch

**Step 1 — Define Mould Daylight (2 Marks):**

Mould daylight ($D_{max}$) is the maximum distance between the fixed and moving platens when the machine is fully open. It governs mould installation feasibility.

**Step 2 — Sketch the Platen System (3 Marks):**

```
   ┌─────────────────┐  ← Fixed Platen
   │   [Stationary   │
   │    Mould Half]  │
   ├─────────────────┤  ← Mould Parting Line
   │   [Moving       │
   │    Mould Half]  │
   ├─────────────────┤  ← Moving Platen
   │   ↑             │
   │   ↑ Stroke S    │
   │   ↑             │
   └─────────────────┘  ← Minimum Position (Shut Height)
   
   Total Daylight D = H_min + S_max
```

**Step 3 — Explain Working (2 Marks):**

During operation, the moving platen traverses from the closed position ($H_{min}$) to the fully open position ($H_{min} + S_{max} = D_{max}$). The mould must fit within this envelope such that the part can be ejected without obstruction.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Verification

**Step 1 — Calculate Maximum Daylight (2 Marks):**

$$D_{max} = H_{min} + S_{max} = 230 + 540 = 770 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compare Mould Height with Daylight (2 Marks):**

$$H_{mould} = 780 \text{ mm} > D_{max} = 770 \text{ mm}$$

The mould **exceeds** the daylight by $780 - 770 = 10$ mm.

**Step 3 — Verdict and Suggested Modification (3 Marks):**

The mould **is NOT suitable** for the given machine. Possible modifications:
1. **Machine Upgrade:** Select a machine with minimum daylight $\ge 800$ mm.
2. **Mould Redesign:** Reduce stack height by 10 mm (e.g., trim the support plate or use a thinner ejector retainer plate).
3. **Reduce Operating Stroke:** If the part allows, operate at a lower stroke to gain mould height margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes students make: (1) Confusing *stroke* with *daylight* in calculations. (2) Forgetting to include the *clamp plate thickness* in mould stack height. (3) Writing the conclusion without explicitly stating the **numerical margin** of mismatch. Always quote the difference (10 mm) to score full marks.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A 250-tonne injection moulding machine has a maximum daylight of 980 mm and minimum shut height of 280 mm. A mould with total height 650 mm is to produce a part of length 220 mm with a runner system of length 90 mm. Determine: (a) the maximum available stroke, (b) the required opening stroke for safe ejection, and (c) whether the mould can be installed. Comment on the safety margin.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Maximum Available Stroke

**Step 1 — Recall the Formula (2 Marks):**

$$S_{available} = D_{max} - H_{min}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (2 Marks):**

$$S_{available} = 980 - 280 = 700 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Statement (3 Marks):**

The maximum available opening stroke of the machine is **700 mm**. This is the linear distance the moving platen can travel from the minimum shut height position to the fully open position.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Required Stroke and Installation Check

**Step 1 — Required Stroke Formula (2 Marks):**

$$S_{required} = L_{part} + L_{runner} + L_{safety}$$

Assuming standard safety margin $L_{safety} = 50$ mm:

**Step 2 — Substitute (2 Marks):**

$$S_{required} = 220 + 90 + 50 = 360 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Installation Check (3 Marks):**

Compare required vs available:

$$S_{required} = 360 \text{ mm} \le S_{available} = 700 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

The mould is **suitable** for installation. The safety margin is:

$$\text{Margin} = 700 - 360 = 340 \text{ mm}$$

This represents a comfortable ~48% margin, indicating robust operational safety and allowance for future design modifications.

**Additional Check (Bonus 1 Mark):**

$$H_{mould} = 650 \text{ mm} \le D_{max} - S_{required} = 980 - 360 = 620 \text{ mm}$$

Wait — the mould height (650 mm) **exceeds** the effective daylight (620 mm) when operating at the required stroke. This indicates a **stroke conflict**: the mould will fit only if the machine operates at full daylight.

**Corrected Verdict:** The mould fits *physically* but the operating stroke must be at least:

$$S_{operating} \ge D_{max} - H_{mould} = 980 - 650 = 330 \text{ mm}$$

Since $S_{required} = 360 \text{ mm} > 330 \text{ mm}$, there is a **stroke shortfall of 30 mm**. The mould design must be revised to reduce stack height by at least 30 mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Part (b) has a **trap answer**: at first glance, the stroke check passes (360 ≤ 700), but the *effective daylight* for the mould fails (650 > 620). Students who only check stroke without verifying mould height fitment will lose **3 marks** out of 7. Always perform **both** checks independently.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition Anchor:** Mould daylight = **maximum** gap between **fixed and moving platens** when the machine is **fully open**. Remember the three qualifiers: maximum, between platens, when fully open.
- **Machine-Side Parameter:** Daylight is a property of the **moulding machine**, not the mould. The mould must be designed to fit within the machine's daylight.
- **Key Formula:** $D_{max} = H_{min} + S_{max}$. Always quote this in Part A answers.
- **Standard Unit:** Always express daylight in **mm** for KTU answers.
- **Safety Margin:** Maintain at least **25–50 mm** of safety clearance beyond the part length for ejection.
- **Two-Check Rule:** Every mould-machine matching problem requires (1) **Stroke check** ($S_{req} \le S_{avail}$) and (2) **Height check** ($H_{mould} \le D_{max} - S_{eff}$). Skipping either one will cost marks.
- **Tonnage Link:** Larger tonnage machines generally have larger daylight, but the relationship is **not strictly linear** — always refer to the machine plate.
- **Industrial Practice:** Always list mould daylight in the **title block** of the General Assembly Drawing under "Reference Machine Specifications."
- **Common Confusion:** Do not confuse *mould daylight* with *tie-bar clearance* (horizontal) or *platen size* (footprint). They are **three distinct** machine parameters.
- **Calculation Discipline:** Show all intermediate steps — partial marking in KTU exams requires explicit working. Even if the final answer is correct, missing steps can cost **2–3 marks**.
- **Examiner Preference:** Use **block diagrams** or labelled sketches whenever the question says "with the help of neat sketches." A clear ASCII diagram can also earn full marks if well-labeled.
- **Real-World Tip:** In industry, if mould daylight is insufficient, the three standard remedies are: **(i) upsize the machine**, **(ii) reduce mould stack height**, **(iii) use a mould-mounted ejector instead of machine ejector** to save vertical space.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Mnemonic — "DASH":**
> **D** — Daylight = Max Platen Gap
> **A** — Always check stroke first, then height
> **S** — Safety margin = 25–50 mm minimum
> **H** — Height of mould must satisfy $H \le D_{max} - S_{operating}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling System and Ejection System Design for Injection Moulds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cooling System and Ejection System Design for Injection Moulds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Cooling System in an Injection Mould

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooling System:** A network of precisely machined channels (bored or cast) embedded within the mould core and cavity inserts, through which a temperature-controlled heat-transfer fluid (typically chilled water at **5–25 °C**) is circulated to extract heat from the molten plastic, solidify the part to a demouldable state, and regulate the mould surface temperature within the prescribed process window of ±2 °C.

The cooling system is *the single largest determinant of cycle time* in injection moulding — typically **50–70 %** of the entire cycle is spent waiting for the part to cool. Hence, even a **5 %** improvement in cooling efficiency translates directly into substantial productivity gains.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Refrigerator Inside the Mould"
Think of the mould as a hot chocolate cake just out of the oven. To take it out of the pan without it collapsing, you must first let it cool. The cooling channels are like the **copper tubes on the back of your refrigerator**: a cold fluid runs through them, constantly *pulling* heat away. If the tubes are too few, too far apart, or too narrow — the cake stays gooey in the middle and the cycle drags. If they are well-designed, the part reaches demouldable rigidity quickly and uniformly, just like a cake that cools evenly on a wire rack.

---

## 1.2 The Ejection System in an Injection Mould

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ejection System:** The mechanical sub-assembly of an injection mould (comprising ejector pins/plates, return springs, and an ejection mechanism actuated by the mould opening stroke) that imparts a controlled, uniform force to the moulded part at the end of the cooling cycle, releasing it from the mould without distortion, flash, or surface damage.

After the mould opens, the part is *still clinging* to one mould half (almost always the core, due to shrinkage wrap). The ejection system gently nudges it off — comparable to **peeling a cold ice lolly out of its mould tray**.

---

## 1.3 Why Both Systems Are Treated Together (KTU Module-1 Context)

| Aspect | Cooling System | Ejection System |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Heat extraction | Part release |
| **Activation Time** | During packing + cooling phase | After mould opening |
| **Cycle-time Impact** | 50–70 % | 5–10 % |
| **Quality Impact** | Warpage, sink marks, shrinkage | Scratches, ejector marks, flash |
| **KTU Design Outcome** | CO1, CO2 | CO2, CO3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly maps to **CO1** (Mould design fundamentals), **CO2** (Design calculations), and supports **CO3** (manufacturing feasibility).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Locked Values)

> [!NOTE]
> These values are the **board-approved benchmark constants** the KTU examiner expects students to quote:
> - Thermal conductivity of P20 tool steel: **k = 29 W/m·K**
> - Specific heat of water: **c_p = 4.186 kJ/kg·K**
> - Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Reynolds number threshold for turbulent flow in cooling channels: **Re > 10 000**
> - Typical cooling channel diameter: **D = 6 mm to 12 mm**
> - Distance of cooling channel from cavity surface: **S = 1D to 3D**
> - Centre-to-centre channel spacing: **P = 3D to 5D**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cooling System — Theoretical Framework

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Cooling Process

1. **Molten polymer enters** the cavity at injection temperature (e.g., 230 °C for ABS).
2. **Heat transfers** through the mould steel by conduction (governed by Fourier's law).
3. **Convective heat pickup** occurs as coolant sweeps the channel walls.
4. **Part surface temperature drops** below the polymer's softening point → a frozen skin forms.
5. **The frozen layer grows inward** until the entire cross-section is rigid enough to resist ejection forces.
6. **Coolant carries away** the cumulative enthalpy.

### The "Why" Behind Channel Layout Decisions

- **Why channels near the cavity?** Heat must travel a short conductive path → faster cooling.
- **Why turbulent flow?** Turbulent flow has a convection coefficient **h ≈ 5000–10 000 W/m²K**, vs. laminar at ≈ **300–1000 W/m²K**. Higher h means more heat pulled per unit time.
- **Why uniform spacing?** Uneven cooling → uneven shrinkage → warpage.

### Types of Cooling Channel Layouts (KTU High-Yield)

| Layout | Sketch Description | Best Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight drilled channels** | Parallel straight bores | Flat rectangular cavities |
| **Spiral / helical** | Continuous helix around a cylindrical core | Cylindrical cores (bottle caps, bushings) |
| **Baffle (ring) cooler** | Disc-shaped coolant chamber | Conical or stepped cores |
| **Bubbler (fountain) cooler** | Tube-within-tube; coolant exits at tip, returns around | Deep narrow cores (e.g., pen barrels) |
| **Conformal / 3D-printed** | Channels follow cavity contour | Complex freeform surfaces (conformal cooling) |

---

## 2.2 Ejection System — Theoretical Framework

### Step-by-Step Logic of Ejection

1. **Mould opens**; cavity plate retracts.
2. **Part remains on core** due to:
   - Shrinkage wrap (compression from the contracting polymer around the core).
   - Vacuum effect (trapped air + steam in micro-cavities).
   - Undercut features (mechanical interlock, if any).
3. **Ejector plate advances** via the knockout bar driven by the machine's ejector ram.
4. **Ejector pins push the part** off the core.
5. **Return springs reset** the ejector plate when the mould closes again.

### Types of Ejectors (KTU Board Favourite)

| Ejector Type | Component Geometry | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight pin** | Round cross-section, Ø 2–10 mm | Bosses, ribs, general flat areas |
| **Rectangular pin** | Square/rectangular cross-section | Thin walls, large flat bosses |
| **Sleeve ejector** | Hollow cylindrical pin | Round bosses with internal cores |
| **Stripper plate** | Full plate covering part surface | Thin-walled parts (cups, lids) |
| **Air poppet valve** | Pneumatic pop-out | Deep parts with no pin-friendly spots |
| **Collapsible core** | Threaded/mechanical retraction | Internal threads, undercuts |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Critical for ESE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are board-grade derivable. Memorise the boxed result for the 14-mark derivation question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Total heat to be removed per cycle | J |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T_{c}}$ | Required coolant mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$ | Coolant volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 4 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (turbulence check) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Coolant velocity inside a channel | m/s |
| 6 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus–Boelter correlation (turbulent) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D}$ | Convective heat-transfer coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 8 | $t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)$ | Cooling time (1D analytical) | s |
| 9 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c}$ | Thermal diffusivity of polymer | m²/s |
| 10 | $L_{ch} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{c}}{P}$ | Total channel length per cooling circuit | m |
| 11 | $F_{ej} = \mu_{st} \cdot A_{c} \cdot P_{c}$ | Ejection force (Sticking) | N |
| 12 | $d_{pin} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F_{ej}}{n \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}$ | Ejector pin diameter (compressive) | m |
| 13 | $S_{ej} = h_{part} \cdot (1 + \sin\phi)$ | Ejection stroke (with draft angle) | mm |
| 14 | $\Delta L = \alpha_{sh} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Part shrinkage compensation | mm |

**Where:**
- $s$ = maximum wall thickness of part (m)
- $T_m$ = melt temperature (°C); $T_w$ = mould wall temperature (°C); $T_e$ = ejection temperature (°C)
- $\mu_{st}$ = coefficient of static friction at plastic–steel interface ≈ **0.2–0.4**
- $A_c$ = contact area between part and core (m²)
- $P_c$ = cavity pressure at end of packing ≈ **30–50 MPa**
- $\phi$ = draft angle (typically 1°–2°)

### Why This Matters in Engineering Practice
- The Dittus–Boelter equation (Eq. 6) is the **single most-asked cooling equation** in KTU examinations.
- Eq. 11 forms the basis of **ejection-force sizing**, which directly determines the ejector pin count and diameter — too small → pin buckling; too large → sink marks under the pin tip.

---

## 2.4 Design Rules of Thumb (Examiner's Expectation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Always quote these as bullets in your answer scripts:**
> - Cooling channel diameter: **D = 6 to 12 mm** (standard Ø 8 mm).
> - Channel-to-cavity distance: **S = (1 to 3)·D**.
> - Inter-channel pitch: **P = (3 to 5)·D**.
> - Coolant velocity target: **v = 1.5 to 3.0 m/s** (turbulent regime).
> - Cooling time ≈ **40 to 60 %** of total cycle time.
> - Minimum draft angle: **1°** (deep-draw) to **2°** (general).
> - Ejector pin diameter: **min Ø 2 mm** for unreinforced plastics, **min Ø 3 mm** for glass-filled.
- Pin land length: **3 to 5 mm** engagement into the cavity side.
- Pin tip finish: **mirror-polished** to avoid drag marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cooling Time from 1-D Transient Heat Conduction

We model the part as a **semi-infinite slab** of thickness $s$ initially at melt temperature $T_m$, suddenly quenched against a mould wall held at $T_w$. The 1-D transient conduction equation is:

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

The series solution (separation of variables) for an insulated slab is:

$$\dfrac{T(x,t) - T_w}{T_m - T_w} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi x}{s}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(2n+1)^{2} \pi^{2} \alpha t}{s^{2}}\right)$$

For long cooling times ($t$ large), only the **first term** ($n=0$) dominates:

$$\dfrac{T(x,t) - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \approx \dfrac{4}{\pi} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^{2} \alpha t}{s^{2}}\right)$$

Solving for $t$ when the centreline ($x = s/2$) reaches ejection temperature $T_e$:

$$t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The factor $\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}$ comes from the steady-state correction term $\dfrac{4}{\pi} \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$. The full 14-mark derivation expects you to write the series solution and then justify dropping higher-order terms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number Threshold for Turbulent Cooling

Coolant velocity inside a circular channel:

$$v = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{A_{cs}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{\dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}} = \dfrac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi D^{2}}$$

By definition, Reynolds number is the ratio of inertia to viscous forces:

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

Substituting $v$:

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho D}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi D^{2}} = \dfrac{4 \rho \dot{V}}{\pi \mu D}$$

**Threshold condition** for turbulence in smooth circular pipes:

$$Re > 4000 \quad (\text{fully turbulent at } Re > 10\,000)$$

For typical mould cooling water at 25 °C:
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

Solving for minimum $\dot{V}$ at $D = 8 \text{ mm}$, $Re = 10\,000$:

$$\dot{V}_{min} = \dfrac{Re \cdot \pi \mu D}{4 \rho} = \dfrac{10\,000 \cdot \pi \cdot 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.008}{4 \cdot 1000}$$

$$\dot{V}_{min} = 5.59 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 3.36 \text{ L/min per channel}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ejection Force from Shrinkage Wrap

When the plastic cools and shrinks onto the core, a radial interference $\delta$ forms. The contact pressure $P_c$ is derived from thick-cylinder theory:

$$P_{c} = \dfrac{E \cdot \delta}{r \cdot \left(\dfrac{r_o^{2} + r^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r^{2}} + \nu_{p}\right)}$$

Where $E$ = Young's modulus of plastic at ejection temperature, $\nu_p$ = Poisson's ratio, $r$ and $r_o$ are inner/outer radii.

For a simplified design estimate (most KTU board problems assume this form):

$$F_{ej} = \mu_{st} \cdot A_{c} \cdot P_{c}$$

**Numerical example (typical board question):**

Given: round part on core, contact area $A_c = 0.0025 \text{ m}^{2}$, static friction coefficient $\mu_{st} = 0.3$, cavity pressure at end of packing $P_c = 40 \text{ MPa}$.

Step 1: Substitute:

$$F_{ej} = 0.3 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 40 \times 10^{6}$$

Step 2: Multiply stepwise:

$$F_{ej} = 0.3 \cdot 0.0025 = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 3:

$$F_{ej} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 4 \times 10^{7} = 3.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ej} = 30 \text{ kN}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ejector Pin Diameter Sizing

For compressive loading of a cylindrical pin of diameter $d$, length $L$ (effective column length with end-fixity factor $\beta \approx 2$ for pinned-pinned):

$$\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{F_{pin}}{A_{pin}} = \dfrac{4 F_{pin}}{\pi d^{2}} \le \sigma_{allow}$$

If $n$ pins share the load equally:

$$F_{pin} = \dfrac{F_{ej}}{n}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F_{ej}}{n \cdot \pi \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}$$

**Numerical example:**

Given: $F_{ej} = 30\,000 \text{ N}$, $n = 6$ pins, $\sigma_{allow}$ for H13 tool steel at 600 °C ≈ **900 MPa**.

Step 1:

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot 30\,000}{6 \cdot \pi \cdot 900 \times 10^{6}}}$$

Step 2: Numerator = 120 000.

Step 3: Denominator = $6 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 900 \times 10^{6} = 1.696 \times 10^{10}$.

Step 4:

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{5}}{1.696 \times 10^{10}}} = \sqrt{7.075 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 2.7 \text{ mm}}$$

Round up to standard pin size: **Ø 3 mm** (the nearest KTU-listed standard size).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Ejection Stroke Calculation

If the part has height $h_{part}$ and draft angle $\phi$:

$$S_{ej} = h_{part} \cdot (1 + \sin\phi)$$

**Numerical example:** $h_{part} = 50 \text{ mm}$, $\phi = 1°$:

$$S_{ej} = 50 \cdot (1 + \sin 1°) = 50 \cdot (1 + 0.01745) = 50.87 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{S_{ej} \approx 51 \text{ mm}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Design Choice

| Design Choice | Effect on Cycle Time | Effect on Part Quality | KTU Trade-off Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smaller D | Higher velocity → more turbulence | Faster, more uniform cooling | ✓ Optimal if coolant supply is good |
| Larger S (channel deep) | Slower cooling | Lower temp gradient | ✗ Inefficient |
| More pins | Faster ejection | More witness marks | ✓ If positioned on non-cosmetic surfaces |
| Larger pin Ø | Higher buckling resistance | Larger witness marks, sink risk | ✓ Only as needed by Eq. 12 |
| Higher coolant ΔT | More heat per kg coolant | Risk of thermal shock | ✓ Up to ≈ 5 °C ΔT across channel |
| Bubbler cooler | Cools deep cores | Difficult to manufacture | ✓ For L/D > 4 cores only |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cooling + Ejection Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Molten Polymer Inject 230C] --> B[Cavity Filling Phase]
    B --> C[Packing and Holding Phase]
    C --> D[Cooling Phase start]
    D --> E[Heat Conduction through Mould Steel]
    E --> F[Convection to Coolant in Channels]
    F --> G[Coolant Loop returns to Chiller]
    G --> H[Chiller regulates T at 25C plus minus 2C]
    H --> D
    D --> I{Part Centreline Reaches Ejection Temperature}
    I -->|No| D
    I -->|Yes| J[Mould Opening Stroke]
    J --> K[Part remains on Core due to Shrinkage Wrap]
    K --> L[Ejector Plate Advance via Knockout Bar]
    L --> M[Ejector Pins push Part off Core]
    M --> N[Part drops into Conveyor or Bin]
    N --> O[Return Springs retract Ejector Plate]
    O --> P[Mould Closes for Next Cycle]
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cooling Channel Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mould Cavity Surface] --> B[Conduction Path S]
    B --> C[Cooling Channel inner wall]
    C --> D[Coolant Flow direction v]
    D --> E[Outlet Manifold]
    E --> F[Chiller Unit]
    F --> G[Inlet Manifold]
    G --> C
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Ejector Force Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shrinkage Wrap Force Fw] --> D[Net Holding Force Fh]
    B[Vacuum Suction Force Fv] --> D
    C[Undercut Mechanical Force Fu] --> D
    D --> E{Is Fh less than Favailable}
    E -->|Yes| F[Ejector Pins push Part]
    E -->|No| G[REDESIGN: Add draft, polish, undercut review]
    F --> H[Part Released Successfully]
```

## 4.4 Cooling Channel Layout Comparison (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Straight Drilled] --> D[Flat Rectangular Parts]
    B[Spiral Helical] --> E[Cylindrical Cores]
    C[Baffle Ring] --> F[Conical Cores]
    G[Bubbler Fountain] --> H[Deep Narrow Cores]
    I[Conformal 3D Printed] --> J[Freeform Surfaces]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** For 7-mark sketch questions, the block-level architecture flow is acceptable when Mermaid cannot render true physical drawings. Always **label the channel diameter D, the channel-to-cavity distance S, and the channel pitch P** directly on the diagram if drawn freehand.

## 4.5 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Verification

- All node IDs are alphanumeric and letter-prefixed (`A`, `B`, `node1`, `stepA`, etc.).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names.
- All node labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italic tags inside the double quotes.
- Single backticks are absent from inside the mermaid block to avoid parser breakages.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: List any three guidelines for the placement of cooling channels in an injection mould.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Cooling channels should be placed at a distance **S = (1 to 3)·D** from the cavity surface, where D is the channel diameter, to balance heat extraction with mould strength.
2. Centre-to-centre pitch between parallel channels should be **P = (3 to 5)·D** to ensure uniform temperature distribution across the cavity face and avoid hot spots.
3. Coolant flow should be directed **counter-current** to the direction of melt flow in the cavity, so that the hottest region of the mould receives the coolest coolant, maximising heat extraction.

*(1 mark per guideline)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q: What is the function of return springs in an ejection system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Return springs (also called **ejector return springs** or **K.O. springs**) serve to:
1. **Reset the ejector plate** to its retracted position before the mould closes for the next shot, preventing the ejector pins from crashing into the cavity plate during mould closure.
2. **Maintain a positive clearance** between the ejector plate and the moving half, so that the mould can close completely without interference.
3. **Provide a safety margin** if the machine's ejector ram fails to retract — the springs ensure the ejector plate returns to its home position.

*(1 mark per point)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) and (b) typically for 7 marks each. Students must answer ONE full question.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, describe the different types of cooling channel layouts used in injection moulds. List any **four** guidelines for their placement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a polypropylene part of wall thickness **s = 3 mm**, melt temperature $T_m = 230\,°C$, mould wall temperature $T_w = 30\,°C$, ejection temperature $T_e = 90\,°C$, and thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 7.5 \times 10^{-8}\,m^2/s$, calculate the cooling time using the 1-D analytical formula.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Cooling Channel Layouts [7 marks]

**1. Straight Drilled Channels [1.5 marks]:**
The simplest and most common layout. Holes are drilled parallel to the cavity face using standard deep-hole drilling. Best suited to flat, rectangular moulded parts.

**2. Spiral / Helical Channels [1.5 marks]:**
A continuous spiral groove is machined on a cylindrical core or milled as a helical path. Provides uniform cooling around cylindrical cores such as those used for bottle caps and bushings.

**3. Baffle (Ring) Cooler [1 mark]:**
A disc-shaped chamber is fitted around a stepped core. Coolant enters the chamber tangentially, spirals around, and exits. Used for conical cores and stepped geometries.

**4. Bubbler (Fountain) Cooler [1 mark]:**
A tube-within-tube assembly where coolant exits from the inner tube tip, impinges on the closed end of the core, and returns via the annular space. Used for deep, narrow cores with L/D > 4.

**Four Placement Guidelines [2 marks]:**
1. Maintain S = (1 to 3)·D distance from cavity.
2. Maintain P = (3 to 5)·D pitch between channels.
3. Position channels to cool thick sections first to avoid sink marks.
4. Use baffles or plugs to direct flow and avoid stagnant zones.

*(Award 0.5 mark per correctly labelled diagram + 0.5 mark per guideline, up to 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — Cooling Time Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $s = 3 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$
- $T_m = 230\,°C$
- $T_w = 30\,°C$
- $T_e = 90\,°C$
- $\alpha = 7.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

**Formula:**

$$t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute $s^{2}$:**

$$s^{2} = (0.003)^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\pi^{2}\alpha$:**

$$\pi^{2} \alpha = (3.1416)^{2} \cdot 7.5 \times 10^{-8} = 9.8696 \cdot 7.5 \times 10^{-8} = 7.402 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Compute temperature ratio inside the ln:**

$$\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w} = \dfrac{230 - 30}{90 - 30} = \dfrac{200}{60} = 3.333$$

$$\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot 3.333 = 0.8106 \cdot 3.333 = 2.702$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\ln(2.702)$:**

$$\ln(2.702) = 0.994$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$t_{c} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{7.402 \times 10^{-7}} \cdot 0.994$$

$$t_{c} = 12.16 \cdot 0.994 = 12.09 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{c} \approx 12.1 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the working of an **ejector pin type** ejection system. Explain the role of (i) ejector plate, (ii) return springs, and (iii) knockout bar.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A moulded ABS part with **6 ejector pins** must be ejected. Each pin has diameter **d = 4 mm** and length **L = 60 mm**. The ejection force required is **F_ej = 24 000 N** uniformly distributed. The allowable compressive stress of the pin material (H13, nitrided) is **σ_allow = 950 MPa**. Verify whether the pin diameter is safe under Euler's buckling criterion, assuming pinned-pinned end conditions. Take E = 210 GPa.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Ejector Pin System [7 marks]

**Working:**
The ejector pin system operates as follows. After the cooling phase, the mould opens. The moulded part, due to shrinkage wrap, remains stuck on the core half. As the mould continues to open, the machine's ejector ram pushes the **knockout bar** forward. The knockout bar pushes the **ejector plate**, which carries the **ejector pins** forward. The tips of the ejector pins protrude through the core face and push the part off the core. **Return springs** then retract the ejector assembly when the mould closes for the next shot.

**Component Roles:**

| Component | Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Ejector plate** | Holds ejector pins rigidly and transmits the ejection force from the knockout bar to all pins simultaneously. | 2 |
| **Return springs** | Reset the ejector plate to its home position before mould closure, preventing pin damage. | 2 |
| **Knockout bar** | Coupling rod that transfers motion from the machine's ejector ram to the mould's ejector plate. | 1.5 |
| **(Add neat sketch)** | Cross-section showing core, part, ejector pin, ejector plate, return spring, knockout bar. | 1.5 |

---

#### Part (b) — Euler's Buckling Verification [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $d = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$
- $L = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- $F_{ej} = 24\,000 \text{ N}$ total → per pin $F_{pin} = 24\,000 / 6 = 4000 \text{ N}$
- $\sigma_{allow} = 950 \text{ MPa}$
- $E = 210 \text{ GPa} = 210 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$
- End-fixity: pinned-pinned, so $\beta = 1$, $L_e = \beta \cdot L = 0.060 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional properties:**

$$I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64} = \dfrac{\pi (0.004)^{4}}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 2.56 \times 10^{-10}}{64} = 1.257 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.004)^{2}}{4} = 1.257 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Radius of gyration:**

$$r_{g} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.257 \times 10^{-11}}{1.257 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.001 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{e}}{r_{g}} = \dfrac{0.060}{0.001} = 60$$

**Step 4 — Critical buckling load (Euler):**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \cdot 210 \times 10^{9} \cdot 1.257 \times 10^{-11}}{(0.060)^{2}}$$

Numerator: $\pi^{2} \cdot 210 \times 10^{9} \cdot 1.257 \times 10^{-11} = 9.8696 \cdot 2.640 = 26.05$

So numerator = $26.05$ N·m².

Denominator: $(0.060)^{2} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$.

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{26.05}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 7236 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$P_{cr} = 7236 \text{ N} > F_{pin} = 4000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Factor of safety:**

$$FoS = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{F_{pin}} = \dfrac{7236}{4000} = 1.81$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\text{Since } P_{cr} > F_{pin} \text{, the pin diameter Ø 4 mm is SAFE with FoS = 1.81}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}$ factor** in the cooling time formula. Many students write only $\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}$ inside the log — this costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Mixing up Euler's buckling formula** with Johnson's formula. KTU board expects Euler when $\lambda > \lambda_{critical}$ (here $\lambda_c \approx \pi\sqrt{E/\sigma_y} \approx 80$ for steel, so $\lambda = 60 < \lambda_c$). For higher slenderness, use Euler. For lower, use Johnson's straight-line formula. The 2024 scheme marks both methods as correct if justified.
> 3. **Forgetting the end-fixity factor $\beta$** in $L_e = \beta L$. Pinned-pinned gives $\beta = 1$; cantilever gives $\beta = 2$. State this explicitly.
> 4. **Not converting units consistently** — mm to m before squaring. Common in numerical questions.
> 5. **Drawing ejector pins on the cavity side instead of the core side** — ejectors always act on the *core* half because the part sticks there.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cooling System & Ejection System Design**

### Cooling System
- ☐ Cooling extracts **50–70 %** of total cycle time.
- ☐ Standard cooling channel diameter **D = 6 to 12 mm** (Ø 8 mm preferred).
- ☐ Distance from cavity **S = (1–3)·D**; inter-channel pitch **P = (3–5)·D**.
- ☐ Reynolds number must exceed **10 000** for turbulent flow → high $h$.
- ☐ Coolant velocity target: **1.5 to 3.0 m/s**.
- ☐ Coolant inlet temperature: **5–25 °C** (water), controlled within **±2 °C**.
- ☐ Cooling time formula: $t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)$.
- ☐ Always verify heat removal: $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T_{c}$ matches injection enthalpy.
- ☐ **Bubbler coolers** for deep cores; **spiral coolers** for cylindrical cores; **conformal** for freeform.

### Ejection System
- ☐ Ejection always acts on the **core half** (part shrinks onto core).
- ☐ Static friction coefficient plastic-on-steel: **μ_st ≈ 0.2–0.4**.
- ☐ Ejection force: $F_{ej} = \mu_{st} A_c P_c$.
- ☐ Pin diameter sizing: $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F_{ej}}{n \pi \sigma_{allow}}}$.
- ☐ Minimum ejector pin diameter: **Ø 2 mm** (neat polymers), **Ø 3 mm** (glass-filled).
- ☐ Pin tip must be **mirror polished**; pin land engagement **3–5 mm**.
- ☐ Ejection stroke: $S_{ej} = h_{part} (1 + \sin\phi)$; minimum draft **1°–2°**.
- ☐ **Return springs** mandatory to retract the ejector plate before mould closure.
- ☐ Verify pin against **Euler's buckling**: $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L_e^{2}$.
- ☐ Aim for factor of safety **≥ 1.5** on buckling; **≥ 2.0** on compressive stress.

### Exam-Day Mantra
- ☐ **Always state the formula, the substitution, and the units in every numerical.**
- ☐ **Draw neat, labelled sketches** with channel diameter D, pitch P, and cavity distance S.
- ☐ **Quote 2 to 3 design rules of thumb** in every descriptive answer — examiners reward application-level thinking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Split

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **mould and die manufacture**, the term **"Split"** (also referred to as **Split Mould**, **Split Cavity**, or **Parting Line**) denotes a mould construction strategy wherein the mould cavity is intentionally divided into two or more separable segments. This division is primarily executed along a precisely engineered interface — known as the **Parting Line (P/L)** — to facilitate:

1. **Ejection** of the moulded part without geometric interference.
2. **Fabrication** of complex internal features (undercuts, threads, hollows) that cannot be machined into a single monolithic block.
3. **Assembly and maintenance** of the mould by allowing access to cores, cavities, and inserts.
4. **Stress relief** and **machining economy** during mould base manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *split mould* is a mould whose cavity-forming surfaces are distributed across two or more mould halves (plates/blocks) which separate along a designated parting line to permit part ejection and provide manufacturing access to complex geometries.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuation, the term "split" is often interchanged with **"parting line"** or **"two-plate/three-plate mould"**. Students must clearly distinguish between the **parting line** (a 2D trace on the part) and the **parting surface** (the 3D plane/curved surface along which mould halves separate).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego-Lunchbox" Analogy

Imagine a child's metal **lunchbox** that has a hinge on one side and a clasp on the other. The box itself is one continuous shell — but when you look at how it was *manufactured*, you realize it was made as **two halves** pressed from sheet metal, then joined. The line where the two halves meet is the "split."

Now apply this to an injection mould:

- The **lunchbox halves** = the **cavity half (A-side)** and **core half (B-side)** of the mould.
- The **hinge line** = the **Parting Line** of the mould.
- The **reason for splitting** = you cannot machine the inside of a closed box, so you split it to access the cavity.

> [!TIP]
> **Why split at all?** Because the moulded plastic part must come out of the mould after solidification. If the cavity were a single, closed block, the part would be **trapped forever**. The split is the mould's "escape route" for the part.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern split mould design under KTU 2024 scheme:

- **Parting line tolerance:** typically **±0.02 mm to ±0.05 mm** for precision injection moulds.
- **Land length (parting land):** generally **0.5 mm to 3 mm** depending on mould size.
- **Draft allowance** on each side of the part relative to the split: minimum **0.5° to 1°**.
- **Mould split angle (draft angle):** $\alpha \geq 0.5°$ for rigid polymers, $\alpha \geq 1°$ for fibre-reinforced polymers.
- **Standard parting surface classifications:** Flat, Stepped, Curved, and Irregular.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Split Moulds

Split moulds are broadly classified by the **number of separable plates** and the **geometry of the parting line**.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Number of Plates

| Type | Number of Separating Plates | Typical Application | Parting Line Position |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Single Cavity Split (Two-Plate)** | 2 (Cavity + Core) | General-purpose parts, simple geometry | Along the largest cross-section of the part |
| **Two-Plate Multi-Cavity** | 2 (Cavity + Core) | Small parts in high volumes | Common plane for all cavities |
| **Three-Plate Mould** | 3 (Cavity + Stripper + Core) | Parts with central gates (e.g., circular discs) | Two parting lines (primary + secondary) |
| **Split Cavity Mould** | 2+ with lateral sliders | Parts with undercuts on sides | Parting line + slider paths |
| **Stack Mould** | 2+ mirrored levels | High-volume, low-tonnage parts | Multiple parting lines at different elevations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 ESE, the most frequently examined split moulds are the **Two-Plate** and **Three-Plate** configurations. Memorize their runner systems, ejection mechanisms, and parting line trade-offs.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Parting Line Geometry

The **parting line trace** projected onto the part determines the split surface:

1. **Flat Parting Line** — Parting plane is a single flat surface (most economical, easiest to machine).
2. **Stepped Parting Line** — Parting surface changes elevation to accommodate asymmetric parts.
3. **Curved Parting Line** — Parting surface follows a curved contour (used for aesthetic parts where P/L must be hidden).
4. **Irregular Parting Line** — Free-form, follows organic geometry of the part.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Why and How the Split Works

The split operates on five core engineering principles:

- **Step 1 — Cavity Formation:** The cavity half (A-plate, fixed) and core half (B-plate, moving) together enclose the negative space that defines the part shape. **Why?** Because the cavity cannot be milled into a single block without destroying access.
- **Step 2 — Melt Injection:** Molten polymer is injected through a sprue bushing (A-side) into the runner system, filling the cavity. **How?** Injection pressure (typically **70–140 MPa**) drives the melt.
- **Step 3 — Cooling & Solidification:** Cooling channels (waterlines) extract heat; the part shrinks onto the core. **Why split now?** The core is on the B-side, so the solidified part releases *with* the moving half.
- **Step 4 — Mould Opening:** Hydraulic/ram force separates the two halves along the parting line. The part remains on the core due to adhesion and shrinkage. **How?** The split provides the geometric clearance for part extraction.
- **Step 5 — Ejection:** Ejector pins (on B-side) push the part off the core, completing the cycle.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations govern split mould design, parting force estimation, and mould opening mechanics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Never confuse **clamping force (tonnage of the press)** with **mould opening force**. They act in opposite directions and serve different purposes.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $F_{\text{clamp}} \geq n \cdot p_{\text{cav}} \cdot A_{\text{proj}} \cdot 10^{-3}$ | Clamping force must resist cavity pressure × projected area | kN |
| 2 | $p_{\text{cav}} \approx p_{\text{inj}} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)^{n}$ | Cavity pressure decay along flow length (where $n$ is power-law index) | MPa |
| 3 | $F_{\text{open}} = F_{\text{part}} + F_{\text{runner}} + F_{\text{ejector}}$ | Total mould opening force (must overcome part + runner adhesion + ejector friction) | N |
| 4 | $F_{\text{part}} = \mu \cdot p_{\text{cav}} \cdot A_{\text{contact}}$ | Part-to-mould adhesion force (friction coefficient $\mu \approx 0.2$–$0.4$) | N |
| 5 | $\tau_{\text{split}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{open}}}{A_{\text{land}}}$ | Shear stress on the parting land during opening | MPa |
| 6 | $\alpha_{\text{draft}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{L}\right)$ | Draft angle calculation (must be applied perpendicular to split) | degrees |
| 7 | $h_{\text{flash}} \approx \dfrac{p_{\text{cav}} \cdot w^{2}}{2 \cdot \sigma_{y,\text{steel}}}$ | Flash thickness estimation at the split gap $w$ | mm |
| 8 | $\Delta L_{\text{shrink}} = L_0 \cdot S$ | Linear mould shrinkage compensation (typical $S = 0.005$ to $0.020$) | mm |
| 9 | $N_{\text{cav,max}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{clamp,max}}}{p_{\text{avg}} \cdot A_{\text{proj}}}$ | Maximum number of cavities limited by press tonnage | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{\text{cool}} = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi^{2} \cdot \alpha_{\text{therm}}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)$ | Cooling time estimate (Fourier-based, $h$ = part thickness) | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Split Force Balance:** The opening force along the split must NEVER exceed the shear strength of the parting land ($\tau_{\text{split}} < \tau_{\text{allow,steel}}$). Otherwise the land will deform and cause **flash** defects.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Split moulds are the **workhorse** of the global plastics industry:

- **Consumer goods** (bottle caps, toys, housings) — predominantly two-plate split moulds.
- **Medical devices** (syringes, vials) — high-cavitation split moulds with multi-drop runners.
- **Automotive** (dashboards, bumpers) — large three-plate or stack moulds.
- **Packaging** (thin-walled containers) — high-speed split moulds with sub-2-second cycles.

The **split design decision** impacts:
- Cycle time (ejection speed).
- Part quality (weld line position relative to P/L).
- Tooling cost (curved splits are 3–5× more expensive than flat splits).
- Maintenance accessibility (split placement determines serviceability).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mould Opening Force Across the Split

The total mould opening force $F_{\text{open}}$ must overcome **three resistance components** acting along the split plane. We derive each component below.

### Step 1 — Identify the Resistance Components

When the mould begins to open, the moving half (B-side) carries the solidified part. Three forces resist opening:

1. **Adhesion force** between the part and the stationary cavity surface.
2. **Friction force** at the part–core interface (due to shrinkage gripping the core).
3. **Runner system drag force** in three-plate moulds.

### Step 2 — Express the Adhesion Force

The part adheres to the cavity wall due to vacuum formation during cooling. The normal force is the residual cavity pressure integrated over the contact area:

$$
F_{\text{adhesion}} = p_{\text{residual}} \cdot A_{\text{contact}}
$$

where $p_{\text{residual}} \approx 5$–$15\,\text{MPa}$ (depends on cooling time and part geometry).

### Step 3 — Express the Friction Force

As the part shrinks onto the core, normal stress $p_{\text{shrink}}$ develops. The friction force resisting opening is:

$$
F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot p_{\text{shrink}} \cdot A_{\text{contact}}
$$

For semi-crystalline polymers like polypropylene, $\mu \approx 0.25$; for glass-filled grades, $\mu \approx 0.40$.

### Step 4 — Combine into Net Opening Force

$$
F_{\text{open}} = F_{\text{adhesion}} + F_{\text{friction}} + F_{\text{ejector-friction}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} = p_{\text{residual}} \cdot A_{\text{c}} + \mu \cdot p_{\text{shrink}} \cdot A_{\text{c}} + F_{\text{ej}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} = A_{\text{c}} \cdot \left(p_{\text{residual}} + \mu \cdot p_{\text{shrink}}\right) + F_{\text{ej}}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{open}} = A_{\text{contact}} \cdot \left(p_{\text{residual}} + \mu \cdot p_{\text{shrink}}\right) + F_{\text{ejector}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The total opening resistance is the **sum of pressure-driven adhesion** (vacuum + residual pressure) plus the **frictional grip** of the part on the core (proportional to shrinkage pressure and surface roughness).

### Step 5 — Apply to a Numerical KTU-Style Problem

**Given:** A polypropylene moulded cup with $A_{\text{contact}} = 8000\,\text{mm}^2$, $p_{\text{residual}} = 8\,\text{MPa}$, $p_{\text{shrink}} = 12\,\text{MPa}$, $\mu = 0.30$, and ejector friction $F_{\text{ej}} = 500\,\text{N}$.

**Solution (KTU valuation style):**

$$
F_{\text{open}} = 8000 \cdot \left(8 + 0.30 \cdot 12\right) + 500
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} = 8000 \cdot \left(8 + 3.6\right) + 500
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} = 8000 \cdot 11.6 + 500
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} = 92800 + 500 = 93300\,\text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{open}} \approx 93.3\,\text{kN}}
$$

This force must be transmitted through the **knock-out bar** and **ejector plate** of the mould base.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Parting Land Shear Stress Check

To prevent **flash** at the split, the parting land must withstand the opening pressure without yielding.

### Step 1 — Pressure Acting on the Land

The melt pressure at the moment of mould opening (when the cavity is still full or partially full) acts on the parting land:

$$
p_{\text{land}} = p_{\text{cavity,avg}}
$$

### Step 2 — Force on the Land

$$
F_{\text{land}} = p_{\text{cavity,avg}} \cdot A_{\text{land}}
$$

### Step 3 — Shear Stress Developed

The land resists this force as shear across its cross-sectional area $A_{\text{land}}$:

$$
\tau_{\text{land}} = \frac{F_{\text{land}}}{A_{\text{land}}} = \frac{p_{\text{cavity,avg}} \cdot A_{\text{land}}}{A_{\text{land}}} = p_{\text{cavity,avg}}
$$

Wait — this trivial result tells us the **average shear stress equals cavity pressure**, but peak stress occurs at the corners. Using a stress concentration factor $K_t \approx 1.5$:

$$
\tau_{\text{peak}} = K_t \cdot p_{\text{cavity,avg}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{peak}} = 1.5 \cdot p_{\text{cavity,avg}}}
$$

### Step 4 — Design Check

For P-20 tool steel with $\tau_{\text{allow}} \approx 415\,\text{MPa}$:

$$
p_{\text{cavity,allow}} = \frac{\tau_{\text{allow}}}{1.5} \approx 277\,\text{MPa}
$$

This is well above typical injection pressures (**70–140 MPa**), confirming that parting land shear is **not** the limiting factor in most splits — **flash at the split is more often caused by poor fit-up, wear, or insufficient clamping force** rather than land shear.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Each Split Type

| Criterion | Two-Plate (Flat) | Two-Plate (Stepped) | Three-Plate | Split Cavity (with Side Action) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Part complexity** | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium (with central gate) | High (with undercuts) |
| **Tool cost (relative)** | 1× | 1.3× | 1.6× | 2.0–3.0× |
| **Cycle time (relative)** | 1× | 1× | 1.15× | 1.25× |
| **Gate vestige quality** | Acceptable | Acceptable | Excellent (auto-degated) | Acceptable |
| **Runner system** | Cold runner | Cold runner | Cold runner (auto-separated) | Cold or hot runner |
| **Best for KTU exam mention** | Most common | Practical exam favourite | Central-gate exam classic | Advanced exam question |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** If a question mentions a "circular disc with a central gate" — **always** answer "three-plate mould." If it mentions "threaded cap" — answer "split mould with collapsible core or unscrewing mechanism."

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Two-Plate Split Mould

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional architecture** of a classic two-plate split mould during the **injection–cooling–ejection** cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clamping Unit Activates] --> B[Fixed Half A Plate Closes]
    B --> C[Moving Half B Plate Closes]
    C --> D[Mold Fully Closed Cavity Sealed at Split Line]
    D --> E[Injection Unit Pushes Molten Polymer Through Sprue]
    E --> F[Runner System Distributes Melt to Cavity]
    F --> G[Cavity Fills Pressure Ramps to Peak]
    G --> H[Cooling Channels Extract Heat]
    H --> I[Polymer Solidifies Shrinks onto Core B Side]
    I --> J[Clamping Unit Releases Mold Opens at Split]
    J --> K[Part Stays on Core B Side Due to Adhesion]
    K --> L[Ejector Pins Push Part Off Core]
    L --> M[Part Drops Free of Mold]
    M --> N[Mold Closes Again Cycle Repeats]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **split line** is the critical transition between states **D** (mould closed) and **J** (mould opening). Every other step exists to prepare for or recover from this single event.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Three-Plate Split Mould

This diagram highlights the **dual split planes** characteristic of three-plate moulds.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Primary Split Plane Between Cavity Plate and Stripper Plate]
    P2[Secondary Split Plane Between Stripper Plate and Core Plate]
    P1 --> Q1[During Mould Opening Runner Stays with Stripper Plate]
    P2 --> Q2[Stripper Plate Pulls Away from Core Pulling Runners with It]
    Q2 --> R1[Runner Auto Separates from Part at Gate]
    R1 --> R2[Part Remains on Core for Ejection]
    R2 --> R3[Ejector Pins Push Part Off Core]
```

> [!TIP]
> The three-plate mould uses **two splits** to achieve automatic degating — the runner system peels off the part as the mould opens, leaving a clean gate mark on the part.

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Split Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Part Geometry Analysis] --> Q1{Does the Part Have a Central Gate Requirement?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| T1[Choose Three Plate Split Mould]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Are There Undercuts or Threads?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| T2[Choose Split Cavity Mould with Side Action]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is the Geometry Symmetric and Simple?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| T3[Choose Standard Two Plate Flat Split]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is the Largest Cross Section Easy to Access?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| T4[Choose Two Plate Stepped Split]
    Q4 -->|No| T5[Reconsider Parting Line Location]
    T5 --> Q4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "select and justify a mould type" for a given part, follow this decision tree in your answer. It demonstrates **systematic engineering reasoning** which examiners reward with full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** Define the term **"split"** as applied to mould design. List the four common classifications of parting line geometry.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A "split" in mould design refers to the deliberate division of the mould cavity into two or more separable halves along a precisely engineered interface (parting line), enabling part ejection, manufacturing access, and maintenance.
- **Four classifications (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
  1. **Flat parting line** — single planar interface.
  2. **Stepped parting line** — changes elevation across the part.
  3. **Curved parting line** — follows a contoured surface.
  4. **Irregular parting line** — free-form, follows organic geometry.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Q:** Differentiate between a **two-plate mould** and a **three-plate mould** with respect to parting line and runner system.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Two-Plate Mould | Three-Plate Mould |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Parting lines** | One (single split) | Two (primary + secondary) |
| **Runner system** | Cold runner, manually degated | Cold runner, auto-degated at secondary split |
| **Gate location** | Side or edge gate only | Central (centre) gate possible |
| **Cycle complexity** | Simpler, faster | Slightly slower due to dual-opening sequence |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

**Q:** A polypropylene container of projected area **6500 mm²** is to be moulded in a **two-plate split mould**. The cavity pressure averages **45 MPa**, the part-to-mould friction coefficient is **0.30**, and the residual cavity pressure at opening is **8 MPa**. The mould has a parting land area of **12000 mm²**.

**(a)** Calculate the **(i)** clamping force required, **(ii)** the total mould opening force, and **(iii)** the peak shear stress on the parting land. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the factors governing the **selection of parting line location** for this container, with sketches (descriptive) of preferred and avoided parting line positions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Projected area: $A_{\text{proj}} = 6500\,\text{mm}^2$
- Average cavity pressure: $p_{\text{cav}} = 45\,\text{MPa}$
- Friction coefficient: $\mu = 0.30$
- Residual cavity pressure: $p_{\text{res}} = 8\,\text{MPa}$
- Parting land area: $A_{\text{land}} = 12000\,\text{mm}^2$

**Step 1 — Clamping Force (2 marks):**

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = p_{\text{cav}} \cdot A_{\text{proj}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = 45 \times 6500 = 292500\,\text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{clamp}} = 292.5\,\text{kN}}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Opening Force (3 marks):**

Assuming shrinkage-induced normal stress $p_{\text{shrink}} = p_{\text{res}} = 8\,\text{MPa}$ (worst case) and neglecting ejector friction:

$$
F_{\text{open}} = A_{\text{contact}} \cdot \left(p_{\text{res}} + \mu \cdot p_{\text{shrink}}\right)
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} = 6500 \cdot \left(8 + 0.30 \cdot 8\right)
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} = 6500 \cdot \left(8 + 2.4\right)
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} = 6500 \cdot 10.4 = 67600\,\text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{open}} = 67.6\,\text{kN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Peak Shear Stress on Land (2 marks):**

Using stress concentration factor $K_t = 1.5$:

$$
\tau_{\text{peak}} = K_t \cdot p_{\text{cav}} = 1.5 \cdot 45 = 67.5\,\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{peak}} = 67.5\,\text{MPa} < \tau_{\text{allow,P20}} = 415\,\text{MPa} \;\;\therefore\;\text{Safe}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Parting Line Selection Criteria (3.5 marks):**

1. **Largest cross-section principle:** The P/L should pass through the largest cross-sectional area of the part to minimize the depth of cavity machining on either side. For a cylindrical container, this means the P/L should pass through the **diameter plane**, not through the side wall.

2. **Draft angle accommodation:** Each side of the part must have adequate draft ($\alpha \geq 0.5°$) measured perpendicular to the P/L. A P/L that runs along the cylindrical wall would require impossible draft on a curved surface.

3. **Gate location:** The P/L should allow the gate to be placed on a non-critical (non-visible) surface. For a food container, a P/L at the rim places the gate on the inside, hiding the vestige.

4. **Ejection direction:** The core must draw in a single direction; undercuts relative to the P/L are forbidden unless side actions are added.

5. **Aesthetic considerations:** P/L should not cross visible cosmetic surfaces to avoid unsightly witness marks.

6. **Manufacturing economy:** Flat P/L is preferred over curved because it is **3–5× cheaper** to machine.

**Preferred vs Avoided P/L (3.5 marks):**

- **Preferred:** A P/L passing through the **top rim** of the container (flat, hides gate inside, easy ejection).
- **Avoided:** A P/L passing **through the side wall** of the container — would create a thin flash-prone seam on the visible surface and require draft on a vertical cylinder.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. Students often forget to apply the **stress concentration factor** ($K_t = 1.5$) when computing land shear stress — losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. Students write "draft angle of 1°" without specifying that it must be measured **perpendicular to the split surface** — losing 0.5 mark.
> 3. Students confuse **clamping force** with **opening force** in the same problem — losing up to 1 mark.
> 4. Sketches of P/L are often drawn without arrows indicating the **ejection direction** — losing 1 mark in the descriptive part.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`**

**Q:** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the construction and working of a **three-plate mould**. Explain why the **split (parting line)** in this mould is located at **two different planes**, and discuss its advantages and limitations over a conventional two-plate mould.

#### Model Solution Outline (14 Marks)

**Construction (5 marks):**
- **Stationary Plate (clamped to fixed platen):** Contains the **sprue bushing** aligned with the machine nozzle.
- **Cavity Plate (A-plate):** Contains the cavity inserts forming the outer shape of the part.
- **Stripper Plate (middle plate):** Contains the runner system and pulls back with the cavity plate initially.
- **Core Plate (B-plate, moving):** Contains the core inserts forming the inner shape.
- **Support Plate, Ejector Plate, Ejector Pins** for final part ejection.

**Working (4 marks):**
1. Mould closes; melt injected through sprue → runner → gate into cavity.
2. Cooling completes; mould begins to open.
3. **First split** opens between **cavity plate and stripper plate** — runner is pulled by the stripper plate.
4. **Second split** opens between **stripper plate and core plate** — runner separates from the part at the gate; runner drops into a runner bin.
5. Part remains on core; ejector pins push it off.

**Why Two Splits (3 marks):**
- The **first split** allows the runner system to be **retained on the stripper plate** during the initial opening.
- The **second split** allows the **automatic separation of the runner from the part** at the gate point, achieving **central gating** with **automatic degating** — a feat impossible with a single split.

**Advantages over Two-Plate (1.5 marks):**
- Central gate possible → better flow balance for symmetric parts.
- Automatic degating → no manual trimming.
- Concentric gate vestige → often acceptable on aesthetic parts.

**Limitations (0.5 marks):**
- Higher tooling cost (~60% more).
- Longer cycle (dual opening sequence).
- More flash-prone (two land surfaces).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students draw the three-plate mould but **omit the stripper plate** or fail to label the **two distinct parting lines**. Both omissions cost up to 2 marks each. Always **explicitly label P/L 1 and P/L 2** in your sketch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Split Moulds (KTU PEPOT756 Module 1)**

- **Definition (must know verbatim):** A split mould is a mould whose cavity is divided into separable halves along a parting line for ejection and fabrication access.
- **Two primary split types for KTU:** Two-plate and three-plate.
- **Parting line (P/L):** The 2D trace on the part; **Parting surface:** the 3D interface along which mould halves separate.
- **Four P/L geometries:** Flat, Stepped, Curved, Irregular.
- **Flat P/L is cheapest to machine;** curved is 3–5× more expensive.
- **Draft angle** ($\alpha$) must be applied **perpendicular to the split surface**: $\alpha \geq 0.5°$ minimum.
- **Clamping force formula:** $F_{\text{clamp}} = p_{\text{cav}} \cdot A_{\text{proj}}$.
- **Opening force formula:** $F_{\text{open}} = A_{\text{c}} (p_{\text{res}} + \mu \cdot p_{\text{shrink}}) + F_{\text{ejector}}$.
- **Parting land shear check:** $\tau_{\text{peak}} = 1.5 \cdot p_{\text{cav}} < \tau_{\text{allow,steel}}$.
- **Shrinkage compensation:** $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot S$, where $S = 0.005$ to $0.020$ for most polymers.
- **Two-plate mould:** one split, side/edge gate only, manually degated runner.
- **Three-plate mould:** two splits, central gate, automatically degated runner.
- **Decision rule:** Central gate → three-plate. Undercut → split cavity with side action. Simple symmetric → two-plate flat.
- **Land length typical range:** 0.5 mm to 3 mm.
- **P/L tolerance:** ±0.02 mm to ±0.05 mm for precision moulds.
- **Common KTU exam traps:**
  1. Forgetting stress concentration factor in land shear.
  2. Confusing clamping force with opening force.
  3. Failing to label both parting lines in three-plate sketches.
  4. Specifying draft angle without specifying measurement direction.
  5. Forgetting shrinkage compensation in mould dimensioning.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Split Selection: "CUSA"** — **C**entral gate → **U**ndercut → **S**imple/flat → **A**symmetric stepped. Walk through CUSA in every mould-type selection question to earn full method marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Undercuts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Undercuts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In the context of **Mould and Die Manufacture (PEPOT756)**, an **undercut** is defined as a geometric feature on a moulded (or die-formed) component that **interferes with the direct, straight-line withdrawal** of the part from the mould cavity, core, or die impression in the principal direction of mould opening. The undercut creates a *re-entrant* surface that traps the part if ejection is attempted without auxiliary mechanical intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> Undercuts are a critical design-for-manufacture (DFM) constraint. The student must identify, classify, and prescribe the **correct side-action mechanism** (slide, lifter, split cavity, or unscrewing device) to release the undercut during the mould opening stroke.

## 1.2 Two Broad Categories of Undercuts

| Category | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **External Undercut** | The obstruction lies on the *outer* surface of the part, projecting outward beyond the cavity wall. | Bottle-handle lugs, gear teeth, external ribs with retention features, snap-fit hooks. |
| **Internal Undercut** | The obstruction lies on the *inner* surface of the part, projecting inward into a bore or cavity. | Internal threads, internal grooves, luer-lock features, hollow bosses with retention lips. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bottle-Neck in a Glass"

Imagine casting a **bottle-shaped part inside a rigid glass mould**:

- The neck of the bottle is *narrower* than the body.
- If the glass is a single-piece cup, you **cannot pull the bottle straight up** — the wider body is trapped under the glass rim.
- This bottle-rim obstruction is the **undercut**.
- The only ways to release it are:
  1. **Break the glass** (split mould),
  2. **Slide a side panel sideways** (side-action slide), or
  3. **Lift an internal piston upward first to clear the rim** (lifter / stripper).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** An undercut is essentially a "hook" or "lip" that mechanically locks the part to one half of the mould. The mould cannot open freely until a *secondary motion* (perpendicular or angled to the main opening) clears this hook.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Exam Favourites)

- **Draft / Taper allowance on undercuts:** typically **0.5° to 2° per side** to allow sliding release.
- **Maximum practical undercut depth (for manual slides):** ≤ **5 mm to 15 mm**, beyond which cam-actuated or hydraulic slides are preferred.
- **Standard slide motion angle:** **0° to 30°** (most commonly 10°–15°) measured from the parting line.
- **Lifter (stripper) lift height:** must exceed the **undercut depth + 2 mm** clearance to ensure total release.

> [!TIP]
> A KTU examiner loves asking: *"Why can't the part be ejected straight up?"* — The answer is **always**: *Because an undercut creates a re-entrant surface that mechanically interlocks with the cavity/core wall.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Why Undercuts Are a Problem

During mould opening, the **only force available** is along the parting line (vertical, for a standard two-plate mould). An undercut creates a **normal force component (N)** that is *perpendicular* to the opening direction. This normal force generates a **frictional retention force**:

$$F_{\text{retention}} = \mu \cdot N$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between the part and the steel (typically **0.15–0.30** for steel-on-polymer; **0.10–0.20** for steel-on-die-cast alloy). Since $\mu N$ can easily exceed the available ejection force, the part either:
- Sticks in the cavity (over-stress on ejector pins), or
- Deforms/damages the mould.

## 2.2 The "How" — Six Standard Methods to Release Undercuts

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) emphasises the following six solutions, ranked by **frequency of industrial use**:

### 1. **Side-Action Slide (Side Core)** ⭐ Most Common
- A hardened steel block ("slide") carrying the undercut-forming pin is mounted in a dovetail groove.
- It is actuated by an **angle pin (cam pin)** attached to the moving platen.
- When the mould opens, the angle pin pulls the slide outward (lateral motion) before the part is ejected.
- **Used for:** external undercuts (e.g., handle holes, side holes).

### 2. **Cam-Actuated Lifter (Stripper)**
- A pin mounted on an angle, lifted by a **heel block** on the ejector plate.
- Provides simultaneous vertical and lateral motion.
- **Used for:** internal undercuts on cylindrical parts (e.g., internal snap ring grooves).

### 3. **Collapsible Core**
- A core made of multiple segments held together by a central spring or hydraulic pressure.
- During ejection, the segments collapse inward, freeing an internal undercut.
- **Used for:** deep internal undercuts (e.g., threaded bottle caps, pipe elbows).

### 4. **Split Cavity (Two-Part Cavity Block)**
- The cavity itself is split into two halves that move apart horizontally.
- **Used for:** large external undercuts where a single slide is impractical.

### 5. **Hand-Loaded Inserts (Loose Pieces)**
- A simple steel piece is placed manually into the mould for each cycle, then removed with the part.
- **Used for:** very low-volume production, prototyping, or extremely complex undercuts.

### 6. **Unscrewing Mechanism (Rotary Core)**
- A threaded core is rotated (by rack-pinion, gear, or hydraulic motor) to unscrew from an internally threaded part.
- **Used for:** threaded bottle caps, pipe fittings, screw closures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Logic (for KTU exam):**
> - External undercut + moderate depth → **Side slide**.
> - Internal undercut on a round part → **Lifter**.
> - Internal thread → **Unscrewing core**.
> - Very deep or complex → **Collapsible core** or **split cavity**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Retention (locking) force on undercut | $F_R = \mu \cdot N$ | **N** | $\mu$ is steel–plastic friction coeff. |
| 2 | Normal force from undercut | $N = p_{\text{inj}} \cdot A_{\text{undercut}}$ | **N** | $p_{\text{inj}}$ = injection pressure (**MPa → N/mm²**), $A$ = projected undercut area. |
| 3 | Slide actuating force (cam pin) | $F_{\text{slide}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{retention}}}{\tan(\alpha)}$ | **N** | $\alpha$ = cam-pin angle to vertical. |
| 4 | Bending stress in slide | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \cdot F_{\text{slide}} \cdot L}{b \cdot t^2}$ | **N/mm²** | $L$ = slide travel, $b$ = width, $t$ = thickness. |
| 5 | Mould opening stroke (clearance) | $S_{\text{open}} \geq h_{\text{uc}} + 2\,\text{mm}$ | **mm** | $h_{\text{uc}}$ = undercut depth. |
| 6 | Slide return spring force | $F_{\text{spring}} \geq F_{\text{slide}} \cdot 1.25$ | **N** | 25 % safety factor mandated. |
| 7 | Unscrewing core torque | $T = F_R \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2}$ | **N·mm** | $d_m$ = mean thread diameter. |
| 8 | Stripper (lifter) lift height | $h_{\text{lift}} = h_{\text{uc}} + 2\,\text{mm}$ | **mm** | Same rule as stroke clearance. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state units explicitly. The most common marks lost in mould-design questions are *forgotten units* and *missing safety factors*. Adopt the 1.25× safety factor on all spring and hydraulic-actuator sizing.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive dashboards:** Side slides form the mounting-clip undercuts on instrument clusters.
- **Medical disposables:** Collapsible cores form the luer-lock undercuts on syringe tips.
- **Beverage industry:** Unscrewing mechanisms are used in every PET-bottle-cap mould (≈ 50,000 cavities/year).
- **Forging dies:** Undercuts on connecting rods require side-action wedges to release the flash gutter geometry.
- **Die casting:** External undercuts on aluminium housing covers are released by hydraulically actuated slides.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Slide Actuating Force from First Principles

**Given:** A mould has an external undercut of projected area $A$ on a part. The injection pressure $p_{\text{inj}}$ presses the molten polymer against the undercut surface. A cam-pin at angle $\alpha$ must actuate the side slide to release the part.

**Step 1 — Identify the normal force on the undercut surface.**
The injection pressure acts uniformly on the projected area of the undercut:

$$N = p_{\text{inj}} \cdot A_{\text{undercut}}$$

This force is directed **perpendicular** to the undercut face, which is *normal* to the mould-opening direction. Hence, the part is locked against the cavity wall.

**Step 2 — Compute the retention (friction) force.**
By Coulomb's law of friction, the force resisting slide motion is:

$$F_R = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot p_{\text{inj}} \cdot A_{\text{undercut}}$$

**Step 3 — Resolve forces on the cam pin.**
The cam pin acts at angle $\alpha$ to the vertical (mould-opening direction). The slide moves horizontally, so only the **horizontal component** of the cam-pin force $F_{\text{cam}}$ does useful work. The vertical component is absorbed by the platen.

Therefore, equilibrium along the slide direction gives:

$$F_{\text{cam}} \cdot \sin(\alpha) = F_R$$

Solving for the required cam-pin (slide-actuating) force:

$$F_{\text{slide}} = F_{\text{cam}} = \frac{F_R}{\sin(\alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Real-World Numerical Example.**

> **Problem:** An external undercut of area **300 mm²** is formed in an ABS part moulded at an injection pressure of **80 MPa**. The coefficient of friction between ABS and P20 steel is **0.20**. The cam-pin angle is **15°**. Find (a) the retention force, and (b) the slide actuating force.

**Step 4a — Compute $F_R$:**

$$F_R = \mu \cdot p_{\text{inj}} \cdot A = 0.20 \times 80\,\text{N/mm}^2 \times 300\,\text{mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{F_R = 4800\,\text{N} = 4.8\,\text{kN}}$$

**Step 4b — Compute $F_{\text{slide}}$:**

$$F_{\text{slide}} = \frac{4800}{\sin(15°)} = \frac{4800}{0.2588}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{slide}} \approx 18{,}547\,\text{N} \approx 18.55\,\text{kN}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A modest undercut area (300 mm²) generates nearly **4× amplification** of the retention force when transmitted through a 15° cam. This is why *steep cam angles* reduce the required slide force but increase wear and side loads on the platen.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Undercut-Release Method

| Undercut Type | Geometry Constraint | Production Volume | Recommended Method | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| External, small depth (≤ 5 mm) | Straight pull desired | High | **Side-action slide** | Fastest cycle, fully automatic. |
| External, large depth (> 15 mm) | Wide opening stroke | Medium–High | **Hydraulic slide** | Mechanical cam is too long. |
| Internal, round part | Cylindrical bore | High | **Cam lifter (stripper)** | Synchronised with ejection. |
| Internal thread | Multi-start threads | High | **Rotary unscrewing core** | Only way to release helical form. |
| Deep internal cavity | Complex internal shape | Medium | **Collapsible core** | Segments collapse inward. |
| Very large undercut | Cavity too big for slide | Low | **Split cavity** | Mechanically simplest. |
| Prototype / one-off | Any complex form | **< 100 shots** | **Hand-loaded insert** | Cheapest tooling. |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — Lifter (Cam-Actuated Stripper) Lift Calculation

**Step 1:** Identify the undercut depth $h_{\text{uc}}$ from the part drawing.

**Step 2:** Add a 2 mm safety clearance:

$$h_{\text{lift}} = h_{\text{uc}} + 2\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the lifter pin length $L_{\text{pin}}$ such that the heel block contact angle $\beta$ is between 10° and 25°:

$$L_{\text{pin}} = \frac{h_{\text{lift}}}{\tan(\beta)}$$

**Step 4:** Verify that the heel-block wear surface is hardened to **58–62 HRC** to withstand cyclic impact.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A very common error is to set the lifter lift height **equal to** the undercut depth. This leaves **zero clearance** and the part will be damaged. Always add **at least 2 mm** of over-travel.
