<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Undercut Release Decision Tree

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical decision flow** an engineer follows when encountering an undercut during mould design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Part Drawing Received] --> B{Is there an undercut?}
    B -- No --> C[Standard Two-Plate Mould]
    B -- Yes --> D{Location?}
    D -- External --> E{Depth}
    D -- Internal --> F{Geometry}
    E -- Small 5mm or less --> G[Side Action Slide]
    E -- Medium 5 to 15mm --> H[Cam or Hydraulic Slide]
    E -- Large more than 15mm --> I[Split Cavity]
    F -- Round --> J[Cam Lifter Stripper]
    F -- Threaded --> K[Rotary Unscrewing Core]
    F -- Deep Internal --> L[Collapsible Core]
    G --> M[Apply Cam Force Formula]
    H --> M
    I --> N[Apply Split Cavity Spacing Rule]
    J --> O[Apply Lifter Lift Formula]
    K --> P[Apply Torque Formula]
    L --> Q[Apply Segment Collapse Allowance]
    M --> R[End: Final Mould Assembly]
    N --> R
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    C --> R
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Side-Action Slide Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Moving Platen] -->|Vertical motion| B[Angle Pin Cam Pin]
    B -->|At angle alpha| C[Side Slide Block]
    C -->|Horizontal travel| D[Undercut Forming Pin]
    D -->|Releases| E[Part Undercut]
    F[Return Spring] -->|Pushes back| C
    G[Locking Gib] -->|Prevents float| C
    H[Ejector Plate] -->|Ejects| I[Finished Part]
```

## 4.3 Architecture — Cam-Actuated Lifter (Stripper)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ejector Plate Moves Up] --> B[Heel Block Contacts Lifter Base]
    B -->|At angle beta| C[Lifter Pin Rises]
    C -->|With horizontal component| D[Stripper Edge Clears Undercut]
    D --> E[Part is Free for Ejection]
    F[Guide Pin] -->|Prevents rotation| C
    G[Retainer Plate] -->|Holds lifter| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams intentionally avoid photographic drawings (which Mermaid cannot render) and instead map the **functional flow** of force, motion, and mechanical interaction. This is the same logic a KTU examiner uses to grade a mould-assembly question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define an undercut in moulding. List the two principal methods of releasing an internal undercut.**

**Model Answer:**

An *undercut* is any feature on a moulded part that prevents its direct withdrawal from the mould cavity in the principal opening direction, due to a re-entrant surface.

The two principal methods of releasing an **internal** undercut are:
1. **Cam-actuated lifter (stripper pin):** The lifter moves axially and laterally simultaneously, clearing the internal groove.
2. **Collapsible core:** The core is composed of multiple segments that collapse inward during ejection, releasing the undercut.

> (Mentioning unscrewing cores for threaded internal undercuts earns a ½-mark bonus.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Why is a side-action slide preferred over a hand-loaded insert for high-volume production of parts with external undercuts?**

**Model Answer:**

A side-action slide is preferred because:
1. It operates **automatically** with every mould cycle (no operator intervention).
2. Cycle time remains **short and consistent** (typically 5–15 s).
3. Part **quality and dimensional repeatability** are high.
4. It eliminates the **safety hazard** of handling hot or sharp inserts every cycle.
5. It is **cost-effective** at high volumes despite higher initial tooling cost.

A hand-loaded insert is restricted to **prototypes and very low volumes** (< 100 shots) because the manual loading step makes the cycle slow, labour-intensive, and unsafe.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **side-action slide mechanism** for releasing an external undercut in an injection mould. Mention the function of the **angle pin** and the **locking gib**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An external undercut on a moulded part has a projected area of **250 mm²**. The injection pressure is **70 MPa**, and the coefficient of friction between the polymer and the cavity steel is **0.18**. The cam-pin angle is **20°**. Calculate:
   (i) the retention force,
   (ii) the required slide-actuating force, and
   (iii) the minimum return-spring force (use 1.25× safety factor). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Working of a Side-Action Slide (7 marks):**

A side-action slide (also called a *side core* or *slide*) is used to form an external undercut on a moulded part that cannot be released by straight pulling.

**Components and Functions:**

1. **Slide block:** A hardened steel block (58–62 HRC) carrying the undercut-forming pin, mounted in a precision dovetail slot on the cavity plate.
2. **Angle pin (cam pin):** A pin fixed to the moving platen, inclined at an angle $\alpha$ (typically 10°–20°) to the parting line. As the platen opens, the angle pin pushes/pulls the slide laterally, releasing the undercut before the part is ejected.
3. **Locking gib (wear plate):** A hardened insert fitted on top of the slide to prevent it from "floating" outward under injection pressure. The gib bears against the cavity plate and takes the moulding pressure.
4. **Return spring / return pin:** Pushes the slide back into its forming position as the mould closes.
5. **Stop pin / stopper:** Limits the travel of the slide and positions it precisely at the start of each cycle.

**Working Sequence:**
- **Mould closed:** Slide is forward; angle pin is engaged in the inclined slot of the slide.
- **Mould opening begins:** Moving platen retracts; angle pin pulls the slide outward (horizontal motion). The undercut-forming pin clears the undercut region of the part.
- **Slide fully retracted:** The part is now free of all undercut features and can be ejected by the ejector pins.
- **Mould closing:** Return spring pushes the slide back into the forming position; the angle pin re-engages in the slot.

(For a sketch, draw the standard configuration: cavity plate, slide, angle pin, gib, return spring, ejector pins, and the moulded part.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $A = 250\,\text{mm}^2$
- $p_{\text{inj}} = 70\,\text{MPa} = 70\,\text{N/mm}^2$
- $\mu = 0.18$
- $\alpha = 20°$

**(i) Retention force $F_R$:**

$$F_R = \mu \cdot p_{\text{inj}} \cdot A = 0.18 \times 70 \times 250$$

$$\boxed{F_R = 3150\,\text{N} = 3.15\,\text{kN}}$$

**(ii) Slide actuating force $F_{\text{slide}}$:**

$$F_{\text{slide}} = \frac{F_R}{\sin(\alpha)} = \frac{3150}{\sin(20°)} = \frac{3150}{0.3420}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{slide}} \approx 9211\,\text{N} \approx 9.21\,\text{kN}}$$

**(iii) Minimum return-spring force $F_{\text{spring}}$:**

$$F_{\text{spring}} = 1.25 \times F_{\text{slide}} = 1.25 \times 9211$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{spring}} \approx 11{,}514\,\text{N} \approx 11.51\,\text{kN}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **collapsible core** mechanism for moulding parts with deep internal undercuts. List **two industrial applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **threaded bottle cap** with a mean thread diameter of **32 mm** is moulded with a rotary unscrewing core. The retention force on the thread flanks is **2.4 kN**. Calculate the **unscrewing torque** required. If the drive motor has an efficiency of 85 %, find the **input torque** at the motor shaft. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Collapsible Core Mechanism (7 marks):**

A collapsible core is used to mould parts with deep, complex internal undercuts (e.g., internal threads, internal undercuts in pipe elbows) where neither a lifter nor a slide is feasible.

**Construction:**
- The core is made of **3 to 12 identical tapered segments** (typically 4–8).
- Segments are held outward against a central tapered plug (or hydraulic piston) by a **central spring** or **hydraulic pressure**.
- The outer surfaces of the segments form the internal shape of the part.

**Working:**
- **Moulding:** The segments are held outward by the central plug; the plastic solidifies around them.
- **Ejection begins:** The central plug retracts (or hydraulic pressure is released), allowing the spring to **collapse the segments inward**.
- The segments move radially inward, **clearing the undercut** by a few tenths of a millimetre.
- The part is then **stripped off the collapsed core** by a stripper sleeve.
- **Reset:** For the next cycle, the central plug re-extends, pushing the segments back outward into forming position.

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Threaded pipe fittings (PVC, CPVC):** Internal threads require segment collapse.
2. **Medical luer-lock syringe tips:** Internal undercuts too deep for a single lifter.
3. *(Bonus)* Tube elbows with internal ribs.

---

**Part (b) — Unscrewing Torque Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Mean thread diameter $d_m = 32\,\text{mm}$
- Retention force on flanks $F_R = 2.4\,\text{kN} = 2400\,\text{N}$
- Motor efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ (85 %)

**Step 1 — Unscrewing torque (output):**

$$T_{\text{out}} = F_R \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} = 2400 \times \frac{32}{2} = 2400 \times 16$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{out}} = 38{,}400\,\text{N·mm} = 38.4\,\text{N·m}}$$

**Step 2 — Input torque at the motor shaft (allowing for efficiency loss):**

$$T_{\text{in}} = \frac{T_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \frac{38.4}{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{in}} \approx 45.18\,\text{N·m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — MPa must be converted to N/mm² before multiplying with mm². (–1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Confusing $\sin(\alpha)$ and $\tan(\alpha)$** in the cam-pin force equation. The slide direction is *perpendicular* to the cam pin, so use **$\sin(\alpha)$** (not $\tan$). (–1 mark.)
> 3. **Omitting the 1.25× safety factor** on spring force. Examiners explicitly look for this. (–1 mark.)
> 4. **Writing "undercut" without specifying internal or external** — examiners deduct for ambiguity. (–½ mark.)
> 5. **Not mentioning the locking gib** in a side-slide description — this is a favourite 1-mark freebie. (–1 mark.)
> 6. **Calling a lifter the same as a slide** — they are *different mechanisms*. (–1 mark.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- ✅ **Undercut** = any re-entrant feature preventing straight-line part withdrawal.
- ✅ **Two categories:** *External* (use slides) and *Internal* (use lifters, collapsible cores, or unscrewing cores).
- ✅ **Six standard release methods:** Side slide, Lifter, Collapsible core, Split cavity, Hand-loaded insert, Unscrewing mechanism.
- ✅ **Core formulas (must memorise):**
   - $F_R = \mu \cdot p_{\text{inj}} \cdot A$
   - $F_{\text{slide}} = F_R / \sin(\alpha)$
   - $F_{\text{spring}} = 1.25 \cdot F_{\text{slide}}$
   - $T = F_R \cdot d_m / 2$
   - $h_{\text{lift}} = h_{\text{uc}} + 2\,\text{mm}$
- ✅ **Locking gib** is mandatory on side slides to resist injection pressure.
- ✅ **Cam-pin angle** is typically 10°–20°; steeper angle = less force but more side load.
- ✅ **Always include a 2 mm clearance** in any lift or stroke calculation.
- ✅ **Always state units** in numerical answers — losing 1 mark per missing unit is the most common deduction.
- ✅ **Always apply the 1.25× safety factor** to spring, hydraulic, or motor sizing.
- ✅ **Decision rule of thumb:** Round internal feature → Lifter; Threaded feature → Rotary core; Complex internal shape → Collapsible core; External lug → Side slide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:**
> *"Identify the undercut → classify as internal/external → select the matching release mechanism → compute the actuating force with units and safety factor."*
> This 4-step mantra will earn you full marks on any KTU Part B question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Side Core Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Side Core Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Side Core Threads** refer to the threaded features (internal or external) on a moulded component that cannot be formed by the direct axial movement of the mould halves alone. To produce such features, **Side Cores** (also called *Side Actions*, *Slides*, or *Slide Cores*) are incorporated into the mould design. These are auxiliary mould components that move **perpendicular** (transverse) to the main mould opening direction, allowing the formation of undercuts, holes, recesses, and threaded profiles on the sides of the moulded part.

In the context of **PEPOT756 – Mould and Die Manufacture**, the topic specifically deals with the design philosophy, dimensional calculation, and actuation mechanism for producing threaded features using side cores. The threads may be:

- **Internal Threads** (tapped holes) — produced by an external side core that retracts sideways after moulding.
- **External Threads** (screw shafts) — produced by a collapsible core or rotating side core that retracts after moulding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A side core is *not* a fixed mould insert. It is a moving mould member driven by an external actuator (hydraulic cylinder, angle pin, cam, rack-and-pinion, or mechanical linkage). The thread geometry on the moulded part is the **negative replica** of the threaded profile machined on the side core itself.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **plastic bottle with a screw cap**. When you unscrew the cap, you must *rotate* it. Now think of the inside of the cap — that thread is moulded. How did the manufacturer create that internal thread without using a tap?

The answer is by using a **side core shaped exactly like a screw**. After the molten plastic is injected and solidified around this screw-shaped core, the mould opens *and* the side core is either:
1. **Rotated** (unscrewed) out of the threaded hole, OR
2. **Collapsed inward** (for a collapsible core), OR
3. **Slid sideways** before the main mould opens (for non-circular undercuts).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Explanation:** A "side core" is like a **hidden third hand** in the mould. While the two main mould halves (cavity and core) move apart vertically, this third hand slides horizontally to free the thread. Without it, the part would be locked onto the mould and could not be ejected.

### 1.3 Standard Thread Metrics (Per ISO 261 / Metric Standard)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Diameter** | **D** | **M3, M4, M6, M8, M10, M12... (mm)** |
| **Pitch** | **P** | **0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75 mm** |
| **Lead** | **L** | **L = n × P** (n = number of starts) |
| **Major Diameter** | **d₁ (external) / D (internal)** | **Nominal diameter** |
| **Minor Diameter** | **d₂ (external) / D₁ (internal)** | **D − 1.0825 × P** |
| **Thread Depth** | **h** | **0.5413 × P** |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU problems, the **most frequently used parameters** are *D*, *P*, and *h*. Memorize `h = 0.5413 × P` and `D₁ = D − 1.0825 × P`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Side Core Threads

The mechanism operates in a strictly timed sequence:

1. **Mould Clamping:** The two main mould halves close and clamp. The side core is held in its "inserted" position by an actuator (hydraulic lock, angle pin, or cam).
2. **Injection Phase:** Molten plastic fills the cavity. The threaded cavity profile is the **negative impression** of the side core's thread.
3. **Cooling & Solidification:** The plastic solidifies around the side core, mechanically interlocking with the thread profile.
4. **Actuator Release:** The hydraulic cylinder (or mechanical cam) retracts, pulling the side core **sideways** out of the threaded feature. The stroke of this side travel is exactly equal to the **major diameter (D)** plus a small clearance (~0.5 mm).
5. **Main Mould Opening:** The cavity plate separates from the core plate. The moulded part remains on the main core.
6. **Ejection:** Ejector pins push the part out of the main core.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Side Cores are Mandatory for Threads:** A thread is a *helical undercut*. Since helical features have geometry on **all sides** in the mould-closing direction, no amount of straight axial mould opening can release the part. The side core must either **rotate (unscrew)** or **slide radially**.

### 2.2 Classification of Side Core Mechanisms for Threads

| Mechanism Type | Application | Actuation | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotating Side Core (Unscrewing Core)** | Internal & external threads, deep threads | Rack-and-pinion, hydraulic motor, gear train | ★★★★★ |
| **Collapsible Core** | Internal threads on small, symmetrical parts | Mechanical collapse (cam fingers) | ★★★★ |
| **Straight Side Slide (Non-Rotating)** | Only for *partial* threads or non-circular undercuts | Angle pin, hydraulic cylinder | ★★ |
| **Stripper Ring + Rotating Core** | External threads on bottles, closures | Continuous rotation during ejection | ★★★ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = 0.5413 \cdot P$ | Thread depth (single-start metric) | mm |
| 2 | $D_1 = D - 1.0825 \cdot P$ | Minor diameter (internal thread) | mm |
| 3 | $d_2 = D - 0.6495 \cdot P$ | Pitch diameter | mm |
| 4 | $F_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L_{thr} \cdot p_{inj} \cdot \tan(\phi + \rho)$ | Stripping force for threaded part | N |
| 5 | $T_{unscrew} = F_s \cdot \dfrac{d_2}{2}$ | Unscrewing torque required | N·mm |
| 6 | $S_{side} = D + c$ | Required side core travel (slide type) | mm |
| 7 | $S_{rotate} = \dfrac{L_{thr}}{P} \times 360°$ | Rotation angle of unscrewing core | degrees |
| 8 | $F_{clamp,min} = F_{inject} \geq \dfrac{A_{part} \cdot p_{inj}}{1000}$ | Minimum mould clamping force | kN |
| 9 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{T_{output}}{T_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Mechanical efficiency of rack-pinion | % |
| 10 | $t_{cool} = k \cdot s^2$ | Cooling time (Stokes' rule) | s |

**Symbol Legend (Critical for KTU Answers):**
- $D$ = Nominal (major) thread diameter
- $P$ = Pitch
- $L_{thr}$ = Length of threaded engagement
- $p_{inj}$ = Injection pressure (typical: **40–80 MPa**)
- $\phi$ = Thread helix angle = $\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{P}{\pi \cdot d_2}\right)$
- $\rho$ = Friction angle (typical: $\rho = 8°$–$12°$ for steel-plastic contact)
- $c$ = Safety clearance (typical: **0.5 mm**)
- $s$ = Maximum wall thickness of the part

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that for **multi-start threads**, the lead $L = n \times P$ must be used in the rotation angle formula, not the pitch. The helix angle $\phi$ also uses the **lead**, not the pitch.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging Industry:** Every PET bottle preform, every shampoo cap, every toothpaste closure — all are produced using **side core unscrewing mechanisms**.
- **Automotive:** Threaded inserts in dashboards, threaded bosses in engine covers.
- **Medical Devices:** Syringe barrels, IV connectors, threaded vial caps (all require Class-A hygienic threads).
- **Electronics:** Threaded inserts for heat sinks, cable glands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stripping Force for a Threaded Moulded Part

**Problem Setup:** Consider an internal thread of nominal diameter $D$, length $L_{thr}$, moulded in plastic over a steel core. The plastic is in tight mechanical interlock with the thread helix.

**Step 1 — Identify the resisting forces on the thread flank.**

The thread flank is a helical surface. When the side core is pulled sideways (or rotated out), the plastic resists along the helix. Resolve this resistance into two components: one normal to the flank (causing friction) and one along the flank (causing the stripping load).

Let the **mean pitch diameter** be $d_2$. The normal force on the helical flank per unit length is:

$$\frac{dN}{dL} = p_{inj} \cdot d_2$$

**Step 2 — Account for the helix angle and friction.**

The flank is inclined at helix angle $\phi$ relative to the axial direction. Friction adds an angle $\rho$. The **effective angle** resisting motion is $(\phi + \rho)$.

The total axial (stripping) force becomes:

$$F_s = \pi \cdot d_2 \cdot L_{thr} \cdot p_{inj} \cdot \tan(\phi + \rho)$$

**Step 3 — Express $\phi$ in terms of known thread parameters.**

The helix angle for a single-start thread is:

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{P}{\pi \cdot d_2}$$

For multi-start threads, replace $P$ with the lead $L = n \times P$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

Plugging $d_2 = D - 0.6495 \cdot P$ into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_s &= \pi \cdot (D - 0.6495 P) \cdot L_{thr} \cdot p_{inj} \cdot \tan(\phi + \rho) \\
&= \pi \cdot d_2 \cdot L_{thr} \cdot p_{inj} \cdot \tan(\phi + \rho)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for KTU practice.**

> Given: Internal thread **M10 × 1.5**, $L_{thr} = 20$ mm, $p_{inj} = 60$ MPa, $\rho = 10°$.
> 
> Step 5a: $d_2 = 10 - 0.6495 \times 1.5 = 9.026$ mm
> 
> Step 5b: $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{1.5}{\pi \times 9.026} = 0.0529 \Rightarrow \phi = 3.03°$
> 
> Step 5c: $(\phi + \rho) = 13.03° \Rightarrow \tan(13.03°) = 0.2316$
> 
> Step 5d: $F_s = \pi \times 9.026 \times 20 \times 60 \times 0.2316 = 7880$ N ≈ **7.88 kN**

### 3.2 Derivation: Unscrewing Torque

The unscrewing torque is the moment required to rotate the side core out of the solidified thread.

The stripping force $F_s$ acts at the pitch radius $d_2 / 2$ (in mm), so:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{unscrew} &= F_s \cdot \frac{d_2}{2} \\
&= \frac{\pi \cdot d_2^2 \cdot L_{thr} \cdot p_{inj} \cdot \tan(\phi + \rho)}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

For the M10 example above:

$$T_{unscrew} = 7880 \times \frac{9.026}{2} = 35{,}562 \text{ N·mm} \approx 35.56 \text{ N·m}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Required Rotation Angle

For a single-start thread, the core must rotate exactly one full turn per pitch length engaged. For multi-start, this is reduced.

$$\theta_{rotate} = \frac{L_{thr}}{L} \times 360° = \frac{L_{thr}}{n \cdot P} \times 360°$$

For the M10 single-start example with $L_{thr} = 20$ mm, $P = 1.5$ mm:

$$\theta_{rotate} = \frac{20}{1} \times \frac{360°}{1.5} = 4800° = 13.33 \text{ full turns}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Side Core Travel (for Slide-Type, Non-Rotating)

For a straight slide used to release a non-threaded undercut adjacent to a thread, the minimum travel is:

$$S_{side} = D + 0.5 \text{ mm (clearance)}$$

This ensures the largest cross-section of the side core has cleared the undercut.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Mechanism

| Condition | Recommended Mechanism | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Thread length $\leq 2 \times D$ | Collapsible core | Lower cost, faster cycle |
| Thread length $> 2 \times D$ | Rotating unscrewing core | Reliable, no thread damage |
| Multi-start thread ($n \geq 2$) | Rotating unscrewing core | Rotation reduced by factor $n$ |
| Soft plastic (PP, PE) | Collapsible core | Stripping may distort part |
| Hard plastic (ABS, PA) | Rotating unscrewing core | Stronger mechanical lock |
| External thread (large diameter) | Stripper ring with rotation | Continuous ejection |
| **High-volume production (>100k/yr)** | Rotating unscrewing core | Long tool life, automation-friendly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (from mould design handbooks):** If the **length-to-diameter ratio** $L_{thr}/D > 2$, **always** use a rotating unscrewing core. Collapsible cores are limited to short threads due to the mechanical difficulty of collapsing over a long length.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mould Architecture: Side Core Thread Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Clamping Plate] --> B[Sprue Bushing]
    B --> C[Cavity Plate Fixed Half]
    C --> D[Moulded Plastic Part with Thread]
    D --> E[Side Core Threaded Insert]
    E --> F[Side Core Retainer Block]
    F --> G[Hydraulic Cylinder Actuator]
    D --> H[Core Plate Moving Half]
    H --> I[Support Plate]
    I --> J[Ejector Plate]
    J --> K[Ejector Pins]
    K --> L[Bottom Clamping Plate]
    G -.Sideways Retraction.-> E
    K -.Vertical Ejection.-> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Cavity Plate (C)** and **Core Plate (H)** are the two main mould halves.
- The **Side Core (E)** moves horizontally (left-right), driven by **Hydraulic Cylinder (G)**.
- **Ejector Pins (K)** move vertically from below, after the side core has cleared.
- The plastic part **D** is the threaded product, held on the main core until ejection.

### 4.2 Process Flow: Side Core Unscrewing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Mould Closed and Clamped] --> S2[Stage 2: Plastic Injection and Cooling]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Hydraulic Valve Opens]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Side Core Rotates and Withdraws]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Main Mould Opens Vertically]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Ejector Pins Push Part Out]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Part Drops into Conveyor]
    S7 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Rotating vs. Collapsible Core Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RotatingType[Rotating Unscrewing Core]
        R1[Rack and Pinion Drive] --> R2[Gear Train]
        R2 --> R3[Threaded Core Shaft]
        R3 --> R4[Output Rotation to Unscrew]
    end
    subgraph CollapsibleType[Collapsible Core]
        K1[Central Pull Rod] --> K2[Cam Fingers Inside Core]
        K2 --> K3[Collapsible Segments Inward]
        K3 --> K4[Core Shrinks to Release Thread]
    end
    RotatingType --- CollapsibleType
```

### 4.4 Force Vector Diagram on Thread Flank (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normal Force N from Plastic Pressure] --> B[Friction Force F equals mu times N]
    B --> C[Axial Stripping Force Fs]
    A --> C
    C --> D[Resultant Force along Helix]
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU written exams, a **neatly drawn force vector triangle** on the thread flank earns full marks. Show $N$ (normal), $F = \mu N$ (friction), and the resultant $R$ at angle $(\phi + \rho)$ to the axis. The vector diagram is mandatory for the **14-mark design questions**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a side core. Why is it specifically required for moulding threaded features?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **side core** is an auxiliary mould insert that moves **perpendicular to the main mould opening direction** to form undercuts, internal cavities, or threaded profiles on a moulded part. (1 mark)

Threaded features are **helical undercuts** — the thread helix extends around the full circumference of the part in the mould-closing direction. A straight axial mould opening cannot release such geometry because the thread flanks mechanically interlock with the part in all radial directions. (1 mark)

Hence, a side core (rotating or collapsible) is used to retract **sideways or radially** from the thread after the plastic solidifies, freeing the part. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a rotating unscrewing core and a collapsible core. State one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Rotating Unscrewing Core | Collapsible Core |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Rotates about its axis to unscrew from thread | Segments collapse radially inward |
| Thread length suitability | Any length, especially long threads | Short threads ($L_{thr} \leq 2D$) |
| Cost | Higher (gear train, motor) | Lower (mechanical cam) |

**Applications:** (1 mark each)
- **Rotating:** Bottle caps, large threaded connectors.
- **Collapsible:** Small threaded inserts in electronic housings, syringe nozzles.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *Explain with a neat sketch the working of a side core unscrewing mechanism for moulding an internal thread. Discuss the role of the rack-and-pinion actuator. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *An internal thread M12 × 1.75 of length 25 mm is moulded in polypropylene. The injection pressure is 65 MPa and the friction angle is 10°. Calculate:* (7 marks)
  - *(i) The pitch diameter* 
  - *(ii) The helix angle* 
  - *(iii) The stripping force* 
  - *(iv) The unscrewing torque*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Side Core Unscrewing Mechanism (7 marks):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** The side core is a steel insert machined with the exact external thread profile (positive). It is mounted in a side core retainer block, guided by a hardened bushing, and connected via a rack-and-pinion or gear train to an external drive (electric motor or hydraulic motor).

2. **Operating Sequence (3 marks):**
   - Clamping: Hydraulic lock pins the side core in the "forward" position.
   - Moulding: Plastic fills and solidifies around the thread.
   - Release: The hydraulic lock retracts. The motor drives the rack, which turns the pinion, rotating the side core.
   - Withdrawal: The side core unscrews itself out of the plastic thread.
   - Main Mould Opens: After full unscrewing, the main mould separates vertically.
   - Ejection: Ejector pins push the part out.

3. **Role of Rack and Pinion (2 marks):** Converts the linear motion of the hydraulic ram (input) into precise rotational motion of the core. The gear ratio is selected so that one full ram stroke corresponds to the exact number of thread turns required for full unscrewing. This provides high torque, precise angular control, and synchronization with mould opening.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 12$ mm, $P = 1.75$ mm, $L_{thr} = 25$ mm, $p_{inj} = 65$ MPa $= 65$ N/mm², $\rho = 10°$, single-start thread.

**(i) Pitch diameter (1.5 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
d_2 &= D - 0.6495 \cdot P \\
&= 12 - 0.6495 \times 1.75 \\
&= 12 - 1.137 = 10.863 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Helix angle (1.5 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\tan(\phi) &= \frac{P}{\pi \cdot d_2} = \frac{1.75}{\pi \times 10.863} = 0.0513 \\
\phi &= 2.94°
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Stripping force (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
F_s &= \pi \cdot d_2 \cdot L_{thr} \cdot p_{inj} \cdot \tan(\phi + \rho) \\
&= \pi \times 10.863 \times 25 \times 65 \times \tan(2.94° + 10°) \\
&= \pi \times 10.863 \times 25 \times 65 \times \tan(12.94°) \\
&= \pi \times 10.863 \times 25 \times 65 \times 0.2298 \\
&= 12{,}752 \text{ N} \approx 12.75 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Unscrewing torque (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{unscrew} &= F_s \times \frac{d_2}{2} \\
&= 12{,}752 \times \frac{10.863}{2} \\
&= 69{,}247 \text{ N·mm} \approx 69.25 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** *With a clear diagram, describe the collapsible core mechanism for moulding internal threads. Mention its limitations. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A side core slides sideways (without rotation) to release a partial thread of M16 × 2.0 mm, length 18 mm, on a polypropylene part. Determine: (7 marks)*
  - *(i) Thread depth* 
  - *(ii) Minimum diameter of the side core* 
  - *(iii) Required side travel distance* 
  - *(iv) Whether a rotating core would be more suitable (justify with $L_{thr}/D$ ratio)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Collapsible Core Mechanism (7 marks):**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** A collapsible core consists of multiple (typically 3 to 16) hardened steel segments arranged in a circle. The outer surface of the assembled segments forms the thread profile. A central pull rod with tapered cam fingers forces the segments **radially inward** when actuated.

2. **Operation (2 marks):** After moulding, the central pull rod is pulled axially (toward the moving mould half). The cam fingers slide along the inner tapered surfaces of the segments, pushing them **inward** by a small amount (≈ 0.5–1 mm). This radial collapse reduces the segment's outer diameter below the minor diameter of the thread, allowing the part to be ejected straight off the collapsed core.

3. **Diagram description (1.5 marks):** Central pull rod with cam fingers → engages 8 segments → segments form an octagonal/circular thread profile → moulding position (segments out) → collapsing position (segments in, part released).

4. **Limitations (1.5 marks):**
   - Limited to **short threads** ($L_{thr}/D \leq 2$) because long collapsible cores are mechanically unstable.
   - Not suitable for **soft plastics** that can creep into segment gaps.
   - Higher maintenance; segments wear unevenly.
   - Difficult to manufacture and assemble with tight tolerances.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 16$ mm, $P = 2.0$ mm, $L_{thr} = 18$ mm.

**(i) Thread depth (1.5 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 0.5413 \times P \\
&= 0.5413 \times 2.0 = 1.083 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Minimum diameter of the side core (1.5 marks):**
The side core's outer diameter equals the **nominal diameter** of the thread, since it forms the negative of the internal thread:
$$D_{core} = D = 16 \text{ mm}$$

(The minimum would be the minor diameter $D_1 = D - 1.0825P = 13.84$ mm, but the core must form the full major diameter profile.)

**(iii) Required side travel (2 marks):**
For a non-rotating slide:
$$S_{side} = D + c = 16 + 0.5 = 16.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Suitability of rotating core (2 marks):**
$$\frac{L_{thr}}{D} = \frac{18}{16} = 1.125$$
Since $L_{thr}/D < 2$, the thread is short. **Either mechanism is feasible**, but a **collapsible core** is recommended for cost reasons in this case. A rotating unscrewing core would be over-engineered for this geometry.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MPa to N/mm²** when computing force — causes a 10³ error, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using pitch $P$ instead of lead $L$** in the rotation formula for multi-start threads — loses full marks on that sub-part.
> 3. **Omitting the force vector diagram** in 14-mark design questions — examiners specifically allocate 1–2 marks for the diagram.
> 4. **Not mentioning safety clearance** ($c = 0.5$ mm) in side travel calculation — common omission.
> 5. **Confusing helix angle formula**: using $D$ instead of $d_2$ in the denominator — wrong answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **side core** is a transversely-moving mould member used to form undercuts and threads.
- ✅ **Threads are helical undercuts** — they **cannot** be released by simple axial mould opening.
- ✅ Two main mechanisms: **Rotating unscrewing core** and **Collapsible core**.
- ✅ **Key formulas** (must memorize):
  - $h = 0.5413 P$
  - $d_2 = D - 0.6495 P$
  - $D_1 = D - 1.0825 P$
  - $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{P}{\pi \cdot d_2}$ (use lead $L$ for multi-start)
  - $F_s = \pi \cdot d_2 \cdot L_{thr} \cdot p_{inj} \cdot \tan(\phi + \rho)$
  - $T_{unscrew} = F_s \cdot (d_2 / 2)$
  - $S_{side} = D + 0.5$ mm
- ✅ **Selection rule:** $L_{thr}/D > 2 \Rightarrow$ rotating core; $L_{thr}/D \leq 2 \Rightarrow$ collapsible core (or rotating for high volume).
- ✅ Always show the **force vector diagram** in design questions.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions**: friction angle $\rho = 10°$ (if not given), safety clearance = **0.5 mm**, single-start thread (if not specified).
- ✅ Convert all pressures to **N/mm²** (i.e., 1 MPa = 1 N/mm²) before substituting into force formulas.
- ✅ The **rack-and-pinion** is the most common actuator for rotating cores; **hydraulic cylinder** is used for straight slides.
- ✅ **Stripper ring with continuous rotation** is the preferred method for external threads (e.g., bottle preforms, closures).
- ✅ In KTU viva/practical: be ready to sketch the unscrewing core mechanism and label the rack, pinion, hydraulic lock pin, and ejector plate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runnerless
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Runnerless

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Runnerless Moulding** is an advanced injection moulding technique in which the molten polymer melt is delivered from the machine nozzle directly into the mould cavity through a *thermally regulated* distribution system, such that the melt in the runner passages **never solidifies** between successive shots. The runner channel therefore becomes a permanent, heated (or self-insulating) passage that produces **zero runner scrap**.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, runnerless moulding is classified into two principal categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Hot Runner Mould (HRM):** An externally heated manifold and nozzle system keeps the runner melt above its melt temperature (Tm) throughout the cycle.
>
> **2. Insulated Runner Mould (IRM):** A conventional cold runner of *enlarged diameter* is used; the polymer freezes against the cold mould wall but a *molten core* persists due to thermal insulation by the frozen skin.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine pouring hot tea through a copper pipe versus a thick wool-wrapped pipe:

- **Cold runner** = Copper pipe: The tea (polymer) freezes solid inside the pipe (runner), and you must break the pipe (ejector pin) and throw away the frozen tea (scrap).
- **Hot runner** = Pipe kept warm by a heating tape: The tea never freezes, the pipe stays clean, and nothing is wasted.
- **Insulated runner** = Wool-wrapped pipe: The outer layer of tea freezes (acts as insulation), but the inner core remains liquid — saving most of the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Runnerless moulding is a direct response to the industry demand for "zero-scrap," "fully-automated," and "high-cavitation" injection moulding — a critical concept in modern polymer product manufacturing.

## 1.3 Industrial Significance Metrics

| Performance Parameter | Cold Runner | Hot Runner | Insulated Runner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material Wastage | **15 – 30 %** | **0 – 2 %** | **5 – 10 %** |
| Cycle Time | Baseline | **– 10 to 25 %** | **– 5 to 15 %** |
| Tool Cost Ratio | **1.0×** | **2.5 – 4.0×** | **1.4 – 1.8×** |
| Cavitation Density | Low | **Very High (16 – 128)** | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" — Operational Logic

### A. Hot Runner Mould (HRM) — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Machine Nozzle Interface:** The injection unit nozzle mates with a *re-entrant* or *extended* sprue bushing. No conventional cold sprue is formed.
2. **Heated Manifold Block:** A central steel block (the *manifold*) distributes melt to multiple cavities. It is heated by **cartridge heaters** (typically 230 V, 100 – 1500 W) embedded in bored pockets.
3. **Nozzle Tips:** Each manifold outlet terminates at a *nozzle* that gates directly into the cavity. Gate styles include *edge gate*, *pinpoint gate*, *hot tip gate*, and *valve gate*.
4. **Thermal Separation Plates:** Two stainless-steel *heat-insulating plates* (often called "support plates" or "air gap plates") are placed above and below the manifold to prevent heat migration into the mould base and cooling system.
5. **Thermocouple Feedback:** Type-J or Type-K thermocouples (one per manifold zone) provide closed-loop PID temperature control to within **± 1 °C** of the set point.
6. **Gate Open/Close:** In valve-gated systems, a hydraulic or pneumatic *actuator* mechanically opens/closes a needle at the gate, eliminating stringing and drool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept — "Manifold Balance":** The runner lengths from the manifold centre to each cavity nozzle must be *pressure-balanced* (equal flow path lengths) and *thermally balanced* (uniform heater wattage distribution) to ensure all cavities fill simultaneously with identical part weight.

### B. Insulated Runner Mould (IRM) — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Large Diameter Runner:** The cold runner channel is enlarged to a diameter of typically **D = 2D<sub>min</sub> to 3D<sub>min</sub>** (where D<sub>min</sub> is the minimum cold-runner diameter for that part).
2. **Skin Formation:** During the dwell phase, a thin polymer layer freezes against the cold runner wall.
3. **Self-Insulation Effect:** The frozen skin has low thermal conductivity (k ≈ 0.15 – 0.30 W/m·K for most amorphous polymers). Heat generated by viscous shear (ηγ̇²) and the incoming hot melt keeps the core fluid.
4. **No Ejection of Runner:** The runner is *not* ejected; it remains in the mould and acts as the feed channel for the next shot.
5. **Discharge with Parts:** When the mould opens, the runner is pulled off the cold sprue by the parts and is later manually or automatically separated (still 5 – 10 % scrap).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** IRM is a *low-cost alternative* to HRM. It is most successful with **high-viscosity, low-thermal-conductivity polymers** such as PE, PP, and PS. It is rarely used with glass-filled or high-temperature engineering plastics.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{s} \cdot n}{3600}$ | Volumetric flow rate from shot volume and cycles/hr | m³/s |
| 2 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}}$ | Apparent wall shear rate in circular runner (Poiseuille flow) | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{r} = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^{4}}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille pressure drop in circular runner | Pa |
| 4 | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L}$ | Wall shear stress in runner | Pa |
| 5 | $q_{visc} = \eta \dot{\gamma}^{2}$ | Volumetric viscous heat generation rate | W/m³ |
| 6 | $q_{cond} = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{\delta}$ | Heat conducted through frozen skin (insulated runner) | W |
| 7 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\pi \alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{4 \Delta T_{0}}{\pi \Delta T_{e}}\right)$ | Simplified cooling/skin freezing time (analogy: part wall) | s |
| 8 | $\eta = \eta_{0} e^{\left(\dfrac{E_{a}}{RT}\right)}$ | Arrhenius viscosity-temperature dependence | Pa·s |
| 9 | $P_{clamp} \ge P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$ | Required mould clamping force check | N |
| 10 | $D_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{32 \mu L \dot{\gamma}_{max}^{-1}}{\pi \Delta P_{max}}\right)^{1/4}$ | Optimal runner diameter (constraint-based) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
> - $L$ = runner length (m)
> - $D$ = runner diameter (m)
> - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
> - $V_s$ = shot volume (m³)
> - $n$ = cycles per hour
> - $k$ = polymer thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
> - $\delta$ = frozen skin thickness (m)
> - $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
> - $E_a$ = activation energy of flow (J/mol)
> - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/mol·K**
> - $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Safeguard Reminder:** In the KTU Formula Sheet table above, all absolute value bars and divisions are rendered with `\vert` / `\dfrac` to keep the markdown table parser stable.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Runnerless Moulding

| Industry Sector | Typical Application | Preferred Runnerless Variant |
|---|---|---|
| **Packaging** | Bottle caps, container lids, thin-wall tubs | Hot Runner (high cavitation, valve gate) |
| **Automotive** | Bumpers, instrument panels, glove box | Hot Runner (sequential valve gate) |
| **Medical** | Syringe barrels, IV components | Hot Runner (cold-tip, pin gate) |
| **Electronics** | Mobile housings, connector housings | Hot Runner (manifold with thermal gating) |
| **Consumer Goods** | Pen barrels, combs, toys | Insulated Runner (low-cost) |
| **PET Preforms** | Beverage bottle preforms | Hot Runner (16 – 96 cavity) |

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop and Shear Rate in a Circular Runner (Hagen-Poiseuille)

This derivation is fundamental for sizing runners in **any** runnerless system (since the cold-tip section and the gate still act as short circular channels).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the Navier-Stokes Equation in cylindrical form (steady, fully-developed, incompressible, axisymmetric flow):**

$$
0 = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left( \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( r \dfrac{\partial v_{z}}{\partial r} \right) \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the constant pressure gradient term and integrate once with respect to r:**

Let $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \text{const.}$ (positive in the direction of flow).

$$
\mu \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{d}{dr}\left( r \dfrac{dv_{z}}{dr} \right) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}
$$

Multiply by $\frac{r}{\mu}$:

$$
\dfrac{d}{dr}\left( r \dfrac{dv_{z}}{dr} \right) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu L} r
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate once:**

$$
r \dfrac{dv_{z}}{dr} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r^{2} + C_{1}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dv_{z}}{dr} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r + \dfrac{C_{1}}{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the no-slip boundary condition at the wall ($r = R = D/2$):** $v_z = 0$, and the velocity must be finite at the centre ($r = 0$), forcing $C_1 = 0$.

$$
\dfrac{dv_{z}}{dr} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate a second time to obtain the velocity profile:**

$$
v_{z}(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)
$$

This is the classical **parabolic Hagen-Poiseuille profile**.

**Step 6 — Compute the volumetric flow rate by integrating over the cross-section:**

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_{z}(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr = \int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right) 2 \pi r \, dr
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} \left( R^{2} r - r^{3} \right) dr
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \dfrac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( \dfrac{R^{4}}{2} - \dfrac{R^{4}}{4} \right) = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $R = D/2$ to express in terms of diameter D:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \left( \dfrac{D}{2} \right)^{4} = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P D^{4}}{128 \mu L}
$$

**Step 9 — Invert to obtain the pressure drop formula (Hagen-Poiseuille equation):**

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^{4}}}
$$

**Step 10 — Derive the apparent wall shear rate:**

By definition, $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = -\dfrac{dv_{z}}{dr}\Big\vert_{r=R}$. From Step 4:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} R = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conversion Logic:** These two boxed equations are the *single most important pair of formulas* for runner design. A 2× increase in diameter reduces pressure drop by 16× (since $\Delta P \propto D^{-4}$), which is why hot runner designers prefer *short, large-diameter* manifold channels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frozen Skin Thickness in an Insulated Runner

For an Insulated Runner Mould (IRM), the heat balance at the frozen-melt interface determines whether the runner stays operational.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry:**
- Outer radius of runner: $R = D/2$
- Frozen skin radius: $r_s$ (melt occupies $0 \le r \le r_s$)
- Frozen skin thickness: $\delta = R - r_s$

**Step 2 — Steady-state radial heat conduction through the cylindrical shell (Fourier's Law):**

$$
q_{cond} = \dfrac{2 \pi k L (T_m - T_w)}{\ln(R / r_s)}
$$

**Step 3 — Approximation for thin skin ($\delta \ll R$):**

Using $\ln(R/r_s) = \ln(1 + \delta/r_s) \approx \delta/r_s \approx \delta/R$ for thin shells:

$$
q_{cond} \approx \dfrac{2 \pi k L R (T_m - T_w)}{\delta} = \dfrac{\pi k L D (T_m - T_w)}{\delta}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy supplied by viscous dissipation in the molten core (Step 5 of Derivation 1 gave the velocity profile; integrate viscous heating):**

$$
q_{visc} = \int_{0}^{r_s} \eta \left( \dfrac{dv_{z}}{dr} \right)^{2} 2 \pi r L \, dr
$$

Substituting $dv_z/dr = (\Delta P / 2\mu L) r$ and evaluating:

$$
q_{visc} = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P^{2} r_s^{4}}{8 \mu L}
$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state thermal equilibrium:** $q_{visc} = q_{cond} + q_{loss}$ (latent heat at the interface is neglected for the steady-state operating point; it is only relevant at start-up).

**Step 6 — Solving for the skin thickness $\delta$:**

$$
\dfrac{\pi k L D (T_m - T_w)}{\delta} = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P^{2} (D/2 - \delta)^{4}}{8 \mu L}
$$

This implicit equation is solved iteratively. The engineering rule of thumb is:

$$
\boxed{\delta_{ss} \approx \dfrac{8 \mu L k (T_m - T_w)}{\Delta P^{2} D^{3}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** A student who *states* the steady-state skin thickness equation, identifies all variables, and correctly applies the no-slip boundary condition earns full marks. Marks are most often lost when students forget to **convert between radius R and diameter D**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Manifold Thermal Balance (Hot Runner System)

**Step 1 — Define energy terms per cycle:**
- $Q_{in}$ = heat input from cartridge heaters (J)
- $Q_{melt}$ = energy carried by incoming polymer melt (J)
- $Q_{loss,top}$ = conductive loss to top mould plate (J)
- $Q_{loss,bot}$ = conductive loss to bottom mould plate (J)
- $Q_{cav}$ = energy absorbed by cavity walls (J)
- $Q_{eject}$ = energy carried out by part + solidified gate (J)

**Step 2 — Steady-state thermal balance (no temperature drift in manifold):**

$$
Q_{in} + Q_{melt} = Q_{loss,top} + Q_{loss,bot} + Q_{cav} + Q_{eject}
$$

**Step 3 — Conductive loss through air-gap plate (Fourier conduction through composite wall):**

$$
Q_{loss} = \dfrac{A_{plate} \cdot (T_{manifold} - T_{coolant})}{\sum \frac{L_i}{k_i}}
$$

where the sum includes manifold plate + air gap + backing plate + cooling channel wall.

**Step 4 — For an air gap of thickness $L_{air}$ with thermal conductivity $k_{air} \approx 0.026$ W/m·K, and stainless-steel plates of conductivity $k_{ss} \approx 16$ W/m·K, the dominant resistance is the air gap.** The required heater wattage simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{W_{heater} = \dfrac{k_{air} \cdot A_{plate} \cdot (T_{manifold} - T_{plate})}{L_{air}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** A typical 4-cavity hot runner for PP at 240 °C requires **W = 600 – 1200 W** of cartridge heater input when the air gap is 3 – 5 mm. This is one of the *first design calculations* a mould designer performs for HRM.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** A hot runner system delivers 50 cm³/s of polypropylene (melt viscosity 250 Pa·s) through a manifold channel of 8 mm diameter and 80 mm length. Calculate: (i) the wall shear rate, (ii) the wall shear stress, and (iii) the pressure drop.

**Given:**
- $Q = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s
- $\mu = 250$ Pa·s
- $D = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $L = 80 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Solution:**

(i) Wall shear rate:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}} = \dfrac{32 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (8 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 5.12 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 994.7 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

(ii) Wall shear stress:

$$
\tau_{w} = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{w} = 250 \times 994.7 \approx 248{,}675 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.49 \text{ MPa}
$$

(iii) Pressure drop:

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^{4}} = \dfrac{128 \times 250 \times 0.08 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (8 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{0.128}{1.6085 \times 10^{-9}} \times 10^{-3} = \dfrac{0.128}{1.6085 \times 10^{-9}} \text{ Pa}
$$

Re-evaluating carefully:

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \times 250 \times 0.08 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{0.128}{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{0.128}{1.2868 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 99{,}471 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.0995 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Result:** The pressure drop in the manifold is **≈ 0.1 MPa** — well within the typical 5 – 10 MPa allowable drop across the manifold.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Hot Runner Mould Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Injection Unit Nozzle] --> node2[Re-entrant Sprue Bushing]
    node2 --> node3a[Heated Manifold Zone 1]
    node2 --> node3b[Heated Manifold Zone 2]
    node3a --> node4a[Hot Tip Nozzle Cavity 1]
    node3a --> node4b[Hot Tip Nozzle Cavity 2]
    node3b --> node4c[Hot Tip Nozzle Cavity 3]
    node3b --> node4d[Hot Tip Nozzle Cavity 4]
    node4a --> node5a[Mould Cavity 1]
    node4b --> node5b[Mould Cavity 2]
    node4c --> node5c[Mould Cavity 3]
    node4d --> node5d[Mould Cavity 4]
    node5a --> node6[Cooling Channels]
    node5b --> node6
    node5c --> node6
    node5d --> node6
    node6 --> node7[Coolant Inlet and Outlet]
    node8[Cartridge Heaters in Manifold] -.-> node3a
    node8 -.-> node3b
    node9[Type-K Thermocouples] -.-> node3a
    node9 -.-> node3b
    node10[Air Gap Insulation Plates] -.-> node3a
    node10 -.-> node3b
    node5a --> node11[Finished Part Ejection]
    node5b --> node11
    node5c --> node11
    node5d --> node11
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** represent the polymer melt flow path.
- **Dashed arrows** represent energy/control signals (heating, sensing, thermal insulation).
- The diagram shows a **4-cavity balanced hot runner mould** with twin manifold zones, each independently heated and temperature-controlled.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Insulated Runner Mould Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Injection Unit Nozzle] --> B[Conventional Sprue Bushing]
    B --> C[Enlarged Diameter Runner]
    C --> D1[Gate to Cavity 1]
    C --> D2[Gate to Cavity 2]
    C --> D3[Gate to Cavity 3]
    D1 --> E1[Cavity 1 with Cold Mould Wall]
    D2 --> E2[Cavity 2 with Cold Mould Wall]
    D3 --> E3[Cavity 3 with Cold Mould Wall]
    E1 --> F[Part and Runner Ejection]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    G[Frozen Polymer Skin Layer] -.-> C
    H[Insulating Effect of Frozen Skin] -.-> C
    I[Shear Heating of Molten Core] -.-> C
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The runner is *not heated externally*. The dashed arrows represent the **self-regulating thermal mechanism**: frozen skin insulates, viscous dissipation heats the core.
- A separate scrap-removal step is required to separate the runner from the parts.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Process Decision Tree for Choosing Runnerless Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Choose Runner System] --> P2{Is Production Volume High?}
    P2 -- Yes, > 100k parts/yr --> P3{Is Cavitation > 8?}
    P2 -- No, low volume --> P4[Use Cold Runner]
    P3 -- Yes --> P5{Is Material High Temp Engineering?}
    P3 -- No --> P6[Use Insulated Runner]
    P5 -- Yes --> P7[Use Hot Runner with Valve Gate]
    P5 -- No --> P8[Use Hot Runner with Hot Tip]
    P4 --> P9[End: Conventional Cold Runner]
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P10[End: Hot Runner System]
    P8 --> P10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Drawing such decision trees in the exam earns the student a **'high' grade descriptor** for the CO (Course Outcome) related to mould design selection — a key differentiator in 14-mark questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

### Question 1: Define runnerless moulding and list its two main types. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Runnerless moulding is an injection moulding technique in which the polymer melt is delivered to the mould cavity through a thermally regulated runner system that does not solidify between cycles, thereby eliminating runner scrap. The two main types are:

1. **Hot Runner Mould (HRM):** Uses externally heated manifold and nozzles.
2. **Insulated Runner Mould (IRM):** Uses a large-diameter cold runner in which a frozen skin insulates a molten core.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Students often write *"hot runner"* and *"cold runner"* as the two types of runnerless moulding. Cold runner is the **opposite** of runnerless; the second type is *insulated runner*, not cold runner.

---

### Question 2: State any three advantages of hot runner moulding over cold runner moulding. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Zero runner scrap** → material saving of 15 – 30 % per part.
2. **Shorter cycle time** → no need to plasticize, inject, cool, and eject the runner.
3. **Higher cavitation density** → enables 16, 32, 64, 96, or even 128-cavity moulds.
4. (Bonus) **Better part quality** due to consistent pressure and temperature at the gate.
5. (Bonus) **Full automation compatibility** (no manual runner removal).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Writing *"cheaper tooling"* as an advantage. Hot runner moulds are **2.5 – 4× costlier** than cold runner moulds.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester Pattern, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — Total 14 Marks

**(a) Explain the construction and working of a hot runner mould with a neat sketch. List at least four essential components of the manifold system.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Hot runner mould is one in which the melt in the runner is maintained above its melting point throughout the cycle.
2. **Construction – main components (4 marks):**
   - *Heated Manifold Block:* Distributes melt to all cavities; made of hot-work tool steel (H13/SKD61); contains machined runner channels.
   - *Nozzle Tips:* Hot tip, edge gate, or valve gate; gate the melt into the cavity.
   - *Heater Bands/Cartridge Heaters:* Embedded in the manifold to maintain temperature.
   - *Thermocouples (Type J/K):* For closed-loop temperature control.
   - *Air Gap / Insulating Plates:* Prevent heat transfer to mould base.
3. **Working principle (2 marks):** Melt from the machine nozzle enters the manifold, is distributed equally to all cavity nozzles via thermally balanced channels, and enters the cavity through the gate. After cooling, the mould opens, parts are ejected, and the runner remains molten for the next cycle.

**(b) Discuss the advantages, limitations, and typical applications of hot runner moulds.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer Outline:**

| Category | Points to be written (each ≈ 0.5 – 1 mark) |
|---|---|
| **Advantages** (3 marks) | (i) No runner scrap, (ii) Shorter cycle, (iii) Higher cavitation, (iv) Lower part cost at high volume, (v) Consistent part quality, (vi) Automation friendly, (vii) Better gate quality (no gate vestige in valve gate) |
| **Limitations** (2.5 marks) | (i) High initial cost, (ii) Complex maintenance, (iii) Difficult colour change, (iv) Thermal management critical, (v) Leakage risk at nozzle, (vi) Limited use with abrasive/glass-filled polymers, (vii) Start-up wastage before equilibrium |
| **Applications** (1.5 marks) | Bottle caps, PET preforms, automotive interior trims, syringe barrels, thin-wall containers, mobile housings |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students who write *only* advantages (no limitations) lose **2 marks** out of 7.
> - Sketches without proper labels (manifold, nozzle, heater, thermocouple) get **0 out of 1** for the sketch portion.
> - Vague phrases like *"hot runner is good"* without numbers (e.g., *"0 % scrap"*, *"16 – 128 cavities"*) lose marks.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — Total 14 Marks

**(a) Describe the principle, construction, and operating requirements of an insulated runner mould (IRM).** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** In IRM, the cold runner is enlarged to a diameter typically 2 – 3× the conventional minimum. During each cycle, a thin layer of polymer freezes against the cold mould wall; this frozen layer has low thermal conductivity and acts as *self-applied insulation*. Viscous shear heating in the molten core keeps the core fluid.
2. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - *Enlarged cold runner* (no external heating)
   - *Standard cold mould base* (water-cooled)
   - *Conventional sprue bushing*
   - *Manually or automatically removed runner* (still some scrap)
3. **Operating requirements (2 marks):**
   - *High melt viscosity* polymer (PE, PP, PS preferred)
   - *Steady-state thermal condition* must be achieved before production parts are accepted
   - *Dwell time* between cycles must be short (5 – 15 s)
   - *Surface finish* of runner polished to delay premature freezing

**(b) Compare hot runner moulding and insulated runner moulding in terms of scrap, cost, applications, and maintenance.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Comparison Table Format — 7 Marks):**

| Comparison Parameter | Hot Runner Mould | Insulated Runner Mould |
|---|---|---|
| **Runner Scrap** | **0 – 2 %** (almost zero) | **5 – 10 %** (runner ejected with parts) |
| **Tool Cost** | **2.5 – 4.0×** of cold runner | **1.4 – 1.8×** of cold runner |
| **Cycle Time** | **10 – 25 % shorter** | **5 – 15 % shorter** |
| **Temperature Control** | External PID-controlled heaters | Self-regulated (no external heating) |
| **Cavitation** | Very high (16 – 128 cavities) | Limited (typically 2 – 8 cavities) |
| **Material Suitability** | Wide range, incl. engineering plastics | Best with PE, PP, PS (low thermal conductivity) |
| **Colour Change** | Difficult (polymer trapped in manifold) | Easy (runner is ejected each cycle) |
| **Maintenance** | Complex (heater/TC replacement) | Simple (similar to cold runner) |
| **Start-up Scrap** | High (melt must reach equilibrium) | Low (runner is discarded anyway) |
| **Applications** | Bottle caps, preforms, automotive | Toys, combs, simple consumer goods |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - A *purely descriptive* answer without a **comparison table** will get a maximum of **5 out of 7**.
> - Students who confuse *insulated runner* with *cold runner* (saying "IRM is just cold runner") will be awarded **0 for principle** — a fatal error.
> - Numerical values (%, cost ratio, cavity count) are **mandatory** for full marks in the 2024 scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU 2024 — Module 1 — Runnerless Moulding: Quick Recall Sheet 🔥**

- ✅ **Runnerless moulding = melt stays molten in runner → zero (or near-zero) scrap.**
- ✅ **Two main types: Hot Runner Mould (HRM) and Insulated Runner Mould (IRM).**
- ✅ **HRM has 4 essential elements:** Manifold, Nozzle, Heater, Thermocouple + Air Gap Plates.
- ✅ **HRM tool cost: 2.5 – 4× cold runner.** Don't say it's cheap.
- ✅ **HRM cavitation: 16 to 128 cavities** in a single mould — a key selling point.
- ✅ **IRM works on self-insulation principle** — frozen skin insulates molten core.
- ✅ **IRM is preferred for PE, PP, PS** (low thermal conductivity, high viscosity).
- ✅ **IRM scrap = 5 – 10 %**, HRM scrap = 0 – 2 %.
- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille equation (most important formula):** $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^{4}}$ — pressure drop is *inversely proportional to the 4th power of diameter*.
- ✅ **Wall shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}}$.
- ✅ **Manifold heater wattage** dominated by air-gap conduction: $W = \dfrac{k_{air} A \Delta T}{L_{air}}$.
- ✅ **Valve gate** = no stringing/drool, ideal for high-cavitation hot runners.
- ✅ **Hot tip gate** = small gate vestige, common for medical/packaging.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 examiner keywords to use in answers:** "thermal balance," "manifold balance," "self-insulating skin," "zero scrap," "high cavitation," "cycle time reduction."
- ✅ **Common traps in exam:** (a) Don't say cold runner is a type of runnerless. (b) Don't say hot runner is cheap. (c) Don't forget to convert $R \leftrightarrow D$ in formulas.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Runner Systems in Injection Moulding

In **Injection Moulding**, the molten polymer melt must travel from the **machine nozzle** through a network of channels before entering the mould cavity. These channels are collectively called the **Runner System**. The runner system is one of the most critical design elements in mould design because it directly influences:

- **Fill Pressure** required to pack the cavity
- **Cycle Time** (especially cooling/solidification of the polymer)
- **Part Quality** (weld lines, air traps, sink marks, warpage)
- **Material Yield** (how much of the shot weight becomes usable part vs. waste)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of moulds into *Cold Runner* and *Hot Runner* systems is a **Module 1 fundamental topic**. Students are expected to draw labelled diagrams, state advantages/limitations, and justify the choice of runner system based on material, part geometry, production volume, and quality requirements.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

### **Cold Runner Mould (Conventional Runner)**

A **Cold Runner Mould** is one in which the runner channels are maintained at a temperature **below the solidification temperature (Ts)** of the polymer. As a result, the melt **solidifies inside the runner** during the cooling phase and is **ejected together with the part** at the end of each cycle. The solidified runner (called the *sprue/runner biscuit*) is then separated from the part, ground, and recycled as regrind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Condition:** Runner surface temperature **T<sub>runner</sub> < T<sub>solidification</sub> (Ts)** of the polymer. Typical mould surface temperatures: 25 °C – 95 °C (depending on polymer).

### **Hot Runner Mould (Heated Runner / Runnerless Mould)**

A **Hot Runner Mould** is one in which the runner channels are kept at a temperature **above the solidification temperature (Ts)** of the polymer throughout the cycle. The melt therefore **remains molten** in the runner and is ready for the next shot immediately. No runner waste is produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Condition:** Runner channel temperature **T<sub>runner</sub> > T<sub>melt</sub> (Tm)** of the polymer, maintained using external heater bands or internally heated manifolds (typical range: 180 °C – 320 °C depending on polymer grade).

### **Intuitive Analogy**

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bakery Frosting Pipe:**
>
> - Imagine piping icing onto a cupcake. If you let the piping bag sit for 10 minutes, the icing left inside the nozzle **hardens (cold runner)** — you have to throw it away and refill the bag every time. This is wasteful.
> - Now imagine a **thermostatically heated piping tip (hot runner)** — the icing stays soft and ready, so every squeeze deposits fresh icing onto a new cupcake without any waste.
> - Cold runner = **wasteful but simple**; Hot runner = **efficient but complex/expensive**.

---

## 1.3 Classification Sub-Tree of Runner Systems

1. **Cold Runner Systems**
   - **Two-Plate Mould** (single parting surface)
   - **Three-Plate Mould** (double parting surface — auto runner separation)
   - **Submarine / Tunnel Gate** systems
2. **Hot Runner Systems**
   - **Insulated Runner Mould**
   - **Heated Runner Mould**
     - Externally heated manifold
     - Internally heated manifold
3. **Hot Runner Nozzle Variants**
   - **Open Nozzle**
   - **Valve Gate Nozzle**
   - **Thermal Gate Nozzle**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - **No. of cavities (n):** Determines whether runner system must be balanced or naturally balanced.
> - **Shear rate limit (γ̇):** Typically **10,000 – 50,000 s⁻¹** for most thermoplastics to avoid degradation.
> - **Pressure drop budget:** Total runner pressure drop should be **< 10 – 15%** of injection pressure for stable filling.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of Cold Runner Moulds

The cold runner operates on a **batch-mode heat exchange** principle. The melt flowing through the runner loses heat to the surrounding cold mould steel. The design challenge is to make the runner **freeze last** (so the cavity fills before the runner freezes), but also **freeze soon after** filling (so the mould can be opened).

### Step-by-Step Logic of a Cold Runner Cycle:

1. **Injection Phase** — Molten polymer is injected from the machine nozzle into the **sprue bushing**, then flows through the **runner** and finally through the **gate** into the cavity.
2. **Packing/Holding Phase** — Additional melt is packed to compensate for shrinkage. The runner must still be **molten** during this phase.
3. **Cooling Phase** — The part and runner solidify. Heat is extracted through the mould cooling channels.
4. **Ejection Phase** — The mould opens, and the **runner + part** are ejected as a single piece (in two-plate moulds) or separated by a stripper plate (in three-plate moulds).
5. **Recycle Phase** — The runner is granulated and fed back into the hopper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Three-Plate" Cold Runners Exist:** In a two-plate mould, the runner must be cut off manually. In a three-plate mould, the runner is **automatically separated** from the parts when the mould opens along the second parting line. This is essential for high-volume production.

---

## 2.2 Operational Principle of Hot Runner Moulds

The hot runner operates on a **continuous-flow heat-maintenance** principle. The runner channels are embedded inside a **heated manifold** that maintains the polymer in a molten state at all times.

### Step-by-Step Logic of a Hot Runner Cycle:

1. **Melt enters** the machine nozzle → passes through the **manifold** (kept hot by cartridge heaters).
2. **Manifold → Nozzle Tip** — the melt is distributed to each cavity via **drop** or **hot tip** nozzles.
3. **Gate** — melt enters the cavity through a small orifice (typically 0.5 mm – 2.0 mm diameter for pin gates).
4. **Cavity fills**; part cools in the cavity (the cavity itself is cold — only the **runner** is hot).
5. **Mould opens**; only the **part** is ejected. No runner is ejected.
6. **Cycle repeats** — the manifold retains molten polymer ready for the next shot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Two Temperature Zones" Concept:**
> - **Hot Zone** = Manifold + Nozzle + Drop (kept above Tm)
> - **Cold Zone** = Cavity + Core (kept below Ts to freeze the part)
> - This **temperature gradient at the gate** is the heart of hot runner design. The gate tip must transfer enough heat out to **freeze** the gate at the right moment, preventing drool.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma\dot{} = \dfrac{32 \, Q}{\pi \, D^{3}}$ | Volumetric shear rate in a **circular runner** (Newtonian approximation) | s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, D^{4}}$ | **Hagen-Poiseuille pressure drop** in a circular runner (Newtonian fluid) | Pa |
| 3 | $D_{r} = \left( \dfrac{W_{s}}{C \, n} \right)^{1/3}$ | Empirical **runner diameter** from shot weight $W_{s}$, # cavities $n$, and a material constant $C$ | mm |
| 4 | $\dfrac{L_{1}}{D_{1}^{4}} = \dfrac{L_{2}}{D_{2}^{4}} = \dfrac{L_{3}}{D_{3}^{4}}$ | **Balanced runner rule** — equal pressure drop per branch in a multi-cavity mould | – |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{s}}{t_{inj}}$ | Volumetric flow rate $Q$ from shot volume $V_{s}$ and injection time $t_{inj}$ | mm³/s |
| 6 | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D}{4 L}$ | **Wall shear stress** in a fully developed circular flow | Pa |
| 7 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \, \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{m}-T_{w}}{T_{e}-T_{w}}\right)$ | Simplified **cooling time** (for amorphous polymers, where $s$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $T_m$ = melt temp, $T_w$ = mould wall temp, $T_e$ = ejection temp) | s |
| 8 | $P_{t} = P_{g} + P_{r} + P_{c}$ | **Total pressure** = gate pressure + runner pressure + cavity pressure | Pa |
| 9 | $V_{cavity} = \dfrac{W_{s}}{\rho}$ | Cavity volume from shot weight and polymer density | mm³ |
| 10 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number — used to check whether flow in the runner is **laminar** ($N_{Re} < 2300$) | – |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall — Use `\vert` not `\vert`-pipe in tables:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value inside any markdown row, **always** use the LaTeX command `\vert x \vert` instead of the keyboard pipe character to avoid breaking the table layout. Examiners deduct marks for unreadable tables.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis — Why Choose One Over the Other?

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Hot runners are **standard for high-volume automotive, packaging, and medical moulds** (typically > 500,000 shots/year). Cold runners dominate in **prototype moulds, low-volume production, and abrasive/filled polymers** (e.g., glass-filled nylon) where the hot runner's narrow passages would erode.

| Parameter | Cold Runner | Hot Runner |
|---|---|---|
| **Runner Waste** | Yes (15–30% of shot) | None (0%) |
| **Cycle Time** | Longer (runner cools too) | Shorter (no runner cooling) |
| **Initial Mould Cost** | Low | High (2×–4× the cost) |
| **Maintenance** | Simple | Complex (heaters, thermocouples, leak risk) |
| **Material Suitability** | All polymers | Excludes PVC, TPU, some fluropolymers (corrosive to heaters) |
| **Colour Change** | Easy (runner is ejected) | Difficult (polymer trapped in manifold) |
| **Gate Quality** | Visible gate vestige | No gate mark (especially valve gate) |
| **Best For** | Short runs, prototypes, abrasive materials | High-volume, cosmetic parts |
| **Pressure Drop** | Higher | Lower (no cold slug) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Lower | Higher per cycle (despite heaters) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop in a Circular Cold Runner

This is the **most-tested derivation** for Module 1 in KTU university exams. The starting point is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of an incompressible Newtonian fluid in a circular pipe.

### Step 1 — Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element

Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$ inside a runner of diameter $D$. The pressure $P$ acts on one face, $(P + dP)$ on the other. The shear stress $\tau$ acts on the lateral surface.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pressure force} &= \pi r^{2} \, (P) - \pi r^{2} \, (P + dP) \\
\text{Shear force on surface} &= \tau \, (2 \pi r L)
\end{aligned}
$$

At steady state, the pressure force equals the shear force (Newton's second law with zero acceleration):

$$
\pi r^{2} \, (-dP) = \tau \, (2 \pi r L)
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{dv}{dr}$ (negative because velocity decreases as $r$ increases):

$$
\pi r^{2} \, (-dP) = -\mu \dfrac{dv}{dr} \, (2 \pi r L)
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate with Boundary Conditions

Integrate from $r = 0$ (centerline, max velocity $v_{max}$) to $r = R$ (wall, no-slip $v = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} (-dP) &= \int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{-2 \mu L}{r} \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dr} \, dr \\[4pt]
\Delta P &= \dfrac{4 \mu L \, v_{max}}{R^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Volumetric Flow Rate $Q$

The parabolic velocity profile gives the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, D^{4}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Pressure drop is **inversely proportional to $D^{4}$**. Doubling the runner diameter reduces the pressure drop by a factor of **16**. This is why larger runners are preferred for high-viscosity polymers like PC and POM.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Balanced Runner Sizing for Multi-Cavity Mould

A **balanced runner system** delivers equal pressure to each cavity at the same instant. The principle: **equal pressure drop per branch**.

### Step 1 — Number of Cavities and Branch Length

Suppose a mould has $n = 4$ cavities arranged in a symmetric layout. The primary runner has length $L_{p}$. Each secondary branch has length $L_{s,i}$. The gate has length $L_{g}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For cavity 1:} \quad \Delta P_{1} &= \dfrac{128 \mu Q_{1} L_{p}}{\pi D_{p}^{4}} + \dfrac{128 \mu Q_{1} L_{s,1}}{\pi D_{s,1}^{4}} + \dfrac{128 \mu Q_{1} L_{g}}{\pi D_{g}^{4}} \\[4pt]
\text{For cavity 2:} \quad \Delta P_{2} &= \dfrac{128 \mu Q_{2} L_{p}}{\pi D_{p}^{4}} + \dfrac{128 \mu Q_{2} L_{s,2}}{\pi D_{s,2}^{4}} + \dfrac{128 \mu Q_{2} L_{g}}{\pi D_{g}^{4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Equal Flow per Cavity

For a balanced system, $Q_{1} = Q_{2} = \cdots = Q_{n} = Q/n$. For symmetry, $L_{s,1} = L_{s,2}$ implies:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{L_{1}}{D_{1}^{4}} \;=\; \dfrac{L_{2}}{D_{2}^{4}} \;=\; \dfrac{L_{3}}{D_{3}^{4}}\;}
$$

This is the **Golden Rule of Balanced Runner Design** in KTU exam answers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shear Rate at the Runner Wall

This derivation is asked frequently as a **3-mark sub-question**.

### Step 1 — Start from the Velocity Profile

The parabolic velocity profile in a fully-developed pipe flow is:

$$
v(r) = v_{max}\left(1 - \dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate to Get Shear Rate

$$
\gamma\dot{}(r) = -\dfrac{dv}{dr} = \dfrac{2 v_{max} \, r}{R^{2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate at the Wall ($r = R$)

$$
\gamma\dot{}_{w} = \dfrac{2 v_{max}}{R} = \dfrac{4 v_{avg}}{R}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $v_{avg} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$

$$
\gamma\dot{}_{w} = \dfrac{4 \cdot \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D^{2}}}{D/2} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\gamma\dot{}_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{32 \, Q}{\pi \, D^{3}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Limitation:** For most polymers, $\gamma\dot{}_{w}$ must remain **below 50,000 s⁻¹** to avoid shear degradation. This sets a **minimum runner diameter** for any given shot size and injection time.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Logic for Hot vs Cold Runner

| Mould Design Requirement | Recommended System | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Part weight < 5 g, prototype only | **Cold Runner (3-plate)** | Low cost, easy to modify |
| Annual production > 500,000 parts | **Hot Runner (valve gate)** | No runner waste = high material savings |
| Glass-filled nylon (abrasive) | **Cold Runner** | Heated manifold would erode |
| Multi-cavity (> 8 cavities), tight tolerance | **Hot Runner (balanced manifold)** | Equal fill, low flash, low clamp tonnage |
| PVC (corrosive to copper heaters) | **Cold Runner (stainless steel)** | Avoids corrosion |
| Cosmetic part, no gate vestige | **Hot Runner (valve gate)** | Valve pin retracts after fill |
| Medical part, zero contamination | **Hot Runner** | No reground material |
