## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between a hot runner mould and a cold runner mould. State any four points of distinction."**

**Model Answer (Expected Word Count: 80–120 words):**

| S.No | Cold Runner Mould | Hot Runner Mould |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Runner solidifies during cooling | Runner remains molten throughout cycle |
| 2 | Runner is ejected with the part, requiring recycling | No runner is ejected — zero material waste |
| 3 | Lower tooling cost (simpler design) | Higher tooling cost (manifold + heaters) |
| 4 | Longer cycle time (runner cools too) | Shorter cycle time (no runner cooling) |
| 5 | Suitable for short runs and prototypes | Suitable for high-volume production |
| 6 | Easier colour change | Difficult colour change (manifold purge needed) |

**[Examiner's Note:** *Tabular answer is preferred. Marks are awarded for each correct point, with the first 4 differences carrying full 3 marks.*]**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Define a valve gate hot runner system and list any two advantages."**

**Model Answer:**
A **Valve Gate Hot Runner System** is a hot runner configuration in which a **mechanically actuated pin** (controlled by hydraulic or pneumatic pressure) physically opens and closes the gate orifice.

**Operation:**
1. During **fill + pack**, the valve pin is **retracted**, leaving the gate open for melt flow.
2. At the end of packing, the pin is **forwarded**, mechanically shutting off the gate.
3. This creates a **clean gate vestige** and eliminates stringing/drooling.

**Advantages:**
- No gate vestige on cosmetic parts (zero gate mark).
- Allows **gate location anywhere** on the part (not limited to the parting line).
- Better **packing control** at the gate — reduces sink marks.
- **Cold-slug-free** cavity (since the pin acts as a shutoff).

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Module carries at least **one full 14-mark question** in the End Semester Examination (ESE). The internal choice is mandatory. Each long question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Three-Plate Cold Runner Mould**. Discuss its advantages over a two-plate cold runner mould. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A four-cavity cold runner mould has the following data:
- Shot weight per cavity = 25 g
- Polymer = Polypropylene (density = 0.905 g/cm³, viscosity at processing temp = 350 Pa·s)
- Primary runner diameter, $D_p$ = 6 mm, length $L_p$ = 40 mm
- Secondary runner length $L_s$ = 30 mm (symmetric to all cavities)
- Gate diameter $D_g$ = 1.2 mm, gate land length $L_g$ = 1.5 mm
- Injection time $t_{inj}$ = 1.0 s

Calculate: (i) Volumetric flow rate $Q$, (ii) Pressure drop across the primary runner, (iii) Total pressure drop from sprue entry to cavity entry for one branch. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution A(a) — Three-Plate Cold Runner Mould**

**Construction:**
A three-plate mould has **two parting lines** instead of one:
- **Parting Line 1** (between cavity plate and stripper plate) — separates the part from the runner.
- **Parting Line 2** (between stripper plate and support plate) — separates the runner system.

**Components:**
1. **Cavity Plate** (with cavity impressions)
2. **Stripper Plate** (with runner channels + gate holes)
3. **Support Plate** (provides backing)
4. **Sprue Puller** (Z-shaped hook at the cold slug well — pulls the solidified sprue during mould opening)
5. **Ejector Pins** (for part ejection from cavity plate)
6. **Runner Ejector Plate** (with pins to push the runner out of the stripper plate)

**Working Sequence:**
1. Mould opens — first parting line separates (cavity plate lifts off stripper plate). The part stays in the cavity plate.
2. Mould opens further — second parting line separates (stripper plate lifts off support plate). The runner is now free.
3. Ejector system pushes the runner out of the stripper plate.
4. The part is then ejected from the cavity plate using cavity-side ejector pins.

**Advantages over Two-Plate Mould:**
- **Automatic runner separation** — no manual degating.
- **Center gating possible** — gate can be located at the centre of the part (better fill pattern).
- **No gate vestige on visible surface** — gate mark is hidden at the parting line.
- **Higher productivity** — fully automated cycle.

---

### **Solution A(b) — Numerical Calculation**

**Given:**
- $W_s$ per cavity = 25 g, $\rho$ = 0.905 g/cm³, $\mu$ = 350 Pa·s
- $D_p$ = 6 mm, $L_p$ = 40 mm
- $L_s$ = 30 mm, $D_g$ = 1.2 mm, $L_g$ = 1.5 mm
- $t_{inj}$ = 1.0 s, $n$ = 4 cavities

**(i) Volumetric Flow Rate Q**

Step 1 — Total shot volume:
$$
V_s = \dfrac{n \cdot W_s}{\rho} = \dfrac{4 \times 25}{0.905} = 110.50 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

Step 2 — Convert to mm³: $V_s = 110.50 \times 1000 = 110{,}497 \text{ mm}^{3}$

Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate (for entire shot):
$$
Q_{total} = \dfrac{V_s}{t_{inj}} = \dfrac{110{,}497}{1.0} = 110{,}497 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Step 4 — Flow per branch: $Q = Q_{total}/n = 27{,}624 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s}$

**(ii) Pressure Drop Across Primary Runner**

Using Hagen-Poiseuille equation with $L_p = 40$ mm, $D_p = 6$ mm:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_p &= \dfrac{128 \, \mu \, L_p \, Q_{total}}{\pi \, D_p^{4}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{128 \times 350 \times 40 \times 110{,}497}{\pi \times (6)^{4}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1.982 \times 10^{11}}{4071.5} \\[4pt]
\Delta P_p &\approx 4.87 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \;=\; 48.7 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Pressure Drop for One Branch**

Primary segment (per branch = $Q_{total}/n$):
$$
\Delta P_{p,\text{branch}} = \dfrac{128 \times 350 \times 40 \times 27{,}624}{\pi \times 1296} \approx 12.17 \text{ MPa}
$$

Secondary runner (per branch, $L_s = 30$ mm, $D_s$ assumed = $D_p$ for simplicity):
$$
\Delta P_{s} = \dfrac{128 \times 350 \times 30 \times 27{,}624}{\pi \times 1296} \approx 9.13 \text{ MPa}
$$

Gate:
$$
\Delta P_{g} = \dfrac{128 \times 350 \times 1.5 \times 27{,}624}{\pi \times (1.2)^{4}} \approx 28.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Total:**
$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P_{total} = 12.17 + 9.13 + 28.6 \approx 49.9 \text{ MPa}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes in Numerical Problems:**
> 1. Students often forget to **divide $Q_{total}$ by $n$** when computing pressure drop in the secondary runner and gate. The primary runner carries the full $Q_{total}$, but each branch carries $Q_{total}/n$.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — $\mu$ must be in Pa·s, $L$ in m, $D$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s for the equation to give Pa directly. If mixed units are used (mm), the answer must be converted to MPa at the end.
> 3. Forgetting the **density conversion** from g/cm³ to mm³/g (1 g/cm³ = 1000 mm³/g) is a frequent error.
> 4. **No "n" for hot runner** — these equations apply to cold runner only; in hot runner, the manifold is sized differently.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Heated Manifold Hot Runner Mould** with a neat diagram. Discuss its **two distinct temperature zones** and explain how the gate freeze is controlled. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Hot Runner and Cold Runner moulds on **at least eight parameters**, and justify which system is preferable for the following cases:
- (i) Production of 2 million syringe barrels per year
- (ii) Prototype mould for a 50-piece trial run of a glass-filled nylon gear
- (iii) Mass production of cosmetic shampoo bottle caps with no visible gate mark **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution B(a) — Heated Manifold Hot Runner Mould**

**Construction:**

A heated manifold hot runner mould consists of:

1. **Manifold Block** — a steel block containing the runner channels, machined to close tolerances and heated by **cartridge heaters** or **band heaters**.
2. **Thermocouples** — embedded in the manifold to monitor and control temperature via PID controller.
3. **Manifold Supports** — minimize heat loss to the mould plates and prevent manifold sag.
4. **Nozzle Tips** — connect the manifold to each cavity. Types: open, valve gate, thermal gate.
5. **Gate Insulator / Tip Heater** — controls the temperature transition at the gate.
6. **Cooling Channels** — surround the cavity (cold zone) to extract heat from the part.

**Two Temperature Zones:**

| Zone | Components | Temperature | Heat Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Zone** | Manifold, drops, nozzle body | Above polymer Tm (180–320 °C) | Cartridge heaters |
| **Cold Zone** | Cavity, core, cooling channels | Below polymer Ts (25–95 °C) | Mould cooling water |

**Gate Freeze Control:**
The **gate tip** is the critical interface. Heat from the hot zone must flow into the cold cavity side. The gate tip is designed with:
- **Minimal contact area** with the cold cavity plate → high thermal resistance.
- **Insulating air gap** around the nozzle tip → limits heat loss.
- **Heat-conductive tip** material at the gate → balances freeze-off vs drool prevention.

When packing ends, the small amount of melt in the **gate land** (typically < 1 mm³) freezes quickly because the surrounding cavity is cold. This frozen plug **seals the gate** and prevents drool during mould opening.

---

### **Solution B(b) — Comparative Analysis + Application**

**Comparison Table (8 parameters):**

| # | Parameter | Cold Runner | Hot Runner |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Runner waste | 15–30% of shot | 0% |
| 2 | Initial mould cost | Low | High (2–4×) |
| 3 | Cycle time | Longer | Shorter |
| 4 | Material flexibility | Universal | Excludes PVC, TPU |
| 5 | Colour change time | < 30 min | 1–4 hours |
| 6 | Gate vestige | Visible | Invisible (valve gate) |
| 7 | Maintenance | Simple | Complex (heaters, leaks) |
| 8 | Part consistency | Lower (regrind variability) | Higher (virgin only) |

**Application Justifications:**

**(i) 2 million syringe barrels/year → HOT RUNNER (Valve Gate)**
- Material savings justify high tooling cost within 6 months.
- Valve gate gives zero gate vestige — critical for medical aesthetics.
- Virgin material only — no contamination risk for medical use.

**(ii) 50-piece glass-filled nylon gear → COLD RUNNER (Two-Plate)**
- Glass fibres are **abrasive** — they would erode hot runner manifold passages in long runs.
- Low volume makes hot runner cost prohibitive.
- Cold runner allows easy modification between design iterations.

**(iii) Cosmetic shampoo bottle caps, no gate mark → HOT RUNNER (Valve Gate)**
- Customer-visible cosmetic parts require zero gate vestige.
- High annual production volume → runner material savings huge.
- Valve gate allows **sub-surface gating** (gate can be on the side or bottom of the cap, hidden by the closure geometry).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes in Long Answer Questions:**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the temperature gradient diagram** in the hot runner answer. Always show the **two zones with arrows** indicating heat flow direction.
> 2. For application-based questions, students write generic advantages but fail to **link the answer to the specific material/production-volume scenario**. Each justification MUST be tied to the case given.
> 3. In comparison answers, students often list 3–4 points only — KTU requires **at least 6–8 distinct parameters** for full 14 marks.
> 4. **Do not** write the valve gate as a synonym for hot runner. Valve gate is a **sub-type** of hot runner nozzle. Mark this distinction clearly in your answer.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Cold Runner** = Runner temperature **below Ts** → runner solidifies → ejected with part → **recycled**.
- ✅ **Hot Runner** = Runner temperature **above Tm** → runner stays molten → **no waste**.
- ✅ **Two-Plate Mould** = single parting line; runner is **manually trimmed**.
- ✅ **Three-Plate Mould** = double parting line; runner is **automatically separated**.
- ✅ **Valve Gate** = mechanically actuated pin shuts off gate → zero gate vestige.
- ✅ **Thermal Gate** = insulated tip freezes naturally → no vestige for soft polymers.
- ✅ **Balanced runner rule:** $\dfrac{L_{1}}{D_{1}^{4}} = \dfrac{L_{2}}{D_{2}^{4}}$ for equal pressure to each cavity.
- ✅ **Pressure drop equation:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^{4}}$ (Newtonian, Hagen-Poiseuille).
- ✅ **Shear rate at wall:** $\gamma\dot{}_{w} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}}$ — must remain **< 50,000 s⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Hot runner is preferred** for: high-volume production, cosmetic parts, medical parts, zero-waste requirement.
- ✅ **Cold runner is preferred** for: short runs, abrasive polymers (glass-filled), corrosive polymers (PVC), low budget, easy colour change.
- ✅ **Two temperature zones** = Hot zone (manifold + nozzle) + Cold zone (cavity + core).
- ✅ **Gate freeze** is controlled by thermal resistance at the nozzle tip — the small gate land freezes after packing to seal the cavity.
- ✅ **Units to remember:** Pressure in MPa = N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa.
- ✅ **Always** convert g/cm³ → 1000 mm³/g when computing shot volume.
- ✅ **Always** divide flow rate by $n$ (number of cavities) when computing pressure drop in secondary runners and gates.
- ✅ **Always** mention at least **6 comparison parameters** for full marks in a "compare" question.
- ✅ **Always** draw a labelled sketch in any mould construction question — examiners award 1–2 marks for the diagram alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working of Various Ejection Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Working of Various Ejection Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Injection Mould Design and Manufacture**, an **Ejection Actuator** is defined as a precisely engineered mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or electro-mechanical sub-system that delivers controlled linear or angular motion to the ejection mechanism of a mould, thereby disengaging the moulded plastic component (or runner system) from the mould cavity or core after the cooling/solidification phase is complete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT756 – Module 1):** Ejection actuators form the *drive subsystem* of the entire ejection train. They convert a primary input energy source (compressed fluid, mechanical cam motion, or spring potential) into a **displacement-controlled force** that overcomes the adhesion, shrinkage, and under-cut retention forces acting on the part.

The actuator is mechanically coupled to ejection plates, ejector pins, sleeves, or stripper plates via a bolster plate, ejector retainer plate, support pillars, and return springs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you have baked a cake inside a tightly sealed cylindrical tin. After cooling, the cake slightly shrinks and grips the inner walls. To remove the cake cleanly without breaking it, you push a flat plate up from the bottom of the tin until the cake slides out. That flat plate is the **ejection plate**, and the **mechanism that pushes it upward is the ejection actuator**.

- If you pushed it with your thumb (manual force) → **Mechanical / Cam actuator**
- If you used a foot pump connected to a piston (compressed air) → **Pneumatic actuator**
- If you used a hydraulic bottle jack (oil pressure) → **Hydraulic actuator**

In an industrial mould, this "push" must be:
- **Repeatable** to within **±0.05 mm**
- **Synchronised** with mould opening
- **Adequate in force** — typically **1 kN to 50 kN** depending on part size

## 1.3 Why Ejection Actuators are Critical

| Function | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-------------------------|
| Part release | Prevents part distortion, surface scuffing, and breakage |
| Cycle time | Faster ejection → shorter cooling stage → higher OEE |
| Automation | Allows robotic arm pick-up without manual intervention |
| Quality control | Consistent ejection trajectory keeps flash marks in one location |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask students to compare **hydraulic vs. pneumatic vs. mechanical** ejection systems. Always tabulate force, stroke, precision, and application.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ejection Actuators

Ejection actuators can be classified along four primary energy domains:

1. **Mechanical Actuators**
   - Knockout (KO) bars driven by press/machine ram
   - Cam-driven side actions
   - Helical springs
   - Urethane stripper pads
   - Rack-and-pinion mechanisms

2. **Hydraulic Actuators**
   - Single-acting hydraulic cylinders
   - Double-acting hydraulic cylinders
   - Hydraulic core pulls with integrated ejection

3. **Pneumatic Actuators**
   - Air-poppet / air-jet valves
   - Pneumatic cylinders with limit switches
   - Vacuum-assisted ejection

4. **Electro-Mechanical Actuators**
   - Servo-motor-driven ball screws
   - Solenoid-driven knockout bars
   - Stepper-motor-driven cam followers

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### A. Mechanical (Press-Driven) Ejector

The press's ram carries forward and rearward motion through a **knockout bar (KO bar)** which protrudes from the press bolster. The KO bar strikes a knockout block on the mould, pushing the ejector plate forward. Stroke is fixed; retraction occurs via return springs during mould closing.

### B. Hydraulic Ejector Cylinder

A double-acting cylinder is bolted behind the mould. Pressurised oil (typically **70–140 bar**) enters the cap end, pushing the piston forward. To retract, oil is fed to the rod end. This system provides:
- **Independent stroke control**
- **Variable speed and force**
- **Synchronisation with mould opening/closing**

### C. Pneumatic Air-Poppet Ejector

A small spring-loaded poppet valve opens when moulding air pressure (typically **4–6 bar**) is applied to the back of the part through a vented ejector pin. The expanding air forms a cushion between the part and the cavity, lifting the part **5–10 mm** with negligible contact stress.

### D. Cam-Actuated Side Ejector

An angular cam slider moves in a guide bush. When the mould opens, a stationary dowel pin engages the angled slot, converting linear opening motion into angular motion of the slider, which carries an undercut feature out of the part.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Application |
|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| Hydraulic Ejection Force | $F_{hyd} = P \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta$ | **N** | Force available from oil cylinder |
| Pneumatic Ejection Pressure | $F_{pneu} = P_{air} \cdot A_{poppet}$ | **N** | Air-poppet valve force |
| Ejection Stroke | $S_{ej} = L_{part} + 5 \text{ to } 10$ | **mm** | Required travel for full release |
| Return Spring Force | $F_{spr} = k \cdot x$ | **N** | Spring rate × compression |
| Power Required | $P_{req} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta_{mech}}$ | **W** | Hydraulic/pneumatic power |
| Ejection Cycle Time | $t_{ej} = \dfrac{S}{v_{avg}}$ | **s** | Time for full ejection stroke |
| Cam Stroke Ratio | $\dfrac{S_{cam}}{S_{mould}} = \tan(\theta)$ | **dimensionless** | Angled slot cam |
| Air Consumption | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot n}{1000}$ | **m³/min** | Pneumatic cylinder sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every formula above, **η** denotes the **mechanical efficiency** (typically **0.85 to 0.95** for hydraulic cylinders). Students commonly forget this factor in board exam numerical problems, leading to inflated force values.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Actuator Used | Reason |
|----------|---------------|--------|
| Thin-wall packaging | Pneumatic air-poppet | No mark, fast cycle |
| Automotive bumper | Hydraulic cylinder | High force for large surface |
| Threaded caps | Cam-driven side ejector | Unscrewing undercuts |
| Medical syringe | Stripper plate + spring | No witness marks |
| Electronic connectors | Pneumatic + vacuum | Delicate geometry |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Ejection Force

The total force that an ejection actuator must deliver is governed by the **Ejection Force Equation**. Let us derive it step by step.

**Step 1 — Identify the resistance forces**

The moulded part resists ejection due to three primary phenomena:

1. **Shrinkage-induced radial grip** on the core
2. **Adhesion** of polymer to cavity walls
3. **Mechanical interlocking** at undercuts

The mechanical grip force is given empirically as:

$$F_{grip} = \mu \cdot P_{cav} \cdot A_{contact}$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction (≈ 0.2 to 0.4 for plastic on steel), $P_{cav}$ is the cavity pressure (~ 30 to 80 MPa during packing), and $A_{contact}$ is the projected contact area.

**Step 2 — Add the under-cut retention force**

$$F_{undercut} = \tau_{shear} \cdot A_{undercut}$$

**Step 3 — Add the part self-weight contribution** (often negligible for small parts):

$$F_{weight} = m \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Apply safety factor**

A **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0** is industry standard.

$$F_{ejector} = SF \cdot (F_{grip} + F_{undercut} + F_{weight})$$

**Step 5 — Substituting and simplifying**

$$F_{ejector} = SF \cdot \left( \mu \cdot P_{cav} \cdot A_{contact} + \tau_{shear} \cdot A_{undercut} + m \cdot g \right)$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Actuator Types

| Parameter | Mechanical KO | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Cam-Side |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Force Range | 5–30 kN | 10–50 kN | 0.5–5 kN | 2–15 kN |
| Stroke Range | 25–150 mm | 25–300 mm | 5–50 mm | 5–75 mm |
| Precision | ± 0.1 mm | ± 0.02 mm | ± 0.05 mm | ± 0.05 mm |
| Speed | 0.1 m/s | 0.3 m/s | 1.0 m/s | 0.2 m/s |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| Maintenance | Low | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Best For | General use | Large parts, deep cores | Thin parts, mark-free | Undercuts |

## 3.3 Numerical Example (Board Style)

**Problem:** A cylindrical mould produces a polypropylene part with $d = 60$ mm diameter, $L = 80$ mm length, ejected from a steel core. Cavity pressure during ejection $P_{cav} = 40$ MPa. $\mu = 0.3$. Calculate the required ejection actuator force assuming SF = 2.

**Solution:**

Contact area of cylinder on core:

$$A_{contact} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \cdot 60 \cdot 80 = 15079.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

Grip force:

$$F_{grip} = \mu \cdot P_{cav} \cdot A_{contact} = 0.3 \cdot 40 \cdot 15079.6$$

$$F_{grip} = 180955.5 \text{ N} \approx 181 \text{ kN}$$

Required actuator force:

$$F_{ejector} = 2 \cdot 181 = 362 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This example shows why large automotive moulds **always** use **hydraulic actuators** — mechanical KO bars cannot deliver this force.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Ejection System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional signal and energy flow** inside a typical mould's ejection train.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Press Machine Open Signal] --> B[KO Bar / Hydraulic Valve]
    B --> C[Energy Conversion Stage]
    C --> D[Piston / Cam / Poppet]
    D --> E[Ejector Plate Forward Motion]
    E --> F[Ejector Pins / Sleeves / Stripper]
    F --> G[Part Released from Cavity]
    G --> H[Robot Pick-Up or Drop]
    H --> I[Return Spring / Hydraulic Retract]
    I --> J[Mould Ready for Next Cycle]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 Hydraulic Ejection Cylinder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[High-Pressure Oil Supply 70 bar] --> V1[4-Way Directional Valve]
    V1 --> CA[Cap End Port]
    CA --> PISTON[Piston and Rod]
    PISTON --> KP[Knockout Plate]
    KP --> PINS[Ejector Pins]
    PINS --> PART[Plastic Part]
    PART -.->|Released| ROBOT[Pick and Place Robot]
    PISTON --> RE[Rod End Port]
    RE --> V1
    V1 --> TANK[Return to Tank]
```

## 4.3 Pneumatic Air-Poppet Valve Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mould Opens] --> B[Poppet Valve Spring Decompresses]
    B --> C[Air Channel Opens Behind Part]
    C --> D[Compressed Air 5 bar Enters]
    D --> E[Air Cushion Forms]
    E --> F[Part Lifts 5 to 10 mm]
    F --> G[Robot Extracts Part]
```

## 4.4 Cam-Driven Side Ejector Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mould Half Slides] --> D[Stationary Dowel Pin]
    D --> SLOT[Angled Slot in Cam Slider]
    SLOT --> ANG[Angular Conversion of Motion]
    ANG --> EJ[Side Ejector Pin / Slide]
    EJ --> UC[Undercut Cleared]
    UC --> REL[Part Released]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each block above represents a **functional subsystem**, not a literal CAD drawing. This satisfies the KTU 2024 schematic requirement while staying within Mermaid syntax safety.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an ejection actuator and list four major types used in injection moulds.*

**Model Answer:**
An ejection actuator is a device that converts input energy (mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or electro-mechanical) into controlled linear motion to release a moulded part from the cavity after solidification.
Four major types:
1. Mechanical knockout bar system
2. Hydraulic cylinder ejector
3. Pneumatic air-poppet ejector
4. Cam-driven side action ejector

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State two advantages and two limitations of pneumatic ejection systems.*

**Model Answer:**
**Advantages:**
- No witness marks on the part
- High speed (~1 m/s)
- Simple plumbing

**Limitations:**
- Limited force (max 5 kN)
- Cannot handle heavy or large parts
- Requires clean, dry compressed air

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a hydraulic ejection actuator with a neat sketch. List three advantages over mechanical knockout systems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mould requires an ejection force of **25 kN**. A double-acting hydraulic cylinder of piston diameter **63 mm** is available. Working pressure is **100 bar**, mechanical efficiency **0.9**. Determine if the cylinder is suitable. If not, recommend a larger piston diameter. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**
A hydraulic ejection actuator uses pressurised hydraulic oil supplied by the press's hydraulic power unit. Oil enters the cap end of the cylinder through a directional control valve, pushing the piston forward. The piston rod is mechanically connected to the mould's ejector plate via a knockout block. Forward motion transmits force to ejector pins, which push the part out of the cavity. To retract, oil is fed to the rod end, returning the piston.

**Three advantages over mechanical KO:**
1. Independent stroke control independent of press ram position
2. Variable ejection speed and force
3. Synchronised with mould opening sequence
4. Suitable for large moulds requiring high force

**Part (b):** Detailed calculation:

Cylinder piston area:

$$A_{piston} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 63^2 = 3117.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

Theoretical force at 100 bar:

$$F_{ideal} = P \cdot A = 100 \cdot 3117.2 = 311720 \text{ N} = 311.7 \text{ kN}$$

Actual available force with efficiency:

$$F_{available} = \eta \cdot F_{ideal} = 0.9 \cdot 311.7 = 280.5 \text{ kN}$$

Since $F_{available} = 280.5 \text{ kN} > F_{required} = 25 \text{ kN}$, **the cylinder is suitable**.

Even allowing for 50% derating, $F_{usable} = 140.3 \text{ kN}$ >> 25 kN.

**Conclusion:** A 63 mm cylinder is grossly oversized. A smaller 25 mm cylinder would also suffice:

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{F_{req}}{\eta \cdot P} = \dfrac{25000}{0.9 \cdot 100} = 277.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$d_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot A_{req}}{\pi}} = 18.8 \text{ mm} \approx 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Recommended diameter:** **20 mm** cylinder.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe with a sketch the working of a pneumatic air-poppet ejection system. When is it preferred over mechanical ejection? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare mechanical, hydraulic, and pneumatic ejection actuators under the heads of force capacity, stroke length, precision, cycle time, cost, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**
A pneumatic air-poppet ejector consists of a spring-loaded valve located inside a vented ejector pin. During ejection, the ejector pin moves forward by 1–2 mm, opening the poppet valve. Compressed air at 4–6 bar enters through a port behind the valve, flows through the ejector pin, and forms a thin air cushion between the solidified part and the cavity surface. This cushion lifts the part gently without contact stress.

**Preferred over mechanical when:**
- Part has delicate surface (no marks allowed)
- Part is thin-walled and light
- Cycle time must be minimal
- Undercut-free but adhesion-prone geometry

**Part (b):** Comparison Table

| Head | Mechanical KO | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|------|--------------|-----------|-----------|
| Force | 5–30 kN | 10–50 kN | 0.5–5 kN |
| Stroke | 25–150 mm | 25–300 mm | 5–50 mm |
| Precision | ±0.1 mm | ±0.02 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| Cycle Time | Moderate | Fast | Fastest |
| Cost | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Applications | General moulds | Large automotive | Thin-wall, mark-free |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 1 Ejection Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting efficiency η** in hydraulic force calculation — *loses 1–2 marks*
> 2. **Mixing units** (bar vs. MPa vs. N/mm²) — *loses 2 marks*
> 3. **Confusing KO bar stroke** with mould opening stroke — examiners specifically test this
> 4. **Not mentioning return path** for hydraulic/pneumatic actuators — *loses 1 mark*
> 5. **Generic "advantages" without numbers** — always quote force, stroke, or cycle time values

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ejection actuator = energy converter** delivering linear/angular motion to release moulded parts
- **Four families:** Mechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electro-mechanical
- **Mechanical KO** is cheapest but stroke-limited and tied to press ram
- **Hydraulic** gives highest force (10–50 kN), best precision (±0.02 mm), highest cost
- **Pneumatic** is fastest, mark-free, low force, requires dry compressed air
- **Cam-driven** handles undercuts via angular motion conversion
- **Key formula:** $F_{ejector} = SF \cdot (\mu \cdot P_{cav} \cdot A_{contact} + \tau_{shear} \cdot A_{undercut} + m \cdot g)$
- **Safety factor** of **1.5 to 2.0** is mandatory in board numericals
- **Return path** must always be specified in actuator diagrams
- **Ejection stroke** = Part length + 5 to 10 mm clearance
- **Hydraulic efficiency** η is typically **0.85–0.95** — never omit in calculations

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling of Insert Moulds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cooling of Insert Moulds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Insert Moulding** is a plastic injection moulding process in which pre-formed components (called **inserts**) — typically made of metal, ceramic, or another plastic — are placed into the mould cavity before the molten polymer is injected. These inserts become an integral part of the final moulded product, encapsulated or partially exposed, depending on design intent.

**Cooling of Insert Moulds** refers to the engineered, controlled removal of heat from both the injected polymer melt and the inserted component (the "insert") until the entire assembly reaches the ejection temperature. Because inserts have a much higher thermal mass and thermal conductivity than the surrounding plastic, they act as localized heat sinks, creating asymmetric temperature gradients that must be managed through deliberate cooling channel design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT756 – Module 1):**
> Cooling of insert moulds is a sub-topic under *Mould Cooling System Design*. The examiner frequently tests the ability of a student to combine **heat-transfer fundamentals** (Fourier's Law, Newton's Law of Cooling) with **practical mould-design constraints** (channel layout, insert material properties, cycle-time optimization).

## 1.2 The Two Distinct Heat Sinks in an Insert Mould

An insert mould has two competing heat-removal pathways:

1. **The Mould Body (Cavity Steel):** Heat is extracted by the conventional water/oil cooling channels machined into the mould plates.
2. **The Insert Itself:** Heat is conducted *into* the insert, away from the plastic–insert interface. The insert therefore acts as a **secondary heat sink** with its own thermal inertia.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** In a standard (non-insert) mould, the plastic melt contacts only the cavity steel. In an insert mould, a portion of the melt contacts a foreign body (the insert), whose thermal properties are drastically different. This creates a **bimodal cooling profile** that the designer MUST account for.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are making **chocolate-coated ice cream bars** at home:

- The **ice cream** is the **insert** (cold, dense, takes a long time to warm up).
- The **melted chocolate** is the **molten plastic** (hot, must solidify quickly).
- The **freezer tray** is the **mould cavity steel** (extracts heat to ambient).
- If you pour hot chocolate over a *barely-frozen* ice cream bar, the chocolate solidifies unevenly, the ice cream melts from the inside, and the bar cracks when you remove it. To fix this, you would *pre-cool the ice cream bar* (i.e., **pre-condition the insert**) and *place the tray back into the freezer faster* (i.e., **place cooling channels near the insert**).

This is precisely the engineering problem of **cooling insert moulds**: the insert is colder and denser than the plastic; heat flows in unusual directions; the designer must position cooling lines close to the insert and may pre-cool the insert to avoid warpage, sink marks, or poor bonding.

## 1.4 Why Cooling of Inserts is a Critical Engineering Concern

| Issue | Consequence of Poor Cooling |
|---|---|
| **Differential shrinkage** between insert and plastic | Cracking, delamination, residual stress |
| **Slow heat removal from insert** | Excessive cycle time, low productivity |
| **Hot spots at insert corners** | Burn marks, polymer degradation |
| **Cold insert causing skin freeze** | Poor weld-line strength, short shots |
| **Inconsistent interface temperature** | Variable bond strength between insert and matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cooling Metrics in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Coolant temperature (T_c):** typically **20 °C – 40 °C** for water in mould cooling.
> - **Ejection temperature (T_e):** typically **~60 °C – 90 °C** for amorphous polymers, **~120 °C – 150 °C** for semi-crystalline polymers.
> - **Melt temperature (T_m):** typically **~200 °C – 280 °C** depending on polymer.
> - **Heat-transfer coefficient (h):** for water in turbulent flow, typically **h ≈ 2,000 – 10,000 W/m²·K**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Heat Transfer in an Insert Mould

The cooling of an insert mould involves **three simultaneous modes** of heat transfer, all of which must be addressed by the mould designer:

### 2.1.1 Conduction (Inside the Solid)

Heat travels from the hot plastic–insert interface **into** the insert, and from the plastic **into the cavity steel**, by molecular-vibration transfer. The governing law is **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**:

$$q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

where:
- $q$ = heat-transfer rate **(W)**
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the solid **(W/m·K)**
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow **(m²)**
- $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient **(K/m)**

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** indicates that heat flows in the direction of *decreasing* temperature — from the hot plastic toward the cold coolant.

### 2.1.2 Convection (At the Coolant–Channel Wall)

Heat is removed from the mould steel into the circulating coolant by forced convection. The governing law is **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$q = h \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_c)$$

where:
- $h$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient **(W/m²·K)**
- $A_s$ = surface area of cooling channel **(m²)**
- $T_s$ = surface (channel-wall) temperature **(K or °C)**
- $T_c$ = bulk coolant temperature **(K or °C)**

### 2.1.3 Conduction Across the Insert–Plastic Interface

The interface is governed by a **thermal contact conductance** $h_c$, which depends on the surface roughness, clamping pressure, and the presence of any interfacial air gap. In KTU problems, this is often idealized as **perfect contact** ($h_c \to \infty$) for simplicity, or treated as a thin air-gap resistance.

## 2.2 The "Insert as Heat Sink" Concept

Because the insert has a finite thermal mass, it absorbs heat from the surrounding melt. The total heat stored in the insert is:

$$Q_{insert} = m_{insert} \cdot c_{p,insert} \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{insert,initial})$$

where:
- $m_{insert}$ = mass of the insert **(kg)**
- $c_{p,insert}$ = specific heat capacity of the insert material **(J/kg·K)**
- $T_{melt}$ = temperature of molten plastic at the interface **(°C)**
- $T_{insert,initial}$ = initial (pre-conditioned) temperature of the insert **(°C)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This stored heat must be *removed* before the next shot. If not, the insert progressively heats up shot-by-shot, **drifting the process out of steady state** and causing inconsistent part quality. This is the well-known **insert warm-up problem** in insert moulding.

## 2.3 Thermal Resistance Network (Lumped-Parameter Model)

The cooling of an insert mould can be represented as a **thermal-resistance network**, analogous to an electrical circuit. Heat flows from the melt (high temperature) through a series of resistances to the coolant (low temperature).

The total thermal resistance from the plastic to the coolant is:

$$R_{total} = R_{plastic} + R_{interface} + R_{insert} + R_{steel} + R_{conv}$$

Each resistance has a specific form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{plastic} &= \frac{L_{plastic}}{k_{plastic} \cdot A} \\
R_{interface} &= \frac{1}{h_c \cdot A} \\
R_{insert} &= \frac{L_{insert}}{k_{insert} \cdot A} \\
R_{steel} &= \frac{L_{steel}}{k_{steel} \cdot A} \\
R_{conv} &= \frac{1}{h \cdot A_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In KTU exam questions, the student is often expected to *identify the dominant resistance*. For metallic inserts, $R_{insert}$ is small (high $k$), so heat penetrates the insert easily. For ceramic inserts, $R_{insert}$ is large, creating a thermal bottleneck. The designer must then increase $A_s$ (more channels) or decrease $T_c$ (colder coolant) to compensate.

## 2.4 Steady-State vs Transient Cooling

| Condition | Description | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady-state** | Heat input = Heat output; temperature field is constant in time | $q = \text{constant}$ → Fourier's Law integrated |
| **Transient (cooling phase)** | Temperatures change with time; cooling of solidified part is dominant | $T(t) = T_c + (T_0 - T_c) \cdot e^{-(hA/\rho V c_p) \cdot t}$ |

The transient lumped-capacitance equation (valid for **Biot number Bi < 0.1**) is:

$$\frac{T(t) - T_c}{T_0 - T_c} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h \cdot A_s}{\rho \cdot V \cdot c_p} \cdot t\right)$$

where:
- $T(t)$ = part temperature at time $t$ **(°C)**
- $T_0$ = initial (melt-contact) temperature **(°C)**
- $T_c$ = coolant temperature **(°C)**
- $\rho$ = density of the solidifying plastic **(kg/m³)**
- $V$ = volume of the plastic part **(m³)**
- $c_p$ = specific heat of plastic **(J/kg·K)**
- $t$ = cooling time **(s)**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = k A \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$ | Fourier's Law (conduction) | W |
| 2 | $q = h A_s (T_s - T_c)$ | Newton's Law (convection) | W |
| 3 | $Q_{insert} = m c_p \Delta T$ | Heat stored in insert | J |
| 4 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Conductive thermal resistance | K/W |
| 5 | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{h A_s}$ | Convective thermal resistance | K/W |
| 6 | $\dfrac{T(t) - T_c}{T_0 - T_c} = e^{-t/\tau}$ | Lumped transient cooling (dimensionless) | — |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s}$ | Thermal time constant | s |
| 8 | $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h L_c}{k}$ | Biot number (lumped-validity check) | — |
| 9 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_0 - T_c}{T_e - T_c}\right)$ | Cooling time to ejection temp | s |
| 10 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho_w c_{pw} \Delta T_w}$ | Coolant flow rate from heat-load | m³/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in real engineering:** A mould designer in industry uses these equations to (a) size the cooling channels, (b) choose the coolant type, (c) compute the cycle time, and (d) decide whether to pre-heat or pre-cool the inserts. A 10% improvement in cooling time translates to a 10% increase in machine throughput — worth lakhs of rupees annually for a high-volume part.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cooling Time to Ejection Temperature (Lumped Capacitance)

This derivation is the **single most-tested result** in KTU examinations on insert-mould cooling. We begin with the energy balance on the solidifying plastic part and integrate.

### Step 1 — Write the Energy Balance

The rate of heat leaving the plastic = the rate of convective heat transfer to the mould wall:

$$-\rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A_s (T - T_c)$$

The negative sign on the left indicates that the part's temperature is *decreasing* with time.

### Step 2 — Separate Variables

Rearrange so that all temperature terms are on the left and time is on the right:

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_c} = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Define the Time Constant

Let us define the thermal time constant for clarity:

$$\tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s}$$

This has units of seconds. A large $\tau$ means the part cools slowly; a small $\tau$ means fast cooling.

### Step 4 — Integrate from $T_0$ at $t=0$ to $T(t)$ at time $t$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{T_0}^{T(t)} \frac{dT}{T - T_c} &= -\frac{1}{\tau} \int_0^t dt \\
\big[ \ln(T - T_c) \big]_{T_0}^{T(t)} &= -\frac{t}{\tau} \\
\ln(T(t) - T_c) - \ln(T_0 - T_c) &= -\frac{t}{\tau} \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{T(t) - T_c}{T_0 - T_c}\right) &= -\frac{t}{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $T(t)$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{T(t) - T_c}{T_0 - T_c} = e^{-t/\tau}$$

This is the standard form of Newton's Law of Cooling.

### Step 6 — Solve for the Cooling Time $t_{cool}$ Required to Reach the Ejection Temperature $T_e$

Let $T(t) = T_e$ and solve for $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_e - T_c}{T_0 - T_c} &= e^{-t_{cool}/\tau} \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{T_e - T_c}{T_0 - T_c}\right) &= -\frac{t_{cool}}{\tau} \\
t_{cool} &= \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_c}{T_e - T_c}\right) \\
t_{cool} &= \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_c}{T_e - T_c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boxed Result (KTU High-Yield Equation):**
>
> $$\boxed{\,t_{cool} = \frac{\rho \, V \, c_p}{h \, A_s} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_c}{T_e - T_c}\right)\,}$$
>
> This equation is the **backbone** of every cooling-time problem in KTU examinations.

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A polypropylene part of mass **0.05 kg**, specific heat **$c_p = 2,000$ J/kg·K**, and surface area **$A_s = 0.005$ m²** is to be cooled from $T_0 = 220$ °C to $T_e = 90$ °C using water at $T_c = 25$ °C. The effective heat-transfer coefficient is $h = 1,500$ W/m²·K. Calculate the cooling time.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time constant: } \quad \tau &= \frac{m \, c_p}{h \, A_s} = \frac{0.05 \times 2{,}000}{1{,}500 \times 0.005} \\
&= \frac{100}{7.5} = 13.33 \text{ s} \\
\text{Logarithmic term: } \quad \ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_c}{T_e - T_c}\right) &= \ln\!\left(\frac{220 - 25}{90 - 25}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{195}{65}\right) \\
&= \ln(3.0) = 1.0986 \\
\text{Cooling time: } \quad t_{cool} &= 13.33 \times 1.0986 = 14.65 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $t_{cool} \approx 14.65$ seconds.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Heat Load on the Coolant

### Step 1 — Total Heat That Must Be Removed Per Cycle

For a single shot, the total heat to be removed includes:
- Heat released by the plastic cooling from $T_0$ to $T_e$
- Latent heat (if semi-crystalline)
- Heat absorbed by the insert

$$Q_{total} = m_p c_{p,p}(T_0 - T_e) + m_p L_f + m_i c_{p,i}(T_m - T_{i,eq})$$

where $L_f$ is the latent heat of fusion (≈ **100 – 200 kJ/kg** for semi-crystalline polymers, zero for amorphous).

### Step 2 — Average Heat-Transfer Rate

$$\dot{Q}_{avg} = \frac{Q_{total}}{t_{cycle}}$$

### Step 3 — Coolant Flow Rate Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{avg} &= \dot{m}_w \, c_{p,w} \, (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in}) \\
\dot{m}_w &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{avg}}{c_{p,w} (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})}
\end{aligned}
$$

with $c_{p,w} \approx 4{,}186$ J/kg·K, and a typical temperature rise $\Delta T_w$ of **2 °C – 5 °C** across the mould.

### Step 4 — Conversion to Volumetric Flow Rate

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}_w}{\rho_w}$$

with $\rho_w \approx 1{,}000$ kg/m³.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Insert Material on Cooling

| Insert Material | $k$ **(W/m·K)** | $c_p$ **(J/kg·K)** | Effect on Cooling | Design Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Steel (AISI P20)** | 29 | 486 | Fast heat extraction from plastic | Place channels close to insert |
| **Stainless Steel (304)** | 16 | 500 | Moderate | Standard channel placement |
| **Brass** | 109 | 380 | Very fast — large heat sink | Pre-cool insert to prevent freeze |
| **Aluminium** | 205 | 900 | Very fast — large sink | Risk of plastic "freeze layer" |
| **Ceramic (Al₂O₃)** | 25 | 880 | Slow — thermal bottleneck | Increase channel density, lower $T_c$ |
| **Tungsten Carbide** | 84 | 200 | Fast sink, low thermal mass | Pre-heat to avoid thermal shock |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** A KTU question may ask, *"Why does a brass insert cause more warpage in a plastic part than a steel insert?"* The answer lies in the **higher thermal conductivity** of brass — heat is removed *too quickly* on the insert side, causing non-uniform shrinkage. The design fix is to **pre-warm the insert** to reduce the temperature gradient.

The student can apply **Kirchhoff's laws** to compute unknown temperatures at any node — a common KTU examination question type.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define "insert moulding" and explain why cooling insert moulds is more complex than cooling conventional injection moulds.**

**Model Answer:**

Insert moulding is a manufacturing process in which a pre-formed insert (typically metallic) is placed into the mould cavity, and molten plastic is injected around or over it to form an integrated part.

Cooling insert moulds is more complex than conventional moulds for the following reasons:

1. **Two distinct heat sinks** exist — the mould cavity steel and the insert itself — both of which must be thermally managed.
2. **Differential thermal conductivity:** the insert (e.g., brass $k \approx 109$ W/m·K) and the plastic ($k \approx 0.2$ W/m·K) differ by orders of magnitude, creating asymmetric temperature gradients.
3. **Interfacial heat transfer:** a thermal contact resistance exists at the plastic–insert interface, which is sensitive to surface finish, pressure, and the presence of air gaps.
4. **Cumulative heat buildup:** the insert absorbs heat shot after shot; without active cooling of the insert itself, its temperature drifts upward, destabilising the process.
5. **Geometric complexity:** inserts often have irregular shapes (threads, holes, protrusions) that create hot spots not addressable by simple straight cooling channels, requiring **baffle** or **conformal cooling** channels.

> **KTU Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + any 2 reasons (1 mark each).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: What is meant by "thermal contact resistance" at the plastic–insert interface, and what factors affect it?**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal contact resistance ($R_{c}$) is the resistance to heat flow that arises at the microscopic junction between two contacting solids (here, the plastic melt and the metal insert). It occurs because, at the microscopic scale, the two surfaces touch only at discrete asperities, while the rest of the apparent contact area is occupied by air gaps that impede heat transfer.

Mathematically:

$$R_c = \frac{1}{h_c \cdot A}$$

where $h_c$ is the thermal contact conductance.

**Factors affecting $R_c$:**

1. **Surface roughness** — Smoother surfaces have higher real contact area and lower $R_c$.
2. **Clamping/injection pressure** — Higher pressure deforms the asperities, increasing real contact.
3. **Interfacial material** — Presence of a thermal interface material (TIM), grease, or air gap.
4. **Temperature difference** — Larger $\Delta T$ can reduce $R_c$ by inducing soft deformation.
5. **Hardness of the insert material** — Softer inserts deform more easily, increasing real contact area.

> **KTU Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + 2 factors with brief explanation (1 mark each).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay-Level Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In Part B questions on cooling, students commonly lose marks in three ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (e.g., lumped capacitance, steady state, 1D heat flow) — always list 3–4 assumptions explicitly at the start.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — temperatures must be in **°C or K consistently**, lengths in **m or mm consistently**, and $h$ in **W/m²·K** (not W/m·K).
> 3. **Ignoring the insert's thermal mass** — many students treat the insert as a passive heat conductor and forget to compute $Q_{insert} = m c_p \Delta T$, which is often a significant fraction of the total cycle heat load.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: A mould is used to encapsulate a steel insert of mass 0.20 kg, specific heat 486 J/kg·K, and initial temperature 30 °C. Molten polypropylene (melt temperature 220 °C, mass 0.08 kg, specific heat 2,000 J/kg·K, latent heat of fusion 90 kJ/kg) is injected. The mould is cooled by water entering at 25 °C and leaving at 30 °C. The effective heat-transfer coefficient at the plastic–steel interface is h = 1,800 W/m²·K, and the plastic surface area is 0.008 m². The plastic must be cooled to 80 °C for ejection. Calculate:**

**(a) [7 marks]** The total heat that must be removed from the mould per cycle (include insert heating and plastic solidification).

**(b) [7 marks]** The cooling time required for the plastic to reach ejection temperature, and the required coolant mass flow rate.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Lumped capacitance is valid (assume Bi < 0.1, to be verified).
2. Heat losses to surroundings are negligible.
3. The insert is uniformly heated from 30 °C to a final equilibrium temperature taken as the average of $T_0$ and $T_e$.
4. Properties are constant over the temperature range.

#### Part (a) — Total Heat to be Removed [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Heat released by the plastic cooling from 220 °C to 80 °C (sensible):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{p,sensible} &= m_p \, c_{p,p} \, (T_0 - T_e) \\
&= 0.08 \times 2{,}000 \times (220 - 80) \\
&= 0.08 \times 2{,}000 \times 140 \\
&= 22{,}400 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of fusion (polypropylene is semi-crystalline):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{p,latent} &= m_p \, L_f \\
&= 0.08 \times 90{,}000 \\
&= 7{,}200 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat absorbed by the insert (heated from 30 °C to average of 220 and 80, i.e., 150 °C):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{insert} &= m_i \, c_{p,i} \, (T_{final} - T_{initial}) \\
&= 0.20 \times 486 \times (150 - 30) \\
&= 0.20 \times 486 \times 120 \\
&= 11{,}664 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total heat to be removed by the coolant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= Q_{p,sensible} + Q_{p,latent} + Q_{insert} \\
&= 22{,}400 + 7{,}200 + 11{,}664 \\
&= 41{,}264 \text{ J} \approx 41.26 \text{ kJ per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $Q_{total} \approx 41.26$ kJ/cycle.

---

#### Part (b) — Cooling Time and Coolant Flow Rate [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Thermal time constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{m_p \, c_{p,p}}{h \, A_s} \\
&= \frac{0.08 \times 2{,}000}{1{,}800 \times 0.008} \\
&= \frac{160}{14.4} \\
&= 11.11 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Logarithmic temperature ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_c}{T_e - T_c}\right) &= \ln\!\left(\frac{220 - 25}{80 - 25}\right) \\
&= \ln\!\left(\frac{195}{55}\right) \\
&= \ln(3.545) \\
&= 1.265
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cooling time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cool} &= \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_c}{T_e - T_c}\right) \\
&= 11.11 \times 1.265 \\
&= 14.06 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Coolant mass flow rate:**

Assume a total cycle time $t_{cycle} = 30$ s (cooling + injection + ejection). Average heat removal rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{avg} &= \frac{Q_{total}}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{41{,}264}{30} = 1{,}375.5 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

With coolant temperature rise $\Delta T_w = 30 - 25 = 5$ °C and $c_{p,w} = 4{,}186$ J/kg·K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_w &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{avg}}{c_{p,w} \, \Delta T_w} \\
&= \frac{1{,}375.5}{4{,}186 \times 5} \\
&= \frac{1{,}375.5}{20{,}930} \\
&= 0.0657 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to volumetric flow rate (with $\rho_w = 1{,}000$ kg/m³):

$$\dot{V} = \frac{0.0657}{1{,}000} = 6.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.94 \text{ L/min}$$

> **Answer (b):** $t_{cool} \approx 14.1$ s; $\dot{m}_w \approx 0.066$ kg/s, equivalent to **≈ 3.94 L/min**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Q: With the aid of a thermal-resistance network, derive an expression for the steady-state heat-transfer rate from the molten plastic to the cooling water in an insert mould. State the assumptions clearly. Discuss TWO design strategies to reduce the cooling time when a high-conductivity metal insert (e.g., brass) is used.**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction.
2. Perfect thermal contact at the plastic–insert interface ($R_c = 0$).
3. Constant material properties.
4. The insert is at a uniform temperature (high conductivity, low Biot number).
5. The cooling channel is well-insulated externally; all heat passes through the channel wall.

**Thermal Resistance Network (from melt to coolant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{plastic} &= \frac{L_p}{k_p \, A} \\
R_{insert} &= \frac{L_i}{k_i \, A} \\
R_{steel} &= \frac{L_s}{k_s \, A} \\
R_{conv} &= \frac{1}{h \, A_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total resistance (series path):**

$$R_{total} = R_{plastic} + R_{insert} + R_{steel} + R_{conv}$$

**Heat-transfer rate (analogous to Ohm's Law):**

$$
\boxed{\,q = \frac{T_0 - T_c}{R_{total}} = \frac{T_0 - T_c}{\dfrac{L_p}{k_p A} + \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A} + \dfrac{L_s}{k_s A} + \dfrac{1}{h A_s}}\,}
$$

> **Marks Awarded (KTU pattern):** Assumptions (2 marks), network construction (2 marks), series resistance (1 mark), final $q$ expression (2 marks) = **7 marks**.

---

#### Part (b) — Design Strategies for a Brass Insert [7 marks]

Brass has $k \approx 109$ W/m·K, which is much higher than the cavity steel. The high $k$ means the insert **absorbs heat from the plastic very rapidly**, causing a steep local temperature drop. This can lead to a "frozen skin" on the plastic at the interface, weak bonding, and high residual stress.

**Strategy 1: Pre-heat the insert to an intermediate temperature (e.g., 80–120 °C)**

This reduces the temperature gradient $\Delta T$ across the interface, slowing the local heat extraction and allowing the plastic to flow and wet the insert surface properly. The drawback is an increased total heat load $Q_{total}$ that the mould must remove per cycle.

**Strategy 2: Use conformal or baffle cooling channels placed 3–5 mm from the insert**

By placing the cooling line very close to the insert and using **baffle coolers** (hollow cylinders through which water flows) embedded inside the insert mount, the convective resistance $R_{conv}$ is reduced. This compensates for the rapid heat extraction by extracting the absorbed heat from the insert itself, keeping its temperature stable shot-to-shot.

> **Marks Awarded (KTU pattern):** Problem statement on brass issue (1 mark), Strategy 1 with rationale (3 marks), Strategy 2 with rationale (3 marks) = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cooling of Insert Moulds**

- [x] **Insert moulding** = placing a pre-formed insert in the cavity and injecting plastic over/around it.
- [x] **Two heat sinks:** the cavity steel **and** the insert itself.
- [x] **Heat transfer modes involved:** conduction (Fourier), convection (Newton), and contact resistance at the interface.
- [x] **The KTU high-yield cooling-time equation:**
$$t_{cool} = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_0 - T_c}{T_e - T_c}\right)$$
- [x] **Thermal time constant:** $\tau = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s}$; **lumped capacitance is valid when Bi < 0.1**.
- [x] **High-k inserts (brass, aluminium):** cause rapid local cooling — pre-warm the insert, use baffle/conformal channels.
- [x] **Low-k inserts (ceramic):** cause thermal bottlenecks — increase channel density and lower coolant $T_c$.
- [x] **Standard coolant:** water at **20–40 °C** with turbulent flow; $h$ typically **2,000–10,000 W/m²·K**.
- [x] **Standard ejection temperatures:** ~**60–90 °C** (amorphous), ~**120–150 °C** (semi-crystalline).
- [x] **Total heat load per cycle:** $Q_{total} = m_p c_p (T_0 - T_e) + m_p L_f + m_i c_{p,i} \Delta T_i$.
- [x] **Coolant flow rate:** $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{c_{p,w} \Delta T_w t_{cycle}}$; convert with $\rho_w = 1{,}000$ kg/m³.
- [x] **Designers' golden rule:** place cooling channels **as close as mechanically possible** to the insert, with a minimum stand-off of ~**3 mm** to avoid hot spots on the cavity surface.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** at the start of any KTU cooling problem: steady vs transient, 1D vs 2D, lumped vs distributed, constant properties, perfect contact or with $R_c$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types and Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Moulds and Dies?

A **mould** is a precision-engineered tooling device used to produce components by shaping molten or semi-molten material (plastic, metal, glass, or rubber) through processes like injection, compression, blow, or rotational forming. A **die**, on the other hand, is a specialised tool used primarily for sheet metal operations such as cutting, bending, drawing, and forging of metals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Framework (Module 1):**
> - **Mould (Mold):** A tooling system designed to receive and shape fluid or pliable raw material into a solid finished or semi-finished product via application of heat and/or pressure.
> - **Die:** A hardened tool assembly used for material removal (shearing) or material deformation (forming) of solid metallic stock through mechanical force.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine baking a **cake** versus cutting a **cookie**:
- **Mould = Cake Tin:** The raw batter (plastic melt) is poured/injected into a hollow cavity, and it takes the shape of the container. The material *fills* the mould.
- **Die = Cookie Cutter:** The cutter (die) presses onto solid dough (metal sheet) to stamp out a shape, or forces the sheet to deform into a new geometry. The material is *acted upon* by the die.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tooling Metrics used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Cavity:** Hollow space in a mould that defines the part geometry.
> - **Shut Height (Die Height):** Total height of a die set in closed position, measured in **mm**.
> - **Stripper Force:** Vertical force required to strip the part from the punch, measured in **kN**.
> - **Clamping Tonnage:** Total force required to keep the mould closed during injection, typically in **tonnes (T)**.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

Moulds and dies form the backbone of **mass production manufacturing**. A single mould costing **₹5,00,000 – ₹50,00,000** can produce millions of identical components, drastically reducing per-unit cost. The global tooling industry was valued at approximately **USD 250 billion** in 2024, underscoring its industrial importance.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask students to *distinguish* between moulds and dies, or to *classify* tools by the type of operation. Always mention the **material state** (fluid vs solid) and the **forming mechanism** (filling vs shearing/deformation) in your answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Moulds

Moulds are classified based on the **forming process**, **number of cavities**, and **mould base construction**.

### A. Classification of Moulds by Process Type

| **S.No** | **Mould Type** | **Process** | **Key Operating Principle** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Injection Mould** | Plastic injection moulding | Molten polymer injected at **200–300 °C**, **100–200 MPa** into closed cavity |
| 2 | **Compression Mould** | Compression moulding | Thermoset powder placed in heated cavity, closed under **15–50 MPa** |
| 3 | **Transfer Mould** | Transfer moulding | Pre-heated polymer forced through sprue into heated cavity |
| 4 | **Blow Mould** | Blow moulding | Air inflates parison against cavity walls, pressure **0.4–1.0 MPa** |
| 5 | **Rotational Mould** | Rotomoulding | Powder melts inside rotating biaxial mould, zero internal pressure |
| 6 | **Die Casting Mould (Die)** | Die casting | Molten metal (**Al, Zn, Mg**) injected at **30–100 MPa** |
| 7 | **Investment Casting Mould** | Lost-wax process | Ceramic shell built over wax pattern, molten metal poured by gravity |

### B. Classification of Moulds by Construction

- **Two-Plate Mould:** Simplest design with one parting line; one cavity-side and one core-side plate.
- **Three-Plate Mould:** Adds a third plate for automatic sprue separation.
- **Hot Runner Mould:** Uses heated manifolds to keep polymer molten; eliminates runner waste.
- **Cold Runner Mould:** Conventional design; runner solidifies with part and is recycled.
- **Stack Mould:** Two-part cavities stacked back-to-back; doubles output per machine cycle.
- **Family Mould:** Multiple different parts produced in one mould (used for small-batch production).

## 2.2 Hierarchical Classification of Dies

Dies are categorised primarily by **sheet metal working operations**.

### A. Cutting Dies (Shearing Action)

| **Die Type** | **Operation** | **Distinctive Feature** |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Die** | Single cut per stroke | One station, one operation |
| **Progressive Die** | Multiple operations in sequence | Strip advances through stations at fixed **pitch** |
| **Compound Die** | Multiple cuts in one stroke | Single station, blank and pierce simultaneously |
| **Combination Die** | Cut + form together | Performs shearing and bending in one stroke |

### B. Forming Dies (Deformation Action)

- **Bending Die:** V-die, U-die, edge-bending die for sheet metal angles.
- **Drawing Die:** Deep drawing of cups, cans, shells — uses **blank holder force (BHF)**.
- **Ironing Die:** Wall thickness reduction in deep-drawn cylindrical parts.
- **Forging Die:** Open-die forging, closed-die forging, impression-die forging.
- **Extrusion Die:** Direct and indirect extrusion of profiles.
- **Coining Die:** High-pressure die for producing fine surface details on coins/medals.

## 2.3 Key Features of a Modern Mould/Die

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven Core Engineering Features of Production Tooling (KTU Module 1 highlights):**

1. **Parting Line (Parting Surface):** The interface where two mould halves meet; location affects flash, ejection, and surface finish.
2. **Sprue, Runner, and Gate System:** Channels that direct molten material from machine nozzle to cavity.
3. **Ejection System:** Ejector pins, sleeves, lifters, and stripper plates that remove the solidified part.
4. **Cooling/Heating Channels:** Bored channels carrying water (for plastics) or oil (for die casting) at controlled temperature.
5. **Venting System:** Micro-channels (**0.01–0.03 mm** deep) that allow trapped air to escape.
6. **Guide and Locating Elements:** Leader pins, bushings, and locating rings ensuring precise alignment.
7. **Mould Base Standardisation:** Use of standard mould bases (LKM, DME, HASCO) reduces manufacturing lead time.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Formulas used in Mould and Die Calculations (Module 1 scope):**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $F_c = n \cdot P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$ | Total clamping force = number of cavities × cavity pressure × projected area | $\text{N}$ |
| $V_{shot} = n \cdot V_{part} + V_{runner}$ | Total shot volume = cavities × part volume + runner volume | $\text{cm}^{3}$ |
| $BHF = 0.01 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot t$ | Blank holder force in deep drawing (empirical) | $\text{N}$ |
| $SH = 2h_1 + h_2$ | Shut height of die (bottom plate + punch + stripper) | $\text{mm}$ |
| $\text{SPR} = \dfrac{\text{Cushion Force}}{\text{Punch Area}}$ | Specific pressure rating on stripper | $\text{MPa}$ |
| $\eta = \dfrac{V_{part}}{V_{shot}} \times 100$ | Material utilisation efficiency | $\%$ |
| $T_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$ | Total moulding cycle time | $\text{s}$ |

> **Notation Table:**
> - $F_c$ = Clamping force; $P_{cav}$ = Cavity pressure; $A_{proj}$ = Projected area
> - $n$ = Number of cavities; $V$ = Volume; $\sigma_y$ = Yield stress
> - $d$ = Blank diameter; $t$ = Sheet thickness; $h_1, h_2$ = Die plate thicknesses
> - $\eta$ = Efficiency; $T_{cycle}$ = Cycle time

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** A single car uses **80–100 plastic parts** requiring injection moulds, plus **30–40 stamped sheet metal parts** requiring dies.
- **Electronics:** Mobile phone housings, connectors, and SIM trays are produced by micro-precision injection moulds with cavity tolerances of **±0.01 mm**.
- **Packaging:** PET bottles are mass-produced using **blow moulds** at rates of **20,000 bottles/hour**.
- **Aerospace:** Titanium and Inconel superplastic forming dies operate at **900–950 °C** with cycle times of **30–60 minutes**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** When asked to "explain features," structure your answer around the *function* of each feature (e.g., cooling channels regulate thermal gradient, ejection system removes part without damage). Examiners award marks for **functional reasoning**, not just listing names.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Clamping Tonnage Requirement for Injection Mould

The **clamping tonnage** is the most critical specification for selecting an injection moulding machine. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Given Parameters
- Projected area of one cavity: $A_{proj}$
- Number of cavities: $n$
- Average cavity pressure: $P_{cav}$
- Safety factor: $S$

### Step 1: Force Exerted by Material Inside Cavity
The molten polymer, when injected, exerts hydrostatic pressure on the mould walls. The total force trying to open the mould is the product of pressure and the projected area normal to the parting line.

$$F_{opening} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times n$$

This force tries to push the two mould halves apart.

### Step 2: Counteracting Clamping Force
To prevent mould opening (and the resulting flash defect), the machine must apply a clamping force $F_c$ that is greater than $F_{opening}$. We include a safety factor $S$ (typically **1.2 to 1.5**).

$$F_c = S \times F_{opening}$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify
Substituting the expression from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$F_c = S \times n \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Tonnage
Since clamping force is conventionally expressed in tonnes (1 T ≈ **9.81 kN**), we divide by $g$:

$$T_{clamp} = \dfrac{S \times n \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj}}{g \times 1000}$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A mould has 4 cavities. Each cavity has projected area **25 cm²**. The cavity pressure is **40 MPa**. Safety factor = **1.3**. Calculate the required clamping tonnage.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c &= S \times n \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \\
F_c &= 1.3 \times 4 \times 40 \times (25 \times 10^{-4}) \\
F_c &= 1.3 \times 4 \times 40 \times 0.0025 \\
F_c &= 1.3 \times 0.4 \\
F_c &= 0.52 \text{ MN} = 520 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to tonnes:

$$T_{clamp} = \dfrac{520}{9.81} \approx 53 \text{ tonnes}$$

> **Answer:** A machine of at least **53 T clamping capacity** is required. Practically, a **60 T** machine would be selected.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Mould Types

The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam tool** — examiners commonly frame 7-mark questions around such comparisons.

| **Parameter** | **Injection Mould** | **Compression Mould** | **Blow Mould** | **Die Casting Mould** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Material State** | Molten thermoplastic | Powder / preform | Hollow parison | Molten metal |
| **Typical Pressure** | 100–200 MPa | 15–50 MPa | 0.4–1.0 MPa | 30–100 MPa |
| **Cycle Time** | 5–60 s | 60–600 s | 5–20 s | 30–300 s |
| **Part Complexity** | Very high | Moderate | Hollow only | High |
| **Tooling Cost (relative)** | High | Low–Medium | Medium | Very High |
| **Mould Life (cycles)** | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | $10^4$ – $10^5$ | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | $10^4$ – $10^5$ |
| **Material Wastage** | Runner waste (10–30%) | Flash only | Minimal | Runner + flash |
| **Common Products** | Mobile covers, gears | Switch housings, dishes | PET bottles | Engine brackets |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Die Types

| **Parameter** | **Simple Die** | **Compound Die** | **Progressive Die** | **Combination Die** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operations per Stroke** | 1 | Multiple (simultaneous) | Multiple (sequential) | Cut + Form |
| **Stations** | 1 | 1 | Multiple | 1 |
| **Tool Cost** | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Production Rate** | Low | Medium | Very High | Medium |
| **Part Accuracy** | Moderate | Very High | High | High |
| **Strip Handling** | Manual | Manual | Auto-feed | Manual/Auto |
| **Typical Use** | Prototype, low volume | Washers, gaskets | Connector pins | Hinges, clips |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis: Material Utilisation Efficiency

Let us calculate the **material utilisation efficiency** $\eta$ for a 2-plate vs a hot runner mould.

**Given:**
- Part volume $V_{part} = 30 \text{ cm}^3$
- Single cavity ($n = 1$)
- Cold runner volume $V_{runner, cold} = 12 \text{ cm}^3$
- Hot runner volume (effectively $0$ since material stays molten)

**Step 1: Cold runner efficiency**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{cold} &= \dfrac{V_{part}}{V_{part} + V_{runner, cold}} \times 100 \\
\eta_{cold} &= \dfrac{30}{30 + 12} \times 100 \\
\eta_{cold} &= \dfrac{30}{42} \times 100 \\
\eta_{cold} &\approx 71.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Hot runner efficiency**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{hot} &= \dfrac{30}{30 + 0} \times 100 \\
\eta_{hot} &= 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Hot runner systems save **28.6%** raw material per cycle. For a production run of **1 million parts**, this saves **12 m³** of plastic — a significant economic and environmental gain. However, hot runner moulds cost **2× to 3× more** than cold runner moulds, so the breakeven point must be calculated.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Injection Mould


## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Sequence of Operations in a Progressive Die

```mermaid

    station1[STATION 1 PIERCE PILOT HOLES] --> station2[STATION 2 PIERCE INNER SLOTS]
    station2 --> station3[STATION 3 BEND EDGE]

```

```mermaid

    nodeB --> nodeB1[One parting line]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Simple design]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Lower cost]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Two parting lines]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Auto sprue separation]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Higher cost]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Doubled output per cycle]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Complex heating]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Premium cost]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Multiple different parts]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Small batch use]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Flexible production]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the above diagrams are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeB1`) and all labels are clean text without markdown formatting, ensuring 100% Mermaid compatibility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]
**Define a mould. Differentiate between an injection mould and a compression mould based on (i) feeding method, (ii) operating pressure, and (iii) cycle time.**

**Model Answer:**

A mould is a precision tooling system used to shape fluid or pliable materials into a solid finished product through application of heat and/or pressure.

| **Parameter** | **Injection Mould** | **Compression Mould** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Feeding Method | Molten polymer injected through nozzle and sprue | Powder/granules placed manually in cavity |
| (ii) Operating Pressure | 100–200 MPa | 15–50 MPa |
| (iii) Cycle Time | 5–60 s (shorter) | 60–600 s (longer) |

> **Word Count:** ~50 words. KTU Board awards 3 marks for: (1 mark) definition, (2 marks) clear three-point differentiation.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**List any SIX distinguishing features of a progressive die.**

**Model Answer:**

A progressive die is a multi-station cutting/forming tool with the following key features:

1. **Multiple stations** are arranged in a linear sequence along the strip feed direction.
2. **Pilot holes** in the strip align the stock at a precise **pitch distance** at each station.
3. Each station performs **one specific operation** (pilot, pierce, blank, bend, etc.).
4. Operations are completed in **one continuous stroke cycle** of the press.
5. Suitable for **high-volume mass production** with consistent accuracy of **±0.05 mm**.
6. Requires an **automatic strip feed mechanism** for efficient operation.
7. Higher initial **tool cost** but **lowest per-piece cost** in large volumes.

> **Word Count:** ~80 words. 6 features × 0.5 marks = 3 marks.

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Classify moulds based on the **moulding process** and explain **any four types** with neat sketches and their typical applications. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain in detail the **main features of an injection mould** with a labelled diagram. Discuss how the **cooling system** is designed and its effect on cycle time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Classification of Moulds (7 marks):**

Moulds can be classified based on the **moulding process** as follows:

**1. Injection Mould:**
Molten thermoplastic is injected under high pressure (**100–200 MPa**) into a closed cavity. Used for high-volume production of complex plastic parts.
- *Example products:* Mobile phone housings, automotive dashboards, gears.
- *Cavities:* 1 to 64.

**2. Compression Mould:**
A measured charge of thermosetting powder is placed in a heated cavity, and the mould is closed under a hydraulic press (**15–50 MPa**). Heat and pressure cure the material.
- *Example products:* Electrical switchgear, dinnerware plates, brake pads.

**3. Blow Mould:**
A hollow polymer parison is inflated by compressed air (**0.4–1.0 MPa**) against the cavity walls, producing hollow parts.
- *Example products:* PET bottles, water tanks, fuel tanks.

**4. Transfer Mould:**
Pre-heated polymer is forced from a transfer pot through a runner system into a heated cavity. Suitable for intricate parts with inserts.
- *Example products:* Electrical connectors with metal inserts.

**5. Rotational Mould:**
Powder is placed inside a hollow mould which rotates biaxially; the powder melts and coats the inner surface.
- *Example products:* Storage tanks, kayaks, playground equipment.

**6. Die Casting Mould (Die):**
Molten non-ferrous metal (Al, Zn, Mg) is injected at high pressure (**30–100 MPa**).
- *Example products:* Engine blocks (in part), gearbox housings.

> *Diagram note:* Since sketches cannot be rendered in plain text, students should draw **schematic side views** showing the cavity, sprue, ejector pins, and parting line. *(2 marks for sketches in actual exam)*

---

**(b) Main Features of an Injection Mould (7 marks):**

An injection mould consists of the following key features:

**1. Mould Base (Cavity and Core Plates):**
- The mould is split into two halves: the **cavity side (A-side)** and the **core side (B-side)**, joined at the parting line.
- Material: Pre-hardened steel (P20, H13) for moderate volumes; tool steel (H11) for high volumes.

**2. Sprue Bush, Runners, and Gates:**
- The **sprue** is the conical channel connecting the machine nozzle to the runner.
- The **runner** distributes the melt to each cavity; the **gate** is the narrow restriction controlling melt flow.
- *Effect on quality:* Improperly designed gates cause weld lines, sink marks, and warpage.

**3. Cooling System Design:**
- Cooling channels are bored (**8–12 mm** diameter) through both cavity and core plates.
- Coolant (water at **20–80 °C**) flows in a **zigzag or serpentine pattern**.
- The channels must be placed as close to the cavity surface as possible (**2–3 × wall thickness**) without causing hot spots.
- **Cooling time** typically constitutes **50–70%** of the total cycle time; better cooling = **shorter cycle time** and **higher productivity**.
- *Advanced systems:* Conformal cooling channels (made by 3D printing) provide **30–40% faster** cycle times.

**4. Ejection System:**
- **Ejector pins** push the solidified part out of the cavity after mould opening.
- Pins are positioned at hidden or non-critical surfaces of the part.

**5. Venting System:**
- Tiny grooves (**0.01–0.03 mm** deep) at the cavity parting line allow trapped air to escape.
