| Plain Side-and-Face | All side teeth aligned | Light slotting | High axial thrust, chatter |
| Staggered-Tooth | Alternating left/right side teeth | Heavy die slotting | Slightly complex to manufacture |
| Half-Side | Teeth on one side only | One-sided milling | Cannot machine closed slots |
| Slitting Saw | Very thin body | Deep narrow slots | Limited rigidity, deflection |

**Part (b) — Taylor Equation Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $V_1 = 40$ m/min, $T_1 = 60$ min
- $V_2 = 60$ m/min, $T_2 = 20$ min

**(i) Taylor's Exponent $n$:**

$$n = \dfrac{\ln(V_1 / V_2)}{\ln(T_2 / T_1)} = \dfrac{\ln(40 / 60)}{\ln(20 / 60)} = \dfrac{\ln(0.6667)}{\ln(0.3333)}$$

Computing the logarithms:

$$\ln(0.6667) \approx -0.4055$$

$$\ln(0.3333) \approx -1.0986$$

$$n = \dfrac{-0.4055}{-1.0986} \approx 0.369$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 0.369}$$

**(ii) Taylor's Constant $C$:**

Using $V_1 \cdot T_1^n = C$:

$$C = 40 \cdot (60)^{0.369}$$

Compute $60^{0.369}$:

$$\ln(60^{0.369}) = 0.369 \cdot \ln(60) = 0.369 \cdot 4.0943 = 1.5108$$

$$60^{0.369} = e^{1.5108} \approx 4.531$$

$$C = 40 \cdot 4.531 \approx 181.24$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 181}$$

**(iii) Recommended Cutting Speed for $T = 90$ min:**

$$V_c = \dfrac{C}{T^n} = \dfrac{181}{(90)^{0.369}}$$

Compute $90^{0.369}$:

$$\ln(90^{0.369}) = 0.369 \cdot \ln(90) = 0.369 \cdot 4.4998 = 1.6604$$

$$90^{0.369} = e^{1.6604} \approx 5.262$$

$$V_c = \dfrac{181}{5.262} \approx 34.40 \text{ m/min}$$

$$\boxed{V_c \approx 34.4 \text{ m/min}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks trap):** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m when computing $V_c$ from $N$ and $D$. Always remember the factor of **1000** in $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$.
> 2. **Tool-Life Equation Sign (–1 mark):** The Taylor exponent $n$ is **always positive** when computed using magnitudes; do not return a negative value.
> 3. **Stagger Geometry (–3 marks trap):** When asked "why staggered?", do **not** answer "for better chip evacuation" alone. Always mention **axial force balance** as the primary reason.
> 4. **Skipping the Sketch (–2 marks):** In KTU board valuation, a part-(a) question without a labelled sketch typically loses 2 marks even if the written content is perfect.
> 5. **Arbor Hole vs Shank Confusion:** Fishtail cutters are predominantly **arbor-mounted**; end mills are shank-mounted. Do not interchange the two.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **fishtail cutter** is a side-and-face milling cutter with peripheral and end-face teeth, named for its tapered, fish-tail-shaped side teeth.
- ✅ The **staggered-tooth variant** is the industry standard for die-making because it cancels axial cutting forces.
- ✅ Standard materials: **HSS** (general), **Carbide-tipped** (hardened die steel), **Coated carbide** (high-performance).
- ✅ Always apply **Taylor's equation** $V_c T^n = C$ for tool-life and cutting-speed related numericals.
- ✅ Use the correct units: $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm (multiply by 1000), $N$ in rpm.
- ✅ Feed rate formula: $v_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ — remember to multiply by the **number of teeth**.
- ✅ MRR in milling: $MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot W$ where $W$ is the **width of cut** (= cutter width for slot milling).
- ✅ Side relief angle ($\alpha_s$) is intentionally **smaller** than peripheral relief angle ($\alpha_p$) to maintain rigidity against the vertical wall of the slot.
- ✅ Applications: **slot milling, parting, side milling, profile milling, T-slot cutting, keyway cutting**.
- ✅ KTU frequently tests: **diagram + classification + one numerical** (Taylor equation OR spindle speed/feed calculation).
- ✅ Remember the **standard arbor hole sizes**: 22, 27, 32, 40, 50 mm.
- ✅ **Tip:** Memorize $V_c$ ranges — HSS on mild steel ≈ **25–35 m/min**, Carbide on hardened steel ≈ **80–150 m/min**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Annular
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Annular # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Mould and Die Manufacture**, an **Annular Die** is a metal-forming tooling assembly possessing a circular, ring-shaped (toroidal) flow channel that converts a polymeric (or metallic) melt/feedstock entering from the centre into a continuous hollow profile of uniform wall thickness and constant outer diameter. The annular geometry is defined by an outer diameter $D_o$, an inner mandrel (or torpedo) diameter $D_i$, and a final calibrating **land length** $L$ which establishes the exit wall thickness $h = \frac{D_o - D_i}{2}$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PEPOT756 / Module 2):** > Annular dies fall under the classification of *extrusion dies for hollow/symmetric profiles*. KTU examiners routinely test the geometry, pressure-flow relations, and the role of the **spider leg / mandrel support** system. The mathematical domain of the annular die may be expressed as: $$\text{Annular Channel Cross-Section} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right)$$ The characteristic **hydraulic diameter** $D_h$ of the annular passage, which governs viscous flow behaviour, is: $$D_h \;=\; D_o - D_i \;=\; 2h$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Straw-Covered Donut Maker" Analogy** > Imagine squeezing toothpaste from a tube, but instead of a single round nozzle, you push the paste through a hollow **doughnut-shaped ring** whose centre is blocked by a fixed pin. The paste cannot go through the centre (because the pin is there), and it cannot escape outward (because of the steel walls). The only escape route is the **thin circular slit between the pin and the outer wall** — exactly like the gap in a shower head ring. What comes out is a continuous hollow tube whose wall thickness equals that narrow gap. That is precisely how an **annular die** manufactures PVC pipes, plastic straws, paratube profiles, wire insulation, and blown polymer films. ## 1.3 Engineering Classification of Annular Dies | Class | Mechanism | Typical Product | |---|---|---| | **Spider-Type Annular Die** | Mandrel held by 3–4 radial "spider legs" | Rigid PVC pipes, HDPE tubes | | **Spiral Mandrel Die** | Helical grooves distribute melt progressively | Blown film, large pipe extrusion | | **Coat-Hanger (Annular Variant)** | Manifold tapers to equalise flow | Wire coating, cable sheathing | | **Side-fed / Cross-head Die** | Material enters tangentially | Tubular profiles, irrigation pipes | ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Benchmark Industry Values used in KTU numericals:** > - Typical land length $L$ : **8 × to 15 × the gap height** ($8h \leq L \leq 15h$) > - Land roughness target $R_a$ : **$\leq 0.4 \, \mu m$** (mirror polish for polymer extrusion) > - Die-steel grade: **H13 hot-work tool steel** (HRC **48–52**) > - Standard melt velocity at die exit: **$0.01$ to $0.05$ m/s** > - Pressure drop range across die: **$5$ to $15$ MPa** # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition The annular die operates on **viscous Poiseuille-type flow** through a thin rectangular slit approximated as an annular channel. The chain of physical events is: 1. **Polymer melt enters** the die body from the extruder through a feed pipe at pressure $P_0$. 2. **Manifold / spider section** distributes the melt circumferentially around the mandrel so that flow is axisymmetric. 3. **Approach zone** (converging taper) accelerates the melt and raises shear rate. 4. **Land region** of length $L$ and gap $h$ acts as the metering section — here flow becomes fully developed and Hagen–Poiseuille relations apply. 5. **Die exit** releases the extrudate; due to **elastic recovery (die swell)**, the tube diameter grows by a factor $B = D_{extrudate} / D_{die}$. 6. **Calibration / cooling** (downstream, not part of die itself) freezes final dimensions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind Each Step** > - The **land** is essential because it straightens velocity profiles; without it, the extrudate has molecular orientation memory and warps. > - The **spider legs** create weld-lines (Knit Lines) — a KTU favourite. These are mechanically weaker zones where flow fronts rejoin after passing the legs. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table) > [!NOTE] > All equations below are valid for **Newtonian melt approximation** under steady, laminar, isothermal, fully-developed flow through a thin annular slit ($D_o \approx D_i \approx D$, $h \ll D$). | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hydraulic diameter | $D_h = D_o - D_i = 2h$ | m | Characterises channel | | 2 | Volumetric flow rate (Newtonian) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D_h^3 \, \Delta P}{12 \, \mu \, L}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Sizing extruder capacity | | 3 | Mean velocity in land | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{D_h^2 \, \Delta P}{12 \, \mu \, L}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Predicts output speed | | 4 | Pressure drop across land | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L}{\pi D_h^3}$ | Pa | Mandrel stiffness design | | 5 | Wall shear stress at land wall | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, h}{2 L}$ | Pa | Checks against melt fracture | | 6 | Shear rate at wall (Newtonian) | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{6 Q}{\pi D_h^3}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Viscosity lookup | | 7 | Annular cross-sectional area | $A = \pi h (D_o - h) = \pi h D_i + \pi h^2$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Output volume per second | | 8 | Die swell ratio (elastic extrudate) | $B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | dimensionless | Calibration sizing | | 9 | Spider-leg weld factor | $K_w = 1 + \alpha \, n_{legs} \, \left(\tfrac{\dot{\gamma}}{\dot{\gamma}_0}\right)^{-\beta}$ | dimensionless | Strength loss prediction | | 10 | Reynolds number (annular) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, \bar{v} \, D_h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Confirms laminar regime | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Table Formatting Rule Applied:** All vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` or rewritten as explicit notation to keep markdown tables intact. The student is expected to reproduce the table structure (without LaTeX pipes) in the answer book. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Polymer Pipe Industry:** Every PVC / HDPE / PPR pipe in Kerala's plumbing market (≥ 20 mm OD) is produced via an annular spider die. - **Wire & Cable:** Insulation on house wiring (e.g., RR Kabel, Polycab) is coated using a **cross-head annular die** that wraps polymer around the copper conductor. - **Packaging Films:** Grocery bags come from **spiral mandrel blown film dies** — a direct descendant of the annular die family. - **Medical Tubing:** Catheters, IV lines, blood-bag tubes — all extruded through precision annular dies with land length tolerances of **± 0.005 mm**. - **Heat-shrink Sleeves:** Produced by **tube extrusion** through annular dies followed by secondary expansion. ## 2.4 Governing Boundary Conditions For the analytical derivations in Section 3, the following **assumptions** are mandatory (and KTU examiners deduct marks if omitted): 1. Steady-state, incompressible flow. 2. Newtonian melt (valid for moderate shear rates). 3. No-slip at all wetted walls. 4. Thin-gap approximation: $h \ll D_i$ (typically $h/D_i \leq 0.1$). 5. Isothermal process (no viscous heating correction). 6. Negligible inertia (creeping flow, $Re < 10$). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Pressure Drop Through an Annular Die Land ### Step 1 — Unwrap the Annulus A thin annular channel, when "unrolled" into a flat rectangular slit, becomes equivalent to a slit of width $w = \pi (D_o + D_i)/2 = \pi D_m$ (mean circumference) and height $h = D_o - D_i$. $$w \;=\; \pi D_m \;=\; \pi \cdot \frac{D_o + D_i}{2}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Navier–Stokes Equation (Slit Approximation) For steady, fully-developed, laminar flow in the gap $h$, neglecting curvature, the momentum equation reduces to: $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \;=\; \mu \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2}$$ where $z$ is along the land length $L$, and $y$ is across the gap $(-h/2 \leq y \leq +h/2)$. ### Step 3 — Integrate Twice Integrating once with respect to $y$: $$\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\, y + C_1$$ Integrating again: $$v_z(y) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\, y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$ ### Step 4 — Apply No-Slip Boundary Conditions At the walls $y = \pm h/2$, we require $v_z = 0$. By symmetry, $C_1 = 0$. Then: $$0 \;=\; \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^2 + C_2$$ $$C_2 \;=\; -\frac{h^2}{8\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}$$ ### Step 5 — Velocity Profile $$\boxed{v_z(y) \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\left[\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right]}$$ This is a **parabolic Poiseuille profile** in the $y$-direction — the same shape as classical pipe flow. ### Step 6 — Volumetric Flow Rate by Integration Integrate $v_z$ over the slit cross-section $A = w \cdot h$: $$Q \;=\; \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} v_z(y)\, w\, dy$$ $$Q \;=\; -\frac{w}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\left[\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right] dy$$ The integral evaluates to: $$\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\left[\frac{h^2}{4} - y^2\right] dy \;=\; \left[\frac{h^2 y}{4} - \frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \;=\; \frac{h^3}{6}$$ Therefore: $$Q \;=\; -\frac{w h^3}{12 \mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}$$ ### Step 7 — Convert to Finite Pressure Drop Over Land Length L Since the pressure gradient is constant along the land: $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta P}{L}$$ Substituting: $$Q \;=\; \frac{w h^3 \, \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$ Replacing $w = \pi D_m$ and $h = D_h/2$, with $D_h = D_o - D_i$: $$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi D_m \, D_h^3 \, \Delta P}{96 \mu L}$$ For the **thin-gap approximation** $D_m \approx D_o - D_i = D_h$, the formula simplifies to the canonical form: $$\boxed{\Delta P \;=\; \frac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L}{\pi \, D_h^3}}$$ ### Step 8 — Shear Stress at the Wall At $y = h/2$: $$\tau_w \;=\; \mu \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y}\bigg|_{y=h/2} \;=\; -\frac{h}{2}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \;=\; \frac{h \, \Delta P}{2L}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_w \;=\; \frac{h \, \Delta P}{2 L}}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem) > **Problem:** An annular die has outer diameter $D_o = 50 \text{ mm}$, mandrel diameter $D_i = 44 \text{ mm}$, and land length $L = 60 \text{ mm}$. A Newtonian polymer melt with viscosity $\mu = 350 \text{ Pa·s}$ is extruded at $Q = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Compute: > (a) The pressure drop across the land. > (b) The wall shear stress. > (c) The mean velocity and confirm laminar flow ($\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$). ### Solution **Given:** $D_o = 0.050 \text{ m}$, $D_i = 0.044 \text{ m}$, $L = 0.060 \text{ m}$, $\mu = 350 \text{ Pa·s}$, $Q = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **Step (a):** Hydraulic diameter: $$D_h = D_o - D_i = 0.050 - 0.044 = 0.006 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ mm}$$ Pressure drop: $$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 350 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.060}{\pi \times (0.006)^3}$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 350 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.060}{3.1416 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-7}}$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{8.82 \times 10^{-4}}{6.786 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.299 \times 10^{3} \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 1.30 \text{ kPa}}$$ **Step (b):** Wall shear stress: $$\tau_w = \frac{h \, \Delta P}{2L} = \frac{0.003 \times 1299}{2 \times 0.060}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_w \approx 32.5 \text{ Pa}}$$ **Step (c):** Cross-sectional area: $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0025 - 0.001936) = 4.429 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ Mean velocity: $$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{-6}}{4.429 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.90 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$ Reynolds number: $$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{v} D_h}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 7.90 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.006}{350} = 1.22 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{Re \ll 2300 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Flow is deeply laminar (creeping flow).} \;\checkmark}$$ ## 3.3 Design Constraint Equation — Linking Land Length to Pressure Drop For fixed die geometry and melt rheology, designers solve: $$L \;=\; \frac{\pi D_h^3 \Delta P_{allow}}{12 \mu Q}$$ where $\Delta P_{allow}$ is dictated by the **extruder screw capacity** (typically **$5$–$15$ MPa** for polymer extrusion). A **longer land** → more pressure drop → better surface finish but risk of **melt fracture** if $\tau_w$ exceeds critical value (typically **$0.1$ to $0.3$ MPa** for polyolefins). ## 3.4 Spider-Leg Analysis (Advanced) The number of spider legs $n$ affects both flow uniformity and weld-line strength. A common engineering rule: $$n_{optimum} \;=\; 3 \;\; \text{for pipes with } D_o \leq 110 \text{ mm}$$ $$n_{optimum} \;=\; 4 \text{ to } 6 \;\; \text{for } 110 \text{ mm} < D_o \leq 400 \text{ mm}$$ $$n_{optimum} \;=\; 8 \text{ to } 12 \;\; \text{for } D_o > 400 \text{ mm}$$ The **leg shadow** (the unmelted/stagnant region behind each leg) must be minimised by **streamlining the leg cross-section** (typically lenticular or airfoil shape rather than rectangular). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Annular Die Assembly ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Extruder Screw
Melt Feed P0] --> B[Adapter / Breaker Plate
Removes Swirl] B --> C[Manifold / Distributor
Spider or Spiral] C --> D[Approach / Converging Zone
Acceleration] D --> E[Land Region
Length L, Gap h] E --> F[Die Exit
Die Swell B] F --> G[Calibration Sleeve
Vacuum Sizing] G --> H[Cooling Bath
Spray or Tank] H --> I[Haul-Off Unit
Caterpillar Belts] I --> J[Cutter / Coiler
Pipe or Reel] K[Mandrel / Torpedo
Inner Body Di] --> C L[Die Body Housing
Outer Body Do] --> C M[Heater Bands
Temperature Control] --> E style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style E fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style F fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style J fill:#548235,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Geometry of the Annular Land ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Outer Wall
Diameter Do] --> B[Gap h
Land Region] B --> C[Inner Mandrel
Diameter Di] C --> D[Spider Leg 1] C --> E[Spider Leg 2] C --> F[Spider Leg 3] D --> G[Weld Line A] E --> H[Weld Line B] F --> I[Weld Line C] style A fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style B fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style D fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000 style E fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000 style F fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000 style G fill:#ed7d31,color:#000000 style H fill:#ed7d31,color:#000000 style I fill:#ed7d31,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Pressure–Velocity Profile Across the Land (Engineering Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Entry of Land
Pressure P0 high
Velocity flat] --> B[Mid-Land
Pressure P0 - dP/2
Parabolic Velocity] B --> C[Exit of Land
Pressure P0 - dP
Velocity recovers] C --> D[Free Extrudate
Die Swell B greater than 1
Elastic Recovery] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style B fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style C fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style D fill:#548235,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Spider-Type vs Spiral-Type Die ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Annular Die Family] --> B[Spider Type] A --> C[Spiral Mandrel Type] A --> D[Cross Head Type] B --> B1[3 to 12 Radial Legs] B --> B2[Discrete Weld Lines] B --> B3[Used for Rigid Pipe] C --> C1[Helical Distribution Grooves] C --> C2[No Weld Lines] C --> C3[Used for Blown Film] D --> D1[Tangential Feed] D --> D2[Concentric Guides] D --> D3[Used for Wire Coating] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style B fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style C fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style D fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define an annular die. State any two products manufactured using annular dies.** **Model Answer:** An annular die is an extrusion tooling component with a circular ring-shaped flow channel defined by an outer die body and an inner mandrel, producing a hollow profile. The flow passes through a thin annular gap of thickness $h = (D_o - D_i)/2$ over a land length $L$. Two products: 1. **PVC / HDPE pipes** (spider-type annular die). 2. **Wire insulation / cable sheathing** (cross-head annular die). *(Acceptable: blown film, medical tubing, irrigation laterals.)* — **Full 3 marks.** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is a weld line (knit line) in a spider-type annular die? How does it affect product quality?** **Model Answer:** A weld line is the seam formed where two or more melt flow fronts rejoin **downstream of a spider leg** that splits the melt circumferentially. **Effects on quality:** - Mechanical weakness (reduced tensile & burst strength by 10–30 %). - Surface blemish ("M-line" visible as a streak). - Potential site for stress concentration and premature failure. *Mitigation: streamline the leg cross-section, optimise number of legs, and use a spiral mandrel die for weld-line-free products.* — **Full 3 marks.** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **spider-type annular die** used for polymer pipe extrusion. Highlight the role of the mandrel, spider legs, and land. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Newtonian polymer melt of viscosity $\mu = 500 \text{ Pa·s}$ flows through an annular die with $D_o = 80 \text{ mm}$, $D_i = 70 \text{ mm}$, land length $L = 100 \text{ mm}$. The required volumetric output is $Q = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate: - (i) The pressure drop $\Delta P$ across the land. **(3 marks)** - (ii) The mean velocity $\bar{v}$ in the land. **(2 marks)** - (iii) The wall shear stress $\tau_w$. **(2 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a): Construction & Working (7 marks) **Constructional Features (to be labelled in the sketch — 3 marks):** 1. **Die body (housing)** — outer steel body, generally split for easy cleaning, with heater band ports. 2. **Mandrel (torpedo)** — inner steel core, mounted on **spider legs** (typically 3–4), forming the inner boundary of the annular channel. 3. **Spider legs** — streamlined supports that hold the mandrel concentric. Typically made of high-grade H13 tool steel, lenticular/airfoil-shaped. 4. **Land region** — the calibrated final cylindrical gap of length $L$ and gap $h$. 5. **Heater bands** — maintain melt temperature; prevent premature solidification. 6. **Pressure sensor port** — at entry to monitor $\Delta P$. **Working (4 marks):** - Polymer melt from the extruder enters the die adapter → flows around the mandrel, splitting into $n$ streams separated by spider legs → the streams rejoin at the **weld lines** immediately downstream of the legs → the recombined melt is then forced through the final annular land of gap $h$ → at exit, the extrudate undergoes **die swell**, increasing diameter by factor $B$ → the swelled tube is then calibrated, cooled, and hauled off. **Role of components:** - *Mandrel* → defines inner diameter. - *Spider legs* → provide mechanical support while allowing melt flow. - *Land* → meters, homogenises, and straightens the velocity profile. --- #### Part (b): Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** $D_o = 0.080$ m, $D_i = 0.070$ m, $L = 0.100$ m, $\mu = 500$ Pa·s, $Q = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s. **Hydraulic diameter:** $$D_h = D_o - D_i = 0.080 - 0.070 = 0.010 \text{ m}$$ **(i) Pressure drop:** $$\Delta P = \frac{12 \mu Q L}{\pi D_h^3} = \frac{12 \times 500 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.100}{\pi \times (0.010)^3}$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-3}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.29 \times 10^{3} \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 2.29 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{(3 marks)}}$$ **(ii) Mean velocity:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064 - 0.0049) = 1.178 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.178 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.019 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{v} \approx 0.0102 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(2 marks)}}$$ **(iii) Wall shear stress:** $$h = D_h / 2 = 0.005 \text{ m}$$ $$\tau_w = \frac{h \, \Delta P}{2 L} = \frac{0.005 \times 2292}{2 \times 0.100} = 57.3 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_w \approx 57.3 \text{ Pa} \quad \text{(2 marks)}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Compare **spider-type** and **spiral mandrel** annular dies with respect to (i) flow distribution, (ii) weld-line formation, (iii) typical applications, and (iv) cost. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An annular die has $D_o = 100 \text{ mm}$ and $D_i = 90 \text{ mm}$. For a Newtonian melt with $\mu = 400 \text{ Pa·s}$ and $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$, find the maximum volumetric flow rate $Q_{max}$ that keeps the flow laminar ($Re \leq 2300$). Assume land length $L = 120 \text{ mm}$. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a): Comparison Table (7 marks) | Criterion | Spider-Type | Spiral Mandrel | |---|---|---| | (i) Flow distribution | Discrete; depends on leg symmetry | Continuous via helical grooves; very uniform | | (ii) Weld lines | Present ($n$ weld lines for $n$ legs) | Essentially absent | | (iii) Applications | Rigid PVC pipe, HDPE tube | Blown film, large diameter pipe | | (iv) Cost | Low to moderate | High (CNC machining of spiral grooves) | *(Any equivalent answer with key distinctions earns full marks.)* --- #### Part (b): Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** $D_o = 0.100$ m, $D_i = 0.090$ m, $\mu = 400$ Pa·s, $\rho = 950$ kg/m³, $L = 0.120$ m, $Re_{max} = 2300$. **Hydraulic diameter:** $$D_h = 0.100 - 0.090 = 0.010 \text{ m}$$ **Cross-sectional area:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100^2 - 0.090^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0100 - 0.0081) = 1.492 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Mean velocity limit:** $$Re_{max} = \frac{\rho \, \bar{v}_{max} \, D_h}{\mu}$$ $$\bar{v}_{max} = \frac{Re_{max} \, \mu}{\rho \, D_h} = \frac{2300 \times 400}{950 \times 0.010}$$ $$\bar{v}_{max} = \frac{9.2 \times 10^{5}}{9.5} = 9.68 \times 10^{4} \text{ m/s} \quad \text{[excessively high — physically unattainable]}$$ > **Physical re-check:** The laminar limit is *not* the binding constraint here. The binding constraint is the maximum **allowable wall shear stress** before melt fracture. KTU students frequently default to $Re \leq 2300$ — but for highly viscous polymer melts, the practical limit is shear-stress driven. Therefore: **Shear-stress based limit:** Assuming critical wall shear stress $\tau_{w,crit} = 0.1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \times 10^5$ Pa for HDPE: $$\Delta P_{max} = \frac{2 L \, \tau_{w,crit}}{h} = \frac{2 \times 0.120 \times 1 \times 10^{5}}{0.005} = 4.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$ $$Q_{max} = \frac{\pi D_h^3 \Delta P_{max}}{12 \mu L} = \frac{\pi \times (0.010)^3 \times 4.8 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 400 \times 0.120}$$ $$Q_{max} = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-6} \times 4.8 \times 10^{6}}{576} = \frac{15.08}{576} = 2.62 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{max} \approx 2.62 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \text{(limited by melt fracture, not } Re\text{)}}$$ *(For full KTU marks, the student should explicitly state that the $Re$-based limit gives an unphysically high velocity, and the practical constraint is melt fracture.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the thin-gap assumption** $h \ll D_i$. Without it, the curvature correction is needed (Eriksson–Yang correction factor). KTU deducts 1 mark if not stated. > 2. **Mixing up $D_h$ with $D_i$ or $D_o$.** The hydraulic diameter of an annulus is $D_o - D_i$, **not** the average. This is the single most common arithmetic error. > 3. **Omitting units** in intermediate steps. Always show $\text{Pa·s} \times \text{m}^3/\text{s} \times \text{m} / \text{m}^3 = \text{Pa}$. > 4. **Drawing a flat rectangular slit** instead of an annular cross-section in the labelled diagram. The sketch MUST show concentric circles, not two parallel lines. > 5. **Not mentioning weld lines** in the spider-die description. It is the single most-tested sub-point. > 6. **Forgetting die swell** in numericals: real extrudate diameter is $B \cdot D_o$, not $D_o$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Annular Dies** - **Definition:** Annular die = ring-shaped extrusion die producing hollow profile; gap $h = (D_o - D_i)/2$. - **Hydraulic diameter:** $D_h = D_o - D_i = 2h$ — *not* the mean diameter. - **Land length range:** $8h \leq L \leq 15h$ — controls pressure drop and surface finish. - **Master formula:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu Q L}{\pi D_h^3}$ — derived from Poiseuille slit flow. - **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{h \Delta P}{2L}$ — must be below melt-fracture limit ($\sim 0.1$ MPa for HDPE). - **Weld lines** form downstream of each spider leg; minimise by streamlining legs or using spiral mandrel. - **Die swell** $B > 1$: extrudate expands elastically upon exit; calibration tooling must compensate. - **Material of construction:** H13 hot-work tool steel, HRC 48–52, polished to $R_a \leq 0.4 \, \mu m$. - **Number of spider legs:** 3 for small pipes, scaling up to 8–12 for large diameters. - **Assumption set (mandatory in derivations):** Newtonian, steady, laminar, isothermal, thin-gap, no-slip. - **Cross-sectional area:** $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$. - **Reynolds number check:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{v} D_h}{\mu} \ll 2300$ — confirm creeping flow regime. - **Industrial applications:** PVC/HDPE pipes, blown film, wire coating, medical tubing, irrigation laterals. - **Design trade-off:** longer land → better finish but higher pressure drop and risk of melt fracture. - **Key KTU sub-topics tested:** constructional features, derivation of $\Delta P$, weld-line analysis, die-swell correction, spider vs spiral comparison.

topics.title: Pipe Die Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pipe Die Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pipe Die** is a precision-engineered tooling component used in the **extrusion process** to continuously produce hollow tubular cross-sections (pipes and tubes) of uniform wall thickness, concentric geometry, and consistent surface finish. The die transforms a pressurized polymer melt (or metal billet, in case of hot extrusion) into a defined hollow profile by forcing the material through an annular orifice formed between an **outer die ring (die bushing)** and an **inner mandrel (torpedo/core)**.

In the context of **KTU PEPOT756 (Mould and Die Manufacture)**, Pipe Die Design encompasses the systematic calculation and selection of geometric parameters, pressure-flow relationships, land dimensions, mandrel support configurations, and thermal management features required to produce a dimensionally stable extruded pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pipe Die Design integrates concepts of polymer rheology, fluid mechanics, heat transfer, and tooling metallurgy. Mastery of this topic is essential for Part B (14-mark) problems in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste. If you press the tube in the middle, toothpaste comes out in a solid cylindrical stream. Now, imagine inserting a thin stick (like a chopstick) through the center of the tube opening before squeezing. The toothpaste now flows **around** the stick, forming a **hollow cylindrical stream** — a pipe!

This is exactly what a **Pipe Die** does:
- The **outer die body (die bushing)** forms the outer wall of the pipe.
- The **inner mandrel** forms the inner wall.
- The pressurized melt is forced into the **annular gap** between them.
- The **"stick" (mandrel)** must be held in place by some support — this is where the famous **spider legs** come in.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Every time the melt passes around a spider leg, the flow splits and re-joins on the other side, forming a **weld line (knit line)**. A weld line is a region of mechanical weakness. The designer's job is to manage these weld lines so that they don't compromise the pipe's strength or appearance.

### 1.3 Fundamental Components of a Pipe Die

A typical pipe die assembly consists of the following components, each with a specific function:

1. **Die Body / Housing** — The main structural shell that contains and aligns all internal components. Usually made from **hardened tool steel (e.g., H13, P20, AISI D2)** with hardness in the range of **58–62 HRC** for wear resistance.

2. **Breaker Plate / Screen Pack Support** — A perforated plate that supports the screen pack, straightens the melt flow, and converts turbulent flow into **laminar flow** with a more uniform velocity profile.

3. **Adapter / Transition Section** — A tapered region that channels the melt from the extruder barrel into the die body. The taper angle is typically **45° to 60°**.

4. **Mandrel (Torpedo / Core)** — The central component that defines the **inner diameter (ID)** of the pipe. It is precisely machined and ground to ensure concentricity with the outer die ring.

5. **Spider Legs / Fins** — Radial supports (typically **2 to 4 legs**) that hold the mandrel in perfect concentricity with the outer die body. The legs cause the melt to split and rejoin, creating weld lines.

6. **Die Land** — The straight, parallel, cylindrical section of length *L* that controls final pipe dimensions, flow uniformity, and surface finish. **Land Length (L) = 5 to 15 times the wall thickness (t)** is a standard rule of thumb.

7. **Die Lips (Inner Lip & Outer Lip)** — The final forming edges. The **outer lip diameter (D)** and **inner lip diameter (d)** define the annular gap and hence the **wall thickness** of the pipe.

8. **Air Vent / Bypass Channel** — A small channel that allows compressed air or vacuum to be introduced into the pipe interior for **dimensional stabilization** and **internal cooling**.

9. **Heating / Cooling Channels** — Cartridge heaters or oil circulation channels embedded in the die body to maintain **isothermal die temperature (T_d)** and prevent premature solidification.

### 1.4 Standard Design Metrics (Industry Benchmarks)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Die Land Length (L)** | $L = (5 \text{ to } 15) \times t$ |
| **Die Entry Angle ($\alpha$)** | $30°$ to $60°$ (commonly $45°$) |
| **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $2:1$ to $4:1$ (typical) |
| **Spider Leg Count** | 2 to 4 (usually 3 for symmetric flow) |
| **Spider Leg Width** | $w = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \times D_{mandrel}$ |
| **Spider Leg Taper** | Streamlined (aerodynamic profile) to minimize stagnation |
| **Pipe Tolerance** | $\pm 0.1$ mm to $\pm 0.5$ mm (depending on diameter) |
| **Concentricity** | Within $0.05$ mm for high-quality pipes |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "list the components of a pipe die," always mention the die body, mandrel, spider legs, die land, breaker plate, and air vent. Mentioning die lips separately fetches extra marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a Pipe Die

The pipe extrusion process follows a sequence of well-defined stages. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU exam answers.

**Step 1 — Material Entry:**
Polymer pellets/granules are fed into the extruder hopper. The rotating screw conveys, compresses, and melts the material through external barrel heaters and shear heating. The molten polymer (melt) exits the extruder as a high-pressure, high-temperature fluid.

**Step 2 — Flow Through Screen Pack & Breaker Plate:**
The melt passes through a **screen pack** (typically 20/40/60/80 mesh screens) and a **breaker plate**. This stage:
- Filters contaminants and unmelted particles.
- Converts the **rotational (helical) flow** from the screw tip into **linear axial flow**.
- Equalizes pressure and temperature fluctuations (improving pulsation damping).

**Step 3 — Flow Through the Adapter:**
The melt enters the **adapter zone**, a converging tapered channel that smoothly directs the flow from the extruder barrel cross-section into the annular region around the mandrel.

**Step 4 — Flow Through Spider Leg Region:**
This is the **most critical** region. The mandrel is supported by **N spider legs** (typically 2, 3, or 4). The melt flow is divided into **N streams** that pass around the legs and re-merge on the downstream side. Each merging zone creates a **weld line**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Weld Lines Form:** When two flow fronts meet, they cannot fully interdiffuse at the molecular level because the polymer melt is highly viscous. This results in a region with reduced mechanical strength, visible surface marks (especially in colored pipes), and potential leak paths.

**Step 5 — Flow Through Die Land:**
The merged flow passes through the **die land** (a straight parallel annular section of length *L*). The land:
- Stabilizes the flow profile.
- Resets the velocity distribution to a uniform plug-like profile.
- Allows final pressure drop and wall shear stress to develop.
- Improves dimensional accuracy and surface finish.

**Step 6 — Extrudate Exit & Die Swell:**
As the melt exits the die lips, it experiences a phenomenon called **die swell** (also known as **extrudate swell** or the **Barus effect**). The cross-section of the extrudate increases due to the recovery of elastic deformation stored in the melt during flow. The die gap must be designed smaller than the final desired wall thickness to compensate.

**Step 7 — Cooling, Sizing, Haul-off & Cutting:**
The molten pipe enters a **vacuum calibration tank** (sizing sleeve) where it is cooled by water spray and dimensional accuracy is locked in. The pipe is then pulled by **haul-off caterpillar belts** and cut to length by an **automatic saw**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Design Step

| Design Decision | The "Why" | The "How" |
|---|---|---|
| **Breaker plate** | To remove melt pulsations from the screw | Perforated plate with conical holes (60° included angle) |
| **Tapered adapter** | To transition flow from circular to annular | Die entry angle 30°–60° to avoid dead spots |
| **Spider legs (N=3)** | To support mandrel centrally | 3 legs give best balance: 2 legs create asymmetric flow, 4 legs create too many weld lines |
| **Streamlined leg shape** | To reduce stagnation & hold-up time | Aerofoil or tear-drop cross-section |
| **Die land (L = 10t)** | To allow flow re-conformation and to set final velocity profile | L too short → poor surface; L too long → excessive pressure drop |
| **Die swell compensation** | Extrudate swells 5%–30% upon exit | Die gap is undersized by the swell ratio factor |
| **Concentric mandrel** | Wall thickness uniformity | Mandrel held in die body with < 0.05 mm eccentricity |
| **Air vent** | To prevent pipe collapse and assist cooling | 1–3 mm diameter channel through mandrel base |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas tested in KTU ESE for this topic. Memorize these — they form the backbone of every numerical problem.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \left(R_o^4 - R_i^4\right)$ | Volumetric flow rate through annular die land (Hagen-Poiseuille) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R_o^3 \left[1 - \kappa^4 - \dfrac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]}$ | Wall shear rate in annular die (Rabinowitsch) | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R_o}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress in annular die | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 4 | $CR = \dfrac{V_{bulk}}{V_{land}}$ | Compression Ratio of the die | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $t_{pipe} = (D_o - D_i) / 2$ | Pipe wall thickness | $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $L = (5 \text{ to } 15) \cdot t$ | Die land length as a function of wall thickness | $\text{m}$ |
| 7 | $Gap = t_{pipe} / S$ | Die gap accounting for die swell factor $S$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 8 | $V_{av} = Q / A_{ann}$ | Average velocity of melt in die land | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{L}{V_{av}} = \dfrac{L \cdot A_{ann}}{Q}$ | Melt residence time in die land | $\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate | $\text{kg}/\text{s}$ |
| 11 | $\eta = \tau_w / \dot{\gamma}_w$ | Apparent melt viscosity | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 12 | $P_{spider} = \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2} \cdot N_{legs} \cdot C_d$ | Pressure loss across spider legs | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 13 | $Swell Ratio = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | Die swell ratio | Dimensionless |

Where:
- $R_o$ = outer radius of annular gap
- $R_i$ = inner radius of annular gap
- $\kappa = R_i / R_o$ (radius ratio)
- $\mu$ = melt viscosity
- $L$ = die land length
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across die land
- $D_o, D_i$ = outer and inner die diameters
- $A_{ann} = \pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ = annular cross-section area

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** For Part B numericals, examiners love testing Equation 1 and Equation 3 together. If you are given flow rate and asked to find pressure drop, use Equation 1 solved for $\Delta P$. If you are given pressure drop and asked to find flow rate, use Equation 1 directly. **Always show the substitution step-by-step.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pipe die design is the cornerstone of:
- **PVC / HDPE pipe manufacturing** for plumbing, irrigation, and gas distribution.
- **Medical tubing** (catheters, IV lines) requiring extremely tight tolerances.
- **Automotive hoses** (fuel lines, brake lines) requiring burst-pressure strength.
- **Optical fiber cable sheathing** requiring concentricity within microns.
- **Food-grade pipes** (dairy, beverage) requiring smooth internal surfaces.

The **compression ratio, land length, and spider leg design** are the three parameters that most directly determine pipe quality, throughput, and production economics.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Flow Rate Through an Annular Die Land (Hagen-Poiseuille for Annulus)

This is the **single most important derivation** in pipe die design and is frequently asked in KTU ESE for 7-mark sub-questions.

**Starting Point:** We consider a fully developed, laminar, steady-state, isothermal, Newtonian flow of melt through a concentric annular channel of length *L*, outer radius $R_o$, and inner radius $R_i$.

**Step 1 — Set up the Navier-Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates (z-direction).**
For fully developed flow with $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = 0$, and $v_z = v_z(r)$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dz}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the pressure gradient is constant along the land.**
Since flow is fully developed and the die land is horizontal:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = \frac{\Delta P}{L} = \text{constant}$$

Substituting into the equation:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$

**Step 3 — First integration (multiply both sides by *r* and integrate with respect to *r*).**

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} r^2 + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second integration with respect to *r*.**

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} r^2 + C_1 \ln(r) + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply the no-slip boundary conditions.**
At $r = R_o$ (outer wall): $v_z = 0$
At $r = R_i$ (inner wall): $v_z = 0$

This gives two equations for $C_1$ and $C_2$:

$$0 = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} R_o^2 + C_1 \ln(R_o) + C_2$$

$$0 = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} R_i^2 + C_1 \ln(R_i) + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Subtract the two equations to eliminate $C_2$.**

$$0 = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} (R_o^2 - R_i^2) + C_1 \ln\left(\frac{R_o}{R_i}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $C_1$.**

$$C_1 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \cdot \frac{R_o^2 - R_i^2}{\ln(R_o / R_i)}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back to obtain the velocity profile $v_z(r)$.**

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left[ r^2 - R_i^2 - \frac{(R_o^2 - R_i^2) \ln(r / R_i)}{\ln(R_o / R_i)} \right]$$

**Step 9 — Compute the volumetric flow rate $Q$ by integrating over the annular cross-section.**

$$Q = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 10 — Perform the integration.** After careful algebra (using integration by parts and standard logarithmic identities):

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \left[ R_o^4 - R_i^4 - \frac{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)^2}{\ln(R_o / R_i)} \right]$$

**Step 11 — Final simplified form using radius ratio $\kappa = R_i / R_o$.**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \cdot R_o^4 \cdot \left[ 1 - \kappa^4 - \frac{(1 - \kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)} \right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** A useful limiting case is when the gap is very thin compared to the radius ($R_o \approx R_i \approx R$, gap $h = R_o - R_i \ll R$). The expression reduces to the simpler **slit-flow approximation**:
> $$Q \approx \frac{\pi D h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$
> This is often the form examiners use in numerical problems to simplify calculations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wall Shear Stress in an Annular Die

**Step 1 — Start from the velocity profile obtained in Step 8 of the previous derivation.**

Differentiate $v_z(r)$ with respect to *r*:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left[ 2r - \frac{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}{r \ln(R_o / R_i)} \right]$$

**Step 2 — The wall shear stress is $\tau = \mu \left( dv_z / dr \right)$ evaluated at the wall.**

At the outer wall ($r = R_o$):

$$\tau_w = \mu \cdot \frac{dv_z}{dr}\bigg|_{R_o} = \frac{\Delta P}{4L} \left[ 2R_o - \frac{R_o^2 - R_i^2}{R_o \ln(R_o / R_i)} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using $\kappa = R_i / R_o$.**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R_o}{2L} \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{1 - \kappa^2}{2 \ln(1/\kappa)} \right]$$

For thin gaps where $\kappa \to 1$, the bracketed term $\to 1$, giving:

$$\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Many students lose marks by writing $\tau_w = \Delta P R / 2L$ without stating the thin-gap assumption. Always specify "for a thin annular gap ($h \ll R$)" to fetch full marks.

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A pipe die is to be designed to extrude an HDPE pipe of outer diameter 50 mm and wall thickness 4 mm. The polymer melt has viscosity $\mu = 500$ Pa·s at the processing temperature. The required production rate is $Q = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s. Calculate: (a) The pressure drop across the die land, and (b) The wall shear stress. Assume the die land length is $L = 10 \times t = 40$ mm.

**Given Data:**
- $D_o = 50$ mm → $R_o = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m
- $t = 4$ mm → $R_i = R_o - t = 25 - 4 = 21$ mm = $0.021$ m
- $\mu = 500$ Pa·s
- $Q = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s
- $L = 40$ mm = $0.040$ m

**Solution — Part (a): Pressure Drop $\Delta P$**

**Step 1 — Compute the radius ratio.**

$$\kappa = \frac{R_i}{R_o} = \frac{0.021}{0.025} = 0.84$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric term inside the brackets.**

$$1 - \kappa^4 = 1 - (0.84)^4 = 1 - 0.4979 = 0.5021$$

$$(1 - \kappa^2)^2 = (1 - 0.7056)^2 = (0.2944)^2 = 0.0867$$

$$\ln(1/\kappa) = \ln(1/0.84) = \ln(1.1905) = 0.1744$$

$$\frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)} = \frac{0.0867}{0.1744} = 0.4971$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracketed factor.**

$$1 - \kappa^4 - \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)} = 0.5021 - 0.4971 = 0.0050$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Hagen-Poiseuille equation solved for $\Delta P$.**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L} R_o^4 \cdot (0.0050)$$

Rearranging:

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R_o^4 \cdot (0.0050)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \times 500 \times 0.040 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.025)^4 \times 0.0050}$$

Numerator:

$$8 \times 500 \times 0.040 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} = 3.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

Denominator:

$$\pi \times (0.025)^4 \times 0.0050 = \pi \times 3.906 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.0050 = 6.135 \times 10^{-9}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta P = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-3}}{6.135 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.215 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 0.522 \text{ MPa}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Wall Shear Stress $\tau_w$**

For a thin gap (h = 4 mm, R = 23 mm, so h/R ≈ 0.17 — reasonable approximation):

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{5.215 \times 10^{5} \times 0.023}{2 \times 0.040}$$

$$\tau_w = \frac{1.199 \times 10^{4}}{0.080} = 1.499 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 150 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:**
- (a) $\Delta P \approx 0.52$ MPa
- (b) $\tau_w \approx 150$ kPa

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** For HDPE extrusion, typical die pressures are 5–15 MPa. A value of 0.52 MPa is on the lower end, suggesting either a low-viscosity grade or a relatively open die. This validates our calculation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Design Parameter Effects

| If this parameter INCREASES... | Then $Q$ (flow rate) | Then $\Delta P$ (pressure drop) | Then $\tau_w$ (shear stress) | Quality Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Die land length $L$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Better surface, higher motor load |
| Wall thickness $t$ (gap) | $\uparrow$ drastically | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Higher throughput, lower pressure |
| Melt viscosity $\mu$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Slower flow, sharper extrudate |
| Radius ratio $\kappa$ (thinner wall) | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | More pressure-sensitive |
| Number of spider legs $N$ | $\downarrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | More weld lines (weaker) |
| Production rate $Q$ | — (independent) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Risk of melt fracture |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pipe Die Assembly

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional interactions and flow path of polymer melt through a complete pipe die assembly. This serves as a comprehensive block schematic suitable for KTU exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Extruder Screw Tip] -->|Melt exits under pressure| B[Screen Pack]
    B -->|Filtration and flow straightening| C[Breaker Plate]
    C -->|Laminar flow established| D[Adapter Zone]
    D -->|Tapered transition 45 to 60 deg| E[Spider Leg Region]
    E -->|Flow splits around N legs| F[Mandrel Supported by Spider Legs]
    F -->|Flow re-merges with weld lines| G[Die Land Annular Section]
    G -->|L equals 5 to 15 times wall thickness| H[Die Lips Outer and Inner]
    H -->|Melt exits with die swell| I[Vacuum Calibration Tank]
    I -->|Cooling and sizing| J[Haul-off Unit]
    J -->|Pulls pipe at constant speed| K[Saw Cutting Unit]
    L[Air Vent Channel] -->|Provides internal air pressure| F
    M[Heater Cartridges] -->|Maintain isothermal temperature| D
    M -->|Temperature control| G
    N[Cooling Water Jackets] -->|Remove excess heat| I
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Breaker Plate Flow Straightening]

    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Spider Leg Flow Splitting]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: Mandrel Reshaping of Flow]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A1[Outer Die Ring OD] --> A2[Outer Die Land]

```
