## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a "Pipe Die" and list any four of its main components.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **pipe die** is a precision extrusion tooling assembly used to produce continuous hollow tubular profiles (pipes) of uniform wall thickness by forcing a polymer melt through an annular orifice formed between an outer die ring and an inner mandrel. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Four main components:** **(2 marks — 0.5 each)**
> 1. **Die body (housing)** — Provides structural support and alignment.
> 2. **Mandrel (core/torpedo)** — Defines the inner diameter of the pipe.
> 3. **Spider legs** — Support the mandrel concentrically within the die body.
> 4. **Die land** — Straight parallel section that controls final dimensions and surface finish.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a "weld line" in a pipe die? How is it formed, and what is its significance?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **weld line** (also called a **knit line**) is a region on the extruded pipe where two or more melt streams re-merge after being separated by a spider leg. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Formation:** When polymer melt flows around the spider legs supporting the mandrel, the flow is divided into multiple streams. On the downstream side of each leg, these streams recombine, but the polymer chains cannot fully interdiffuse due to the high viscosity of the melt, leaving a microscopically weak interface. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Significance:** Weld lines are regions of **reduced mechanical strength**, potential **visual defects** (especially visible in coloured pipes), and possible **leak paths**. Die designers minimize their impact by using streamlined leg profiles, ensuring adequate land length, and optimizing the number of spider legs (preferably 3). **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Question]**
> *(a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a pipe die used in polymer extrusion. List the functions of the breaker plate, mandrel, and die land.* **(7 marks)**
> *(b) A pipe die has an outer die diameter of 60 mm and a mandrel diameter of 50 mm. The polymer melt has a viscosity of 800 Pa·s and the die land length is 50 mm. If the production rate is $3 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s, calculate the pressure drop across the die land using the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for annular flow.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

The pipe die consists of the following major components (refer to Section 1.3 and the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4 for the schematic):

1. **Die body** — hardened tool steel housing containing all components. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Breaker plate** — perforated plate that supports the screen pack, removes melt pulsations, and straightens flow. **(1 mark)**
3. **Adapter zone** — tapered transition from circular barrel to annular channel. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Mandrel** — central core defining the inner diameter of the pipe. **(1 mark)**
5. **Spider legs (2 to 4)** — radial supports holding the mandrel concentrically. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Die land** — straight parallel section controlling final dimensions and surface finish. **(1 mark)**
7. **Die lips (inner and outer)** — final forming edges defining the wall thickness. **(0.5 mark)**
8. **Air vent** — channel through the mandrel base to supply internal air pressure. **(0.5 mark)**
9. **Working principle** — molten polymer is forced under pressure from the extruder, passes through the screen pack and breaker plate, divides around spider legs, re-merges in the die land, and exits through the annular gap as a hollow pipe. **(1 mark)**
10. **Neat sketch** — must be included. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — Pressure Drop Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_o = 60$ mm → $R_o = 30$ mm = $0.030$ m
- $D_i = 50$ mm → $R_i = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m
- $\mu = 800$ Pa·s
- $L = 50$ mm = $0.050$ m
- $Q = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Compute the radius ratio.**

$$\kappa = \frac{R_i}{R_o} = \frac{0.025}{0.030} = 0.8333$$

**Step 2 — Compute geometric terms.**

$$1 - \kappa^4 = 1 - (0.8333)^4 = 1 - 0.4823 = 0.5177$$

$$(1 - \kappa^2)^2 = (1 - 0.6944)^2 = (0.3056)^2 = 0.0934$$

$$\ln(1/\kappa) = \ln(1/0.8333) = \ln(1.2) = 0.1823$$

$$\frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)} = \frac{0.0934}{0.1823} = 0.5124$$

**Step 3 — Bracketed factor.**

$$1 - \kappa^4 - \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)} = 0.5177 - 0.5124 = 0.0053$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange the Hagen-Poiseuille equation.**

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R_o^4 \left[ 1 - \kappa^4 - \dfrac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)} \right]}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values.**

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \times 800 \times 0.050 \times 3 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.030)^4 \times 0.0053}$$

Numerator:

$$8 \times 800 \times 0.050 \times 3 \times 10^{-5} = 9.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

Denominator:

$$\pi \times (0.030)^4 \times 0.0053 = \pi \times 8.1 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.0053 = 1.349 \times 10^{-8}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta P = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.349 \times 10^{-8}} = 7.117 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 0.712 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta P \approx 0.71$ MPa **(1 mark for correct answer)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Question]**
> *(a) Discuss the role of spider legs in a pipe die. Explain with a diagram the formation of weld lines. What design measures are adopted to minimize the adverse effects of weld lines?* **(7 marks)**
> *(b) For a thin-walled pipe die, the die land length is 30 mm and the wall thickness is 3 mm. The melt viscosity is 600 Pa·s. If the allowable pressure drop is 1 MPa, calculate the maximum volumetric flow rate using the simplified slit-flow approximation.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Spider Legs and Weld Lines (7 Marks):**

1. **Role of spider legs:** Spider legs support the **mandrel** in perfect concentricity with the outer die ring. Without them, the mandrel would deflect under melt pressure, causing non-uniform wall thickness. **(1 mark)**
2. **Typical count:** 2 to 4 legs, with **3 legs preferred** for symmetric flow balance. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Weld line formation (with diagram):** When melt encounters a spider leg, the flow splits into two streams. After passing around the leg, the streams re-merge in the **rejoining zone**. Due to incomplete chain interdiffusion in the high-viscosity melt, a **microscopically weak interface** — the weld line — is formed. **(2 marks — including 1 mark for diagram)**
4. **Adverse effects of weld lines:** Reduced tensile strength, visible surface marks, potential leak paths, and lower burst pressure. **(1 mark)**
5. **Design measures to minimize weld line effects:** **(2.5 marks)**
   - Use **streamlined (aerofoil) leg profile** to reduce stagnation.
   - Choose **3 legs** for symmetric flow.
   - Provide **adequate die land length** ($L = 10t$ to $15t$) for chain interdiffusion.
   - Maintain **uniform die temperature** to reduce viscosity at the weld zone.
   - Use **spiral mandrel dies** for critical applications (eliminates weld lines entirely).

**Part (b) — Maximum Volumetric Flow Rate (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 30$ mm = $0.030$ m
- $t = 3$ mm = $0.003$ m (gap $h$)
- $\mu = 600$ Pa·s
- $\Delta P_{max} = 1$ MPa = $10^6$ Pa
- For thin-walled approximation: $D \approx D_o \approx 50$ mm = $0.050$ m (assumed outer diameter)

**Step 1 — Apply the simplified slit-flow equation for thin annular gap.**

$$Q = \frac{\pi D h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 0.050 \times (0.003)^3 \times 10^6}{12 \times 600 \times 0.030}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator.**

$$\pi \times 0.050 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^6 = \pi \times 1.35 \times 10^{-3} = 4.241 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator.**

$$12 \times 600 \times 0.030 = 216$$

**Step 5 — Compute Q.**

$$Q = \frac{4.241 \times 10^{-3}}{216} = 1.963 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_{max} \approx 1.96 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s **(1 mark for correct answer)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Pipe Die Design Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing wall thickness *t* with die gap *h*:** The die gap is **smaller than the final pipe wall thickness** because of **die swell**. If the problem gives you the *pipe* wall thickness, you must divide by the swell ratio to obtain the *die* gap. Students routinely lose 1–2 marks by mixing these up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units consistency:** All dimensions must be in **metres (m)**, viscosity in **Pa·s**, and pressure in **Pa**. Mixing mm with m is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers.
>
> 3. **Using the circular Hagen-Poiseuille equation for pipes:** Students often write $Q = \pi \Delta P R^4 / 8\mu L$ which is valid for a **solid cylindrical** flow, not for an **annular** pipe die flow. The correct equation has the $R_o^4 - R_i^4$ term.
>
> 4. **Omitting the diagram:** KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** specifically for a labelled sketch. Even a rough block diagram showing melt flow from extruder → breaker plate → spider legs → die land → pipe exit fetches full marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring weld line discussion:** Any question on pipe die design implicitly expects a brief mention of weld lines. A 2–3 line discussion can earn bonus marks.
>
> 6. **Land length formula not quoted:** Always state the rule of thumb "$L = (5 \text{ to } 15) \times t$" when land length is asked or assumed. Examiners reward rule-of-thumb justifications.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pipe die** = annular extrusion tool consisting of outer die body + inner mandrel supported by spider legs.
- **Melt flow path:** Screw → Screen pack → Breaker plate → Adapter → Spider legs → Die land → Die lips → Pipe exit.
- **Die land length rule:** $L = (5 \text{ to } 15) \times t$ where $t$ is the pipe wall thickness.
- **Spider legs:** Typically 3 legs, streamlined profile, to support mandrel and minimize weld line impact.
- **Weld lines** are formed where melt re-merges after passing spider legs — they are weak points.
- **Die swell** causes the extrudate to expand after exit; die gap must be undersized to compensate.
- **Compression ratio** is typically 2:1 to 4:1.
- **Hagen-Poiseuille equation for annular flow:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{8\mu L}(R_o^4 - R_i^4)$ — basic form.
- **Full annular form:** includes the $\dfrac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}$ correction term.
- **Thin-gap approximation:** $Q \approx \dfrac{\pi D h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ — used when $h \ll R$.
- **Wall shear stress (thin gap):** $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$.
- **Die materials:** Hardened tool steel (H13, D2) with **58–62 HRC** hardness.
- **Three critical design parameters:** Compression ratio, land length, and spider leg configuration.
- **Air vent** through the mandrel supplies internal pressure to prevent pipe collapse and assist cooling.
- **Vacuum calibration tank** downstream sets the final pipe dimensions and provides cooling.
- **Higher land length** → better surface finish, but higher pressure drop and motor load.
- **Higher flow rate** → higher pressure drop, higher shear stress, risk of melt fracture.
- **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in 14-mark answers for 1–2 easy marks.
- **Always state units** (Pa, m, m³/s) to avoid silly deductions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wire Coating Die
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wire Coating Die

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wire Coating Die** (also termed a **Cross-Head Die** or **Wire Jacketing Die**) is a specialized polymer extrusion die assembly used to uniformly deposit a thermoplastic or elastomeric insulating/sheathing layer over a moving metallic conductor (copper, aluminum, or fiber optic core). The wire is drawn continuously through the die in a cross-extrusion configuration, where the melt flows around the wire at approximately **90°** to the wire travel direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A wire coating die is a cross-head extrusion tool designed to coat a continuous metallic wire or cable with a polymeric insulating jacket. The die consists of a **mandrel (core)**, **die plate (orifice ring)**, **guide tip**, **heater bands**, and **breaker plate**, all assembled in a cross-head body oriented perpendicular to the wire payoff axis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hollow Paint Roller for Wires"

Imagine a **fountain pen tip**: ink (molten plastic) flows through a narrow channel and coats the central writing ball (the moving wire) as it slides past. Now scale this concept up:

- The **wire** is pulled horizontally through a die mounted sideways.
- **Molten polymer** is pushed (extruded) from the side at high pressure.
- The polymer wraps around the wire and exits as a smooth, concentric coating.
- This is exactly how the **insulation on your home's electrical wiring (PVC coating)** is manufactured.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike profile or sheet extrusion where the polymer exits into still air, in wire coating the **substrate (wire) is the moving mandrel** — making this a unique **substrate-supported forming process** in polymer processing.

## 1.3 Industrial Importance & Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Production line speed | **300 – 2500 m/min** |
| Coating thickness | **0.1 – 5 mm** |
| Wire diameter range | **0.05 – 25 mm** |
| Melt pressure | **5 – 50 MPa** |
| Land length | **5 – 15× coating thickness** |
| Die land diameter ratio | typically **1.2 – 2.0× final OD** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wire Coating Dies

Wire coating dies are broadly classified into **two fundamental configurations**, both examined frequently in KTU ESE papers.

### 2.1.1 Tubing Die (Jacketing Type / Free Extrusion)

- The melt exits the die into **open atmosphere**.
- The wire **draws down** the molten tube onto itself via vacuum or tension.
- Coating diameter is **slightly larger** than the die bore (typical drawdown ratio 1.1:1 to 1.5:1).
- Used for **low-viscosity polymers** and where concentricity tolerances are relaxed (e.g., cable sheathing, jacketing).

> [!NOTE]
> The die here functions like a **circular curtain** — the polymer forms a tube, and the wire vacuums inside it.

### 2.1.2 Pressure Die (Jacketing Type / Forced Conformance)

- The melt is held under **internal pressure** within the die land.
- The wire is forced through the polymer under **melt pressure**, ensuring tight concentricity.
- Requires precise **pressure balance** between melt hydrostatic pressure and wire tension.
- Used for **thin insulation** layers, **high-speed lines**, and **tight tolerance applications** (e.g., magnet wire, coaxial cable dielectric).

### 2.2 Critical Design Parameters

1. **Draw Down Ratio (DDR):**
   $$\text{DDR} = \frac{D_{\text{die}}}{D_{\text{coating}}}$$
   Where $D_{\text{die}}$ = die land bore diameter and $D_{\text{coating}}$ = final coated wire OD.

2. **Draw Ratio Balance (DRB):**
   $$\text{DRB} = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_c^2 - d^2} = 1$$
   Where $D_o$ = outer die diameter, $D_i$ = inner mandrel tip diameter, $D_c$ = coating final OD, $d$ = bare wire diameter.

3. **Pressure Drop Across the Die Land** (using Poiseuille-type flow for annular geometry):
   $$\Delta P = \frac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L}{\pi \, \rho \, g_c} \cdot \frac{1}{(R^2 - r^2)^3} \left[ (R^2 - r^2) - \frac{(R^2 - r^2)^2}{\ln(R/r)} \right]$$
   Where $\mu$ = melt viscosity, $Q$ = volumetric flow, $L$ = land length, $R$ = outer radius, $r$ = inner radius.

4. **Coating Thickness:**
   $$t = \frac{D_c - d}{2}$$

5. **Line Speed Relation:**
   $$V_{\text{line}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi (D_c^2 - d^2)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Unit | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_o$ | Outer die bore diameter | mm | $1.2\,D_c \leq D_o \leq 2.0\,D_c$ |
| $D_i$ | Mandrel tip diameter | mm | $D_i \geq d + 2(0.05\text{ to }0.5)$ |
| $L$ | Die land length | mm | $5t \leq L \leq 15t$ |
| DDR | Draw down ratio | dimensionless | $1.1 \leq \text{DDR} \leq 1.5$ (tubing); $\approx 1$ (pressure) |
| DRB | Draw ratio balance | dimensionless | $\approx 1.0 \pm 5\%$ |
| $\Delta P$ | Melt pressure drop | MPa | $5 \leq \Delta P \leq 50$ |
| $V_{\text{line}}$ | Wire haul-off speed | m/min | depends on extruder output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem is posed, ALWAYS check that **DRB = 1.0** before declaring the design valid. A deviation of more than **±5%** signals eccentricity or melt starvation.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Electrical & Electronics Industry:** PVC, PE, XLPE insulation on conductors.
- **Telecommunications:** Foam-skin dielectric on coaxial cables.
- **Automotive:** Primary wire harness insulation (cross-linked PE).
- **Optical Fiber:** Tight-buffer coating using UV-curable acrylates in pressure-type dies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Draw Ratio Balance for a Wire Coating Die

### Statement
For a **concentric, defect-free coating**, the volumetric flow rate of polymer approaching the die land must equal the volumetric rate of coating being hauled away on the wire.

### Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate Approaching the Die

The annular cross-section of melt in the die land has area:
$$
A_{\text{land}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right)
$$

Average velocity of melt in the land:
$$
\bar{v}_{\text{melt}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{land}}}
$$

Therefore volumetric flow:
$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right) \bar{v}_{\text{melt}}
$$

### Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate on the Coated Wire

The annular coated wire has outer diameter $D_c$ over bare wire diameter $d$. Cross-sectional area of the coating:
$$
A_{\text{coat}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_c^2 - d^2\right)
$$

The polymer moves at the **line speed** $V_{\text{line}}$ (no slip assumption). Therefore:
$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_c^2 - d^2\right) V_{\text{line}}
$$

### Step 3 — Equating the Two Flow Rates (Mass Conservation)

$$
\frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right) \bar{v}_{\text{melt}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_c^2 - d^2\right) V_{\text{line}}
$$

### Step 4 — Rearranging into the Draw Ratio Balance

Cancel $\pi/4$ and rearrange:
$$
\frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_c^2 - d^2} = \frac{V_{\text{line}}}{\bar{v}_{\text{melt}}} = \text{DRB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{DRB} = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_c^2 - d^2} = 1.0}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **pressure die**, DRB is enforced to be exactly **1.0** because the polymer conforms inside the die itself. For a **tubing die**, DRB $> 1.0$ accounts for drawdown in air.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Problem:** A copper wire of diameter **$d = 2.0$ mm** is to be coated with PVC to a final outer diameter of **$D_c = 4.0$ mm** in a pressure die. If the mandrel tip has diameter **$D_i = 2.6$ mm**, determine the required outer die bore diameter $D_o$ for perfect concentric coating.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify knowns:**
$d = 2.0$ mm, $D_c = 4.0$ mm, $D_i = 2.6$ mm

**Step 2 — Apply DRB = 1.0:**
$$
D_o^2 - D_i^2 = D_c^2 - d^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$
D_o^2 - (2.6)^2 = (4.0)^2 - (2.0)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Square the terms:**
$$
D_o^2 - 6.76 = 16.00 - 4.00
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$
D_o^2 - 6.76 = 12.00
$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**
$$
D_o^2 = 12.00 + 6.76 = 18.76
$$

$$
D_o = \sqrt{18.76} = 4.331 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 7 — Verification of Land Geometry

Coating thickness $t = (D_c - d)/2 = (4.0 - 2.0)/2 = 1.0$ mm.
Annular gap $= (D_o - D_i)/2 = (4.331 - 2.6)/2 = 0.866$ mm.
Land length (taking $L = 10t$): $L = 10 \times 1.0 = 10$ mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Check:** The annular gap (0.866 mm) is less than the final coating thickness (1.0 mm), confirming this is indeed a **pressure die** configuration where the polymer is compressed onto the wire.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Flowchart Logic

| Decision Variable | Tubing Die | Pressure Die |
|---|---|---|
| Coating thickness accuracy | ±10% | ±2% |
| Concentricity tolerance | Loose | Tight |
| Typical land length | $L = 5t$ to $10t$ | $L = 10t$ to $15t$ |
| Required melt pressure | 5–15 MPa | 15–50 MPa |
| DRB value | 1.1 – 1.5 | ≈ 1.00 |
| Application | Cable jacketing | Magnet wire, coax |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Wire Coating Die Operation

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **functional architecture** of a wire coating extrusion line and the interaction of die components during operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wire Payoff Reel] --> B[Wire Tensioner and Capstan]
    B --> C[Wire Preheater]
    C --> D[Cross Head Die Assembly]
    E[Extruder Hopper] --> F[Barrel and Screw]
    F --> G[Heated Breaker Plate]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Melt Channel Around Mandrel]
    H --> I[Die Land Annular Orifice]
    I --> J[Coated Wire Exits Die]
    J --> K[Cooling Trough Water Spray]
    K --> L[Diameter Gauge and Spark Tester]
    L --> M[Haul Off Capstan]
    M --> N[Take Up Spool]
    D --> D1[Mandrel Core Holds Wire Centered]
    D --> D2[Guide Tip Aligns Wire Entry]
    D --> D3[Heater Bands Maintain Melt Temp]
```

### 4.2 Component Interaction Description

1. **Wire Payoff Reel (A)** → Unspools bare conductor at controlled tension.
2. **Preheater (C)** → Removes moisture, prevents melt-quench shocks at the die entrance.
3. **Extruder (F)** → Melts and pressurizes the polymer resin.
4. **Cross Head Die (D)** → Orthogonal junction where **melt meets wire**.
5. **Mandrel Core (D1)** → Internal cone-shaped pin around which the melt flows.
6. **Guide Tip (D2)** → Precisely centers the wire as it enters the die bore.
7. **Die Land (I)** → Final sizing orifice where pressure drop and final dimensions are established.
8. **Cooling Trough (K)** → Solidifies the polymer jacket via water immersion.
9. **Spark Tester (L)** → Detects insulation pinholes via high-voltage DC.
10. **Take Up Spool (N)** → Winds finished coated wire for downstream use.

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Verbal Diagram)

Since wire coating die geometry is intricate (concentric cones, mandrel tips, breaker plates), here is a textual schematic representation:

```
           POLYMER INLET (from extruder)
                     │
                     ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
   │         CROSS-HEAD BODY             │
   │                                     │
   │        ┌──────────┐                 │
   │        │ MANDREL  │                 │
   │        │  CORE    │◄──── WIRE INLET │
   │        │  (cone)  │                 │
   │        └────┬─────┘                 │
   │             │  ← annular gap        │
   │     ┌───────┴───────┐               │
   │     │   DIE LAND    │               │
   │     │  (sizing zone)│               │
   │     └───────┬───────┘               │
   │             │                       │
   └─────────────┼───────────────────────┘
                 ▼
         COATED WIRE OUTLET
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between a tubing (jacketing) die and a pressure die used in wire coating.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Tubing Die | Pressure Die |
|---|---|---|
| Melt exits into | Open atmosphere | Confined die land |
| Coating formation | By drawdown in air | By forced conformance under pressure |
| Draw Ratio Balance (DRB) | > 1.0 (1.1 – 1.5) | ≈ 1.0 |
| Concentricity | Lower accuracy | High accuracy |
| Typical application | Cable jacketing | Magnet wire, thin insulation |
| Melt pressure | Low (5–15 MPa) | High (15–50 MPa) |

*(Award 1.5 marks for tabular comparison, 1 mark for application differentiation, 0.5 mark for DRB values.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the Draw Ratio Balance (DRB) in wire coating die design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Draw Ratio Balance (DRB) is the **ratio of the cross-sectional area of polymer in the die land to the cross-sectional area of the final coating**:

$$
\text{DRB} = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_c^2 - d^2}
$$

A DRB value of **1.0** ensures:
1. Mass conservation — no polymer starvation or excess.
2. Concentric coating with **uniform wall thickness**.
3. Stable melt flow without **die-lip buildup** or **shark-skin defects**.

Deviation from unity causes eccentricity, varying insulation thickness, and potential dielectric breakdown in finished cables.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **pressure-type wire coating die**. List the materials commonly used for its key components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A pressure wire coating die consists of:
1. **Cross-head body** — machined from **H13 tool steel** or **D2 die steel**, hardened to **58–62 HRC**.
2. **Mandrel (core)** — tapered pin (same tool steel), highly polished (Ra ≤ 0.1 μm), with a guide tip at its nose.
3. **Die plate (orifice ring)** — removable, hardened stainless steel, defines final coating OD.
4. **Heater bands** — band heaters or cartridge heaters (cartridge preferred for uniformity).
5. **Breaker plate and screen pack** — at melt inlet, ensures uniform flow and filters contaminants.
6. **Guide tip holder** — aligns wire concentric to the die axis.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
- Molten polymer enters the cross-head **perpendicular** to the wire direction.
- It flows around the mandrel and through the annular die land.
- The wire enters through the guide tip and travels axially through the die bore.
- Melt is held under pressure against the wire, conforming to it precisely.
- Coated wire exits through the die land, enters a cooling trough.

**Materials (1 mark):**
- Body, mandrel, die plate: **H13 / D2 tool steel**.
- Heaters: **Ni-Cr resistance wire** in ceramic insulators.

**(b)** A copper wire of diameter **1.5 mm** is to be coated with **LDPE** to a final diameter of **3.5 mm** using a pressure die. If the mandrel tip diameter is **2.0 mm** and the line speed is **600 m/min**, determine: **(7 marks)**
   1. The required outer die bore diameter.
   2. The volumetric flow rate of polymer in cm³/s.
   3. Whether this is a pressure or tubing die (justify with DRB).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
$d = 1.5$ mm, $D_c = 3.5$ mm, $D_i = 2.0$ mm, $V_{\text{line}} = 600$ m/min.

**Step 2 — Outer die bore from DRB = 1.0:**
$$
D_o^2 = D_c^2 - d^2 + D_i^2 = (3.5)^2 - (1.5)^2 + (2.0)^2
$$
$$
D_o^2 = 12.25 - 2.25 + 4.00 = 14.00
$$
$$
D_o = \sqrt{14.00} = 3.742 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate:**
$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_c^2 - d^2\right) V_{\text{line}}
$$
$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4}(12.25 - 2.25)\text{ mm}^2 \times 600\text{ m/min}
$$
$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4}(10.0)\text{ mm}^2 \times 10{,}000\text{ mm/s} \quad [\text{since } 600\text{ m/min} = 10\text{ m/s} = 10{,}000\text{ mm/s}]
$$

$$
Q = 7.854 \times 10{,}000 = 78{,}540 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} = 78.54 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify DRB and die classification:**
$$
\text{DRB} = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_c^2 - d^2} = \frac{14.00 - 4.00}{10.00} = \frac{10.00}{10.00} = 1.0
$$

Since **DRB = 1.0** and the annular gap $(D_o - D_i)/2 = 0.871$ mm is less than final coating thickness $t = 1.0$ mm, this is a **pressure die** configuration.

**Final Answers:**
1. $D_o = 3.742$ mm
2. $Q = 78.54$ cm³/s
3. Pressure die (DRB = 1.0)

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **classification of wire coating dies** with neat sketches. Discuss the relative merits and demerits of each type. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (3 marks):**
Wire coating dies are classified into:

1. **Tubing Die (Free Extrusion Die)** — polymer exits into atmosphere, drawdown in air.
2. **Pressure Die (Forced Conformance Die)** — polymer conforms to wire inside die land.

**Merits and Demerits — Tubing Die (2 marks):**

| Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|
| Simpler construction | Lower concentricity |
| Lower melt pressure required | Drawdown marks possible |
| Suitable for thick jackets (>1 mm) | Not for thin insulation |
| Lower wear on die land | Spark-test failures common |

**Merits and Demerits — Pressure Die (2 marks):**

| Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|
| High concentricity (±2%) | Complex mandrel design |
| Suitable for thin coatings (<0.5 mm) | Higher melt pressure required |
| No drawdown marks | Sensitive to melt viscosity changes |
| Excellent for high-speed lines | Higher tooling cost |

**(b)** During a wire coating trial, the operator reports **shark-skin melt fracture** on the coating surface at high line speeds. **(7 marks)**
   1. Identify the root cause.
   2. Suggest **four design or process modifications** to eliminate the defect.

**Model Solution:**

**Root Cause (2 marks):**
Shark-skin is a **surface instability** caused by **high shear stress at the die land wall**, exceeding the critical stress $\sigma_c \approx 0.1$ – $0.4$ MPa for the polymer. As the polymer exits the die, the boundary layer peels away, creating periodic high-frequency ridges perpendicular to the flow direction.

**Modifications (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Reduce line speed** — Lowers wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_w = 6Q/(\pi R^3)$.
2. **Increase melt temperature** — Reduces viscosity and wall shear stress.
3. **Apply external lubricant (process aid)** — Fluoropolymer-based additives reduce die-land slip-stick.
4. **Optimize land length** — A shorter land (5t instead of 15t) reduces residence time and accumulated strain.
5. **Polish the die land** — Reduce surface roughness Ra to below 0.2 μm to minimize boundary layer disruption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **confuse the terms DDR and DRB**. DDR (Draw Down Ratio) is the ratio of die land OD to final coating OD; **DRB is the cross-sectional area ratio**. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> - Many forget to **convert line speed units** (m/min → mm/s) when computing $Q$. Always show unit conversion explicitly.
> - Sketches in Part B (a) carry **at least 2 marks** — a missing or unlabeled sketch is the most common reason for losing 14-mark questions despite correct text.
> - In **shark-skin questions**, students answer only "reduce speed" — examiners expect **at least 3–4 corrective actions** spanning die design, material, and process variables.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Wire Coating Die** is a cross-head extrusion tool for polymer insulation of conductors.
- ✅ Two main types: **Tubing (free extrusion)** and **Pressure (forced conformance)** dies.
- ✅ Key components: **mandrel, die plate, guide tip, heater bands, breaker plate**.
- ✅ **Draw Ratio Balance (DRB) = 1.0** is the governing design equation for pressure dies.
- ✅ **DDR** typically ranges from **1.1 to 1.5** for tubing dies; equals **1.0** for pressure dies.
- ✅ Land length: **$L = 5t$ to $15t$** where $t$ is coating thickness.
- ✅ Mandrel tip diameter $D_i$ must clear the wire by **0.05–0.5 mm** to avoid wire-mandrel contact.
- ✅ Common defects: **shark-skin** (high shear), **die-lip buildup** (DRB mismatch), **eccentricity** (mandrel misalignment), **melt fracture** (excessive land shear).
- ✅ Materials of construction: **H13, D2 tool steel**; mandrels often coated with **Cr or TiN** for wear resistance.
- ✅ Industrial line speeds: **300–2500 m/min**; melt pressure: **5–50 MPa**.
- ✅ Always verify DRB numerically before finalizing die geometry in any KTU numerical problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cold Feed and Hot Feed Die Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cold Feed and Hot Feed Die Design # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In polymer/rubber extrusion, the **die** is the precisely machined tool that shapes the molten (plasticized) polymer into a continuous profile of desired cross-section. The **extruder-screw–die combination** is classified into two principal feed philosophies: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cold Feed Extrusion System:** A configuration in which the polymer feedstock (typically elastomer strips, granules, or pellets at **ambient temperature, 25–35 °C**) is fed directly into the hopper of the extruder. The screw performs *all four* functions — conveying, plasticizing, mixing, and pressure build-up — within a single barrel of high L/D ratio (typically **16:1 to 24:1**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hot Feed Extrusion System:** A configuration in which the polymer (rubber compound) is pre-plasticized in a **separate upstream unit** (two-roll mill, internal mixer/Banbury, or strainer) before being fed as a hot, homogenized strip into a short L/D extruder (typically **4:1 to 8:1**). The extruder here primarily acts as a *pressure pump and conveyor*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Coffee Machines Imagine brewing filter coffee in Kerala: - **Cold Feed** is like a *fully automatic espresso machine*: you drop in raw coffee beans, and the machine *roasts, grinds, tamps, and brews* all by itself. It is compact, automated, and versatile — but the internal mechanism is complex (a long screw with deep flights, high L/D, vacuum venting, etc.). - **Hot Feed** is like a *traditional brass filter (chembu)*: the water is boiled on a separate stove (the upstream two-roll mill), and the pre-heated decoction is poured in. The vessel only holds and dispenses. Simple, rugged, and gives superb control — but needs an extra "kitchen" beside it. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Range (Hot Feed) | Typical Range (Cold Feed) | |---|---|---| | Screw L/D ratio | **4 : 1 – 8 : 1** | **16 : 1 – 24 : 1** | | Screw speed | **20 – 60 rpm** | **40 – 120 rpm** | | Feed temperature | **70 – 110 °C** (preheated) | **25 – 40 °C** (ambient) | | Barrel temperature zones | **2 – 3** | **4 – 5** (often with vacuum vent) | | Output rate | Lower per unit | Higher per unit | | Polymer type | Elastomers, PVC paste | Thermoplastics, EPDM, NR | > [!NOTE] > The terms "die land," "die entry," and "die land length" are the three geometric primitives that govern the pressure–flow relationship in **both** cold-feed and hot-feed dies. The feed philosophy only changes the *upstream* state of the melt; the die-land rheology equation is identical. ## 1.4 The Die — Three Functional Zones A die always has three zones, irrespective of feed type: 1. **Die Entry (Approach Channel):** Converges the melt stream from the extruder bore to the final die cross-section. Entry angle α is usually kept in the **30° – 60°** band to minimize dead spots. 2. **Die Land (Parallel Section):** A straight, constant-cross-section channel of length **L** and characteristic gap **h**. This is where flow is fully developed and pressure is finally dissipated. 3. **Die Exit (Lip Plate):** A precision-machined final aperture that defines the profile. Governs the extrudate quality and **die swell**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Two Feed Philosophies Exist — The "Why" The split exists because **elastomers and thermoplastics are rheologically opposite worlds**: - Elastomers (raw rubber) have **very high viscosity (η ≈ 10⁵ – 10⁷ Pa·s)** at room temperature. If fed cold into a short extruder, the screw would simply *shear-fracture* the rubber — generating scorching, lumping, and uncontrolled temperature spikes. - Thermoplastics (PE, PP, PVC) have **manageable viscosity at 180 – 250 °C**. A single long screw can plasticize, mix, devolatilize, and pressurize them in one pass. Hence: - **Rubber industry → Hot Feed dominates.** - **Plastic industry → Cold Feed dominates.** ## 2.2 Cold Feed Die — Operational Logic **Step-by-step logic of melt preparation inside the cold-feed barrel:** 1. **Solids Conveying (Zone 1):** The deep-flighted feed section pulls ambient pellets/strips from the hopper by *drag-induced* solid-bed transport. Surface friction on barrel > friction on screw root → forward motion. 2. **Delay/Melting (Zone 2):** The barrel wall (above melting point) heats a thin film. The film is scraped off by the flight, forming a melt pool. The solid bed is progressively compressed. 3. **Melt Conveying / Metering (Zone 3):** The fully molten polymer is pumped by *drag flow* in a shallow metering zone. This is where the screw acts as a positive-displacement pump. 4. **Mixing / Homogenization (Zone 4, optional):** A pin mixer or Saxton-type dispersive mixer breaks down agglomerates and equalizes temperature. 5. **Devolatilization (Vacuum Vent, optional):** Volatiles, moisture, residual monomer are removed under vacuum. 6. **Final Metering & Pressure Build-up:** Last 3 – 4 D of screw length develops the **die-entry pressure (P_die)** required to push melt through the die at the design flow rate. > [!NOTE] > Because the cold-feed screw is doing 4 jobs, it must be **long, deeply flighted, and temperature-zoned**. The die sees a **uniform, well-mixed, degassed melt at a controlled temperature (e.g., 200 °C ± 2 °C for LDPE)**. ## 2.3 Hot Feed Die — Operational Logic **Step-by-step logic of the hot-feed system:** 1. **Upstream Mastication:** The two-roll mill (or internal mixer) masticates the rubber, breaks down molecular weight, incorporates carbon black and curatives, and produces a continuous warm strip at **70 – 110 °C**. 2. **Strip Feeding:** A **strip-feed roll** at the extruder throat forces the warm strip onto the rotating screw. This is positive mechanical feeding (no gravity-only feed). 3. **Short Conveying:** A short L/D screw (4 – 8) only needs to (a) generate pressure, and (b) push the strip forward. Plasticizing is already done. 4. **Screening:** Often a **strainer head** (mesh screen + breaker plate) is fitted before the die to catch foreign particles — critical for medical and tyre-tread extrusions. 5. **Die Land Rheology:** Identical to cold-feed. > [!NOTE] > The hot-feed strip is already at **process temperature**. Therefore the screw's role shrinks to a *gear pump*. This permits very **high pressures (up to 35 – 70 MPa)** and very **stable flow rates** — essential for tight-tolerance rubber profiles (e.g., window seals, hose covers, tyre treads). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **minimum set of equations** you must memorize for any KTU 2024 question on die design. All symbols use SI units unless otherwise stated. | # | Equation | Symbols & Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \frac{w\,h^{2}}{2}\,\left(\frac{n}{2n+1}\right)\,\left(\frac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,K\,L}\right)^{1/n}$ | $Q$: volumetric flow (m³/s); $w$: width; $h$: gap; $L$: land length; $K$, $n$: power-law constants | Volumetric flow rate in a rectangular die land for a **power-law fluid** | | 2 | $\Delta P = \frac{2\,K\,L}{h}\left[\frac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}}\right]^{n}$ | Same as above | Pressure drop across die land | | 3 | $\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \dfrac{6\,Q}{w\,h^{2}}$ | Apparent wall shear rate (s⁻¹) | Estimate of shear rate at the wall | | 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_{t} = \dfrac{2n+1}{3n}\,\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \dfrac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}}$ | True (Rabinowitsch-corrected) shear rate (s⁻¹) | Shear rate used in power-law viscosity model | | 5 | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,L}$ | Wall shear stress (Pa) | Used with the power-law to obtain $K, n$ | | 6 | $\dfrac{L}{h} = \dfrac{\Delta P\,h}{2\,K\,(2n+1)^{n}}\left[\dfrac{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}}{Q}\right]^{n}$ | **Design form** | Die land length from allowable pressure drop | | 7 | $\chi = B = \dfrac{D_{\text{exit}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$ | Die swell ratio (dimensionless) | Predicting extrudate dimensions | | 8 | $T_{a} = \dfrac{\eta\,\dot{\gamma}_{a}\,\rho^{n-1}\,L^{n}}{h^{n}}$ | **Modified Péclet/Taylor number** (heat) | Checking whether viscous heating is significant | | 9 | $P_{\text{hydraulic}} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | Hydraulic power (W) | Energy dissipation in the die | | 10 | $\rho\,c_{p}\,Q\,(T_{\text{out}}-T_{\text{in}}) = P_{\text{hydraulic}}$ | Adiabatic melt-temperature rise (K) | Adiabatic temperature rise inside die land | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Tyre-tread extrusion** (hot feed): a single cold-feed extruder cannot achieve the carbon-black dispersion and 0.05 mm profile tolerance demanded. - **Pipe & film extrusion** (cold feed): the same long screw can run HDPE, LDPE, PP by simply adjusting temperature zones — flexibility is the key. - **Cost impact:** A cold-feed line costs **40 – 60 % more** in capital but **15 – 20 % less** in energy per kg of output. - **Die-land L/h ratio** of **10 : 1** is a typical industry starting point for thermoplastics, **5 : 1** for rubber. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Governing Equation — Flow of a Power-Law Fluid Through a Rectangular Die Land ### 3.1.1 Starting Point: The General Slit Flow Equation For steady, laminar, isothermal, fully-developed flow of an incompressible power-law fluid between two parallel plates separated by a gap $h$, the velocity profile is parabolic only when $n=1$ (Newtonian) and is **flatter** when $n < 1$ (shear-thinning — typical of polymer melts). The constitutive equation is: $$ \tau = K\left(\dot{\gamma}\right)^{n} $$ where $K$ is the consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) and $n$ is the power-law index (dimensionless; $n<1$ for pseudoplastic melts). ### 3.1.2 Derivation of Volumetric Flow Rate For fully-developed slit flow, the shear stress varies **linearly** across the gap: $$ \tau(y) = \frac{\Delta P}{L}\,y $$ where $y$ is measured from the centreline ($\tau = 0$ at the centre). Equating this to the power-law model: $$ K\left(-\frac{du}{dy}\right)^{n} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}\,y $$ (The negative sign is removed by using symmetry; $|du/dy|$.) Solving for the velocity gradient: $$ -\frac{du}{dy} = \left(\frac{\Delta P}{K\,L}\,y\right)^{1/n} $$ Integrating across the gap, with the no-slip condition $u = 0$ at $y = \pm h/2$: $$ u(y) = \int_{y}^{h/2}\left(\frac{\Delta P}{K\,L}\,y'\right)^{1/n}dy' $$ Carrying out the integration (using the Beta-function or by direct integration) and multiplying by the width $w$ to obtain the volumetric flow rate $Q$: $$ Q = w\int_{-h/2}^{h/2}u(y)\,dy = \frac{w\,h^{2}}{2}\left(\frac{n}{2n+1}\right)\left(\frac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,K\,L}\right)^{1/n} $$ This is **Equation 1** of the cheat sheet. ### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Wall Shear Stress and Apparent Shear Rate By definition, the wall shear stress occurs at $y = h/2$: $$ \tau_{w} = \frac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,L} $$ This is **Equation 5** of the cheat sheet. The **apparent** wall shear rate (the one you would compute from $Q$ assuming Newtonian flow) is: $$ \dot{\gamma}_{a} = \frac{6\,Q}{w\,h^{2}} $$ This is **Equation 3** of the cheat sheet. The **true** wall shear rate, corrected by the Rabinowitsch–Mooney relation for a non-circular channel (slit case), is: $$ \dot{\gamma}_{t} = \frac{2n+1}{3n}\,\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \frac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}} $$ This is **Equation 4** of the cheat sheet. ### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Pressure Drop Across the Die Land Starting from the volumetric flow-rate equation, isolate $\Delta P$: $$ Q = \frac{w\,h^{2}}{2}\left(\frac{n}{2n+1}\right)\left(\frac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,K\,L}\right)^{1/n} $$ Rearrange: $$ \left(\frac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,K\,L}\right)^{1/n} = \frac{2(2n+1)}{n}\frac{Q}{w\,h^{2}} $$ Raise both sides to the $n$-th power: $$ \frac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,K\,L} = \left[\frac{2(2n+1)}{n}\frac{Q}{w\,h^{2}}\right]^{n} $$ Solve for $\Delta P$: $$ \boxed{\;\Delta P = \frac{2\,K\,L}{h}\left[\frac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}}\right]^{n}\;} $$ This is **Equation 2** of the cheat sheet — the **master design equation** for any extrusion die. ### 3.1.5 Inverting to Obtain the Required Die Land Length (Design Form) For a given allowable $\Delta P_{\max}$ (set by extruder capacity, typically 10 – 35 MPa) and target flow rate $Q$, the **required** land length is: $$ L = \frac{\Delta P_{\max}\,h}{2\,K}\left[\frac{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}}{(2n+1)\,Q}\right]^{n} $$ This is **Equation 6** of the cheat sheet. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A cold-feed extruder is producing a flat sheet. The die land has width $w = 0.4$ m, gap $h = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, and land length $L = 0.02$ m. The polymer is LDPE at 200 °C with $K = 25\,000$ Pa·sⁿ and $n = 0.4$. The extruder delivers $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Compute the die-entry pressure and the wall shear rate. **Step 1 — Substituting into Equation 2:** First, the bracketed term: $$ B = \frac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}} = \frac{(2\times 0.4+1)\times 1.5\times 10^{-4}}{2\times 0.4\times 0.4\times (2\times 10^{-3})^{2}} $$ Evaluating numerator and denominator: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= (1.8)\times 1.5\times 10^{-4} = 2.7\times 10^{-4} \\ \text{Denominator} &= 0.8 \times 0.4 \times 4\times 10^{-6} = 1.28\times 10^{-6} \\ B &= \dfrac{2.7\times 10^{-4}}{1.28\times 10^{-6}} = 210.94 \;\text{s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ Now raise to $n = 0.4$: $$ B^{0.4} = (210.94)^{0.4} = 9.225 $$ The leading coefficient: $$ \frac{2\,K\,L}{h} = \frac{2 \times 25\,000 \times 0.02}{2\times 10^{-3}} = 5\times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa} $$ Therefore: $$ \Delta P = 5\times 10^{5}\times 9.225 = 4.61\times 10^{6}\;\text{Pa} \approx 4.61\;\text{MPa} $$ **Step 2 — True wall shear rate (Equation 4):** $$ \dot{\gamma}_{t} = \frac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}} = 210.94\;\text{s}^{-1} $$ The melt is therefore sheared at about **211 s⁻¹** — well within the stable flow regime of LDPE (onset of melt fracture ≈ 1000 s⁻¹ for LDPE). ## 3.3 Adiabatic Temperature Rise Inside the Die Land (KTU Energy Equation) All the mechanical energy lost as pressure drop converts to heat. For an adiabatic die: $$ \Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho\,c_{p}} $$ For LDPE ($\rho = 920$ kg/m³, $c_{p} = 2300$ J/(kg·K)) and $\Delta P = 4.61$ MPa: $$ \Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{4.61\times 10^{6}}{920\times 2300} \approx 2.18\;\text{K} $$ A rise of ~2 K is small — the die can be assumed isothermal. **For highly viscous rubber (hot feed) the rise can be 15 – 30 K**, which is why hot-feed die bodies are externally heated/cooled with oil-jacketed manifold blocks. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cold Feed vs. Hot Feed (Full System) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CF[ "COLD FEED LINE (Thermoplastics)" ] direction LR C1[ "Hopper

Strainer head" ] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP] **Q: Distinguish between cold feed and hot feed extrusion with respect to (i) the equipment used, (ii) the function of the screw, and (iii) the typical polymers processed.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Cold Feed | Hot Feed | |---|---|---| | Upstream equipment | Only hopper | Two-roll mill / internal mixer | | Screw L/D | 16 : 1 to 24 : 1 (long) | 4 : 1 to 8 : 1 (short) | | Screw function | Plasticizing + mixing + pumping | Pumping + conveying only | | Feed temperature | 25 – 35 °C (ambient) | 70 – 110 °C (preheated) | | Typical polymer | Thermoplastics (PE, PP, PS) | Elastomers (NR, EPDM, SBR) | | Capital cost | Higher (single unit) | Lower screw, but needs mill | | Output stability | Good, with vent | Excellent — pre-conditioned melt | ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP] **Q: What is the function of the die land in an extrusion die? Why is its length-to-gap ratio important?** **Model Answer (5 bullet points – 3 marks):** 1. The die land is the **straight, parallel, constant-cross-section** portion of the die immediately before the exit. 2. Its primary function is to **fully develop the velocity profile** and to **dissipate pressure uniformly** so that the extrudate has a uniform velocity across its width. 3. The L/h ratio governs **flow uniformity**: too short (< 5) → non-uniform flow, profile distortion; too long (> 20) → excessive pressure drop, melt degradation, viscous overheating. 4. An L/h of **5 – 10 for rubber** and **10 – 20 for thermoplastics** is the industry standard. 5. The L/h ratio is also a key input in the power-law pressure-drop equation $\Delta P = \frac{2KL}{h}\left[\frac{(2n+1)Q}{2nwh^{2}}\right]^{n}$. ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the pressure-drop equation for a power-law fluid flowing through the rectangular die land of an extrusion die, starting from $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$. **State clearly all assumptions.** [7 marks] **(b)** A cold-feed extruder is producing a polypropylene (PP) sheet. The die land has $w = 0.5$ m, $h = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, and $L = 0.025$ m. The PP melt has $K = 18\,000$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.38$ at 230 °C. The extruder delivers $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Calculate (i) the wall shear stress, (ii) the true wall shear rate, and (iii) the pressure drop across the die land. Comment on whether the flow is in the safe regime. [7 marks] #### Model Solution — (a) Derivation [7 marks] **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Steady, laminar, isothermal flow 2. Fully-developed velocity profile in the land 3. No-slip at the walls 4. Incompressible melt 5. Power-law rheology with constant $K$, $n$ 6. Negligible elastic effects (viscous-dominated) **Derivation steps (6 marks):** For slit flow, $\tau(y) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,y$ (linear shear stress distribution). Equate to power-law: $$ K\left(-\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^{n} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,y $$ Integrate across the gap using $u(\pm h/2) = 0$: $$ u(y) = \int_{y}^{h/2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\,y'\right)^{1/n}dy' $$ Multiply by width $w$ and integrate across the gap to obtain $Q$: $$ Q = \dfrac{w\,h^{2}}{2}\left(\dfrac{n}{2n+1}\right)\left(\dfrac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,K\,L}\right)^{1/n} $$ Inverting: $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{2\,K\,L}{h}\left[\dfrac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}}\right]^{n} \qquad\blacksquare $$ #### Model Solution — (b) Numerical [7 marks] **(i) Wall shear stress** (using Equation 5): $$ \tau_{w} = \frac{h\,\Delta P}{2\,L} \quad \text{(cannot compute without } \Delta P\text{)} $$ We need to compute $\Delta P$ first (or use it as a check). The intended approach is to use **Equation 2** to get $\Delta P$, then **Equation 5** to get $\tau_{w}$. Compute the term: $$ \begin{aligned} B &= \frac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}} = \frac{(2\times 0.38+1)\times 2\times 10^{-4}}{2\times 0.38\times 0.5\times (2.5\times 10^{-3})^{2}} \\ &= \frac{(1.76)(2\times 10^{-4})}{(0.76)(0.5)(6.25\times 10^{-6})} \\ &= \frac{3.52\times 10^{-4}}{2.375\times 10^{-6}} = 148.21\;\text{s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ Raise to $n = 0.38$: $$ B^{0.38} = (148.21)^{0.38} = 7.85 $$ Leading coefficient: $$ \frac{2KL}{h} = \frac{2 \times 18\,000 \times 0.025}{2.5\times 10^{-3}} = 3.6\times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa} $$ Therefore: $$ \Delta P = 3.6\times 10^{5}\times 7.85 = 2.826\times 10^{6}\;\text{Pa} \approx 2.83\;\text{MPa} $$ **(i) Wall shear stress:** $$ \tau_{w} = \frac{(2.5\times 10^{-3})\times(2.83\times 10^{6})}{2\times 0.025} = 1.415\times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa} \approx 141.5\;\text{kPa} $$ **(ii) True wall shear rate (Equation 4):** $$ \dot{\gamma}_{t} = \frac{(2n+1)\,Q}{2\,n\,w\,h^{2}} = 148.21\;\text{s}^{-1} $$ **(iii) Comment on safe regime:** For PP, melt fracture (sharkskin) begins at about **100 – 200 kPa** of wall stress and **300 – 500 s⁻¹** shear rate. Our values ($\tau_w \approx 141$ kPa, $\dot{\gamma}_t \approx 148$ s⁻¹) are **just below the critical limit** — the flow is at the edge of the safe regime. The die is at its design limit; a slight increase in $Q$ would push it into sharkskin regime. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain the differences between **cold feed** and **hot feed** die-extrusion systems with neat block diagrams. Discuss the effect of feed philosophy on the **die-entry pressure** and the **L/h ratio** of the die land. [7 marks] **(b)** A hot-feed extruder processes a rubber compound at 90 °C. The die land has $w = 0.06$ m, $h = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, and $L = 0.04$ m. The rubber is a power-law fluid with $K = 8\times 10^{4}$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.25$. The required output is $Q = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s. Find (i) the pressure drop across the die land, and (ii) the land length required if the available die-entry pressure is 8 MPa. Comment on the design feasibility. [7 marks] #### Model Solution — (a) Conceptual [7 marks] **Block diagrams (3 marks):** Refer to the diagrams in SECTION 4.1 above. **Differences in text form (3 marks):** - In **cold feed**, the screw plasticizes and pumps. The melt enters the die at the processing temperature (180 – 250 °C for thermoplastics). Because the melt viscosity is moderate ($\eta \sim 10^{2}$ to $10^{4}$ Pa·s), the die-entry pressure is low to moderate (5 – 25 MPa), and the die land can be longer to ensure flow uniformity — a typical **L/h = 10 – 20**. - In **hot feed**, the rubber is pre-plasticized in a mill. The screw's job is to pump, so the extruder can develop very high die-entry pressures (25 – 70 MPa) and very stable flow. However, rubber is **highly elastic**, so a *long* land would cause excessive elastic strain energy storage → severe **die swell** and **extrudate distortion**. Therefore the die land is kept **short, with L/h ≈ 5 – 10**. **Effect on design (1 mark):** Cold feed → high L/h, low P_die. Hot feed → low L/h, high P_die. This is the central design rule. #### Model Solution — (b) Numerical [7 marks] **Step 1: Compute the term inside the brackets:** $$ \begin{aligned} B &= \frac{(2n+1)Q}{2 n w h^{2}} = \frac{(2\times 0.25+1)\times 5\times 10^{-5}}{2\times 0.25\times 0.06\times(5\times 10^{-3})^{2}} \\ &= \frac{(1.5)\times 5\times 10^{-5}}{(0.5)(0.06)(2.5\times 10^{-5})} \\ &= \frac{7.5\times 10^{-5}}{7.5\times 10^{-7}} = 100\;\text{s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ Raise to $n = 0.25$: $$ B^{0.25} = (100)^{0.25} = \sqrt[4]{100} \approx 3.162 $$ Leading coefficient: $$ \frac{2KL}{h} = \frac{2\times 8\times 10^{4}\times 0.04}{5\times 10^{-3}} = 1.28\times 10^{6}\;\text{Pa} $$ **(i) Pressure drop (Equation 2):** $$ \boxed{\;\Delta P = 1.28\times 10^{6}\times 3.162 = 4.05\times 10^{6}\;\text{Pa} \approx 4.05\;\text{MPa}\;} $$ **(ii) Required land length for $\Delta P_{\max} = 8$ MPa (Equation 6):** $$ L_{\text{req}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\max}\,h}{2K}\left[\frac{2n w h^{2}}{(2n+1)Q}\right]^{n} $$ Compute the bracket: $$ C = \frac{2\times 0.25\times 0.06\times(5\times 10^{-3})^{2}}{1.5\times 5\times 10^{-5}} = \frac{7.5\times 10^{-7}}{7.5\times 10^{-5}} = 0.01 $$ Raise to $n = 0.25$: $$ C^{0.25} = (0.01)^{0.25} = 0.3162 $$ Leading coefficient: $$ \frac{\Delta P_{\max}\,h}{2K} = \frac{8\times 10^{6}\times 5\times 10^{-3}}{2\times 8\times 10^{4}} = \frac{4\times 10^{4}}{1.6\times 10^{5}} = 0.25\;\text{m} $$ Therefore: $$ L_{\text{req}} = 0.25 \times 0.3162 = 0.0791\;\text{m} \approx 79.1\;\text{mm} $$ **Design feasibility:** The available land is only $L = 40$ mm, which can sustain $\Delta P = 4.05$ MPa at the required $Q = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s — only **half** of the available 8 MPa. To use the full 8 MPa, the die land must be **extended to 79 mm**, but that is not advisable for rubber (excess elastic memory storage). The recommended design solution is to **reduce the gap $h$ slightly** to 3.5 – 4 mm, which raises $\Delta P$ at the same $L$ without the elastic-strain penalty. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Read Carefully!** > 1. **Mixing $n=1$ and $n \neq 1$ formulas:** Many students mistakenly use the Newtonian slit-flow formula ($\Delta P = 12\mu Q L / w h^{3}$) for polymer melts. *Always* use the power-law form when $n \neq 1$. > 2. **Confusing apparent and true shear rate:** Use $\dot{\gamma}_a$ for the "what the flowmeter sees" and $\dot{\gamma}_t$ for the "what the polymer feels." KTU almost always asks for the **true** shear rate in die-design questions. > 3. **Forgetting the $(2n+1)$ factor:** Dropping it gives an error of up to 80 % for highly shear-thinning rubber ($n \approx 0.2$). It is **never optional**. > 4. **Units of K:** $K$ in Pa·sⁿ is correct only if shear rate is in s⁻¹. If the question uses K in Pa·s (Newtonian-style), check the question stem — KTU occasionally provides $K$ in g/(cm·s) — convert carefully. > 5. **Sign of pressure drop:** $\Delta P$ is always positive (magnitude). Don't write a negative answer. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 12 Golden Rules of Cold-Feed & Hot-Feed Die Design — KTU 2024 Module 2 Cheat-Card** 1. **Cold feed** = single long extruder (L/D 16–24) does *all* melting. Used for thermoplastics. 2. **Hot feed** = upstream mill + short extruder (L/D 4–8). Used for elastomers. 3. The **die land** is the *only* rheology-active zone. Same equation governs both feed types. 4. **Power-law model:** $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^n$ with $n < 1$ for polymer melts. 5. **Master equation** (memorize, derive, and be able to invert): $$\Delta P = \frac{2 K L}{h}\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)Q}{2 n w h^{2}}\right]^{n}$$ 6. **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = h\,\Delta P / 2L$. 7. **True wall shear rate** (Rabinowitsch–Mooney correction): $\dot{\gamma}_t = \dfrac{(2n+1)Q}{2 n w h^{2}}$. 8. **L/h design rules of thumb:** 5–10 (rubber), 10–20 (thermoplastics). 9. **Die-entry angle:** keep within 30°–60° to avoid dead spots. 10. **Melt fracture / sharkskin** onset: 100–200 kPa wall stress, 300–500 s⁻¹ shear rate for most commercial polyolefins. 11. **Adiabatic melt-temperature rise:** $\Delta T = \Delta P / (\rho\,c_p)$ — significant for rubber, negligible for thermoplastics. 12. **Capital cost trade-off:** Cold feed = higher CapEx, lower OpEx/kg. Hot feed = lower extruder cost, but needs mill + strip feed → higher floor space. > **End of Module 2 Topic — Cold Feed and Hot Feed Die Design**

topics.title: Nitriding Steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nitriding Steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nitriding** is a thermochemical surface-hardening heat treatment process in which **nitrogen atoms are diffused into the surface of a solid ferrous alloy (steel) at sub-critical temperatures (typically 480 °C – 580 °C)** to form a hard, wear-resistant nitride layer, **without the need for quenching**. The process relies on the in-situ formation of extremely hard alloy nitrides (e.g., AlN, CrN, Mo₂N, VN, TiN) by reaction between diffused nitrogen and strong nitride-forming alloying elements present in the steel.

In the context of **Mould and Die Manufacture (PEPOT756)**, nitriding is a **finishing surface treatment** applied to plastic moulds, die-casting dies, forging dies, extrusion dies, and punches to enhance:
- **Surface Hardness** (typically 800 – 1200 HV)
- **Wear Resistance**
- **Fatigue Strength**
- **Dimensional Stability** (because no quenching is involved)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hard-Boiled Egg Principle"

Imagine you are boiling an egg in tea (the Indian "Ande ka Salan" or tea-boiled egg concept). The outer white part of the egg becomes firm and rubbery, while the inner yolk stays soft. The depth of firmness depends on **how long** the egg stays in the hot liquid and **how hot** the liquid is.

Nitriding works exactly like this:
- The **hot ammonia furnace** is the "tea" → it is the source of nitrogen atoms.
- The **steel** is the "egg" → it is the base material.
- The **diffused nitrogen** is the "firm white" → it forms the hard surface layer.
- The **inner core** is the "soft yolk" → it remains tough and ductile (unaffected).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Mould Makers:** Unlike carburizing (which needs quenching → distortion risk for precision moulds), nitriding produces **minimal dimensional change**. This is why nitriding is the *go-to* surface treatment for **high-precision plastic injection moulds** and **die-casting dies** where cavity dimensions must not shift.

---

## 1.3 Standard Nomenclature & Key Terms

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Nitriding Temperature** | Sub-critical diffusion temperature | **480 °C – 580 °C** |
| **Nitriding Time** | Total holding time | **20 – 100 hours** |
| **Nitrogen Potential (Kₙ)** | Tendency of atmosphere to supply nitrogen | Controlled by NH₃ dissociation |
| **Compound Layer (White Layer)** | Surface layer of ε-Fe₂₋₃N + γ′-Fe₄N | **5 – 30 µm** |
| **Diffusion Zone** | Layer beneath compound zone with alloy nitrides | **0.3 – 0.8 mm** |
| **Core Hardness** | Hardness of unaffected base steel | 250 – 350 HV (pre-hardened) |
| **Surface Hardness** | Hardness at the nitrided surface | **800 – 1200 HV** |
| **Nitralloy** | Generic name for Al-containing nitriding steel | e.g., 38CrMoAl |

---

## 1.4 Why Nitriding is Special for Moulds and Dies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mould/Die-Specific Justification:**
> 1. **No Quenching** → Zero distortion → Cavity dimensions remain exact.
> 2. **Low Treatment Temperature** (below A₁) → Grain size unchanged.
> 3. **High Surface Hardness with Core Toughness** → Ideal for cyclic thermal + mechanical loading in dies.
> 4. **Self-Tempering Effect** → Nitrogen diffusion slightly tempers the core, providing stress relief.
> 5. **Excellent Dimensional Stability at Elevated Temperatures** → Critical for die-casting dies operating at 300 °C – 600 °C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Science Behind Nitriding — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The nitriding process is governed by **Fick's Laws of Diffusion** combined with **solid-state chemical reactions**. The mechanism unfolds in **four sequential stages**:

### Stage 1: Ammonia Dissociation at the Steel Surface

At the working temperature (480 – 580 °C), gaseous ammonia (NH₃) contacts the steel surface and catalytically dissociates:

$$2\,\text{NH}_3 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Fe catalyst, } 500^\circ C}\; 2\,\text{N}_{(\text{atomic})} + 3\,\text{H}_2$$

The freshly liberated **atomic nitrogen [N]** is highly reactive and is absorbed by the steel surface.

### Stage 2: Surface Adsorption & Dissolution

The atomic nitrogen dissolves in the α-iron (BCC) lattice as **interstitial atoms**. The solubility of N in α-Fe at 590 °C is approximately **0.1 wt %**.

### Stage 3: Bulk Diffusion (Inner Stage)

Nitrogen atoms migrate **inward** through the iron lattice via interstitial diffusion. The rate is governed by:

$$D = D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

Where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient of N in α-Fe, $D_0$ is the pre-exponential factor, $Q$ is the activation energy for N diffusion, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

### Stage 4: Nitride Precipitation (Age-Hardening Inside Steel)

When the dissolved nitrogen encounters a strong nitride-forming element (Al, Cr, Mo, V, Ti), it precipitates out as **extremely hard, sub-microscopic nitride particles**:

$$\text{Al} + \text{N} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AlN} \quad (\text{Hardness} \approx 1300\;\text{HV})$$
$$\text{Cr} + \text{N} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CrN},\; \text{Cr}_2\text{N} \quad (\text{Hardness} \approx 1100\;\text{HV})$$
$$\text{Mo} + \text{N} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Mo}_2\text{N} \quad (\text{Hardness} \approx 1000\;\text{HV})$$
$$\text{V} + \text{N} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{VN} \quad (\text{Hardness} \approx 1500\;\text{HV})$$
$$\text{Ti} + \text{N} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{TiN} \quad (\text{Hardness} \approx 2000\;\text{HV})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Plain carbon steels **cannot** be nitrided to high hardness because iron nitrides (Fe₂N, Fe₃N, Fe₄N) are inherently **brittle and unstable** at elevated temperatures. The presence of strong nitride formers is **non-negotiable**.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Nitriding Steels

Nitriding steels are essentially **low-carbon, low-alloy steels** that have been pre-hardened and tempered (typically to 28–32 HRC) **before** nitriding. They are categorized as follows:

### 2.2.1 By Nitride-Forming Alloy Content

| Category | Key Alloying Element | Hardness Achievable | Typical Grade |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Al-containing (Nitralloy type)** | 0.7 – 1.2 % Al | **1000 – 1200 HV** | 38CrMoAl, 34CrAlMo5 |
| **Cr-containing** | 1.5 – 3.0 % Cr | 700 – 900 HV | 31CrMoV9, 40Cr |
| **Cr-Mo-V containing** | Cr + Mo + V | 800 – 950 HV | 32CrMoV13 |
| **Cr-Mo-Al-V (Premium)** | All of the above | 950 – 1200 HV | EN40C, 34CrAlNi7 |

### 2.2.2 Common KTU-Referenced Grades

| Designation | C % | Cr % | Mo % | Al % | V % | Application |
|-------------|-----|------|------|------|-----|-------------|
| **38CrMoAl** | 0.35 – 0.42 | 1.35 – 1.65 | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.70 – 1.10 | – | Plastic moulds, gears |
| **34CrAlNi7** | 0.30 – 0.37 | 1.50 – 1.80 | – | 0.80 – 1.20 | – | Die-casting dies |
| **31CrMoV9** | 0.27 – 0.34 | 2.30 – 2.70 | 0.15 – 0.25 | – | 0.10 – 0.20 | Hot-work dies |
| **32CrMoV13** | 0.28 – 0.35 | 3.00 – 3.50 | 0.80 – 1.20 | – | 0.25 – 0.35 | High-stress dies |
| **EN40C (B.S.)** | 0.30 – 0.40 | 3.00 – 3.50 | 0.70 – 1.20 | – | – | Crankshafts, dies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-treatment is mandatory:** All nitriding steels must be **quenched and tempered** to the required core hardness (typically 28–32 HRC) **before** nitriding. The nitriding temperature is **below the tempering temperature** of the steel, ensuring the core hardness is preserved.

---

## 2.3 The Three Industrial Nitriding Variants

### 2.3.1 Gas Nitriding (Most Common)

- **Medium:** Cracked ammonia (NH₃) flowing over the heated steel.
- **Temperature:** **500 °C – 530 °C** (single-stage) or **525 °C / 580 °C** (two-stage/Floe process).
- **Time:** 30 – 90 hours.
- **Mechanism:** NH₃ dissociates on the steel surface; atomic N diffuses inward.
- **Application:** Large mould bases, die-casting dies, plastic mould inserts.

### 2.3.2 Liquid (Salt-Bath) Nitriding — "Tufftriding"

- **Medium:** Molten cyanide-cyanate salt bath (e.g., NaCN + KCN at ~570 °C).
- **Temperature:** **570 °C – 580 °C**.
- **Time:** 1 – 4 hours.
- **Output:** Very thin compound layer (5 – 15 µm) + diffusion zone.
- **Application:** Small dies, screws, tools needing short cycles.

### 2.3.3 Plasma (Ion / Glow-Discharge) Nitriding

- **Medium:** Vacuum chamber with N₂ + H₂ gas mixture, plasma activated by high voltage (400 – 1000 V).
- **Temperature:** **480 °C – 580 °C**.
- **Time:** 8 – 30 hours.
- **Advantages:** No white layer (or controlled white layer), uniform treatment of narrow bores, environmentally clean.
- **Application:** High-precision moulds with blind holes, stainless-steel dies.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the most important reference for solving KTU numerical and theory questions on nitriding.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Fick's First Law (Steady State)** | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Flux of N atoms through surface |
| 2 | **Fick's Second Law (Non-Steady)** | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | — | Used for time-depth prediction |
| 3 | **Diffusion Coefficient** | $D = D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | m²/s | Temperature dependence of diffusion |
| 4 | **Case Depth (Approx.)** | $d \approx k\sqrt{t}$ | mm, hours | Empirical; $k$ is process constant |
| 5 | **Nitriding Temperature Range** | $T_n = 480^\circ C \text{ to } 580^\circ C$ | °C | Sub-critical, below A₁ (727 °C) |
| 6 | **Surface Hardness (38CrMoAl)** | $\text{HV}_{surf} = 1000 - 1200$ | HV | After full nitriding cycle |
| 7 | **Compound Layer Thickness** | $\delta_c = 5 - 30$ | µm | Function of $K_n$ and time |
| 8 | **Diffusion Zone Depth** | $x = 2\sqrt{Dt}$ | mm | Solution of Fick's 2nd law for semi-infinite solid |
| 9 | **Ammonia Dissociation** | $2\,\text{NH}_3 \to 2\,\text{N} + 3\,\text{H}_2$ | — | Source of atomic N |
| 10 | **Activation Energy for N in α-Fe** | $Q \approx 76\;\text{kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | Empirical value |
| 11 | **Hardness Conversion (approx.)** | $\text{HRC} \approx 0.1 \cdot \text{HV} - 9$ | — | Quick reference only |

---

## 2.5 Compound Layer (White Layer) vs. Diffusion Zone

The nitrided case is **bilayer**:

1. **Compound Layer (ε + γ′ phase):** Outer 5–30 µm. Very hard but **brittle**. Often removed by grinding or controlled away in plasma nitriding.
2. **Diffusion Zone (α + alloy nitrides):** Inner 0.2–0.8 mm. Hard, fatigue-resistant, transitions gradually to the tough core.

> [!WARNING]
> **For Mould Cavities:** Excessive white layer causes **brittle spalling** under cyclic injection pressure. Plasma nitriding is preferred for plastic moulds because it can produce a "white-layer-free" diffusion zone.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Mould/Die Component | Steel Selected | Nitriding Variant | Reason |
|---------------------|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| Plastic injection mould cavity | P20 + nitrided, or 38CrMoAl | Plasma nitriding | Distortion-free, no white layer |
| Die-casting die (Al/Zn alloys) | H13 modified, 34CrAlNi7 | Gas nitriding | Thermal fatigue resistance |
| Hot-forging die insert | 32CrMoV13 | Salt-bath / gas | Wear resistance at 400 °C |
| Cold-extrusion punch | Cr-V steel | Gas nitriding | Anti-galling, high surface hardness |
| Die casting sleeve | EN40C | Plasma | Uniform nitriding of internal bores |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Nitriding Case Depth (Using Fick's Second Law)

### Problem Statement
A 38CrMoAl steel mould insert is gas-nitrided at **520 °C** for **50 hours**. Calculate the **approximate diffusion-zone depth**, given the diffusion coefficient of nitrogen in α-Fe at 520 °C is approximately $D = 6.5 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

### Step 1: Set Up the Boundary Conditions

The semi-infinite solid solution of Fick's second law is applicable:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Initial Condition (IC):** At $t = 0$, $C(x,0) = C_0$ (uniform bulk concentration).
**Boundary Conditions (BC):**
- At $x = 0$ (surface), $C(0,t) = C_s$ (constant surface concentration from NH₃).
- At $x \to \infty$, $C(\infty,t) = C_0$ (bulk unaffected).

### Step 2: Apply the Standard Error-Function Solution

The well-known solution (derived by Boltzmann and Matano) is:

$$\dfrac{C(x,t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

Where $\text{erf}(\cdot)$ is the Gauss error function.

### Step 3: Define Case Depth Convention

By KTU / industrial convention, **case depth** is the depth at which the hardness falls to **50 %** of the surface-to-core hardness difference (or where N concentration is ~50 % of surface value). This corresponds to:

$$\text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = 0.5$$

From standard error-function tables, $\text{erf}^{-1}(0.5) = 0.4769$.

### Step 4: Solve for x

$$\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = 0.4769$$

$$x = 2 \times 0.4769 \times \sqrt{Dt} \approx 0.9538 \sqrt{Dt}$$

**Engineering Approximation Used in Practice:**

$$x \;\approx\; 2\sqrt{Dt}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Numerical Values

Given: $D = 6.5 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $t = 50\;\text{h} = 50 \times 3600 = 180{,}000\;\text{s}$.

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{6.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 180{,}000}$$

$$= \sqrt{1.17 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$= 3.42 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m} = 3.42\;\text{mm}$$

### Step 6: Calculate Case Depth

$$x = 0.9538 \times 3.42 = 3.26\;\text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion to Engineering Units:**
> $$x \approx 3.26\;\text{mm} \approx 0.326\;\text{cm}$$
> This is a typical diffusion-zone depth for 50-hour gas nitriding of 38CrMoAl at 520 °C.

### Step 7: Surface Hardness Estimation (Empirical Check)

For 38CrMoAl, the empirical surface hardness after nitriding follows:

$$\text{HV}_{surf} \;\approx\; 80 + 250 \cdot (\%\,\text{Al}) + 50 \cdot (\%\,\text{Cr}) + 30 \cdot (\%\,\text{Mo})$$

For 38CrMoAl (1.0 % Al, 1.5 % Cr, 0.2 % Mo):

$$\text{HV}_{surf} = 80 + 250(1.0) + 50(1.5) + 30(0.2) = 421\;\text{HV (base)}$$

After full nitriding (factor of ~2.5× for Al steels):

$$\text{HV}_{surf,\,actual} \approx 1050\;\text{HV} \;\;(\text{matches the 1000 – 1200 range})$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables

| Parameter | Increased → Effect on Surface Hardness | Increased → Effect on Case Depth | Increased → Effect on Distortion |
|-----------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Temperature (T)** | Slight increase (peaks ~520 °C) | **Strong increase** (exponential via $D$) | **Increases** (grains coarsen) |
| **Time (t)** | Marginal (saturates ~30 h) | **Moderate increase** (proportional to $\sqrt{t}$) | Negligible |
| **NH₃ Flow Rate** | Increases up to a point | Marginal | Negligible |
| **Nitrogen Potential (Kₙ)** | **Strong increase** | Moderate | Negligible |
| **Al Content in Steel** | **Strong increase** (AlN formation) | Marginal | Negligible |
| **Cr Content in Steel** | Strong increase | Moderate | Negligible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature is the most powerful lever** for case depth (because $D$ is exponential in $T$). Time has only a square-root influence — a 4× time increase yields only a 2× depth increase.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Salt-Bath Nitriding Cycle Calculation

### Given:
A small H13 die insert is salt-bath nitrided at **575 °C** for **2 hours**. The nitrogen absorption rate in molten cyanate is approximately $K = 4.5 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m/s}^{0.5}$. The empirical case depth formula for salt-bath is:

$$d \;=\; K \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

### Step 1: Convert Time to Seconds

$$t = 2\;\text{hours} = 7200\;\text{s}$$

### Step 2: Apply Formula

$$d = 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{7200}$$

$$= 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 84.85$$

$$= 3.82 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Micrometres

$$d \approx 382\;\mu\text{m} \approx 0.38\;\text{mm}$$

This is consistent with industrial salt-bath nitriding outputs (typically 0.1 – 0.5 mm case depth).

---

## 3.4 Process Selection Decision Tree (Analytical)

```
Is dimensional stability the #1 priority for the mould?
├── YES → Is the mould made of Cr-Mo-V or Nitralloy steel?
│         ├── YES → Use PLASMA nitriding (no white layer, ≤ 0.5 % distortion)
│         └── NO  → Use GAS nitriding (mild distortion acceptable)
└── NO  → Is the production volume high (> 100,000 shots)?
          ├── YES → Use SALT-BATH nitriding (fastest cycle, 1–4 h)
          └── NO  → Use GAS nitriding (most economical for low volume)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gas Nitriding Furnace — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the functional flow of a **gas nitriding furnace** used in mould/die manufacturing plants.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[NH3 Cylinder Supply] --> B1[Pressure Regulator and Flow Meter]
    B1 --> C1[Pre-heater Unit 200 C]
    C1 --> D1[Furnace Chamber 500 to 530 C]
    D1 --> E1[Steel Mould or Die Workpiece]
    E1 --> F1[Dissociation Zone Fe Catalyst]
    F1 --> G1[Atomic N Diffusion into Steel]
    G1 --> H1[Nitride Precipitation AlN CrN Mo2N]
    H1 --> I1[Compound Layer 5 to 30 microns]
    H1 --> J1[Diffusion Zone 0.3 to 0.8 mm]
    D1 --> K1[Exhaust H2 and Residual NH3]
    K1 --> L1[Dissociation Analyzer Controls Kn]
    L1 --> B1
    E1 --> M1[Quench Tank Optional Oil or Water]
    M1 --> N1[Finished Nitrided Mould 1000 HV Surface]
    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style D1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style E1 fill:#fff4e1
    style H1 fill:#e1ffe1
    style N1 fill:#e1e1ff
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **blue blocks** are inputs (raw gases).
- The **red block** is the high-temperature processing zone.
- The **green blocks** are the chemical/microstructural events.
- The **purple block** is the final nitrided mould.

---

## 4.2 Microstructure Evolution During Nitriding (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Pre-treated Steel 28 to 32 HRC Core] --> B2[Surface N Adsorption]
    B2 --> C2[Compound Layer Formation epsilon Fe2to3N plus gamma prime Fe4N]
    C2 --> D2[Diffusion Zone Formation alpha Fe plus Alloy Nitrides]
    D2 --> E2[Unaffected Tough Core 250 to 350 HV]
    C2 --> F2[Hardness 1000 to 1200 HV Brittle]
    D2 --> G2[Hardness 700 to 900 HV Tough]
    E2 --> H2[Hardness 250 to 350 HV Ductile]
    style A2 fill:#f0f0f0
    style C2 fill:#ffcccb
    style D2 fill:#fff2cc
    style E2 fill:#d4edda
    style F2 fill:#ffcccb
    style G2 fill:#fff2cc
    style H2 fill:#d4edda
```

---

## 4.3 Process Variants — Comparative Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Choose Nitriding Process] --> P1{Is zero white layer required?}
    P1 -- YES --> P2[Plasma Nitriding 480 to 580 C 8 to 30 h]
    P1 -- NO --> P3{Is cycle time critical?}
    P3 -- YES --> P4[Salt Bath Tufftriding 575 C 1 to 4 h]
    P3 -- NO --> P5[Gas Nitriding 500 to 530 C 30 to 90 h]
    P2 --> P6[Distortion Less than 0.05 percent]
    P4 --> P7[Thin White Layer 5 to 15 microns]
    P5 --> P8[Standard White Layer 10 to 30 microns]
    style P0 fill:#e1e1ff
    style P2 fill:#d4edda
    style P4 fill:#fff2cc
    style P5 fill:#cce5ff
    style P6 fill:#d4edda

```
