# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define nitriding. Why is it preferred over carburizing for precision plastic moulds?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — 12–15 lines expected):

Nitriding is a thermochemical surface-hardening process in which **nitrogen atoms** are diffused into the surface of a specially alloyed steel at sub-critical temperatures (**480 – 580 °C**) to form hard alloy nitrides (e.g., AlN, CrN), without any subsequent quenching.

It is preferred over carburizing for precision plastic moulds for three principal reasons:
1. **No quenching is required** → negligible distortion → mould cavity dimensions remain accurate.
2. **Treatment is performed below the tempering temperature** → the pre-hardened core (28 – 32 HRC) is preserved.
3. **Higher retained surface hardness at elevated temperatures** → mould cavities maintain hardness during cyclic heating in injection moulding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** All three reasons must be stated explicitly for full marks. A definition alone scores only 1 mark.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why are plain carbon steels unsuitable for nitriding? Name two nitride-forming elements.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

Plain carbon steels form **iron nitrides (Fe₂N, Fe₃N, Fe₄N)** upon nitriding, which are **brittle, thermally unstable, and decompose** at temperatures above ~300 °C. As a result, the surface hardness achieved is very low (only 200 – 300 HV) and the layer disintegrates during service.

Nitriding requires strong nitride-forming elements that form **stable, hard, finely dispersed nitrides**, such as:
- **Aluminium (Al)** → forms AlN (~1300 HV)
- **Chromium (Cr)** → forms CrN, Cr₂N (~1100 HV)
- **Molybdenum (Mo)** → forms Mo₂N
- **Vanadium (V)** → forms VN (~1500 HV)
- **Titanium (Ti)** → forms TiN (~2000 HV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Mention the reason (brittle/unstable iron nitrides) and list at least two alloying elements for full marks.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Format:** Answer any ONE full question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

#### **(a) Explain the mechanism of gas nitriding with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of alloying elements in nitriding steel. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Mechanism of Gas Nitriding (4 Marks):**
The mechanism proceeds in **four steps**:

**Step 1 — Ammonia Dissociation:**
Ammonia gas is passed into a sealed furnace containing the pre-heated steel mould. At the steel surface (acting as a catalyst), ammonia dissociates:

$$2\,\text{NH}_3 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Fe, 500-580}^\circ\text{C}}\; 2\,[\text{N}] + 3\,\text{H}_2$$

**Step 2 — Surface Absorption:**
The freshly liberated atomic nitrogen is absorbed by the surface of the α-iron (BCC) lattice in atomic form.

**Step 3 — Interstitial Diffusion:**
Atomic nitrogen migrates inward through the iron lattice. The depth of diffusion is governed by Fick's second law:

$$x \approx 2\sqrt{Dt}, \quad D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Nitride Precipitation:**
Inside the steel, nitrogen combines with strong nitride-forming elements to form hard, sub-microscopic nitride precipitates (AlN, CrN, Mo₂N, VN) that block dislocation motion and produce extreme hardness (1000 – 1200 HV).

**2. Role of Alloying Elements (3 Marks):**
- **Aluminium (0.7 – 1.2 %):** Most potent nitride former. Forms AlN which is the **primary contributor** to surface hardness in Nitralloy-type steels.
- **Chromium (1 – 3 %):** Forms CrN and Cr₂N; provides **secondary hardening** and corrosion resistance.
- **Molybdenum (0.2 – 0.5 %):** Forms Mo₂N; reduces **nitriding brittleness** and prevents temper brittleness.
- **Vanadium (0.1 – 0.3 %):** Forms VN; provides **secondary hardening** and grain refinement.
- **Carbon:** Kept low (0.3 – 0.4 %) to avoid carbide interference with nitrogen diffusion.

A simple **sketch description for answer script**:
- Draw a rectangular furnace (500 °C).
- Inlet arrow showing NH₃ entering.
- Mould/die kept on a tray.
- Show surface region: "N atoms diffuse → AlN, CrN precipitates".
- Label "Compound Layer" and "Diffusion Zone" → "Tough Core".

---

#### **(b) List and briefly explain the three industrial nitriding processes. Compare their case depth, surface hardness, and typical applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Process | Medium | Temperature | Time | Case Depth | Surface Hardness | Application |
|---------|--------|-------------|------|------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Gas Nitriding** | Cracked NH₃ | 500 – 530 °C | 30 – 90 h | 0.3 – 0.8 mm | 1000 – 1200 HV | Large mould bases, gears |
| **Salt-Bath (Tufftriding)** | Cyanide-cyanate salt | 575 – 580 °C | 1 – 4 h | 0.1 – 0.5 mm | 800 – 1000 HV | Small dies, screws, tools |
| **Plasma (Ion) Nitriding** | N₂ + H₂ plasma in vacuum | 480 – 580 °C | 8 – 30 h | 0.3 – 0.6 mm | 900 – 1200 HV | Precision moulds, blind holes, stainless dies |

**Key Comparative Points (3 Marks):**
- **Slowest but deepest** → Gas nitriding.
- **Fastest but shallowest** → Salt-bath (Tufftriding).
- **Cleanest, no white layer, uniform on complex shapes** → Plasma nitriding.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Do not confuse tufftriding with carburizing** — many students write "carburizing" when the question asks for nitriding comparison.
> 2. **Do not list case depths in mm interchangeably with µm** — always specify units clearly.
> 3. **Do not skip the "role of plasma glow discharge"** when discussing plasma nitriding — it is the energy source for ionization.

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

#### **(a) What are the essential requirements of a steel to be suitable for nitriding? Explain with reference to the composition and microstructure of 38CrMoAl steel. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Essential Requirements of a Nitriding Steel (4 Marks):**
1. **Strong nitride-forming elements** must be present (Al, Cr, Mo, V, Ti) to form hard, stable nitrides.
2. **Low-to-medium carbon content** (0.25 – 0.40 %) — to avoid competing carbide formation and to retain a tough core.
3. **Pre-hardened and tempered condition** — typically 28 – 32 HRC, so that the nitriding temperature (sub-critical) does not soften the core.
4. **Fine, homogeneous grain structure** — to ensure uniform nitrogen diffusion and consistent hardness.
5. **Absence of free ferrite bands** — to prevent uneven nitriding.

**2. Composition and Microstructure of 38CrMoAl (3 Marks):**
- **C:** 0.35 – 0.42 % → provides core strength.
- **Cr:** 1.35 – 1.65 % → secondary hardening, corrosion resistance.
- **Mo:** 0.15 – 0.25 % → reduces nitriding brittleness, prevents temper embrittlement.
- **Al:** 0.70 – 1.10 % → **primary nitride former**, gives peak surface hardness.
- **Si, Mn, S, P:** Residual/deoxidizer levels.

**Microstructure (before nitriding):** Tempered martensite / sorbite with finely dispersed carbides.
**Microstructure (after nitriding):** Outer compound layer (ε + γ′ iron nitrides) + diffusion zone (α-Fe + sub-microscopic AlN, CrN, Mo₂N precipitates) + tough tempered-martensite core.

---

#### **(b) Explain the formation of the compound layer (white layer) and diffusion zone during nitriding. Discuss why plasma nitriding is preferred for plastic injection moulds. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Compound Layer (White Layer) Formation (3 Marks):**
The compound layer is the **outermost 5 – 30 µm** of the nitrided case. It forms when the surface concentration of nitrogen exceeds the solubility limit of α-Fe (~0.1 wt % at 590 °C). Excess nitrogen reacts with iron to form two distinct nitride phases:

- **γ′-Fe₄N phase** (FCC) — softer, more ductile.
- **ε-Fe₂₋₃N phase** (HCP) — harder, more brittle.

These nitrides appear **white** under an optical microscope (because they do not etch easily), hence the name "white layer." Although very hard (1000 – 1200 HV), it is **brittle** and prone to spalling under cyclic loading.

**2. Diffusion Zone Formation (2 Marks):**
Beneath the white layer, nitrogen diffuses inward but its concentration remains **below** the solubility limit, so it stays dissolved in α-Fe. It reacts with alloying elements (Al, Cr, Mo, V) to form **finely dispersed alloy nitrides** (AlN, CrN, Mo₂N, VN). These nano-scale precipitates block dislocation motion, producing a **hardness gradient** from 800 HV near the surface to ~320 HV at the core. The diffusion zone is the **engineering useful zone** — hard, tough, and fatigue-resistant.

**3. Why Plasma Nitriding is Preferred for Plastic Moulds (2 Marks):**
1. **White layer can be eliminated or controlled** — preventing brittle spalling inside mould cavities.
2. **Negligible distortion** — critical for sub-micron cavity tolerance.
3. **Uniform treatment of narrow blind holes and slots** — common in mould cooling channels.
4. **Environmentally clean** — no toxic ammonia or cyanide salts.
5. **Suitable for stainless moulds** — preserves corrosion resistance (unlike gas nitriding which can cause "nitrogen sensitization").

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In Part (a), students often forget to mention that the steel must be **pre-hardened before nitriding** — this is a frequently tested 2-mark sub-question.
> 2. In Part (b), students confuse "white layer" with "diffusion zone." Remember: **white layer = compound layer (brittle); diffusion zone = engineering useful (tough).**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — General Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Areas in Nitriding Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing nitriding with carburizing** — Nitriding uses NH₃/N₂, NOT carbon. No quenching.
> 2. **Writing wrong temperature** — Always 480 – 580 °C. Carburizing is 850 – 950 °C.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of Al** — Aluminium is the **most important** nitride former; mention it explicitly.
> 4. **Not mentioning the "no-quench" advantage** — This is the single most important reason nitriding is used for moulds.
> 5. **Confusing white layer with diffusion zone** — White layer is brittle and unwanted; diffusion zone is the useful engineering layer.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nitriding Steels — Key Points to Remember (Last-Minute Revision):**

- **Definition:** Diffusion of nitrogen into steel at 480 – 580 °C to form hard nitrides **without quenching**.
- **Temperature range:** **480 – 580 °C** (sub-critical, below A₁ = 727 °C).
- **Time range:** 1 – 100 hours depending on process variant.
- **Three process variants:**
  - **Gas nitriding** — NH₃, slow, deep case.
  - **Salt-bath (Tufftriding)** — Cyanide salt, fast, shallow case.
  - **Plasma nitriding** — Vacuum + glow discharge, no white layer, clean.
- **Key nitriding steels:** **38CrMoAl, 34CrAlNi7, 31CrMoV9, EN40C, 32CrMoV13**.
- **Critical alloying elements:** **Al (most potent), Cr, Mo, V, Ti** — all are strong nitride formers.
- **Plain carbon steels CANNOT be nitrided to high hardness** — only forms brittle iron nitrides.
- **Two-layer nitrided case structure:**
  - **Compound (white) layer** — ε-Fe₂₋₃N + γ′-Fe₄N, 5 – 30 µm, hard + brittle.
  - **Diffusion zone** — α-Fe + alloy nitrides, 0.3 – 0.8 mm, hard + tough.
- **Governing equation:** $x \approx 2\sqrt{Dt}$ (Fick's 2nd law solution).
- **Surface hardness:** **1000 – 1200 HV** for Al-containing nitralloys.
- **Diffusion coefficient:** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ — temperature is the most powerful lever.
- **Mould/die advantage:** **No quenching = no distortion** → ideal for precision cavities.
- **Pre-treatment:** Steel must be **quenched and tempered to 28 – 32 HRC** before nitriding.
- **Plastic moulds → Plasma nitriding; Hot-work dies → Gas nitriding; Small tools → Salt-bath.**
- **Equation to memorize:** $2\text{NH}_3 \to 2[\text{N}] + 3\text{H}_2$ (ammonia dissociation).

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Hardened Steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case Hardened Steels** are a specialized class of low-carbon (typically **0.1% to 0.25% C**) or medium-carbon alloy steels whose **surface layer (case)** is selectively hardened through thermochemical diffusion or surface heat treatment, while the **interior (core)** retains its original toughness and ductility. This produces a composite structure: a hard, wear-resistant exterior shell (case depth: **0.5 mm to 4 mm**) supported by a tough, shock-absorbing interior — a metallurgical design principle vital for moulds, dies, punches, and press tools.

In the context of **PEEOT756 (Mould and Die Manufacture)**, case hardened steels are classified based on the **hardening medium** (carbon, nitrogen, or both) and the **process route** (diffusion-based, transformation-based).

---

## 1.2 Why Case Hardening? — The Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Coconut Model 🥥**
> Imagine a coconut. The outer fibrous husk is **hard and abrasion-resistant** (protects against wear), while the inner white kernel is **soft, fibrous, and tough** (absorbs shocks and sudden loads). A case hardened steel behaves exactly like this: the **surface is the husk**, the **core is the kernel**.
>
> In die-making, when a punch strikes a workpiece, the **impact is absorbed by the tough core**, while the **surface resists wear, galling, and indentation**. This dual property cannot be achieved by through-hardening alone.

### 1.3 Engineering Necessity in Mould/Die Context

| Service Condition | Required Surface Property | Required Core Property |
|---|---|---|
| Punching / Blanking | High hardness (60–65 HRC) | High toughness (no fracture) |
| Drawing dies | Wear resistance + low friction | Compressive strength |
| Plastic injection moulds | Polishability + wear resistance | Toughness to resist cracking |
| Hot forging dies | Thermal fatigue resistance | Hot toughness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Principle:** A monolithic through-hardened steel is **brittle** when fully hard. Case hardening breaks the trade-off between **hardness vs. toughness** — a *composite property profile* that single-phase heat treatment cannot deliver.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Case Hardening Processes

```
CASE HARDENING
├── 1. Diffusion-Based (Thermochemical)
│   ├── Carburizing (C diffusion)
│   │   ├── Pack carburizing
│   │   ├── Liquid carburizing
│   │   └── Gas carburizing
│   ├── Nitriding (N diffusion)
│   │   ├── Gas nitriding
│   │   ├── Plasma (ion) nitriding
│   │   └── Salt-bath nitriding
│   └── Carbonitriding / Cyaniding (C+N)
│
├── 2. Surface Transformation Hardening
│   ├── Flame hardening
│   ├── Induction hardening
│   ├── Laser hardening
│   └── Electron beam hardening
│
└── 3. Hybrid / Specialty
    ├── Vacuum carburizing
    └── Low-pressure carburizing
```

> [!NOTE]
> For **KTU 2024 Module 3**, primary focus is on **Carburizing**, **Nitriding**, **Carbonitriding**, and **Surface Hardening methods (Induction & Flame)** as applied to **die and mould steels**.

---

## 1.5 Standard Hardness Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

- **Case Hardness:** **58–65 HRC** (carburized), **800–1200 HV** (nitrided)
- **Core Hardness:** **30–45 HRC** (carburized), **25–35 HRC** (nitrided)
- **Effective Case Depth (ECD):** Depth where hardness falls to **550 HV (≈ 52 HRC)**
- **Total Case Depth (TCD):** Full depth of carbon/nitrogen enrichment
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Process 1 — CARBURIZING

### 2.1.1 Definition
**Carburizing** is a thermochemical diffusion process in which **carbon atoms** are introduced into the surface layer of low-carbon steel held at elevated temperature (**850 °C – 950 °C**) in a carbon-rich environment, followed by **quenching** to produce a hard surface.

### 2.1.2 Governing Metallurgical Reaction
The process relies on **Fick's Laws of Diffusion** to drive carbon atoms into the austenite (γ-Fe) lattice.

### 2.1.3 Why Austenite Phase is Mandatory
- Austenite has a **FCC structure** with large interstitial voids.
- Solubility of carbon in γ-Fe at 912 °C ≈ **2.0 wt%** (vs. only 0.02 wt% in α-Fe at room temperature).
- This high solubility enables deep, uniform diffusion of carbon.

---

## 2.2 The Fick's Diffusion Framework

The **case depth** achieved during carburizing is governed by the diffusion of carbon in austenite.

### Fick's First Law (Steady State)
Describes flux under constant concentration gradient.

### Fick's Second Law (Non-Steady State) — **Most Relevant for Carburizing**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

Where:

- $C$ = Carbon concentration at depth $x$ and time $t$ (wt%)
- $D$ = Diffusion coefficient of carbon in austenite ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$)
- $x$ = Depth below surface (m)
- $t$ = Time of exposure (s)

The solution, assuming semi-infinite medium with surface concentration $C_s$ held constant and initial bulk concentration $C_0$:

$$\frac{C(x, t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **erf** = **Error Function** (Gaussian integral). This is the *standard analytical solution* for diffusion-controlled surface enrichment in KTU metallurgy courses.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Carburizing & Case Hardening

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{C(x,t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | Fick's 2nd law solution for carbon profile | Predict case depth |
| 2 | $D = D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius-type diffusion coefficient | Compute $D$ at carburizing $T$ |
| 3 | $x \approx k\sqrt{t}$ | Empirical case depth vs. time | Quick design estimate |
| 4 | $C_s \approx$ Acm saturation at $T$ | Maximum surface C attainable | Verify carbon potential |
| 5 | $\text{Case Depth} \propto \sqrt{t}$ | Square-root-of-time law | Process planning |
| 6 | $\text{Time Ratio} = \dfrac{t_2}{t_1} = \left(\dfrac{x_2}{x_1}\right)^{2}$ | Time scaling with depth | Process parameter scaling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Square-root-of-time law:** Doubling the case depth requires **four times** the soaking time. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU exams.

---

## 2.4 Process 2 — NITRIDING

### 2.4.1 Definition
**Nitriding** is a thermochemical process where **nitrogen atoms** diffuse into the surface of **pre-hardened** alloy steel (containing Cr, Al, Mo, V) at **500 °C – 580 °C** (below the eutectoid temperature of 590 °C for Fe-N). **No quenching required.**

### 2.4.2 Key Difference vs. Carburizing

| Parameter | Carburizing | Nitriding |
|---|---|---|
| Diffusing element | Carbon | Nitrogen |
| Temperature | 850–950 °C | 500–580 °C |
| Quenching needed? | Yes | **No** (minimal distortion) |
| Pre-hardened? | No (hardens after C-diffusion) | Yes (must be hardened + tempered first) |
| Surface hardness | 58–65 HRC | 800–1200 HV (≈ 65–72 HRC) |
| Case depth | 0.5–4 mm | 0.1–0.8 mm (thin) |
| Distortion | High | **Very low** |
| Steel grade | Low-carbon (1020, 8620) | Alloy steels (AISI 4140, 4340, H13) |

> [!NOTE]
> **For moulds & dies**, nitriding is preferred for **plastic injection moulds** and **die-casting dies** because of minimal distortion and excellent surface finish retention.

---

## 2.5 Process 3 — CARBONITRIDING & CYANIDING

### Carbonitriding
- Simultaneous diffusion of **C + N** at **700–880 °C** in gaseous atmosphere (NH₃ + hydrocarbon gas).
- Result: **Faster diffusion** than pure carburizing due to nitrogen-enhanced carbon activity.
- Case depth: **0.1–0.75 mm**.
- Used for **thin-case** die components.

### Cyaniding
- Liquid bath process using **NaCN salt** at **750–870 °C**.
- Highly toxic — largely **discontinued** in industry.
- Of historical importance only.

---

## 2.6 Process 4 — INDUCTION HARDENING

### Definition
Surface layer is rapidly heated by **electromagnetic induction** (eddy currents), then immediately quenched. Heating rate: **100–1000 °C/s**.

### Key Equations

$$\text{Skin Depth } (\delta) = 503 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r \cdot f}}$$

Where:

- $\rho$ = Electrical resistivity of steel (Ω·m)
- $\mu_r$ = Relative magnetic permeability
- $f$ = Frequency (Hz)

| Frequency | Skin Depth | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1–10 kHz | Deep (≥ 3 mm) | Large shafts, rolls |
| 100–500 kHz | Medium (1–3 mm) | Gears, dies |
| 1–10 MHz | Shallow (< 1 mm) | Fine surfaces, cutting tools |

---

## 2.7 Process 5 — FLAME HARDENING

- Uses **oxy-acetylene or oxy-propane flame** to heat surface to **Austenitization temperature**, followed by water spray quench.
- Hardness: **55–65 HRC**.
- Case depth: **1.5–6 mm**.
- Used for **large die surfaces** (e.g., press brake dies, shear blades).

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility in Mould/Die Field

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU, remember these industrial linkages:**
> - **Carburizing (gas):** Gear dies, blanking punches, press tools
> - **Nitriding (plasma):** Plastic mould cores, die-casting dies, extrusion dies
> - **Induction hardening:** Die edges, large flat die surfaces
> - **Flame hardening:** Hammer dies, large shear blades

---

## 2.9 Step-by-Step Diffusion Logic (Why It Works)

1. **Surface adsorption:** Carbon/nitrogen atoms from atmosphere deposit on steel surface.
2. **Surface reaction:** Formation of unstable compounds → release of active atoms.
3. **Interstitial diffusion:** Atoms enter the FCC austenite (or BCC ferrite for low-T nitriding) lattice.
4. **Bulk migration:** Concentration gradient drives atoms deeper.
5. **Quenching / precipitation:** Diffused layer transforms to martensite (carburizing) or fine nitrides (nitriding) on cooling.

The **depth** is governed by $\sqrt{Dt}$ — hence the famous *parabolic growth law*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Carbon Profile After Carburizing

### Problem Setup (Standard KTU Numerical)
A low-carbon steel (initial carbon $C_0 = 0.20\%$) is gas-carburized at $T = 925 \, °C$ in an atmosphere with surface carbon potential $C_s = 1.10\%$. The diffusion coefficient of C in γ-Fe at 925 °C is $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Find:** Carbon concentration at depth $x = 0.5 \, \text{mm}$ after time $t = 4 \, \text{hours}$.

### Step 1 — Convert Units

$$x = 0.5 \, \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$$

$$t = 4 \, \text{hours} = 4 \times 3600 = 14400 \, \text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Diffusion Length

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{(1.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (14400)}$$

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{2.16 \times 10^{-7}} \, \text{m}$$

$$\sqrt{Dt} \approx 4.65 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Argument of Error Function

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 4.65 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\eta = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{9.30 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\eta \approx 0.5376$$

### Step 4 — Lookup erf(0.5376)

From standard error function tables:

$$\text{erf}(0.5376) \approx 0.5582$$

### Step 5 — Apply Fick's Solution

$$\frac{C(x,t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \text{erf}(\eta)$$

$$\frac{C - 0.20}{1.10 - 0.20} = 1 - 0.5582$$

$$\frac{C - 0.20}{0.90} = 0.4418$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Carbon Concentration

$$C - 0.20 = 0.90 \times 0.4418$$

$$C - 0.20 = 0.3976$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 0.598 \, \text{wt\% C}}$$

### Step 7 — Interpretation
> At a depth of **0.5 mm**, after **4 hours** of carburizing at 925 °C, the carbon concentration is approximately **0.60%** — sufficient to form hard martensite upon quenching (since martensite hardness is significant above ~0.40% C).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Time Required for a Given Case Depth

### Problem
For the same steel and temperature, find the time required to obtain an **effective case depth of 0.8 mm**, where effective case depth is defined as the depth at which carbon content reaches **0.40%** (the threshold for significant hardening).

### Step 1 — Set Up Concentration Condition

$$C(x, t) = 0.40\% \quad \text{at} \quad x = 0.8 \, \text{mm}$$

$$\frac{0.40 - 0.20}{1.10 - 0.20} = 1 - \text{erf}(\eta)$$

$$\frac{0.20}{0.90} = 1 - \text{erf}(\eta)$$

$$0.2222 = 1 - \text{erf}(\eta)$$

$$\text{erf}(\eta) = 0.7778$$

### Step 2 — Inverse Error Function Lookup

$$\eta = \text{erf}^{-1}(0.7778) \approx 0.856$$

### Step 3 — Relate η to Time

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{Dt} = \frac{x}{2\eta}$$

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \frac{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.856} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.712}$$

$$\sqrt{Dt} \approx 4.673 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Time

$$Dt = (4.673 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.184 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$t = \frac{2.184 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$t \approx 14560 \, \text{s} \approx 4.04 \, \text{hours}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 4 \, \text{hours}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Square-Root-of-Time Law

### Step 1 — Start with Fick's 2nd Law
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

### Step 2 — Non-dimensionalize
Introduce similarity variable $\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$.

Using chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{x}{4\sqrt{D}} \cdot t^{-3/2} \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back
$$-\frac{x}{4\sqrt{D}} \cdot t^{-3/2} \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta} = D \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

Substituting $x = 2\eta\sqrt{Dt}$:

$$-\frac{\eta}{2t} \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta} = \frac{1}{4t} \cdot \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

### Step 4 — Resulting ODE
$$\frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta \frac{dC}{d\eta} = 0$$

This is the standard form whose solution is $C(\eta) = A + B \cdot \text{erf}(\eta)$.

### Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions

- At $x = 0$ ($\eta = 0$): $C = C_s$ → $A + B \cdot 0 = C_s$ → $A = C_s$
- At $x \to \infty$ ($\eta \to \infty$): $C = C_0$ → $A + B \cdot 1 = C_0$ → $B = C_0 - C_s$

### Step 6 — Final Solution

$$C(x,t) = C_s - (C_s - C_0) \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

Rearranged:

$$\frac{C(x,t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Pack Carburizing | Gas Carburizing | Liquid Carburizing | Nitriding | Induction | Flame |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Medium | Solid (charcoal + BaCO₃) | Gas (CH₄, C₃H₈) | Salt bath (NaCN) | NH₃ gas | Eddy current | Oxy-fuel flame |
| Temp (°C) | 850–950 | 850–950 | 850–950 | 500–580 | 900–1000 (surface) | 850–950 (surface) |
| Time | 4–12 h | 1–8 h | 1–4 h | 20–90 h | seconds–minutes | minutes |
| Case depth (mm) | 0.5–4 | 0.5–4 | 0.1–2 | 0.1–0.8 | 1–10 | 1.5–6 |
| Hardness | 58–65 HRC | 58–65 HRC | 58–62 HRC | 800–1200 HV | 55–62 HRC | 55–62 HRC |
| Distortion | High | Medium | Medium | **Very low** | Low | Medium |
| Control | Poor | **Excellent** | Good | Excellent | **Excellent** | Manual |
| KTU typical use | Educational/low-end | **Industrial standard** | Specialized | **Mould/die finishing** | Localized hardening | Large dies |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem on Induction Hardening Skin Depth

**Given:** Steel with $\rho = 1.7 \times 10^{-7} \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, $\mu_r = 100$, frequency $f = 200 \, \text{kHz}$.

### Step 1 — Apply Skin Depth Formula

$$\delta = 503 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r \cdot f}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$\delta = 503 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.7 \times 10^{-7}}{100 \times 200 \times 10^{3}}}$$

$$\delta = 503 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.7 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$\delta = 503 \cdot \sqrt{8.5 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$\delta = 503 \times 9.22 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.046 \, \text{mm} \approx 46 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **electrical skin depth** — actual hardened depth is typically **2–3 times** this value, so hardened depth ≈ 0.1–0.15 mm.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Gas Carburizing Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Load Low Carbon Steel Components into Retort] --> B[Seal Furnace and Purge with Carrier Gas]
    B --> C[Heat to Carburizing Temperature 850 to 950 deg C]
    C --> D[Introduce Hydrocarbon Gas CH4 or C3H8]
    D --> E[Surface Reaction CO plus H2 yields Active Carbon]
    E --> F[Carbon Diffuses into Austenite]
    F --> G{Quench Stage}
    G -->|Direct Quench| H[Oil Quench for Martensite Formation]
    G -->|Diffusion Cycle| I[Reheat for Through Hardening]
    H --> J[Temper at 150 to 200 deg C]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Hard Case plus Tough Core Component]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Plasma Nitriding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Pre Hardened and Tempered to Core Hardness] --> B[Load into Vacuum Chamber]
    B --> C[Evacuate to Low Pressure 1 to 10 mbar]
    C --> D[Introduce NH3 Gas]
    D --> E[Apply High Voltage 400 to 1000 V DC]
    E --> F[Plasma Forms N plus Ions Bombard Surface]
    F --> G[Surface Heats to 500 to 580 deg C]
    G --> H[Nitrogen Diffuses into BCC Ferrite]
    H --> I[Cr Al Mo Form Hard Nitrides]
    I --> J[Cool under Vacuum]
    J --> K[Finished Nitride Component with Minimal Distortion]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Microstructure Gradient in Case Hardened Steel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surface Case 58 to 65 HRC] -->|Transition Zone| B[Subsurface Diffused Layer]
    B -->|Case Core Interface| C[Core 25 to 45 HRC]
    A1[Martensite plus Retained Carbides] -.-> A
    B1[Martensite plus Bainite Mix] -.-> B
    C1[Tempered Martensite or Ferrite Pearlite] -.-> C
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Carburizing vs Nitriding

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Carburizing_Process
        C1[Low Carbon Steel 0.1 to 0.25 C] --> C2[Heat 850 to 950 C]
        C2 --> C3[Carbon Diffusion in Austenite]
        C3 --> C4[Quench to Form Martensite]
        C4 --> C5[Case 58 to 65 HRC, Core 30 to 45 HRC]
    end

    subgraph Nitriding_Process
        N1[Alloy Steel Pre Hardened and Tempered] --> N2[Heat 500 to 580 C]
        N2 --> N3[Nitrogen Diffusion in Ferrite]
        N3 --> N4[No Quench Required]
        N4 --> N5[Case 800 to 1200 HV, Core 25 to 35 HRC]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Selection of Case Hardening for Dies/Moulds

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Case Hardening Process] --> B{Component Size}
    B -->|Small and Thin| C{Needs Minimal Distortion?}
    B -->|Large and Heavy| D{Needs Deep Case?}

    C -->|Yes| E[Plasma Nitriding]
    C -->|No| F[Gas Carburizing]

    D -->|Yes more than 2 mm| G[Flame Hardening]
    D -->|No less than 2 mm| H[Induction Hardening]

    E --> I[Plastic Moulds and Die Casting Dies]
    F --> J[Blanking Punches and Gear Dies]
    G --> K[Large Shear Blades and Press Dies]
    H --> L[Localized Die Edges]
```

---

## 4.6 Surface Hardening Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Steel Surface] --> B{Heat Source Type}
    B -->|Chemical| C1[Thermochemical Process]
    B -->|Electrical| C2[Induction Heating]
    B -->|Thermal| C3[Flame Heating]
    B -->|Radiation| C4[Laser or Electron Beam]

    C1 --> D1[Diffusion of C or N]
    C2 --> D2[Eddy Current Heating then Quench]
    C3 --> D3[Oxy Fuel then Quench]
    C4 --> D4[Rapid Localized Austenitization]

    D1 --> E[Hardened Case Layer]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**"Define case hardening. List any two methods of case hardening used for die steels."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Case hardening is a metallurgical surface treatment process in which the **outer layer (case)** of a low or medium-carbon steel is hardened by **diffusion of carbon or nitrogen** (or by rapid surface heating and quenching), while the **interior (core)** remains comparatively soft and tough. This produces a component with a **hard, wear-resistant surface** and a **tough, shock-absorbing core** — ideal for dies, moulds, punches, and gears.

**Two Methods (1 mark):**
1. **Carburizing** — diffusion of carbon at 850–950 °C followed by quenching.
2. **Nitriding** — diffusion of nitrogen at 500–580 °C in pre-hardened alloy steels.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**"Differentiate between carburizing and nitriding in terms of temperature and the need for quenching."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Parameter | Carburizing | Nitriding |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **850–950 °C** (austenitic range) | **500–580 °C** (ferritic range) |
| Quenching | **Required** after carbon diffusion to form martensite | **Not required**; hardening occurs via nitride precipitation on cooling |
| Steel type | Low-carbon (0.1–0.25% C) | Alloy steels with nitride formers (Cr, Al, Mo) |

The lower temperature of nitriding results in **minimal distortion** and **better dimensional control**, which is advantageous for **precision mould components**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two full alternative questions (Q-A and Q-B) provided below for student choice.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[Carburizing — Numerical + Theory]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A low-carbon steel component (initial C = 0.18%) is gas-carburized at **900 °C** for **5 hours** in an atmosphere with carbon potential **1.0%**. The diffusion coefficient of carbon in austenite at 900 °C is **D = 1.2 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s**.

**Calculate the carbon concentration at a depth of 0.6 mm below the surface.**

#### Model Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Convert Units**

$$x = 0.6 \, \text{mm} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$

$$t = 5 \, \text{h} = 5 \times 3600 = 18000 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Diffusion Length**

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^{-11}) \times (18000)}$$

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{2.16 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.648 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute η**

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 4.648 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{6.0}{9.296} \approx 0.6454$$

**Step 4 — Lookup erf(0.6454)**

From standard tables: erf(0.6454) ≈ 0.6404

**Step 5 — Apply Fick's Equation**

$$\frac{C - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \text{erf}(\eta)$$

$$\frac{C - 0.18}{1.0 - 0.18} = 1 - 0.6404 = 0.3596$$

$$\frac{C - 0.18}{0.82} = 0.3596$$

$$C = 0.18 + 0.82 \times 0.3596 = 0.18 + 0.2949$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 0.475 \, \text{wt\% C}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the gas carburizing process with a neat sketch. State two advantages over pack carburizing.**

#### Model Answer Outline
1. **Process Description (4 marks):** Component placed in sealed furnace; carrier gas (endothermic) plus enriching gas (CH₄/C₃H₈); temperature 850–950 °C; reaction $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow [\text{C}] + \text{H}_2\text{O}$; carbon dissolves in austenite; quench to form martensite.
2. **Advantages over pack carburizing (3 marks):**
   - **Better process control** — carbon potential can be precisely maintained.
   - **Uniform case depth** across complex geometries.
   - **Faster operation** — no heating lag from solid pack.
   - **Cleaner operation** — no toxic fumes or solid handling.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[Nitriding + Surface Hardening — Theory + Comparison]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected Style]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the nitriding process in detail. Explain why nitriding is preferred for plastic injection moulds.**

#### Model Answer Outline

1. **Process (4 marks):**
   - Component must be **pre-hardened and tempered** to required core hardness (e.g., H13 steel → 45–50 HRC).
   - Loaded into a sealed furnace; ammonia (NH₃) gas introduced at **500–580 °C**.
   - Reaction: $2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 2[\text{N}] + 3\text{H}_2$.
   - Active nitrogen diffuses into surface; forms **hard nitrides** with Cr, Al, Mo, V.
   - **No quenching** required — extremely low distortion.

2. **Why Preferred for Plastic Moulds (3 marks):**
   - **Minimal distortion** preserves mirror-finish polish.
   - **High surface hardness (800–1200 HV)** resists abrasion from glass-filled polymers.
   - **Retains dimensional accuracy** at elevated mould operating temperatures (~200 °C).
   - **No quenching cracks** — critical for complex mould cavities.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare induction hardening and flame hardening as surface hardening methods for dies.**

#### Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected)

| Parameter | Induction Hardening | Flame Hardening |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Electromagnetic induction | Oxy-acetylene / oxy-propane flame |
| Heating rate | Very fast (100–1000 °C/s) | Moderate |
| Process control | **Excellent** (frequency, power) | Manual / operator-dependent |
| Distortion | Minimal | Moderate |
| Case depth | 1–10 mm (frequency tunable) | 1.5–6 mm |
| Suitable for | Localized zones, complex profiles | Large flat/cylindrical surfaces |
| Production rate | High (automation-friendly) | Lower |
| Die applications | Punch tips, die edges | Hammer dies, shear blades |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Induction hardening is preferred where **precision and automation** are required; flame hardening for **large-area, low-volume die surfaces**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Case Hardening Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing effective case depth (550 HV) with total case depth (carbon penetration boundary).** Examiners explicitly distinguish these — losing 1–2 marks if conflated.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in Fick's law calculations.** A common error: using $x$ in mm and $D$ in m²/s without conversion. Always convert $x$ to meters.
>
> 3. **Stating "nitriding requires quenching"** — this is FALSE and costs full marks. Nitriding is a **sub-critical** process; hardness develops from nitride precipitation, not martensitic transformation.
>
> 4. **Ignoring that carburizing needs post-quench tempering.** Without tempering at 150–200 °C, the as-quenched martensite is brittle and prone to cracking in service.
>
> 5. **Drawing carburizing furnace diagrams without labeling temperature zones, gas inlets, and quench tank** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for incomplete sketches.
>
> 6. **Mixing up "case" and "core" hardness values** in numerical questions — always state **two separate values**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Case Hardened Steels**

- ✅ Case hardening produces a **hard surface + tough core** — a composite property impossible with through-hardening.
- ✅ **Carburizing**: C diffusion at 850–950 °C in low-carbon steels; **requires quenching**; typical case 58–65 HRC.
- ✅ **Nitriding**: N diffusion at 500–580 °C in pre-hardened **alloy** steels (Cr/Al/Mo/V); **no quenching**; case 800–1200 HV.
- ✅ **Fick's 2nd law** governs diffusion: $\dfrac{C-C_0}{C_s-C_0} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$.
- ✅ **Square-root-of-time law**: Doubling case depth requires **4× time**.
- ✅ **Arrhenius behavior** of $D$: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ — higher $T$ → much faster diffusion.
- ✅ **Induction hardening** depth controlled by frequency: lower $f$ → deeper case.
- ✅ **Skin depth formula**: $\delta = 503\sqrt{\rho/(\mu_r f)}$.
- ✅ **For moulds & dies**: prefer **plasma nitriding** (low distortion) and **gas carburizing** (deep case).
- ✅ **Carburized case depth**: typically **0.5–4 mm**; **Nitrided case depth**: typically **0.1–0.8 mm**.
- ✅ **Cyaniding is obsolete** due to cyanide toxicity — mention only as historical context.
- ✅ **Carbonitriding** combines C+N diffusion for **thin, hard cases** with reduced distortion.
- ✅ **Effective case depth (ECD)** is measured at **550 HV** (≈ 52 HRC) — a KTU favourite.
- ✅ **Steel selection** rule: low C for carburizing; alloy steels (4140, H13, 4340) for nitriding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Resistant Steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Resistant Steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Corrosion Resistant Steels (CRS)**, in the context of mould and die manufacture, are a specialized class of alloy steels containing a minimum of **10.5% Chromium (Cr)** as the principal alloying element, often supplemented with Nickel (Ni), Molybdenum (Mo), Nitrogen (N), and Copper (Cu). The high chromium content enables the formation of a thin, tenacious, self-healing passive oxide layer (predominantly **Cr₂O₃**) on the steel surface, which acts as a kinetic barrier against electrochemical degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT756 - Module 3):** The 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes the classification, metallurgy, heat treatment, and selection criteria of corrosion resistant steels for plastic injection moulds, die-casting dies, and compression moulds operating in aggressive thermal and chemical environments.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you bought an expensive new car. To protect it from rust during monsoon rains, you get a high-quality **ceramic coating** applied. This thin transparent layer doesn't stop the water from touching the surface completely, but it *chemically bonds* with the paint and, if scratched, naturally re-forms to seal the damage. 

**Corrosion resistant steels work exactly the same way:**
- The **Chromium** inside the steel is the "ceramic coating factory" — it migrates to the surface and reacts with oxygen to form a **2–3 nanometer thick Cr₂O₃ passive film**.
- This film is **self-healing** (re-forms instantly if scratched in the presence of oxygen).
- **Nickel and Molybdenum** are like "upgraded additives" that strengthen this film against specific threats (acids, chlorides).

Without enough Cr (<10.5%), the car has no factory to build its protective skin — and it rusts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** The **10.5% Cr threshold** is the universally accepted lower limit (per ISO 15510 and ASTM A941) below which a steel cannot be classified as "stainless" or corrosion resistant.

## 1.3 Relevance in Mould and Die Manufacture

Moulds and dies are subjected to some of the harshest service conditions in manufacturing:

| Service Threat | Source | Affected Mould Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical attack** | Molten polymers releasing HCl, acetic acid, formaldehyde | PVC, POM, ABS injection moulds |
| **Galvanic corrosion** | Dissimilar metals in contact | Die-casting dies with copper inserts |
| **Condensation corrosion** | Cooling water channels | All water-cooled moulds |
| **Oxidation at high T** | Hot-work environment | Forging dies, die-casting |
| **Pitting/crevice** | Chloride-rich coolants | Marine-grade mould applications |

The **failure of a single mould** due to corrosion can cost anywhere from **₹50,000 to ₹50,00,000+** in repair/replacement, justifying the premium cost of CRS.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (Preview)

The three principal families used in mould/die work are:

1. **Martensitic Stainless Steels** (e.g., AISI 420, 440C) — Hardenable, used for plastic moulds needing wear resistance.
2. **Austenitic Stainless Steels** (e.g., AISI 304, 316) — Non-hardenable, used for mirror-finish moulds and food-grade dies.
3. **Precipitation Hardening (PH) Stainless Steels** (e.g., 17-4 PH, 15-5 PH, PH 13-8 Mo) — Combines corrosion resistance with high strength via aging.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "M A P"** for the three families: **M**artensitic, **A**ustenitic, **P**H steels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Classification & Metallurgical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Martensitic Stainless Steels (M-Series)

**Composition Range:** 11.5–18% Cr, 0.1–1.2% C, optional Ni/Mo.

**Key Grades for Mould Work:**
- **AISI 420 (1.4028):** 12–14% Cr, ~0.35% C — workhorse for plastic moulds.
- **AISI 440C (1.4125):** 16–18% Cr, ~1.0% C — highest hardness (~60 HRC), for highly wear-resistant inserts.

**Microstructure:** Body-Centered Tetragonal (BCT) martensite formed by quenching from austenite. Hardenable by **quench + temper**, similar to tool steels.

**Step Logic — Why Martensitic Works for Moulds:**
- Step 1: Austenitize at 1000–1050 °C → Cr fully dissolves in FCC austenite.
- Step 2: Rapid quench (oil/air) → diffusionless transformation to hard BCT martensite.
- Step 3: Temper at 150–300 °C → secondary carbide precipitation + Cr redistribution to form passive film.
- Step 4: **Result:** Hardness 50–60 HRC + moderate corrosion resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Corrosion Trade-off:** Higher C content (better hardness) **consumes Cr** by forming Cr-carbides, **reducing** the free Cr available for the passive film. Therefore, AISI 440C is harder but slightly less corrosion resistant than AISI 420.

### 2.1.2 Austenitic Stainless Steels (A-Series)

**Composition Range:** 16–26% Cr, 8–22% Ni, <0.08% C.

**Key Grades:**
- **AISI 304 (1.4301):** 18% Cr + 8% Ni — the classic "18/8" stainless.
- **AISI 316 (1.4401):** 16% Cr + 10% Ni + 2% Mo — superior chloride resistance ("marine grade").

**Microstructure:** Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) austenite, retained at all temperatures. **Non-magnetic** and **non-hardenable** by conventional heat treatment.

**Step Logic — Why Austenitic is Used for Optical/Polished Moulds:**
- Step 1: Single-phase FCC structure means no phase transformation on cooling → **no distortion**.
- Step 2: Soft (~200–250 HV) → easily polished to **mirror finish (Ra < 0.01 μm)**.
- Step 3: High Ni stabilizes austenite → excellent toughness, even at sub-zero.
- Step 4: Excellent general corrosion resistance due to high Cr + Ni synergy.

### 2.1.3 Precipitation Hardening (PH) Stainless Steels

**Composition:** 15–17% Cr, 3–5% Ni, 3–5% Cu (or Al, Ti, Mo), <0.07% C.

**The Three Workhorses:**
| Grade | Key Alloying | Typical Hardness (Aged) | Mould Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **17-4 PH** | 4% Cu | 32–44 HRC | Injection mould cores, inserts |
| **15-5 PH** | 4% Cu + low C | 28–42 HRC | Large plastic moulds, aerospace dies |
| **PH 13-8 Mo** | 8% Al + 1% Mo | 33–48 HRC | High-strength die-casting components |

**Step Logic — The PH Heat Treatment Cycle:**
- Step 1: **Solution Annealing** at 1020–1060 °C → dissolves Cu in austenite/FCC matrix, then cool to obtain supersaturated martensite (or austenite in 15-5).
- Step 2: **Cooling** (air) → diffusionless transformation to low-carbon martensite (BCT), still soft (~30 HRC) and machinable.
- Step 3: **Aging (Precipitation Hardening)** at 480–620 °C for 1–4 hours → fine Cu-rich ε-phase precipitates form → massive secondary hardening.
- Step 4: **Result:** High strength (up to 1500 MPa UTS) + excellent corrosion resistance + minimal distortion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** PH steels are the **most exam-relevant** family in mould/die courses because they uniquely combine **tool-steel strength** with **stainless-steel corrosion resistance**, making them the modern default for high-performance plastic injection moulds.

## 2.2 The Passivation Mechanism — The "Why" Behind Corrosion Resistance

The passive film forms via the following electrochemical sequence:

1. Cr atoms in the steel migrate to the surface (driven by Cr's higher oxygen affinity vs. Fe).
2. Surface Cr reacts with dissolved O₂ in the environment: **4 Cr + 3 O₂ → 2 Cr₂O₃**
3. A continuous, amorphous, ~2 nm Cr₂O₃ film covers the surface.
4. If mechanically damaged, the film **re-passivates** within milliseconds (provided O₂ is present).

This film is **insoluble**, **electrically insulating**, and **self-repairing** — making the underlying metal kinetically inert.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application in Mould/Die |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number)** | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | Cr, Mo, N in weight % | Quantifies chloride pitting resistance. PREN > 32 → "marine grade" |
| **Corrosion Rate (mm/yr)** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | W: mass loss (mg), D: density (g/cm³), A: area (cm²), T: time (hr) | Predicts mould service life in corrosive polymer processing |
| **Corrosion Rate (mpy)** | $\text{CR}_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | Same variables (W in mg, A in in²) | Imperial version, used in ASTM G1 |
| **Faraday's Law Mass Loss** | $W = \dfrac{I \times t \times M}{n \times F}$ | I: current (A), t: time (s), M: molar mass, n: valence, F: 96,485 C/mol | Theoretical mass loss during galvanic corrosion |
| **Yield Strength (PH steels, approx)** | $\sigma_{y} \approx 250 + k \cdot V_{f}^{1/2}$ | V_f: volume fraction of precipitates; k: strengthening coefficient | Predicts effect of aging on hardness |
| **Nernst Equation (Passivation potential)** | $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | E: electrode potential, R: 8.314 J/(mol·K), T: K, F: 96,485 C/mol | Defines conditions for stable passive film |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Point:** The **PREN formula** has appeared in multiple KTU past papers. Memorize it as: **"Cr + 3.3 Mo + 16 N"** — Mo is weighted ×3.3 because it is ~3.3× more effective than Cr per weight % in resisting pitting; N is weighted ×16.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plastic Injection Moulding:** PVC and flame-retardant grades release HCl during processing → 17-4 PH or 420 moulds mandatory to survive 100,000+ cycles.
- **Die Casting:** Molten aluminum (660 °C) is less aggressive than plastics chemically, but **soldering** and **erosion-corrosion** demand CRS die inserts.
- **Medical Moulds:** Surgical instruments and implant moulds require 316L (low C variant) for biocompatibility.
- **Marine/Corrosive Moulds:** Offshore components require grades with PREN > 35 (e.g., 254 SMO, AL-6XN, or super-austenitic).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PREN Threshold Logic for Mould Selection

The PREN formula is **empirically derived** from linear regression of thousands of corrosion tests. The derivation logic is as follows:

**Step 1:** Begin with a single-element contribution concept.
Each element that improves pitting resistance contributes a *linear* term to the total resistance. Let R be the pitting resistance.

$$R = a \cdot \%\text{Cr} + b \cdot \%\text{Mo} + c \cdot \%\text{N}$$

**Step 2:** Set Cr as the reference (coefficient = 1).
By convention, Cr's contribution is normalized to unity, so a = 1.

$$R = \%\text{Cr} + b \cdot \%\text{Mo} + c \cdot \%\text{N}$$

**Step 3:** Determine Mo coefficient from experimental data.
Statistical regression on chloride pitting data shows Mo is ~3.3× more effective than Cr per weight % (because MoO₄²⁻ is more stable than Cr₂O₃ in chloride environments).

$$b = 3.3$$

**Step 4:** Determine N coefficient.
Nitrogen stabilizes austenite and forms nitrides at pit initiation sites, giving N a much higher effectiveness (~16× Cr).

$$c = 16$$

**Step 5:** Finalize the empirical formula.

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is not a fundamental physical law but a **dimensionless ranking index** — higher PREN means better pitting resistance. The ASTM G48 standardized test (ferric chloride pitting test) is used to validate PREN values.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law

This derivation shows the theoretical basis of the mass-loss corrosion rate formula.

**Step 1:** State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis.
The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is proportional to the charge passed.

$$W \propto Q = I \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the molar mass and valence.
One mole of electrons (1 Faraday = F = 96,485 C) liberates M/n grams of substance, where M is the molar mass and n is the valence (number of electrons transferred per atom).

$$W = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F} \cdot \dfrac{M}{n}$$

**Step 3:** Convert mass loss to corrosion penetration depth.
Penetration depth = mass loss / (density × area). Let D = density (g/cm³), A = exposed area (cm²), t = exposure time (hours).

$$\text{Depth} = \dfrac{W}{D \cdot A} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot D \cdot A}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mm/year using unit conversions.
A = exposed area in cm², t = time in hours. Multiply by (1 cm / 10 mm) and (8760 hr / 1 yr):

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

The constant **87.6** arises from the unit conversion: (10 mm/cm) × (8760 hr/yr) / 1000 mg/g = 87.6.

**Step 5:** Sanity check with units.

$$\dfrac{\text{mg}}{\left(\text{g/cm}^3\right) \cdot \text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{hr}} = \dfrac{\text{cm}}{\text{hr}} \to \dfrac{\text{mm}}{\text{yr}} \;\;\checkmark$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** A 17-4 PH mould insert (17% Cr, 4% Ni, 3% Cu, 0.02% N) is exposed to a chloride-rich coolant. Calculate its PREN and classify its corrosion resistance. Compare with 316 stainless (17% Cr, 2% Mo, 10% Ni, 0.02% N).

**Given (17-4 PH):**
- Cr = 17%, Mo = 0% (negligible), N = 0.02%

**Given (316):**
- Cr = 17%, Mo = 2%, N = 0.02%

**Step 1: Calculate PREN for 17-4 PH**

$$\text{PREN}_{17\text{-}4} = 17 + 3.3 \times 0 + 16 \times 0.02$$

$$\text{PREN}_{17\text{-}4} = 17 + 0 + 0.32 = 17.32$$

**Step 2: Calculate PREN for 316**

$$\text{PREN}_{316} = 17 + 3.3 \times 2 + 16 \times 0.02$$

$$\text{PREN}_{316} = 17 + 6.6 + 0.32 = 23.92$$

**Step 3: Classify using the standard PREN scale**

| PREN Range | Classification | Application |
|---|---|---|
| < 18 | Basic stainless (mild) | Indoor moulds, dry environment |
| 18–25 | Standard stainless (austenitic 304/316) | General plastic moulds |
| 25–32 | Improved / marine grade | Chloride-exposed moulds |
| > 32 | Super-austenitic / super-duplex | Severe chemical/oceanic moulds |

**Step 4: Interpretation**

$$\text{PREN}_{17\text{-}4} = 17.32 \;\;<\;\; 18 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{ Basic stainless class (suitable for dry/indoor moulds only)}$$

$$\text{PREN}_{316} = 23.92 \;\; \in \;\; [18, 25] \;\; \Rightarrow \text{ Standard stainless (suitable for general chemical moulds)}$$

**Conclusion:** 17-4 PH has *inferior* chloride pitting resistance compared to 316, despite its higher strength. For moulds in chloride-rich environments, **316 (or even 316L) is preferred** unless high strength is also needed, in which case a **PH steel with added Mo (e.g., PH 13-8 Mo)** should be considered.

## 3.4 Corrosion Rate Numerical Example

**Problem:** A mould cavity of surface area 250 cm² loses 120 mg of mass after 720 hours of exposure to a corrosive PVC processing environment. The density of the mould steel (AISI 420) is 7.75 g/cm³. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/year.

**Given:**
- W = 120 mg
- D = 7.75 g/cm³
- A = 250 cm²
- T = 720 hr

**Step 1: Substitute into the formula**

$$\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 120}{7.75 \times 250 \times 720}$$

**Step 2: Numerator**

$$87.6 \times 120 = 10{,}512$$

**Step 3: Denominator**

$$7.75 \times 250 \times 720 = 1{,}395{,}000$$

**Step 4: Final division**

$$\text{CR} = \dfrac{10{,}512}{1{,}395{,}000} = 0.00754 \;\; \text{mm/yr}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

A corrosion rate of < 0.01 mm/yr is **"Excellent"** per NACE classification. The AISI 420 mould is performing well, but if continued exposure accumulates, the **service life can be estimated:**

$$\text{Service Life} \approx \dfrac{\text{Corrosion allowance (mm)}}{\text{CR}} = \dfrac{0.5 \;\text{mm}}{0.00754} \approx 66 \;\text{years}$$

This indicates the mould is **safe for long-term service** under the given conditions.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Property | Martensitic (420) | Austenitic (304) | PH Steel (17-4 PH) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness (HRC)** | 50–56 (as-hardened) | 15–25 (cannot be hardened) | 32–44 (aged) |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 700–1800 | 500–750 | 1000–1500 |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Moderate | Excellent | Very Good |
| **Weldability** | Poor (preheat required) | Excellent | Good (in solution-annealed condition) |
| **Magnetism** | Magnetic | Non-magnetic | Slightly magnetic (martensitic matrix) |
| **Polishability (Ra μm)** | 0.05–0.1 | < 0.01 (mirror) | 0.02–0.05 |
| **Distortion in Heat Treatment** | High | None | Very Low |
| **Relative Cost** | Low | Medium | High (3–5×) |
| **Best Mould Use** | Wear-resistant plastic moulds | Optical/lens moulds | High-performance engineering plastic moulds |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions in ESE (End Semester Exam) are compulsory, testing direct conceptual recall. Each carries 3 marks with strict keyword-based valuation.

### **Question 1: Define PREN and state its significance in mould material selection.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) is an empirical index that quantifies a stainless steel's resistance to pitting corrosion in chloride-rich environments. It is calculated as **PREN = %Cr + 3.3 × %Mo + 16 × %N**.
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** In mould material selection, PREN helps the designer choose between grades for chloride-exposed moulds (e.g., PVC processing, marine applications). Higher PREN (typically > 25) indicates suitability for aggressive chloride environments, while PREN < 18 limits use to dry/indoor conditions. For example, AISI 316 (PREN ≈ 24) is preferred over 17-4 PH (PREN ≈ 17) for chloride-exposed plastic moulds.

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Martensitic and Austenitic stainless steels with respect to microstructure and mould applications.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Martensitic (e.g., 420) | Austenitic (e.g., 304) |
|---|---|---|
| **Microstructure** | BCT martensite after quenching | Stable FCC austenite at all temperatures |
| **Hardenability** | Hardenable by quench + temper | Non-hardenable by heat treatment |
| **Magnetism** | Magnetic | Non-magnetic |
| **Mould Application** | Plastic moulds needing wear resistance and strength (cores, inserts) | Optical/lens moulds, food-grade moulds needing mirror polish and superior corrosion resistance |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts typically split as **7 + 7**. Students must answer with neat diagrams, tabulated data, and stepwise reasoning to score full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Classify corrosion resistant steels used in mould and die manufacture. Explain the composition, microstructure, and properties of Martensitic stainless steels with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Classification (2 marks):**
Corrosion resistant steels for moulds and dies are classified into four main families based on microstructure:
- Martensitic (e.g., AISI 420, 440C)
- Austenitic (e.g., AISI 304, 316, 316L)
- Precipitation Hardening (e.g., 17-4 PH, 15-5 PH, PH 13-8 Mo)
- Duplex (e.g., 2205, 2507)

**2. Composition of Martensitic Stainless Steels (2 marks):**
- Chromium: 11.5–18%
- Carbon: 0.1–1.2% (high C for hardness, low C for corrosion)
- Optional: Ni (1–2%) and Mo (up to 1%) for enhanced properties
- **AISI 420** has 12–14% Cr + 0.35% C; **AISI 440C** has 16–18% Cr + 1.0% C.

**3. Microstructure (2 marks):**
- At high temperature (~1000 °C): FCC austenite with dissolved Cr and C.
- On quenching: Diffusionless transformation to Body-Centered Tetragonal (BCT) martensite with dispersed Cr-carbides (M₂₃C₆).
- On tempering (150–300 °C): Fine carbide precipitation + Cr redistribution to enable passive film formation.

**4. Properties and Mould Application (1 mark):**
- Hardness: 50–60 HRC after heat treatment
- Good wear resistance and moderate corrosion resistance
- Magnetic, moderate toughness
- **Application:** Plastic injection mould cores, inserts, and die-casting components requiring both wear and corrosion resistance.

---

**(b)** Describe the heat treatment of 17-4 PH steel. Explain how the aging temperature affects its properties for mould applications. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. The Three-Stage Heat Treatment Process (3 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Solution Annealing:**
- Heat to 1020–1060 °C for 30 min per inch of thickness.
- Purpose: Dissolve Cu-rich phases into a single-phase austenitic matrix.
- Cool rapidly (air cool) → forms low-carbon martensite (BCT) at room temperature.
- Hardness after this stage: ~30–35 HRC (soft, machinable).

**Stage 2 — Optional Stress Relief:**
- Hold at ~200 °C for 1 hour to relieve machining stresses without precipitating Cu.

**Stage 3 — Aging (Precipitation Hardening):**
- Reheat to 480–620 °C for 1–4 hours.
- Fine Cu-rich ε-phase precipitates form, producing secondary hardening.
- Air cool to room temperature.

**2. Effect of Aging Temperature on Properties (3 marks):**

| Aging Condition | Temperature (°C) | Time (hr) | Hardness (HRC) | Best Mould Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A (overaged)** | 480 | 1 | ~42 | Highest strength |
| **H900** | 482 | 1 | ~44 | Max hardness |
| **H1025** | 552 | 4 | ~38 | Balanced strength/toughness |
| **H1150** | 621 | 4 | ~33 | Max toughness, best corrosion resistance |

- **Lower aging temperature** → finer precipitates → higher hardness but lower toughness.
- **Higher aging temperature** → coarser precipitates → lower hardness but better toughness and corrosion resistance.
- For moulds, **H1025 is often preferred** as it offers a balance between machinability, strength, and corrosion resistance.

**3. Why This is Ideal for Moulds (1 mark):**
- The ability to machine in the soft solution-annealed condition and then harden by simple aging (no quenching) **minimizes distortion** — critical for precision mould cavities.
- Combines tool-steel strength with stainless-steel corrosion resistance, ideal for PVC, POM, and corrosive polymer processing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of passivation in corrosion resistant steels. Discuss the role of chromium, nickel, and molybdenum in enhancing corrosion resistance. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. The Passivation Mechanism (3 marks):**
- Passivation is the formation of a thin, invisible, self-healing oxide film (~2–3 nm) on the steel surface that drastically reduces the corrosion rate.
- The film is primarily **chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃)** formed by the reaction: **4 Cr + 3 O₂ → 2 Cr₂O₃**.
- The Cr required for this film comes from the bulk steel, which must contain at least **10.5% Cr** to ensure a continuous, defect-free passive film.
- The film is **amorphous, electrically insulating, and self-repairing** — if mechanically damaged, it re-forms within milliseconds in the presence of oxygen.

**2. Role of Alloying Elements (4 marks):**

| Element | Role | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Chromium (Cr)** | Primary passivator | Forms Cr₂O₃ film; minimum 10.5% required |
| **Nickel (Ni)** | Austenite stabilizer | Promotes FCC structure; improves resistance to reducing acids (e.g., H₂SO₄); enhances toughness |
| **Molybdenum (Mo)** | Chloride resistance | Forms MoO₄²⁻ in passive film, which is more stable than Cr₂O₃ in chloride environments; raises PREN by 3.3× per wt% |

**3. Conclusion (1 mark):**
The synergy of Cr + Ni + Mo provides comprehensive corrosion protection in mould/die applications: Cr gives general corrosion resistance, Ni adds acid resistance, and Mo adds chloride pitting resistance. This is why AISI 316 (with Mo) outperforms AISI 304 in chloride environments, and why PH 13-8 Mo outperforms 17-4 PH in aggressive mould services.

---

**(b)** Compare AISI 420, AISI 304, and 17-4 PH steels for plastic injection mould applications. Justify your selection with reference to properties, applications, and cost. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Tabulated Comparison (3 marks):**

| Property | AISI 420 (Martensitic) | AISI 304 (Austenitic) | 17-4 PH (Precipitation Hardening) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness (HRC)** | 50–56 | 15–25 | 32–44 (aged) |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 700–1800 | 500–750 | 1000–1500 |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Moderate | Excellent | Very Good |
| **Polishability (Ra μm)** | 0.05–0.1 | < 0.01 (mirror) | 0.02–0.05 |
| **Distortion in HT** | High | None | Very Low |
| **Relative Cost (per kg)** | ₹250–400 | ₹350–500 | ₹1200–2000 |
| **Weldability** | Poor | Excellent | Good (annealed only) |
| **PREN** | ~12 | ~19 | ~17 |

**2. Application-Specific Selection (3 marks):**

- **For general-purpose plastic moulds (PP, PE, PS moulding):** **AISI 420** is the most cost-effective choice, offering good hardness and adequate corrosion resistance.
- **For optical/lens/medical moulds requiring mirror finish and food-grade compliance:** **AISI 304** is the best choice due to its superior polishability, biocompatibility, and non-magnetic nature.
- **For high-performance engineering plastic moulds (PVC, POM, PPS, fluoropolymers) requiring high strength + corrosion resistance:** **17-4 PH (H1025 condition)** is the optimal choice despite its higher cost, because the long service life (often >1 million cycles) and minimal distortion justify the premium.

**3. Cost Justification (1 mark):**
While 17-4 PH costs 4–5× more than 420, its **service life is typically 3–4× longer** in corrosive polymer environments, leading to a lower total cost of ownership (TCO) over the mould's life cycle.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CRS Questions:**
> 1. **PREN formula error:** Students frequently write "Cr + Mo + N" without the **3.3 and 16 coefficients** — this is a 1-mark deduction. Always write the **complete weighted formula**.
> 2. **Confusing "stainless" with "corrosion resistant":** All stainless steels are corrosion resistant, but not all CRS are stainless (e.g., weathering steels). Use the term **"stainless" only for Cr > 10.5%.**
> 3. **Heat treatment confusion:** Students mix up the **tempering temperature** for martensitic (150–300 °C) with the **aging temperature** for PH steels (480–620 °C). Remember: **Tempering = martensitic; Aging = PH.**
> 4. **Forgetting units in corrosion rate:** Always specify **mm/yr or mpy** — bare numbers without units get 0.5 mark deducted.
> 5. **Neglecting to draw TTT/CCT diagrams:** For 17-4 PH or 420 questions, **always include a labeled heat treatment cycle diagram** — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for neat schematics.

---

- **Three primary CRS families for moulds:** Martensitic, Austenitic, PH — remember the **"M A P"** mnemonic.
- **PREN formula:** %Cr + 3.3·%Mo + 16·%N — **must memorize with coefficients** for KTU exams.
- **Martensitic (420):** High hardness (50–60 HRC), magnetic, hardenable, used for wear-resistant plastic moulds.
- **Austenitic (304/316):** Non-magnetic, non-hardenable, best polishability, used for optical/food-grade moulds.
- **17-4 PH (most exam-relevant):** Solution anneal → machine → age. Aging at 482 °C = H900 (max hardness), 552 °C = H1025 (balanced), 621 °C = H1150 (max toughness).
- **Passivation mechanism:** Cr + O₂ → Cr₂O₃ (2–3 nm film) — self-healing, electrically insulating.
- **Molybdenum's role:** Boosts chloride pitting resistance — gives "marine grade" status to 316 over 304.
- **Corrosion rate formula:** CR (mm/yr) = (87.6 × W) / (D × A × T) — always state units in the final answer.
- **Selection logic:** PVC/aggressive plastics → 17-4 PH; Optical → 304/316; Wear-resistant cores → 420; High-temp die casting → H13 (not CRS alone, but often used with CRS coatings).
- **Distortion advantage:** PH steels cause minimal distortion during heat treatment — critical for precision mould cavities.
- **Cost ranking:** 420 < 304 < 316 < 17-4 PH < PH 13-8 Mo (cheapest to most expensive).
- **Hardness ranking (typical):** 440C (~60 HRC) > 420 (~52 HRC) > 17-4 PH H900 (44 HRC) > 17-4 PH H1025 (38 HRC) > 17-4 PH H1150 (33 HRC) > 304 (~20 HRC).
- **For diagrams in exams:** Always draw a TTT/CCT sketch for 420, a heat treatment cycle for 17-4 PH, and a labeled PREN bar chart when comparing grades.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maraging Steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Maraging Steels # MARAGING STEELS – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Maraging Steels is a *tool-and-die material topic* that bridges **metallurgy** (phase transformations) with **manufacturing** (tool life, machinability, dimensional stability). Expect it in **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition/classification question and in **Part B (14 marks)** as a heat-treatment or application-oriented essay. --- ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition **Maraging Steels** are a special class of **ultra-high-strength low-carbon martensitic steels** (carbon content typically **< 0.03 %**) that derive their strength not from carbon-induced martensite hardening, but from the **precipitation of intermetallic compounds** during a low-temperature **aging treatment** carried out after solution annealing. The name itself is a portmanteau: - **MAR** → **MARTENSITE** (the tough, ductile, low-carbon Fe-Ni lath martensite formed on quenching) - **AGING** → the precipitation-hardening (age-hardening) heat treatment that develops the strength > [!NOTE] > **Standard Composition Window (KTU-recommended):** > - **Ni:** 17 – 25 % > - **Co:** 8 – 13 % > - **Mo:** 3 – 5 % > - **Ti:** 0.2 – 1.8 % > - **Al:** 0.1 – 0.2 % > - **C:** ≤ 0.03 % > - **Fe:** Balance The most industrially important family is the **18 Ni (250, 300, 350) series**, where the digits denote the **nominal yield strength in ksi** (kilo-pounds per square inch). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Plain Cake vs. Decorated Cake" Imagine baking a plain sponge cake — soft, fluffy, easy to cut (this is **annealed maraging steel**, easy to machine, soft ~ 30 HRC). Now, you spread a layer of rich, hard icing on top, layer by layer, in a warm oven for a few hours (this is **aging**, where tiny hard particles of Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, Fe₂Mo "decorate" the soft martensite matrix). The cake is now extremely strong — it can support heavy weights — but it kept its shape and didn't crack (the steel retained its **toughness** and **dimensional stability**). This is exactly what maraging does: - **Soft sponge (low C martensite)** → easy to machine into complex die geometry. - **Hard icing (intermetallic precipitates)** → gives final hardness of **50–60 HRC**. --- ## 1.3 Why Maraging Steels Matter in Mould & Die Manufacture | Engineering Need | How Maraging Steel Answers It | |---|---| | Complex die cavities that must be machined before hardening | **Solution-annealed state is soft (~ 30 HRC)** → excellent machinability | | Large moulds prone to distortion during quenching | **Aging at 480 °C is below the martensite finish (Mf)** → minimal distortion | | Need to repair/repair-weld a die in service | **Carbon-free martensite is weldable without pre-heating** | | Polished plastic mould surfaces need mirror finish | **Clean lath martensite + no carbides → excellent polishability** | | Long die life under cyclic loading | **High fracture toughness (~ 80–110 MPa·√m)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hardness–Toughness Paradox Resolved:** Conventional tool steels (H13, D2, P20) gain hardness at the cost of toughness. Maraging steels break this trade-off — they combine **> 2000 MPa tensile strength with K_IC values comparable to much softer alloys**, making them the material of choice for **high-performance plastic moulds, die-casting inserts, and aerospace die tooling**. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet --- ## 2.1 The Three-Stage Strengthening Logic Maraging steels exploit **three simultaneous strengthening mechanisms** acting inside an essentially carbon-free martensitic matrix. KTU examiners reward answers that *explicitly name* all three. ### Stage 1 — Solution Annealing (Homogenization) - **Temperature:** $820^{\circ}\text{C} - 900^{\circ}\text{C}$ (typically **$820^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 1 hour per 25 mm of section**) - **Cooling:** Air cool (or inert gas) — austenite (γ-Fe, FCC) transforms to **lath martensite (α', BCT)** because of massive Ni content - **Result:** Soft, ductile, fully martensitic, all alloying elements in **supersaturated solid solution** - **Hardness after this stage:** ~ **28 – 32 HRC** (280 – 320 HV) ### Stage 2 — Quench / Air-Cool to Room Temperature - No separate quench tank needed — Ni lowers the Mf (martensite finish) temperature but also stabilizes retained austenite to negligible levels - **Critical point:** Because carbon is virtually absent, the martensite is **NOT brittle**. It is ductile lath martensite (dislocated, not twinned). - The matrix is now a *saturated* but *weak* foundation. ### Stage 3 — Aging (Precipitation Hardening) - **Temperature:** $450^{\circ}\text{C} - 510^{\circ}\text{C}$, most common is **$480^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 3 – 6 hours** - Mechanism: nucleation and growth of **coherent/semi-coherent intermetallic precipitates** - **Key strengthening precipitates:** - $\text{Ni}_3\text{Ti}$ — principal strengthener (forms first) - $\text{Ni}_3\text{Mo}$ — secondary - $\text{Fe}_2\text{Mo}$ (Laves phase) — co-precipitates - $\text{Ni}_3\text{Al}$ — contributes to high-temperature stability > [!NOTE] > **Over-Aging Warning (Examiner Favourite!):** If aging temperature exceeds ~ $540^{\circ}\text{C}$ or time is extended excessively, precipitates **coarsen (Ostwald ripening)**, lose coherency with the matrix, and the strength **drops**. The classic **tempered-martensite embrittlement zone** (300 – 400 °C) is *avoided* because there is no carbon to form cementite. --- ## 2.2 Strength–Toughness Microstructural Rationale The lath martensite matrix of maraging steels is **dislocation-rich but carbon-free**, which means: 1. Dislocation motion is not pinned by interstitial C atoms → ductility preserved. 2. Grain boundaries are not decorated by brittle carbide films → high toughness. 3. Aging precipitates are *finely dispersed* and remain coherent → dislocation–precipitate interaction (Orowan looping / particle shearing) gives high strength **without** the decohesion seen in high-carbon tempered martensite. This is why 18Ni(300) achieves: - $\sigma_y \approx 2070 \text{ MPa}$ - $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 2100 \text{ MPa}$ - $K_{IC} \approx 80 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}$ - Elongation $\approx 8 - 10\%$ A combination **virtually unattainable in carbon-based tool steels**. --- ## 2.3 Industrial Grade System (KTU Must-Know Table) | Grade Designation | Nominal $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | Approx. Hardness (HRC) | Typical Tool/Die Application | |---|---|---|---| | **18 Ni (200)** | 1380 | 44 – 48 | General-purpose injection mould cores | | **18 Ni (250)** | 1720 | 48 – 52 | Aluminium die-casting dies, rubber moulds | | **18 Ni (300)** | 2070 | 53 – 58 | Aerospace dies, large plastic moulds | | **18 Ni (350)** | 2400 | 58 – 60 | High-stress forging die inserts | | **18 Ni (400)** (Cast) | 2760 | 60+ | Missile motor cases, ultra-high-strength parts | | **C-250 / C-300 (Cast variants)** | 1720 / 2070 | Same as wrought | Large mould bases where forging is impractical | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorise the equations below — they appear directly in numerical problems.** | Equation | Meaning / Engineering Use | Units | |---|---|---| | $\sigma_y \text{ (MPa)} \approx 6.9 \times \text{Grade Number}$ | Converts grade (ksi) to MPa (e.g., 300 ksi → 2070 MPa) | MPa | | $\text{HRC} \approx 0.025\,\sigma_y$ (approx., linear fit 1000–2500 MPa) | Quick yield-strength ↔ hardness conversion | – | | $d_{p} = \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{N_v}}$ | Hall–Petch-like refinement; finer precipitate spacing → higher strength | nm | | $\Delta\sigma_{ppt} \propto \dfrac{G\,b\,f^{1/2}}{r}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{b}\right)$ | Ashby–Orowan looping (precipitation strengthening) | MPa | | $K_{IC} = \alpha\,\sigma_y\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture criterion; α = geometry factor, $a$ = flaw size | MPa·√m | | $\tau_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{\gamma_{S}}{b\,r_{cr}}$ | Critical shear stress for precipitate shearing | MPa | | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A\,e^{-Q/RT}$ | Creep rate at aging temperature (used for over-aging analysis) | s⁻¹ | > ⚠️ **Markdown safety note:** In the formulas above, $\vert$ for absolute value or modulus is rendered using standard LaTeX, so the markdown table is not broken. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Die-casting die inserts (Al, Zn alloys):** Maraging 250 resists heat-checking better than H13 due to lower Coffin–Manson fatigue exponent. - **Injection moulds for optical lenses:** Polishability to < 5 nm Ra because no hard primary carbides. - **Aerospace die forging:** Weight-critical components use 18Ni(300) because of strength-to-mass ratio. - **Missile motor cases & rocket motor liners:** 18Ni(400) cast variant used by aerospace agencies for its exceptional specific strength. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion of Grade Number to Yield Strength (MPa) The grade designation "18 Ni (300)" means **nominal yield strength = 300 ksi**. Since $1 \text{ ksi} = 6.89476 \text{ MPa}$, the conversion is: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_y \text{ (MPa)} &= \sigma_y \text{ (ksi)} \times 6.89476 \\ \sigma_y \text{ (MPa)} &= 300 \times 6.89476 \\ \sigma_y \text{ (MPa)} &= 2068.43 \\ \sigma_y \text{ (MPa)} &\approx \boxed{2070 \text{ MPa}} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic step-by-step:** 1. Read the grade number → it is **yield strength in ksi**, not UTS, not hardness. 2. Apply conversion factor $1 \text{ ksi} = 6.895 \text{ MPa}$. 3. Multiply; round to 3 significant figures → **2070 MPa**. > **Verification for 18 Ni (250):** $250 \times 6.895 = 1723.7 \approx 1724 \text{ MPa}$ ✓ (matches ASM data sheet for VascoMax 250). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ashby–Orowan Strengthening Contribution The yield strength of an aged maraging steel can be decomposed additively: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_y &= \sigma_0 + \sigma_{ss} + \sigma_{ppt} + \sigma_{disl} + \sigma_{gb} \end{aligned} $$ where: - $\sigma_0$ = lattice friction stress (≈ 50 MPa for Fe) - $\sigma_{ss}$ = solid-solution strengthening from Ni, Mo, Co (≈ 600 MPa) - $\sigma_{ppt}$ = precipitation strengthening (dominant term) - $\sigma_{disl}$ = dislocation strengthening from lath martensite -
