$\sigma_{gb}$ = grain-boundary (Hall–Petch) strengthening For a precipitate of volume fraction $f$ and mean radius $r$ bypassed by **Orowan looping**, the strengthening contribution is: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{ppt} &= \frac{0.84\, M\, G\, b}{2\pi\,(1-\nu)^{1/2}\,\lambda} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{b}\right) \end{aligned} $$ with $$ \lambda = r \left( \frac{2\pi}{3f} \right)^{1/2} - 2r $$ **Worked Example (KTU-style numerical):** Take 18 Ni (300) aged to peak hardness at $480^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 4 hours: - $M = 3$ (Taylor factor for BCC) - $G = 75 \text{ GPa}$ (shear modulus of Fe-Ni martensite) - $b = 0.248 \text{ nm}$ (Burgers vector) - $\nu = 0.33$ - $r = 5 \text{ nm}$ (typical Ni₃Ti radius) - $f = 0.025$ (2.5 % volume fraction of precipitates) **Step 1 — compute inter-particle spacing $\lambda$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda &= 5\,\text{nm} \times \left(\frac{2\pi}{3 \times 0.025}\right)^{1/2} - 2 \times 5\,\text{nm} \\ \lambda &= 5 \times \left(\frac{2\pi}{0.075}\right)^{1/2} - 10 \\ \lambda &= 5 \times (83.78)^{1/2} - 10 \\ \lambda &= 5 \times 9.15 - 10 \\ \lambda &= 45.76 - 10 \\ \lambda &= 35.76 \text{ nm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — substitute into Orowan equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{ppt} &= \frac{0.84 \times 3 \times 75{,}000 \times 0.248 \times 10^{-9}}{2\pi \times (1-0.33)^{1/2} \times 35.76 \times 10^{-9}} \times \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.248 \times 10^{-9}}\right) \\ \sigma_{ppt} &= \frac{46{,}872}{2\pi \times 0.8185 \times 35.76 \times 10^{-9}} \times \ln(20.16) \\ \sigma_{ppt} &= \frac{46{,}872}{1.838 \times 10^{-7}} \times 3.004 \\ \sigma_{ppt} &= 2.55 \times 10^{11} \times 3.004 \\ \sigma_{ppt} &\approx 7.65 \times 10^{11} \text{ (in Pa)} \\ \sigma_{ppt} &\approx \boxed{765 \text{ MPa}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic comment:** This single mechanism contributes ~ **765 MPa** to the total yield strength. Combined with solid-solution (≈ 600 MPa), dislocation (≈ 400 MPa), and grain-boundary (≈ 250 MPa) contributions, it matches the experimentally observed **2070 MPa** for 18Ni(300). This explains why **fine, coherent Ni₃Ti precipitates are the heart of maraging metallurgy**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aging Time–Temperature Trade-off (Iso-Strength Curve) A common KTU-style question: *"How long must 18Ni(300) be aged at 460 °C to obtain the same hardness as 4 hours at 480 °C?"* The aging kinetics approximate an **Arrhenius-type relation**: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{t_1}{t_2} &= \exp\!\left[\frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right] \end{aligned} $$ For Ni₃Ti precipitation, $Q \approx 220 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$. **Plug in:** $T_1 = 480 + 273 = 753 \text{ K}$, $t_1 = 4 \text{ h}$, $T_2 = 460 + 273 = 733 \text{ K}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{t_2}{4} &= \exp\!\left[\frac{220{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{753} - \frac{1}{733}\right)\right] \\ \frac{t_2}{4} &= \exp\!\left[26{,}462 \times (-3.62 \times 10^{-5})\right] \\ \frac{t_2}{4} &= \exp(-0.958) \\ \frac{t_2}{4} &= 0.384 \\ t_2 &= 4 \times 3.69 \\ t_2 &\approx \boxed{14.8 \text{ hours}} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Reducing aging temperature by only $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ **quadruples the required aging time**. This is why die-makers operate maraging heat treatment in **tightly controlled furnaces**, and why time–temperature charts are mandatory shop-floor documents. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Maraging Grade for a Die | Criterion / Requirement | 18 Ni (200) | 18 Ni (250) | 18 Ni (300) | 18 Ni (350) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Machining cost (lower = easier) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | | Wear resistance | Low | Moderate | High | Very high | | Toughness | Excellent | Very good | Good | Moderate | | Cost per kg | Lowest | Moderate | High | Very high | | Typical die size | Small inserts | Medium moulds | Large moulds, forging | Aerospace only | | Recommended for | Prototype tooling | Rubber/plastic moulds | Injection moulds | Critical forging dies | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics --- ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Maraging Heat Treatment The following Mermaid block renders a **fully labelled process flow** for maraging steel heat treatment, mapping every input, transformation, and intermediate property. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Stock: Annealed Maraging Steel Bar / Block
Hardness 28 to 32 HRC] --> A1[Rough Machining
CNC Milling and Turning] A1 --> A2[Solution Annealing
820 to 900 deg C
1 hour per 25 mm section] A2 --> A3[Air Cool to Room Temperature
Gamma to Alpha Prime Lath Martensite] A3 --> A4[Finish Machining
Grinding and EDM if needed] A4 --> A5{Q1: Dimensional
Check Passed?} A5 -- No --> A6[Re-machine and Stress Relieve] A6 --> A4 A5 -- Yes --> A7[Aging Treatment
480 deg C for 3 to 6 hours] A7 --> A8[Air Cool to Room Temperature] A8 --> A9[Final Inspection
Hardness 50 to 60 HRC] A9 --> A10{Q2: Hardness and
Dimensions OK?} A10 -- Yes --> A11[Finished Die / Mould
Ready for Service] A10 -- No --> A12[Re-Aging or Scrap Decision] A12 --> A7 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** > - `A0 → A1 → A2 → A3 → A4` represents the *soft-state* processing path. > - `A5` is the **dimensional gate** — this is where distortion-free machining is verified *before* the irreversible aging step. > - `A7 → A8 → A9` is the **age-hardening block** where Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, Fe₂Mo precipitates nucleate. > - `A11` is the **deliverable**: a fully hardened, dimensionally accurate die. --- ## 4.2 Block Architecture — Strengthening Mechanisms Acting on a Maraging Steel ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Yield Strength of Aged Maraging Steel
Sigma y approximately 2070 MPa] --> M1[Base Lattice Strength
Sigma 0 approx 50 MPa] M --> M2[Solid Solution Strengthening
Ni Mo Co atoms in Fe matrix
approx 600 MPa] M --> M3[Precipitation Strengthening
Ni3Ti Ni3Mo Fe2Mo precipitates
approx 765 MPa] M --> M4[Dislocation Strengthening
Dense dislocations in lath martensite
approx 400 MPa] M --> M5[Grain Boundary Strengthening
Fine prior austenite grains
approx 250 MPa] M3 --> M3a[Coherent Particles
Radius less than 5 nm] M3 --> M3b[Semi Coherent Particles
Radius 5 to 15 nm] M3 --> M3c[Incoherent Particles
Radius greater than 15 nm
OVER AGED] M3c -.->|causes| OW[Strength Drop
KTU Exam Question] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** > - `M` is the *yield-strength budget* — each block contributes additively. > - `M3` is the **ageing-controlled** contribution, which is *tunable* by the heat-treatment technician. > - `M3c` is the **over-aged** regime — the single most-tested failure mode in KTU questions on maraging. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Application Map for Maraging Steels in Tool & Die Industry ```mermaid flowchart TD APP[Maraging Steel Applications] --> APP1[Aerospace Tooling] APP --> APP2[Plastic Injection Moulds] APP --> APP3[Die Casting Dies] APP --> APP4[Forging Die Inserts] APP --> APP5[Extrusion Tooling] APP1 --> APP1a[Aircraft structural dies] APP1 --> APP1b[Rocket motor casings] APP1 --> APP1c[Lightweight forging dies] APP2 --> APP2a[Optical lens moulds] APP2 --> APP2b[Large automotive panels] APP2 --> APP2c[Mirror polish surfaces] APP3 --> APP3a[Aluminium alloy dies] APP3 --> APP3b[Zinc alloy dies] APP3 --> APP3c[Hot chamber die inserts] APP4 --> APP4a[Titanium forging] APP4 --> APP4b[Steel billet forging] APP4 --> APP4c[Aerospace turbine blades] APP5 --> APP5a[Aluminium extrusion] APP5 --> APP5b[Cold heading dies] APP5 --> APP5c[Tube drawing tools] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** `APP` is the root application node. Three levels of drill-down show how a *single* maraging grade family serves **diverse** mould/die applications — making it a *versatile* answer in KTU comparative-tooling questions. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define maraging steel. What is the role of nickel in it?** **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** > **Maraging steel** is an ultra-high-strength low-carbon steel containing ~ 18 % Ni, hardened by **precipitation of intermetallic compounds** such as $\text{Ni}_3\text{Ti}$ and $\text{Ni}_3\text{Mo}$ during aging at ~ $480^{\circ}\text{C}$, rather than by carbon-induced martensitic hardening. > > **Role of Nickel (Ni) — three critical functions:** > 1. **Forms lath martensite** on cooling even with < 0.03 % C, giving a tough, ductile matrix. > 2. **Provides solute atoms** for solid-solution strengthening. > 3. **Combines with Ti, Mo, Al** to form fine intermetallic precipitates that give the final age-hardening. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Why are maraging steels preferred for large plastic moulds and die-casting dies?** **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** > 1. **Soft in annealed condition (~ 30 HRC):** Easy to machine complex cavity geometries before hardening. > 2. **Age hardening at low temperature (480 °C):** Negligible distortion or size change — critical for large moulds. > 3. **Excellent weldability** (no carbon) → easy in-service repair. > 4. **High fracture toughness** → resists cracking under cyclic thermal/mechanical loading of die casting. > 5. **Mirror polishability** due to absence of primary carbides — ideal for optical-grade plastic parts. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, every Part-B question from Module 3 carries an internal choice. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.** --- ### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Heat Treatment Focus **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** **Q.A (a)** Explain in detail the **heat treatment cycle** of 18 Ni (300) maraging steel, with a neat labelled temperature–time diagram. Mention the metallurgical changes at each stage. **(7 marks)** **Q.A (b)** A maraging steel component is solution annealed at $820^{\circ}\text{C}$ and aged at $460^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 4 hours. What should be the aging time at $500^{\circ}\text{C}$ to obtain equivalent hardness? Given activation energy $Q = 220 \text{ kJ/mol}$ and gas constant $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (a) **Stage 1 — Solution Annealing (Homogenization):** The steel is heated to **$820^{\circ}\text{C} - 900^{\circ}\text{C}$** and held for ~ **1 hour per 25 mm** of section thickness. At this temperature: - The microstructure becomes single-phase **austenite (γ, FCC)**. - All alloying elements (Ni, Co, Mo, Ti, Al) dissolve into solid solution. - The grain size is refined (important for final toughness). **Stage 2 — Air Cooling (Martensite Formation):** Cooling in air to room temperature causes **γ → α′ (lath martensite, BCT)** transformation. Because carbon is essentially absent, the martensite is **ductile and tough**, with high dislocation density. Hardness ≈ **30 HRC**. **Stage 3 — Aging (Precipitation Hardening):** The component is reheated to **$480^{\circ}\text{C} \pm 10^{\circ}\text{C}$** and held for **3 – 6 hours**. During this isothermal hold: - **$\text{Ni}_3\text{Ti}$** precipitates nucleate first (coherent, ~ 5 nm radius). - **$\text{Ni}_3\text{Mo}$** and **$\text{Fe}_2\text{Mo}$** co-precipitate. - Dislocations are pinned (Orowan mechanism); strength rises sharply. - Hardness reaches **53 – 58 HRC**; yield strength ≈ **2070 MPa**. **Schematic temperature–time diagram (textual representation):** ``` T (°C) | 900 |- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - | _____ 820 |- - - -/ \________________ | / \ 480 |- - -/- - - - - - - - - - - -\- - \- - - - - - | | | | |____|__________________________|___|________ t (h) 1h/25mm Aging 3-6h Anneal Quench Aging ``` **Metallurgical summary table:** | Stage | Temperature | Microstructure | Hardness (HRC) | |---|---|---|---| | Solution anneal | 820 – 900 °C | γ-austenite (FCC) | – | | After air cool | RT | α′ lath martensite (BCT) | 28 – 32 | | Aged | 480 °C / 3 – 6 h | α′ + Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, Fe₂Mo precipitates | 53 – 58 | #### Model Solution — Q.A (b) **Given:** - $T_1 = 460 + 273 = 733 \text{ K}$, $t_1 = 4 \text{ h}$ - $T_2 = 500 + 273 = 773 \text{ K}$, $t_2 = ?$ - $Q = 220{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ **Use Arrhenius iso-strength relation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{t_1}{t_2} &= \exp\!\left[\frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Substituting:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{4}{t_2} &= \exp\!\left[\frac{220{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{733} - \frac{1}{773}\right)\right] \\ \frac{4}{t_2} &= \exp\!\left[26{,}462 \times (0.001364 - 0.001294)\right] \\ \frac{4}{t_2} &= \exp\!\left[26{,}462 \times 7.07 \times 10^{-5}\right] \\ \frac{4}{t_2} &= \exp(1.871) \\ \frac{4}{t_2} &= 6.49 \\ t_2 &= \frac{4}{6.49} \\ t_2 &\approx \boxed{0.62 \text{ hours} \approx 37 \text{ minutes}} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering interpretation:** Raising aging temperature by $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ reduces aging time by ~ **85 %**. This shows why production heat-treatment shops prefer **higher aging temperatures within the 480 – 510 °C band** to maximise furnace throughput. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall Callout):** > - Forgetting to convert °C → K. The 273 offset cancels in some problems but **not in exponentials** — you will lose 3 marks here. > - Inverting $t_1/t_2$ (writing the equation upside-down). Memorise: **higher temperature → shorter time**. > - Skipping the engineering interpretation — examiners allocate **1 mark** for "real-world relevance" comments. --- ### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Metallurgy & Applications Focus **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]** **Q.B (a)** Compare maraging steels with conventional tool steels (H13, D2, P20) with respect to **composition, hardenability, achievable hardness, toughness, dimensional change during hardening, and weldability**. **(7 marks)** **Q.B (b)** Discuss the **specific applications of maraging steels in mould and die manufacture**, citing at least four real-world case studies. Justify the choice of maraging grade in each case. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (a) | Property | Maraging Steel (18 Ni 300) | H13 (Hot-work tool steel) | D2 (Cold-work tool steel) | P20 (Plastic mould steel) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **C content** | ≤ 0.03 % | 0.40 % | 1.55 % | 0.35 % | | **Key alloying** | 18 Ni, 9 Co, 5 Mo, 1 Ti | 5 Cr, 1.5 Mo, 1 V | 12 Cr, 1 V, 1 Mo | 1.7 Cr, 0.4 Mn | | **Hardening mechanism** | Precipitation of Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo | Quench + temper (carbides) | Quench + temper (M₇C₃ carbides) | Quench + temper | | **Achievable hardness** | 53 – 58 HRC | 48 – 54 HRC | 58 – 62 HRC | 30 – 36 HRC (pre-hardened) | | **Hardening temperature** | 480 °C (aging) | 1020 °C (austenitize) | 1050 °C (austenitize) | 850 °C | | **Dimensional change on hardening** | **Negligible (< 0.05 %)** | ~ 0.2 % | ~ 0.3 % | ~ 0.1 % | | **Fracture toughness (K_IC)** | ~ 80 MPa·√m | ~ 25 MPa·√m | ~ 20 MPa·√m | ~ 50 MPa·√m | | **Weldability** | **Excellent** (no pre-heat) | Poor (cracking risk) | Very poor | Moderate | | **Machinability in soft state** | Excellent (30 HRC) | Moderate (annealed ~ 200 HB) | Poor | Good (pre-hardened 30 HRC) | | **Cost** | Very high | High | High | Low | **Examiner's synthesis line:** > *"Maraging steels combine the **dimensional stability** of P20, the **toughness** approaching wrought nickel alloys, and **strength comparable to D2**, with the additional advantage of **weldability** — but at 3 to 5 × the raw-material cost. They are chosen when **die performance, not die cost**, is the priority."* #### Model Solution — Q.B (b) — Four Real-World Case Studies **Case 1 — Optical Lens Plastic Injection Mould (18 Ni 300):** - *Application:* Moulds for polycarbonate / PMMA lenses in cameras and projectors. - *Why maraging:* Mirror polish to < 5 nm Ra; absence of carbide stringers prevents orange-peel polishing defects. - *Grade rationale:* 300 ksi grade — high strength to resist injection pressure (~ 100 MPa) without cavity deflection. **Case 2 — Aluminium Die-Casting Insert (18 Ni 250):** - *Application:* Cores and cavities for high-pressure die casting of aluminium alloy components. - *Why maraging:* Excellent resistance to heat-checking (thermal fatigue) due to high toughness; can be welded to repair heat-check cracks without preheating. - *Grade rationale:* 250 grade balances strength and toughness; 300 grade is over-spec for Al alloys (solubility limit of Fe in molten Al limits the need for > 55 HRC). **Case 3 — Large Automotive Dashboard Mould (18 Ni 300 cast variant C-300):** - *Application:* Single large mould base (≈ 2 m × 1.5 m) for injection moulded dashboards. - *Why maraging:* Wrought maraging of this size is uneconomical — the **cast C-300** variant gives similar properties at lower cost. - *Grade rationale:* Cast variant chosen because the mould cavity is machined post-casting, and the section size requires uniform through-thickness hardness. **Case 4 — Aerospace Turbine Blade Forging Die Insert (18 Ni 350):** - *Application:* Die inserts used in isothermal forging of Ti-6Al-4V turbine blades at $950^{\circ}\text{C}$ workpiece temperature. - *Why maraging:* At $480^{\circ}\text{C}$ aging, the die operates below the over-aging temperature — it stays hard even at elevated die temperatures (~ $600^{\circ}\text{C}$). - *Grade rationale:* 350 grade — highest commercially available wrought grade — required because forging pressures for titanium exceed **1200 MPa** and softer dies would creep. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes on Q.B (a):** > - Comparing *only hardness* — examiners allocate marks for **all seven** properties listed in the question. > - Writing "maraging is better than H13" without **numerical values** — always quote specific numbers (HRC, MPa, %). > - On Q.B (b), students often give generic applications without **justifying grade selection**. Linking grade → property → application is the KTU marking key. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print and paste this section on your study wall.** - ✅ **Definition:** Maraging = **MAR**tensite + **AGING**; ultra-high-strength steel with **C < 0.03 %**. - ✅ **Composition window:** 18 Ni, 9 Co, 5 Mo, 1 Ti, 0.1 Al, balance Fe. - ✅ **Grade number = yield strength in ksi:** 18 Ni (300) → **2070 MPa**, hardness ~ **55 HRC**. - ✅ **Heat treatment sequence:** Solution anneal (**820–900 °C**) → air cool (martensite) → age (**480 °C / 3–6 h**). - ✅ **Strengthening precipitates:** **Ni₃Ti** (principal), **Ni₃Mo**, **Fe₂Mo**, **Ni₃Al** — all intermetallic, no carbides. - ✅ **Why low carbon?** Eliminates brittle cementite → preserves toughness. - ✅ **Over-aging > 540 °C** → precipitate coarsening → strength drop. - ✅ **Avoid temper martensite embrittlement (300–400 °C)** — no carbon to form cementite. - ✅ **Dimensional change on hardening: < 0.05 %** — the lowest of all tool steels. - ✅ **Weldability: excellent** — repair welding possible without preheating. - ✅ **Polishability: best in class** — no primary carbides, mirror finish achievable. - ✅ **Conversion factor:** $1 \text{ ksi} = 6.895 \text{ MPa}$. - ✅ **Arrhenius relation for aging:** $\dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{Q}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$. - ✅ **Compare & contrast:** Maraging vs H13 / D2 / P20 — must mention **all seven** properties in exams. - ✅ **Industrial applications:** Optical moulds, die-casting inserts, large automotive moulds (cast C-300), aerospace forging dies. - ✅ **Cast variants (C-250, C-300):** Used for **large moulds** where forging is impractical. > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** A 14-mark answer should occupy **3–4 handwritten pages**. Allocate ~ **2 pages** for Q.A (a) / Q.B (a) and ~ **1.5 pages** for Q.A (b) / Q.B (b). Always include the labelled diagram — even a hand-drawn sketch scores 2 extra marks.

topics.title: Hard Material Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hard Material Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Material Alloys** are a specialized class of metallurgical alloys engineered to withstand the extreme mechanical, thermal, and tribological loads encountered in **mould and die manufacturing operations**. They are characterized by **high hardness (typically ≥ 60 HRC)**, **superior wear resistance**, **adequate toughness**, **dimensional stability at elevated temperatures**, and **resistance to plastic deformation** during stamping, forging, casting, or plastic injection processes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEPOT756 – Mould and Die Manufacture**, the term *Hard Material Alloys* encompasses four engineering families:

1. **Die / Tool Steels** (cold work, hot work, high-speed, plastic mould steels)
2. **Cemented Carbides** (WC-Co, WC-TiC-Co systems)
3. **Cast Hard Alloys** (Stellites, Ni-Hard, high-chromium cast irons)
4. **Specialty Powders & Cermets** for surface engineering and thermal spray coatings

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEPOT756 Module 3):** Students are expected to classify these alloys, recall their **AISI/SAE / DIN / JIS grade designations**, justify their selection for specific die operations, and outline the heat-treatment cycle that activates their hardening mechanism.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spartan Shield" of Manufacturing

Imagine you are forging a sword. The blade must be:
- **Hard** → to keep its sharp edge when striking metal (resistance to abrasive wear)
- **Tough** → so it does not shatter on impact (resistance to brittle fracture)
- **Heat-Resistant** → because repeated hammering makes it red-hot (thermal fatigue resistance)
- **Dimensionally Stable** → so the blade does not warp between strikes

A **die in a press shop is exactly this Spartan shield**. Every time the press closes, the die experiences:
- A sudden **impact load** (10³ – 10⁴ MPa contact stress)
- **Frictional sliding** of hot/cold work-piece material
- **Thermal cycling** (room temperature → 600 °C → room temperature)

Hard material alloys are the **metallurgical Spartans** chosen to survive these punishing conditions. Each alloy family represents a different tactical specialization — carbides are the **phalanx wall** (max hardness, low toughness), hot-work steels are the **hoplite** (balance of heat and shock resistance), and cold-work steels are the **legionnaire** (shear hardness at room temperature).

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Engineering Benchmarks)

> [!NOTE]
> The standard KTU 2024 metrics used to benchmark hard material alloys are:
> - **Hardness:** expressed in **HRC (Rockwell C)**, **HV (Vickers)**, or **HB (Brinell)**
> - **Toughness:** measured by **Charpy V-notch impact energy (J)** or **K_IC fracture toughness (MPa·m^½)**
> - **Wear Resistance:** quantified by the **Archard wear coefficient (K)**
> - **Hot Hardness:** retention of hardness at elevated temperatures (e.g., 600 °C)
> - **Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K)** and **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (×10⁻⁶ /°C)**

These metrics are **bolded** in board examiner's mark-allocation sheets because they form the rubric of any numerical or comparison question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic — The Four-Tier Pyramid

Hard material alloys form a hierarchy based on **operating temperature** and **hardness-toughness trade-off**.

| Family | Typical Operating Temp | Hardness Range (HRC) | Toughness | Primary Die Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cold Work Die Steels | ≤ 200 °C | 58 – 64 | Medium | Blanking, drawing, piercing |
| Hot Work Die Steels | 200 – 700 °C | 45 – 55 | High | Forging, extrusion, die casting |
| High Speed Steels (HSS) | ≤ 600 °C (red-hard) | 62 – 68 | Medium | Cutting tools, fine blanking |
| Cemented Carbides | ≤ 900 °C | 70 – 92 (HRA) | Low | Wear parts, drawing dies |
| Cast Hard Alloys (Stellite) | ≤ 800 °C | 50 – 60 HRC | Medium-High | Valve seats, hot trimming |

### 2.2 Cold Work Die Steels — The Cutting Edge of Forming

#### 2.2.1 Water-Hardening (W-Series) — e.g., **W1, W2**
- High carbon (0.6 – 1.4 % C), no significant alloying additions.
- Quenched in **brine/water** to achieve martensite.
- **Drawback:** High distortion, low hardenability, prone to quench cracking.
- **Use:** Short-run blanking dies, simple punches.

#### 2.2.2 Oil-Hardening (O-Series) — e.g., **O1, O2**
- Contains **Mn (1.0 %)**, **Cr (0.5 %)** for grain refinement.
- Oil quench → **lower distortion** than W-series.
- **Use:** general-purpose dies, gauges, medium-run blanking.

#### 2.2.3 Air-Hardening (A-Series) — e.g., **A2, A6**
- High **Cr (5 %)** → excellent dimensional stability.
- Harden by **air cooling** (very low distortion).
- **Use:** precision dies, thread-rolling, intricate forming tools.

#### 2.2.4 High-Carbon High-Chromium (D-Series) — e.g., **D2, D3, D6**
- Composition: **1.5 % C, 12 % Cr, 1 % Mo, 1 % V**.
- Massive **Cr-rich carbides (M₂₃C₆, M₇C₃)** distributed in martensite.
- **Wear Resistance:** exceptionally high (the "abrasion king").
- **Drawback:** lower impact toughness → brittle.
- **Use:** long-run blanking, deep drawing, wire-drawing dies.

### 2.3 Hot Work Die Steels — Surviving the Thermal Onslaught

These must withstand **thermal cycling, thermal fatigue, and softening at elevated temperatures**.

#### 2.3.1 Chromium-Based Hot Work Steels — **H11, H12, H13**
- Composition: **0.35 % C, 5 % Cr, 1.5 % Mo, 1 % V, 1 % Si**.
- **H13** is the **industry benchmark** for **die-casting dies, forging dies, and extrusion tooling**.
- Secondary hardening from **Mo/V carbide precipitation** during tempering at **550 – 600 °C**.
- Excellent **toughness** (Charpy ~ 20 – 40 J) and **thermal fatigue resistance**.

#### 2.3.2 Tungsten-Based Hot Work Steels — **H21, H24, H26**
- Composition: **0.3 % C, 9 – 18 % W, 3 – 4 % Cr**.
- Higher **hot hardness** but lower thermal conductivity → prone to heat-checking.
- **Use:** hot extrusion of brass/steel.

#### 2.3.3 Molybdenum-Based — **H42**
- Substitute for tungsten (cost reduction) — **6 % W + 5 % Mo**.

### 2.4 High Speed Steels (HSS) — The Red-Hard Champions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Red hardness** = the ability to retain hardness ≥ 60 HRC at temperatures approaching 600 °C (the dull-red glow temperature). This is achieved by **secondary hardening** from **Mo₂C, W₂C, VC, and Fe₄W₂C** carbides.

#### 2.4.1 Tungsten-Type — **T1 (18-4-1)**
- Composition: **0.75 % C, 18 % W, 4 % Cr, 1 % V**.
- Reference grade; superseded by Mo-types due to cost.

#### 2.4.2 Molybdenum-Type — **M2 (6-5-4-2)**
- Composition: **0.85 % C, 6 % W, 5 % Mo, 4 % Cr, 2 % V**.
- **World's most-used HSS** for taps, drills, broaches, and fine-blanking punches.

#### 2.4.3 Cobalt-Modified — **M35, M42**
- Addition of **5 – 10 % Co** boosts hot hardness and red-hardness.
- M42 → **68 – 70 HRC**, used for **high-performance form tools and gear hobs**.

### 2.5 Cemented Carbides — The Composite Armory

> [!NOTE]
> Cemented carbides are **powder-metallurgy composites**, NOT cast alloys. They consist of **hard refractory carbide particles** (WC, TiC, TaC) bonded by a **ductile metallic binder** (typically Co, sometimes Ni).

#### 2.5.1 WC-Co System (Straight Tungsten Carbide)
- Hardness ↑ and Toughness ↓ as **Co content decreases** and **WC grain size decreases**.
- ISO classification: **K01 → K50** (Co binder decreases, hardness increases).
- **Use:** metal-cutting inserts (machining cast iron), wire-drawing dies.

#### 2.5.2 WC-TiC-Co System (Steel-Grade Carbide)
- TiC addition **suppresses crater wear** when machining steel (chemical affinity barrier).
- ISO classification: **P01 → P50**.
- **Use:** turning, milling of steels.

#### 2.5.3 WC-TaC(NbC)-Co System — Universal Grade
- For tough interrupted cuts on steel and stainless (ISO M10 – M40).

#### 2.5.4 Cermets — TiC-Ni / TiCN-Mo₂C-Ni
- Ceramic-metal hybrid; superior **creep resistance** at high cutting speeds.

### 2.6 Cast Hard Alloys — The Workhorse Overlays

#### 2.6.1 Stellite (Co-Cr-W-C System)
- Composition: **Co base, 25 – 35 % Cr, 4 – 15 % W, 0.5 – 3 % C**.
- Forms **hard M₇C₃ / M₂₃C₆ eutectic carbides** in a Co-rich matrix.
- Retains hardness up to **800 °C**, corrosion-resistant, **non-magnetic**.
- **Use:** valve seats, hot-trimming dies, weld overlays on forging dies (Stellite 6, Stellite 12).

#### 2.6.2 Ni-Hard (High-Chromium White Cast Iron)
- Composition: **3 – 4 % C, 2 – 4 % Cr, 4 – 6 % Ni** (sometimes 10 – 25 % Cr).
- Carbides: **M₇C₃, M₃C** in an austenitic/martensitic matrix.
- **Hardness: 550 – 700 HB**, used in **shot-blast nozzles, slurry pumps, crusher liners**.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **board examiner's "high-yield" reference** — every equation is examinable. Note that the **vertical bar symbol** uses `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{HRC} \approx 0.1 \cdot \text{HV} - 9$ | Approx. conversion (valid HV 400 – 900). Used in tool-data sheets. |
| 2 | $V \approx 0.1891 \cdot \text{HB}$ | Brinell-to-Vickers approximation (metallic alloys). |
| 3 | $V_{\text{wear}} \;=\; \dfrac{K \cdot F \cdot s}{H}$ | **Archard equation** — wear volume $V_{\text{wear}}$ (mm³), $K$ = wear coeff., $F$ = normal load (N), $s$ = sliding distance (mm), $H$ = hardness (MPa). Used to predict die life. |
| 4 | $K_{IC} \;=\; \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \cdot Y$ | Linear-elastic fracture toughness; $Y$ = geometry factor. Predicts brittle failure of carbide dies. |
| 5 | $\sigma_{f} \;=\; \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Flexural (bending) stress in a cantilever punch. |
| 6 | $T \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} \;=\; \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\Delta x}$ | Heat conduction rate through die body. Critical for hot-work die cooling design. |
| 7 | $\Delta L \;=\; \alpha \cdot L_{0} \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion of die insert. Helps predict **heat-checking**. |
| 8 | $\text{Martensite fraction} \;=\; 1 - \exp(-k \cdot \Delta T)$ | Koistinen-Marburger relationship — amount of retained austenite. |
| 9 | $\text{Tempering hardness} \; \propto \; \dfrac{1}{1 + (T \cdot t)^{n}}$ | Hollomon-Jaffe tempering parameter ($T$ absolute, $t$ time). |
| 10 | $\text{Tool life} \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{V^{n}}$ | Taylor's tool-life equation ($V$ = cutting speed). |
| 11 | $\text{Hot hardness ratio} \;=\; \dfrac{H_{T}}{H_{25^{\circ}C}}$ | Measures red-hardness (e.g., H13 retains ~ 0.6× at 600 °C; M42 retains ~ 0.85×). |
| 12 | $\text{Carbide volume fraction} \; f_v \;=\; \dfrac{W_c / \rho_c}{W_c / \rho_c + W_b / \rho_b}$ | Determines effective composite hardness in WC-Co. |
| 13 | $R_a \;=\; \dfrac{K}{H^{m}}$ | Surface roughness vs. hardness under sliding. |
| 14 | $\text{Rake angle force} \; F \;=\; \tau_s \cdot A \cdot \csc(\phi - \gamma)$ | Shear-plane model for blanking force. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility Summary:**
> - **Equations 3, 4, 11** are the **tribology/fracture triad** — examiners love to combine them in design problems.
> - **Equations 8, 9** are the **metallurgical core** of any heat-treatment numerical.
> - **Equation 14** is the **sheet-metal forming flagship** — appears in nearly every die-design problem.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Field Applications

| Industry Sector | Hard Alloy Used | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive Stamping | D2, DC53 | Long-run blanking of high-strength-steel body panels |
| Aluminum Die-Casting | H13 | Cores, cavities, ejector pins |
| Plastic Injection Moulding | P20, H13, S7 | Cavities (with nitrided/coated surface) |
| Hot Forging | H11, H13, H21 | Hammer/impression dies |
| Wire Drawing | WC-Co (K20) | Drawing dies for steel wire, EDM wire guide |
| Hot Trimming | Stellite 6 | Welded overlay on trimming blades |
| Mining / Slurry | Ni-Hard IV | Slurry pump casings, crusher jaws |
| Cutting Tools | M2, M42, cemented carbide P30 | Drills, taps, end-mills, inserts |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Archard's Wear Equation (Foundational to Die-Life Prediction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Archard equation** is the standard analytical tool used by KTU examiners to test whether students understand the **inverse proportionality between wear volume and surface hardness**. We will derive it from first principles.

#### Step 1: Define a single asperity contact event

Consider a single hard asperity (protrusion on the harder surface) sliding against a softer counter-surface. Let:
- $r$ = tip radius of the asperity
- $F_n$ = normal load supported by this asperity
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength of the softer material (≈ $H$ / 3 for fully plastic contact)

The asperity penetrates the softer surface to a depth $h$. From Hertzian-like contact mechanics for fully plastic indentation:

$$
A \;=\; \pi r h
$$

#### Step 2: Compute the wear particle volume

When the asperity slides a small distance $\Delta s$, it scrapes out a wear particle of volume $\Delta V_{asp}$. Assuming hemispherical debris:

$$
\Delta V_{asp} \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \pi r h^{2}
$$

#### Step 3: Relate penetration depth $h$ to normal load

From the plastic indentation relation $F_n = H \cdot A = H \cdot \pi r h$:

$$
h \;=\; \frac{F_n}{H \pi r}
$$

#### Step 4: Substitute $h$ into the wear-particle expression

$$
\Delta V_{asp} \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \pi r \left( \frac{F_n}{H \pi r} \right)^{2}
$$

Expanding the squared term:

$$
\Delta V_{asp} \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \pi r \cdot \frac{F_{n}^{2}}{H^{2} \pi^{2} r^{2}}
$$

Simplify by canceling one factor of $\pi r$:

$$
\Delta V_{asp} \;=\; \frac{2 F_{n}^{2}}{3 \pi r H^{2}}
$$

#### Step 5: Sum over all asperities

Let $N$ asperities participate per unit sliding distance. The total wear volume per unit sliding distance is:

$$
\frac{dV}{ds} \;=\; N \cdot \frac{2 F_{n}^{2}}{3 \pi r H^{2}}
$$

#### Step 6: Introduce the dimensionless wear coefficient $K$

By definition, the wear coefficient $K$ bundles the geometric and statistical terms:

$$
K \;=\; \frac{2 N F_{n}}{3 \pi r H}
$$

#### Step 7: Assemble the final form

Multiplying by sliding distance $s$ and total normal load $F = N F_n$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{\text{wear}} \;=\; \frac{K \cdot F \cdot s}{H}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU exams:** Doubling hardness $H$ halves wear volume (all else equal). This is why D2 steel (62 HRC) outlasts O1 steel (60 HRC) by an order of magnitude in abrasive blanking.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Koistinen-Marburger Equation (Heat Treatment Numericals)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Predicts the **fraction of austenite that transforms to martensite** during quenching. Essential for designing **retained-austenite-controlled** die steels.

#### Step 1: State the empirical relation

$$
f_m \;=\; 1 - \exp\bigl[-k \cdot (M_s - T_q)\bigr]
$$

where:
- $f_m$ = volume fraction of martensite
- $M_s$ = martensite-start temperature (°C)
- $T_q$ = quench temperature (°C)
- $k$ = material constant (typically $k \approx 0.011$ for plain-carbon steels)

#### Step 2: Apply to a die-steel problem

Given **D2 steel** with $M_s = 220 °C$ and oil-quench to $T_q = 25 °C$:

$$
f_m \;=\; 1 - \exp\bigl[-0.011 \cdot (220 - 25)\bigr]
$$

$$
f_m \;=\; 1 - \exp\bigl[-0.011 \cdot 195\bigr]
$$

$$
f_m \;=\; 1 - \exp(-2.145)
$$

$$
f_m \;=\; 1 - 0.1172 \;=\; 0.8828
$$

#### Step 3: Interpretation

$$
f_{\gamma} \;=\; 1 - f_m \;=\; 0.1172 \;\approx\; 11.7\% \text{ retained austenite}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Retention of > 10 % austenite** in D2 is undesirable for **dimensional stability** of dies. Mitigation: **cryogenic treatment** (sub-zero cooling to $-196 °C$) to convert retained $\gamma$ → martensite.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Volume Fraction of Carbides in WC-Co Cemented Carbides

#### Step 1: Mass-to-volume conversion

For a cemented carbide with mass fractions $W_c$ (WC), $W_b$ (binder Co), densities $\rho_c$ and $\rho_b$:

$$
f_v \;=\; \frac{V_c}{V_c + V_b} \;=\; \frac{W_c / \rho_c}{W_c / \rho_c + W_b / \rho_b}
$$

#### Step 2: Sample calculation — K20 grade (94 % WC + 6 % Co by mass)

Take $\rho_{WC} = 15.6$ g/cm³ and $\rho_{Co} = 8.9$ g/cm³:

$$
V_{WC} \;=\; \frac{94}{15.6} \;=\; 6.026 \text{ cm}^{3}\!/\!100\text{g}
$$

$$
V_{Co} \;=\; \frac{6}{8.9} \;=\; 0.674 \text{ cm}^{3}\!/\!100\text{g}
$$

$$
f_v \;=\; \frac{6.026}{6.026 + 0.674} \;=\; \frac{6.026}{6.700} \;\approx\; 0.8994
$$

#### Step 3: Interpretation

$$
f_v \;\approx\; 90\% \text{ hard carbide phase, } 10\% \text{ Co binder}
$$

This high carbide fraction explains the **exceptional wear resistance** of K20 drawing dies.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree (for numerical & reasoning questions)

| Operation Type | Temperature | Abrasiveness | Recommended Alloy | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sheet-metal blanking, long run | ≤ 150 °C | High (HSLA) | **D2 / DC53** | Massive Cr-carbides resist abrasion |
| Hot forging of steel | 700 – 1100 °C | High | **H13** | Secondary hardening, thermal fatigue resistance |
| Aluminum die-casting | 350 – 700 °C | Medium | **H13 (nitrided)** | Surface nitrides → die soldering resistance |
| Plastic injection (filled polymer) | 150 – 280 °C | High (glass fiber) | **P20 + Beryllium-Cu core** | Toughness + fast heat removal |
| Wire drawing, steel | 200 – 400 °C | Very high | **WC-Co K20** | 90 % WC → extreme abrasion resistance |
| Fine blanking | ≤ 80 °C | Medium-High | **M2 / M42** | High hardness + red hardness for sustained pressure |
| Hot trimming | 600 – 900 °C | High | **Stellite 6 overlay on H13** | Weldable, hot-hard, oxidation-resistant |
| Slurry pump casing | 50 °C | Extreme | **Ni-Hard IV (high-Cr cast iron)** | Cheap, 600 HB, abrasion-resistant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Classification of Hard Material Alloys

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["HARD MATERIAL ALLOYS\nFOR DIE MANUFACTURE"]
    nodeB["Die / Tool Steels"]
    nodeC["Cemented Carbides\n(P/M Composites)"]
    nodeD["Cast Hard Alloys"]
    nodeE["Specialty / Cermets"]

    nodeF["Cold Work Steels\nW, O, A, D series"]
    nodeG["Hot Work Steels\nH11, H13, H21, H26"]
    nodeH["High Speed Steels\nM2, M42, T1"]
    nodeI["Plastic Mould Steels\nP20, P21, S7"]

    nodeJ["WC-Co\nK-grade"]
    nodeK["WC-TiC-Co\nP-grade"]
    nodeL["WC-TaC-Co\nM-grade"]
    nodeM["TiC-Ni Cermets"]

    nodeN["Stellites\nCo-Cr-W-C"]
    nodeO["Ni-Hard\nHigh-Cr Cast Iron"]
    nodeP["High-Cr White Iron"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE

    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeI

    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeC --> nodeM

    nodeD --> nodeN
    nodeD --> nodeO
    nodeD --> nodeP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each "leaf" node corresponds to a sub-family. The KTU examiner typically asks students to **trace a path** from the root (e.g., "blanking of stainless steel at room temperature" → nodeA → nodeB → nodeF → **D2 steel**).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Heat-Treatment Process Flow for H13 Die Steel

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["Annealed H13\n~ 200 HB"]
    stepB["Pre-heat 1\n650 C - 30 min"]
    stepC["Pre-heat 2\n850 C - 20 min"]
    stepD["Austenitize\n1020 C - 30 min"]
    stepE["Quench\nAir or Oil"]
    stepF["As-Quenched\n~ 62 HRC"]
    stepG["First Temper\n550 C - 2 h"]
    stepH["Second Temper\n550 C - 2 h"]
    stepI["Final Hardness\n48 - 52 HRC"]
    stepJ["Optional:\nNitriding\n520 C, 20 h"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double tempering** is mandatory for H13 to decompose **retained austenite** and stabilize dimensions. Single tempering leaves ~ 5 – 10 % soft retained $\gamma$, which transforms during service → **dimensional drift and heat-checking**.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Die Service Failure Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    inp["Applied Service Load\n+ Heat + Friction"]
    failA["Abrasive Wear"]
    failB["Adhesive Wear\n(Galling / Die Soldering)"]
    failC["Thermal Fatigue\n(Heat Checking)"]
    failD["Plastic Deformation"]
    failE["Brittle Fracture\n(Chipping / Cracking)"]
    out["End of Die Life"]

    solA["Solution:\nHigh Cr / V carbides\ne.g., D2, WC-Co"]
    solB["Solution:\nSurface coating\nTiN, CrN, DLC"]
    solC["Solution:\nLow-C H-series\nH13, H11 + nitriding"]
    solD["Solution:\nHigher hardness\n+ Mo/V carbides"]
    solE["Solution:\nBalanced toughness\nH13, S7, M2"]

    inp --> failA --> out
    inp --> failB --> out
    inp --> failC --> out
    inp --> failD --> out
    inp --> failE --> out

    failA --> solA
    failB --> solB
    failC --> solC
    failD --> solD
    failE --> solE
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram maps **failure mode → metallurgical countermeasure** — a frequently tested concept under KTU 2024's outcome-based assessment for CO3 (Material Selection).

---

### 4.4 Schematic — Microstructural Comparison Table

| Alloy | Primary Microstructure | Hard Phase | Matrix Phase | Carbide Fraction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| D2 (62 HRC) | Martensite + undissolved carbides | M₇C₃, M₂₃C₆ (Cr-rich) | Tempered martensite | ~ 15 – 20 % |
| H13 (50 HRC) | Tempered martensite | Mo/V secondary carbides | Tough tempered $\alpha$ | ~ 5 % |
| M2 (65 HRC) | Tempered martensite | M₆C, MC (W, V, Mo rich) | Martensite | ~ 25 % |
| WC-Co K20 (90 HRA) | WC grains in Co binder | WC (hexagonal) | Co (fcc) | ~ 90 vol % |
| Stellite 6 (40 HRC) | Co-rich dendrites | M₇C₃ / M₂₃C₆ carbides | Co solid solution | ~ 25 – 30 % |
| Ni-Hard (600 HB) | Austenite + eutectic carbides | M₇C₃ | Austenite / martensite | ~ 30 – 50 % |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 marking scheme:** Part A = 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks. Answers expected in **60 – 80 words**, with a **clear definition** and **one application example**.

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Define the term "red hardness" as applied to high-speed steels. Name two HSS grades and state why red hardness matters in die manufacture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Red hardness** is the ability of a tool/die material to **retain its hardness at elevated temperatures** (typically ≥ 600 °C), resisting softening when the cutting edge or die contact zone turns red-hot due to frictional heat. It is achieved by **secondary hardening** through precipitation of fine, stable alloy carbides (Mo₂C, W₂C, VC) during tempering at 500 – 600 °C.
>
> **Two HSS grades:** **M2** (6W–5Mo–4Cr–2V) and **M42** (M2 + 8 % Co).
>
> **Importance in die manufacture:** In high-speed blanking, fine-blanking, and gear hobbing, the die/workpiece interface briefly reaches 500 – 600 °C. Without red hardness, the cutting edge would soften, lose dimensional accuracy, and fail prematurely. M42, with its Co addition, retains ~ 85 % hardness at 600 °C, giving **3 – 5× longer tool life** in interrupted-cut operations.

**[Mark Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark two grades + 1 mark die-application justification]**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Distinguish between cold-work, hot-work, and plastic-mould die steels. Give one AISI grade example for each category.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Category | Operating Temperature | Required Property | Example Grade |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold-work die steel** | ≤ 200 °C | High surface hardness & wear resistance | **D2** (1.5 C – 12 Cr) |
| **Hot-work die steel** | 200 – 700 °C | Hot hardness + thermal fatigue resistance | **H13** (0.35 C – 5 Cr – 1.5 Mo – 1 V) |
| **Plastic-mould steel** | 150 – 280 °C | Toughness + polishability + corrosion resistance | **P20** (0.35 C – 1.7 Cr – 0.4 Mo) |

> **Cold-work steels** rely on **untempered martensite + Cr carbides** for abrasion resistance. **Hot-work steels** rely on **Mo/V secondary hardening** for red hardness. **Plastic-mould steels** emphasize **toughness and surface finish**, often supplied pre-hardened to 30 – 36 HRC, and may contain **Ni** (P21) for through-hardenability of large mould cavities.

**[Mark Allocation: 1 mark per row + key composition/example]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 marking scheme:** Part B = 1 question (with choice A or B) × 14 marks. Each choice has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A — Option A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **classification of cemented carbides** based on the **WC-Co, WC-TiC-Co, and WC-TaC-Co systems**. State the ISO application codes (P, M, K) and give **two industrial uses** for each.
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** A **WC-Co drawing die** for stainless-steel wire has 90 % WC and 10 % Co by mass. Given densities $\rho_{WC} = 15.6$ g/cm³ and $\rho_{Co} = 8.9$ g/cm³, compute the **volume fraction of binder phase**. If the die is used to draw 1 mm-diameter wire at a normal contact load of 500 N over a sliding distance of 50 km with a wear coefficient $K = 5 \times 10^{-5}$, calculate the **wear volume in mm³** (assume hardness $H = 1800$ HV ≈ 18000 MPa).

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

Cemented carbides are **powder-metallurgical composites** of refractory carbides (WC, TiC, TaC) bonded by a ductile metallic binder (Co, Ni). They are classified by composition into three principal systems:

**1. WC-Co System (Straight Tungsten Carbide)**
- Hardness: 1500 – 2200 HV
- Toughness: highest among carbides (Co binder absorbs crack energy)
- ISO code: **K-series (K01 – K50)** → for **machining cast iron, non-ferrous metals, and wear parts**.
- *Example grades:* **K10** (fine grain, high wear resistance) and **K20** (medium grain, general purpose).
- **Uses:** (i) Drawing dies for steel wire, (ii) Mining/rock-drill bits, (iii) Stamping dies for abrasive stock.

**2. WC-TiC-Co System (Steel-Grade Carbide)**
- TiC addition forms a **diffusion barrier** that suppresses **crater wear** during steel machining (because TiC has lower solubility in Fe).
- ISO code: **P-series (P01 – P50)** → for **machining steel and steel castings**.
- *Example grades:* **P10** (roughing), **P30** (general purpose).
- **Uses:** (i) Turning inserts for mild-steel shafts, (ii) Milling cutter inserts.

**3. WC-TaC(NbC)-Co System (Universal / Stainless Grade)**
- Addition of **TaC** improves **hot hardness, thermal-shock resistance, and crater-wear resistance**.
- ISO code: **M-series (M10 – M40)** → for **machining stainless steel, ductile cast iron, and difficult-to-cut alloys**.
- *Example grades:* **M20**, **M30**.
- **Uses:** (i) End-mills for stainless-steel aerospace parts, (ii) Drills for Inconel.

**4. TiC/TiCN-Ni Cermets**
- Alternative to WC-Co for **high-speed finishing** due to superior **creep resistance** and lower density.

```mermaid
graph LR
    sys1["WC-Co System\nISO K-series\nCast Iron"]
    sys2["WC-TiC-Co\nISO P-series\nSteel"]
    sys3["WC-TaC-Co\nISO M-series\nStainless"]
    sys4["TiC-Ni Cermets\nISO S-series\nSuperalloys"]

    sys1 --> matK["K-grade inserts\nand drawing dies"]
    sys2 --> matP["P-grade turning\nand milling inserts"]
    sys3 --> matM["M-grade drills\nand end-mills"]
    sys4 --> matS["S-grade high-speed\nfinishing inserts"]
```

**[Mark Allocation: 1 mark diagram + 3 marks systems + 2 marks ISO codes + 1 mark applications]**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute binder volume fraction:**

Using the mass-to-volume conversion:

$$
V_{WC} \;=\; \frac{W_{WC}}{\rho_{WC}} \;=\; \frac{90}{15.6} \;=\; 5.769 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{Co} \;=\; \frac{W_{Co}}{\rho_{Co}} \;=\; \frac{10}{8.9} \;=\; 1.124 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

$$
f_{v,\,Co} \;=\; \frac{V_{Co}}{V_{WC} + V_{Co}} \;=\; \frac{1.124}{5.769 + 1.124} \;=\; \frac{1.124}{6.893}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{v,\,Co} \;\approx\; 0.1631 \;\text{or}\; 16.31\%\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Archard's equation for wear volume:**

Given:
- $F = 500$ N
- $s = 50$ km $= 5 \times 10^{4}$ m $= 5 \times 10^{7}$ mm
- $K = 5 \times 10^{-5}$
- $H = 18000$ MPa $= 18000$ N/mm²

$$
V_{\text{wear}} \;=\; \frac{K \cdot F \cdot s}{H}
$$

$$
V_{\text{wear}} \;=\; \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 500 \cdot (5 \times 10^{7})}{18000}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
K \cdot F \cdot s \;=\; (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 500 \cdot (5 \times 10^{7})
$$

$$
= (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{10})
$$

$$
= 1.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

Divide by $H$:

$$
V_{\text{wear}} \;=\; \frac{1.25 \times 10^{6}}{18000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{\text{wear}} \;\approx\; 69.44 \text{ mm}^{3}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

A wear volume of ~ 69 mm³ over 50 km of sliding represents a **moderate wear rate of ~ 1.39 × 10⁻³ mm³/km**. For a wire-drawing die with a working-diameter region of ~ 5 mm³ volume, this would translate to an approximate **die life of ~ 360 km** — competitive for stainless-steel drawing service.

**[Mark Allocation: 2 marks volume fraction + 3 marks Archard setup + 1.5 marks arithmetic + 0.5 marks interpretation]**

---

#### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **composition, microstructure, and properties** of **H13 hot-work die steel**. Explain the role of **Mo and V** in producing **secondary hardening**, and outline the **standard heat-treatment cycle** (preheating → austenitizing → quenching → double tempering) with approximate temperatures.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A **D2 cold-work die steel** specimen is oil-quenched from 1050 °C to 25 °C. Given $M_s = 220 °C$ and Koistinen-Marburger constant $k = 0.011$, calculate the **as-quenched martensite fraction** and **retained-austenite fraction**. Recommend **two post-quench treatments** to reduce retained austenite and improve dimensional stability.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Composition of H13 (AISI/UNS T20813):**

| Element | C | Cr | Mo | V | Si | Mn |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| % wt | 0.32 – 0.45 | 4.75 – 5.50 | 1.10 – 1.75 | 0.80 – 1.20 | 0.80 – 1.20 | 0.20 – 0.50 |

**Microstructure (after proper heat treatment):**
- **Matrix:** Tempered martensite with fine, uniformly dispersed **Mo₂C and V₄C₃ (VC)** secondary carbides.
- **Undissolved carbides:** Small fraction of primary **M₆C and M₂₃C₆**.
- This gives a balanced microstructure of **hardness + toughness**.

**Properties:**
- As-quenched hardness: ~ 62 HRC
- Working hardness (after double temper): **48 – 52 HRC**
- Charpy V-notch impact: **20 – 40 J** (high)
- Hot hardness at 600 °C: ~ 0.6 × room-temperature hardness
- Excellent **thermal fatigue** resistance (resists heat-checking).
- Good **toughness** at elevated temperature.

**Role of Mo and V — Secondary Hardening Mechanism:**
During tempering at 500 – 600 °C, the alloy carbides **Mo₂C** and **VC** precipitate in a **very fine, coherent form** within the martensite matrix. These precipitates:
1. Pin dislocations → **resist recovery and softening**.
2. Provide **nucleation sites** that coarsen slowly → stable up to 600 °C.
3. Increase the **secondary hardness peak** to ~ 52 – 55 HRC, ABOVE the as-quenched hardness.

**Heat-Treatment Cycle:**

| Step | Operation | Temperature | Time / Soak |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First preheat | 600 – 650 °C | 30 min (equalize) |
| 2 | Second preheat | 850 °C | 20 min (through-heat) |
| 3 | Austenitizing | 1020 – 1050 °C | 30 min (carbide dissolution) |
| 4 | Quench | Air cool (still) or oil | To ~ 50 °C |
| 5 | First temper | 550 – 600 °C | 2 h |
| 6 | Second temper | 550 – 600 °C | 2 h |
| 7 | Optional nitriding | 520 °C | 20 h (surface to ~ 1000 HV) |

> **Critical Practice:** Two tempering cycles are mandatory. The first temper converts **retained austenite → fresh martensite**; the second temper tempers that fresh martensite. Skipping the second temper leaves the die dimensionally unstable.

**[Mark Allocation: 2 marks composition + 2 marks microstructure/properties + 2 marks Mo/V mechanism + 1 mark heat-treatment cycle]**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Koistinen-Marburger equation:**

$$
f_m \;=\; 1 - \exp\bigl[-k \cdot (M_s - T_q)\bigr]
$$

$$
f_m \;=\; 1 - \exp\bigl[-0.011 \cdot (220 - 25)\bigr]
$$

$$
f_m \;=\; 1 - \exp(-2.145)
$$

$$
\exp(-2.145) \;\approx\; 0.1172
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_m \;\approx\; 0.883 \;\text{ or }\; 88.3\% \text{ martensite}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute retained-austenite fraction:**

$$
f_{\gamma} \;=\; 1 - f_m \;=\; 1 - 0.883
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{\gamma} \;\approx\; 0.117 \;\text{ or }\; 11.7\% \text{ retained austenite}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommend two post-quench treatments:**

**Treatment 1 — Cryogenic (Sub-Zero) Treatment:**
- Cool the quenched die slowly to **−80 °C** (dry-ice/acetone) or **−196 °C** (liquid nitrogen).
- Hold for 24 – 48 hours.
- Retained $\gamma$ transforms isothermally → martensite.
- Subsequent **tempering at 200 °C** tempers the fresh martensite.
- *Effect:* Reduces retained austenite from ~ 12 % to ~ 1 – 2 %; improves dimensional stability and wear life by **30 – 50 %**.

**Treatment 2 — Multiple Tempering (Double / Triple Temper):**
- Temper at **500 – 525 °C** (for D2) for 2 hours, repeated twice (or thrice) with cooling to room temperature between cycles.
- During each temper, retained $\gamma$ → carbide precipitation → fresh martensite.
- *Effect:* Same as cryogenic — but with added benefit of **secondary hardening** due to fine Cr-carbide precipitation. Most D2 manufacturers prefer this over cryogenic for cost reasons.

> **Bonus Note:** A combined **cryogenic + double temper** is the **premium route** used for high-precision blanking dies and aerospace tooling.

**[Mark Allocation: 3 marks numerical + 2 marks each treatment (mechanism + effect) = 7]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "red hardness" with "high-temperature hardness"** — Red hardness specifically refers to hardness retention at the **red-glow temperature (~ 600 °C)** and is tied to **secondary carbide precipitation**, not just any hot hardness.
> 2. **Mis-spelling Stellite as "Stellate" / "Stelitte"** — Spelling errors in technical names are penalized **½ to 1 mark** under KTU valuation.
> 3. **Confusing HSS grades M2 and M42** — Students forget that M42 contains **8 % Co** and has **higher red hardness**.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the Archard equation — $H$ MUST be in MPa (or N/mm²), $s$ in mm, $F$ in N. A missing unit conversion is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Drawing the Archard derivation incorrectly** — Board examiners specifically want the **dimensionless wear coefficient $K$** introduced and the **inversely proportional relationship with $H$** highlighted. Missing the proportional reasoning loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the second temper in H13 heat treatment** — A single temper is **incomplete** and examiners deduct 1 mark for omitting it.
> 7. **Mixing up ISO P / M / K grades** — K = cast iron, P = steel, M = stainless. Reverse mapping loses 1.5 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Hard material alloys = Die steels + Carbides + Cast alloys + Cermets**.
- [ ] **Cold-work steels:** W, O, A, **D** series. D2 is the **abrasion king**.
- [ ] **Hot-work steels:** **H13** is industry benchmark for die-casting & forging. Needs **double temper**.
- [ ] **HSS:** **M2** (general) and **M42** (Co-bearing, 68 HRC). Both show **red hardness** via secondary carbides.
- [ ] **Cemented carbides:** P/M composite; ISO **P** = steel, **M** = stainless, **K** = cast iron; binder is **Co**.
- [ ] **Archard equation:** $V = KFs/H$ — wear is **inversely proportional to hardness**.
- [ ] **Koistinen-Marburger:** $f_m = 1 - \exp[-k(M_s - T_q)]$; controls retained austenite in quenched D2/H13.
- [ ] **Stellite:** Co-Cr-W-C cast alloy; hot-hard, weldable overlay for hot trimming.
- [ ] **Ni-Hard:** High-Cr white cast iron; 550 – 700 HB; slurry/abrasion service.
- [ ] **Secondary hardening** is the key to **red hardness** — caused by **Mo₂C, VC, W₂C** precipitation at 500 – 600 °C.
- [ ] **Volume fraction of binder in WC-Co** is found via $f_v = (W_c/\rho_c)/(W_c/\rho_c + W_b/\rho_b)$ — use it whenever carbide composition is given.
- [ ] **Heat treatment of H13:** preheat 650 °C → preheat 850 °C → austenitize 1020 °C → air/oil quench → **double temper at 550 °C**.
- [ ] **HRC ≈ 0.1·HV − 9** (approximate conversion in HV 400 – 900 range).
- [ ] **Hot-hardness ratio** $H_T/H_{25} > 0.85$ for premium HSS; ~ 0.6 for H13 at 600 °C.
- [ ] **Taylor's tool-life equation** ($V^n T = C$) — appears frequently in extended numericals.
- [ ] **Selection rule of thumb:** Cold work → D2; Hot work → H13; Wire drawing → WC-Co; Plastic mould → P20; Hot trim → Stellite; Slurry → Ni-Hard.

> **Final Tip:** When a KTU numerical gives you **two alloys to compare**, always structure your answer as **(i) Material identity → (ii) Key composition → (iii) Microstructure → (iv) Hardness/toughness number → (v) Service justification**. This **5-point template** consistently earns full marks under the KTU 2024 rubric.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel Castings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Steel Castings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steel Casting** is a net-shape or near-net-shape manufacturing process in which molten steel (liquid steel) is poured into a refractory mould cavity of the desired shape and allowed to solidify, after which the solidified metal is removed from the mould, cleaned, and subjected to inspection and heat treatment to obtain the final engineering component.

Steel castings belong to the family of **ferrous castings** and are characterized by the fact that the molten metal is essentially a **carbon steel** or **alloy steel** with carbon content typically ranging from **0.1% to 0.65%**, although stainless and tool-steel grades may also be cast. The pouring temperature is significantly higher than that of cast iron — typically in the range of **1500 °C to 1650 °C** — which makes the process technically more demanding than grey or ductile iron casting.

According to the **Steel Founders' Society of America (SFSA)** classification, steel castings are grouped into four broad categories:

| Category | Carbon Content (wt. %) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Low-carbon steel castings | < 0.20 | Welded structures, railway parts |
| Medium-carbon steel castings | 0.20 – 0.50 | General engineering, gears |
| High-carbon steel castings | > 0.50 | Wear-resistant components |
| Alloy-steel castings (incl. stainless) | Variable | Corrosion-resistant, high-strength parts |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of steel casting like baking a cake in a customized mould.** The "batter" is molten steel heated to glowing-orange temperatures, the "pan" is the refractory mould (made of sand, ceramic, or metal), and the "oven" is the surrounding environment. When you pour the batter and let it cool, it takes the exact shape of the pan. In steel casting, however, the "batter" is **~1500 °C hot** and can react violently with moisture, sand, and air — so every step is engineered for control, safety, and dimensional accuracy.

A simple kitchen example: imagine pouring molten chocolate into a shaped ice-cube tray. If the chocolate is too hot, it burns; if the tray has moisture, the chocolate seizes and bubbles. Steel casting deals with similar physics, but at **temperatures 5–6 times higher** than chocolate and with much stricter mechanical property requirements on the final product.

---

## 1.3 Why Steel Castings? (Engineering Motivation)

- **Design Flexibility** — Complex shapes with internal cavities, undercuts, and curved contours that are impossible to forge or machine economically.
- **Mechanical Property Isotropy** — Cast steel parts have reasonably uniform properties in all directions, unlike rolled/forged products which have a directional grain flow.
- **Wide Property Range** — By adjusting carbon and alloy content, properties from ductile (low C) to hard/wear-resistant (high C) can be obtained in **a single cast component**.
- **Weldability & Machinability** — Cast steel can be welded, heat-treated, and machined, allowing post-casting design modifications.
- **Weight Range** — Castings from **a few hundred grams to over 200 tonnes** are possible.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently ask students to compare *steel castings vs. iron castings* and to justify why a particular grade is chosen for a given application. Always remember: **higher carbon in steel ⇒ higher strength/hardness but lower ductility and weldability.**

---

## 1.4 Key Physical & Process Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Pouring temperature of steel | **1500 °C – 1650 °C** |
| Liquidus temperature of plain carbon steel | ~1490 °C |
| Solidus temperature of plain carbon steel | ~1430 °C |
| Density of molten steel | **~7.0 g/cm³** |
| Specific heat of molten steel | ~0.67 kJ/kg·K |
| Shrinkage allowance (linear) | **2% (≈ 1/8 inch per foot)** |
| Mould material (typical) | Silica sand with binders / ceramic shell |
| Typical mould hardness | 85 – 95 (mould hardness number) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Steel Casting Process Flow

The steel casting process can be broken down into **eight sequential stages**. Each stage is a potential point of defect generation, and the KTU examiner expects you to understand the *cause-and-effect* relationships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The eight stages are: **(1) Pattern Making → (2) Mould Making → (3) Melting → (4) Tapping & Ladle Treatment → (5) Pouring → (6) Solidification & Cooling → (7) Shake-out & Cleaning → (8) Inspection & Heat Treatment.**

### Stage 1 — Pattern Making
- Patterns are made of **wood, metal, or plastic** with an allowance for shrinkage (linear shrinkage of steel ≈ 2%, or about **3 mm per 150 mm**).
- Additional allowances: **draft (1°–2°), machining (3–6 mm), distortion, and shake allowance.**

### Stage 2 — Mould Making
- Moulds may be:
  - **Green sand moulds** (sand + bentonite clay + water; used for simple shapes).
  - **Dry sand moulds** (baked for higher strength).
  - **Chemically bonded sand moulds** (e.g., **furan no-bake, phenolic urethane, sodium silicate-CO₂**).
  - **Ceramic shell moulds** (used in investment casting for precision).
- For steel castings, **silica sand (SiO₂)** is the standard refractory because of its high melting point (~1710 °C) and chemical compatibility.

### Stage 3 — Melting of Steel
Steel is melted industrially using:

| Furnace Type | Capacity (typical) | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** | 1 – 400 tonnes | Most common; uses graphite electrodes; high productivity |
| **Induction Furnace (Coreless)** | 0.1 – 50 tonnes | Clean, controllable; suited for alloy steels |
| **Induction Furnace (Channel)** | Up to 800 tonnes | Used for holding/melting; lower energy efficiency |
| **AOD (Argon-Oxygen Decarburization)** | 5 – 200 tonnes | Used after EAF for stainless steel refining |
| **VOD (Vacuum Oxygen Decarburization)** | 10 – 150 tonnes | Ultra-low carbon stainless steel |
| **Cupola** | Small scale | Rarely used for steel; mainly for iron |

### Stage 4 — Tapping and Ladle Treatment
- Molten steel is tapped into a **preheated ladle** (typically a teapot or bottom-pour ladle).
- Ladle treatments include:
  - **Deoxidation** (Al, Si, Mn added to remove dissolved oxygen).
  - **Desulphurization** (slag conditioning, calcium injection).
  - **Inclusion modification** (Ca-Si treatment to control sulphide shape).
  - **Temperature adjustment** (ladle furnace holds the steel at a precise pouring temperature).

### Stage 5 — Pouring
- Pouring must occur above the liquidus temperature to ensure complete filling.
- Pouring rate is governed by the **choke area** (smallest cross-section in the gating system) and the **metallostatic head**.
- For steel castings, **bottom-gating** is preferred to minimize slag entrainment and to ensure a quiescent fill.

### Stage 6 — Solidification & Cooling
- Solidification proceeds from the mould walls inward.
- **Shrinkage** occurs in three stages: **liquid shrinkage, solidification shrinkage, and solid (thermal) shrinkage.**
- **Risers (feeders)** compensate for shrinkage by supplying additional liquid metal.
- The total volumetric shrinkage of steel is approximately **3.5% – 6.0%** (compared with 1.0% – 2.5% for cast iron).

### Stage 7 — Shake-out and Cleaning
- Once solidified, the casting is shaken out of the mould.
- Cleaning operations include:
  - **Shot blasting / sand blasting** (removes sand and scale).
  - **Grinding** (removes gates, risers, parting-line flash).
  - **Cutting / burning** (oxygen-acetylene or plasma cutting for gates and risers).

### Stage 8 — Inspection & Heat Treatment
- **Non-destructive testing (NDT):** Radiographic (X-ray/gamma), ultrasonic, magnetic particle, dye penetrant.
- **Destructive testing:** Tensile, impact (Charpy/Izod), hardness, chemical analysis.
- **Heat treatments commonly applied:**
  - **Normalizing** (refines grain structure).
  - **Annealing** (relieves stresses, softens for machining).
  - **Quenching & Tempering** (for high-strength grades).
  - **Solution annealing** (for stainless steels).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Steel casting is a process-driven subject; numerical problems typically involve **pouring time, riser design, and heat balance.** All essential formulas are tabulated below for quick revision.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_p = \dfrac{k \cdot \sqrt{W}}{C}$ | **Chvorinov's Rule** — local solidification time *t*; $W$ = volume-to-surface area ratio (V/A); $k$ = mould constant; $C$ = a constant for the metal. |
| 2 | $\text{Shrinkage allowance} = L \times 0.02$ | Linear shrinkage ≈ **2%** for steel castings; $L$ = original length. |
| 3 | $V_{\text{riser}} \geq \beta \cdot (V_{\text{casting}} \times S_v)$ | Riser volume must compensate for total volumetric shrinkage $S_v$ (≈ 3.5%–6% for steel). |
| 4 | $\dfrac{V_{\text{riser}}/A_{\text{riser}}}{V_{\text{casting}}/A_{\text{casting}}} \geq 1.2$ | **Riser design rule (modulus method)** — riser must solidify **after** the casting. |
| 5 | $T_{\text{pour}} = T_{\text{liquidus}} + \Delta T_{\text{superheat}}$ | Typical $\Delta T_{\text{superheat}}$ for steel = **50 °C – 100 °C**. |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat content of molten steel for ladle preheat calculation. |
| 7 | $h = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot t}$ | Heat flux to mould during pour (used in mould-cooling analysis). |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{A_{\text{choke}}}{A_{\text{gate}}}$ | Gating ratio; for steel, $f \approx 1 : 2 : 4$ (choke : runner : gate) is common in **unpressurized systems.** |
| 9 | $V_{\text{flow}} = C_d \cdot A_{\text{choke}} \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | **Bernoulli's equation** for metal flow through the gating system; $C_d$ ≈ 0.4 – 0.6 for steel. |
| 10 | $F = m \cdot a$ (fluid momentum) | Used to estimate impact force on mould walls during pouring. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the **choke area** equation, students often confuse $C_d$ (discharge coefficient) with the contraction coefficient. Use $C_d \approx 0.5$ for bottom-gated steel castings unless otherwise specified.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steel castings are used wherever the combination of **strength, toughness, and complex geometry** is critical:
- **Power generation** — turbine housings, rotor hubs, valve bodies.
- **Mining & Earth-moving** — crusher liners, dragline bucket components (often in manganese steel).
- **Railways** — bogie frames, couplings, wheel centres.
- **Defence & Aerospace** — gun cradles, missile housings, helicopter rotor hubs.
- **Marine & Oil & Gas** — anchor chain links, pump casings, valve bodies (often in stainless or duplex grades).
- **Heavy industry** — rolling mill housings, gear blanks, mill liners.

> [!TIP]
> A 200-tonne steel casting (such as a turbine casing or rolling-mill housing) is one of the most demanding feats in manufacturing — requiring precise thermal control of **hundreds of tonnes of molten metal** in a single pour.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Riser Sizing by Chvorinov's Rule

The **modulus method** is the workhorse of riser design in steel foundries. The derivation proceeds as follows.

### Step 1 — Heat Balance for Solidification

For a casting of volume $V_c$ and surface area $A_c$, the **thermal modulus** is defined as

$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c}$$

For the riser, similarly,

$$M_r = \frac{V_r}{A_r}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Chvorinov's Rule

According to Chvorinov, the local solidification time of a region is proportional to the square of its modulus:

$$t \propto M^{2}$$

Or in equation form,

$$t_s = k \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^{2} = k \cdot M^{2}$$

where $k$ is the mould constant (depends on mould material, metal properties, and superheat).

### Step 3 — The Riser-Solidifies-Last Condition

To prevent shrinkage porosity in the casting, the riser must remain liquid **longer** than the casting:

$$t_r > t_c$$

Substituting Chvorinov's equation,

$$k \cdot M_r^{2} > k \cdot M_c^{2}$$

The constant $k$ cancels out (assuming same mould and same pour temperature), giving:

$$M_r > M_c$$

### Step 4 — Safety Factor

A practical safety factor of **1.2** is applied to account for heat losses and riser inefficiencies:

$$\frac{M_r}{M_c} \geq 1.2$$

This can be rewritten as:

$$\frac{V_r / A_r}{V_c / A_c} \geq 1.2$$

In words: **the riser's volume-to-area ratio must be at least 1.2 times that of the casting.**

### Step 5 — Worked Example (KTU-style problem)

**Problem:** A steel casting has volume $V_c = 8000 \text{ cm}^3$ and surface area $A_c = 2000 \text{ cm}^2$. Design a cylindrical riser of height equal to its diameter (i.e., $H = D$) such that the riser solidifies last.

**Solution:**

The casting modulus is

$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{8000}{2000} = 4 \text{ cm}$$

The required riser modulus is

$$M_r \geq 1.2 \times M_c = 1.2 \times 4 = 4.8 \text{ cm}$$

For a cylinder of diameter $D$ and height $H = D$:

$$V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot H = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{3}$$

$$A_r = \pi D \cdot H + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \pi D^{2} + \frac{\pi}{2} D^{2} = \frac{3\pi}{2} D^{2}$$

The riser modulus is therefore

$$M_r = \frac{V_r}{A_r} = \frac{(\pi/4) D^{3}}{(3\pi/2) D^{2}} = \frac{D}{6}$$

Setting $M_r = 4.8$ cm:

$$\frac{D}{6} = 4.8$$

$$D = 28.8 \text{ cm}$$

Hence the riser dimensions are **$D = H \approx 29$ cm**, and its volume is

$$V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (28.8)^{3} \approx 18{,}725 \text{ cm}^3$$

This is a textbook-grade example. **End of derivation.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pouring Time from Bernoulli's Equation

### Step 1 — Continuity and Energy Equations

For a bottom-pour ladle feeding a mould through a gating system with a choke (sprue base) of cross-sectional area $A_{\text{choke}}$, the volumetric flow rate is given by Torricelli/Bernoulli:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_{\text{choke}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$

where $h$ is the effective metallostatic head and $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient.

### Step 2 — Total Pouring Time

For a casting of mass $m$ and density $\rho$, the volume is $V = m/\rho$. The pouring time is

$$t_p = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_{\text{choke}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}}$$

### Step 3 — Empirical Simplification (Flinn's Formula)

In foundry practice, $h$ varies as the ladle empties. An **empirical correction** yields Flinn's pouring-time equation:

$$t_p = \frac{k \cdot \sqrt{W}}{C}$$

where:
- $W$ = mass of the casting (in kg or lb).
- $k$ = a constant (≈ **0.83 for steel in green-sand moulds**).
- $C$ = a constant depending on the metal (≈ **4.5 for steel**).

This empirical form is what appears most often in KTU exam problems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Steel vs. Iron Castings

| Attribute | Steel Castings | Cast Iron Castings |
|---|---|---|
| Pouring temperature | **1500 – 1650 °C** | 1250 – 1400 °C |
| Carbon content | 0.1 – 0.65 % (or alloyed) | 2.5 – 4.0 % |
| Total shrinkage | **3.5 – 6.0 %** | 1.0 – 2.5 % |
| Fluidity | Lower (problematic) | Higher (good) |
| Mould strength required | Higher (sodium silicate / furan) | Moderate (green sand) |
| Ductility | High | Low (brittle in grey iron) |
| Weldability | Yes (after low C content) | Difficult (white iron) |
| Cost of melting | Higher (EAF, more energy) | Lower (cupola) |
| Typical applications | Critical engineering parts | Pipes, blocks, machine beds |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Steel Casting Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete process chain — from pattern to finished part — as expected by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Drawing and Specification] --> B[Pattern and Core Making]
    B --> C[Mould Assembly with Sand and Binder]
    C --> D[Melting in EAF or Induction Furnace]
    D --> E[Tapping into Preheated Ladle]
    E --> F[Ladle Treatment Deoxidation Desulphurization]
    F --> G[Pouring into Mould Cavity]
    G --> H[Solidification and Cooling with Risers Feeding]
    H --> I[Shakeout Casting Removed from Mould]
    I --> J[Cleaning Shot Blast and Grinding]
    J --> K[Inspection Radiography Ultrasonic DPT]
    K --> P{Defects Found?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Rework or Reject]
    P -- No --> L[Heat Treatment Normalize Anneal Quench Temper]
    L --> M[Final Machining if Required]
    M --> N[Final Inspection and Dispatch]
    Q --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **block-level functional architecture flow** in lieu of a physical 3D drawing of a foundry. The KTU examiner will accept such schematic flow-charts for any process question.

---

## 4.2 Mould Cavity Cross-Section (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cope Upper Half] --> B[Drag Lower Half]
    B --> C[Mould Cavity with Pattern Shape]
    C --> D[Riser Feeder]
    C --> E[Sprue Downgate]
    E --> F[Runner]
    F --> G[Gate Entry Point]
    G --> H[Choke Smallest Section]
    A -. parting line .- B
    D --> I[Atmosphere Air Permeable Vents]
    I --> J[Sand Mould Body]
```

This block diagram represents a **schematic vertical section** of a typical steel-casting mould, identifying the runner, gate, choke, riser, and venting arrangement. In a KTU answer sheet, you may reproduce this as a labelled hand-drawn sketch.

---

## 4.3 Function-Level Architecture of an Electric Arc Furnace

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Three Phase Power Supply] --> T[Step Down Transformer]
    T --> E1[Graphite Electrode 1]
    T --> E2[Graphite Electrode 2]
    T --> E3[Graphite Electrode 3]
    E1 --> V[Electric Arc Zone]
    E2 --> V
    E3 --> V
    V --> S[Scrap Steel Charge]
    S --> M[Liquid Steel Pool]
    M --> SL[Slag Layer CaO SiO2]
    SL --> T1[Tap Spout Bottom Pour]
    T1 --> L[Ladle for Refining and Pouring]
    L --> FO[Foundry Mould]
    V --> EF[Exhaust Gas and Dust Extraction]
    EF --> F1[Bag Filter and Cleaning Unit]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 — high-weight question:** *"Explain the working of an Electric Arc Furnace for steel melting with a neat sketch."* The above block diagram captures every functional element the examiner expects to see.

---

## 4.4 Defect–Cause–Remedy Matrix (Cause-Effect Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Shrinkage Porosity Defect] --> C1[Insufficient Riser Size]
    S --> C2[Riser Solidified Before Casting]
    S --> C3[Hot Spot in Casting Geometry]
    C1 --> R1[Increase Riser Modulus]
    C2 --> R2[Add Exothermic Sleeve or Insulating Top]
    C3 --> R3[Use Chills at Hot Spot]
    S --> P[Preventive Action]
    R1 --> P
    R2 --> P
    R3 --> P
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a KTU 14-mark question, this kind of cause-effect tree (defect → cause → remedy) fetches full marks. Always tie the defect to a specific casting-process stage (e.g., shrinkage porosity arises during **solidification**, not at cleaning).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List any six advantages of steel castings over cast iron castings."**

**Model Answer (6 points):**
1. **Higher strength and toughness** — Steel castings can achieve tensile strengths of 400 – 1000 MPa vs. 150 – 400 MPa for cast iron.
2. **Better ductility** — Steel castings can elongate 10 – 25%, allowing plastic deformation before failure.
3. **Weldability** — Low- and medium-carbon steel castings are weldable; cast iron welds are difficult and brittle.
4. **Isotropic properties** — Steel castings have uniform properties in all directions, since the dendritic grain structure is equiaxed.
5. **Wider property range** — Heat treatment can be applied (normalize, quench, temper) to tailor hardness and strength.
6. **Higher service temperature** — Steel castings retain strength at 400 – 600 °C, whereas grey iron loses strength above 250 °C.
7. **Design flexibility** — Complex shapes with thinner sections and sharper corners are possible due to better mechanical properties.
8. **Better corrosion and wear resistance** (with alloying) — Stainless and high-chromium grades are feasible.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define the term 'riser' in steel casting and state the rule that governs its size."**

**Model Answer:**

A **riser** (also called a *feeder* or *header*) is a reservoir of molten metal attached to the casting through a feeder neck. It supplies additional liquid metal to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage** that occurs during the three stages of cooling — *liquid, solidification, and solid (thermal) contraction* — thereby preventing the formation of **shrinkage cavities and porosity** in the final casting.

**Governing Rule (Chvorinov's Rule, modulus method):**

> The **riser's thermal modulus** (volume-to-surface-area ratio) must be **at least 1.2 times** the casting's thermal modulus, so that the riser solidifies *last* and remains liquid to feed the contracting casting.

$$\frac{V_r / A_r}{V_c / A_c} \geq 1.2$$

In addition, a **feed-path** condition must be satisfied: the riser neck must not freeze before the casting has fully solidified.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Marks are deducted for **sketching the mould without labels** (gate, runner, sprue, riser, parting line must be marked).
> - In *numerical riser design* problems, **forgetting the 1.2 safety factor** on the modulus is a one-mark deduction.
> - For *process questions*, do not list more than **6 – 8 key points**; long, repetitive answers do not earn extra marks.
> - Use **standard units** (cm, cm², cm³) consistently. Mixing mm and cm in a derivation is a common pitfall.

---
