### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Explain in detail the various methods of melting steel for foundry applications. Compare the Electric Arc Furnace and Induction Furnace processes with respect to energy efficiency, melt quality, and applicability."**

**Model Answer Layout (14 marks distribution shown):**

#### (a) Methods of Melting Steel — 7 Marks
The principal methods are:

**1. Electric Arc Furnace (EAF):**
- Cold-charge process using scrap steel.
- Three-phase graphite electrodes create an arc; temperatures exceed 1600 °C.
- Refining via **slag–metal reactions**; oxygen lancing for decarburization.
- Capacities: **1 tonne to 400 tonnes**.
- Productivity: ~30 – 60 tonnes/hour in large furnaces.
- Slag basicity controlled for desulphurization.

**2. Induction Furnace:**
- **Coreless type** — used for foundry melting; operates at 1 – 10 kHz (medium frequency).
- **Channel type** — used for holding; very large capacities.
- **Electromagnetic stirring** is inherent → homogeneous melt.
- No electrodes → cleaner melt, less carbon pick-up.
- Limitation: cannot refine — no decarburization, no slag control for P and S removal.
- Best for **alloy steel** and **stainless steel** (using AOD downstream).

**3. Argon-Oxygen Decarburization (AOD):**
- A refining vessel, not a primary melter.
- Argon/O₂ mixture decarburizes stainless steel without excessive Cr oxidation.
- Used after EAF or induction melting.

**4. Vacuum Oxygen Decarburization (VOD):**
- For ultra-low-carbon stainless steel.
- Lower pressure reduces CO partial pressure → decarburization at low C without Cr loss.

**5. Cupola Furnace:**
- Vertical shaft; coke-fired.
- Limited use for steel; energy-intensive, limited control.

#### (b) Comparison EAF vs. Induction Furnace — 7 Marks

| Parameter | EAF | Induction Furnace |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Electrical arc | Electromagnetic induction |
| Capacity | Up to 400 t | Up to 50 t (coreless) |
| Energy efficiency | **50 – 60 %** | **60 – 75 %** |
| Melt cleanliness | Moderate (electrodes add C) | High (no electrodes) |
| Refining capability | Excellent (slag control) | Limited (no refining) |
| Capital cost | High | Moderate |
| Best suited for | **Plain carbon steel, large tonnage** | **Alloy steel, stainless, precision** |
| Electromagnetic stirring | None (slag raked) | Inherent, vigorous |
| Start-up time | Long (40 – 60 min) | Short (15 – 20 min) |

**Conclusion:** EAF is the workhorse for bulk steel casting; induction furnaces dominate for **alloy, tool, and stainless steel** foundry work.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Design a cylindrical riser (height equal to diameter) for a steel casting of volume 10,000 cm³ and surface area 2500 cm². The mould is green-sand and the riser-to-casting modulus ratio should be at least 1.2. State the Chvorinov's rule clearly and justify each step of your calculation."**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Statement of Chvorinov's Rule and Modulus Concept — 7 Marks

Chvorinov's rule states that the **local solidification time** of a casting region is directly proportional to the square of its **thermal modulus** $M$, defined as the volume-to-surface-area ratio:

$$t_s = k \cdot M^{2} = k \cdot \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^{2}$$

where $k$ is the mould constant, depending on the **mould material, metal, and pouring conditions.**

**Design Implication:** For a riser to remain liquid until the casting has fully solidified, the riser's modulus must be **larger** than the casting's modulus:

$$M_r > M_c$$

In practice, a safety factor $F$ is applied:

$$\frac{M_r}{M_c} \geq F = 1.2$$

This ensures that even with heat losses from the riser top, the riser does not freeze prematurely.

#### (b) Numerical Design of the Riser — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Casting modulus:**

$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{10{,}000}{2500} = 4.0 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Required riser modulus (with safety factor 1.2):**

$$M_r \geq 1.2 \times M_c = 1.2 \times 4.0 = 4.8 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Geometry of cylindrical riser with H = D:**

For a cylinder of diameter $D$ and height $H = D$:

- Volume: $V_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} H = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{3}$
- Surface area: $A_r = \pi D H + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \pi D^{2} + \dfrac{\pi}{2} D^{2} = \dfrac{3\pi}{2} D^{2}$

**Step 4 — Riser modulus expression:**

$$M_r = \frac{V_r}{A_r} = \frac{(\pi/4) D^{3}}{(3\pi/2) D^{2}} = \frac{D}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for D:**

Setting $M_r = 4.8$ cm:

$$\frac{D}{6} = 4.8$$

$$D = 28.8 \text{ cm} \approx 29 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 6 — Verify riser volume:**

$$V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (28.8)^{3} \approx 18{,}725 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

The riser must hold **at least** $18{,}725$ cm³ of molten steel, which is approximately **1.87 times the casting volume** — acceptable for a steel casting (which has a volumetric shrinkage of 3.5%–6%).

**Step 7 — Final answer:**

> The cylindrical riser should have **$D = H \approx 29$ cm** with a feeder neck area of at least 25% of the riser cross-section to ensure unrestricted feed.

**Step 8 — Practical additions:**
- Use an **exothermic or insulating sleeve** on top of the riser to extend the feeding time.
- The riser should be placed at the **highest hot spot** of the casting.
- Use a **chill** at the thermal centre if necessary to promote directional solidification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Final Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Steel Casting Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. Forgetting to mention that **steel castings require higher mould strength** than cast iron — sand + binder must be **furan or sodium silicate**, not just green sand, for large parts.
> 2. Using **3% shrinkage** for steel instead of the correct **2% linear / 3.5%–6% volumetric**.
> 3. Confusing **choke** with **gate** in the gating system — the choke is the **smallest section** controlling flow, located at the **bottom of the sprue** in an unpressurized system.
> 4. Stating *pouring temperature* as "around 1500 °C" without acknowledging the **superheat** above the liquidus (i.e., $T_{\text{pour}} = T_{\text{liquidus}} + \Delta T$).
> 5. For **AOD**, forgetting that it is a **refining** vessel, not a melting vessel.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024):**

- **Definition:** Steel casting is the process of pouring molten steel (Fe + 0.1–0.65 % C, possibly alloyed) into a refractory mould and solidifying it to shape.
- **Pouring temperature:** **1500 – 1650 °C** (superheat ≈ 50 – 100 °C above liquidus).
- **Linear shrinkage allowance:** **2%**; volumetric shrinkage: **3.5% – 6%**.
- **Melting furnaces (KTU focus):** EAF (most common), induction (for alloys), AOD/VOD (for stainless).
- **Mould materials:** Green sand (simple shapes), chemically bonded sand (furan, phenolic, CO₂-silicate), ceramic shell (precision).
- **Riser design rule (Chvorinov's Rule):** $M_r / M_c \geq 1.2$.
- **Gating preference:** Bottom-gated for steel; gating ratio **1 : 2 : 4** in unpressurized systems.
- **Heat treatments:** Normalize, anneal, quench + temper, solution anneal (for stainless).
- **Defects to know:** Shrinkage porosity, gas porosity, hot tears, sand inclusions, cold shuts, misruns.
- **Defect remedies:** Riser placement, chills, venting, slag control, deoxidation, sand control.
- **Common comparison questions:** Steel vs. cast iron; EAF vs. induction; green sand vs. chemically bonded moulds.
- **Key equations to memorize:** Chvorinov's $t = k(V/A)^2$, Flinn's pouring-time $t_p = k\sqrt{W}/C$, Bernoulli's flow equation.
- **Industrial applications to mention in answers:** Power generation, mining, railways, marine, oil & gas, heavy machinery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Physical Properties (Tool Material Context)

In the context of **mould and die manufacture**, **Physical Properties** refer to the inherent, measurable, and characteristic attributes of a tool/die material that govern its response to external mechanical, thermal, and chemical stimuli during service. These properties dictate the suitability of a material (typically high-grade tool steels, die steels, carbides, or specialty alloys) for fabricating dies, moulds, jigs, and fixtures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> *Physical properties of die and mould materials encompass the measurable bulk characteristics — such as hardness, toughness, wear resistance, dimensional stability, thermal conductivity, heat resistance, machinability, and polishability — that collectively determine the working life, surface integrity, and dimensional accuracy of the finished tool under cyclic loading, elevated temperature, and abrasive wear conditions.*

The physical property profile is **decisive** because a mould/die must simultaneously withstand:
- **Mechanical stresses** (injection/compression/forming pressure).
- **Thermal cycling** (heating, holding, cooling phases).
- **Chemical interaction** (with molten polymers, lubricants, coolants).
- **Abrasive/adhesive wear** (from the workpiece flowing over die surfaces).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Choosing the Right Shoe for the Right Job"**
>
> Imagine you are buying shoes for a marathon runner. You would NOT pick soft cotton slippers — you'd choose shoes with a hard rubber sole (wear resistance), a cushioned insole (toughness), breathable fabric (thermal behavior), and a flexible but firm upper (elasticity). Likewise, when a manufacturing engineer picks a material for a die, they evaluate its **physical properties** to ensure the die "survives" millions of forming cycles without cracking, deforming, or wearing out.

### 1.3 Broad Classification of Physical Properties

Physical properties relevant to mould and die materials are classified into **four major families**:

1. **Mechanical Properties** — Hardness, Toughness, Wear Resistance, Fatigue Strength, Ductility, Compressive/Tensile Strength, Elastic Modulus.
2. **Thermal Properties** — Heat Resistance (Red Hardness), Thermal Conductivity, Coefficient of Thermal Expansion, Specific Heat.
3. **Surface/Integrity Properties** — Polishability, Surface Finish Retention, Grain Structure, Dimensional Stability.
4. **Manufacturability Properties** — Machinability, Hardenability, Grindability, Weldability (for repair).

### 1.4 Why Physical Properties Matter in Mould and Die Manufacture

| Property Family | Why It Matters |
|-----------------|----------------|
| Hardness | Resists indentation, plastic deformation, and abrasive wear. |
| Toughness | Resists sudden fracture under shock/impact loading. |
| Wear Resistance | Prolongs die life during sliding contact with workpiece. |
| Dimensional Stability | Maintains tolerance over thermal cycling. |
| Heat Resistance | Allows die to retain hardness at elevated working temperature. |
| Machinability | Determines ease of shaping the die cavity in soft (annealed) state. |
| Polishability | Crucial for plastic moulds requiring mirror finish. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Principle (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
> Almost all desirable physical properties in die materials are in a **trade-off relationship**. For example, **higher hardness ⇒ lower toughness**; **higher wear resistance ⇒ lower machinability**. Selecting a die material is therefore an *optimization problem*, not a maximization problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Property-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

### A. Hardness

**Definition:** The resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation, typically measured by indentation.

**Why it matters:** The harder the die surface, the greater its resistance to abrasive wear, galling, and plastic deformation under high forming pressures.

**How it works:** Hardness is a direct consequence of the material's microstructure — particularly the amount, distribution, and morphology of carbides (e.g., M₇C₃, M₂₃C₆, MC) in a tempered martensitic matrix.

**Common Scales Used in Die Industry:**

| Scale | Indenter | Load (Typical) | Unit |
|-------|----------|----------------|------|
| Rockwell C (HRC) | Diamond Cone | 150 kgf | dimensionless |
| Vickers (HV) | Diamond Pyramid | Variable (e.g., 30 kgf) | kgf/mm² |
| Brinell (HB) | Tungsten Carbide Ball | 3000 kgf | kgf/mm² |
| Shore (HS) | Hardened Steel Pin | Spring-loaded | dimensionless |

**Empirical Correlation (Important):**

$$\text{Tensile Strength (MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times \text{HB} \quad \text{(for steels, HB} < 450\text{)}$$

**Typical Hardness Ranges for Die Materials:**

| Die Type | Hardness (HRC) |
|----------|----------------|
| Cold working die (punching, blanking) | 58 – 64 |
| Cold working die (drawing, bending) | 55 – 60 |
| Hot working die (forging, die casting) | 40 – 55 |
| Plastic injection mould (cavity) | 45 – 60 |
| Plastic injection mould (core) | 50 – 62 |

---

### B. Toughness

**Definition:** The ability of a material to absorb energy and undergo plastic deformation **before** fracturing.

**Why it matters:** Dies experience cyclic impact loading (e.g., blanking punches strike metal sheets at high velocity; forging dies endure hammer blows). A die that is hard but brittle will **shatter** under such loading.

**How it works:** Toughness is enhanced by:
- Refined, fine grain structure.
- Presence of tough, ductile matrix phases.
- Reduction of brittle carbide networks.
- Tempering at appropriate temperature to relieve residual stresses.

**Quantified by Impact Tests:**

| Test | Specimen | Energy Unit |
|------|----------|-------------|
| Charpy V-Notch | 10×10×55 mm with V-notch | Joules (J) |
| Izod | Notched cantilever | Joules (J) |

$$\text{Impact Toughness} = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{A_{cross-section}} \quad \text{[J/mm²]}$$

**Key Insight:** Toughness *decreases* with increasing hardness — this is the classic **hardness–toughness trade-off**.

---

### C. Wear Resistance

**Definition:** The ability of a material to resist progressive loss of surface material due to relative motion against a contacting surface.

**Why it matters:** Die wear directly affects **dimensional accuracy** of the produced part, **surface finish**, and **tool life**. Excessive wear leads to part rejection and die refurbishment costs.

**Two Principal Wear Mechanisms in Dies:**

1. **Abrasive Wear** — Hard particles (inclusions in workpiece, carbides) plow/scratch the die surface.
2. **Adhesive Wear** — Microscopic welding at asperity junctions, followed by fracture.

**Adhesive Wear Volume (Archard Equation):**

$$V = \frac{k \cdot F \cdot L}{H}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Volume of worn material [mm³]
- $k$ = Wear coefficient (dimensionless)
- $F$ = Normal load [N]
- $L$ = Sliding distance [mm]
- $H$ = Hardness of the softer material [N/mm²]

**Engineering Utility:** This equation is used in KTU numericals to predict die life and compare material performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why high-carbon, high-chromium die steels (e.g., D2, D3) have superior wear resistance:** They contain 12–18% Cr, forming hard, voluminous chromium carbides (M₇C₃, ~1500–1800 HV) that act as wear-resistant particles embedded in a tougher matrix.

---

### D. Dimensional Stability

**Definition:** The ability of a die/mould to retain its original geometry and size over time, under thermal cycling and stress exposure.

**Why it matters:** Plastic injection moulds must produce millions of dimensionally identical parts. Any warping, shrinkage, or distortion of the die cavity translates to **out-of-tolerance parts**.

**How it works:** Controlled by:
- **Residual stress relief** (stress relieving, multiple tempering).
- **Cryogenic treatment** (sub-zero treatment to convert retained austenite to martensite).
- **Use of maraging steels** (low-carbon, age-hardened — minimal distortion).
- **Pre-hardened die steels** (delivered at 30–40 HRC, requiring no post-machining heat treatment).

---

### E. Heat Resistance (Red Hardness / Hot Hardness)

**Definition:** The ability of a die material to retain its hardness at elevated operating temperatures.

**Why it matters:** Hot-work dies (die casting, forging, extrusion) operate at 200°C – 600°C. If the die softens at temperature, it deforms plastically — a phenomenon called **"hot deformation"** or **"die sinking."**

**How it works:** Achieved by:
- Alloying with strong carbide formers: **Mo, V, W, Cr** (e.g., H13 die steel: 5% Cr, 1.5% Mo, 1% V).
- Secondary hardening during tempering (precipitation of fine alloy carbides).
- Avoidance of coarse carbides that dissolve easily.

**Typical Hot Hardness Values:**

| Temperature (°C) | H13 (HRC) | H11 (HRC) |
|------------------|-----------|-----------|
| 25 | 54 | 52 |
| 300 | 48 | 46 |
| 500 | 42 | 40 |
| 600 | 36 | 33 |

---

### F. Thermal Conductivity

**Definition:** The rate at which heat flows through a material per unit area per unit temperature gradient.

**Why it matters:** In plastic injection moulds, heat must be removed rapidly from the cavity to shorten cycle time. In die casting, rapid heat removal prevents soldering and prolongs die life.

$$\dot{Q} = k \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = Heat flow rate [W]
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity [W/m·K]
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area [m²]
- $\Delta T / \Delta x$ = Temperature gradient [K/m]

**Typical values:** H13 die steel: ~28 W/m·K; P20 mould steel: ~35 W/m·K; Tungsten carbide: ~80 W/m·K; Beryllium copper (insert): ~200 W/m·K.

---

### G. Machinability

**Definition:** The ease with which a material can be cut, shaped, and finished using conventional machining processes (turning, milling, drilling, EDM).

**Why it matters:** Die cavities have complex 3D geometry. Poor machinability → high manufacturing cost, longer lead time, and poor surface finish.

**Quantitative Index (relative to AISI 1212 steel, rated 100%):**

$$\text{Machinability Rating (\%)} = \frac{\text{Cutting speed for 60 min tool life (test material)}}{\text{Cutting speed for 60 min tool life (B1112 steel)}} \times 100$$

| Material (Annealed) | Machinability Rating (%) | HRC |
|---------------------|--------------------------|-----|
| AISI 1212 (reference) | 100 | ~10 |
| AISI P20 | 65 | ~30 |
| AISI H13 | 50 | ~20 |
| AISI D2 | 25 | ~22 |
| Tungsten Carbide | < 5 | ~70 |

**Why this is the inverse of wear resistance:** The hard carbides that make D2 wear-resistant also blunt cutting tools rapidly.

---

### H. Polishability

**Definition:** The ability of a die surface to be polished to a mirror-smooth finish (Ra < 0.05 μm for optical-grade moulds).

**Why it matters:** Plastic moulds for lenses, DVDs, medical devices, and transparent parts require optical-grade polish. Any micro-defect transfers to the part.

**How it works:** Requires:
- **Homogeneous, fine-grained microstructure** (no segregation bands).
- **Low inclusion content** (vacuum-arc-remelted steels preferred).
- **No primary carbides at surface** (carbide pull-out during polishing).
- **Low non-metallic inclusion rating** (ASTM E45 standard).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stainless mould steels (e.g., 420, S136)** are favored for high-polish applications because their martensitic matrix contains very fine, uniformly distributed carbides that polish to optical smoothness.

---

### I. Grain Structure

**Definition:** The size, shape, orientation, and distribution of grains (crystallites) in the metallic material.

**Why it matters:** Per the **Hall-Petch relationship**, finer grain → higher strength, higher toughness, better fatigue life, and better polishability.

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield strength [MPa]
- $\sigma_0$ = Friction stress [MPa]
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient [MPa·μm^(1/2)]
- $d$ = Average grain diameter [μm]

**Practical Implication:** KTU frequently tests this formula. Note that grain refinement provides a **simultaneous** improvement in both strength and toughness — a rare exception to the trade-off principle.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary Table)

| # | Property | Key Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Hardness-Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times HB$ | MPa, valid HB < 450 |
| 2 | Impact Toughness | $IT = E/A_{cs}$ | J/mm² |
| 3 | Adhesive Wear (Archard) | $V = k F L / H$ | mm³ |
| 4 | Heat Conduction (Fourier) | $\dot{Q} = kA \Delta T/\Delta x$ | W |
| 5 | Hall-Petch (Grain) | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$ | MPa |
| 6 | Machinability Rating | $\%M = (V_{test}/V_{ref}) \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Wear Rate | $W_r = V/(F \cdot L)$ | mm³/N·mm |
| 8 | Hardness Conversion | $HRC \approx (HB - 10)/10$ | Approx. for steels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Units:** KTU board exams expect **SI units** (Pa, m, kg) unless the question specifies otherwise. Conversions: 1 kgf/mm² = 9.81 MPa; 1 HRC ≈ 10 HB (approximate).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: Archard's Wear Equation (Step-by-Step)

The **Archard equation** is the most commonly tested analytical model in KTU examinations on physical properties. The full logical derivation is presented below.

### Step 1 — Physical Setup
Consider a hard asperity of one surface sliding over a softer mating surface under a normal load $F$. The asperity penetrates the softer surface to a depth $h$, producing a wear scar of cross-sectional area $A_s$.

### Step 2 — Volume of Material Displaced per Asperity Encounter
The volume of the softer material displaced (forming a wear fragment) per sliding encounter is approximated as:

$$V_p = A_s \cdot L$$

Where $L$ is the sliding distance. This assumes a uniform wear groove.

### Step 3 — Real (Rough) Contact Area
For a plastically deforming asperity, the projected real area of contact is proportional to the normal load through hardness:

$$A_r = \frac{F}{H}$$

Where $H$ is the indentation hardness of the softer material [N/mm²].

### Step 4 — Proportion of Encounters Producing Wear Fragments
Not every asperity encounter produces a detached wear particle. Let $k$ denote the **dimensionless wear coefficient** representing the probability of fragment formation per encounter.

The actual volume of wear is therefore:

$$V = k \cdot A_r \cdot L$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $A_r$ from Step 3

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= k \cdot \left(\frac{F}{H}\right) \cdot L \\
\therefore V &= \frac{k \cdot F \cdot L}{H}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Archard wear equation** in its standard form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the load or sliding distance doubles the wear volume. However, **doubling the hardness halves the wear volume** — this is the theoretical justification for using very hard die materials.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Hall-Petch Relation (Grain-Size Strengthening)

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation
Experiments show that yield strength $\sigma_y$ of a polycrystalline metal increases as the average grain diameter $d$ decreases.

### Step 2 — Pile-Up Dislocation Model
At a grain boundary, slip dislocations generated under applied stress pile up against the boundary. The stress concentration at the tip of the pile-up is:

$$\tau_{tip} = n \cdot \tau$$

Where $n$ is the number of dislocations in the pile-up and $\tau$ is the applied shear stress.

### Step 3 — Number of Dislocations in a Pile-Up
For a pile-up of length $L$ (limited by grain boundary):

$$n = \frac{k_1 \cdot L \cdot \tau}{G \cdot b}$$

Where $G$ is the shear modulus and $b$ is the Burgers vector.

### Step 4 — Relation of $L$ to Grain Size
For polycrystalline metals, the pile-up length is proportional to the grain diameter:

$$L \propto d \implies L = k_2 \cdot d$$

### Step 5 — Yield Criterion
Yielding initiates when $\tau_{tip}$ reaches a critical value $\tau_c$ required to nucleate slip in the neighboring grain:

$$\tau_{tip} = \tau_c$$

### Step 6 — Combining the Relations

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_c &= n \cdot \tau = \left(\frac{k_1 \cdot L \cdot \tau}{G \cdot b}\right) \cdot \tau \\
\tau_c &= \frac{k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot d \cdot \tau^2}{G \cdot b} \\
\tau &= \tau_0 + \frac{k}{\sqrt{d}} \quad \text{(after re-arranging and empirical fit)}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting shear to normal stress (Tensile) by the Schmid factor, the final **Hall-Petch equation** is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_0$ = Lattice friction stress (single-crystal yield strength)
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch strengthening coefficient (material dependent, ~0.7 MPa·m^(1/2) for steels)
- $d$ = Mean grain diameter

### Step 3.2A — Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A die steel has a yield strength of 800 MPa at an average grain size of 25 μm. When subjected to a special thermomechanical treatment, the grain size is reduced to 10 μm. The Hall-Petch coefficient for this steel is $k_y = 17.5$ MPa·mm^(1/2). Calculate the new yield strength. (Take $\sigma_0$ from the original data.)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find $\sigma_0$ from the first data point.

$$\begin{aligned}
800 &= \sigma_0 + \frac{17.5}{\sqrt{25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mm}}} \\
800 &= \sigma_0 + \frac{17.5}{\sqrt{0.025}} \\
800 &= \sigma_0 + \frac{17.5}{0.1581} \\
800 &= \sigma_0 + 110.7 \\
\sigma_0 &= 689.3\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute new yield strength at $d = 10\ \mu m = 0.010$ mm.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= 689.3 + \frac{17.5}{\sqrt{0.010}} \\
\sigma_y &= 689.3 + \frac{17.5}{0.1000} \\
\sigma_y &= 689.3 + 175 \\
\boxed{\sigma_y &= 864.3\ \text{MPa}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Refining the grain from 25 μm to 10 μm increased the yield strength by **64.3 MPa (~8% improvement)** — a direct numerical demonstration of the Hall-Petch principle in die materials.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: Wear Rate Comparison (Archard Numerical)

**Problem:** A blanking punch of HSS (M2) with hardness 62 HRC (≈ 870 HV) is replaced by a carbide punch of hardness 1500 HV. Both operate under the same normal load $F = 5000$ N and sliding distance $L = 10^5$ mm. Assume identical wear coefficient $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$. Compare the wear volumes.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert Vickers hardness to N/mm² (1 HV = 9.81 N/mm²).

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{HSS} &= 870 \times 9.81 = 8534.7\ \text{N/mm}^2 \\
H_{WC} &= 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715\ \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Archard's equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{HSS} &= \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})(5000)(10^5)}{8534.7} \\
V_{HSS} &= \frac{25000}{8534.7} = 2.929\ \text{mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{WC} &= \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})(5000)(10^5)}{14715} \\
V_{WC} &= \frac{25000}{14715} = 1.699\ \text{mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute reduction in wear.

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\ \text{Reduction} &= \frac{V_{HSS} - V_{WC}}{V_{HSS}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2.929 - 1.699}{2.929} \times 100 \\
&\approx 42.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Switching from HSS to tungsten carbide reduces wear volume by approximately **42%** under identical service conditions — illustrating why carbide punches are preferred for high-volume blanking despite higher cost.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Property Comparison Matrix (Tool Steels)

| Property | AISI D2 (Cold-work) | AISI H13 (Hot-work) | AISI P20 (Plastic Mould) | Tungsten Carbide |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|------------------|
| Hardness (HRC) | 58–62 | 48–54 | 30–36 (pre-hard) | 70–82 |
| Toughness | Low–Medium | High | High | Very Low |
| Wear Resistance | Excellent | Good | Fair | Outstanding |
| Heat Resistance (°C) | Up to 200 | Up to 600 | Up to 200 | Up to 900 |
| Machinability (annealed) | 25% | 50% | 65% | < 5% |
| Polishability | Good (with care) | Good | Excellent | Difficult |
| Dimensional Stability | Good (with cryo) | Good | Excellent (pre-hard) | Excellent |
| Typical Cost Index | 3× | 2× | 1.5× | 10× |
| Typical Application | Blanking, drawing | Die casting, forging | Injection moulds | High-speed blanking |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **wear resistance**. State **any two factors** affecting the wear resistance of a die material.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Wear resistance is the ability of a die material to resist progressive loss of surface material due to relative motion between the die and the workpiece (or between mating die components) under load.
- **Two factors (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Hardness** of the die material (higher hardness → higher wear resistance, per Archard's equation).
  2. **Carbide content and type** (e.g., hard chromium/vanadium carbides improve abrasive wear resistance).
  3. **Surface finish and lubrication** at the die-workpiece interface.
  4. **Sliding speed, load, and temperature** during operation.
  5. **Microstructural homogeneity** (fine, uniform grains perform better than segregated structures).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is **dimensional stability**? Why is it particularly important in **plastic injection moulds**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Dimensional stability is the ability of a mould/die to retain its original dimensions and geometrical accuracy throughout its service life, despite exposure to thermal cycling, mechanical loading, and time-dependent phenomena.
- **Importance in plastic moulds (1.5 marks):**
  1. Injection moulds must produce **millions of parts to identical tolerances** (often ±0.01–0.05 mm); any warping or distortion of the cavity directly translates to out-of-tolerance parts.
  2. The mould undergoes **repeated thermal cycling** (melt injection at ~200–300 °C, cooling to ~50 °C), which can cause distortion if residual stresses are present.
  3. **Residual austenite** in improperly heat-treated mould steel transforms during service, causing size change.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks, ESE Module 3, Choice A]**

### Question

**(a)** Explain in detail the following **mechanical physical properties** of die and mould materials, with suitable examples and values:
1. **Hardness** (3 marks)
2. **Toughness** (2 marks)
3. **Wear resistance** (2 marks)

**(b)** A **cold-work die punch** made of AISI D2 steel is used to blank 1.5 mm thick mild steel sheets. The punch operates under a normal blanking force of $F = 8000$ N and slides a distance of $L = 8 \times 10^4$ mm per shift. The punch has a hardness of 60 HRC (≈ 780 HV). AISI D2 is proposed to be replaced with tungsten carbide (WC) of hardness 1500 HV, with the same wear coefficient $k = 4 \times 10^{-5}$. Compare the wear volumes and comment on the **economic feasibility** of the change. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Mechanical Properties Explanation (7 marks)**

**1. Hardness (3 marks):**
Hardness is the resistance of a die material to localized plastic deformation, typically measured by indentation. It is the single most quoted property in die manufacture because it directly correlates with wear resistance and load-bearing capacity. The most common scales are **Rockwell C (HRC)** and **Vickers (HV)**. In the die industry, cold-work punches are typically hardened to 58–62 HRC; hot-work dies operate at 45–55 HRC; plastic moulds are at 30–60 HRC. The hardness of a die steel is controlled by the carbon content (forming martensite), the type and amount of alloy carbides, and the heat treatment route (austenitising temperature, quench medium, tempering temperature).

**2. Toughness (2 marks):**
Toughness is the ability of the material to absorb energy and undergo plastic deformation before fracture. It is critical for dies subjected to impact loading, such as blanking punches and forging dies. Toughness is measured by the **Charpy V-notch impact test** and is reported in Joules. Die materials exhibit a trade-off: increasing hardness (e.g., by raising tempering temperature once vs. secondary) generally reduces toughness. Toughness is improved by **fine grain size**, **low inclusion content**, and **appropriate tempering** (over-tempering reduces hardness; under-tempering retains brittleness).

**3. Wear Resistance (2 marks):**
Wear resistance is the ability to resist material loss from the die surface due to sliding contact with the workpiece. It is the **single biggest determinant of die life**. Two principal mechanisms are **abrasive wear** (plowing by hard particles) and **adhesive wear** (microwelding and fracture). Per **Archard's equation**, $V = kFL/H$, wear volume is inversely proportional to hardness. High-carbon, high-chromium die steels (D2, D3) with 12–18% Cr and abundant M₇C₃ carbides (~1500–1800 HV) have excellent wear resistance, hence their popularity for blanking and drawing dies.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Comparison (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert Vickers to N/mm²** (1 HV = 9.81 N/mm²)

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{D2} &= 780 \times 9.81 = 7651.8\ \text{N/mm}^2 \\
H_{WC} &= 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715\ \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Wear volume for D2 punch** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{D2} &= \frac{k \cdot F \cdot L}{H_{D2}} \\
&= \frac{(4 \times 10^{-5})(8000)(8 \times 10^4)}{7651.8} \\
&= \frac{25600}{7651.8} \\
&= 3.346\ \text{mm}^3 \text{ per shift}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Wear volume for WC punch** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{WC} &= \frac{k \cdot F \cdot L}{H_{WC}} \\
&= \frac{(4 \times 10^{-5})(8000)(8 \times 10^4)}{14715} \\
&= \frac{25600}{14715} \\
&= 1.740\ \text{mm}^3 \text{ per shift}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute reduction in wear** (1 mark)

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= 3.346 - 1.740 = 1.606\ \text{mm}^3 \\
\%\ \text{Reduction} &= \frac{1.606}{3.346} \times 100 = 48.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Economic feasibility comment** (1 mark)
The tungsten carbide punch reduces wear volume by **~48%**, which can **double the service life** of the punch. However, WC punches cost **8–10× more** than D2 punches. The change is economically justified **only if** the production volume is high (e.g., > 1 million strokes per year) and the cost of **tool change downtime** exceeds the price premium. For low-volume production, D2 is more cost-effective.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks, ESE Module 3, Choice B]**

### Question

**(a)** Discuss the following **physical properties** of die and mould materials with reference to their **engineering significance**:
1. **Heat resistance (Red Hardness)** (3 marks)
2. **Thermal conductivity** (2 marks)
3. **Polishability** (2 marks)

**(b)** A die steel exhibits a yield strength of **950 MPa** at an average grain size of **20 μm**. After a refined thermomechanical treatment, the grain size is reduced to **8 μm**. Using the **Hall-Petch equation** with $k_y = 18\ \text{MPa·mm}^{1/2}$, determine:
- (i) The lattice friction stress $\sigma_0$. (2 marks)
- (ii) The new yield strength. (3 marks)
- (iii) Comment on how this improvement benefits die life. (2 marks)

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Discussion of Properties (7 marks)**

**1. Heat Resistance / Red Hardness (3 marks):**
Heat resistance, also called **red hardness** or **hot hardness**, is the ability of a die material to retain its hardness at elevated operating temperatures. This property is critical for **hot-work dies** such as die-casting dies, forging dies, and hot-extrusion dies, where the working surface is exposed to 200°C – 600°C. If the die softens at operating temperature, it undergoes **plastic deformation (die sinking)**, losing dimensional accuracy. Heat resistance is achieved by alloying with strong carbide formers — **Mo, V, W, and Cr** — which form thermally stable, fine secondary carbides during tempering. AISI H13 (5% Cr, 1.5% Mo, 1% V) is the industry-standard hot-work die steel because of its superior red hardness. Tungsten carbide has excellent red hardness up to 900°C.

**2. Thermal Conductivity (2 marks):**
Thermal conductivity is the rate at which heat flows through a material under a temperature gradient. In plastic injection moulds and die-casting dies, rapid heat removal is essential to:
- **Reduce cycle time** (faster cooling → more parts per hour).
- **Prevent soldering** (molten metal sticking to die surface) in die casting.
- **Minimize thermal fatigue** (reduce temperature differential and thermal stress).
Materials with high thermal conductivity (e.g., beryllium copper at ~200 W/m·K) are used as **inserts** in high-cooling-rate zones of die-casting dies. Tool steels typically have $k \approx 25$–$35$ W/m·K.

**3. Polishability (2 marks):**
Polishability is the ability of a die surface to be polished to a mirror-smooth finish (Ra < 0.05 μm for optical applications). It depends on:
- **Microstructural homogeneity** — no segregation bands or carbide clusters.
- **Fine grain size** — coarse grains form pull-out pits.
- **Low inclusion content** — oxides and sulfides form polishing defects.
- **Carbide type and distribution** — primary carbides pull out during polishing.

Stainless mould steels (AISI 420, AISI S136) are preferred for high-polish applications because their **fine, uniform martensitic structure** with dispersed chromium carbides polishes to optical quality. Polishability is critical for plastic moulds producing **lenses, CDs/DVDs, medical disposables, and transparent products**.

---

**Part (b) — Hall-Petch Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert grain size to mm** (1 mark)

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 20\ \mu m = 0.020\ \text{mm} \\
d_2 &= 8\ \mu m = 0.008\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply Hall-Petch equation with original data** (1 mark)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{y1} &= \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d_1}} \\
950 &= \sigma_0 + \frac{18}{\sqrt{0.020}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the second term and solve for $\sigma_0$** (1 mark)

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{18}{\sqrt{0.020}} &= \frac{18}{0.1414} = 127.3\ \text{MPa} \\
\sigma_0 &= 950 - 127.3 = 822.7\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute new yield strength at $d_2 = 0.008$ mm** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{y2} &= 822.7 + \frac{18}{\sqrt{0.008}} \\
\frac{18}{\sqrt{0.008}} &= \frac{18}{0.0894} = 201.3\ \text{MPa} \\
\sigma_{y2} &= 822.7 + 201.3 \\
\boxed{\sigma_{y2} &= 1024.0\ \text{MPa}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute percentage improvement** (0.5 marks)

$$\%\ \text{Improvement} = \frac{1024.0 - 950}{950} \times 100 = 7.79\%$$

**Step 6: Comment on die-life benefit** (1.5 marks)
The grain refinement from 20 μm to 8 μm increases yield strength by **~7.8%** (74 MPa). This translates to:
- **Improved fatigue strength** — finer grains resist crack initiation.
- **Higher toughness** — finer grains arrest crack propagation.
- **Better polishability** — finer grain surface polishes to lower Ra.
- **Longer die life** — overall improvement in cyclic loading endurance.

The Hall-Petch relationship is one of the few mechanisms that improves **both** strength and toughness simultaneously — a major reason why die manufacturers invest in thermomechanical processing and rapid solidification routes.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams on Physical Properties:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (2–3 marks lost):** Students frequently forget to convert HV to N/mm² (1 HV = 9.81 N/mm²) when applying Archard's equation. The result is numerical answers off by an order of magnitude.
> 2. **Confusing Hardness Scales (1–2 marks lost):** Mixing HRC, HB, and HV values in the same calculation. KTU accepts these only with **explicit conversion** and a stated relation.
> 3. **Forgetting Square Root in Hall-Petch (2 marks lost):** Writing $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d$ instead of $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$. This is a frequently tested error.
> 4. **Writing "Hardness = Toughness" (1 mark lost):** These are **inverse** properties, not the same. Always mention the trade-off explicitly.
> 5. **Omitting Examples:** KTU awards marks for **example material** (e.g., D2, H13, P20) in Part B answers. Generic answers without specific die materials lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Not Drawing Flow Diagrams:** When asked to "explain" or "discuss," a labeled flow diagram earns an extra 1–2 marks even if the written content is brief.
> 7. **Skipping the Conclusion in Numerical Problems:** KTU always awards 1 mark for a concluding sentence on "economic feasibility" or "practical implication."

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bulletins — Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- **Hardness** is resistance to localized plastic deformation; measured by HRC, HV, HB. Cold-work dies need 58–62 HRC; hot-work dies 45–55 HRC; plastic moulds 30–60 HRC.
- **Toughness** is energy absorbed before fracture; measured by Charpy V-notch in Joules. **INVERSELY related to hardness.**
- **Wear resistance** is governed by **Archard's equation**: $V = kFL/H$. Higher hardness → lower wear. Carbide-rich steels (D2, D3) have superior wear resistance.
- **Dimensional stability** = retention of geometry over time. Achieved by stress relief, multiple tempering, cryogenic treatment, and pre-hardened steels.
- **Heat resistance (Red Hardness)** = retention of hardness at high temperature. Critical for H13, H11 hot-work dies. Achieved by **Mo, V, W, Cr** alloying.
- **Thermal conductivity** = rate of heat flow. High conductivity shortens cycle time and prevents soldering. BeCu inserts used in die casting.
- **Machinability** = ease of cutting. In **inverse** relation to wear resistance. D2 = 25%, H13 = 50%, P20 = 65% (vs. 1212 steel = 100%).
- **Polishability** = ability to achieve mirror finish (Ra < 0.05 μm). Needs **fine grains, low inclusions, uniform carbides**. Use **420, S136** stainless mould steels.
- **Grain structure** follows **Hall-Petch**: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$. **Finer grain = higher strength AND higher toughness** (rare exception to trade-off).
- **Trade-off Principle:** Almost all physical properties are in mutual conflict. Material selection is an **optimization**, not maximization.
- **Hall-Petch is KTU-favorite** numerical: always convert $d$ from μm to mm before applying.
- **Archard is KTU-favorite** numerical: always convert HV (kgf/mm²) to N/mm² (multiply by 9.81).
- **Tungsten carbide** = highest hardness + highest wear resistance + highest heat resistance + lowest toughness + lowest machinability. Used for high-volume blanking and drawing punches.
- **P20** = pre-hardened plastic mould steel (30–36 HRC); used as-supplied; no post-machining heat treatment required → excellent dimensional stability.
- **D2** = cold-work die steel (1.5% C, 12% Cr, 1% Mo); 58–62 HRC after heat treatment; wear-resistant but not tough; **NOT** suitable for shock loading.
- **H13** = hot-work die steel (0.4% C, 5% Cr, 1.5% Mo, 1% V); 48–54 HRC; balanced hardness-toughness-red hardness; **industry standard for die casting and forging**.
- **Maraging steels (e.g., 18Ni300)** = ultra-low carbon, age-hardened; **near-zero distortion** during heat treatment; used for high-precision dies and moulds.
- **AISI 420 / DIN 1.2083 / Bohler S136** = stainless mould steels; corrosion-resistant + mirror-polishable; used for PVC, transparent, and medical plastic moulds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aluminium Mould Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aluminium Mould Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aluminium Mould Making** is the specialised branch of tool engineering that deals with the design, material selection, manufacturing, heat treatment, and surface enhancement of moulds and dies fabricated predominantly from high-strength aluminium (Al) alloys (e.g., Al-7075, Al-2024, Al-6061) rather than traditional tool steels. It is widely adopted in the plastics injection moulding, blow moulding, thermoforming, and die-casting industries where short production runs, rapid lead times, and high thermal conductivity are critical.

In the KTU 2024 framework, aluminium mould making is positioned as an **economical, time-efficient alternative** to hardened steel moulds, particularly when the production batch size is small-to-medium (typically **< 100,000 parts**), and the emphasis is on **cycle-time reduction** through superior heat dissipation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Ice-Cube Tray vs. Metal Baking Pan" Concept**
> Imagine you have to freeze 1,000 small ice cubes. A traditional **steel mould** behaves like a thick, dense metal baking pan — it absorbs heat slowly, holds it for a long time, and is incredibly tough but takes ages to cool down. An **aluminium mould**, on the other hand, behaves like a high-quality **aluminium ice-cube tray** sitting in your freezer. It pulls heat *out* of the plastic almost five times faster, so each ice cube (or plastic part) freezes (or solidifies) in much less time.
> Of course, you wouldn't use an aluminium tray to make 10 million ice cubes — it would dent and warp. But for a few thousand pieces, it is the perfect compromise between **speed, cost, and acceptable durability**. This is exactly the philosophy of aluminium mould making.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used universally in aluminium mould design:

- **Thermal Conductivity of Al-7075:** ≈ **130 W/m·K** (vs. tool steel ≈ 25 W/m·K)
- **Density of Al-7075:** ≈ **2.81 g/cm³** (vs. tool steel ≈ 7.85 g/cm³) — about **one-third** the weight of steel
- **Typical Hardness (after T6 treatment):** **150–180 HB**
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE):** ≈ **23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** (nearly double that of steel)
- **Standard Production Threshold:** **< 1 × 10⁵ parts** (aluminium) vs. **> 1 × 10⁶ parts** (steel)

### 1.4 Why Aluminium? — The Three Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of aluminium over steel is governed by **three engineering pillars**:
> 1. **Thermal Efficiency (Pillar 1):** High thermal conductivity → faster cooling → shorter cycle time.
> 2. **Machinability (Pillar 2):** Cutting speeds up to **5–10× higher** than tool steel → low machining cost.
> 3. **Weight & Handling (Pillar 3):** Lower density → easier handling, faster mould changeover, reduced press tonnage requirements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Aluminium Alloys Used for Mould Making

Not all aluminium alloys are suitable for moulds. The KTU syllabus emphasises the following grades:

| Alloy Designation | Key Alloying Elements | Hardness (T6) | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Al-7075** | Zn, Mg, Cu | 150–175 HB | Cavity inserts, high-stress moulds |
| **Al-2024** | Cu, Mg | 120–140 HB | Core pins, ejector pins, structural mould parts |
| **Al-6061** | Mg, Si | 95–110 HB | Mould base, frame components |
| **Al-5083** | Mg, Mn | 75–90 HB | Large mould frames, blow-mould bodies |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How an Aluminium Mould Is Selected

1. **Step 1 — Identify Production Volume:** If the part requirement is **< 10⁵ parts**, aluminium becomes the prime candidate.
2. **Step 2 — Identify the Mould Component:** Decide whether it is the *cavity*, *core*, *frame*, or *ejector pin* — each demands a different alloy.
3. **Step 3 — Evaluate Thermal Demand:** For thin-walled plastics, high conductivity is essential → prefer **Al-7075**.
4. **Step 4 — Check Wear Resistance:** Aluminium cannot match steel in abrasive conditions → avoid use with glass-filled or carbon-filled polymers.
5. **Step 5 — Apply Surface Treatment:** Decide on hard anodising, nitriding, or PVD coating to extend life.
6. **Step 6 — Validate Through Prototyping:** Pilot run with **< 1,000 parts** to verify dimensional stability.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises the **key engineering equations and design constants** for aluminium mould design.

| Concept | Formula / Value | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat removal rate (Fourier) | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{x}$ | W | $k$ = thermal conductivity |
| Cycle-time reduction ratio | $\dfrac{t_{steel}}{t_{al}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{al}}{k_{steel}}}$ | — | Compare relative cooling times |
| Thermal expansion | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | m | $\alpha_{Al} \approx 23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ |
| Density ratio | $\dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{steel}} = 0.36$ | — | Weight-saving factor |
| Hardness ratio (HB) | $\dfrac{HB_{Al}}{HB_{steel}} \approx 0.25$ | — | Aluminium is ~4× softer |
| Machinability rating | $R_m = \dfrac{V_{Al}}{V_{steel}} = 5 \text{ to } 10$ | — | Aluminium cuts 5–10× faster |
| Cooling channel distance | $d \approx 3 \cdot s$ | mm | $s$ = wall thickness of plastic part |
| Safe moulding temperature | $T_{inj} \leq 0.4 \cdot T_{melt,Al}$ | °C | Prevent mould softening |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Industries

- **Automotive Industry:** Rapid prototyping of dashboard and bumper moulds in **Al-7075** reduces lead time from 12 weeks (steel) to **3 weeks**.
- **Medical Device Industry:** Thermoforming moulds for disposable trays in **Al-5083** allow fine surface replication.
- **Packaging Industry:** Injection moulds for thin-wall containers in **Al-7075 T6** achieve cycle times of **< 4 seconds**.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Phone covers, housings, and small bezels in **Al-2024** benefit from high surface polish and rapid heat extraction.

> [!NOTE]
> The **real-world engineering trade-off** is best captured by the rule of thumb:
> *"Use steel for volume, use aluminium for velocity, and use beryllium-copper only for ultra-localised cooling in steel moulds."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cycle-Time Reduction Due to Aluminium's Higher Thermal Conductivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the relationship between cycle times of steel and aluminium moulds based on thermal conductivity.

**Step 1 — Start with the 1-D transient heat-conduction equation (Fourier's Law):**

$$
Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}
$$

**Step 2 — For a mould wall, the cooling time is proportional to the square of wall thickness and inversely proportional to thermal diffusivity:**

$$
t_{cool} \propto \dfrac{x^{2}}{\alpha}
$$

where thermal diffusivity is:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\alpha$ into the cooling-time expression:**

$$
t_{cool} \propto \dfrac{x^{2} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}{k}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the ratio of cycle times for steel and aluminium moulds (same thickness $x$):**

$$
\dfrac{t_{steel}}{t_{al}} = \dfrac{\rho_{steel} \cdot c_{p,steel} / k_{steel}}{\rho_{al} \cdot c_{p,al} / k_{al}}
$$

**Step 5 — Insert typical values:**

$$
\rho_{steel} = 7850 \; kg/m^{3}, \quad c_{p,steel} = 490 \; J/kg\cdot K, \quad k_{steel} = 25 \; W/m\cdot K
$$

$$
\rho_{al} = 2810 \; kg/m^{3}, \quad c_{p,al} = 960 \; J/kg\cdot K, \quad k_{al} = 130 \; W/m\cdot K
$$

**Step 6 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\rho_{steel} \cdot c_{p,steel} / k_{steel} = \dfrac{7850 \times 490}{25} = 153{,}860 \; s/m^{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute denominator:**

$$
\rho_{al} \cdot c_{p,al} / k_{al} = \dfrac{2810 \times 960}{130} = 20{,}751 \; s/m^{2}
$$

**Step 8 — Form the ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{t_{steel}}{t_{al}} = \dfrac{153{,}860}{20{,}751} \approx 7.4
$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the result:**

An aluminium mould cools the same plastic part in roughly **1/7th the time** of a steel mould, yielding substantial productivity gains — a key KTU high-yield concept.

### 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Expansion Compensation for an Aluminium Cavity

**Step 1 — Express linear expansion:**

$$
\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — For an aluminium cavity of original length $L_0 = 200 \; mm$ operating between $T_{room} = 25°C$ and $T_{mould} = 150°C$:**

$$
\Delta T = 150 - 25 = 125°C
$$

**Step 3 — Insert $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$:**

$$
\Delta L = 200 \times (23 \times 10^{-6}) \times 125
$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$
\Delta L = 0.575 \; mm
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with a steel mould (same dimensions, $\alpha_{steel} = 11 \times 10^{-6} /°C$):**

$$
\Delta L_{steel} = 200 \times (11 \times 10^{-6}) \times 125 = 0.275 \; mm
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret:**

The aluminium mould expands **more than twice as much** as the steel mould. This is the **fundamental reason** why aluminium moulds require:
- **Tighter temperature-control systems**
- **Generous mould-closing clearances**
- **Pre-heated start-up procedures**

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Aluminium vs. Steel Mould

| Selection Parameter | Aluminium Mould | Steel Mould (P20/H13) |
|---|---|---|
| Production volume | **< 10⁵ parts** | **> 10⁵ parts** |
| Cycle time | **Low** (1/5 to 1/7 of steel) | Higher |
| Lead time (machining) | **2–4 weeks** | 6–12 weeks |
| Cost of raw material | Lower (per kg) | Higher |
| Machining cost | **~40% lower** | High |
| Hardness | **150–180 HB** | 280–620 HB |
| Wear resistance | Limited | Excellent |
| Weight of mould | **~36% of steel** | Full |
| Polishing quality | Excellent (mirror finish possible) | Excellent |
| Repairability (welding) | Easy | Difficult |
| Suitable for abrasive plastics | No (needs coating) | Yes |
| Thermal expansion | **2× steel** | Lower |
| Recyclability | High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Aluminium Mould Manufacturing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Part Design & Volume Estimation] --> B[Step 2: Alloy Selection Al-7075 / 2024 / 6061 / 5083]
    B --> C[Step 3: Rough Machining High Speed CNC Milling]
    C --> D[Step 4: Stress Relieving Annealing at 345C]
    D --> E[Step 5: Finish Machining & EDM if Required]
    E --> F[Step 6: Heat Treatment T6 Solution plus Age Hardening]
    F --> G[Step 7: Surface Treatment Hard Anodising or Nitriding]
    G --> H[Step 8: Polishing and Texture Generation]
    H --> I[Step 9: Assembly of Cavity Core and Cooling Lines]
    I --> J[Step 10: Trial Shot and Dimensional Validation]
    J --> K{Part Within Tolerance?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Step 11: Production Approval]
    K -- No --> M[Step 12: Correction and Re-Validation Loop]
    M --> F
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Heat & Material Flow in an Aluminium Mould

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Molten Plastic Injected] --> B[Aluminium Cavity Wall]
    B --> C[Conductive Heat Transfer k equals 130 W per mK]
    C --> D[Cooling Channels Water or Oil]
    D --> E[Chiller Unit]
    E --> F[Cooled Plastic Ejection]
    B --> G[Surface Coating Hard Anodising 50 microns]
    G --> H[Enhanced Wear Resistance]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Mould Material Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Production Volume Decision] --> Low{Volume less than 1 lakh parts}
    Start --> High{Volume greater than 1 lakh parts}
    Low --> A[Choose Aluminium Mould]
    High --> B{Plastic contains glass or carbon fibre}
    A --> C{Plastic contains abrasive filler}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply PVD or Hard Anodising]
    C -- No --> E[Direct Use of Al 7075 T6]
    B -- Yes --> F[Choose Hardened Steel H13 or P20]
    B -- No --> G[Choose P20 Steel or Aluminium with Coating]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages of using aluminium alloys for mould making over conventional tool steels.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Award 1 mark per correct point):**

1. **Higher thermal conductivity (≈ 130 W/m·K)** leads to faster cooling and a shorter cycle time.
2. **Lower density (≈ 2.81 g/cm³)** reduces mould weight by approximately **64%** compared to steel.
3. **Superior machinability** permits cutting speeds **5–10× higher** than tool steel, reducing machining time and cost.
4. *(Bonus point)* Easier weldability and repairability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Name the most commonly used aluminium alloy for plastic injection mould cavities. Why is it preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- The most commonly used alloy is **Al-7075 in the T6 condition**.
- It is preferred because of its:
  1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** (yield strength ≈ 503 MPa).
  2. **Good thermal conductivity (≈ 130 W/m·K)** for rapid heat extraction.
  3. **Excellent machinability** and ability to take a mirror polish.
  4. *(Bonus)* Good response to hard anodising for surface hardening.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) Module 3 generally offers an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. Both options are presented below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Aluminium Mould Material Selection**

**(a)** *Discuss in detail the properties required in an aluminium alloy for use as an injection-mould cavity. Compare the properties of Al-7075 and Al-6061 for this application.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the various heat-treatment processes applied to aluminium moulds. Why is the T6 condition preferred?* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Properties Required in an Aluminium Alloy for Mould Cavity:**

The cavity is the most critical mould component — it determines the part's surface, geometry, and dimensional accuracy. The aluminium alloy chosen for the cavity must possess:

1. **High Strength and Hardness** — To resist the injection pressure (typically 70–140 MPa) and ejection forces. Al-7075-T6 offers a yield strength of ~503 MPa and hardness of 150–175 HB.
2. **High Thermal Conductivity** — Essential for fast heat removal. Al-7075 (130 W/m·K) outperforms Al-6061 (167 W/m·K nominally, but lower strength).
3. **Good Dimensional Stability** — Resistance to warping during heat treatment.
4. **Excellent Machinability** — To allow complex cavity geometry at low cost.
5. **Polishability** — To obtain SPI-A1 to SPI-A3 mirror finishes.
6. **Weldability and Repairability** — To allow in-house repair of worn edges.

**Comparison Table — Al-7075 vs. Al-6061 (Award 1 mark for each correct row):**

| Property | Al-7075 T6 | Al-6061 T6 |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength | 503 MPa (superior) | 275 MPa |
| Hardness | 150–175 HB | 95–110 HB |
| Thermal Conductivity | 130 W/m·K | 167 W/m·K |
| Machinability Rating | Excellent (highest) | Good |
| Weldability | Moderate | Excellent |
| Typical Use | Cavities, cores | Mould frames, bases |

**Verdict:** Al-7075 is preferred for cavities (high strength), Al-6061 is preferred for mould bases (good weldability and cost).

---

**(b) Heat-Treatment Processes for Aluminium Moulds:**

The standard heat-treatment sequence is:

1. **Solution Treatment (≈ 465–480 °C for Al-7075):** The alloy is heated above the solvus temperature to dissolve all soluble precipitates into a single-phase solid solution, then rapidly quenched in water.
2. **Quenching:** Rapid cooling (water/polymer) suppresses the formation of coarse precipitates, retaining a supersaturated solid solution at room temperature.
3. **Age Hardening (Artificial Ageing at 120–175 °C for 8–24 hours):** Controlled precipitation of fine, uniformly dispersed intermetallic particles (e.g., MgZn₂ in Al-7075) that hinder dislocation motion and increase hardness.

**Why T6 is Preferred:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T6 = Solution Treatment + Quench + Artificial Ageing.**
> It provides the **optimum combination of strength, hardness, dimensional stability, and machinability** for mould service. The peak hardness and yield strength are reached, while residual stresses are minimised. This combination is essential because moulds must resist:
> - Clamping forces of the press,
> - Repeated thermal cycling (25 °C ↔ 200 °C),
> - Wear from plastic flow and ejection.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Manufacturing & Surface Treatment**

**(a)** *Describe the step-by-step procedure for manufacturing an aluminium injection mould. List the various machining operations involved.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the surface-treatment methods used to enhance the wear resistance of aluminium moulds. Compare hard anodising with nitriding.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Aluminium Mould Manufacturing Procedure:**

1. **Design & CAD Modelling:** Cavity, core, parting line, gating, cooling channels, and ejection system designed in 3D CAD.
2. **Material Procurement & Incoming Inspection:** Aluminium alloy (typically Al-7075) supplied in T651 or T6 temper.
3. **Rough Machining:** High-speed CNC milling at spindle speeds up to **20,000 rpm**; feed rates up to **10 m/min**.
4. **Stress Relieving:** Heat at 345 °C for 2 hours to relieve machining-induced stresses.
5. **Finish Machining:** Cavity and core machined to ±0.01 mm tolerance using ball-end mills and high-speed CNC.
6. **EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining):** For sharp internal corners, deep ribs, or micro-features where the cutter cannot reach.
7. **Heat Treatment (T6):** Solution treat, quench, age-harden.
8. **Surface Treatment:** Hard anodising or nitriding applied for wear resistance.
9. **Polishing & Texturing:** Hand polishing for SPI-A1 finish, or chemical etching for grain pattern.
10. **Assembly & Fitting:** Cavity, core, sprue bush, ejector pins, cooling-line fittings assembled.
11. **Trial & Validation:** First-article inspection (FAI) using CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine).

**Key Machining Operations (1 mark for naming ≥ 4 correctly):**
- CNC milling (roughing & finishing)
- CNC turning
- EDM (wire & sinker)
- Drilling & tapping
- Grinding (finishing)
- Polishing & buffing

---

**(b) Surface Treatment Methods for Aluminium Moulds:**

Aluminium in T6 condition (≤ 180 HB) cannot withstand abrasive plastics. Hence, **surface hardening** is mandatory.

**1. Hard Anodising (Type III):**
- Electrolytic process using **sulphuric acid** at low temperature (−5 to 5 °C).
- Produces an **aluminium-oxide (Al₂O₃) layer** of **25–150 µm**.
- Surface hardness reaches **400–600 HV** (≈ 40–60 HRC equivalent).
- Excellent corrosion resistance, but the coating is **brittle** and adds dimensional growth (≈ 50% of coating thickness per side).

**2. Plasma Nitriding (Ion Nitriding):**
- Aluminium surfaces are nitrided in a nitrogen-rich plasma at **400–500 °C**.
- Forms a thin **AlN (aluminium nitride)** layer of **10–30 µm**.
- Surface hardness ≈ **800–1200 HV** — the hardest of all aluminium surface treatments.
- Minimal dimensional change, but longer cycle time and higher cost.

**Comparison Table — Hard Anodising vs. Nitriding (Award 1 mark per correct row):**

| Parameter | Hard Anodising | Plasma Nitriding |
|---|---|---|
| Coating Type | Al₂O₃ | AlN |
| Thickness | 25–150 µm | 10–30 µm |
| Hardness | 400–600 HV | 800–1200 HV |
| Process Temp. | −5 to 5 °C | 400–500 °C |
| Dimensional Change | Significant | Minimal |
| Brittleness | High | Low |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Best For | General moulds | High-wear inserts |

**3. PVD Coatings (Physical Vapour Deposition):**
- Deposits a thin film of **TiN, CrN, or DLC** (5–10 µm) on the aluminium surface.
- Hardness up to **2000–3000 HV** (DLC).
- Best suited for **core pins, ejector pins, and slides**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> Many students lose marks because they:
> 1. **Confuse the units of hardness.** Aluminium moulds are rated in **HB (Brinell)** for bulk and **HV (Vickers)** for coatings — never HRC.
> 2. **Forget the T6 sequence order.** The correct order is *Solution → Quench → Age*. Writing *Quench → Solution* is treated as wrong and costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mix up the T6 and T651 tempers.** T6 has been solution-treated + aged. T651 additionally has **stress-relieved by stretching** to improve dimensional stability — a frequent KTU trick question.
> 4. **Omit the dimensional growth** caused by hard anodising. This growth (≈ 50% of coating thickness per side) must be **subtracted from the cavity dimension** during EDM/machining design. Omission costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Fail to link cycle-time reduction to thermal conductivity.** Always state that the cycle time reduces by a factor of ≈ $\sqrt{k_{al}/k_{steel}}$ for a fair comparison — not just "aluminium cools faster".

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Aluminium Mould Making**

- **Definition:** Moulds/dies fabricated from high-strength Al alloys (primarily **Al-7075 T6**) instead of tool steel.
- **Three Pillars of Selection:** Thermal efficiency, machinability, low weight.
- **Most Common Alloy:** Al-7075 in **T6 condition** (yield strength ≈ 503 MPa, hardness ≈ 150–175 HB).
- **Alloy Comparison:** Al-7075 = high strength; Al-6061 = high weldability; Al-2024 = fatigue resistance; Al-5083 = large frames.
- **Production Volume Limit:** Aluminium is economical up to **~ 1 × 10⁵ parts**.
- **Thermal Conductivity:** $k_{Al} \approx 130 \; W/m\cdot K$ vs. $k_{steel} \approx 25 \; W/m\cdot K$ (≈ 5× faster heat removal).
- **Cycle-Time Reduction Ratio:** $t_{steel} / t_{al} \approx 7$ for typical settings.
- **Density:** $\rho_{Al} \approx 2.81 \; g/cm^{3}$ (≈ 36% of steel).
- **CTE:** $\alpha_{Al} \approx 23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ — **twice that of steel** → requires generous clearances and tight temperature control.
- **Machinability:** Aluminium cuts **5–10× faster** than tool steel.
- **Heat-Treatment Sequence (T6):** **Solution Treat → Quench → Artificial Age** — Peak strength obtained.
- **Stress Relieving:** Performed at ≈ 345 °C for 2 h after rough machining to prevent distortion.
- **Surface Treatments:**
  - **Hard Anodising (Type III):** Al₂O₃ layer, 25–150 µm, 400–600 HV.
  - **Plasma Nitriding:** AlN layer, 10–30 µm, 800–1200 HV.
  - **PVD (TiN / DLC):** 5–10 µm, up to 3000 HV.
- **Unsuitable For:** Glass-filled, carbon-filled, or other highly abrasive plastics without coating.
- **Cooling-Channel Design Rule:** $d \approx 3 \cdot s$, where $s$ = wall thickness of plastic part.
- **Moulding Temperature Limit:** $T_{inj} \leq 0.4 \cdot T_{melt,Al}$ to prevent mould softening.
- **Engineering Trade-off:** *"Use steel for volume, use aluminium for velocity."*
- **Top Industries:** Automotive (rapid prototyping), medical (thermoforming), packaging (thin-wall containers), consumer electronics.
- **Recycling:** Aluminium moulds are fully recyclable, supporting sustainable manufacturing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electroforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electroforming** is an electrochemical additive manufacturing process in which metal ions from an electrolytic solution are deposited, atom-by-atom, onto a conductive pre-shaped pattern (called a **mandrel** or **matrix**), thereby fabricating a rigid, free-standing metallic replica or shell that can be subsequently separated from the mandrel. The deposition thickness is controlled precisely using **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**, and the process is widely employed in mould and die manufacture for producing complex cavities, EDM electrodes, micro-features, and seamless nickel mould inserts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Electroforming is treated as a **non-traditional tooling process** in mould manufacture. The student MUST clearly distinguish Electroforming from **Electroplating** (thickness only a few microns, decorative/protective) — the key differentiator is **thickness** (typically 0.05 mm to several mm) and the fact that the deposited layer becomes the **functional component itself**, not a coating.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **building a chocolate shell around a balloon**:
- The balloon = the **mandrel** (your desired cavity shape).
- The melted chocolate = the **electrolyte** carrying dissolved metal "flavours".
- A spoon pouring chocolate atom-by-atom = the **electric current** attracting metal ions.
- After the chocolate hardens, you **pop and remove the balloon** → you are left with a perfect hollow chocolate mould.

Electroforming works on exactly the same principle, except:
- The "balloon" (mandrel) is a precisely machined or master-patterned conductive object.
- The "chocolate" is **dissolved metal salt** (e.g., NiSO₄ for nickel).
- The "hardening" is **electrochemical reduction** of metal ions at the cathode surface.

The result is a **dimensionally accurate, mechanically strong metallic shell** that mirrors every micro-feature of the mandrel, even down to surface roughness values in the **sub-micron range**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Favourites)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mandrel / Matrix** | The master pattern (positive or negative) on which metal is deposited |
| **Cathode** | The mandrel itself (negatively charged; metal deposits here) |
| **Anode** | The source metal (e.g., nickel pellets) that dissolves to replenish ions |
| **Electrolyte** | Aqueous solution of metal salts (e.g., NiSO₄·6H₂O + NiCl₂ + H₃BO₃) |
| **Current Density** | Current per unit area of cathode (A/dm²), typical 2–8 A/dm² for nickel |
| **Deposition Rate** | Linear thickness gained per hour (typically 25–50 µm/hr for Ni) |
| **Throw Power** | Ability of electrolyte to deposit uniformly in recesses and cavities |
| **Passivation** | Loss of anode activity due to oxide layer formation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorise for KTU:**
> - **Faraday's Constant F = 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)**
> - **Atomic weight of Nickel (Ni) = 58.69 g/mol**
> - **Valency of Ni²⁺ = 2**
> - **Density of Nickel = 8.908 g/cm³**
> - **1 dm² = 100 cm² = 0.01 m²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Principle — The Electrochemical Reaction

When DC current is passed through the electrolytic cell, the following reactions occur:

**At the Cathode (Mandrel):**
$$\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Ni (solid deposit)}$$

**At the Anode (Nickel source):**
$$\text{Ni (solid)} \longrightarrow \text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

The net result is transfer of nickel from anode to cathode. The **rate** of deposition is governed by **Faraday's Two Laws**.

## 2.2 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis

### Faraday's First Law (Mass–Charge Relationship)
> *"The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte."*

$$m \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- **m** = mass of metal deposited (grams)
- **I** = current in amperes (A)
- **t** = time in seconds (s)
- **A** = atomic weight of the metal (g/mol)
- **n** = valency (number of electrons per ion)
- **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**

### Faraday's Second Law (Equivalent Weight Relationship)
> *"When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights."*

$$\frac{m_{1}}{E_{1}} \;=\; \frac{m_{2}}{E_{2}}$$

Where E = Equivalent weight = A / n.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield formulas for Electroforming — table cell separator handled with `\vert` to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal deposited | g |
| 2 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Total charge passed | C (A·s) |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{s}}$ | Current density (A per dm²) | A/dm² |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A_{s}}$ | Average thickness deposited | cm or mm |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \tau \cdot n \cdot F}{J \cdot A_{s} \cdot A}$ | Time required for target thickness | seconds |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Cathode Current Efficiency (CCE) | % |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{A}{n}$ | Equivalent weight | g/eq |
| 8 | $v_{dep} = \dfrac{J \cdot A \cdot 60}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot 100}$ | Deposition rate | mm/min or µm/hr |

**Where:**
- $A_{s}$ = surface area being plated (typically in dm²)
- $\rho$ = density of deposit (g/cm³)
- $\tau$ (tau) = thickness
- $\eta$ (eta) = current efficiency (typical 95–98% for nickel sulfamate)
- $v_{dep}$ = linear deposition velocity

## 2.4 Process Variables Affecting Electroforming Quality

**1. Current Density (J)**
- Low J → smooth, fine-grained deposit but slow.
- High J → rough, burnt, dendritic (tree-like) deposit.
- **KTU rule of thumb:** For Ni-sulfamate baths, optimal J = **2–8 A/dm²**.

**2. Electrolyte Temperature**
- Higher T → better ion mobility, finer grain, lower stress.
- Typical Ni-sulfamate bath: **40 °C – 60 °C**.

**3. pH of Electrolyte**
- Acidic (pH 3.5–4.5) for most nickel baths.
- Alkalinity shifts deposit colour and causes pitting.

**4. Agitation**
- Air or mechanical agitation improves **throw power** into deep recesses.
- Reduces hydrogen bubble entrapment (causing pits).

**5. Mandrel Material**
- Stainless steel, aluminium, brass — must be **chemically separable** from the deposit.
- For complex undercuts → use **low-melting alloy mandrels** that are later melted out.

**6. Additives**
- **Wetting agents** → reduce pitting.
- **Stress reducers** (e.g., saccharin) → minimise internal stress in Ni deposit.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Injection Moulding:** Seamless nickel shells for blow moulds and rotational moulds.
- **EDM Electrodes:** Precise copper or graphite electrodes for die-sinking.
- **Micro-Features:** LIGA-style mould inserts for microfluidic devices.
- **Optical Moulds:** Replicating optical-grade surface finishes on Fresnel lenses.
- **Aerospace:** Lightweight honeycomb mould structures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Time Required for a Given Thickness

**Given:**
- A nickel mandrel of surface area $A_{s}$ is to be electroformed.
- Desired average thickness = $\tau$.
- Operating current density = $J$ (in A/dm²).

**Step 1 — Compute the mass needed.**
The deposited metal forms a thin shell of volume $V = A_{s,cm} \cdot \tau_{cm}$ over the mandrel surface.

$$m \;=\; V \cdot \rho \;=\; A_{s,cm} \cdot \tau_{cm} \cdot \rho$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's First Law.**

$$m \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Express current I in terms of current density.**
Since $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{s,dm^2}}$, we have $I = J \cdot A_{s,dm^2}$.

**Step 4 — Convert area units carefully.**
Surface area $A_{s,dm^2} = \dfrac{A_{s,cm^2}}{100}$ (because 1 dm² = 100 cm²).

So:
$$m \;=\; \frac{(J \cdot A_{s,dm^2}) \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F} \;=\; \frac{J \cdot A_{s,dm^2} \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 5 — Equate mass expressions (Step 1 and Step 4).**

$$A_{s,cm} \cdot \tau_{cm} \cdot \rho \;=\; \frac{J \cdot A_{s,dm^2} \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel area from both sides.**

$$\tau_{cm} \cdot \rho \;=\; \frac{J \cdot t \cdot A}{100 \cdot n \cdot F}$$

(Since $A_{s,dm^2} = A_{s,cm^2}/100$, the surface area cancels.)

**Step 7 — Solve for time t.**

$$\boxed{\,t \;=\; \frac{\rho \cdot \tau \cdot n \cdot F \cdot 100}{J \cdot A}\,}$$

**This is the KTU exam-ready master formula for time-of-deposition.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam Style — Dec 2023 Model]**
> A nickel electroforming bath is used to deposit a uniform nickel shell of thickness **2 mm** on a mandrel of total surface area **5 dm²**. The bath operates at a current density of **4 A/dm²**. Given:
> - Atomic weight of Ni, A = **58.69 g/mol**
> - Valency, n = **2**
> - Density of Ni, ρ = **8.908 g/cm³**
> - Faraday's constant, F = **96,485 C/mol**
> - Current efficiency, η = **95%**
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Theoretical time required for deposition.
> (b) Actual time required accounting for current efficiency.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**(a) Theoretical Time:**

Given:
- $\tau = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ cm}$
- $J = 4 \text{ A/dm}^2$
- $A_{s} = 5 \text{ dm}^2$
- $A = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$
- $n = 2$
- $\rho = 8.908 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$

**Step A1:** Total current.

$$I \;=\; J \cdot A_{s} \;=\; 4 \cdot 5 \;=\; 20 \text{ A}$$

**Step A2:** Total mass of nickel to be deposited.

$$m \;=\; A_{s,cm} \cdot \tau_{cm} \cdot \rho$$

Note: $A_{s,cm} = 5 \text{ dm}^2 \times 100 = 500 \text{ cm}^2$.

$$m \;=\; 500 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 8.908 \;=\; 890.8 \text{ g}$$

**Step A3:** Apply Faraday's First Law rearranged for time.

$$t \;=\; \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot A}$$

$$t \;=\; \frac{890.8 \cdot 2 \cdot 96{,}485}{20 \cdot 58.69}$$

$$t \;=\; \frac{171{,}909{,}116}{1173.8}$$

$$t \;=\; 146{,}471 \text{ seconds} \;\approx\; 40.69 \text{ hours}$$

**(b) Actual Time with Current Efficiency η = 95%:**

Since efficiency < 100%, **more time** is required.

$$t_{actual} \;=\; \frac{t_{theoretical}}{\eta}$$

$$t_{actual} \;=\; \frac{40.69}{0.95}$$

$$t_{actual} \;\approx\; 42.83 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Insight:** KTU evaluators often award 2 bonus marks to students who explicitly state that **η reduces the effective charge available for metal deposition**, and therefore actual time = theoretical time / η.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Set-Ups

| Problem Type | What is Given | What to Find | Master Formula to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass deposition | I, t, electrolyte | Mass m | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$ |
| Time for thickness | τ, ρ, J, A_s | Time t | $t = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \tau \cdot n \cdot F \cdot 100}{J \cdot A}$ |
| Current efficiency | m_actual, m_theoretical | η | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{act}}{m_{theo}} \times 100$ |
| Deposition rate | J, ρ, A | Rate (mm/hr) | $v = \dfrac{J \cdot A \cdot 3.6}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ |
| Thickness from charge | Q = I·t, A_s | Thickness τ | $\tau = \dfrac{Q \cdot A \cdot \eta}{\rho \cdot n \cdot F \cdot A_s}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Electroforming Cell — Process Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[DC Power Supply Positive Terminal] --> A2[Anode Nickel Pellets Bagged in Ti Basket]
    A2 --> A3[Electrolyte Ni Sulfamate Bath 40 to 60 deg C]
    A3 --> A4[Agitation System Air or Mechanical]
    A3 --> A5[Mandrel Cathode Mounted on Cathode Rod]
    A5 --> A6[DC Power Supply Negative Terminal]
    A5 --> A7[Nickel Ions Migrate to Mandrel]
    A7 --> A8[Ni2 plus Gains 2 Electrons and Deposits as Solid Ni]
    A8 --> A9[Deposit Grows to Required Thickness Tau]
    A9 --> A10[Mandrel Separated Chemically or Mechanically]
    A10 --> A11[Free Standing Nickel Shell Ready as Mould Insert]

    B1[Bath Parameters] --> B2[pH 3.8 to 4.2]
```
