```mermaid
    B1 --> B3[Current Density 2 to 8 A per dm2]
    B1 --> B4[Additives Wetting Agent Stress Reducer]

    A2 --> C1[Anode Dissolves Ni minus minus Ni2 plus plus 2e]
    C1 --> A3
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Electroforming Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Mandrel Fabrication] --> S2[Stage 2 Surface Cleaning and Activation]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Rinsing and Strike Coat]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Main Electroforming Bath]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Thickness Monitoring]
    S5 --> S6{Thickness Reached Target?}
    S6 -- No --> S4
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Stage 6 Mandrel Removal]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 7 Backing and Mounting]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 8 Final Mould Inspection]

    Q1[Quality Checks] --> Q2[Surface Roughness Ra]
    Q1 --> Q3[Hardness HV]
    Q1 --> Q4[Dimensional Accuracy micron]
    Q1 --> Q5[Internal Stress MPa]

    S9 --> Q1
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Electroforming vs Electroplating

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0{Process Selection Based on Application}
    R0 --> R1[Need Functional Self Supporting Metal Part]
    R0 --> R2[Need Thin Protective or Decorative Coating]

    R1 --> E1[Electroforming]
    E1 --> E2[Thickness 50 micron to several mm]
    E1 --> E3[Mandrel Sacrificed or Reused]
    E1 --> E4[Typical Material Ni Cu Fe]
    E1 --> E5[Application Moulds EDM Electrodes Micro Features]

    R2 --> P1[Electroplating]
    P1 --> P2[Thickness 5 to 25 micron typical]
    P1 --> P3[Substrate Retained Permanently]
    P1 --> P4[Typical Material Cr Au Ag Zn]
    P1 --> P5[Application Decorative Corrosion Resistance]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"With a neat sketch, explain the electroforming process. List any three advantages of electroforming over conventional machining of mould cavities."*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Electroforming is an electrochemical process in which metal is deposited on a conductive mandrel through electrolysis to produce a free-standing metallic shell that is later separated to form a mould insert or die component.

2. **Sketch description (1 mark):** The setup consists of a DC power supply, an anode (pure metal pellets, e.g., Ni), a cathode (mandrel), and an electrolyte bath (e.g., nickel sulfamate solution at 40–60 °C, pH 3.8–4.2).

3. **Three advantages (1 mark — any three):**
   - Can replicate micro-features down to sub-micron level (unmatched by conventional machining).
   - No tooling force or thermal distortion → excellent dimensional accuracy.
   - Suitable for complex internal cavities, undercuts, and free-form surfaces without draft angles.
   - Surface finish of the mandrel is perfectly reproduced (Ra < 0.1 µm achievable).

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"Differentiate between electroforming and electroplating. Why is electroforming classified as an additive manufacturing process?"*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Electroforming | Electroplating |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Manufacture a free-standing part | Apply a thin coating |
| Thickness | 50 µm to several mm | 5–25 µm typical |
| Mandrel/Substrate | Removed or sacrificed | Retained as part |
| Process duration | Hours to days | Minutes |
| Application | Moulds, EDM electrodes | Decorative, corrosion protection |

**Additive nature (2 marks):** Material is **added atom-by-atom** from the electrolyte onto the mandrel surface. There is no material removal, no cutting force, and no chip formation — making it a true **additive tooling process** similar in philosophy to 3D printing, except the "ink" is dissolved metal ions.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis. Derive the expression for the mass of metal deposited during electroforming. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A nickel electroforming bath is operated at a current density of **5 A/dm²** to deposit nickel on a mandrel of surface area **8 dm²**. The target deposit thickness is **1.5 mm**. Using the given data, calculate:
- (i) The theoretical mass of nickel deposited.
- (ii) The theoretical time required for deposition.
- (iii) The actual time if current efficiency is 92%.

**Data:** A (Ni) = 58.69 g/mol, n = 2, ρ (Ni) = 8.908 g/cm³, F = 96,485 C/mol. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution (A-a):

**Faraday's First Law (1.5 marks):** *The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.*

$$m \;\propto\; Q \;\Rightarrow\; m \;=\; \frac{Q \cdot A}{n \cdot F} \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$$

**Faraday's Second Law (1.5 marks):** *When the same charge is passed through different electrolytes, the masses of substances deposited are proportional to their equivalent weights.*

$$\frac{m_1}{E_1} \;=\; \frac{m_2}{E_2}$$

**Derivation of mass deposited (4 marks):**

At the cathode, the reduction reaction is:
$$\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Ni}$$

For 1 mole of Ni (58.69 g) to be deposited, **2 moles of electrons** must be supplied. The charge required for 1 mole of electrons = **1 Faraday = 96,485 C**.

Therefore, charge required for 1 mole of Ni = $2 \cdot F = 192{,}970$ C.

If charge $Q = I \cdot t$ is passed, moles of Ni deposited:

$$n_{Ni} \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Mass deposited:

$$\boxed{\,m \;=\; n_{Ni} \cdot A \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}\,}$$

#### Model Solution (A-b):

**Given:**
- $J = 5$ A/dm², $A_s = 8$ dm², $\tau = 1.5$ mm $= 0.15$ cm
- $A = 58.69$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.908$ g/cm³, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $\eta = 0.92$

**(i) Mass of nickel deposited (2 marks):**

Area in cm²: $A_s = 8 \cdot 100 = 800$ cm².

$$m \;=\; A_s \cdot \tau \cdot \rho \;=\; 800 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 8.908 \;=\; 1068.96 \text{ g} \;\approx\; 1069 \text{ g}$$

**(ii) Theoretical time (3 marks):**

Total current $I = J \cdot A_s = 5 \cdot 8 = 40$ A.

Using $t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot A}$:

$$t_{theo} \;=\; \frac{1068.96 \cdot 2 \cdot 96{,}485}{40 \cdot 58.69}$$

$$t_{theo} \;=\; \frac{206{,}290{,}786.4}{2347.6}$$

$$t_{theo} \;=\; 87{,}873 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 24.41 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Actual time (2 marks):**

$$t_{actual} \;=\; \frac{t_{theo}}{\eta} \;=\; \frac{24.41}{0.92} \;\approx\; 26.53 \text{ hours}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for electroforming a nickel mould insert. Discuss the role of the mandrel, electrolyte composition, and process parameters in determining the final mould quality. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An electroforming shop uses a copper sulfate bath to deposit copper on a steel mandrel of area **6 dm²**. The current passed is **30 A** for **4 hours**.
- (i) Calculate the mass of copper deposited (theoretically).
- (ii) If the actual mass obtained is **140 g**, calculate the cathode current efficiency.
- (iii) Comment on the significance of current efficiency in industrial electroforming.

**Data:** A (Cu) = 63.55 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution (B-a):

**Step-by-step procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Mandrel design and fabrication:** The mandrel is made of stainless steel, aluminium, or a low-melting alloy. Its dimensions account for **2× the desired deposit thickness** (mandrel is oversized, then machined back).
2. **Surface preparation:** Cleaning, degreasing, and activation ensure good adhesion of the initial deposit.
3. **Strike coating:** A thin, high-current initial layer ensures uniform coverage.
4. **Main electroforming:** Mandrel is immersed in the bath; current density 2–8 A/dm²; bath at 40–60 °C; pH 3.8–4.2; continuous filtration and agitation.
5. **Thickness monitoring:** Visual inspection or ultrasonic thickness gauge; deposit built to required τ (often 1–5 mm).
6. **Mandrel separation:** Mechanical pulling, chemical dissolution (for Al), or melting (for low-melting alloys).
7. **Backing and finishing:** Epoxy or metal backing is poured into the shell to provide rigidity. Final machining and polishing are done.

**Role of key factors (2 marks):**
- **Mandrel:** Dictates final geometry; surface finish of mandrel = surface finish of mould cavity.
- **Electrolyte composition:** Affects grain structure, internal stress, and throwing power.
- **Process parameters:** Current density controls deposition rate and grain refinement; temperature affects stress and brightness; agitation prevents pitting.

#### Model Solution (B-b):

**(i) Theoretical mass of copper (3 marks):**

$$m_{theo} \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$$

$$I = 30 \text{ A}, \quad t = 4 \cdot 3600 = 14{,}400 \text{ s}$$

$$m_{theo} \;=\; \frac{30 \cdot 14{,}400 \cdot 63.55}{2 \cdot 96{,}485}$$

$$m_{theo} \;=\; \frac{27{,}453{,}600}{192{,}970}$$

$$m_{theo} \;\approx\; 142.27 \text{ g}$$

**(ii) Cathode current efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta \;=\; \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta \;=\; \frac{140}{142.27} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 98.4\%$$

**(iii) Significance of current efficiency (2 marks):**

- High CCE (close to 100%) means most of the supplied charge goes into metal deposition, **reducing process time and energy cost**.
- Low CCE indicates parasitic side reactions (hydrogen evolution at cathode, oxygen at anode), causing pitting, porosity, and poor mechanical properties.
- For **precision mould manufacture**, η > 95% is desirable to ensure **uniform thickness, low internal stress, and predictable deposition rates**.
- CCE directly impacts **production economics** in industrial electroforming shops.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Electroforming Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Students forget to convert dm² to cm² (×100) and mm to cm (÷10). Always show the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Misapplying Current Efficiency (–2 marks):** η < 1 means **MORE time is needed**, not less. $t_{actual} = t_{theo} / \eta$, NOT multiplied.
> 3. **Confusing Plating & Forming (–1 mark):** Plating thickness = microns; Forming thickness = millimetres. Don't interchange.
> 4. **Forgetting to State n = 2 for Ni²⁺ and Cu²⁺ (–1 mark):** Always specify valency when invoking Faraday's Law.
> 5. **Skipping the Sketch in 3-mark questions (–1 mark):** Even a hand-drawn block diagram of the cell fetches full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electroforming = Additive Electrochemical Tooling Process** for moulds/dies using Faraday's Laws.
- **Faraday's Constant F = 96,485 C/mol** — must be quoted verbatim in derivations.
- **Master mass formula:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$.
- **Master time formula:** $t = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \tau \cdot n \cdot F \cdot 100}{J \cdot A}$ (with surface area cancellation).
- **Typical Ni-sulfamate operating window:** J = 2–8 A/dm², T = 40–60 °C, pH = 3.8–4.2, η ≈ 95–98%.
- **Distinction from electroplating:** Thickness (mm vs µm), Purpose (functional part vs coating), Process duration (hours vs minutes).
- **Cathode = mandrel** (negative terminal); **Anode = pure metal** source (positive terminal).
- **Applications in mould making:** EDM electrodes, blow-mould shells, micro-feature inserts, optical moulds, LIGA-style microfluidic tools.
- **Advantages:** No cutting force, micro-feature replication, no draft angle needed, repeatable accuracy.
- **Limitations:** Slow deposition rate, mandrel design constraints, bath chemistry maintenance, internal stress in thick deposits.
- **Always convert units BEFORE substituting** into formulas — examiners check this strictly.
- **Internal choice in KTU Part B:** Choose the question you can solve fully, not the one with the "easier-looking" sub-parts.
- **Mention η whenever deposition time is calculated** — a full-mark answer always includes the efficiency correction.
- **Draw the cell diagram** even if not explicitly asked — it consistently earns 1–2 marks in 3-mark and 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hobbing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hobbing

# 🛠️ HOBBING — Cold Forming of Die Cavities

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOBBING (also called Cold Die Hobbing or Hubbing):**
> *Hobbing is a precision cold-working (cold forging) process used in tool and die making, in which a hardened steel master tool called a **Hob** is pressed under very high static pressure into an annealed (softened) die-block material, causing the die material to undergo plastic flow and permanently conform to the inverse shape of the hob, thereby producing a finished die cavity without any metal cutting.*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Terminology — Verbatim Definition (Tool Design / Die Making):**
> Hobbing is classified as a *chipless material-shaping process* belonging to the family of **Cold Extrusion / Cold Forging** operations, specifically used for the manufacture of **forging dies, blanking dies, drawing dies and plastic mould inserts**.

---

## 2. Intuitive Overview — The "Stamp & Wax" Analogy

Imagine you have a **wax candle** and a brass seal with your college emblem engraved on it. If you push that brass seal firmly into the soft wax with your hand, the wax flows around the emblem and takes a perfect negative impression of it. You now have a mould for the emblem — made **without carving** the wax.

This is exactly what **hobbing** does in the industrial world:

| Real-World Analogy | Industrial Hobbing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Brass seal with raised emblem | **Hardened Master Hob** (positive shape) |
| Soft candle/wax | **Annealed Die Block** (soft die steel) |
| Push by hand | **Hydraulic Press** (~2000 MPa) |
| Wax that flowed sideways | **Plastic flow** of die steel |
| Negative impression in wax | **Die cavity** (final mould/die impression) |

The key insight is that **material is displaced, not removed**. The displaced volume of die steel flows *radially outward and downward* into a relief pocket, exactly conserving volume (law of constancy of volume).

---

## 3. Where Hobbing Fits in Die Manufacturing

```
Traditional Die Making          VS          HOBBING
─────────────────────                       ────────
1. Rough machining                          1. Machine die block blank
2. Annealing                               2. Anneal (soften) die block
3. Die sinking / CNC milling               3. Hob directly (single press stroke)
4. Hand finishing / benching                4. Stress relieve + light benching
5. Heat treatment                          5. Heat treatment
6. Polishing                               6. Polishing → READY

Time: 8 – 40 hours (per cavity)           Time: 5 – 30 minutes (per cavity)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU Module 3:** Hobbing falls under *"Non-conventional / chipless methods of cavity manufacture"*. The other methods typically compared with hobbing in the KTU syllabus are: (i) Die Casting of Die Cavities, (ii) Sinker EDM, (iii) Copy Milling, and (iv) CNC Die Sinking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis of the Hobbing Process

## 1. The Fundamental Principle

When the hardened hob is pressed into the annealed die block, the material of the die block is subjected to a triaxial state of compressive stress. Under such conditions, **ductile metals behave almost like a fluid** — they yield and flow plastically to conform to the geometry of the punch.

The governing physics is captured by the **Von Mises Yield Criterion** (in simplified form):

$$\sigma_{flow} \geq \sigma_{y} \quad \text{(material yields and flows)}$$

Because the hob does not rotate or cut, there is **no chip formation** — only **plastic displacement**. This is the defining characteristic of hobbing.

---

## 2. Classification of Hobbing Methods

### 2.1 Based on Geometry of Cavity Produced

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Classical Variants of Hobbing (high-yield for KTU exams):**

#### (a) **Closed-Die Hobbing** *(also called "restricted" or "enclosed" hobbing)*
- The die block is **completely surrounded** by a rigid retainer ring (shrinking ring / bolster).
- The displaced die material cannot escape upward — it is forced to flow **sideways into a relief cavity** cut below the block.
- Produces **very accurate, deep cavities** with sharp detail reproduction.
- Used for: **forging dies, coining dies, intricate mould inserts**.

#### (b) **Open-Die Hobbing** *(also called "free" hobbing)*
- No restricting ring is used; the die block is free to deform upward around the hob.
- Material flows upward, forming a **flash (upset) ring** around the hob.
- Cavity accuracy is lower; used for shallow, simple impressions.
- Used for: shallow forging impressions, simple blanking dies.

#### (c) **Pierce Hobbing** *(also called "through" hobbing)*
- The hob is pressed **all the way through** the die block, ejecting a slug from the bottom.
- A **pierce punch** rather than a forming punch.
- Produces **through-holes** in the die plate.
- Used for: **piercing dies, perforating dies, blanking die cutting edges** (preforming).

### 2.2 Based on Temperature

| Type | Temperature | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Cold Hobbing** | Room temperature (~25 °C) | Most common; small to medium cavities |
| **Warm Hobbing** | 300 – 600 °C | Larger cavities; reduces required force |
| **Hot Hobbing** | 700 – 950 °C | Very large forging dies |

---

## 3. The Hob — The Master Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A Hob is NOT a cutting tool — it is a forming (positive-shape) tool made of HARDENED steel, hardened to 58–62 HRC.**

### 3.1 Design Requirements of a Hob
- **Hardness:** 58 – 62 HRC (must be **harder** than die block, which is ~150 HB).
- **Surface finish:** Mirror polish (Ra < 0.2 µm) — directly determines the die cavity finish.
- **Taper / Draft:** Typically **0.5° to 1.5° draft** on sidewalls to facilitate **ejection (stripping)** of the hob after pressing.
- **Undercut / Back-taper:** NOT permitted (hobbing cannot produce undercuts in a single pressing).

### 3.2 Common Hob Materials
- **AISI D2 / D3** (High-C, High-Cr cold-work die steel) — most common.
- **AISI H11 / H13** (hot-work die steel) — for warm hobbing.
- **Carbide (WC-Co)** — for very long production runs.

### 3.3 Hob Manufacturing
The hob is typically produced by:
1. **Die sinking (EDM)** or **CNC milling** of a tool-steel blank.
2. **Hardening** (quenching + tempering).
3. **Grinding and polishing** to mirror finish.

---

## 4. The Die Block — The Workpiece

### 4.1 Required Properties
- Must be **soft (annealed)** during hobbing: typically **150 – 200 HB** (≈ 80 HRB).
- Sufficient **ductility** to flow without cracking.
- After hobbing, the die is **heat-treated** to working hardness (58 – 62 HRC).

### 4.2 Common Die Block Materials (KTU frequently asked)

| Material (AISI) | Pre-hobbing Hardness | After Hardening |
|-----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| AISI W1 / W2   | 150 – 180 HB         | 58 – 62 HRC    |
| AISI O1        | 180 – 200 HB         | 58 – 62 HRC    |
| AISI P20       | 190 – 220 HB         | 30 – 36 HRC (pre-hardened; for plastic moulds) |
| AISI H11 / H13 | 180 – 220 HB         | 48 – 54 HRC    |

### 4.3 Preparation Steps Before Hobbing
1. **Rough machine** the die block to size (with **± 0.5 mm** tolerance).
2. **Anneal** to soften.
3. **Face-mill** the top surface flat (essential for uniform hob entry).
4. **Drill or mill relief cavity** below the block (for closed-die hobbing).
5. **Clean and lubricate** both hob and die block.

---

## 5. Lubrication in Hobbing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubrication is CRITICAL — without it, the hob will gall, seize, or break under the enormous compression loads.**

Common lubricants:
- **Molybdenum disulphide (MoS₂)** paste — most widely used.
- **Zinc stearate / calcium stearate** — for warm hobbing.
- **Graphite-based lubricants** — for hot hobbing.
- **PTFE (Teflon) coatings** — premium applications.

The lubricant is applied **only to the sidewalls** of the hob — **not on the working face** (otherwise the die impression will be defective).

---

## 6. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas required for numerical problems on Hobbing in KTU Module-3 examinations:**

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks / Limits |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Hobbing Force (F)** | $F = k \cdot A_p \cdot \sigma_{flow}$ | N | $A_p$ = projected area of hob (mm²); $\sigma_{flow}$ = flow stress of die material at cold-working condition (MPa). |
| 2 | Projected Area of Hob | $A_p = \sum (L_i \cdot w_i)$ for rectangular features; $A_p = \pi d^2/4$ for circular | mm² | Sum of all projected areas of hob face. |
| 3 | **Specific Hobbing Pressure (p)** | $p = \dfrac{F}{A_p}$ | MPa | Typical range **1500 – 2500 MPa** for cold hobbing of tool steel. |
| 4 | Empirical Pressure Estimate (cold hobbing of tool steel) | $p \approx 2000$ to $2500$ MPa (constant) | MPa | Used when only area is known; from KTU data tables. |
| 5 | **Ejection / Stripping Force** | $F_{strip} \approx (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \times F_{hobbing}$ | N | 5–10 % of the hobbing force. Used to push hob out of die block. |
| 6 | Volume conservation | $V_{cavity} = A_p \times h = V_{displaced}$ | mm³ | Used to verify relief cavity design. |
| 7 | Depth of relief cavity (closed hobbing) | $h_{relief} = \dfrac{A_p \cdot h_{hob}}{A_{relief}}$ | mm | Must be sufficient to accommodate displaced volume. |
| 8 | Stripping stress | $\sigma_{strip} = p_{contact} \cdot \mu$ | MPa | Where $\mu$ ≈ 0.05–0.10 with good lubrication. |
| 9 | Press capacity selection | $F_{press} \geq 1.25 \times F_{hobbing}$ | N | Apply **25 % safety factor** on calculated force. |
| 10 | Number of hobbing strokes | N = 1 (single stroke); N > 1 only for very deep cavities with intermediate annealing. | — | Multiple strokes are an exception, not the rule. |

**Critical rule** (no table-breaking symbols): Stripping force expression uses approximation range with the em-dash style: $\sigma_{strip} = p_{contact} \cdot \mu$, with $\mu$ between 0.05 and 0.10.

---

## 7. Engineering Significance of Hobbing

Hobbing enables **mass production of identical die cavities** with sub-micron reproduction of surface texture. The method is responsible for the economic production of:
- Drop-forging dies for automotive connecting rods, crankshafts.
- Cold-heading die inserts for fasteners.
- Blanking and piercing die working parts.
- Plastic-injection mould cavities for low-melting-point polymers (via hobbed P20 moulds).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations and Worked Numerical Analysis

## Worked Example 1 — Calculating Hobbing Force

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A forging die cavity of dimensions **60 mm × 80 mm × 25 mm (depth)** is to be produced by cold hobbing. The flow stress of the annealed die-block material (AISI O1) under cold-working conditions is **σflow = 750 MPa**. An empirical multiplier of **k = 2.5** accounts for friction, die-block confinement, and triaxial stress state. Calculate: (a) the projected area, (b) the required hobbing force, (c) the press capacity to be selected.

### Step 1 — Identify the Geometry and Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length } (L) &= 80 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Width } (W) &= 60 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Depth of cavity } (h) &= 25 \text{ mm} \\
\sigma_{flow} &= 750 \text{ MPa} \\
k &= 2.5 \quad \text{(dimensionless multiplier)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Projected Area $A_p$

Projected area is the **area of the cavity footprint** (top view), which is the area the hob sees when pressing down:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_p &= L \times W \\
    &= 80 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm} \\
    &= 4800 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Required Hobbing Force

Using the master formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{hobbing} &= k \cdot A_p \cdot \sigma_{flow} \\
            &= 2.5 \times 4800 \text{ mm}^2 \times 750 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
            &= 9{,}000{,}000 \text{ N} \\
            &= 9{,}000 \text{ kN} \\
            &= 9000 \text{ kN} \;=\; 900 \text{ tonnes-force (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Select Press Capacity (Apply Safety Factor)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{press} &\geq 1.25 \times F_{hobbing} \\
          &= 1.25 \times 9000 \text{ kN} \\
          &= 11{,}250 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Selected press capacity → standard size: 12,000 kN (≈ 1200 t) hydraulic press.**
> This is the standard size nearest to and above the required force.

### Step 5 — Verification Using Specific Pressure Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= k \cdot \sigma_{flow} \\
  &= 2.5 \times 750 \\
  &= 1875 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

This lies within the typical cold-hobbing range of **1500 – 2500 MPa** ✓ — answer is dimensionally and physically consistent.

---

## Worked Example 2 — Stripping Force

Using the same example, the stripping (ejection) force to withdraw the hob from the die block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{strip} &= (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \times F_{hobbing} \\
          &= 0.07 \times 9000 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(taking mean value 7 \%)} \\
          &= 630 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Stripping:** The stripping force is **not** provided by the main hobbing press in most setups. A separate hydraulic stripping cylinder (mounted in the press table or bolster) is required to push the hob OUT of the die block after forming. Students frequently lose marks by confusing the **hobbing force** with the **stripping force**.

---

## Worked Example 3 — Relief Cavity Depth (Closed-Die Hobbing)

> **Problem:** Determine the depth of relief cavity needed below the die block if the relief area = 12,000 mm² and the displaced material volume = 4800 × 25 mm³.

### Step 1 — Compute Displaced Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{displaced} &= A_p \times h_{hob} \\
              &= 4800 \text{ mm}^2 \times 25 \text{ mm} \\
              &= 120{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Relief Depth

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{relief} &= \frac{V_{displaced}}{A_{relief}} \\
           &= \frac{120{,}000 \text{ mm}^3}{12{,}000 \text{ mm}^2} \\
           &= 10 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Design Rule:** Always provide **20–30 % extra depth** in the relief cavity to prevent the die block from cracking due to material back-pressure. Here, design $h_{relief}$ = **12 – 13 mm**.

---

## Worked Example 4 — Circular Hobbing (Pierce / Through Hobbing)

> **Problem:** A circular hob of diameter 40 mm is to pierce a die plate of thickness 15 mm. Flow stress = 700 MPa, k = 2.6. Calculate (a) hobbing force, (b) confirm if a 600 kN press is sufficient.

### Step 1 — Projected Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_p &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \\
    &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (40)^2 \\
    &= 1256.64 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Hobbing Force

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= k \cdot A_p \cdot \sigma_{flow} \\
  &= 2.6 \times 1256.64 \times 700 \\
  &= 2{,}287{,}085 \text{ N} \\
  &\approx 2287 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Press Capacity Selection

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{press} &\geq 1.25 \times 2287 = 2859 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A 600 kN press is grossly insufficient** — required minimum ≈ **2900 kN**. The student must select the next standard press size: **3000 kN (≈ 300 t)**.

---

## KTU-Style Decision Matrix — When to Choose Hobbing

| Criterion | Hobbing Suitable? | Justification |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Cavity depth < 1.5 × minimum width | ✅ Yes | Material flows uniformly |
| Cavity has undercuts | ❌ No | Hobbing cannot produce undercuts |
| Cavity has sharp corners (< 0.5 mm radius) | ⚠ Limited | Stress concentration cracks die block |
| Production volume > 100 identical dies | ✅ Yes | High hob cost amortised |
| Material to be hobbed is hardened | ❌ No | Must be annealed |
| Cavity is very deep (> 50 mm) | ❌ No | Excessive force; use EDM instead |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📊 Structural Schematics — Mermaid Diagrams

## 4.1 Process Flow of the Hobbing Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design forging die cavity] --> B[Manufacture Master Hob]
    B --> B1[Machine tool steel blank]
    B1 --> B2[Harden to 58-62 HRC]
    B2 --> B3[Grind and polish to mirror finish]
    B3 --> C[Prepare Die Block]
    C --> C1[Rough machine die block to size]
    C1 --> C2[Anneal die block to 150-200 HB]
    C2 --> C3[Face-mill top surface flat]
    C3 --> C4[Mill relief cavity beneath block]
    C4 --> D[Apply Lubricant to Hob Sidewalls]
    D --> E[Position Die Block in Hydraulic Press]
    E --> F[Align Hob Above Die Block]
    F --> G[Apply Hobbing Force 1500-2500 MPa]
    G --> H[Monitor Die Material Plastic Flow]
    H --> I[Hob Reaches Required Depth]
    I --> J[Release Pressure]
    J --> K[Strip Hob from Die Block]
    K --> L[Inspect Formed Cavity]
    L --> M[Stress Relieve Die Block]
    M --> N[Heat Treat to Final Hardness 58-62 HRC]
    N --> O[Final Polish and Bench]
    O --> P[End: Finished Die Ready for Production]
```

---

## 4.2 Three Variants of Hobbing — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        C1[Rigid retainer ring]
        C2[Die block confined]
        C3[Relief cavity below]
        C4[Material flows DOWN]
        C5[High accuracy, deep cavity]
    end

    subgraph openMod["OPEN-DIE HOBBING"]

        O3[Material flows UP as flash]
        O4[Lower accuracy, shallow only]
    end

    subgraph pierce["PIERCE HOBBING"]

        P2[Through-penetration of plate]
        P3[Slug ejected from bottom]
        P4[For through-holes]
    end

    closed --- common[Common: Plastic flow, no chip removal]
    openMod --- common
    pierce --- common
```

---

## 4.3 Force and Stress System During Hobbing

```mermaid
        PR[Press Ram applies F down]
    end

    subgraph hob["HARDENED HOB at 60 HRC"]

        HB3[Lubricant film MoS2]

    HB1 -->|Pressure transmits| DB1
    DB1 --> DB2

```
