# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme — Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define hobbing. Why is it classified as a chipless manufacturing process?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hobbing** is a cold-forming (cold-forging) process in which a hardened master tool called a *hob* is pressed under very high static pressure into an annealed die-block material, causing the die material to flow plastically and take the inverse shape of the hob, thereby forming a die cavity.

> It is called **chipless** because **no material is removed** during the process. Unlike milling or EDM, there is no generation of chips, swarf, or molten material. The displaced die material flows into a relief cavity or upward as flash — conserving total volume.

> The process typically operates at pressures of **1500 – 2500 MPa**, with the hob hardened to **58–62 HRC** and the die block annealed to **150–200 HB**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for explanation of chipless nature, 1 for typical process parameters)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**List any three differences between closed-die hobbing and open-die hobbing.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Closed-Die Hobbing | Open-Die Hobbing |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Retainer ring | Rigid ring confines the die block | No restraining ring used |
| 2 | Direction of material flow | Downward into relief cavity | Upward as flash around hob |
| 3 | Cavity depth achievable | Deep cavities (good accuracy) | Only shallow cavities |
| 4 | Accuracy | High dimensional accuracy | Lower accuracy |
| 5 | Applications | Intricate forging/coining dies | Simple shallow impressions |

*(3 marks: any three differences, 1 mark each)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Internal Choice Format:** Answer **ANY ONE** from the following:
> **(a) Question A** OR **(b) Question B**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **cold hobbing process** for manufacturing forging dies. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction of the hob and the die block, and explain the role of lubrication.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A forging die cavity with a circular projection of **diameter 50 mm** is to be made by closed-die hobbing. The depth of hobbing is **30 mm**. The flow stress of the die-block material is **700 MPa**. Take the empirical multiplier **k = 2.4**.
Calculate: (i) the projected area, (ii) the hobbing force, (iii) the stripping force, and (iv) the recommended press capacity.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**1. Definition and Principle:**
Hobbing is a cold-working process wherein a hardened steel master tool (hob) is forced under high pressure (~2000 MPa) into an annealed die block. The die material undergoes **plastic deformation** and flows to fill the inverse geometry of the hob, producing a finished die cavity.

**2. Construction of the Hob:**
- **Material:** AISI D2 / D3 cold-work die steel.
- **Hardness:** 58 – 62 HRC (must be harder than die block).
- **Geometry:** Positive replica of the final die cavity, with **1° – 1.5° draft** on sidewalls for stripping.
- **Surface finish:** Mirror-polished (Ra < 0.2 µm) — directly transferred to die cavity.
- Manufactured by EDM or CNC milling, followed by grinding and polishing.

**3. Construction of the Die Block:**
- **Material:** AISI W1, W2, O1 (carbon tool steel); P20 for plastic moulds.
- **Hardness before hobbing:** 150 – 200 HB (annealed condition).
- **Preparation:** Rough machined to size, top face milled flat, relief cavity machined beneath (for closed-die hobbing).
- After hobbing, heat-treated to 58 – 62 HRC.

**4. Role of Lubrication:**
- Reduces friction between hob sidewalls and die material.
- Prevents galling, seizing and hob fracture.
- Common lubricant: **MoS₂ paste** (cold); graphite (hot).
- Applied **only to sidewalls**, not on working face.

**5. Process Steps:**
1. Anneal die block → 2. Face-mill top → 3. Mill relief → 4. Lubricate hob → 5. Align and press → 6. Strip hob → 7. Stress relieve → 8. Heat-treat → 9. Polish.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 50$ mm
- Hobbing depth $h = 30$ mm
- $\sigma_{flow} = 700$ MPa
- $k = 2.4$

**(i) Projected Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_p &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2 \\
    &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (50)^2 \\
    &= 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Hobbing Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= k \cdot A_p \cdot \sigma_{flow} \\
  &= 2.4 \times 1963.5 \times 700 \\
  &= 3{,}298{,}680 \text{ N} \\
  &\approx 3299 \text{ kN} \; (\approx 330 \text{ tonnes-force})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Stripping Force (taking 7 % of hobbing force):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{strip} &= 0.07 \times 3299 \text{ kN} \\
          &= 230.9 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Recommended Press Capacity (with 25 % safety factor):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{press} &\geq 1.25 \times 3299 \\
          &= 4124 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Recommended standard size: 4500 kN hydraulic press.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three types of hobbing** (closed, open and pierce hobbing) with diagrams. Compare their suitability for forging dies, plastic moulds and blanking dies.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A forging die cavity has a **rectangular projection 100 mm × 40 mm** and is to be hobbed to a depth of **20 mm**. The flow stress is **800 MPa** and the empirical constant is **k = 2.5**. A hydraulic press of capacity **10,000 kN** is available. Verify whether this press is suitable. If not, suggest the next standard press size.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**1. Closed-Die Hobbing:**
- Die block is enclosed in a rigid retainer ring (shrinking ring).
- Displaced material flows downward into a relief cavity pre-machined beneath the block.
- Produces accurate, deep cavities. Used for **forging dies**.

**2. Open-Die Hobbing:**
- No retaining ring. Material flows upward as a flash upset around the hob.
- Less accurate, limited to shallow impressions. Used for **simple shallow forging dies and embossing dies**.

**3. Pierce Hobbing:**
- A piercing punch (sharp-edged) penetrates fully through the die plate.
- A slug is ejected from the bottom; through-hole is formed.
- Used for **blanking and piercing dies** (forming the cutting edge geometry).

| Variant | Forging Dies | Plastic Moulds | Blanking Dies |
|---------|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| Closed | ✅ Excellent (deep impressions) | ✅ Good | Limited |
| Open | Limited (shallow only) | Limited | ❌ Not used |
| Pierce | ❌ Not used | ❌ Not used | ✅ Excellent (pre-forming cutting edges) |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Given:**
- $L = 100$ mm, $W = 40$ mm
- $h = 20$ mm
- $\sigma_{flow} = 800$ MPa
- $k = 2.5$
- Available press capacity = 10,000 kN

**Step 1 — Projected Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_p &= L \times W \\
    &= 100 \times 40 = 4000 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Hobbing Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= k \cdot A_p \cdot \sigma_{flow} \\
  &= 2.5 \times 4000 \times 800 \\
  &= 8{,}000{,}000 \text{ N} \\
  &= 8000 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Required Press Capacity (25 % safety factor):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{required} &= 1.25 \times 8000 = 10{,}000 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$
F_{required} \; (10{,}000 \text{ kN}) \; = \; F_{available} \; (10{,}000 \text{ kN})
$$

> **The available press capacity is JUST SUFFICIENT — but with ZERO safety margin.** This is risky for production use (press wear, alignment errors, material variability can push force beyond limit).

> **Recommended action:** Select the next standard press size: **12,000 kN (≈ 1200 tonnes)** for safe, reliable operation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Hobbing Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing k (empirical multiplier) with σ (flow stress):** They are different — $k$ is dimensionless (typically 2.0 – 2.8), $\sigma_{flow}$ is in MPa.
> 2. **Forgetting the 25 % safety factor on press capacity selection.** Students often quote the raw hobbing force as the press capacity — this loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing hobbing force with stripping force** — they are different and require different mechanisms in the press.
> 4. **Forgetting the draft angle** on the hob — a hob without draft cannot be stripped; the die block will crack.
> 5. **Applying lubricant to the working face** of the hob — this causes a defective cavity.
> 6. **Stating that hobbing can produce undercuts** — it cannot (single-stroke limitation).
> 7. **Mixing up closed-die and open-die hobbing** — the direction of material flow and the location of the relief cavity must be clearly distinguished.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Hobbing = cold forging of die cavities; NO chip removal; material flows plastically.**
- **Hob hardness (58–62 HRC) > Die block hardness (150–200 HB)** — this is mandatory.
- **Three types:** Closed (deep, accurate), Open (shallow, simple), Pierce (through-holes).
- **Hobbing pressure range:** 1500 – 2500 MPa for cold hobbing.
- **Master formula:** $F = k \cdot A_p \cdot \sigma_{flow}$.
- **Projected area $A_p$** = top-view area of hob (footprint).
- **Stripping force** = 5–10 % of hobbing force (separate cylinder needed).
- **Press selection rule:** Capacity ≥ **1.25 × calculated hobbing force**.
- **Lubricant:** MoS₂ (cold); graphite (hot); applied **only to sidewalls**.
- **Draft on hob sidewalls:** 0.5° – 1.5° (mandatory for stripping).
- **Undercuts are NOT possible** in single-stroke hobbing.
- **Relief cavity design** for closed-die hobbing: depth + 20–30 % extra.
- **Volume conservation** governs the design of the relief cavity.
- **Stress-relief heat treatment** is required after hobbing but before final hardening.
- **Cannot hob hardened material** — die block MUST be in annealed condition.
- **Cannot produce very deep narrow cavities** (depth/width > 1.5) — use EDM instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polishing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polishing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polishing (in Mould & Die Manufacture)** is a precision surface finishing process in which controlled, progressive abrasion is applied to the working surfaces of a mould cavity, core, or die using successively finer abrasive media, in order to achieve a specified micro-roughness (Ra in **µm** or **µin**), mirror reflectivity, and release behaviour required for the moulded/formed part.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, polishing is classified under **Secondary Finishing Operations** (performed after milling, EDM, or grinding) and is considered a **value-adding process** because it directly governs:

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | **0.05 – 1.6 µm** |
| Gloss (Gloss Units @ 60°) | **80 – 100 GU** |
| SPI Finish Grade | **A-1 to A-3 (Mirror)** |
| VDI 3400 Grade | **12 – 18 (Mirror)** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of polishing a die surface like **sanding a piece of rough wood before varnishing it**. You do not start with the finest sandpaper — that would just polish the bumps. You start with coarse sandpaper (e.g., 80 grit) to remove the big scratches, then move progressively to medium (220), fine (600), very fine (1200), and finally to a mirror paste (diamond compound). Each successive step only removes the scratches left by the previous, coarser step. The rule is simple: **"The finishing step can only remove the marks of the step before it."**

The same principle applies to a hardened tool-steel mould cavity. The polisher begins with a stone or file matched to the deepest EDM/cutter marks and systematically works down to sub-micron roughness, using finer abrasives at every stage.

## 1.3 Why Polishing is Non-Negotiable in Mould & Die Work

- **Release behaviour:** A mirror-polished cavity (Ra < 0.1 µm) allows plastic melt to fill, cool, and eject without sticking or leaving drag marks.
- **Part aesthetics:** Transparent parts (lenses, light guides, acrylic covers) replicate every defect of the cavity surface.
- **Tool life:** A smooth surface resists corrosion, reduces wear, and limits plastic adhesion.
- **Cycle time:** Lower surface friction = faster part ejection = reduced cycle time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Polishing Operations

The polishing process in a die/mould shop follows a strict **top-down (coarse-to-fine)** sequence. Skipping a step is the #1 cause of polishing failures in KTU lab exams and industry.

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

1. **Stock Removal / Stoneing**
   - Removes EDM recast layer, cutter marks, and grind lines.
   - Tools: **Aluminium-oxide stones, silicon-carbide sticks, diamond files**.
   - Rule: Use the **hardest abrasive** that is **softer than the workpiece** to avoid embedding abrasive particles in the die steel.

2. **Grinding / Sanding**
   - Successive grits: **#80 → #120 → #220 → #320 → #400 → #600 → #800 → #1000 → #1200**.
   - Tools: **Abrasive papers, rubber-bonded sticks, abrasive-impregnated rubber wheels**.

3. **Lapping / Pre-Polishing**
   - Uses **emery compounds (Al₂O₃, SiC) of 15 µm, 9 µm, 6 µm, 3 µm, 1 µm**.
   - Lubricant: **kerosene, mineral oil, or kerosene-paraffin mix** to flush debris and cool the surface.

4. **Mirror / Final Polishing**
   - Uses **diamond paste (polycrystalline or monocrystalline)** from **6 µm → 3 µm → 1 µm → 0.5 µm → 0.25 µm**.
   - Carrier: **felt bob, wooden stick, or cotton swab** rotating at **800 – 1500 RPM**.

5. **Buffing / Coloring**
   - For ultra-glossy surfaces (Ra < 0.05 µm).
   - Uses **rouge (Fe₂O₃) or cerium-oxide (CeO₂)** on soft cotton buff wheels.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why a lubricant?"** — The cutting fluid carries away swarf, prevents re-deposition of abrasive onto the die surface, and dissipates the heat that could otherwise cause **heat-checking** on hardened H13/D2 tool steel.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean Roughness | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$ | µm | General surface finish metric |
| 2 | Mean Roughness Depth | $R_z = \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} Z_i$ | µm | Peak-to-valley average |
| 3 | Material Removal Rate (lapping) | $MRR = K \cdot P \cdot V$ | mm³/min | Preston’s equation for abrasive polishing |
| 4 | Preston’s Coefficient | $K = \dfrac{MRR}{P \cdot V}$ | mm²/N | Abrasive–workpiece pair constant |
| 5 | Abrasive Cut Depth (approx.) | $h \approx \dfrac{d_{grain}}{2}$ | µm | Single-pass scratch depth |
| 6 | Equivalent SPI to VDI Conversion | $\text{VDI} \approx 30 - 2 \cdot R_a[\mu m]$ | — | Cross-reference (approx.) |
| 7 | Grit number to µin Ra | $R_a \approx \dfrac{25000}{\text{Grit}}$ | µin | Quick shop-floor rule |
| 8 | Specific Energy (polishing) | $u = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{MRR}$ | J/mm³ | Energy required per unit volume removed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Preston’s Equation matters for KTU problems:** It is the **only quantitative model** the examiner expects you to know for polishing. If a numerical question is asked, the answer almost always reduces to plugging values into $MRR = K \cdot P \cdot V$ where **P** = contact pressure (N/mm²) and **V** = relative velocity (m/s).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive clear-lens headlamps:** Require SPI A-1 finish to avoid visible orange-peel.
- **Optical moulds (CD/DVD, eyeglass lenses):** Demand sub-0.05 µm Ra for light transmission without scatter.
- **Medical devices (syringes, vials):** Polished cavities prevent drug contamination and improve cleanability.
- **Die-casting dies:** Polished cores reduce thermal fatigue and soldering of aluminium to the die.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Preston’s Equation (Board-Exam Favourite)

**Statement:** The volume of material removed during abrasive polishing is proportional to the contact pressure and the relative sliding velocity between the abrasive and the workpiece.

### Step 1 — Define the rate of material removal

Let $h(t)$ be the instantaneous depth of cut. The volumetric removal rate per unit area is:

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}
$$

where $A$ is the apparent contact area (m²).

### Step 2 — Relate cut depth to sliding distance

For a single abrasive grain, the incremental cut is proportional to the sliding distance $ds$:

$$
dh = K' \, ds
$$

Dividing both sides by $dt$:

$$
\frac{dh}{dt} = K' \, \frac{ds}{dt} = K' \, v
$$

where $v = ds/dt$ is the relative velocity (m/s).

### Step 3 — Introduce pressure dependence

The proportionality constant $K'$ is not absolute — it grows with the contact pressure $p$ (Pa). Empirically, for low pressures, the relationship is **linear**:

$$
K' = K \cdot p
$$

where $K$ is **Preston’s coefficient** (m²/N), a material–abrasive constant.

### Step 4 — Combine to obtain the Master Equation

Substituting back:

$$
\frac{dh}{dt} = K \cdot p \cdot v
$$

Multiplying by contact area $A$:

$$
\boxed{MRR = A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} = K \cdot P \cdot v}
$$

where $P = p \cdot A$ is the total contact load (N).

> **Interpretation:** To remove more material per minute, you can either press harder, slide faster, or use a more aggressive abrasive (larger $K$). However, exceeding critical $P$ causes **micro-cracking** in hardened die steel, and exceeding critical $v$ causes **thermal burn** — both are common KTU viva questions.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A polishing operation uses a felt bob of area $25 \text{ cm}^2$ pressing against a die cavity with a force of $20 \text{ N}$. The rotational speed is $1200 \text{ RPM}$ and the bob radius is $15 \text{ mm}$. If Preston’s coefficient for diamond-on-steel is $K = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{N}$, compute the MRR.

**Solution (Line by Line):**

Step 1 — Compute contact pressure:

$$
p = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{20 \text{ N}}{25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2} = 8000 \text{ Pa}
$$

Step 2 — Compute relative sliding velocity at the contact radius (assume the felt bob contacts at the rim):

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
v = \omega \cdot r = 125.66 \times 0.015 = 1.885 \text{ m/s}
$$

Step 3 — Apply Preston’s equation:

$$
MRR = K \cdot p \cdot v
$$

$$
MRR = (1.2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8000 \times 1.885
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR = 1.81 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \approx 1.09 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Polishing Methods vs. Application

| Polishing Method | Abrasive Media | Achievable Ra (µm) | Best Suited For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Manual Stoneing | Al₂O₃ / SiC stones | 0.8 – 3.2 | EDM re-cast removal | Labour-intensive |
| Hand Sanding | Abrasive paper | 0.4 – 1.6 | Large flat surfaces | Inconsistent pressure |
| Belt Polishing | Coated abrasive belts | 0.2 – 0.8 | External die surfaces | Not for deep cavities |
| Rubber-Bob Polishing | SiC / Al₂O₃ in rubber | 0.1 – 0.4 | Curved profiles | Generates heat |
| Felt-Bob + Diamond | Diamond paste 6 – 0.25 µm | 0.025 – 0.1 | Mirror polish of cavities | Slow |
| Buffing (rouge) | Fe₂O₃ / CeO₂ | < 0.025 | Final gloss | Cannot remove scratches |
| Electrolytic Polishing | Chemical (acid) bath | 0.05 – 0.2 | Complex 3D cavities | Cannot level |
| Ultrasonic Polishing | Abrasive slurry + US | 0.1 – 0.4 | Micro-features | Small work area |
| Magnetic Abrasive | Magnetic particles | 0.05 – 0.2 | Internal bores | Limited to ferrous dies |
| Robot-Assisted | Force-controlled tool | 0.05 – 0.2 | Repetitive production | High capital cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board tip:** If the question says “mirror finish for a plastic lens mould” → answer is **Diamond paste on felt bob (Stage 4)**. If it says “EDM scale removal” → answer is **Al₂O₃/SiC stoneing (Stage 1)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Polishing Process Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete decision-flow a die-polisher follows, from receiving a rough EDM-finished cavity to releasing a mirror-finished tool.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Rough Die or Mould Cavity] --> B{Inspection of Initial Surface}
    B --> C[Identify Defects: EDM Recast, Cutter Marks, Grind Lines]
    C --> D[Stage 1: Stoneing with Al2O3 or SiC Sticks]
    D --> E[Stage 2: Coarse Sanding 80 to 220 Grit]
    E --> F[Stage 3: Fine Sanding 320 to 600 Grit]
    F --> G[Stage 4: Lapping with 15 to 6 Micron Emery]
    G --> H[Stage 5: Pre-Polish with 3 to 1 Micron Diamond]
    H --> I[Stage 6: Final Mirror Polish 0.5 to 0.25 Micron Diamond]
    I --> J{Quality Check: Ra less than 0.1 micron}
    J -->|Pass| K[Buffing with Rouge or Cerium Oxide]
    J -->|Fail| L[Return to Stage Identified by Defect Type]
    L --> D
    K --> M[Final Cleaning with Ultrasonic Bath]
    M --> N[Release for Assembly or Hard Chrome Plating]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Robotic Polishing Cell

Modern mould shops (especially for high-volume automotive tooling) use **force-controlled robotic polishing cells**. The architecture below shows how the subsystems interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ctrl[Control Subsystem]
        C1[Path Planning Software]
        C2[Force Feedback Controller]
        C3[Process Database]
    end
    subgraph mech[Mechanical Subsystem]
        M1[6 Axis Articulated Robot Arm]
        M2[Compliant End Effector]
        M3[Abrasive Tool Spindle 0 to 3000 RPM]
    end
    subgraph sense[Sensing Subsystem]
        S1[6 Axis Force Torque Sensor]
        S2[Laser Profilometer]
        S3[In Process Ra Measurement Probe]
    end
    subgraph ops[Operations Subsystem]
        O1[Coolant and Lubricant Supply]
        O2[Diamond Paste Dispenser]
        O3[Chip and Sludge Collector]
    end
    C1 --> M1
    C2 --> M1
    C3 --> C1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    S1 --> C2
    S2 --> C1
    S3 --> C1
    O1 --> M3
    O2 --> M3
    M3 --> O3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Differentiate between **Lapping** and **Buffing** in mould polishing. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lapping | Buffing |
|---|---|---|
| Abrasive | Diamond paste (1 – 6 µm) or emery | Rouge (Fe₂O₃) or cerium oxide |
| Carrier | Felt bob, wooden stick, glass plate | Soft cotton or muslin buff wheel |
| Purpose | Material removal + surface refinement | Final gloss enhancement, no significant removal |
| Surface Achieved | Pre-mirror to mirror (Ra 0.1 – 0.4 µm) | High gloss (Ra < 0.05 µm) |
| Action | Abrasive cutting (2-body or 3-body) | Smearing + light abrasive burnishing |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. What is the **SPI finish standard**? Mention the four grades of steel mould finishes defined by it. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **SPI (Society of the Plastics Industry)** finish standard is a globally accepted visual-and-metric classification of plastic-mould cavity surfaces, codified into **12 grades** under 4 broad categories:

1. **Grade A — Diamond Finish (A-1, A-2, A-3):** Highest gloss; achieved by **diamond polishing** with grades 6, 3, 1, 0.5 µm successively. Used for optical and transparent parts.
2. **Grade B — Paper Finish (B-1, B-2, B-3):** Satin finish; obtained by dry or wet **abrasive paper** in 600, 400, 320 grit sequence. Used for general mouldings.
3. **Grade C — Stone Finish (C-1, C-2, C-3):** Coarser finish; obtained by **stones and grinding**. Used for textured or non-aesthetic parts.
4. **Grade D — Other (D-1, D-2, D-3):** Achieved by blasting, etching, EDM, or chemical texturing. Used for leather-grain, wood-grain, or functional surfaces.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for polishing an EDM-finished H13 die-steel cavity** to achieve a mirror finish suitable for an optical lens mould. Include the role of abrasives, lubricants, and tools at every stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using **Preston’s equation**, calculate the material removal rate (in mm³/min) for a polishing operation with: contact pressure = 5 MPa, sliding velocity = 2 m/s, Preston’s coefficient = $1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/N. Comment on whether the obtained MRR is suitable for rough polishing or fine polishing. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step EDM-to-Mirror Polishing Procedure:**

> The polisher first removes the **white recast layer (typically 5–20 µm thick)** produced by the EDM process; this layer is brittle and contains un-tempered martensite, so it must be ground off completely before finer polishing begins.

1. **Stoneing (EDM Scale Removal):** Use a **silicon-carbide or aluminium-oxide stick** (grit ≈ 220) shaped to follow the cavity contour. Apply with firm pressure, parallel strokes, and flood with cutting oil. Inspect under 10× loupe — all white recast must be gone.
2. **Coarse Sanding (#320 – #400):** Wrap abrasive paper around a **hard rubber or wooden stick** sized to the cavity radius. Sand in a single consistent direction; cross-sanding hides scratches.
3. **Fine Sanding (#600 – #800 – #1000):** Repeat with progressively finer papers. Lubricant: **kerosene** to flush debris. Switch to **wet/dry silicon-carbide paper** to maintain flatness.
4. **Lapping (15 µm → 6 µm emery):** Use **emery paste on a felt bob** running at 1000 RPM. This step removes all previous scratch directions by randomizing them.
5. **Diamond Pre-Polish (6 µm → 3 µm → 1 µm):** Apply **polycrystalline diamond paste** on a clean felt bob. Use light pressure and high speed; diamond’s hardness (~10000 HV) cuts H13 (~600 HV) easily.
6. **Final Mirror (0.5 µm → 0.25 µm diamond):** Hand-polish with a **wooden stick or cotton swab** in a **figure-8 pattern**. Wipe with acetone between grades.
7. **Buffing (Optional):** Use a soft cotton buff with **rouge (Fe₂O₃)** for photographic-grade gloss.
8. **Cleaning:** Ultrasonic bath in **deionized water + neutral detergent** for 10 minutes; air-dry with filtered air.
9. **Inspection:** Measure **Ra with a profilometer** at ≥ 3 locations; visually inspect under **raking light** (a single fluorescent tube held at 30° to the surface) for residual scratches.

> **Why each step matters:** The polisher must remember that the **finish achievable at any stage = (abrasive size) × constant**. A 6 µm scratch from a missed step requires 6 µm diamond to remove — a 1 µm diamond will simply follow the 6 µm scratch and produce a wavy but reflective surface (a defect called **“orange peel”**).

**Part (b) — Preston’s Equation Numerical:**

**Given:** $p = 5 \text{ MPa} = 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$, $v = 2 \text{ m/s}$, $K = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{N}$

**Step 1 — Recall the equation:**

$$
MRR = K \cdot p \cdot v
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
MRR = (1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (5 \times 10^{6}) \times 2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute stepwise:**

$$
K \cdot p = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{6} = 7.5
$$

$$
MRR = 7.5 \times 2 = 15 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to per minute:**

$$
\boxed{MRR = 15 \times 60 = 900 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

**Comment:** An MRR of **900 mm³/min** is **high** — typical of **rough lapping with 15 µm emery on cast iron**. For fine diamond polishing (1 µm), the achievable MRR is roughly 0.5 – 5 mm³/min. The student should therefore conclude that the operating point is suitable for **rough polishing**, not final mirror work.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

### Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **classification of polishing methods** used in mould and die manufacture. Compare at least three methods on the basis of abrasive type, achievable Ra, and typical application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain the **common polishing defects** encountered in die finishing. What corrective action would you recommend for each? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution Outline — Question B

**Part (a):**
- Block diagram of classification: **Mechanical (manual/robotic)**, **Chemical (electrolytic)**, **Hybrid (electro-chemical, ultrasonic, magnetic abrasive)**.
- Compare: **Stoneing vs. Diamond Polishing vs. Buffing** using the matrix from Section 3.2 of these notes.
- Highlight the application of **Robotic Polishing** for high-volume automotive die production.

**Part (b) — Polishing Defects & Remedies:**

1. **Orange Peel:** Wavy but reflective surface → caused by skipping grit sizes. **Fix:** Return to the missing intermediate grade.
2. **Polishing Burns / Heat Checks:** Discoloration + micro-cracks → excessive pressure or speed. **Fix:** Reduce $P$ and $v$; flood with coolant.
3. **Embedded Abrasive:** Black specks in the surface → abrasive harder than workpiece. **Fix:** Switch to a softer abrasive (rule: abrasive must be 1.5× – 2× harder than workpiece, no more).
4. **Rounded Edges:** Loss of sharp cavity edges → over-polishing. **Fix:** Mask edges with soft solder or use edge-protection jigs.
5. **Pitting:** Crater-like voids → from inclusions in steel or inadequate EDM recast removal. **Fix:** Re-EDM, re-harden, or use a different die-steel grade.
6. **Drag Marks / Lapping Tracks:** Directional scratches visible at low angles → inconsistent motion. **Fix:** Use figure-8 or circular pattern with frequent tool rotation.
7. **Gloss Variation:** Uneven shine across the cavity → non-uniform pressure. **Fix:** Use a **compliant (force-controlled) tool holder** or a robot.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do not** list polishing steps without specifying the **grit size** at each step. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for "states the correct grit sequence (#80 → #220 → #600 → #1000 → diamond 6 → 1 µm)." A generic answer like "sand it smooth" scores **zero**.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **Ra (arithmetic mean)** with **Rz (mean peak-to-valley)** in numerical problems — they are **not interchangeable**. KTU 2024 specifically tests this in 2-mark sub-questions.
> 3. **Do not** state that buffing removes material. Buffing is a **smearing/gloss-enhancing** operation; writing "buffing removes scratches" is an automatic **minus-2 mark** error.
> 4. **Do not** write Preston’s equation as $MRR = K \cdot P \cdot V$ without defining the units of $K$ as **m²/N**. Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for missing units.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Page Before Exam)**

- ☐ Polishing is a **secondary finishing** process — it comes after milling, EDM, or grinding; **never before**.
- ☐ The cardinal rule: **“The next finer step can only remove the marks of the previous step.”** Skipping grits = guaranteed orange peel.
- ☐ Standard grit sequence: **#80 → #120 → #220 → #320 → #400 → #600 → #800 → #1000 → #1200 → Diamond 6 → 3 → 1 → 0.5 → 0.25 µm**.
- ☐ **Stoneing** uses Al₂O₃ or SiC sticks; **Lapping** uses diamond or emery pastes; **Buffing** uses rouge or cerium oxide on cotton.
- ☐ Lubricant functions: **debris flushing + heat dissipation + prevention of abrasive re-deposition**.
- ☐ **Abrasive hardness rule:** Abrasive must be **1.5× – 2×** the hardness of the workpiece, no harder (or it embeds).
- ☐ Master equation: $\boxed{MRR = K \cdot p \cdot v}$ (Preston’s Equation). Units: $K$ in m²/N, $p$ in Pa, $v$ in m/s → MRR in m³/s.
- ☐ SPI finish standards: **A (Diamond)**, **B (Paper)**, **C (Stone)**, **D (Other)**.
- ☐ Common defects and their causes: **Orange peel** (skipped grit), **Burns** (excess P or v), **Embedded abrasive** (wrong abrasive), **Rounded edges** (over-polishing), **Pitting** (steel inclusions).
- ☐ Inspection tools: **Profilometer (quantitative Ra)**, **Raking light (qualitative scratch check)**, **10× loupe (recast layer)**.
- ☐ Modern trends: **Force-controlled robotic polishing** for consistency, **electrolytic polishing** for inaccessible 3D features, **magnetic abrasive finishing** for internal bores.
- ☐ Typical Ra targets: **0.8 µm** for general mouldings, **0.2 µm** for transparent parts, **0.05 µm** for optical lenses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Structuring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surface Structuring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Structuring** is the deliberate, controlled modification of the micro- and macro-geometry of the working (cavity) surfaces of a mould or die to generate a specific texture, pattern, or topographical feature. It is a value-added finishing operation that tailors surface topography to meet **functional, aesthetic, optical, or tribological** requirements of the final moulded or stamped product.

In the context of PEPOT756 (Mould and Die Manufacture), surface structuring is treated as a **secondary / post-machining process** performed on the hardened die or mould insert — *after* CNC roughing, finishing, and heat treatment — to impart deterministic or stochastic features on the cavity surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction in KTU parlance:**
> - **Surface Finish** → refers to the *overall smoothness* (Ra, Rz values) achieved by grinding, polishing, or lapping.
> - **Surface Structuring** → refers to the *deliberate creation of geometric features* (lines, dots, grains, logos, micro-channels) on an otherwise finished surface.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Cake-Mould Story"**
>
> Imagine baking a cake. The **shape** of the cake comes from the *geometry* of the mould. The **texture on the outside** of the cake (smooth, ridged, patterned, with a strawberry embossed on top) comes from the *texture carved on the inside of the mould*. Surface structuring is exactly that — it is the "embossing pattern" the mould carries inside it. When molten plastic, molten aluminium, or sheet metal is pressed against this textured mould surface, it faithfully copies the texture. The cake gets a strawberry; the phone cover gets a leather-grain look; the dashboard gets a wood-pine feel. **The mould is the "master stamp" and surface structuring is the art of carving that stamp.**

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

The following **standardised surface metrology parameters** govern all surface structuring work in mould/die manufacturing:

- **Ra** — Arithmetic Mean Roughness (µm) — the *industry default*
- **Rz** — Mean Peak-to-Valley Height (µm) — used in **DIN/ISO specifications**
- **Rq (Rrms)** — Root-Mean-Square Roughness (µm)
- **Rt (Rmax)** — Maximum Peak-to-Valley Height (µm)
- **Rsk** — Skewness (dimensionless) — distinguishes *peak-dominated* vs *valley-dominated* textures
- **Rku** — Kurtosis (dimensionless) — measures *sharpness* of peaks/valleys
- **Sm** — Mean Spacing of profile irregularities (mm)
- **λc / λs** — Cut-off wavelengths for roughness vs waviness separation (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Mnemonic — "Ra Rules"**
> Ra is the **most commonly asked** roughness parameter in KTU examinations because it is the **universally accepted single-value descriptor** of surface finish, even though it cannot distinguish between a *peaky* and a *valley-rich* texture of the same Ra value.

---

### 1.4 Engineering Relevance

| Industry Sector | Typical Structured Surface on Mould/Die | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive interior | Leather grain, wood grain, fabric weave | Aesthetic appeal, haptics |
| Consumer electronics (mobiles, laptops) | Brushed metal, matte, micro-pyramids | Anti-fingerprint, anti-glare |
| Optical lens moulds | Sub-wavelength micro-lens array | Light diffusion, anti-reflection |
| Sheet-metal forming dies | Micro-grooves / cross-hatch (EDM) | Lubricant pockets, drawability |
| Medical implant moulds | Porous / nano-textures | Bio-compatibility, cell adhesion |
| Anti-counterfeit packaging | Micro-holograms, logos | Brand security |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Surface Structuring Methods

Surface structuring techniques are classified in KTU Module 3 under three broad families:

#### A. Subtractive (Material-Removal) Methods
The surface is *engraved* by controlled removal of material.

1. **Photo-Chemical Etching (PCM) / Chemical Milling**
2. **Laser Engraving / Laser Texturing (LT)**
3. **Electrical Discharge Texturing (EDT) — die texturing for rolls**
4. **Micro-milling / Micro-EDM**
5. **Abrasive / Sand Blasting (Stochastic)**
6. **Manual Engraving / Hand-Chasing (Artistic)**

#### B. Additive (Material-Deposition) Methods
A pattern is *built up* on the surface.

1. **Micro-3D-Printing (Two-Photon Polymerisation)**
2. **Electroforming (Ni-shells with texture)**
3. **Selective Laser Sintering / Melting (SLS/SLM)**

#### C. Formative / Replication Methods
A textured master is pressed, embossed, or stamped.

1. **Electroformed Replication Masters**
2. **Embossed Foil Inserts**
3. **Ni-Shim Insertion in Moulds**

---

### 2.2 Working Principle of the Major Processes

#### 2.2.1 Photo-Chemical Etching (PCM)
- The mould face is **coated with photoresist**, **exposed to UV light through a photo-mask** carrying the desired pattern, then **developed** and **etched in an acid bath** (FeCl₃ for steel, CuCl₂ for beryllium-copper).
- Etch depth is controlled by **time × etch rate**.

#### 2.2.2 Laser Texturing (LT) — *the KTU-favoured modern method*
- A **pulsed fibre laser** (commonly **1064 nm Nd:YAG** or **355 nm UV**) vaporises micro-cavities called *laser-induced periodic surface structures* (LIPSS).
- Patterns achievable: **dimples (cross-hatch), lines, spirals, logos, freeform 3D micro-features**.
- Advantages: **non-contact**, **no mask**, **high flexibility**, **suitable for 3D freeform mould surfaces**.

#### 2.2.3 Electrical Discharge Texturing (EDT)
- An **electrode (graphite or copper roll)** is pulsed against the workpiece in a **dielectric fluid** (EDM oil).
- Spark craters form a **stochastic matte surface** — extensively used for **cold-rolling mill work rolls** in sheet steel production.

#### 2.2.4 Sand/Abrasive Blasting
- Hard particles (Al₂O₃, glass beads, SiC) are accelerated by **compressed air** and impinge on the surface, creating a **stochastic matt texture**.
- Cheap, but **no controlled pattern**; used for *decorative matte* finishes.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **exam-relevant numerical core** of this topic. Memorise the variables and the units — they appear in KTU Part A and Part B questions almost every cycle.

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ra = (1/L) ∫₀ᴸ $\vert z(x) \vert$ dx** | z(x): profile height (µm), L: sampling length (mm) | Arithmetic mean line — *average deviation from mean line* |
| 2 | **Rz = (Rp1 + Rp2 + … + Rp5)/5 − (Rv1 + … + Rv5)/5** | Rp, Rv: peak and valley heights (µm) | Mean of the 5 highest peaks minus mean of 5 lowest valleys |
| 3 | **Rq = √[(1/L) ∫₀ᴸ z²(x) dx]** | z(x): profile height (µm) | RMS roughness — *amplifies the weight of deep scratches* |
| 4 | **Etch depth (PCM): d = r · t** | d: depth (µm), r: etch rate (µm/min), t: time (min) | Linear etch law; assumes constant chemistry |
| 5 | **Laser pulse fluence: F = E / A** | E: pulse energy (J), A: spot area (cm²) | Energy per unit area — *above ablation threshold* to vaporise material |
| 6 | **Ablation threshold: Fₜₕ = (2 · L · ρ · ΔT) / (1 − R)** | L: latent heat (J/g), ρ: density (g/cm³), ΔT: temp rise, R: reflectivity | Minimum fluence to remove material |
| 7 | **EDT crater volume: V = (π · d² · h) / 12** | d: crater diameter (µm), h: depth (µm) | Volume of single spark crater — *controls Ra* |
| 8 | **Surface area ratio: Sdr = (Aₐ / Aₚ) − 1** | Aₐ: actual area, Aₚ: projected area (mm²) | Areal (3D) parameter — extra area over flat |
| 9 | **Bearings: tp = (η₀ − η₁) / (η₀ − η₂) · 100%** | tp: bearing ratio, η: profile heights | Percentage of surface that *bears load* |
| 10 | **Stribeck parameter: η = (μ · v) / (p · a)** | μ: viscosity, v: speed, p: pressure, a: geometry | Used in textured-lubrication analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Don't confuse Ra with Rz!**
> In KTU exams, students frequently swap Ra and Rz. Remember:
> - **Ra = average** of all deviations.
> - **Rz = peak-to-valley range** (always *larger* than Ra for the same surface).
> - Typical ratio: **Rz ≈ 4 to 7 × Ra** for stochastic surfaces.

---

### 2.4 Selection Criteria — Which Method When?

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love a "compare-and-justify" question. Use this decision matrix in your answers.

| Criterion | PCM | Laser Texturing | EDT | Sand Blasting |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern fidelity | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ | ★ |
| Cost per m² | Medium | Medium-High | High | Low |
| Suitable for freeform 3D | Limited | **Yes** | Limited | Yes (uniformly) |
| Hardened tool steel (HRC ≥ 60) | Yes | **Yes** | Yes | Yes |
| Sub-micron resolution | Limited | **Yes (fs-laser)** | No | No |
| Mass production speed | Slow | **Fast** | Fast | Fast |
| Ecologically clean | No (acids) | **Yes** | No (oil) | No (dust) |
| Typical Ra range (µm) | 0.4 – 6.3 | 0.05 – 12 | 0.6 – 4.0 | 1.6 – 6.3 |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Concept

- **Automotive sheet-metal draw dies** are EDT-textured so that lubricant pockets trap oil and prevent galling during deep-drawing of high-strength-steel body panels.
- **Mobile phone back-covers** (faux-brushed look) are laser-textured on the steel mould insert, then injection-moulded with polycarbonate to replicate the texture — one mould can produce > 1 million identical textured parts.
- **Optical diffuser moulds** use laser-induced micro-lens arrays to scatter LED light evenly.
- **Medical implant moulds** use nano-texturing (via LIPSS) to promote osteoblast (bone-cell) adhesion on titanium surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Arithmetic Mean Roughness (Ra)

The Ra parameter is the **integral of the absolute vertical deviation of the surface profile from its mean line, averaged over the sampling length**.

**Step 1 — Define the mean line.**

The mean line is a reference line that divides the profile such that the area above it equals the area below it.

$$
A_{\text{above}} = A_{\text{below}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the deviation from the mean line.**

Let the surface profile be $z(x)$ and the mean line height be $\bar{z}$.

The vertical deviation at any point $x$ is:

$$
y(x) = z(x) - \bar{z}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the absolute value of the deviation.**

Since deviations are *both positive (peaks) and negative (valleys)*, we take the absolute value to capture both equally:

$$
\vert y(x) \vert = \vert z(x) - \bar{z} \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the sampling length L.**

The total "roughness area" is the integral of the absolute deviation over the sampling length:

$$
A_{\text{rough}} = \int_0^L \vert z(x) - \bar{z} \vert \, dx
$$

**Step 5 — Average by dividing by L.**

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert z(x) - \bar{z} \vert \, dx
$$

**Step 6 — Discrete form (used in profilometers).**

In practice, a profilometer samples $N$ discrete points spaced by $\Delta x$, so the integral becomes a summation:

$$
R_a \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_i - \bar{z} \vert
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> Because Ra uses **absolute values**, the peaks and valleys contribute equally. This is *both its strength* (simple, robust) and its *weakness* (it cannot distinguish a *spiky* surface from a *scratched* surface of the same average value). This is why Rsk and Rku are used as *supplementary* descriptors in modern mould-texture specifications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Etch Depth vs Time in Photo-Chemical Etching

**Step 1 — Chemical reaction assumption.**

In PCM, the dominant reaction for steel is:

$$
\text{Fe} + \text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2
$$

The reaction rate is limited by **diffusion of fresh etchant into the cavity and by-product removal**.

**Step 2 — Define etch rate.**

The etch rate $r$ is the depth of material removed per unit time, measured in µm/min.

$$
r = \frac{d(d_{\text{depth}})}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume constant etch rate.**

For a well-stirred, temperature-controlled bath, $r$ is constant.

$$
\frac{d(d_{\text{depth}})}{dt} = r = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over time.**

$$
d_{\text{depth}} = \int_0^t r \, dt = r \cdot t
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{\text{depth}} = r \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5 — Typical numerical value (for KTU problems).**

For H13 tool steel in **42 % FeCl₃ at 50 °C**, typical etch rate is:

$$
r \approx 25 \, \mu\text{m/min}
$$

So a 50 µm deep pocket requires:

$$
t = \frac{d}{r} = \frac{50 \, \mu\text{m}}{25 \, \mu\text{m/min}} = 2 \, \text{min}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Ablation Depth per Laser Pulse

**Step 1 — Energy balance per pulse.**

Each laser pulse deposits energy $E$ over spot area $A$. The material is removed if the absorbed fluence $F$ exceeds the ablation threshold $F_{\text{th}}$.

$$
F = \frac{E}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Ablation depth per pulse.**

Assuming the absorbed energy creates a melt/vapour layer of uniform density $\rho$ and latent heat of vaporisation $L_v$:

$$
d_{\text{pulse}} = \frac{F - F_{\text{th}}}{\rho \cdot (L_v + c_p \cdot \Delta T)}
$$

where $c_p$ is the specific heat and $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise from ambient to boiling.

**Step 3 — Simplification for metals (UV laser, short pulse).**

For short-pulse lasers, the thermal diffusion length is shorter than the absorption depth, so the heat-affected zone (HAZ) is minimal. The depth per pulse simplifies to the **logarithmic ablation law**:

$$
d_{\text{pulse}} = \alpha^{-1} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{F}{F_{\text{th}}} \right)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the optical absorption coefficient of the material at the laser wavelength.

**Step 4 — Total ablation depth for N overlapping pulses:**

$$
d_{\text{total}} = N \cdot d_{\text{pulse}} = N \cdot \alpha^{-1} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{F}{F_{\text{th}}} \right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:**
> The logarithmic ablation law explains *why doubling the pulse energy does not double the ablation depth* — it only increases it by **ln 2 ≈ 0.69 × α⁻¹** additional depth. Students who assume linear ablation will get the wrong answer.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Spark-Crater-Based Ra for EDT

**Step 1 — Geometry of an EDM spark crater.**

A single spark produces a roughly spherical-cap crater of diameter $d$ and depth $h$, with empirical ratio:

$$
h \approx \frac{d}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of one crater.**

$$
V_{\text{crater}} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot h}{12} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3}{36}
$$

**Step 3 — Crater density on the surface.**

If the discharge current $I$ and pulse-on time $t_{\text{on}}$ are constant, the number of craters per unit area is $\rho_c$ (craters/cm²).

**Step 4 — Resulting Ra for a stochastic EDT surface:**

$$
R_a \approx k \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{\text{on}}^{0.3}
$$

where $k$ is an empirical constant for the workpiece-dielectric combination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for Process Control:**
> - To *increase Ra* (rougher, more lubricant retention): **higher current** and **longer pulse-on time**.
> - To *decrease Ra* (smoother, mirror-like): lower current, shorter pulse, finer finishing EDM passes (often with **graphite electrodes** in place of copper).

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:**
A mould-cavity insert of H13 tool steel is to be chemically etched to a depth of 75 µm to form a logo recess. The etchant (FeCl₃ 42 %) at 48 °C gives an etch rate of 22 µm/min. The process uses a **10 % over-etch** to compensate for the lateral undercut (isotropic etching).

**(a)** Find the **etching time** required.
**(b)** Find the **lateral undercut** if the etch is fully isotropic.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Required depth (including 10 % over-etch):

$$
d_{\text{etch}} = 75 \, \mu\text{m} \times 1.10 = 82.5 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

Time required:

$$
t = \frac{d_{\text{etch}}}{r} = \frac{82.5 \, \mu\text{m}}{22 \, \mu\text{m/min}} = 3.75 \, \text{min}
$$

**Part (b):**

In isotropic etching, lateral undercut $\Delta x$ equals the vertical etch depth. Total undercut on each side:

$$
\Delta x = d_{\text{etch}} = 82.5 \, \mu\text{m} \approx 0.0825 \, \text{mm}
$$

If the original mask opening was $w_0 = 0.5$ mm, the final cavity width will be:

$$
w_{\text{final}} = w_0 + 2 \cdot \Delta x = 0.5 + 2(0.0825) = 0.665 \, \text{mm}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the *units* in the final answer. Examiners *specifically deduct* ½ mark for missing units in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
