<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Define)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define surface structuring. Differentiate it from surface finish.*

**Model Answer:**
Surface structuring is the **deliberate creation of specific geometric patterns** (lines, dimples, grains, logos, micro-channels) on the working surface of a mould or die, in order to impart *functional, aesthetic, optical or tribological* properties to the moulded part.

| Surface Finish | Surface Structuring |
|---|---|
| Refers to *overall smoothness* (Ra, Rz) of the surface | Refers to *specific geometric features* engraved on a surface |
| Achieved by grinding, polishing, lapping | Achieved by etching, laser, EDM, blasting, etc. |
| *Removes* peaks uniformly | *Adds/creates* deterministic or stochastic features |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for differentiation table, 1 for example)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any four surface structuring techniques used in mould manufacture and state one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**
| Method | One Advantage |
|---|---|
| 1. Photo-Chemical Etching (PCM) | High pattern fidelity and resolution (~10 µm) |
| 2. Laser Texturing (LT) | Non-contact; suitable for freeform 3D mould surfaces |
| 3. Electrical Discharge Texturing (EDT) | Mass-production friendly for rolling-mill rolls |
| 4. Sand/Abrasive Blasting | Lowest cost; simple operation |
| 5. *(optional)* Micro-3D Printing | Sub-micron resolution, true 3D freeform |

*(3 marks — ½ mark each for naming + ¼ for advantage)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**  *(PCM and Laser Texturing)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a **process flow diagram**, explain the **Photo-Chemical Etching (PCM)** method of surface structuring for mould cavities. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of **Laser Texturing (LT)**. Derive the relation between ablation depth per pulse and laser fluence, stating the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — PCM Process Flow:**

The PCM process consists of the following steps (each step = 1 mark for explanation + ½ for diagram annotation):

1. **Surface Preparation:** The mould insert is degreased and deoxidised to ensure clean adhesion of the photoresist.
2. **Photoresist Coating:** A UV-sensitive photoresist is spin-coated or dip-coated on the mould face and soft-baked.
3. **Mask Alignment:** A photomask carrying the desired pattern is placed in contact (or projection-aligned) with the coated insert.
4. **UV Exposure:** The assembly is exposed to UV light. Exposed regions (positive resist) become soluble in the developer; unexposed regions remain insoluble.
5. **Development:** The soluble resist is washed away, exposing bare metal in the pattern of the design.
6. **Etching:** The insert is dipped in an etchant bath — typically **FeCl₃ for steel, CuCl₂ for beryllium-copper** at 40–55 °C. The exposed metal dissolves; the protected metal remains untouched.
7. **Resist Stripping:** The remaining photoresist is stripped using a solvent, leaving the final patterned cavity.

**Advantages:**
- Excellent resolution (~10 µm) and edge fidelity
- No mechanical / thermal stress on the workpiece
- Can texture **pre-hardened** (HRC 60+) tool steel
- Lower tooling cost than EDM/laser for large areas

**Limitations:**
- Isotropic etching causes **lateral undercut** → over-etch correction needed
- Acid handling — environmental and safety hazards
- Not suitable for deep 3D freeform features
- Mask fabrication is expensive for small batches

---

**Part (b) — Laser Texturing Principle & Derivation:**

**Principle:**
A **pulsed laser beam** (typically fibre-laser at 1064 nm, or UV laser at 355 nm) is focused on the mould surface. The high peak-power density at the focal spot causes the metal to **ablate** (vaporise) locally, forming a micro-crater. By scanning the beam in a programmed path, any 2D pattern (dimples, lines, logos) can be written. By varying the energy and focus along the scan, **3D micro-features** can also be produced.

**Derivation:**

Let the pulse energy be $E$ and the focused spot area be $A$. The fluence is:

$$
F = \frac{E}{A}
$$

The absorbed fluence is $F_a = (1 - R) \cdot F$, where $R$ is the surface reflectivity at the laser wavelength.

Ablation occurs only when $F_a$ exceeds the ablation threshold $F_{\text{th}}$. For a short pulse, the heat-affected zone (HAZ) is negligible and the ablation depth per pulse follows the **logarithmic ablation law**:

$$
d_{\text{pulse}} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln\!\left( \frac{F_a}{F_{\text{th}}} \right) = \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln\!\left( \frac{(1 - R) F}{F_{\text{th}}} \right)
$$

where:
- $d_{\text{pulse}}$ = ablation depth per pulse (µm)
- $\alpha$ = optical absorption coefficient of the mould material (cm⁻¹)
- $F$ = incident laser fluence (J/cm²)
- $R$ = surface reflectivity (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $F_{\text{th}}$ = ablation threshold fluence (J/cm²) — the minimum fluence needed to vaporise the material

For $N$ overlapping pulses (used to deepen a single crater):

$$
d_{\text{total}} = N \cdot d_{\text{pulse}} = \frac{N}{\alpha} \ln\!\left( \frac{(1 - R) F}{F_{\text{th}}} \right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why logarithmic?**
> As the pulse removes material, the next pulse sees a *thinner* layer above the bulk; hence each additional pulse removes progressively *less* material. This is the *Beer–Lambert* behaviour of light in the ablated plume.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(EDT and Selection of Methods)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Electrical Discharge Texturing (EDT)** process used for surface structuring of cold-rolling mill rolls. Compare it with **sand blasting**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive **B-pillar draw die** for deep-drawing of high-strength steel is to be surface-structured. The customer requires:
- Ra = 1.6 ± 0.3 µm
- Lubricant retention pockets in a **cross-hatch** pattern
- Production of **5 die sets**

Recommend the most suitable structuring method and justify. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — EDT vs Sand Blasting:**

**EDT Process:**
- The roll rotates under a stationary **electrode** (copper or graphite) submerged in a **dielectric fluid** (EDM oil).
- A pulsed **DC voltage** (typically 50–200 V) is applied; each pulse generates a spark that locally melts and vaporises a tiny volume of roll material.
- The molten pool is quenched by the dielectric, forming a **spherical-cap crater**.
- The stochastic distribution of craters creates a uniform **matte texture**.
- The discharge current $I$ and pulse-on time $t_{\text{on}}$ control the crater size and hence the resulting Ra.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | EDT | Sand Blasting |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern type | Stochastic, controlled Ra | Stochastic, less controlled |
| Typical Ra range | 0.6 – 4.0 µm | 1.6 – 6.3 µm |
| Suitable for large rolls | **Yes** (continuous process) | Limited by nozzle stand-off |
| Cost for large area | **Lower per m²** | Higher (labour + consumable) |
| Dielectric / abrasive fluid | EDM oil | Al₂O₃ or glass beads + air |
| Environmental | Oil handling needed | Dust extraction needed |
| Pattern customisation | **No** (uniform matte) | No (uniform matte) |

---

**Part (b) — Method Selection (KTU Real-World Scenario):**

**Recommended Method: Photo-Chemical Etching (PCM) with a brass mask.**

**Justification (each bullet ≈ 1 mark):**

1. **Pattern requirement (cross-hatch) is deterministic** → stochastic methods (EDT, sand blasting) cannot produce a cross-hatch pattern. **Eliminates EDT, sand blasting.**
2. **Material is hardened draw-die steel (HRC ≥ 58)** → laser texturing is possible but expensive; PCM is well-established for such steels. **PCM fits.**
3. **Batch size = 5 die sets** → mask cost is *amortised* over 5 dies, making PCM economical. (For a single die, laser would be cheaper; for 100 dies, laser would dominate again.)
4. **Ra = 1.6 µm** is *intermediate* — achievable with PCM by controlling etch time. Sand blasting produces Ra > 1.6 µm typically.
5. **Lubricant-retention pockets** must be of defined *geometry* (cross-hatch valleys act as oil reservoirs) — only PCM can replicate the customer's cross-hatch CAD drawing.
6. **Hardness preservation** — PCM does not induce HAZ or residual stress; the die's heat-treated hardness is preserved.
7. **Repeatability** — once the mask is made, all 5 die sets are *identical*, ensuring part-to-part consistency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't** recommend laser for "lubricant pocket" needs without specifying that *cross-hatch dimple arrays* are the *only* deterministic micro-feature easily produced by laser. Examiners expect a *reasoned* choice.
> 2. **Don't** write "PCM is best" — *always compare* against at least 2 alternatives (the question rewards comparison thinking).
> 3. **Don't** forget to state the *mask material* (brass or chrome-on-glass) and the *etchant* (FeCl₃ for steel) — these are 1-mark items that students routinely skip.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Surface Structuring**

- [x] **Definition:** Deliberate creation of geometric features (not just smoothness) on mould/die working surfaces.
- [x] **Three families:** Subtractive, Additive, Formative/Replication.
- [x] **Four dominant industrial methods:** PCM, Laser Texturing, EDT, Sand Blasting.
- [x] **Ra** = arithmetic mean deviation; **Rz** = peak-to-valley range; **Rz ≈ 4–7 × Ra**.
- [x] **PCM etch depth formula:** $d = r \cdot t$ — linear with time; *isotropic undercut = depth*.
- [x] **Laser ablation law is logarithmic, not linear:** $d = \alpha^{-1} \cdot \ln(F / F_{\text{th}})$.
- [x] **EDT Ra increases with current and pulse-on time** to the power of 0.3–0.4.
- [x] **Stochastic vs Deterministic:** Sand blasting & EDT = stochastic; PCM & laser = deterministic patterns.
- [x] **Pattern selection rule:** Lubricant retention in draw dies → cross-hatch PCM or laser; cosmetic grain → PCM or sand blast; optical diffusion → laser LIPSS.
- [x] **Standard:** All parameters are reported per **ISO 4287** (profile) and **ISO 25178** (areal).
- [x] **Material compatibility:** All four methods work on HRC ≥ 60 hardened tool steel.
- [x] **Cost trade-off:** Mask-based methods (PCM) for *small batch*; mask-less methods (laser) for *medium batch*; stochastic methods (EDT) for *large continuous area* (rolling-mill rolls).
- [x] **Hardness preservation:** PCM and laser preserve base-material hardness (no HAZ for cold processes / ultra-short pulses); grinding must precede structuring on hardened steel.
- [x] **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Confusing *Ra* with *Rz*, and forgetting the *units (µm)* in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical and Physical Vapour Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chemical and Physical Vapour Deposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)** is a thin-film deposition process in which volatile chemical precursors (gaseous compounds) are introduced into a reaction chamber where they undergo **chemical reactions** on or near a heated substrate surface to produce a non-volatile, solid thin-film coating. The volatile by-products are exhausted from the chamber.

In the context of mould and die manufacturing, CVD is the principal route for depositing **wear-resistant ceramic coatings** such as **Titanium Carbide (TiC)**, **Titanium Nitride (TiN)**, **Chromium Carbide (CrC)**, and **Diamond-Like Carbon (DLC)** onto punches, drawing dies, plastic injection mould cores, and stamping dies.

### 1.2 Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)** is a thin-film deposition process in which a target material is converted from a condensed phase (solid or liquid) into the vapour phase by **physical means** (thermal evaporation, sputtering, or arc ablation) and transported under vacuum to a substrate where it condenses to form a thin coating — typically with no chemical reaction between source and substrate.

PVD is widely used for **low-temperature coating** of hardened and tempered tool steels used in moulds and dies, preserving substrate hardness while gaining surface hardness values exceeding **2000–3000 HV**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Icing the Cake" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine two ways to ice a cake.
> - **CVD** is like **baking the icing on the cake in the oven** — the sugar (precursor gas) is transformed by heat (chemical reaction) into a hard glaze that bonds permanently to the surface. The chemistry of the sugar changes.
> - **PVD** is like **spraying powdered sugar through a sieve onto a cold cake in a vacuum chamber** — the sugar (target atoms) is physically knocked loose and lands on the surface. The sugar remains chemically identical to its original form; it just changes location.

### 1.4 Why These Processes Matter for Moulds and Dies

Moulds and dies operate under extreme conditions: cyclic loading, abrasive wear, adhesive wear, galling, corrosion from polymer additives, and thermal fatigue. Bulk hardened steel (HRC 60–62) is often insufficient at the surface. Surface engineering via CVD/PVD pushes surface hardness to **2000–4000 HV**, drastically improving:

- **Tool life** (often 3× to 10× improvement)
- **Release properties** of plastic moulded parts
- **Dimensional stability** of precision dies
- **Resistance to adhesive wear and galling**

### 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics

| Property | CVD Typical | PVD Typical |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **800 °C – 1050 °C** | **150 °C – 600 °C** |
| Coating Thickness | **5 μm – 25 μm** | **1 μm – 8 μm** |
| Adhesion | **Excellent** (metallurgical bond) | **Good to Excellent** (depends on interlayer) |
| Surface Finish | **Requires post-polish** | **As-coated finish acceptable** |
| Distortion Risk | **Higher** (high temperature) | **Minimal** (low temperature) |
| Hounsfield Equivalent Hardness | **1800–3500 HV** | **2000–4000 HV** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CVD Process — Step-by-Step Mechanism

CVD is governed by a sequence of **seven coupled transport and reaction steps**. Mastery of this sequence is essential for KTU ESE questions.

1. **Mass Transport of Reactants:** Precursor gases (e.g., TiCl₄, CH₄, N₂, H₂) flow from the gas inlet into the reaction chamber via forced convection or diffusion.
2. **Gas-Phase Reaction:** Homogeneous reactions may occur in the gas phase, forming intermediate species (e.g., TiCl₄ + ½N₂ + 2H₂ → TiN + 4HCl).
3. **Diffusion to Substrate Surface:** Reactant species diffuse through the boundary layer (stagnant gas layer) adjacent to the hot substrate.
4. **Adsorption on Surface:** Atoms/molecules physically adsorb (physisorb) and then chemically adsorb (chemisorb) at favourable nucleation sites.
5. **Surface Diffusion & Reaction:** Adsorbed species migrate along the surface and undergo surface reactions to form the deposit.
6. **Nucleation and Film Growth:** Stable nuclei form, coalesce, and propagate laterally to create a continuous film.
7. **Desorption & Exhaust of By-products:** Volatile by-products (e.g., HCl) desorb, diffuse out of the boundary layer, and are swept away by the gas flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **rate-limiting step** determines whether the CVD process is in a **surface-reaction-controlled regime** (low temperature) or a **mass-transport-controlled regime** (high temperature). For die-coating applications, surface-reaction control is preferred for uniform thickness.

### 2.2 CVD Process Variants

| Variant | Acronym | Pressure | Temperature | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric Pressure CVD | APCVD | 1 atm | 850–1050 °C | Simple, no vacuum needed |
| Low Pressure CVD | LPCVD | 10–100 Pa | 600–850 °C | Better uniformity, conformal step coverage |
| Plasma Enhanced CVD | PECVD | 1–500 Pa | 250–400 °C | Plasma assists reaction, lower temp |
| Metal-Organic CVD | MOCVD | 1–100 mbar | 500–800 °C | Uses metal-organic precursors |
| Hot Filament CVD | HFCVD | 1–10 kPa | 700–900 °C | Used for **diamond coatings on dies** |
| Thermally Activated CVD | TACVD | varies | 600–1000 °C | Conventional hot-wall reactor |

### 2.3 The PVD Process — Step-by-Step Mechanism

Unlike CVD, PVD is fundamentally a **physical atom-by-atom transfer** process:

1. **Vapour Generation:** A target material is vaporized by one of three mechanisms:
   - **Thermal Evaporation** — resistive heating, electron-beam heating, or laser ablation
   - **Sputtering** — energetic ion bombardment knocks atoms loose
   - **Cathodic Arc** — high-current, low-voltage arc vaporizes target material with high ionization
2. **Vapour Transport:** Vapour atoms travel ballistically through a vacuum chamber (pressure typically **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ Pa**).
3. **Reactive Gas Introduction (optional):** For nitride or oxide coatings, N₂ or O₂ is introduced to form compounds (e.g., Ti + N₂ → TiN).
4. **Film Nucleation & Growth on Substrate:** Atoms condense, nucleate, and grow into a continuous film.
5. **Ion Bombardment (Bias Enhancement):** Substrate is often biased negatively (–50 V to –200 V) to draw positive ions, densifying the film and improving adhesion.

### 2.4 PVD Process Variants

| Variant | Vaporization Method | Ionization % | Typical Coatings |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Evaporation | Resistive/EB heating | <1 % | Al, Ag, Au |
| Electron Beam PVD (EB-PVD) | Focused e-beam | <5 % | MCrAlY thermal barriers |
| Sputter Deposition (DC/RF) | Ion bombardment | ~5 % | TiN, CrN, TiAlN |
| Magnetron Sputtering | Magnet-enhanced plasma | ~10 % | Wear coatings |
| Cathodic Arc PVD (Arc-PVD) | Vacuum arc | **60–100 %** | **TiN, TiAlN, AlCrN, DLC** |
| Ion Plating | Sputtering + bias | 10–30 % | TiN, CrN on tools |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For mould and die applications, **Cathodic Arc PVD** and **Magnetron Sputter PVD** dominate commercial production. The high ionization fraction in cathodic arc deposition (used by companies like Oerlikon Balzers, CemeCon, and Voestalpine eifeler) produces **exceptionally dense, adherent coatings** ideal for stamping, drawing, and injection moulding tooling.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following equations are essential for numerical and analytical questions in the ESE.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i) Arrhenius CVD Growth Rate:} \quad R_g = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T}\right) \\
&\text{where } A = \text{pre-exponential factor (m/s)}, \; E_a = \text{activation energy (J/mol)}, \\
& R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}, \; T = \text{substrate temperature (K)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(ii) Mean Free Path (vacuum):} \quad \lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi d^{2}\, P} \\
&k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}, \; d = \text{effective molecular diameter (m)}, \\
& P = \text{pressure (Pa)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(iii) Langmuir Evaporation Flux (PVD):} \quad \Phi_e = \dfrac{p_{sat}(T)}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \\
&p_{sat}(T) = \text{saturation vapour pressure (Pa)}, \; m = \text{atomic mass (kg)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(iv) Sputter Yield:} \quad Y = \dfrac{N_{ejected}}{N_{incident}} \\
&Y \text{ is dimensionless; typical } Y \text{ for Ti under Ar}^{+}: 0.5 \text{ to } 1.5.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(v) Hertz-Knudsen Deposition Rate:} \quad \dot{m} = \alpha \cdot \Phi_e \cdot m \cdot A_s \\
&\alpha = \text{condensation coefficient (0–1)}, \; A_s = \text{substrate area (m}^{2}\text{)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(vi) Sticking Coefficient: } S_c = \dfrac{\text{atoms incorporated into film}}{\text{atoms arriving at substrate}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Sheet Metal Stamping Dies:** TiN or TiCN PVD coatings extend die life by 4–8× for drawing high-strength steel (HSS) panels.
- **Aluminium Die-Casting Dies:** CrN and CrAlN PVD coatings resist soldering and thermal fatigue.
- **Plastic Injection Mould Cores:** DLC and TiAlN coatings improve release and resist corrosive flame retardants.
- **Wire Drawing Dies:** CVD-diamond coatings on tungsten carbide nibs reduce friction and wear dramatically.
- **Forging Dies:** TiC/Al₂O₃ multilayer CVD coatings handle the high-temperature impact loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Temperature Dependence of CVD Growth Rate

The rate-limiting step in surface-reaction-controlled CVD obeys an **Arrhenius-type law**. Derivation from the activated complex theory:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Rate-limiting surface reaction} \\
&\text{Reactant}(g) \xrightleftharpoons{\Delta G^{\ddagger}} \text{Activated Complex}^{\ddagger} \rightarrow \text{Products}(s) \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Equilibrium concentration of activated complex} \\
&K^{\ddagger} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right) = \dfrac{[\text{Complex}^{\ddagger}]}{[\text{Reactant}]} \\
&\textbf{Step 3: Substituting Gibbs free energy } \Delta G^{\ddagger} = \Delta H^{\ddagger} - T \Delta S^{\ddagger} \\
&K^{\ddagger} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta S^{\ddagger}}{R}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right) \\
&\textbf{Step 4: Reaction rate proportional to } K^{\ddagger} \text{ times frequency } \nu \\
&R_g = \nu \cdot K^{\ddagger} = \underbrace{\nu \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta S^{\ddagger}}{R}\right)}_{A} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right) \\
&\textbf{Step 5: Since } \Delta H^{\ddagger} \approx E_a, \text{ we arrive at:} \\
&R_g = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample Numerical Evaluation:**

For deposition of TiN from TiCl₄ + ½N₂ + 2H₂ at **T = 1000 °C = 1273 K**, with $E_a = 180 \; \text{kJ/mol}$ and $A = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \; \mu\text{m/h}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&R_g = (1.5 \times 10^{6}) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{180{,}000}{8.314 \times 1273}\right) \\
&R_g = (1.5 \times 10^{6}) \cdot \exp(-17.005) \\
&R_g = (1.5 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (3.997 \times 10^{-8}) \\
&R_g \approx 60 \; \mu\text{m/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

This explains why commercial CVD TiN coatings of **5–10 μm** can be deposited in roughly **5–10 minutes** at industrial throughput.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Free Path and Vacuum Quality in PVD

For atoms to travel **ballistically** from target to substrate in PVD without scattering, $\lambda$ must exceed the target-to-substrate distance (typically **10–30 cm**).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Molecular collision cross-section} \\
&\sigma = \pi d^{2} \quad \text{(hard-sphere model)} \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Mean free path between collisions} \\
&\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}\, n\, \sigma} \\
&\textbf{Step 3: Expressing } n \text{ via ideal gas law } n = \dfrac{P}{k_B T} \\
&\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi d^{2}\, P} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample Numerical Evaluation:**

For Ar atoms ($d \approx 3.66 \times 10^{-10}$ m) at **T = 500 K**, what is the mean free path at $P = 0.1 \; \text{Pa}$ and $P = 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}$?

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Case (a) } P = 0.1 \; \text{Pa:} \\
&\lambda = \dfrac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(500)}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi (3.66 \times 10^{-10})^{2}\, (0.1)} \\
&\lambda = \dfrac{6.905 \times 10^{-21}}{5.939 \times 10^{-20}} \\
&\lambda \approx 0.116 \; \text{m} \;(\approx 11.6 \; \text{cm}) \\
&\textbf{Case (b) } P = 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa:} \\
&\lambda \approx 11.6 \; \text{m} \;(\text{>> target-to-substrate distance})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation justifies why **PVD requires a high vacuum**: at low pressures the mean free path greatly exceeds chamber dimensions, ensuring energetic atoms arrive at the substrate without losing energy in collisions, producing dense, adherent films.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | CVD | PVD |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Physics** | Chemical reaction | Physical vapor transport |
| **Substrate Temperature** | 600–1050 °C | 150–600 °C |
| **Pressure Regime** | 10 Pa – 1 atm | 10⁻⁵ Pa – 10⁻¹ Pa |
| **Coating Adhesion** | Metallurgical (strongest) | Mechanical + ion-mixed (very strong) |
| **Coating Density** | 95–100 % theoretical | 70–95 % theoretical |
| **Coating Uniformity** | Excellent (conformal) | Good (line-of-sight) |
| **Complex Geometry** | Conformal coating | Requires rotation/fixturing |
| **Throughput** | High (batch) | Moderate |
| **Capital Cost** | High | Moderate |
| **Tool Steel Compatibility** | Requires tempering > CVD temp | Compatible with tempered dies |
| **Typical Coating** | TiC, TiN, Al₂O₃, Diamond | TiN, TiCN, TiAlN, CrN, DLC |
| **Substrate Distortion** | **Possible** | **Negligible** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CVD Reactor Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1([Precursor Source Tank TiCl4 Liquid]) --> node2[Mass Flow Controller MFC1]
    node3([Carrier Gas H2 Cylinder]) --> node2
    node4([Reactive Gas N2 Cylinder]) --> node5[Mass Flow Controller MFC2]
    node2 --> node6[Vaporizer Mixer Assembly]
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7[Hot Wall CVD Reactor Chamber]
    node8([Die or Mould Component on Susceptor]) --> node7
    node7 --> node9[Coating Deposition Zone T 900 to 1000 C]
    node9 --> node10[Gas Exhaust HCl Byproduct]
    node10 --> node11[Scrubber and Neutralizer]
    node11 --> node12([Safe Exhaust to Atmosphere])
    node7 --> node13[Wall Heaters and Temperature Control]
    node13 --> node7
    node9 --> node14[CVD Coating Layer TiN TiC]
```

### 4.2 PVD Cathodic Arc Coating Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Cathodic Arc Source Titanium Target]) --> nodeB[Arc Ignition at Low Voltage High Current]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Vapour Plume with High Ionization 60 to 100 Percent]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Reactive Gas Inlet N2 for TiN]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Vacuum Chamber 10 power minus 3 to 10 power minus 5 Pa]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Substrate Holder with Planetary Rotation]
    nodeG([Die Component Mounted on Holder]) --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH[Negative Bias Supply minus 50 to minus 200 V]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Ion Bombardment and Film Densification]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Coating Growth on Die Surface]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Vacuum Pumping System Cryo plus Turbo]
    nodeJ --> nodeL([Finished Coated Die Tool])
```

### 4.3 Process Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS([Mould or Die Surface Coating Requirement]) --> nodeQ1{Is Substrate Tempered below 600 C?}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ2{Is Coating Complex 3D Geometry?}
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeCVD1[Choose CVD Process]
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeCVD1
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodePVD1[Choose PVD Sputtering]
    nodeCVD1 --> nodeCVD2{Is Extreme Hardness above 3000 HV Needed?}
    nodeCVD2 -- Yes --> nodeDIAM[Choose CVD Diamond Coating]
    nodeCVD2 -- No --> nodeTiN[Choose CVD TiN or TiCN]
    nodePVD1 --> nodePVD2{Is High Temperature Stability Required?}
    nodePVD2 -- Yes --> nodeAlCrN[Choose PVD AlCrN or TiAlN]
    nodePVD2 -- No --> nodeGenTiN[Choose PVD TiN Standard]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block Architecture — CVD vs PVD Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CVD_BLOCK[CVD Process Block]
        CVD1[Precursor Gas Tank] --> CVD2[Heated Reactor Chamber]
        CVD2 --> CVD3[Chemical Reaction Zone]
        CVD3 --> CVD4[Byproduct Exhaust]
    end
    subgraph PVD_BLOCK[PVD Process Block]
        PVD1[Target Material Cathode] --> PVD2[Vacuum Chamber]
        PVD2 --> PVD3[Vapour Generation Sputter or Arc]
        PVD3 --> PVD4[Substrate with Bias]
    end
    CVD3 --> COAT1[CVD Coating Growth]
    PVD3 --> COAT2[PVD Coating Growth]
    COAT1 --> DIE([Coated Die or Mould])
    COAT2 --> DIE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** Define Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD). List any **two advantages** and **two limitations** of CVD in mould and die manufacturing.

**Model Answer:**

> **CVD Definition:** Chemical Vapour Deposition is a thin-film deposition technique in which one or more volatile precursors react chemically on or near a heated substrate surface inside a reaction chamber, producing a solid, adherent coating while volatile by-products are removed by gas flow.
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. Excellent **conformal step coverage** — uniform coating on complex 3D mould geometries.
> 2. Strong **metallurgical adhesion** due to diffusion bonding at high temperature.
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. **High operating temperature (800–1050 °C)** causes distortion and softening of pre-hardened tool steels.
> 2. Requires **post-coating polishing** because the surface finish replicates the substrate roughness plus grain roughness.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Distinguish between **Thermal Evaporation PVD** and **Cathodic Arc PVD** with respect to ionization fraction, deposition rate, and die-coating applicability.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Thermal Evaporation PVD | Cathodic Arc PVD |
|---|---|---|
| Vaporization Mechanism | Resistive/EB heating | High-current arc on target |
| Ionization Fraction | <1 % | **60–100 %** |
| Deposition Rate | Low | **High** |
| Film Density | Porous columnar | **Very dense, adherent** |
| Die-coating Applicability | Limited (decorative only) | **Excellent (industrial standard)** |

> Cathodic Arc PVD is preferred for mould and die coatings because the highly ionized vapour produces **denser films** with **better adhesion** due to ion bombardment when substrate bias is applied.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step mechanism of the CVD process** with a neat schematic of a typical hot-wall CVD reactor used for die coating. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **process variables that influence the quality, adhesion, and microstructure** of a CVD coating on a die surface. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The CVD process proceeds through the following sequential steps inside a hot-wall reactor:

1. **Gas Delivery (1 Mark):** Volatile precursors — typically TiCl₄ (liquid source vaporized in a bubbler), CH₄ or N₂, and H₂ carrier — are metered by **Mass Flow Controllers (MFCs)** into the reactor inlet.

2. **Mass Transport & Gas-Phase Reaction (1 Mark):** The gas mixture enters the heated chamber where homogeneous gas-phase reactions may form intermediate species such as TiCl₃ or TiCl₂.

3. **Diffusion Through Boundary Layer (1 Mark):** Reactant molecules diffuse across the stagnant **thermal boundary layer** adjacent to the hot substrate (susceptor carrying the die).

4. **Adsorption and Surface Reaction (1 Mark):** Molecules adsorb on the die surface, where heterogeneous surface reactions occur (e.g., TiCl₄ + ½N₂ + 2H₂ → TiN(s) + 4HCl↑).

5. **Nucleation and Film Growth (1 Mark):** Stable nuclei form, coalesce, and propagate laterally to build a continuous TiN film of **5–10 μm** thickness.

6. **Desorption and Exhaust (1 Mark):** Volatile by-products (HCl) desorb and are swept out to a scrubber.

7. **Schematic Explanation (1 Mark):** The hot-wall reactor consists of a quartz tube externally heated by resistance furnaces; the dies are stacked vertically inside; thermocouples monitor temperature; gas inlet at one end, exhaust at the other.

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Process Variable | Influence on Coating Quality |
|---|---|
| **Substrate Temperature** | Controls rate-limiting step; low T → rough/amorphous, high T → coarse grains |
| **Reactor Pressure** | Lower pressure (LPCVD) improves uniformity and reduces gas-phase nucleation |
| **Gas Flow Rates & Ratio** | Determines stoichiometry — TiCl₄:N₂ ratio controls TiN vs TiC formation |
| **Gas Velocity** | Affects boundary layer thickness and uniformity across multiple dies |
| **Reactor Geometry** | Influences depletion effects and uniform heating |
| **Substrate Preparation** | Cleanliness, surface roughness, and pre-nitriding directly affect adhesion |
| **Cooling Rate** | Slow cooling minimizes thermal stress and prevents cracking |

> The optimal TiN CVD coating on an H13 die steel typically uses **T ≈ 950–1000 °C, P ≈ 50–100 mbar, TiCl₄:H₂:N₂ ratio ≈ 1:4:1**, achieving hardness of **1800–2400 HV**.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the aid of a functional schematic, describe the **Cathodic Arc PVD process** and explain why it is the preferred PVD method for die coatings. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare CVD and PVD processes with respect to **substrate temperature, adhesion mechanism, coating uniformity on 3D moulds, and effect on die dimensions**. Conclude with a justified recommendation for coating an **H13 hot-work die steel plastic injection mould core**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Cathodic Arc PVD Mechanism (5 Marks):**

1. **Arc Ignition:** A high current (50–200 A) at low voltage (15–30 V) is struck between a **cathodic target** (e.g., pure Ti or TiAl alloy) and an anode. The arc spot is a few microns in size and moves randomly over the target surface.

2. **Vapour Generation:** At the cathode spot, the target material is vaporized **with extreme ionization (60–100 %)**. This is the defining advantage of cathodic arc PVD.

3. **Macroparticle Ejection:** Liquid droplets ("macros") may be ejected along with the vapour; these are filtered out using a **magnetic plasma duct (90° filter)** in advanced systems.

4. **Reactive Gas Introduction:** N₂ gas is bled into the chamber to form **TiN, TiCN, or TiAlN** compounds with the metal vapour.

5. **Substrate Bias:** The die is held at a **negative bias of –50 V to –200 V**, drawing positive metal ions toward it with high energy. This causes **ion bombardment**, densifies the growing film, and creates a **graded interface** that enhances adhesion.

6. **Planetary Rotation:** The die rotates on a planetary fixture to ensure uniform coating on all surfaces.

**Why Preferred for Dies (2 Marks):**
- High ionization = dense, low-porosity films.
- Strong adhesion due to ion mixing at the interface.
- Lower deposition temperatures (**400–500 °C**) preserve die hardness.
- Excellent for **sharp-edge punches** and **drawing dies** where edge retention is critical.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Criterion | CVD | PVD (Cathodic Arc) |
|---|---|---|
| Substrate Temperature | 800–1050 °C | 400–500 °C |
| Adhesion Mechanism | Diffusion bonding (metallurgical) | Ion mixing + mechanical |
| Coating on 3D Mould | Conformal, all surfaces | Line-of-sight, requires rotation |
| Dimensional Distortion | Possible (high T) | Negligible |

**Recommendation (2 Marks):**

> For an **H13 hot-work die steel plastic injection mould core** (already hardened to HRC 50–54), **PVD is strongly recommended** because:
> 1. The lower deposition temperature (~450 °C) preserves the through-thickness hardness of H13, avoiding re-tempering embrittlement.
> 2. The high dimensional accuracy required for mould cavities (typically ±5 μm) is maintained, since PVD does not cause distortion.
> 3. A **TiN or TiAlN PVD coating** of 2–4 μm provides **>2000 HV** surface hardness, excellent polymer release, and resistance to corrosive additives.
>
> CVD would require tempering the H13 above its tempering temperature, causing softening and distortion — **unsuitable for precision injection moulds**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse PVD with painting or electroplating.** Examiners award zero credit for non-vapour-phase processes.
> 2. **Always state the temperature and pressure regimes** in any CVD/PVD comparison question — students frequently omit these and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Distinguish "line-of-sight" PVD from "conformal" CVD** when discussing 3D mould coverage.
> 4. For PVD, **never** describe only thermal evaporation; mention **sputtering or arc**, which are the actual industrial processes.
> 5. In numerical problems, **units must be consistent** — convert μm/h to m/s if comparing with deposition rate equations.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CVD = Chemical reaction at high T (800–1050 °C); conformal coating; metallurgical adhesion; risk of distortion.**
- **PVD = Physical vapor transport at low T (150–600 °C); line-of-sight unless fixtures rotated; preserves substrate hardness.**
- **Cathodic Arc PVD is the industrial standard for moulds and dies** due to high ionization, dense films, and excellent adhesion.
- **Key equation:** CVD growth $R_g = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$; Mean free path $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P)$.
- **Coating hardness:** TiN ≈ 2000–2400 HV; TiC ≈ 2800–3500 HV; TiAlN ≈ 2800–3300 HV; CVD Diamond ≈ 8000–10000 HV; DLC ≈ 1500–3000 HV.
- **PVD requires high vacuum** so that mean free path >> target-to-substrate distance (≥10 cm).
- **CVD by-products (HCl) must be neutralized** before exhaust — environmental and safety concern.
- **For tempered die steels (HRC ≥ 50), PVD is preferred**; for carbide or ceramic substrates, CVD is acceptable.
- **Typical coating thickness:** CVD = 5–25 μm, PVD = 1–8 μm.
- **Industries using these coatings:** automotive stamping, sheet metal drawing, plastic injection moulding, die casting, forging, extrusion, wire drawing.
- **Recent trend:** **Multilayer and nanocomposite coatings** (e.g., TiAlN/AlCrN superlattices, AlTiN/Si₃N₄ nanocomposites) for next-generation die performance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CAM Mould Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CAM Mould Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing) Mould Making** is the integrated, software-driven methodology used to manufacture moulds, dies, and tooling inserts by translating a digitally created 3D model (from a CAD package) into automated, computer-controlled machining instructions (G-code / M-code) that drive subtractive, additive, or hybrid machine tools such as **CNC milling centres, CNC EDM (die-sink and wire-cut), CNC turning centres, and rapid-prototyping systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the 2024 scheme, CAM mould making is no longer treated as just "programming a CNC." It is a **digital thread workflow** that begins with the mould-flow analysed CAD model and ends with a hardened, polished, ready-for-trial mould cavity. The examiner expects students to understand this *end-to-end digital pipeline*, not merely the operation of any single machine.

Formally, the CAM mould-making pipeline can be expressed as:

$$\text{CAD Model} \xrightarrow{\text{CAM Software}} \text{Toolpaths (CL-DATA)} \xrightarrow{\text{Post-Processor}} \text{NC Code} \xrightarrow{\text{CNC / EDM}} \text{Mould Cavity}$$

Where **CL-DATA** stands for *Cutter Location Data* — the intermediate, machine-independent description of the cutting tool's spatial trajectory.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Digital Sculptor + Robotic Carpenter":**
> Imagine you are an expert sculptor who can carve a perfect wooden statue. Now imagine you never actually touch the wood yourself. Instead, you write a very detailed instruction manual in a language the robotic carpenter understands, telling it exactly *where* to cut, *how deep* to cut, *how fast* to move, and *which tool* to hold. CAM mould making works exactly like this. The **CAM programmer** is the sculptor who plans every cut, the **CAM software** is the translator that converts plans into the instruction manual, and the **CNC machine** is the robotic carpenter that performs the work — flawlessly, repetitively, 24/7.

Another helpful analogy:
- **Conventional Mould Making** = Driving a car using only a paper map and verbal directions (manual, error-prone, depends on the skill of the craftsman).
- **CAM Mould Making** = Driving a car using a **GPS with pre-loaded coordinates** — the route is pre-computed, optimised, and verified before the journey begins.

This is why modern mould-making shops quote lead times in **weeks rather than months**, and achieve dimensional accuracies of **±0.005 mm to ±0.01 mm** consistently.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology Used in CAM Mould Making

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | Closeness of machined dimension to nominal | **±0.005 mm – ±0.05 mm** |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | Arithmetic mean roughness of cavity | **0.05 µm – 1.6 µm** |
| **Spindle Speed (N)** | Rotational speed of cutting tool | **5,000 – 40,000 rpm** |
| **Feed Rate (F)** | Linear travel of tool per minute | **500 – 6,000 mm/min** |
| **MRR** | Material Removal Rate | **cm³/min** or **mm³/min** |
| **TWR** | Tool Wear Ratio | dimensionless |

---

## 1.4 Scope of CAM in the Mould Industry

The following operations are routinely executed under the CAM umbrella for mould fabrication:

1. **3-axis to 5-axis CNC milling** of cavity and core blocks.
2. **CNC EDM (Die-Sinking EDM)** for sharp internal corners and hardened-steel features.
3. **Wire-EDM (WEDM)** for punches, sliders, and intricate profiles.
4. **CNC cylindrical / surface / profile grinding** for finishing.
5. **CNC EDM Drilling** (small-hole drilling) for ejector pin holes and cooling channels.
6. **Additive CAM** — Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) for conformal cooling inserts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CAM Mould-Making Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The CAM workflow in mould making can be broken into **seven sequential logical stages**. Each stage feeds the next, and a defect in any one propagates downstream.

### Stage 1 — CAD Model Receipt and Verification
The mould designer delivers a **STEP / IGES / Parasolid** file. The CAM engineer verifies:
- Model is **watertight** (no gaps, no overlapping surfaces).
- Drawing unit is consistent (typically **millimetres**).
- Draft angles, parting lines, and undercut features are correctly modelled.

> [!NOTE]
> *Why this matters:* The CAM software mathematically slices the model into horizontal layers (Z-level roughing) or builds iso-scallop tool paths. A single hole in the model causes the tool to plunge into empty space and possibly break the cutter.

### Stage 2 — Workpiece Setup Definition (Stock & Fixture)
The CAM programmer defines:
- **Stock geometry** (raw block dimensions, e.g., 250 mm × 200 mm × 80 mm P20 steel).
- **Fixture / work-holding strategy** (vice, magnetic chuck, vacuum table, 4th-axis tombstone).
- **Datum / work coordinate system (WCS)** — usually on top-centre of the stock with Z+ pointing up.

### Stage 3 — Machining Strategy Selection
Different regions of the mould require different strategies:

| Region | CAM Strategy | Reason |
|--------|--------------|--------|
| Bulk cavity volume | **Z-Level Roughing / Plunge Roughing** | Fast removal of large stock |
| Steep walls (drafted faces) | **Contour / Parallel Finishing** | Maintains draft angle |
| Shallow floors | **Steep-and-Shallow / Scallop Finishing** | Uniform cusp height |
| Sharp internal corners | **Leaves stock → EDM** | End mills cannot cut sharp internal radii |
| Hardened features | **Die-Sinking EDM** | Carbide or HSS tools cannot machine HRC > 60 efficiently |
| Through slots & punches | **Wire-EDM (2-axis / 4-axis)** | High precision, no mechanical force |

### Stage 4 — Tool Selection and Holder Definition
The CAM library is populated with:
- **Ball-end mills** (Ø2 – Ø20 mm) for 3D free-form surfaces.
- **Bull-nose / corner-radius end mills** for semi-finish passes.
- **Flat end mills** for 2D pockets, ribs, and ribs.
- **Tapered conical cutters** for deep ribbed regions.
- **Graphite / copper electrodes** for EDM.

### Stage 5 — Toolpath Generation (Cutter Location Data)
The CAM kernel computes:
- **Engagement angle** (angle between tool and material).
- **Cusp height** (scallop left between two adjacent passes).
- **Stepover distance** (lateral distance between passes).
- **Entry / exit moves** (ramp, plunge, helix, or pre-drilled hole).

### Stage 6 — Post-Processing
A **post-processor** translates the generic CL-DATA into machine-specific **G-code / M-code** (e.g., Fanuc, Heidenhain, Siemens Sinumerik). Differences include:
- Spindle on/off codes (M03 / M04 / M05).
- Coolant codes (M08 / M09).
- Tool-change codes (M06).
- Tool-length compensation (G43 / G44).

### Stage 7 — Verification and NC Output
The G-code is **back-plotted / machine-simulated** to detect:
- **Gouging** (tool cuts into part).
- **Collisions** (holder hits fixture).
- **Excess material left** (insufficient roughing).
- **Over-travel** (axis limit exceeded).

Only after a clean simulation is the NC file released to the shop floor.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **core quantitative backbone** for CAM-related numerical questions in KTU ESE. Memorise the units carefully — marks are routinely lost in unit conversion.

### 2.2.1 CNC Milling — Spindle Speed and Feed Rate

The cutting speed $V_c$ (m/min) is converted to spindle rotational speed $N$ (rpm):

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = cutting speed in **m/min** (typical: 80–200 m/min for P20 steel with carbide).
- $D$ = tool diameter in **mm**.
- $N$ = spindle rotational speed in **rpm**.

The table feed $V_f$ (mm/min) is:

$$V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$$

Where:
- $f_z$ = feed per tooth in **mm/tooth**.
- $Z$ = number of teeth on the cutter.

### 2.2.2 Stepover and Cusp Height Relationship

For a ball-end mill of radius $R$ with stepover $s$, the resulting scallop height $h$ (cusp height) on a flat surface is:

$$h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - \left(\frac{s}{2}\right)^2}$$

The required stepover $s$ to achieve a desired cusp height $h$ is:

$$s = 2 \cdot \sqrt{h \cdot (2R - h)}$$

### 2.2.3 EDM Material Removal Rate

For die-sinking EDM, the volumetric material removal rate is:

$$\text{MRR} = K \cdot I \cdot t_p$$

Where:
- $K$ = work-material constant (mm³/(A·s)) — depends on the workpiece thermal properties.
- $I$ = peak discharge current in **Amperes**.
- $t_p$ = pulse-on time in **seconds**.

In practice, MRR is more accurately expressed as:

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{V_w}{T}$$

Where $V_w$ is the volume of workpiece removed and $T$ is the machining time.

### 2.2.4 EDM Tool Wear Ratio

$$\text{TWR} = \frac{V_e}{V_w} \times 100\%$$

Where $V_e$ is the volume of electrode worn away and $V_w$ is the volume of workpiece removed. A **lower TWR** indicates better electrode economy (typical: 1%–10% for steel machined with graphite electrodes).

### 2.2.5 EDM Surface Roughness (Empirical)

$$R_a \approx K_s \cdot I^{0.5}$$

Where $K_s$ is a process constant. Surface roughness increases with the **square root of peak current**.

### 2.2.6 Wire-EDM Material Removal Rate

$$\text{MRR}_{\text{WEDM}} = \pi \cdot d \cdot v_f \cdot h$$

Where:
- $d$ = wire diameter.
- $v_f$ = wire feed velocity.
- $h$ = workpiece thickness.

### 2.2.7 Machining Time (General)

$$T_m = \frac{L}{V_f}$$

Where $L$ is the total length of the toolpath (mm) and $V_f$ is the feed rate (mm/min).

### 2.2.8 Cost per Mould (Simplified CAM Cost Model)

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{machine}} \cdot T_m + C_{\text{tool}} + C_{\text{setup}} + C_{\text{EDM}} + C_{\text{finishing}}$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CAM-driven mould-making pipeline is the **backbone of the global plastics, die-casting, and sheet-metal stamping industries**. Without CAM, the production of a single automotive dashboard mould (with over **300 individual cavity features, 25 cooling channels, 8 sliders, and tolerances of ±0.02 mm**) would take **6–9 months** by conventional die-sinking. With CAM-integrated CNC + EDM + WEDM, the same mould is delivered in **8–14 weeks** with consistent quality across global production sites.

The same digital workflow also enables **digital twin verification**, where the entire machining process is simulated virtually before any metal is touched — a key Industry-4.0 capability highlighted in KTU's NEP-2020 outcome-based curriculum.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **exhaustive, line-by-line derivation** of the high-yield formulas introduced in Section 2. Each algebraic transition is justified, and the underlying physical meaning is explained in plain English.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Speed (V_c)

### Starting Principle
The cutting speed $V_c$ is defined as the **linear velocity at which the cutting edge engages the workpiece surface**, measured at the periphery of a rotating cutting tool.

### Step 1 — Relate Linear Velocity to Rotational Velocity
If a tool of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then in one minute, every point on the tool's periphery travels a circular distance equal to $N$ circumferences.

$$
\text{Distance per minute} = N \cdot (\pi \cdot D)
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Units to Match $V_c$
The product $\pi \cdot D$ gives the circumference in **mm** when $D$ is in **mm**, so $N \cdot \pi \cdot D$ has units of **mm/min**. To convert to **m/min** (the standard unit of cutting speed), we divide by 1000.

$$
V_c \, (\text{m/min}) = \frac{N \cdot \pi \cdot D}{1000}
$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange to Solve for N
Multiply both sides by 1000 and divide by $\pi \cdot D$:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
Given $V_c = 150$ m/min and $D = 10$ mm:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 10}
$$

$$
N = \frac{150{,}000}{31.4159}
$$

$$
N \approx 4774.65 \, \text{rpm}
$$

In practice, the operator rounds this to the nearest available spindle speed on the CNC, e.g., **N = 4800 rpm**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Cusp Height vs. Stepover for a Ball-End Mill

### Geometric Setup
A ball-end mill of radius $R$ passes over a flat horizontal surface. Adjacent passes are separated by a stepover distance $s$. Between two passes, the uncut material forms a circular arc whose peak rises a height $h$ above the previously machined surface.

### Step 1 — Identify the Geometric Triangle
Consider the right triangle formed by:
- The ball's centre.
- The midpoint between two adjacent passes (at depth $h$ below the ball's centre).
- The contact point of the ball on the previous pass.

The horizontal distance from the ball's centre to the midpoint is $s/2$. The vertical distance is $(R - h)$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Pythagorean Theorem

$$
R^2 = \left(\frac{s}{2}\right)^2 + (R - h)^2
$$

### Step 3 — Expand and Simplify

$$
R^2 = \frac{s^2}{4} + R^2 - 2Rh + h^2
$$

Cancel $R^2$ from both sides:

$$
0 = \frac{s^2}{4} - 2Rh + h^2
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $h$

$$
h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - \left(\frac{s}{2}\right)^2}
$$

This is the **cusp height formula** presented in the cheat sheet.

### Step 5 — Inverse Formula — Solve for $s$ Given a Desired $h$
Starting from:

$$
(R - h)^2 = R^2 - \frac{s^2}{4}
$$

Expand the left-hand side:

$$
R^2 - 2Rh + h^2 = R^2 - \frac{s^2}{4}
$$

Cancel $R^2$ and solve for $s^2$:

$$
\frac{s^2}{4} = 2Rh - h^2 = h(2R - h)
$$

$$
s = 2\sqrt{h(2R - h)}
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Example
For $R = 6$ mm (ball-end mill of Ø12 mm) and desired cusp height $h = 0.05$ mm:

$$
s = 2\sqrt{0.05 \times (12 - 0.05)}
$$

$$
s = 2\sqrt{0.05 \times 11.95}
$$

$$
s = 2\sqrt{0.5975}
$$

$$
s = 2 \times 0.7730
$$

$$
s \approx 1.546 \, \text{mm}
$$

Therefore, to keep the cusp height below **50 µm**, the CAM programmer must set the **stepover distance ≤ 1.5 mm**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — EDM Material Removal Rate (MRR)

### Physical Basis
In EDM, each electrical discharge melts and vaporises a small crater of material. The volume removed per discharge is approximately proportional to the discharge energy, which equals:

$$
E = V \cdot I \cdot t_p
$$

Where $V$ is the discharge voltage, $I$ is the peak current, and $t_p$ is the pulse-on time. Assuming voltage is approximately constant for a given machine setting, the removed volume per pulse is proportional to $I \cdot t_p$.

### Step 1 — Express MRR as Volume per Unit Time
The number of pulses per second equals $1 / t_p$ (assuming ideal continuous pulsing). Therefore, the volume removed per second is:

$$
\text{MRR} = \frac{V_{\text{pulse}}}{t_p} = \left(\frac{V \cdot \alpha}{t_p}\right) \cdot I \cdot t_p = V \cdot \alpha \cdot I
$$

Where $\alpha$ is a proportionality constant that captures the fraction of discharge energy that actually vaporises material (typically 5%–20%).

### Step 2 — Introduce the Combined Constant K
By absorbing $V$ and $\alpha$ into a single material-dependent constant $K$:

$$
\text{MRR} = K \cdot I \cdot t_p
$$

This is the **standard industrial MRR formula** used in KTU numerical questions.

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example
For P20 mould steel machined with graphite electrode:
- $K = 9 \times 10^{-2}$ mm³/(A·s) (typical value from EDM handbook).
- $I = 12$ A.
- $t_p = 100 \, \mu\text{s} = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

$$
\text{MRR} = 9 \times 10^{-2} \times 12 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 9 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 1.08 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} \approx 0.648 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

To erode a feature of volume $V_w = 5000$ mm³, the EDM time required is:

$$
T = \frac{V_w}{\text{MRR}} = \frac{5000}{0.648} \approx 7716 \, \text{min} \approx 128.6 \, \text{hours}
$$

This illustrates why EDM is **never used for bulk material removal** — it is reserved for finishing and sharp-corner features only.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of CAM Process vs. Feature Type

| Feature Type | Material Hardness | Tolerance Required | Recommended CAM Process | Reason |
|--------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|--------|
| Deep pocket, open | Soft (≤ HRC 30) | ±0.05 mm | 3-axis CNC milling | High MRR, low cost |
| Free-form 3D cavity | Soft–Medium (HRC 30–50) | ±0.02 mm | 3-axis ball-end milling + finish pass | Captures complex geometry |
| Sharp internal corner | Hard (HRC 50–62) | ±0.01 mm | Die-sink EDM | End mills leave a radius equal to tool radius |
| Thin rib (depth/width > 5) | Any | ±0.02 mm | Tapered ball-end mill + WEDM if needed | Avoids tool deflection |
| Through slot in punch | Hard (HRC 60+) | ±0.005 mm | Wire-EDM | No mechanical cutting force, no deflection |
| Conformal cooling channel | Any | ±0.05 mm | DMLS additive + finish mill | Impossible to drill complex 3D paths |
| Micro-hole (Ø < 1 mm) | Any | ±0.02 mm | EDM drilling | Mechanical drill would break |

---

## 3.5 Numerical — EDM Surface Roughness Verification

Given $I = 25$ A and process constant $K_s = 0.8 \, \mu\text{m}/\text{A}^{0.5}$:

$$
R_a = 0.8 \times (25)^{0.5}
$$

$$
R_a = 0.8 \times 5
$$

$$
R_a = 4.0 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

This is typical for a **rough EDM pass**; a finishing pass with lower current (e.g., $I = 4$ A) would give:

$$
R_a = 0.8 \times (4)^{0.5} = 0.8 \times 2 = 1.6 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

This shows the **predictability and reproducibility** of EDM surface finish — a major reason CAM-based EDM is preferred over manual EDM for mould polishing.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The diagrams below provide a **block-level functional architecture** of the CAM mould-making workflow and the relationships between its sub-systems. These diagrams are designed to be reproduced directly in answer scripts.

## 4.1 End-to-End CAM Mould-Making Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model from Mould Designer] --> B[CAD Verification Step or IGES File]
    B --> C[Workpiece Stock Definition in CAM]

    J -->|Yes| K[Release NC to Shop Floor]
    J -->|No| G
    K --> L[CNC Milling of Cavity]
    L --> M[EDM for Sharp Corners]
    M --> N[Wire EDM for Sliders and Punches]
    N --> O[Surface Grinding and Polishing]
    O --> P[Trial and Final Mould]
```

---

## 4.2 CAD–CAM–CNC–EDM Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        CAD[CAD Software SolidWorks NX Creo]
    end
    subgraph CamLayer["CAM Layer"]
        CAM[CAM Software Delcam Mastercam]
        PP[Post Processor]
        SIM[NC Simulation Vericut]
    end
    subgraph MachineLayer["Machine Layer"]
        CNC[CNC Milling Machine 3 to 5 Axis]
        EDM[Die Sinking EDM]
        WEDM[Wire EDM 4 Axis]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"]
        MOULD[Finished Mould Cavity]
    end
    CAD --> CAM
    CAM --> PP
    PP --> SIM
    SIM --> CNC
    SIM --> EDM
    SIM --> WEDM
    CNC --> MOULD
    EDM --> MOULD
    WEDM --> MOULD
```

---

## 4.3 CAM Toolpath Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Toolpath Strategy] --> B[Roughing]
    A --> C[Semi Finishing]
    A --> D[Finishing]
    B --> B1[Z Level Roughing]
    B --> B2[Plunge Roughing]
    B --> B3[Adaptive Clearing]
    C --> C1[Rest Material Roughing]
    C --> C2[Steep Area Semi Finish]
    D --> D1[Parallel Finishing]
    D --> D2[Contour Finishing]
    D --> D3[Steep and Shallow]
    D --> D4[Spiral Finishing]

```

---

## 4.4 EDM Process Parameter Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Peak Current I] --> B[MRR Material Removal Rate]
    A --> C[Surface Roughness Ra]
    A --> D[Tool Wear Ratio TWR]
    E[Pulse On Time tp] --> B
    E --> F[Recast Layer Thickness]
    E --> D
    G[Duty Cycle] --> B
    G --> F
    H[Dielectric Flushing] --> F
    H --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the EDM block diagram:** The peak current $I$ simultaneously influences three competing outcomes — higher $I$ increases MRR (good for productivity) but also increases $R_a$ (worse surface) and TWR (higher electrode cost). The CAM programmer's job is to **balance** these parameters by selecting a current-time combination that meets the part's surface and tolerance requirements while staying within budget.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`
**"List any six advantages of CAM-based mould making over conventional (manual) mould making."**

**Model Answer (6 compact points, each ~0.5 mark):**

1. **Higher dimensional accuracy** — achievable tolerances of **±0.005 mm** consistently, against ±0.05 mm manually.
2. **Reduced lead time** — direct NC code generation from CAD eliminates hand-drafted routing.
3. **Repeatability** — identical moulds can be re-produced simply by re-running the verified NC program.
4. **Complex geometry realisation** — 5-axis CNC and WEDM enable free-form and undercut features impossible manually.
5. **Lower dependence on operator skill** — once verified, even semi-skilled operators can run production.
6. **Digital archiving & traceability** — every toolpath, parameter, and revision is stored digitally for ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 audits.
7. **Reduced human error** through NC simulation (collision, gouging, over-travel detection).

*(Any six points = 3 marks; seventh is a bonus.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`
**"What is a post-processor in CAM? Why is it machine-specific?"**

**Model Answer:**

A **post-processor** is a software module (or sub-program within CAM) that converts the **machine-independent Cutter Location (CL) data** produced by the CAM kernel into the **specific G-code and M-code dialect** understood by a particular CNC controller (e.g., Fanuc, Heidenhain, Siemens Sinumerik, Mazak Mazatrol).

It is **machine-specific** because:
- Different controllers use different codes for the same function (e.g., spindle on = M03 on Fanuc, vs. a custom M-function on Siemens).
- Axis configurations differ (3-axis vs. 4-axis vs. 5-axis kinematics).
- Tool-change sequences and safety interlocks vary.
- Feed-rate programming modes (per-minute vs. per-revolution) differ.

Without the correct post-processor, even a perfect toolpath will produce an **incorrect NC file** that either crashes the machine or scrapes the part.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for explanation of why machine-specific.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module-Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both questions are 14 marks, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **end-to-end CAD–CAM workflow for manufacturing an injection-mould cavity**. Clearly distinguish between CAD, CAM, post-processing, and NC simulation stages. (7 marks)

**(b)** A CNC milling machine is to machine an injection mould cavity in **P20 steel (HRC 32)** using a **ball-end mill of diameter 12 mm**. The recommended cutting speed for P20 with carbide tooling is **120 m/min**, the feed per tooth is **0.06 mm/tooth**, and the cutter has **2 flutes**.
    **(i)** Calculate the required **spindle speed (N)** in rpm.
    **(ii)** Calculate the **table feed rate (V_f)** in mm/min.
    **(iii)** If the total toolpath length is **4,800 mm**, estimate the **machining time** in minutes.
    **(iv)** The CAM programmer wishes to keep the **cusp height below 30 µm** on the cavity floor. Calculate the **maximum permissible stepover distance**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

### (a) Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks)

**Diagram** *(to be drawn in answer script)*:

```
CAD Model ──► CAM (Toolpath Gen.) ──► CL-DATA ──► Post-Processor ──► NC Code (G/M) ──► NC Simulation ──► CNC Machine ──► Mould Cavity
```

**Stage-wise explanation:**

1. **CAD Stage:** Mould designer creates the 3D model (cavity, core, sliders) in a CAD package such as SolidWorks or NX. Draft angles, parting line, and undercut regions are finalised. Output file: **STEP / IGES / Parasolid**.

2. **CAM Stage:** The CAM engineer imports the CAD file, defines the **stock block**, **fixture**, and **work coordinate system**. Machining strategies are chosen — Z-level roughing for bulk material, parallel finishing for shallow floors, and contour finishing for drafted walls. **Cutter Location (CL) data** is generated, describing the tool centre's path in 3D space, machine-independently.

3. **Post-Processing Stage:** The CL data is fed into a **machine-specific post-processor** that translates it into the **G-code and M-code** (e.g., G01 linear interpolation, M06 tool change, M08 coolant on) understood by the target CNC controller (Fanuc / Heidenhain / Siemens).

4. **NC Simulation Stage:** The generated NC code is **back-plotted and machine-simulated** in software such as Vericut. This detects gouging, collisions between tool holder and fixture, axis over-travel, and excess material. Any error sends the loop back to the CAM stage.

5. **NC Execution Stage:** Verified NC code is transferred to the CNC machine via USB or DNC network. The operator mounts the workpiece, sets the datum, calls up the program, and runs it. The cavity is produced within ±0.02 mm accuracy and Ra 0.4 µm surface finish.

*(Marks: 2 for diagram, 5 for stage-wise explanation.)*

### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V_c = 120$ m/min.
- $D = 12$ mm.
- $f_z = 0.06$ mm/tooth.
- $Z = 2$ teeth.
- $L = 4800$ mm.
- $R = 6$ mm (ball-end mill radius).
- $h = 30 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.03$ mm.

**(i) Spindle speed N:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 120}{\pi \times 12}
$$

$$
N = \frac{120{,}000}{37.699}
$$

$$
N \approx 3183.1 \, \text{rpm}
$$

**Final answer:** $N \approx 3183 \, \text{rpm}$ *(1.5 marks)*.

**(ii) Table feed rate V_f:**

$$
V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N
$$

$$
V_f = 0.06 \times 2 \times 3183.1
$$

$$
V_f \approx 381.97 \, \text{mm/min}
$$

**Final answer:** $V_f \approx 382 \, \text{mm/min}$ *(1.5 marks)*.

**(iii) Machining time T_m:**

$$
T_m = \frac{L}{V_f}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{4800}{382}
$$

$$
T_m \approx 12.57 \, \text{minutes}
$$

**Final answer:** $T_m \approx 12.6 \, \text{min}$ *(1.5 marks)*.

**(iv) Maximum stepover s for h = 0.03 mm:**

$$
s = 2\sqrt{h(2R - h)}
$$

$$
s = 2\sqrt{0.03 \times (12 - 0.03)}
$$

$$
s = 2\sqrt{0.03 \times 11.97}
$$

$$
s = 2\sqrt{0.3591}
$$

$$
s = 2 \times 0.5993
$$

$$
s \approx 1.199 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Final answer:** Maximum permissible stepover $\approx 1.2 \, \text{mm}$ *(2.5 marks)*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe the **principle, working, and process parameters of Die-Sinking EDM**. Explain why EDM is indispensable in CAM-based mould making despite being a slower process than CNC milling. (7 marks)

**(b)** A die-sinking EDM operation is performed on a **hardened H13 tool-steel mould cavity (HRC 58)** using a **copper electrode**. The process parameters are:
- Peak current $I = 20$ A.
- Pulse-on time $t_p = 200 \, \mu\text{s}$.
- Material constant $K = 1.2 \times 10^{-1}$ mm³/(A·s).
- Surface-roughness constant $K_s = 0.9 \, \mu\text{m}/\text{A}^{0.5}$.

Calculate:
    **(i)** The **material removal rate (MRR)** in mm³/min.
    **(ii)** The expected **surface roughness (R_a)** in µm.
    **(iii)** The time required to erode a feature of volume $V_w = 12{,}000 \, \text{mm}^3$.
    **(iv)** If the electrode volume worn is $V_e = 480 \, \text{mm}^3$, calculate the **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** in %. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

### (a) Die-Sinking EDM — Principle, Working, and Significance (7 marks)

**Principle:**
Die-sinking EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining) removes material through **controlled, repetitive electrical sparks** between a shaped **electrode (tool)** and the **workpiece**, both submerged in a **dielectric fluid** (typically EDM oil or deionised water). Each spark generates temperatures of **8,000 °C – 12,000 °C**, melting and vaporising a microscopic crater of material from both electrode and workpiece.

**Working:**
1. The electrode is moved close to the workpiece until the **spark gap** is approximately **0.01 – 0.05 mm**.
2. A pulsed DC voltage is applied. When the dielectric's breakdown strength is exceeded, a spark jumps across the gap.
3. The spark melts/vaporises a small volume of metal at both the electrode and the workpiece.
4. The dielectric fluid **flushes away the debris** (eroded particles) and **quenches the molten craters**, forming small solidified spheres that are also flushed away.
5. The electrode is servo-fed downward to maintain a constant gap, gradually eroding the **inverse shape** of the electrode into the workpiece.

**Key Process Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Ra |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Peak current $I$ | Increases strongly | Increases (worse) |
| Pulse-on time $t_p$ | Increases | Increases (worse) |
| Pulse-off time | Reduces MRR but improves flushing | Reduces recast layer |
| Duty cycle ($\tau$) | Higher = higher MRR | Higher = rougher |
| Polarity (electrode +) | Higher MRR (anodic dissolution) | Slightly rougher |

**Why EDM is indispensable in CAM mould making:**
1. **Sharp internal corners** — end mills leave a radius; EDM can replicate the electrode's sharp corner faithfully.
2. **Hardened-steel machining** — HRC > 55 cannot be cut efficiently by HSS/carbide; EDM removes material irrespective of hardness.
3. **No mechanical force** — delicate, thin features are not deflected or broken.
4. **Complex 3D cavities** — a shaped electrode directly copies the desired cavity geometry.
5. **Repeatable, predictable finish** — Ra is controlled by current selection, not by operator skill.

*(Marks: 2 for principle/working, 2 for process parameters, 3 for significance.)*

### (b) EDM Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $I = 20$ A.
- $t_p = 200 \, \mu\text{s} = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ s.
- $K = 1.2 \times 10^{-1} = 0.12$ mm³/(A·s).
- $K_s = 0.9 \, \mu\text{m}/\text{A}^{0.5}$.
- $V_w = 12{,}000$ mm³.
- $V_e = 480$ mm³.

**(i) MRR:**

$$
\text{MRR} = K \cdot I \cdot t_p
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 0.12 \times 20 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 0.12 \times 20 \times 0.0002
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 0.12 \times 0.004
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

Converting to mm³/min (multiply by 60):

$$
\text{MRR} = 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 = 0.0288 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Final answer:** $\text{MRR} = 2.88 \times 10^{-2}$ mm³/min *(2 marks)*.

**(ii) Surface roughness R_a:**

$$
R_a = K_s \cdot I^{0.5}
$$

$$
R_a = 0.9 \times (20)^{0.5}
$$

$$
R_a = 0.9 \times 4.472
$$

$$
R_a \approx 4.025 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**Final answer:** $R_a \approx 4.0 \, \mu\text{m}$ *(1.5 marks)*.

**(iii) Erosion time:**

$$
T = \frac{V_w}{\text{MRR}}
$$

$$
T = \frac{12{,}000}{0.0288}
$$

$$
T = 416{,}666.67 \, \text{minutes}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
T = \frac{416{,}666.67}{60} \approx 6944.4 \, \text{hours}
$$

**Final answer:** $T \approx 416{,}667$ min $\approx 6944$ h *(2 marks)*.

*(Note: This extraordinarily long time confirms why EDM is reserved only for finishing and small feature volumes.)*

**(iv) Tool Wear Ratio (TWR):**

$$
\text{TWR} = \frac{V_e}{V_w} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{TWR} = \frac{480}{12{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{TWR} = 0.04 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{TWR} = 4.0\%
$$

**Final answer:** $\text{TWR} = 4.0\%$ *(1.5 marks)*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in CAM Mould-Making Answers:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in spindle speed formula** — using $D$ in metres instead of mm, or $V_c$ in mm/min instead of m/min. Always write $N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ with units explicitly shown.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm³/s to mm³/min** — EDM MRR is usually required in cm³/min; examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if units are not shown.
> 3. **Confusing tool radius with tool diameter** in the cusp-height formula — using $D$ instead of $R$ is a frequent error.
> 4. **Writing only the EDM formula without explaining the working principle** — Part B (a) questions on EDM need both formula and physical explanation for full marks.
> 5. **Omitting the post-processor step** in the workflow diagram — it is the **most-often-skipped yet most-often-asked** stage in KTU 2024 papers.
> 6. **Stating "CAM = CNC"** — CAM is the *entire digital planning chain*; CNC is only the *execution* machine. Marks lost if the two are conflated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "CAM Mould Making":**

- **CAM = entire digital chain**, not just CNC. The chain is **CAD → CAM → CL-DATA → Post-Processor → NC Code → Simulation → Machine**.
- **CNC milling** is used for **bulk material removal** and **3D free-form surfaces**, typically with ball-end mills.
- **Spindle speed formula:** $N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$.
- **Table feed formula:** $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$.
- **Cusp height formula:** $h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (s/2)^2}$.
- **EDM** is used for **sharp internal corners, hardened-steel features, and thin/delicate geometries** that cannot be cut mechanically.
- **EDM MRR formula:** $\text{MRR} = K \cdot I \cdot t_p$, with units of mm³/s or mm³/min.
- **EDM surface finish:** $R_a \approx K_s \cdot I^{0.5}$ — higher current = rougher surface.
- **EDM Tool Wear Ratio:** $\text{TWR} = \frac{V_e}{V_w} \times 100\%$ — lower is better.
- **Wire-EDM** is ideal for **through-cuts in punches and EDM of hard materials**, with no mechanical cutting force.
- **Post-processor** is **machine-specific** — converts generic CL-DATA to controller-specific G/M-code.
- **NC simulation** is mandatory before releasing NC code to the shop floor to detect gouging, collision, and over-travel.
- **Typical accuracies:** CNC milling ±0.02 mm; EDM ±0.01 mm; WEDM ±0.005 mm.
- **Typical surface finishes:** CNC fine finishing Ra 0.4 µm; EDM finish Ra 1.6 µm; WEDM finish Ra 0.8 µm.
- **CAM reduces lead time** from 6–9 months (conventional) to 8–14 weeks (CAM-integrated) for a typical automotive mould.
- **CAM enables digital archiving**, supporting ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 quality audits.

---

*End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — PEPOT756, Module 4: CAM Mould Making.*

topics.title: EDP Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** EDP Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**EDP (Electrical Discharge Machining / Electrical Discharge Processing)** is a non-conventional, thermo-electric material removal process in which a controlled sequence of discrete electrical discharges (sparks) is generated between a **tool electrode (cathode)** and a **workpiece (anode)** immersed in a **dielectric fluid**. Each spark produces intense localized heat (**8,000 °C to 12,000 °C**), which vaporizes and erodes a tiny volume of metal from the workpiece surface without any mechanical contact between the tool and the work.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim concept):**
> "EDP (Electrical Discharge Machining) is a spark-erosion process in which material is removed from the workpiece by a series of rapidly recurring electrical discharges between a shaped electrode (tool) and the workpiece, separated by a dielectric medium. In mould and die manufacture, EDP is the **primary process** used to machine hardened tool-steel cavities, sharp internal corners, deep ribs, and micro-features that are impossible to obtain by conventional milling or turning."

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the full **EDP System** comprises:
- **Pulse generator (power supply unit, PSU)**
- **Servo-feed / gap-control mechanism**
- **Tool electrode and its holder**
- **Dielectric circulation & filtration system**
- **Work tank and CNC table** (in modern die-sinking and wire-EDM machines)
- **Process-monitoring and adaptive-control electronics**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Lightning Carving a Sculpture"

Imagine you are a sculptor trying to carve a complex dragon out of a **solid block of titanium**. You cannot touch the metal with chisels because the dragon has tiny eyes, thin whiskers, and a maze of internal cavities. So instead, you bring a **shaped copper wand** close to the block inside a **bath of oil**, and you trigger thousands of tiny **controlled lightning bolts** per second. Each bolt lasts only **2 – 200 microseconds** and removes a microscopic droplet of metal — the cumulative effect of millions of such bolts carves the dragon exactly as per the wand's shape. **That is EDP.**

The wand is the **electrode**, the oil is the **dielectric**, the lightning is the **spark**, and the sculpting happens **without any force or vibration** — perfect for fragile electrodes and hardened mould steel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EDP is the heart of modern die & mould making:**
> - Works on **fully hardened steel (HRC 60–65)** — no need for soft-state machining + heat treatment cycle.
> - Can produce **sharp internal corners (radius ≈ 0.05–0.1 mm)** — impossible by milling.
> - No mechanical force — hence **no tool deflection or vibration marks**.
> - Excellent **surface finish (Ra 0.1 – 3.2 µm)** when finish-cut.
> - Forms **recast layer + heat-affected zone (HAZ)** that needs subsequent polishing/EDM-finish.

## 1.3 Classification of EDP Systems (KTU High-Yield)

| Variant | Full Name | Typical Use in Mould/Die |
|---|---|---|
| **Sinker / Die-Sinking EDP** | Vertical die-sinking EDM | Plastic-injection mould cavities, forging dies, die-casting dies |
| **Wire EDP (WEDM)** | Wire-cut EDM | Punches, profile cutting, through-cuts in dies, trimming |
| **Micro-EDP** | Micro-EDM | Micro-holes, micro-channels, micro-features in connectors |
| **EDM Drilling** | Small-hole EDM | Coolant holes, start holes for WEDM, ejector pin holes |
| **Ram EDP** | Ram-type EDC | Large forging die cavities |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a question says *"complex mould cavity in hardened H13/D2 steel"*, the expected answer is **Sinker EDP**. Whenever it says *"through-profile cutting of a punch"*, the expected answer is **Wire EDP**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The EDP process occurs in **five repetitive stages** for every single spark:

1. **Approach & gap formation:** The tool electrode is fed toward the workpiece until the inter-electrode gap becomes **5 – 50 µm** (the "spark gap").
2. **Dielectric ionization:** The high open-circuit voltage (**80 – 250 V**) ionizes the dielectric fluid, creating a **plasma channel** of electrically conductive ionized gas.
3. **Spark / discharge:** A current pulse (**0.5 – 500 A**) flows for **2 – 2000 µs**, generating **temperature 8,000–12,000 °C** at the discharge column.
4. **Material removal:** The intense heat **melts and vaporizes** a small volume of metal at both electrode and workpiece. The molten droplet is **flushed away** by the dielectric.
5. **De-ionization (off-time):** The pulse stops; dielectric cools and de-ionizes, restoring dielectric strength; the cycle repeats **50,000 – 500,000 times per second**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Polarity (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **Roughing cuts:** Tool = **negative (−)**, Workpiece = **positive (+)** → gives high MRR.
> - **Finishing cuts:** Tool = **positive (+)**, Workpiece = **negative (−)** → gives low electrode wear.

## 2.2 The Four Key Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Range | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open-circuit voltage | Vₒ | 80–250 V | Determines spark initiation |
| Discharge current | I | 0.5–500 A | Directly proportional to MRR |
| Pulse on-time | Tₒₙ | 2–2000 µs | Longer = deeper crater |
| Pulse off-time | Tₒff | 2–200 µs | Allows dielectric recovery |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{I \cdot T_{\text{on}}}{V_m}$ | mm³/min | Empirical; Vₘ = specific volume of metal removed per unit energy (mm³/A·s) |
| 2 | Volumetric MRR (theoretical) | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot f}{4}$ | mm³/min | d = crater diameter, f = discharge frequency |
| 3 | Crater volume (single spark) | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot h}{4}$ | mm³ | h = crater depth |
| 4 | Heat input per spark | $Q = V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{on}}$ | J | Energy balance |
| 5 | Average power | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}$ | W | Duty cycle included |
