| 6 | Volumetric wear ratio (TWR) | $\text{TWR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{electrode wear}}}{V_{\text{workpiece removed}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Tool wear indicator |
| 7 | Surface roughness (Ra) | $R_a = k \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot T_{\text{on}}^{0.4}$ | µm | Empirical (Sommer's relation) |
| 8 | Wire-EDM feed rate | $F = K \cdot \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | mm/min | K = process constant |
| 9 | Heat-affected zone depth | $\text{HAZ} \approx k_1 \cdot \sqrt{T_{\text{on}}}$ | µm | Thin = good |
| 10 | Recast layer thickness | $\delta = k_2 \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{on}}^{0.5}$ | µm | Thin = good |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All `|` (absolute-value) notations are written as `\vert` so they do NOT break the markdown table — as required by KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of EDP |
|---|---|
| Plastic injection moulds | Cavity and core machining in P20, H13, S7 steels |
| Forging industry | Die impressions for connecting rods, gears |
| Aerospace | Turbine blade cooling holes (micro-EDM) |
| Medical implants | Micro-features in titanium orthopaedic dies |
| Electronics | Lead frames, connector dies |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) — From Energy Basis

**Starting premise:** A single electrical spark of energy $E$ removes a volume $V_c$ of metal, where $V_c$ is proportional to $E$ via a constant $K_m$ called the **specific metal-removal coefficient** (mm³/J).

**Step 1 — Energy of one discharge**

$$E_{\text{spark}} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{on}}$$

where $V$ is discharge voltage, $I$ is discharge current, $T_{\text{on}}$ is pulse on-time.

**Step 2 — Volume removed by one spark**

$$V_c = K_m \cdot E_{\text{spark}} = K_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{on}}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency of sparks per second**

Let $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}$ be the total cycle time. The number of sparks fired per second is:

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}$$

**Step 4 — Volume removed per second**

$$V_{\dot{}} = V_c \cdot f = K_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{on}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm³/min (multiply by 60)**

$$\text{MRR} = 60 \cdot K_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}$$

**Step 6 — Define duty factor (D) for elegance**

Let $D = \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}$ (the **duty cycle**), giving the compact form:

$$\text{MRR} = 60 \cdot K_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot D$$

> [!NOTE]
> This proves the key qualitative result required in KTU exams: **MRR increases with current $I$ and pulse on-time $T_{\text{on}}$** — which is the *trade-off against surface finish*.

## 3.2 Derivation of Tool-Wear Ratio (TWR)

**Step 1 — Wear on tool electrode per spark**

$$V_{e} = K_e \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{on}}$$

where $K_e$ is the electrode-specific wear constant (mm³/J).

**Step 2 — Wear on workpiece per spark**

$$V_{w} = K_w \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{on}}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio**

$$\text{TWR} = \dfrac{V_e}{V_w} = \dfrac{K_e}{K_w}$$

> **Conclusion:** TWR is *independent of* $I$, $T_{\text{on}}$, and $V$ — it depends only on **material pair (tool vs. workpiece) and polarity**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Wire-EDM Feed Rate Trade-off

For a given workpiece thickness $H$ and wire tension $T$:

$$F = \dfrac{K \cdot V}{H \cdot R_a}$$

where $F$ is the feed rate, $V$ is the discharge voltage, $R_a$ is the achieved surface roughness, and $K$ is a constant. Higher $V$ and lower $R_a$ demand slower feed.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Settings vs. Outcomes

| Setting | MRR | Surface Finish (Ra) | Electrode Wear | HAZ Depth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Current (I) | **Increases sharply** | Worsens (rougher) | Slight increase | Increases |
| ↑ Pulse on-time (Tₒₙ) | Increases | Worsens | Increases | **Increases strongly** |
| ↑ Off-time (Tₒff) | Decreases | Improves | Decreases | Decreases |
| ↑ Open-circuit V | Marginal | Worsens | Marginal | Marginal |
| ↑ Dielectric flushing | Stable spark | Improves | Reduces | Reduces |
| Reverse polarity | Decreases | Improves | **Greatly reduces** | Decreases |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Modern EDP System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PS[Power Supply Unit / Pulse Generator] --> SV[Servo Control System]
    PS --> SG[Spark Gap Sensor]
    SV --> EL[Tool Electrode Holder]
    SG --> SV
    EL --> SP[Inter-electrode Spark Gap 5 to 50 micrometre]
    SP --> WP[Workpiece in Dielectric Tank]
    WT[Dielectric Reservoir and Heater] --> SP
    SP --> WT
    WT --> FL[Dielectric Filtration Unit]
    FL --> WT
    CNC[CNC Controller Axis X Y Z] --> SV
    CNC --> WP
    PM[Process Monitor Arc Detection Adaptive Logic] --> PS
    PM --> SV
    HMI[Operator HMI Panel] --> CNC
    HMI --> PM
    HMI --> PS
```

## 4.2 Sequence of a Single EDP Discharge (State Transition Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Approach
    Approach --> Ionization : Gap below 50 micrometre and open circuit voltage applied
    Ionization --> Discharge : Dielectric breaks down and plasma channel forms
    Discharge --> Melting : Temperature 8000 to 12000 degree Celsius
    Melting --> Flushing : Dielectric flush expels molten droplet
    Flushing --> Deionization : Current pulse stops and gap cools
    Deionization --> Approach : Electrode re-feeds and next pulse begins
    Deionization --> [*] : Machining completed
```

## 4.3 EDP System Sub-architecture for Mould Cavity Machining

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Graphite or Copper Electrode] --> B[Electrode Holder with Clamping and Z axis]
    B --> C[Dielectric Tank filled with EDM Oil]
    C --> D[Workpiece Mould Steel H13 D2 P20]
    E[Pulse Generator 80 to 250 V] --> F[Servo Drive]
    F --> B
    G[Dielectric Pump and Filter] --> C
    H[CNC Controller with 3 axis] --> F
    H --> G
    I[Adaptive Control Unit ARC SENSE] --> E
    I --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams in KTU Exam:** Always label **five items** (Power Supply, Servo, Electrode, Dielectric, Workpiece) when asked to "draw the block diagram of an EDP system" — board examiners award 1 mark per correctly labelled block.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define EDP. Mention any two of its applications in mould and die manufacture. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EDP (Electrical Discharge Machining) is a non-conventional thermal material-removal process in which a series of rapidly recurring electrical discharges between a shaped tool electrode and the conductive workpiece, separated by a dielectric medium, removes metal by melting and vaporization. **(2 marks)**
Two applications: **(1) Machining of hardened-steel mould cavities (H13/D2) without pre-softening, and (2) Wire-EDP cutting of punch profiles and deep narrow slots in forging dies.** **(1 mark)**

### Q2. Why is a dielectric fluid used in EDP? Name two common dielectrics. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Functions of dielectric: **(1)** acts as an insulating medium until spark-gap breakdown, **(2)** cools the workpiece and electrode, **(3)** flushes away eroded metal particles (chips), and **(4)** restores dielectric strength between two successive pulses. **(2 marks)**
Two common dielectrics: **EDM oil (hydrocarbon oil) and de-ionized water (used in micro-EDM and finish cuts).** **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Format (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Die-Sinking EDP system. List its main components and state the function of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

The Die-Sinking EDP system (also called Ram-EDM or Sinker-EDM) is used to produce the **cavity** of an injection mould or forging die by plunging a shaped electrode into the workpiece. The process works on the principle of **spark erosion** — material is removed by localized melting/vaporization due to controlled electrical discharges.

**Block diagram (text representation to be drawn in exam):**

```
   ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐
   │  PULSE         │───▶│  SERVO         │───▶ ELECTRODE
   │  GENERATOR     │    │  CONTROLLER    │     (Tool)
   │ (PSU)          │    │ (Z-axis feed)  │
   └────────────────┘    └────────────────┘
          │                     │
          ▼                     ▼
      SPARK GAP (5–50 µm) in DIELECTRIC TANK
          │
          ▼
       WORKPIECE (Mould steel)
          ▲
          │
   ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐
   │  DIELECTRIC    │◀──▶│  FILTRATION &  │
   │  CIRCULATION   │    │  COOLING UNIT  │
   └────────────────┘    └────────────────┘
```

**Main Components and Functions (write any 5 for full marks):**

1. **Pulse Generator (PSU):** Produces controlled DC pulses of voltage (80–250 V) and current (0.5–500 A) with adjustable on-time and off-time. **(1 mark)**
2. **Servo-Control System:** Maintains a constant spark-gap of 5–50 µm by moving the electrode down as metal is eroded. **(1 mark)**
3. **Tool Electrode & Holder:** Shaped (negative of cavity) electrode, usually made of **graphite** (roughing) or **copper** (finishing). **(1 mark)**
4. **Dielectric Tank & Circulation System:** Holds EDM oil; pump circulates it to flush debris and cool the gap. **(1 mark)**
5. **Dielectric Filtration Unit:** Removes metal particles to keep dielectric clean and prevent short-circuiting. **(1 mark)**
6. **CNC Controller (modern machines):** Controls 3-axis or 4-axis electrode path for multi-cavity moulds. **(1 mark)**
7. **Process Monitor (Arc-Sense Unit):** Detects abnormal arc and modifies parameters in real time. **(1 mark)**

**Working Principle (write in 4–5 lines):**
The electrode is fed toward the workpiece. When the gap is small, the dielectric ionizes, a spark jumps, melts a small volume of metal, and the molten droplet is flushed away. The servo automatically maintains the gap; the cycle repeats thousands of times per second, gradually eroding the cavity to the shape of the electrode. **(1 mark for the principle text)**

---

**(b) Discuss the role of process parameters — pulse on-time (Tₒₙ), pulse off-time (Tₒff), discharge current (I), and open-circuit voltage (V) — on MRR, surface roughness, and tool wear in EDP. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Ra (Finish) | Effect on Tool Wear |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pulse on-time Tₒₙ ↑** | Increases (longer spark → bigger crater) | **Worsens** (deeper, larger craters) | Increases (more heat input to electrode) |
| **Pulse off-time Tₒff ↑** | **Decreases** (less sparking per second) | **Improves** (better dielectric recovery) | Decreases (electrode gets to cool) |
| **Discharge current I ↑** | **Increases strongly** | Worsens | Increases |
| **Open-circuit voltage Vₒ ↑** | Marginal increase | Worsens (larger spark initiation) | Marginal |

**Analytical comments to write:**
- Increasing $T_{\text{on}}$ increases the energy per spark $E = V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{on}}$, thus MRR rises linearly with $T_{\text{on}}$ (from $MRR = 60 \cdot K_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot D$). **(1.5 marks)**
- $T_{\text{off}}$ is essentially the *dielectric recovery time*. Longer $T_{\text{off}}$ allows better flushing → stable sparking → better surface finish. **(1.5 marks)**
- Current $I$ is the **strongest MRR driver** but the *worst* for finish — hence rough cuts use high $I$, finish cuts use low $I$. **(1.5 marks)**
- Tool wear (TWR) is **independent of $I$, $V$, and $T_{\text{on}}$** — it depends only on the **material pair and polarity** (proved by $TWR = K_e / K_w$). **(1 mark)**
- **Engineering rule of thumb** in die-shop: use *high current + long Tₒₙ + short Tₒff* for roughing; *low current + short Tₒₙ + long Tₒff* for finishing. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) Explain the working of Wire-EDP (WEDM) with a schematic. Why is brass wire used as the electrode? State two advantages and two limitations of WEDM. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Working Principle:**
In Wire-EDP, a thin continuously-moving **brass wire (Ø 0.1–0.3 mm)** is used as the tool electrode. The wire is fed from a spool, passes through the workpiece, and is collected on a take-up spool. Spark discharges occur between the wire and the workpiece across a de-ionized water gap. As the wire erodes, fresh wire is continuously supplied, eliminating tool wear issues. CNC controls the X-Y path, allowing complex 2-D profiles (and 4-axis taper cuts).

**Schematic (text representation to draw):**

```
   SPOOL ──▶ WIRE ──▶ ┌─ UPPER GUIDE ─┐
                      │                │
                  SPARK GAP in        │
                  DE-IONIZED WATER    │
                      │                │
   SPOOL ◀── WIRE ◀── └─ LOWER GUIDE ─┘
                  WORKPIECE TABLE X-Y
```

**Why brass wire?** Brass (Cu–Zn alloy) has **high electrical conductivity, good tensile strength, low cost, and uniform erosion characteristics**. It also produces a stable spark with most die steels. **(1 mark)**

**Advantages (any 2):**
- No tool wear problem (wire is continuously renewed). **(0.5)**
- Can cut **very hard materials** (HRC 60+) and complex 2-D profiles. **(0.5)**
- Very **narrow kerf (0.02–0.05 mm)** → less material wastage. **(0.5)**
- High accuracy (≈ ± 0.005 mm) and excellent surface finish. **(0.5)**

**Limitations (any 2):**
- Limited to **2.5-D cutting** (cannot produce deep closed cavities like die-sinking EDM). **(0.5)**
- Slow cutting speed compared to milling. **(0.5)**
- Workpiece must be electrically conductive. **(0.5)**
- Wire breakage may occur if parameters are wrong. **(0.5)**

**Award 1 mark for a clean schematic, 1 mark for the brass explanation, 2 marks for advantages, 2 marks for limitations — total 7.**

---

**(b) Differentiate between Die-Sinking EDP and Wire-EDP in terms of electrode, dielectric, gap, application, MRR, and accuracy. Also mention the role of the recast layer and how it is minimised. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

| Feature | Die-Sinking EDP | Wire-EDP (WEDM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrode** | Shaped graphite / copper electrode (negative of cavity) | Continuous moving brass wire (Ø 0.1–0.3 mm) |
| **Dielectric** | EDM oil (hydrocarbon) | De-ionized water |
| **Spark gap** | 0.05–0.5 mm (controlled) | 0.01–0.05 mm (very small) |
| **Geometry produced** | 3-D cavities, deep closed impressions | 2-D through-cuts, profile cutting |
| **MRR** | Higher (large craters possible) | Lower (small wire, fine sparks) |
| **Accuracy** | ± 0.01–0.05 mm | ± 0.002–0.005 mm (higher) |
| **Surface finish Ra** | 0.8–3.2 µm | 0.1–0.8 µm (better) |
| **Tool wear** | Yes — needs electrode design compensation | Negligible (fresh wire continuously) |
| **Typical application** | Plastic-injection mould cavity, forging die | Punch profile, die plates, prototype parts |

**Recast Layer — Definition and Minimization (1 mark each point):**
The **recast layer (white layer)** is a thin, hard, brittle layer of re-solidified metal (≈ 1–50 µm thick) that is formed on the EDP-machined surface due to rapid quenching of molten metal by the dielectric. It is undesirable because it is **micro-cracked and contains tensile residual stress**, which reduces fatigue life of the mould.

**Minimization methods:**
- Use **shorter pulse on-time (Tₒₙ)** → smaller craters → thinner recast. **(0.5)**
- Use **reverse polarity** for finishing → less heat input to workpiece. **(0.5)**
- Use **proper flushing** to remove debris quickly. **(0.5)**
- Final **polishing / EDM-finishing** pass at very low energy. **(0.5)**
- Use **micro-EDM or WEDM** with controlled pulse for thin recast. **(0.5)**
- Some shops apply **dry-ice blasting or chemical etching** to remove recast. **(0.5)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where students LOSE marks:**
> 1. Writing **"EDP is a chemical process"** — *WRONG*; it is **thermo-electric**.
> 2. Confusing **die-sinking EDP with wire-EDP** — they are *not* the same; the electrode geometry and dielectric differ.
> 3. Forgetting to **mention dielectric** when asked about EDP — KTU board **deducts 1 mark** for missing it.
> 4. Writing "dielectric is for cooling only" — *partial*; **flushing + insulation + cooling** are the three roles.
> 5. Not stating the **temperature range 8,000–12,000 °C** in EDP — examiners expect this number verbatim.
> 6. Writing **"pulse off-time increases MRR"** — *WRONG*; off-time **decreases** MRR but **improves finish**.
> 7. Confusing **TWR** with **MRR**; TWR = tool wear ratio, MRR = material removal rate.
> 8. Forgetting to draw the **block diagram** in part-(a) of 7-mark questions — minimum **3 marks lost**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EDP = Electrical Discharge Machining** — a **non-conventional, thermo-electric, no-force** material-removal process.
- The four indispensable ingredients are: **Tool electrode + Workpiece + Dielectric + Pulse generator**.
- **Spark gap = 5–50 µm** — too small → short circuit; too large → no spark.
- **Temperature per spark = 8,000–12,000 °C**.
- **Two main variants in die/mould shop:** **Die-Sinking EDP (cavities)** and **Wire EDP (profiles)**.
- **Brass wire (Cu–Zn)** is the standard wire-EDP electrode; **graphite/copper** for sinker electrodes.
- **Dielectric oil** for die-sinking; **de-ionized water** for WEDM.
- **MRR ↑ with current I and Tₒₙ**; **Surface finish ↑ (better) with Tₒff ↑ and I ↓**.
- **TWR depends only on material pair and polarity** — NOT on I, V, or Tₒₙ.
- **Reverse polarity** → low TWR → used for finishing.
- **Recast layer (white layer)** is a hard, brittle, micro-cracked surface film; minimized by short Tₒₙ, low I, and good flushing.
- **HAZ depth** also increases with Tₒₙ; keep Tₒₙ low for fine surface integrity.
- **EDP is the only process** that can machine **fully hardened tool steel (HRC 60+)** to micron accuracy.
- Modern EDP machines have **adaptive control (ARC-SENSE)** to detect abnormal arcs and auto-correct parameters.
- Always remember to **draw a block diagram** in 7-mark theory questions — at least **3 marks are reserved for it**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Preparation for Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Preparation for Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Preparation for Machining** is the systematic process of converting a designed mould/die geometry (typically a 3D CAD model) into a structured set of numerical instructions, tool paths, cutting parameters, and machine commands that enable a CNC machine tool to accurately manufacture the required cavity, core, or die component.

In the context of **PEPOT756 – Mould and Die Manufacture**, data preparation is the **critical bridge between CAD design and CAM execution**. It encompasses:

- **Geometric interpretation** of the 3D model (surfaces, curves, features)
- **Process planning** (selection of machines, tools, fixtures, and sequences)
- **Tool path generation** (CL – Cutter Location data)
- **Cutting parameter assignment** (spindle speed, feed rate, depth of cut)
- **Post-processing** (machine-specific G-code / M-code generation)
- **Verification and simulation** (collision detection, cycle time estimation)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Data preparation is the *most time-consuming* phase in mould/die manufacturing — often consuming **60–80%** of the total CAM programming effort. A single error in this stage can lead to scrapped tooling worth lakhs of rupees.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a **blindfolded chef** to bake a complex 3-tier wedding cake based on an architectural drawing of the cake.

- The **architectural drawing** = your **3D CAD model** of the mould/die
- The **chef** = the **CNC machine tool**
- The **step-by-step recipe in the chef's language** (which knife to use, how deep to cut, how fast to move) = **Machining Data**

You cannot simply hand the drawing to the chef. You must first **translate the drawing into a sequence of explicit, unambiguous, machine-readable instructions**. That translation process — gathering knives (tools), writing movements (tool paths), specifying speeds and cuts (parameters), and converting to the chef's dialect (post-processor output) — is exactly what **Data Preparation for Machining** is.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a recipe has ingredients (stock), utensils (tools), and steps (operations), data preparation has **workpiece definition**, **tool library**, and **operation sequencing**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Mould/Die Data Preparation

| Metric | Typical Value (Mould Steel P20/H13) | Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | **0.4 – 1.6 µm** (mirror polishing for injection moulds) | Determines finishing strategy |
| **Tolerance** | **±0.01 mm to ±0.02 mm** for cavity/cores | Drives precision machining step |
| **Stock Allowance** | **2 – 5 mm** per side on raw block | Material left for machining |
| **Number of Operations** | **15 – 60+** for a complex mould | Indicates data volume |
| **CAM File Size** | **5 MB – 500 MB** per mould | Indicates data complexity |

---

## 1.4 Overview of the Data Preparation Workflow

The data preparation workflow follows a strict **hierarchical flow**:

1. **CAD Model Import** (STEP, IGES, Parasolid, native format)
2. **Workpiece (Stock) Definition**
3. **Work Coordinate System (WCS) Setup**
4. **Feature Recognition / Machining Region Identification**
5. **Operation Strategy Selection** (roughing, semi-finishing, finishing)
6. **Tool Selection** (from tool database)
7. **Tool Path Generation** (CL data)
8. **Cutting Parameter Assignment** (Vc, f, ap, ae)
9. **Simulation & Verification**
10. **Post-Processing** (machine-specific NC code)
11. **Documentation & Release to Shop Floor**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Machining Data

Data preparation for mould/die manufacturing rests on five conceptual pillars. Each pillar must be explicitly defined before any tool path is generated.

### Pillar 1: Workpiece (Stock) Definition
The **stock** is the raw material blank from which the mould/die cavity is carved. It is defined as a geometric primitive — typically a **rectangular block**, **cylinder**, or **user-defined shape derived from the CAD bounding box**.

- **Bounding Box Method:** Stock = Bounding box of the finished part + uniform offset
- **Stock Offset (s):** typically **2–5 mm** per face
- **Stock dimensions:**
$$L_{stock} = L_{part} + 2s$$
$$W_{stock} = W_{part} + 2s$$
$$H_{stock} = H_{part} + 2s$$

### Pillar 2: Work Coordinate System (WCS) / Datum Setting
The **WCS** is the origin point from which all CNC coordinates are measured. For moulds/dies, the WCS is typically located at:
- The **center of the cavity top face** (most common for 3-axis milling)
- The **corner of the stock** (for simple 2D features)
- The **intersection of symmetry planes** (for symmetric dies)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Wrong WCS selection is the **#1 cause** of scrapped mould blanks in industry. Always verify the WCS by simulating a single rapid move to a known point.

### Pillar 3: Tool Selection (Cutter Geometry)
The cutter is defined by a **tool assembly record** containing:
- **Type:** flat end mill, ball end mill, bull nose, tapered, lollipop, etc.
- **Diameter (D):** measured in mm
- **Number of flutes (z):** typically **2 (HSS), 4 (carbide), 6+ (high-feed)**
- **Corner radius (rε):** critical for surface finish
- **Length (L) and Holder type:** affects rigidity
- **Material:** HSS, carbide, coated (TiN, AlTiN, DLC)

### Pillar 4: Tool Path Strategy
The tool path strategy determines **how the cutter moves through the stock**. For mould/die applications, common strategies are:

| Strategy | Purpose | Typical Stepover |
|---|---|---|
| **Z-Level (Contour) Roughing** | Bulk material removal in horizontal layers | ap = 1–3 mm, ae = 5–15 mm |
| **Adaptive (Trochoidal) Clearing** | Constant tool load, deep cavities | ap = full Hm, ae = 5–10% D |
| **Parallel Finishing** | Flat surface finishing | Stepover = 0.2–0.5 mm |
| **Scallop / Spiral Finishing** | Curved surface finishing (3D) | Scallop height = 0.005–0.02 mm |
| **Pencil Cleanup** | Corner/edge cleanup with small tools | Manual or automatic |
| **Rest Machining** | Material left by larger tools | Follows previous tool shape |

### Pillar 5: Cutting Parameters
Defined by four primary variables — **cutting speed (Vc), feed rate (f), depth of cut (ap), and width of cut (ae)**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-critical formulas**. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $n = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | **rev/min (rpm)** | $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| 2 | Feed Rate | $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot n$ | **mm/min** | $f_z$ = feed/tooth, $z$ = flutes |
| 3 | Feed per Tooth | $f_z = \dfrac{V_f}{z \cdot n}$ | **mm/tooth** | Reverse calculation |
| 4 | MRR (Roughing) | $Q = V_c \cdot a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f$ — wait, use: $Q = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ | **mm³/min** | Volumetric removal rate |
| 5 | Specific Cutting Energy | $P_s = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | **W·s/mm³** (or J/mm³) | Material property |
| 6 | Spindle Power | $P = \dfrac{a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | **kW** | $k_c$ in N/mm², $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 7 | Cusp Height (Scallop) | $h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (b/2)^2}$ | **mm** | $R$ = ball radius, $b$ = stepover |
| 8 | Stepover for desired cusp | $b = 2\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}$ | **mm** | Inverse of #7 |
| 9 | Tool Engagement Angle | $\theta_e = \arccos\!\left(1 - \dfrac{2a_e}{D}\right)$ | **radians** | For radial engagement |
| 10 | Effective Diameter (Ball Mill) | $D_{eff} = 2\sqrt{a_p(D - a_p)}$ | **mm** | At cutting depth $a_p$ |
| 11 | Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L_{path}}{V_f} + \dfrac{N_{tool\_changes} \cdot t_c}{60}$ | **minutes** | $t_c$ in seconds |
| 12 | Cutting Force (Tangential) | $F_c = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot z$ | **N** | Empirical |

### 2.2.1 Boundary Conditions & Standard Cutting Speeds (Mould Steels)

| Material | Hardness | $V_c$ (m/min) — Carbide | $f_z$ (mm/tooth) | Coolant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P20 (pre-hardened) | 30–36 HRC | **80–120** | 0.05–0.15 | Flood / Through-tool |
| H13 (die steel) | 50–55 HRC | **50–80** | 0.03–0.10 | Flood / MQL |
| S136 (stainless) | 28–32 HRC | **60–100** | 0.04–0.12 | Flood mandatory |
| SKD11 (D2) | 58–62 HRC | **30–50** | 0.02–0.06 | Dry / MQL |
| Aluminium (mould plate) | — | **300–800** | 0.05–0.20 | Flood / MQL |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The data preparation system is what enables **complex freeform mould cavities** (e.g., bumper moulds, dashboard moulds, die-cast dies) to be manufactured repeatably with **micron-level accuracy**. Without rigorous data preparation:

- **Cycle times explode** (inefficient tool paths)
- **Tool breakage increases** (wrong parameters)
- **Surface finish fails** (wrong stepover/cusp)
- **Moulds are scrapped** (datum errors)

Industries relying on this process: **Automotive, Aerospace, Medical Devices, Consumer Electronics, Packaging.**

---

## 2.4 The CAM Software Stack (Industry Standard)

| Software | Vendor | Specialty |
|---|---|---|
| **CATIA V5 / 3DEXPERIENCE** | Dassault Systèmes | Aerospace, complex surfacing |
| **NX CAM (Unigraphics)** | Siemens | Automotive, mould/die |
| **Mastercam** | CNC Software | General machining |
| **Delcam (PowerMill / FeatureCAM)** | Autodesk | High-speed mould machining |
| **CREO (Pro/E)** | PTC | Parametric mould design + CAM |
| **HyperMill** | OPEN MIND | 5-axis simultaneous mould |
| **WorkNC** | Vero Software | Automotive die/mould specialist |
| **SolidCAM** | SolidCAM | iMachining — adaptive roughing |
| **Fusion 360** | Autodesk | SMEs, integrated CAD/CAM |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Stepover for a Desired Cusp Height (Ball End Mill)

> **Problem:** A mould designer requires a surface finish of **Ra 0.8 µm** on a freeform cavity. The chosen ball end mill has a radius **R = 6 mm**. What is the maximum permissible stepover (b)?

### Step 1: Relate Cusp Height to Surface Roughness
For mould/die finishing, an empirical rule is:
$$R_a \approx 0.25 \cdot h_{cusp}$$

For the desired $R_a = 0.8\ \mu m = 0.0008\ mm$:
$$h_{cusp} = 4 \cdot R_a = 4 \cdot 0.0008 = 0.0032\ mm$$

### Step 2: Apply the Scallop Geometry
For a ball end mill moving in parallel passes with stepover **b**, the cusp (scallop) height **h** is geometrically given by:
$$h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2}$$

### Step 3: Solve for b
Rearranging:
$$\sqrt{R^2 - \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2} = R - h$$

Squaring both sides:
$$R^2 - \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2 = (R - h)^2 = R^2 - 2Rh + h^2$$

The $R^2$ terms cancel:
$$-\left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2 = -2Rh + h^2$$

Multiplying by −1:
$$\left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2 = 2Rh - h^2$$

Taking the square root:
$$\dfrac{b}{2} = \sqrt{2Rh - h^2}$$

Therefore:
$$b = 2\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Values
With $R = 6\ mm$ and $h = 0.0032\ mm$:
$$2Rh = 2 \cdot 6 \cdot 0.0032 = 0.0384\ mm$$
$$h^2 = (0.0032)^2 = 1.024 \times 10^{-5}\ mm^2$$
$$2Rh - h^2 = 0.0384 - 0.00001024 \approx 0.038390\ mm$$
$$\sqrt{0.038390} \approx 0.19594\ mm$$
$$b = 2 \cdot 0.19594 \approx 0.3919\ mm$$

### Step 5: Result and Practical Adjustment
$$\boxed{b \approx 0.39\ mm}$$

**Practical CAM practice** adds a safety margin of 10–20%:
$$b_{practical} \approx 0.3\ mm$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spindle Speed and Feed Rate Calculation

> **Problem:** A 12 mm diameter 4-flute carbide end mill is to machine P20 mould steel (30 HRC). Recommended cutting speed $V_c = 100\ m/min$, recommended feed per tooth $f_z = 0.08\ mm/tooth$. Calculate spindle speed (n) and feed rate (V_f).

### Step 1: Spindle Speed
$$n = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

Substitute $V_c = 100\ m/min$ and $D = 12\ mm$:
$$n = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 100}{\pi \cdot 12}$$

Compute the numerator and denominator separately:
$$1000 \cdot 100 = 100{,}000$$
$$\pi \cdot 12 \approx 37.699$$

Therefore:
$$n = \dfrac{100{,}000}{37.699} \approx 2652.6\ rpm$$

### Step 2: Feed Rate
$$V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot n$$

Substitute $f_z = 0.08\ mm/tooth$, $z = 4$, $n = 2653\ rpm$:
$$V_f = 0.08 \cdot 4 \cdot 2653$$
$$V_f = 0.32 \cdot 2653$$
$$V_f \approx 849\ mm/min$$

### Step 3: Final Answer
$$\boxed{n \approx 2653\ rpm, \quad V_f \approx 849\ mm/min}$$

### Step 4: Verification (chip thickness logic)
Average chip thickness $h_{m} \approx f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)$, where $\kappa = 90°$ for end mill → $\sin(90°) = 1$:
$$h_m \approx 0.08\ mm \quad \checkmark \text{ (acceptable for P20)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Spindle Power Requirement for Roughing

> **Problem:** During a roughing operation on H13 die steel, $a_p = 2\ mm$, $a_e = 8\ mm$, $V_f = 1500\ mm/min$, and specific cutting force $k_c = 2200\ N/mm^2$. Machine efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. Calculate required spindle power.

### Step 1: MRR
$$Q = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$$

$$Q = 2 \cdot 8 \cdot 1500 = 24{,}000\ mm^3/min$$

### Step 2: Convert to mm³/s
$$Q = \dfrac{24{,}000}{60} = 400\ mm^3/s$$

### Step 3: Power in Watts
$$P = \dfrac{Q \cdot k_c}{\eta} = \dfrac{400 \cdot 2200}{0.8} = \dfrac{880{,}000}{0.8} = 1{,}100{,}000\ W = 1100\ kW$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The above used $k_c$ as a power coefficient. The corrected, dimensionally-consistent formula is:**
> $$P\ (kW) = \dfrac{a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$$
> Substituting:
> $$P = \dfrac{2 \cdot 8 \cdot 1500 \cdot 2200}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{52{,}800{,}000}{48 \cdot 10^6} = 1.1\ kW$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 1.1\ kW \text{ (well within a typical 15 kW mould CNC spindle)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Data Preparation Decision Logic

| Decision Point | Question Asked | Default Choice | Alternative Choice | Trigger to Switch |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stock Definition** | Bounding box or user-defined? | Bounding box + 3 mm offset | Casting/forging shape | Pre-formed forging available |
| **Roughing Strategy** | Z-level or adaptive? | Adaptive (HSC) | Z-level | Open pocket, no deep cavity |
| **Tool for Roughing** | Indexable or solid carbide? | Ø12–Ø20 mm indexable | Ø6–Ø10 mm solid | Cavity width < 12 mm |
| **Finishing Strategy** | Parallel or spiral? | Spiral (3D) | Parallel (2.5D) | Mostly flat surfaces |
| **Stepover** | Cusp-limited or time-limited? | Cusp-limited (Ra spec) | Maximum stepover | Time-pressured prototype |
| **Coolant** | Flood or MQL or dry? | Flood | MQL | Hardened steel > 55 HRC |
| **WCS Origin** | Top-center or corner? | Top-center symmetric | Corner of stock | Asymmetric blank |
| **Post-Processor** | Fanuc, Heidenhain, Siemens? | Match machine controller | Manual edit | New machine with no PP |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Tool Engagement Angle and Its Effect on Tool Life

For a peripheral milling operation, the radial engagement (stepover width) $a_e$ relates to the cutter diameter $D$ via:

$$\theta_e = \arccos\!\left(1 - \dfrac{2 a_e}{D}\right)$$

> **Example:** D = 10 mm, $a_e = 2$ mm.
> $$1 - \dfrac{2 \cdot 2}{10} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$
> $$\theta_e = \arccos(0.6) \approx 0.9273\ rad \approx 53.13°$$

**Implication:** When $\theta_e < 90°$, milling is **climb/up** (preferred for HSM); when $\theta_e > 90°$, conventional milling dominates. For die/mould HSC, **always aim for $\theta_e \in [20°, 60°]$** to maintain constant chip load and avoid thermal shock.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Data Preparation Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model of Mould Die] --> B[Import STEP IGES or Parasolid]
    B --> C[Define Stock Workpiece Geometry]
    C --> D[Set Work Coordinate System WCS]
    D --> E[Feature Recognition and Machining Zones]
    E --> F[Select Tool from Tool Database]
    F --> G[Choose Operation Strategy Roughing Semi Finishing]
    G --> H[Assign Cutting Parameters Vc f ap ae]
    H --> I[Generate Tool Path CL Data]
    I --> J[Simulate and Verify Tool Path]
    J --> K{Collisions or Errors Detected}
    K -->|Yes| L[Modify Tool Path or Parameters]
    L --> I
    K -->|No| M[Run Cycle Time and MRR Analysis]
    M --> N[Post Process to Machine Specific G Code]
    N --> O[Documentation and Shop Floor Release]
    O --> P[CNC Machining of Mould Die]
```

## 4.2 Tool Path Strategy Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tool Path Strategy] --> B[Roughing]
    A --> C[Semi Finishing]
    A --> D[Finishing]
    A --> E[Cleanup]

    B --> B1[Z Level Roughing]
    B --> B2[Adaptive or Trochoidal Clearing]
    B --> B3[Plunge Roughing]

    C --> C1[Rest Machining]
    C --> C2[Corner Rest Cleanup]

    D --> D1[Parallel Finishing]
    D --> D2[Spiral Finishing]
    D --> D3[Scallop Finishing]
    D --> D4[Steep Shallow Finishing]

    E --> E1[Pencil Cleanup]
    E --> E2[Manual Touch Up]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a CAM System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1[CAD Model STEP IGES]
        I2[Stock Definition]
        I3[Tool Library]
        I4[Material Database]
    end

    subgraph ProcessLayer["PROCESS PLANNING LAYER"]
        P1[Feature Recognizer]
        P2[Operation Strategist]
        P3[Parameter Calculator]
        P4[Tool Path Generator]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1[Simulation Module]
        O2[Post Processor]
        O3[NC Code G Code M Code]
        O4[Setup Sheet]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    I4 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
```

## 4.4 Cutting Parameter Logic (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Parameter Selection] --> B[Identify Workpiece Material]
    B --> C[Identify Tool Material]
    C --> D[Look up Vc from Table]
    D --> E[Compute Spindle Speed n]
    E --> F[Look up fz from Table]
    F --> G[Compute Feed Rate Vf]
    G --> H[Determine ap and ae from Operation]
    H --> I[Calculate MRR]
    I --> J[Check Spindle Power Limit]
    J --> K{Power Exceeds Limit}
    K -->|Yes| L[Reduce ap or ae]
    L --> H
    K -->|No| M[Finalize Parameters]
```

## 4.5 Schematic: Scallop (Cusp) Geometry of a Ball End Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BallMillGeometry["BALL END MILL SCALLOP GEOMETRY"]
        A1[Ball Cutter Radius R] --> A2[Previous Pass Center]
        A1 --> A3[Current Pass Center]
        A3 --> A4[Stepover b between Passes]
        A2 --> A5[Cusp Peak h above Cut Surface]
    end
```

> **Visual interpretation:** A ball end mill of radius **R** traverses in parallel passes separated by stepover **b**. The uncut material between passes forms a **cusp (scallop)** of height **h**, which directly determines the surface finish **Ra**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define "Cutter Location (CL) Data" in the context of data preparation for mould/die machining. Mention any two advantages of using CL data over direct NC code generation.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cutter Location (CL) Data** is the intermediate, machine-independent file generated by the CAM software that records the precise 3D coordinates of the cutter tip (or tool reference point) at every instant along the programmed tool path. It is typically stored in a format like **APT (Automatically Programmed Tool)** or **CLS (Cutter Location Source)**.

**Two advantages of CL data over direct NC code:**
1. **Machine independence** — the same CL data can be post-processed for different CNC controllers (Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain) without regenerating the tool path.
2. **Easy editing & verification** — the CL file can be graphically back-plotted to verify path correctness before generating machine code, reducing debugging time.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is a "post-processor" in CAM data preparation? Why is it machine-specific?**

**Model Answer:**

A **post-processor** is a software module (or template file) within the CAM system that converts the generic, machine-independent **CL (Cutter Location) data** into a specific **G-code and M-code program** that a particular CNC machine tool's controller can execute.

It is **machine-specific** because each CNC controller uses:
- **Different G-code dialects** (e.g., Fanuc G81 vs Siemens CYCLE81)
- **Different tool-change macros** (M06 vs T-call)
- **Different coolant/auxiliary codes** (M08/M09 vs custom)
- **Different axis limits and feed-rapid formats**

Therefore, a **TOS VMC machine** needs a different post-processor than a **DMG MORI 5-axis centre**, even for the same mould geometry.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: **Choice between Question A OR Question B**. Each carries 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the complete data preparation workflow for manufacturing a 3D freeform injection mould cavity. List the major CAM operations used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a mould cavity finishing operation using a ball end mill of diameter **D = 10 mm** (radius **R = 5 mm**), the required surface roughness is **Ra = 0.4 µm**. Calculate the maximum permissible stepover. If the practical CAM value is reduced by **25%** for safety, what is the actual stepover used?

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Data Preparation Workflow (Flowchart):**

```
CAD Model → Stock Definition → WCS Setup → Feature Recognition
   → Operation Planning (Roughing → Semi-Finishing → Finishing)
   → Tool Selection → Cutting Parameter Assignment
   → CL Data Generation → Simulation/Verification
   → Post-Processing → NC Code → Documentation → Shop Floor
```

**Major CAM Operations for 3D freeform mould cavity:**

| # | Operation | Purpose | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Adaptive Roughing** | Bulk removal, deep cavity | Ø10–Ø16 mm carbide |
| 2 | **Z-Level Roughing** | Initial layer removal | Ø8–Ø12 mm flat end |
| 3 | **Rest Machining** | Remove material left by Ø16 tool | Ø8 mm ball end |
| 4 | **Semi-Finishing (Steep + Shallow)** | Approach to final profile | Ø6 mm ball end |
| 5 | **Spiral / Parallel Finishing** | Final surface profile | Ø4–Ø6 mm ball end |
| 6 | **Pencil Cleanup** | Sharp internal corners | Ø2–Ø3 mm ball end |
| 7 | **EDM Electrode Machining** (if required) | Sharp corners un-machinable | Graphite/copper electrode |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- D = 10 mm, so R = 5 mm
- Ra = 0.4 µm = 0.0004 mm

**Step 1:** Convert Ra to cusp height
$$h = 4 \cdot R_a = 4 \cdot 0.0004 = 0.0016\ mm$$

**Step 2:** Apply the scallop formula
$$b = 2\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}$$

$$2Rh = 2 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.0016 = 0.016\ mm$$
$$h^2 = (0.0016)^2 = 2.56 \times 10^{-6}\ mm^2$$
$$2Rh - h^2 \approx 0.016 - 0.00000256 \approx 0.015997\ mm$$
$$\sqrt{0.015997} \approx 0.12648\ mm$$
$$b = 2 \cdot 0.12648 \approx 0.2530\ mm$$

**Step 3:** Maximum permissible stepover
$$\boxed{b_{max} \approx 0.253\ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Practical stepover with 25% reduction
$$b_{practical} = 0.253 \cdot (1 - 0.25) = 0.253 \cdot 0.75$$
$$\boxed{b_{practical} \approx 0.190\ mm}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with suitable formulas: (i) Spindle speed (n), (ii) Feed rate (V_f), and (iii) MRR. A Ø16 mm 4-flute carbide end mill is used to rough an H13 die-steel mould block. Given $V_c = 60\ m/min$ and $f_z = 0.08\ mm/tooth$, calculate n and V_f.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **adaptive (trochoidal) roughing**? Compare it with conventional **Z-level roughing** for mould cavity machining, citing four advantages of adaptive roughing.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Spindle speed:**
$$n = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

**(ii) Feed rate:**
$$V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot n$$

**(iii) MRR (Material Removal Rate):**
$$Q = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f \quad [mm^3/min]$$

**Numerical calculation:**

$$n = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot 16} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{50.265} \approx 1193.7\ rpm$$
$$V_f = 0.08 \cdot 4 \cdot 1193.7 = 0.32 \cdot 1193.7 \approx 382\ mm/min$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 1194\ rpm, \quad V_f \approx 382\ mm/min}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Adaptive (Trochoidal) Roughing:**
Adaptive roughing is a high-speed tool-path strategy in which the cutter moves in **small circular or trochoidal loops** while maintaining a **constant radial engagement** with the workpiece, regardless of the cavity geometry. The tool path "spirals" within a defined region, never fully engaging the full diameter in the cut.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Z-Level Roughing | Adaptive Roughing |
|---|---|---|
| **Radial engagement** | Variable, can be full diameter | Constant (typically 5–15% of D) |
| **Chip load** | Fluctuates, leads to tool wear | Stable, predictable |
| **Depth of cut** | Limited by machine rigidity | Can be 1–2×D in HSM machines |
| **Heat generation** | High (full cutter in cut) | Low (small engagement) |
| **Cycle time** | Longer (more passes) | Shorter (fewer passes) |
| **Tool life** | Lower | Higher (2–5× typical) |
| **Suitable for** | Open pockets | Deep, narrow mould cavities |

**Four advantages of adaptive roughing:**
1. **Constant tool load** — extends tool life and prevents chatter.
2. **Full-depth cutting** — reduces number of passes and total cycle time.
3. **Lower heat generation** — improves dimensional stability in H13 dies.
4. **Higher MRR** — increased productivity in mould manufacturing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert **m/min → mm/min** when computing $n = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$. Writing it as $\dfrac{V_c}{\pi D}$ gives a 1000× wrong answer. **Always show unit substitution explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $f_z$ and $f$:** $f_z$ is **feed per tooth** (mm/tooth), $f$ is **feed per revolution** (mm/rev), $V_f$ is **table feed** (mm/min). Mixing them up = 4 marks lost.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $z$ (number of flutes):** The formula $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot n$ **must include $z$**. Writing $V_f = f_z \cdot n$ loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Cusp Formula Misuse:** Using $b = \sqrt{2Rh}$ (incorrect, missing $h^2$ term). For very small $h$, the difference is negligible, but the examiner expects the **exact formula** $b = 2\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}$.
>
> 5. **No mention of post-processor:** Many students describe CAM but forget the post-processing step. Always include it in any data preparation workflow.
>
> 6. **Confusing CL data with NC code:** CL data is **machine-independent**; NC code (G-code) is **machine-specific**. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 7. **Missing safety factor on stepover:** The calculated $b$ is the **theoretical maximum**. In practice, apply a **20–30% safety reduction** and state so.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Preparation for Machining (Module 4)**

### **Conceptual Pillars**
- ☐ Data preparation is the **bridge between CAD and CNC machining**.
- ☐ The five pillars: **Stock → WCS → Tool → Strategy → Parameters**.
- ☐ **CL data** is machine-independent; **G-code** is machine-specific.
- ☐ **Post-processor** converts CL → NC code; one per machine controller.

### **Key Formulas (Memorize)**
- ☐ $n = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ (spindle speed in rpm)
- ☐ $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot n$ (feed rate in mm/min)
- ☐ $Q = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ (MRR in mm³/min)
- ☐ $P = \dfrac{a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ (spindle power in kW)
- ☐ $h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (b/2)^2}$ (cusp height for ball end mill)
- ☐ $b = 2\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}$ (max stepover for desired cusp)

### **Standard Mould-Steel Cutting Speeds**
- ☐ P20 → $V_c$ = **80–120 m/min** (carbide)
- ☐ H13 → $V_c$ = **50–80 m/min**
- ☐ S136 → $V_c$ = **60–100 m/min**
- ☐ SKD11 (D2) → $V_c$ = **30–50 m/min** (dry/MQL)

### **Tool Path Strategies**
- ☐ **Adaptive (trochoidal)** = constant chip load, deep cavities, HSC.
- ☐ **Z-level** = horizontal contouring, initial roughing.
- ☐ **Spiral finishing** = freeform surfaces, no tool-direction changes.
- ☐ **Parallel finishing** = 2.5D regions.
- ☐ **Rest machining** = cleanup of material left by previous tool.
- ☐ **Pencil cleanup** = sharp internal corners (Ø2–3 mm).

### **Common Pitfalls**
- ☐ Wrong WCS = scrapped blank.
- ☐ Forgetting to set **stock offset** before simulation.
- ☐ **Tool under-engagement** in adaptive roughing leads to rubbing, not cutting.
- ☐ **Over-engagement** in Z-level roughing causes cutter breakage in H13.
- ☐ Always **simulate full tool path** including tool holder to avoid collisions.

### **Mould/Die Industry Standards**
- ☐ Cavity tolerance: **±0.01 – ±0.02 mm**
- ☐ Surface finish for plastic moulds: **Ra 0.4 – 1.6 µm**
- ☐ Stock allowance: **2 – 5 mm** per side
- ☐ Tool material: **Carbide (coated)** is industry default
- ☐ Coolant: **Flood** for P20/S136; **MQL/Dry** for H13/SKD11

### **CAM Software Awareness**
- ☐ **NX, CATIA, Mastercam, PowerMill, WorkNC, HyperMill** are industry standards.
- ☐ **WorkNC** is the gold standard for automotive dies.
- ☐ **HyperMill** excels at 5-axis simultaneous mould machining.

### **Examination Quick-Wins**
- ☐ Always draw a **flowchart** for workflow questions.
- ☐ State **units** explicitly in every numerical answer.
- ☐ Mention the **post-processor** role when discussing CAM.
- ☐ For stepover/cusp problems, **show the derivation**, not just the substitution.
- ☐ Compare strategies in a **table** — examiners reward structured answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machining Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machining Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Machining Process in Mould & Die Manufacture

A **Machining Process** in the context of Mould and Die Manufacture refers to the controlled removal of material from a workpiece (typically hardened tool steel, alloy steel, P20, H13, D2, or carbide) using sharp cutting tools, abrasive wheels, electrical discharges, chemical reactions, or thermal energy, in order to produce the intricate cavity, core, and profile geometry required for plastic injection moulds, die-casting dies, forging dies, stamping dies, and press tools.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT756)** syllabus, the machining processes relevant to mould and die making are classified into two major categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 4 - Machining Process):**
> - **Conventional Machining Processes:** Turning, Milling, Drilling, Boring, Broaching, Shaping, Planing, and **Abrasive Machining (Grinding)**.
> - **Non-Conventional / Unconventional Machining Processes:** **Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)**, **Wire-EDM (WEDM)**, **Electro-Chemical Machining (ECM)**, **Laser Beam Machining (LBM)**, **Ultrasonic Machining (USM)**, and **Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)**.
> - **Computer-Controlled Machining:** **CNC Milling**, **CNC Turning**, and **High-Speed Machining (HSM)** / **Hard Milling**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **block of granite (raw workpiece)** and you need to carve a beautiful **sculpture of a lion (final mould cavity)**:

| Stage | Real Sculpture | Mould & Die Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Rough shaping | Chipping off large pieces with a hammer and chisel | **Rough Milling, CNC Roughing, Turning** |
| Refining shape | Using finer chisels and files | **Finishing Milling, Shaping, EDM Rough Cut** |
| Polishing | Sanding with progressively finer sandpaper | **Grinding, EDM Finish Cut, Hand Polishing** |
| Creating fine details (eyes, whiskers) | Using delicate needles and acids | **WEDM, Micro-EDM, Laser Engraving** |
| Final mirror finish | Buffing with soft cloth and polish | **Diamond Polishing, Electro-Polishing** |

Just as a sculptor cannot create fine details using only a heavy hammer, a mould maker cannot machine hardened die steel (HRC 60+) using only conventional cutters — they need specialized **non-conventional processes** like EDM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The harder the die steel, the longer it lasts in production. But harder steel is **extremely difficult to cut with conventional tools**. This is why **EDM and Hard Milling** are the *backbone* of modern mould and die making.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Mould/Die Machining

- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** — measured in **µm** (micrometres). For mould cavities, Ra typically ranges from **0.1 µm to 0.8 µm**.
- **Dimensional Accuracy / Tolerance** — measured in **µm** or **mm** (typical mould tolerance: **±0.01 mm to ±0.03 mm**).
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — measured in **mm³/min**.
- **Tool Wear Rate (TWR)** — measured in **µm/min** or **mm³ of tool material lost per mm³ of workpiece removed**.
- **Hardness of Workpiece** — measured in **HRC** (Rockwell C) — die steels range from **HRC 50 to HRC 62+**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Machining Processes — Structured Logic

**STEP 1 → Why classify?**
Different mould features (deep ribs, sharp corners, micro-channels, micro-holes, mirror surfaces) demand different machining methods.

**STEP 2 → Conventional Processes (Mechanical Energy Based)**
Use physical contact between a *harder* cutting tool and a *softer* workpiece. Material is removed as chips.

- **Turning** — used to machine cylindrical die components (e.g., ejector pins, guide posts, die bushes).
- **Milling** — the **most widely used** process for shaping mould cavities and cores. Uses rotating multi-tooth cutters.
- **Drilling** — for making ejector pin holes, cooling channels, and sprue bushings.
- **Grinding (Surface, Cylindrical, Tool & Cutter)** — for finishing hardened surfaces to micron-level accuracy.
- **Boring** — enlarging pre-drilled holes to high precision.

**STEP 3 → Non-Conventional Processes (Use Other Forms of Energy)**
Bypass the *hardness limitation* of conventional tools. They can machine materials with hardness > HRC 60.

- **EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining):** Uses controlled electrical sparks (typically **20,000 to 30,000 sparks/second**) to erode material. No mechanical force — ideal for hardened tool steel.
- **WEDM (Wire EDM):** Uses a thin brass wire (Ø **0.05 mm to 0.30 mm**) as an electrode to cut intricate 2D profiles (punches, die openings).
- **ECM (Electrochemical Machining):** Removes metal by controlled electrochemical dissolution in an electrolyte. **No heat-affected zone, no tool wear.**
- **LBM (Laser Beam Machining):** Uses a focused high-energy laser beam for micro-machining and engraving.
- **AWJM (Abrasive Water Jet Machining):** High-pressure water mixed with garnet abrasive — for cutting thick hardened plates without heat distortion.

**STEP 4 → CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Machining**
Software-driven control of multi-axis machines. For mould making, **3-axis, 4-axis, and 5-axis CNC milling centers** are standard. **5-axis simultaneous** machining is used for deep ribbed cavities and complex undercuts.

**STEP 5 → Why HSM (High Speed Machining) is critical for moulds?**
- Spindle speeds of **20,000 to 60,000 RPM**.
- Reduces hand-polishing time by up to **70%**.
- Allows direct machining of pre-hardened steel (e.g., P20 at HRC 32–38).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application in Mould/Die |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Removal Rate (Milling) | $MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ | mm³/min | Productivity estimation |
| 2 | Material Removal Rate (Turning) | $MRR = v_f \cdot d \cdot f$ | mm³/min | Die bush turning |
| 3 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | RPM | All milling/turning |
| 4 | Feed Rate | $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | CNC programming |
| 5 | Surface Roughness (Turning) | $Ra \approx \dfrac{f^2}{32 \cdot R}$ | µm | Predict finish |
| 6 | MRR in EDM | $MRR_{EDM} = K \cdot I \cdot t$ | mm³/min | Spark erosion rate |
| 7 | WEDM Wire Wear | $Wire\ Wear \approx \dfrac{I_{avg}}{Wire\ Tensile\ Strength}$ | µm | Wire life |
| 8 | ECM Material Removal | $MRR_{ECM} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot A}{F \cdot \rho}$ | mm³/s | Electrochemical rate |
| 9 | Grinding Ratio | $G = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s}$ | dimensionless | Wheel efficiency |
| 10 | Spark Energy in EDM | $E_p = V \cdot I \cdot t_p$ | Joules | Surface integrity |
| 11 | HSM Cutting Speed | $v_c > 1500\ m/min$ | m/min | Hard milling |
| 12 | Corner Radius vs Ra | $R_a = \dfrac{f_z^2}{8 \cdot r_\varepsilon}$ | µm | Tool selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used:** $v_f$ = feed rate, $a_p$ = axial depth of cut, $a_e$ = radial depth of cut, $v_c$ = cutting speed, $D$ = tool diameter, $f_z$ = feed per tooth, $z$ = number of teeth, $N$ = spindle speed, $I$ = current, $t_p$ = pulse on-time, $V$ = voltage, $\eta$ = current efficiency, $A$ = atomic weight, $F$ = Faraday constant (**96485 C/mol**), $\rho$ = density, $G$ = grinding ratio, $V_w$ = volume of workpiece removed, $V_s$ = volume of wheel worn, $R$ = nose radius, $r_\varepsilon$ = tool nose radius.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive plastic parts** (bumpers, dashboards) → Machined using **5-axis CNC + EDM + Polishing**.
- **Press tools for sheet metal** → Machined using **CNC milling + WEDM for die opening profiles**.
- **Forging dies** → **Conventional roughing + EDM finishing** (because forging dies experience thermal shock and must be hardened).
- **Medical device moulds** → **Micro-EDM + Laser** for micro-features.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in CNC Milling

The **MRR** is the volume of material removed per unit time. In milling, the cutting tool sweeps a region defined by feed, depth, and width of cut.

**Step 1 — Define the swept volume per tooth per revolution:**
Each tooth removes a chip of cross-section = $f_z \cdot a_p$ and length = $\pi \cdot D$.

**Step 2 — Volume removed per minute:**
The number of teeth engaging per minute is $z \cdot N$.

$$
V_{removed} = f_z \cdot a_p \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot z \cdot N
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the relationship between $N$ and $v_c$:**
From $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi \cdot D}$, we get $\pi \cdot D \cdot N = 1000 \cdot v_c$.

$$
V_{removed} = f_z \cdot a_p \cdot (1000 \cdot v_c) \cdot z
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using feed rate $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ and effective width $a_e$:**

$$
\boxed{MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Surface Roughness in Turning

**Step 1 — Geometry of the tool nose:**
The tool nose has a radius $R$ (typically 0.4, 0.8, 1.2 mm). The feed $f$ creates a scallop.

**Step 2 — Height of the scallop (peak-to-valley):**
By geometry of a circle of radius $R$ cut by a chord of length $f$:

$$
R^2 = \left(\dfrac{R}{1}\right)^2 - \left(\dfrac{f}{2}\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - \left(\dfrac{f}{2}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Surface roughness Ra is approximately h/4 (empirical factor):**

$$
Ra = \dfrac{R - \sqrt{R^2 - \left(\dfrac{f}{2}\right)^2}}{4} \approx \dfrac{f^2}{32 \cdot R}
$$

**Step 4 — Example Numerical Substitution:**
Given $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $R = 0.8$ mm:

$$
Ra \approx \dfrac{(0.2)^2}{32 \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{0.04}{25.6} = 0.0015625\ mm = 1.56\ \mu m
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: EDM Material Removal Rate

**Step 1 — Faraday's law of electrolysis adapted for EDM:**
Mass removed per unit time is proportional to current and time, and inversely proportional to valency and atomic mass.

$$
m = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F} \cdot \dfrac{A}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass to volume using density $\rho$:**

$$
V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce current efficiency $\eta$ (empirical, 0.7–0.9 for EDM):**

$$
\boxed{MRR_{EDM} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot A}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho} \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]}
$$

**Step 4 — Example:**
For die steel (Fe, $A = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$ (assumed), $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³), $I = 20$ A, $\eta = 0.85$:

$$
MRR = \dfrac{0.85 \cdot 20 \cdot 55.85}{96485 \cdot 2 \cdot 7.85} = \dfrac{949.45}{1514814.5} \approx 6.27 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}^3/\text{s} = 37.6\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

**Step 1 — Cutting speed is the tangential velocity at the tool periphery:**

$$
v_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad [\text{m/min}]
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $N$ and convert units (D in mm, N in RPM):**

$$
N = \dfrac{v_c}{\pi \cdot D}\ \text{[rev/min]} \Rightarrow N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

**Step 3 — Example:**
$v_c = 150$ m/min, $D = 10$ mm:

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 150}{\pi \cdot 10} = \dfrac{150000}{31.416} \approx 4774\ \text{RPM}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection for Common Mould/Die Features

| Feature in Mould/Die | Recommended Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Deep pocket (cavity) | **CNC End Milling + HSM** | High material removal in pre-hardened steel |
| Sharp internal corner (radius < 0.5 mm) | **EDM** | Milling cutter cannot create such sharp corners |
| Micro-hole (Ø < 1 mm) | **Micro-EDM or Laser** | Drill bits break at this size in HRC 60+ |
| Through-slot with complex 2D profile | **WEDM** | Single pass contour cut, no separate tool |
| Mirror-finish cavity (Ra < 0.2 µm) | **EDM (finish cut) + Diamond Polishing** | Spark-free mirror surface |
| Hardened ejector pin | **Cylindrical Grinding** | Holds ±0.005 mm tolerance |
| Text/Logo engraving | **Laser Engraving / Micro-EDM** | Fine detail without mechanical force |
| Cooling channel (curved) | **5-axis CNC Drilling + Tubing** | Curved channels need articulated spindle |
| Spline in die punch | **Wire EDM** | Profile cut on through-hole |
| Die set pocket | **Vertical Milling + Boring** | Standard rectangular pocket |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Mould/Die Manufacturing Machining Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Steel Block P20 H13 D2] --> B[Stage 1 CNC Rough Milling]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Heat Treatment Hardening to HRC 58-62]
    C --> D[Stage 3 Hard Milling Finishing]
    D --> E{Sharp Corners and Deep Details}
    E -->|Yes| F[Stage 4 EDM Rough Cut]
    F --> G[Stage 5 EDM Finish Cut]
    E -->|No| H[Stage 4 Direct Grinding]
    H --> I[Stage 5 Hand Polishing]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Stage 6 Diamond Mirror Polishing]
    J --> K{Texture or Engraving Required}
    K -->|Yes| L[Stage 7 Laser or Micro EDM Engraving]
    K -->|No| M[Stage 7 Assembly and Tryout]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Stage 8 Inspection and Handover]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block: Classification of Machining Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Machining Process for Mould and Die] --> B[Conventional]
    A --> C[Non Conventional]
    A --> D[CNC Controlled]
    B --> B1[Turning]
    B --> B2[Milling]
    B --> B3[Drilling]
    B --> B4[Grinding]
    B --> B5[Boring and Broaching]
    C --> C1[EDM]
    C --> C2[Wire EDM]
    C --> C3[ECM]
    C --> C4[Laser Beam]
    C --> C5[Water Jet]
    D --> D1[3 Axis CNC]
    D --> D2[4 Axis CNC]
    D --> D3[5 Axis CNC]
    D --> D4[High Speed Machining HSM]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block: EDM Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[DC Pulse Generator] --> P2[Tool Electrode Cathode Negative]
    P3[Workpiece Anode Positive] --> P4[Dielectric Fluid Flush]
    P2 --> P5[Spark Gap 0.01 to 0.05 mm]
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Material Removal by Vaporization and Melting]
    P6 --> P7[Recast Layer Formed]
    P7 --> P8[Finished Cavity Surface]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block: Process Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Mould Component Feature] --> S2{Material Hardness}
    S2 -->|Below HRC 40| S3[Use Conventional CNC Milling]
    S2 -->|Above HRC 50| S4[Use Hard Milling plus EDM]
    S1 --> T1{Feature Type}
    T1 -->|Sharp Internal Corner| T2[EDM]
    T1 -->|Through Profile| T3[Wire EDM]
    T1 -->|Mirror Surface| T4[EDM Finish plus Polishing]
    T1 -->|Complex 3D Undercut| T5[5 Axis CNC plus EDM]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define EDM. List any four advantages of EDM in mould and die manufacture.**

**Model Answer:**
**Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)** is a non-conventional, thermo-electric material removal process in which material is removed from the workpiece by a series of rapidly recurring electrical discharges (sparks) between an electrode (tool) and the workpiece, both submerged in a dielectric fluid.

**Advantages:**
1. Can machine **hardened tool steel** (HRC 60+) without difficulty.
2. No mechanical force → no tool deflection, suitable for **delicate and thin components**.
3. Can create **sharp internal corners** and complex cavities impossible with milling cutters.
4. Achieves **high surface finish** (Ra up to 0.1 µm in finish cut mode).

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Distinguish between conventional and non-conventional machining processes with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Conventional Machining | Non-Conventional Machining |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Used | Mechanical (cutting force) | Thermal, electrical, chemical |
| Hardness Limit | Tool must be harder than workpiece | No such limit |
| Examples | Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding | EDM, WEDM, ECM, LBM |
| Heat Affected Zone | Usually present (chip formation) | Depends on process |
| Surface Integrity | May have residual stress | EDM creates recast layer; ECM has none |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4 Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of **Wire-EDM (WEDM)** with a neat sketch. List its key process parameters.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of WEDM:**
A thin, continuously travelling brass or molybdenum wire (Ø 0.05–0.30 mm) is used as a **moving electrode**. The wire is fed from a spool, passes through the workpiece, and is collected on a take-up spool. Deionized water is used as a dielectric. Sparks generated between the wire and the workpiece erode the material, allowing the wire to cut through the entire thickness of the workpiece, producing a 2D profile.

**Key Process Parameters of WEDM:**
1. **Wire material** — Brass (most common), molybdenum.
2. **Wire diameter** — 0.10, 0.20, 0.25 mm.
3. **Wire tension** — typically 5–25 N (higher tension → better accuracy but more wire breaks).
4. **Wire feed rate** — 2–10 m/min.
5. **Peak current (I)** — 0.5 to 30 A. Higher current → faster cut but rougher surface.
6. **Pulse on-time (t_on)** — 1 to 200 µs.
7. **Pulse off-time (t_off)** — allows flushing of debris.
8. **Servo voltage / Spark gap** — typically 0.01–0.05 mm.
9. **Dielectric flush pressure** — 0.5 to 2 MPa.
10. **Surface roughness target** — Ra 0.4 to 1.6 µm in finishing.

**Application in Die Making:** Used to cut intricate punch profiles, die openings, and tapered shapes using taper-cut attachments.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **MRR** and **spindle speed** for an end-milling operation on a P20 mould steel using the following data:
- Tool diameter, $D = 12$ mm
- Number of teeth, $z = 4$
- Feed per tooth, $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth
- Axial depth of cut, $a_p = 2$ mm
- Radial depth of cut, $a_e = 4$ mm
- Cutting speed, $v_c = 100$ m/min

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 100}{\pi \cdot 12} = \dfrac{100000}{37.699} \approx 2652\ \text{RPM}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate:**

$$
v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.05 \cdot 4 \cdot 2652 = 530.4\ \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate:**

$$
MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e = 530.4 \cdot 2 \cdot 4 = 4243.2\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Final Answer:** $N \approx 2652$ RPM, $MRR \approx 4243.2$ mm³/min.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4 Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **High-Speed Machining (HSM)** strategy for mould manufacture. List its benefits.

**Model Answer:**

**HSM Strategy:**
High-Speed Machining is a CNC milling strategy that uses **spindle speeds of 20,000 to 60,000 RPM** and high feed rates, with shallow depths of cut (a_p = 0.5 to 2 mm) and high radial engagement (a_e = 5–25% of tool diameter), employing a **trochoidal (pecking) tool path**.

**Key Elements:**
1. **Small diameter ball/end mills** (Ø 1 to 6 mm) for fine details.
2. **Shallow, multiple passes** instead of one deep pass.
3. **Trochoidal tool path** to reduce tool load.
4. **High-pressure through-tool coolant.**
5. **CAD/CAM software** (e.g., Delcam, Mastercam) for optimized tool paths.

**Benefits:**
1. **70–90% reduction in hand-polishing time.**
2. Can machine **pre-hardened P20 (HRC 32–38)**, eliminating post-machining heat treatment distortion.
3. **Better surface finish** directly off the machine (Ra 0.4 µm achievable).
4. **Reduced thermal damage** to the workpiece.
5. **Shorter lead time** for mould delivery.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a flowchart, explain the **step-by-step workflow of manufacturing a plastic injection mould cavity** using a combination of CNC and EDM.

**Model Answer:**

**Workflow (10 Steps):**

1. **CAD Design** — Create the 3D model of the mould cavity and core in CAD (e.g., NX, CATIA, SolidWorks).
2. **CAM Programming** — Generate the CNC tool paths for rough and finish milling.
3. **CNC Rough Milling** — Remove bulk material from the soft annealed steel block (HRC ~20) using a Ø 20–50 mm end mill, leaving 0.5–1 mm stock.
4. **Stress Relieving / Pre-Hardening** — Heat-treat to HRC 30–38 (P20 steel) to prevent distortion in later steps.
5. **Hard Milling / Semi-Finishing** — Use HSM with Ø 6–10 mm ball end mills to bring the cavity to within 0.05–0.1 mm of final shape.
6. **EDM Rough Cut (if sharp corners exist)** — Use a copper or graphite electrode to EDM features that milling cutters cannot reach (sharp corners, deep ribs).
7. **EDM Finish Cut** — Use low-energy, high-frequency pulses for fine surface finish (Ra 0.4–0.8 µm).
8. **Manual / Diamond Polishing** — Skilled polishers use diamond pastes (Grade 600 → 3000) to achieve mirror finish (Ra < 0.2 µm).
9. **Texturing / Engraving** — Apply EDM texturing, chemical etching, or laser engraving for grain pattern (e.g., SPI standards: MT, VDI 3400).
10. **Inspection, Fitting, and Assembly** — CMM inspection, fitting the cavity into the mould base, and tryout on the injection moulding machine.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing EDM with ECM** — In EDM, material is removed by **thermal vaporization**; in ECM, by **electrochemical dissolution**. Marks deducted for wrong energy form.
> 2. **Forgetting the dielectric** in EDM — Many students skip mentioning the dielectric fluid (kerosene, EDM oil, or deionized water for WEDM). This is a 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Wrong unit for MRR** — Always write **mm³/min**, not cm³/min.
> 4. **Spark gap value** — Memorize **0.01–0.05 mm** for EDM. Wrong value = ½ mark cut.
> 5. **Forgetting Faraday's constant** in EDM derivation — must state **F = 96,485 C/mol**.
> 6. **5-axis vs 3-axis** — In moulds, deep ribs and undercuts require 5-axis, NOT 3-axis. Examiners specifically check this.
> 7. **HSM is NOT HMC** — HSM = High Speed Machining; HMC = Horizontal Machining Center. Do not interchange.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **EDM** uses **electrical sparks** in a **dielectric** — ideal for **hardened die steel**.
- [x] **WEDM** uses a **moving brass wire** — best for **2D profile cutting** and **punch/die pairs**.
- [x] **ECM** is **chemical** (no heat, no tool wear) — used for **turbine blades** and **delicate profiles**.
- [x] **HSM** uses **> 20,000 RPM** and **trochoidal paths** — eliminates polishing time.
- [x] **MRR (Milling)** = $v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$.
- [x] **N (RPM)** = $\dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi \cdot D}$.
- [x] **Ra (Turning)** $\approx \dfrac{f^2}{32 \cdot R}$ — *larger nose radius → better finish*.
- [x] **Faraday's constant** = **96,485 C/mol** — required for EDM/ECM derivations.
- [x] **5-axis CNC** is the **industry standard** for complex moulds.
- [x] **Mould steel grades:** **P20** (pre-hardened), **H13** (hot work), **D2** (cold work), **S136 / Stavax** (stainless, polished).
- [x] **Surface finish grades:** SPI Standard: **A-1 (diamond, Ra 0.012 µm) → A-3 (mirror) → B, C, D grades**.
- [x] **Spark gap** in EDM: **0.01–0.05 mm**.
- [x] **Dielectric fluids:** EDM oil (sink EDM), deionized water (WEDM), NaNO₃ solution (ECM).
- [x] **Key distinction:** EDM = thermal; ECM = electrochemical; LBM = photothermal; AWJM = mechanical-abrasive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steps Involved
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steps Involved

# Steps Involved in Mould and Die Manufacture

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Steps Involved in Mould and Die Manufacture** refers to the systematic, sequential operations — starting from product analysis and ending at final inspection — that transform raw tool steel into a precision mould or die capable of producing plastic parts or sheet metal stampings consistently within micron-level tolerances.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic covers the **complete process chain** from **conceptual design to final try-out** of a mould/die. Each step is governed by engineering tolerances (typically **±0.01 mm** to **±0.025 mm**), surface finish requirements (often **Ra 0.2 µm** to **Ra 0.8 µm**), and metallurgical specifications dictated by the parent material (e.g., P20, H13, D2, SKD11).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sculptor's Workflow"**
>
> Imagine a sculptor commissioned to create an identical statue a thousand times. The sculptor first studies the model (product analysis), chooses the right marble (material selection), carves a rough shape (rough machining), tempers the stone for strength (heat treatment), refines details with fine chisels (finish machining), polishes surfaces (grinding/polishing), assembles a frame to hold it (fitting/assembly), and finally inspects every curve before delivery (quality control). Mould and die making follows **exactly this disciplined choreography** — every step prepares the workpiece for the next.

### Key Parameters & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensional Tolerance | **±0.01 mm – ±0.05 mm** | Functional fit & part quality |
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | **0.2 µm – 0.8 µm** | Polish transfer to part |
| Hardness (HRC) | **48 – 62 HRC** | Wear resistance |
| Lead Time | 4 – 16 weeks | Production planning |
| EDM Wire Diameter | **0.10 mm – 0.30 mm** | Corner radius control |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Process Sheet

### The 10 Universal Steps — Logic Breakdown

1. **Product / Part Analysis**
   * Study the plastic/metal part drawing.
   * Identify parting line, draft angles, wall thickness, ribs, bosses.
   * Decide mould type (two-plate, three-plate, hot runner, progressive die, deep draw die).

2. **Mould/Die Design & Drawing Preparation**
   * Compute parting line geometry.
   * Design cavity, core, ejector, cooling, and gating layout.
   * Generate **2D manufacturing drawings** and **3D CAM models**.

3. **Material Selection**
   * For moulds: P20 (pre-hardened), H13 (hot work), S50C.
   * For dies: D2, SKD11, DC53 (cold work tool steel).
   * Factor: production volume, working temperature, wear resistance.

4. **Rough Machining (Stock Removal)**
   * Remove bulk material using **milling, turning, sawing**.
   * Leave **2 mm – 3 mm** finishing allowance on critical surfaces.
   * Performed on **VMC / HMC** with roughing inserts.

5. **Stress Relieving**
   * Heat to **550 °C – 650 °C**, hold, slow cool.
   * Removes residual stresses from rough machining.
   * Prevents distortion in subsequent operations.

6. **Heat Treatment (Hardening & Tempering)**
   * Hardening: heat to **1000 °C – 1050 °C**, quench in oil/air.
   * Tempering: re-heat to **150 °C – 600 °C** (double tempering recommended).
   * Achieves target hardness (**58 – 62 HRC** for cold work dies).

7. **Finish Machining (CNC Milling & Turning)**
   * High-speed CNC milling of cavity/core profiles.
   * Ball-end mills of **Ø2 – Ø10 mm** for deep pockets.
   * Achieves dimensional accuracy of **±0.02 mm**.

8. **EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining)**
   * **Sinker EDM** for deep ribs, sharp corners (radius < **0.2 mm**).
   * **Wire EDM** for profile cutting, punch shapes, intricate contours.
   * Wire diameter typically **0.25 mm** (produces **0.30 mm** corner radius).

9. **Grinding, Polishing & Hand Fitting**
   * **Surface grinding** for flat reference surfaces (flatness **0.005 mm**).
   * **Profile / contour grinding** for 3D surfaces.
   * **Diamond polishing** to **mirror finish (Ra ≤ 0.05 µm)** for optical moulds.

10. **Assembly, Try-Out & Inspection**
    * Fit cavity into bolster, core into die set.
    * **Trial run** on injection press / press brake.
    * Inspect first-off part with **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)**.
    * Iterate corrections until part meets drawing specifications.

### KTU High-Yield Process Parameter Sheet

| Step | Primary Machine / Process | Typical Tolerance Achieved | Surface Finish (Ra) | Critical Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rough Machining | VMC, Lathe | ±0.1 mm | 3.2 µm | Leave 2–3 mm allowance |
| Stress Relieving | Heat Treatment Furnace | — | — | Avoid distortion later |
| Heat Treatment | Vacuum Furnace | ±1 HRC | — | Temper twice for stability |
| CNC Milling | 3-axis / 5-axis VMC | ±0.02 mm | 0.8 µm | Use ball-end mills |
| Sinker EDM | EDM Die-Sinking | ±0.01 mm | 0.8 µm | Best for sharp corners |
| Wire EDM | Wire-Cut EDM | ±0.005 mm | 0.4 µm | Corner radius = wire Ø/2 |
| Surface Grinding | Surface Grinder | ±0.005 mm | 0.2 µm | Reference flatness |
| Polishing | Manual + Diamond Paste | ±0.002 mm | 0.05 µm | For optical-grade parts |
| CMM Inspection | CMM | ±0.003 mm | — | Final QC verification |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> These steps are the **backbone of automotive, aerospace, medical device, and consumer electronics manufacturing**. A single injection mould for a smartphone housing undergoes every one of these 10 steps. Mastery of this process chain is what distinguishes a tool maker from a general machinist — and is a **high-frequency question area** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Process Flow & Analytical Matrix

### Detailed Sequential Workflow

Since this topic is process-oriented (not equation-driven), the "derivation" here is the **logical and operational derivation of the manufacturing sequence** — explaining why each step must occur in a specific order and what happens if reordered.

#### Step 1: Product Analysis

$$
\text{Draft Angle} \geq 1^\circ \text{ (for plastic parts)}
$$

$$
\text{Wall Thickness Uniformity} = \Delta t \leq 25\% \text{ of nominal } t
$$

The designer inspects the part drawing to extract:
* Parting line location
* Gate location and type
* Ejection direction
* Cooling channel feasibility

> **Logic:** Without correct analysis, subsequent design steps produce a mould that cannot eject the part, has weld lines in visible areas, or warps due to uneven cooling.

#### Step 2: Design & Drawing Generation

$$
\text{Cavity Shrinkage Allowance} = (1 + S_f) \times L_{\text{part}}
$$
