**Step 1 — Describe the geometry.**
The mould consists of:
- A **cavity plate** (cavity face on top).
- **Cartridge heater wells** drilled from the underside of the cavity plate, parallel to the parting line.
- **Cooling channels** drilled through the cavity plate (and core plate) perpendicular to the ejector pins, between the cavity and the heater wells.

**Step 2 — State the rules.**
- **Heater well stand-off S ≥ 1.5 d** (where d is the heater diameter).
- **Heater well tolerance: +0.02 / 0.00 mm** (DME H series / HASCO Z 25).
- **Cooling channel pitch P = 3d to 5d**.
- **Cooling channel stand-off from cavity S_c ≥ 1.5 d**, preferably **2d–3d**.
- **Channel diameter d: 6, 8, 10, 12 mm** (preferred values).
- **Bend radius ≥ 2 d** (centreline).

**Step 3 — Sketch description (refer to the ASCII schematic in Section 4.4 of these notes).**

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Reynolds number relation.**

$$
Re = \dfrac{4 \rho \dot{V}}{\pi d \mu}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\dot{V}$.**

$$
\dot{V} = \dfrac{Re \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot \mu}{4 \rho}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (d = 0.010 m).**

$$
\dot{V} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 3.1416 \times 0.010 \times 0.80 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 996}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator.**

$$
10{,}000 \times 3.1416 \times 0.010 = 314.16
$$

$$
314.16 \times 0.80 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2513
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator.**

$$
4 \times 996 = 3984
$$

**Step 6 — Final division.**

$$
\dot{V} = \dfrac{0.2513}{3984} = 6.31 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to L/min.**

$$
\dot{V} = 6.31 \times 10^{-5} \times 60{,}000 = 3.79 \ \text{L/min}
$$

**Conclusion:** A minimum of **3.8 L/min** ensures turbulent flow and a stable convective coefficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Two common errors here: (i) using kinematic viscosity in place of dynamic viscosity (the Reynolds number formula here uses **μ**, not **ν**), and (ii) forgetting to convert m³/s → L/min. Each error costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Mould heating = controlled thermal energy to cavity surface**, not "just heating".
- ✅ **SPI (USA)** is the *de facto* mould-classification standard; **ISO** for test methods; **Euromap** for machine–mould interface.
- ✅ **Cartridge heater placement rule:** stand-off **S ≥ 1.5 d**, well tolerance **+0.02 / 0.00 mm**.
- ✅ **Cooling channel rule:** **P = 3d to 5d**, **S_c ≥ 1.5 d** (preferred 2d–3d), **bend radius ≥ 2 d**.
- ✅ **Fourier's law** $Q = k A \Delta T / L$ — the workhorse for steady-state heat-loss calculation.
- ✅ **Turbulent flow rule:** $Re \geq 10{,}000$ → $\dot{V} \approx 3.8$ L/min for a 10 mm water channel.
- ✅ **Dittus–Boelter** gives the convective coefficient once Re and Pr are known.
- ✅ **Conduction-dominated through steel; convection-dominated at channel wall** — *always* state this in your answer.
- ✅ **Heater efficiency η ≈ 0.85–0.95** — include it in cartridge-count calculations.
- ✅ **Thermal shadowing** is the *physical reason* behind the 3d pitch rule.
- ✅ **BIS (India)** safety codes apply for pressure-vessel rating of heating channels.
- ✅ Always quote **units** (W, m, K, m/s, L/min) — examiners penalise unitless answers.
- ✅ A **sketch** of the heater + cooling layout can fetch **3–4 bonus marks** on a 14-mark question.
- ✅ **Lumped-capacitance model** $t_{ss} = \rho c V / (h A)$ is the simplest way to estimate warm-up time.
- ✅ **Conformal cooling** (additive-manufactured channels) is the modern SPI Class 4 trend — mention it for full marks on a "state-of-the-art" question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mould Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mould Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Mould Maintenance

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mould Maintenance** refers to the systematic, planned, and documented set of technical, operational, and managerial activities carried out to preserve, restore, or enhance the functional condition of a mould used in polymer processing (injection moulding, blow moulding, compression moulding, etc.) so that it continues to produce dimensionally accurate, defect-free, and aesthetically acceptable plastic components at rated productivity and quality levels.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology aligned with **ISO 9001:2015 (Quality Management)** and **ISO 12100:2010 (Safety of Machinery)**, mould maintenance is treated as a sub-domain of **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** and encompasses three primary lifecycle phases:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)**
> *Mould maintenance is the integrated practice of cleaning, inspection, lubrication, repair, refurbishment, and documentation of moulds to ensure zero-defect production, extended mould life, and minimized unplanned downtime in plastics processing industries.*

### 1.2 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in Mould Maintenance

The following standard industry metrics are mandatory for KTU-level conceptual understanding:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — typically **20,000 to 100,000 shots** for injection moulds
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — targeted at **0.5 to 4 hours**
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** — benchmark of **≥ 85%**
- **Mould Life** — **500,000 to 5,000,000 shots** depending on steel grade

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Car Service Center**
> Imagine you own a car. You do not wait for the engine to seize before you take action. You follow a service schedule — change engine oil every 5,000 km, rotate tyres every 10,000 km, inspect brakes every 20,000 km, and replace the timing belt at 100,000 km. These are **preventive maintenance** actions. Occasionally, the car makes a strange noise (vibration, smoke) and you take it to the mechanic before a breakdown — that is **predictive maintenance**. If a part actually fails (punctured tyre, dead battery), you replace it — that is **corrective (breakdown) maintenance**.
> A mould in a plastics factory behaves *exactly* like this car. It works under extreme conditions (high pressure, high temperature, cyclic loading, abrasive polymers, corrosive additives, moisture) and degrades with every shot. **Mould maintenance is the structured "service schedule" of this precision tool.**

### 1.4 Why Mould Maintenance is a High-Weightage KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Relevance & KTU Justification**
> In an Indian plastics processing SME, mould maintenance typically accounts for **8–15% of total production cost** and **30–40% of total machine downtime**. A single mould failure can halt a production line worth **₹50,000 to ₹5,00,000 per day** in lost output. Therefore, KTU 2024 Scheme assigns this topic significant marks under **Course Outcome CO4**: *Apply maintenance strategies to enhance mould life and productivity*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Mould Maintenance Strategies

The hierarchy of maintenance strategies (from least to most proactive) is as follows:

1. **Breakdown (Reactive) Maintenance**
   - Action taken *after* the mould has failed.
   - High downtime cost, low initial planning cost.
   - Used only for **non-critical, low-cost moulds**.

2. **Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance**
   - Time-based or shot-count-based interventions.
   - Example: Cleaning cavity and cores every **50,000 shots**; lubrication of ejector pins every **10,000 shots**.

3. **Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance**
   - Uses sensors, thermal imaging, vibration analysis, and wear monitoring.
   - Maintenance is performed *only when* a parameter crosses a threshold.

4. **Corrective Maintenance**
   - Planned repair to restore the mould to its original specifications.
   - Includes welding, EDM, polishing, re-hardening.

5. **Proactive Maintenance (Root-Cause-Based)**
   - Identifies and eliminates the *root cause* of failure (e.g., poor venting design, wrong steel selection, inadequate cooling).

### 2.2 Standard Mould Maintenance Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 — Mould Removal from Machine:** Stop IMM, open clamp, disconnect water lines, ejector linkages, and hot runner connections. Lift using crane with proper slinging.

- **Step 2 — Visual Inspection:** Examine parting line flash, ejector pin marks, gate vestige, surface rust, dents, and wear marks on cavity/cores.

- **Step 3 — Disassembly:** Carefully dismantle components in a clean, dust-free mould maintenance shop. Tag every part with serial numbers.

- **Step 4 — Cleaning:** Use ultrasonic cleaners, mould cleaners (non-corrosive), copper/brass brushes, and lint-free cloths. Never use steel tools on polished surfaces.

- **Step 5 — Detailed Inspection:** Measure critical dimensions using **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)**, **surface roughness tester (Ra)**, **hardness tester (HRC)**.

- **Step 6 — Repair/Refurbishment:** Polish worn areas, re-machine damaged features via EDM, weld cracks using **TIG welding with matching filler wire**.

- **Step 7 — Reassembly & Trial:** Reassemble in reverse order, reinstall in machine, and conduct a dry-cycle + sample trial to verify performance.

- **Step 8 — Documentation:** Update mould history card, log shot count, record all observations and repairs.

### 2.3 Common Mould Defects & Their Maintenance Remedies

- **Flash at Parting Line** → Re-machine parting surfaces, check clamp tonnage.
- **Ejector Pin Marks** → Polish or replace pins, check ejector stroke.
- **Surface Rust / Pitting** → Polish, apply anti-rust coating, improve storage humidity (< 50% RH).
- **Worn Cavity Inserts** → Re-hardening (vacuum heat treatment) or EDM recutting.
- **Cooling Channel Blockage** → Descaling using chemical flush, ultrasonic cleaning.
- **Gate Bush Wear** → EDM machining of new gate, or replacement of gate bush.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verified and are part of the KTU 2024 expected answer key for numerical questions.**

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | **System Availability** | $A$ = Availability (decimal), MTBF in hours, MTTR in hours |
| $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality (all as decimals) |
| $C_m = \dfrac{C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{spare}} + C_{\text{downtime}}}{N_{\text{shots}}}$ | **Maintenance Cost per Shot** | $C_m$ in ₹/shot, $N_{\text{shots}}$ = total shots produced |
| $L_m = \dfrac{\text{Total Allowable Wear}}{\text{Wear per Shot}}$ | **Theoretical Mould Life (shots)** | $L_m$ in shots |
| $T_{\text{schedule}} = T_{\text{PM}} \cdot N_{\text{cycles}}$ | **Preventive Maintenance Time** | Time in hours |
| $\text{MTBF}_{\text{system}} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}_i} \right)^{-1}$ | **Series System Reliability** | For $n$ components in series |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plastic Component Manufacturer:** Reduces scrap rate from **5% to 0.5%** through predictive maintenance.
- **Automotive OEM:** A 1% increase in mould availability translates to **₹2–3 crore annual savings** in a medium-scale plant.
- **Tool Room:** Effective maintenance extends mould life by **30–50%**, deferring capital expenditure on new moulds.
- **Quality Lab:** Maintained moulds produce parts with tighter tolerances (±0.02 mm), meeting **IATF 16949** automotive standards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maintenance Cost per Shot ($C_m$)

This is a high-frequency KTU numerical problem. We derive it from first principles.

**Statement:** The total maintenance expenditure over the life of a mould is the sum of (a) labour cost, (b) spare parts cost, and (c) opportunity cost of production downtime. Divide this by the total number of shots produced during the mould's life to obtain the per-shot maintenance cost.

Let:
- $C_L$ = Total labour cost over mould life, in ₹
- $C_S$ = Total cost of spare parts consumed, in ₹
- $C_D$ = Downtime cost = (Downtime hours) $\times$ (Hourly production value), in ₹
- $N$ = Total shots produced during the mould's useful life

$$
C_m = \frac{C_L + C_S + C_D}{N}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This formula is the foundation of **life-cycle cost analysis (LCCA)** of moulds and is a direct application of **cost-per-unit engineering** in polymer processing.

### 3.2 Derivation: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

OEE is the product of three independent factors:

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

Where:
- $A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$
- $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}$
- $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces Produced}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$

**Expansion:**

$$
\text{OEE} = \frac{\text{Good Pieces} \times \text{Ideal Cycle Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Format)

**Problem:** An injection mould has the following operational data:
- Operating time = 400 hours/month
- Downtime due to maintenance = 20 hours/month
- Ideal cycle time = 30 seconds
- Total pieces produced = 42,000/month
- Rejected pieces = 1,400/month

**Calculate the OEE of the mould.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Availability ($A$)**

$$
A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \frac{400}{400 + 20} = \frac{400}{420}
$$

$$
A = 0.9524 = 95.24\%
$$

**Step 2: Performance ($P$)**

Theoretical output in 400 hours at 30 s/cycle:

$$
N_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{400 \times 3600}{30} = 48{,}000 \text{ pieces}
$$

$$
P = \frac{N_{\text{ideal}}}{N_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{48{,}000}{42{,}000} = 1.1429
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Performance is **capped at 100%** in standard OEE calculations. Therefore $P = 1.0$ (or 100%). The above value > 1 indicates that ideal cycle time was overestimated, but in OEE world-class standards, we cap it at 1.

**Step 3: Quality ($Q$)**

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} = \frac{42{,}000 - 1{,}400}{42{,}000} = \frac{40{,}600}{42{,}000}
$$

$$
Q = 0.9667 = 96.67\%
$$

**Step 4: OEE**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9524 \times 1.0 \times 0.9667
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{OEE} = 0.9207 = 92.07\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is **world-class** performance (target ≥ 85%).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mould Failure Modes and Maintenance Actions

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Maintenance Action | Prevention Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Flash at parting line | Worn parting surface | Re-machine parting surface, EDM | Use harder tool steel (H13, SKD61) |
| Ejector pin seizure | Lack of lubrication, rust | Clean, lubricate, replace pin | Schedule lubrication every 10,000 shots |
| Cavity surface pitting | Corrosion from PVC, flame retardants | Polish, passivate | Use stainless mould steel (SS 420, P20+Ni) |
| Cooling channel blockage | Scale, rust in water lines | Chemical descaling | Use closed-loop chilled water with filter |
| Gate freeze-off | Insufficient heater band wattage | Replace heater band | Calibrate hot runner controller quarterly |
| Cracked core insert | Thermal fatigue, stress concentration | TIG weld + stress relieve | Use uniform wall thickness, generous fillets |
| Warped cavity plate | Uneven cooling, clamp force imbalance | Re-machine flat surface, stress relieve | Verify cooling symmetry during design |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Mould Maintenance Procedure Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the sequential workflow of a structured mould maintenance operation, as per KTU 2024 scheme expectations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Mould in Production]) --> B1{Routine Stop Scheduled?}
    B1 -- Yes --> C1[Stop IMM and Eject Mould]
    B1 -- No --> B2{Mould Defect Detected?}
    B2 -- No --> A0
    B2 -- Yes --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Visual Inspection]
    D1 --> E1[Disassemble Mould]
    E1 --> F1[Ultrasonic Cleaning]
    F1 --> G1[Detailed Dimensional Check]
    G1 --> H1{Fault Found?}
    H1 -- No --> I1[Reassemble Mould]
    H1 -- Yes --> J1[Repair / Refurbish]
    J1 --> K1[Re-Harden if Required]
    K1 --> L1[Final Polishing]
    L1 --> I1
    I1 --> M1[Trial Shot in IMM]
    M1 --> N1{Quality OK?}
    N1 -- No --> J1
    N1 -- Yes --> O1[Update Mould History Card]
    O1 --> P1[Return Mould to Production]
    P1 --> A0
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mould Maintenance Management System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Shot Count Sensor]
        I2[Temperature Sensor]
        I3[Vibration Sensor]
        I4[Operator Log]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer]
        PROC1[Data Acquisition Unit]
        PROC2[Condition Monitoring Module]
        PROC3[Predictive Analytics Engine]
        PROC4[Maintenance Scheduler]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[CMMS Dashboard]
        O2[Maintenance Work Order]
        O3[Spare Parts Requisition]
        O4[Operator Alert]
    end

    subgraph ACTION[Action Layer]
        A1[Preventive Maintenance]
        A2[Corrective Repair]
        A3[Spare Replacement]
        A4[Root Cause Analysis]
    end

    I1 --> PROC1
    I2 --> PROC1
    I3 --> PROC1
    I4 --> PROC1
    PROC1 --> PROC2
    PROC2 --> PROC3
    PROC3 --> PROC4
    PROC4 --> O1
    PROC4 --> O2
    PROC4 --> O3
    PROC4 --> O4
    O1 --> A1
    O2 --> A2
    O3 --> A3
    O1 --> A4
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Maintenance Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Mould Type?]) --> Q1{High-Cost Mould?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Predictive + Preventive]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Production Volume?}
    Q2 -- High --> S2[Preventive Maintenance]
    Q2 -- Low --> S3[Breakdown Maintenance]
    S1 --> END([Optimum Strategy])
    S2 --> END
    S3 --> END
```

### 4.4 Mould History Card (Schematic Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CARD[Mould History Card]
        F1[Mould ID and Drawing Number]
        F2[Material of Construction]
        F3[Date of Commissioning]
        F4[Total Shot Count]
        F5[Last Maintenance Date]
        F6[Next Scheduled Maintenance]
        F7[List of Replaced Parts]
        F8[Operator Remarks]
        F9[Quality Inspector Sign]
        F10[Maintenance Engineer Sign]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define mould maintenance. List any four common mould defects encountered in injection moulding.*

**Model Answer:**
Mould maintenance refers to the systematic set of activities — cleaning, inspection, lubrication, repair, and documentation — performed on a mould to ensure it produces defect-free parts consistently over its service life.
Four common mould defects:
1. Flash at parting line
2. Ejector pin seizure
3. Cooling channel blockage
4. Gate freeze-off

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between preventive maintenance and predictive maintenance of moulds.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trigger | Time or shot-count based | Condition-based (sensors) |
| Frequency | Fixed schedule | On-demand |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher initial, lower long-term |
| Tools | Calendar, logbook | Vibration analyzer, thermal camera |
| Downtime | Planned | Minimal, planned |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure of mould maintenance with a neat block diagram. State the safety precautions to be observed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An injection mould produces 38,000 parts/month with an ideal cycle time of 28 seconds. The monthly downtime for maintenance is 18 hours, and 1,200 parts are rejected per month due to mould-related defects. Calculate the OEE and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
The mould maintenance procedure consists of the following steps (as detailed in Section 2.2):
1. Mould removal (2 marks)
2. Visual inspection (1 mark)
3. Disassembly with tagging (1 mark)
4. Cleaning (ultrasonic) (1 mark)
5. Dimensional inspection using CMM, surface roughness, hardness tester (1 mark)
6. Repair and refurbishment (1 mark)

**Safety Precautions:** (up to 2 marks)
- Use PPE (gloves, safety glasses, heat-resistant aprons)
- Ensure mould is at room temperature before handling
- Disconnect all electrical, water, and pneumatic lines
- Use proper lifting gear; never sling a mould manually
- Store mould in a dry, rust-free environment

**Model Solution for (b):**

Operating time = 400 hours/month (assumed planned time = 400 + 18 = 418 hours)

**Step 1: Availability**
$$
A = \frac{400}{418} = 0.9569 = 95.69\%
$$

**Step 2: Performance**
$$
N_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{400 \times 3600}{28} = 51{,}428.57 \text{ pieces}
$$

$$
P = \frac{51{,}428.57}{38{,}000} = 1.353 \Rightarrow \text{Capped at } 1.0
$$

**Step 3: Quality**
$$
Q = \frac{38{,}000 - 1{,}200}{38{,}000} = \frac{36{,}800}{38{,}000} = 0.9684 = 96.84\%
$$

**Step 4: OEE**
$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9569 \times 1.0 \times 0.9684 = 0.9267 = 92.67\%
$$

**Comment:** OEE of 92.67% is **world-class** (≥ 85% benchmark). The mould is operating efficiently. To improve further, focus on reducing the 18 hours of monthly downtime through predictive maintenance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank 2024]**

**(a)** What is Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)? Explain its eight pillars as applied to mould maintenance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mould has the following failure data over one year:
- Total operating time = 4,000 hours
- Number of failures = 8
- Total downtime for repair = 64 hours
Calculate the MTBF, MTTR, and Availability of the mould. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
TPM is a holistic maintenance philosophy aimed at achieving **zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents** through the active involvement of all employees, from operators to top management.

**Eight Pillars of TPM applied to Mould Maintenance:**
1. **Autonomous Maintenance** — Operators perform daily cleaning, lubrication, and inspection of the mould.
2. **Planned Maintenance** — Scheduled preventive activities.
3. **Quality Maintenance** — Zero-defect mould output.
4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)** — Small-group activities to solve specific mould issues.
5. **Early Management** — Designing new moulds for easy maintenance.
6. **Training** — Skill development of mould setters and maintenance staff.
7. **Safety, Health, Environment** — Safe mould handling and storage.
8. **TPM in Administration** — Supporting functions like procurement of spares.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**
$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{4{,}000}{8} = 500 \text{ hours}
$$

**MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**
$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{64}{8} = 8 \text{ hours}
$$

**Availability**
$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{500}{500 + 8} = \frac{500}{508}
$$

$$
A = 0.9843 = 98.43\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Excellent availability, but the MTTR of 8 hours is on the higher side. The maintenance team should focus on **reducing repair time** through better spare parts availability and standardized repair procedures.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **OEE Calculation Error:** Students forget to **cap Performance at 100%**. If $P > 1$, you must write "$P$ capped at 1.0". Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** MTBF/MTTR must be in the **same units** (hours). Mixing minutes and hours is a frequent error.
> 3. **Missing Block Diagram:** In 7-mark questions on "explain the procedure", KTU examiners **mandate a diagram**. Drawing only text loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting TPM Pillars:** Many students write only 4–5 pillars. The question usually asks for **all eight** — partial marking is strict.
> 5. **No Numerical Units in Final Answer:** Always write "%" or "hours" or "shots" in the final boxed answer.
> 6. **Skipping Documentation:** Mould history card / logbook entry is often missed in step-by-step answers. Always include the **documentation step** as the final step of the procedure.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Mould maintenance = Cleaning + Inspection + Lubrication + Repair + Documentation.
- [x] **Five strategies:** Breakdown, Preventive, Predictive, Corrective, Proactive.
- [x] **TPM has 8 pillars** — remember all 8 for full marks.
- [x] Key formula: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- [x] OEE benchmark: **≥ 85% is world-class**.
- [x] Always **cap Performance at 100%** in OEE.
- [x] Common mould steel: **P20, H13, SKD61, SS 420**.
- [x] **MTBF for moulds:** 20,000 to 100,000 shots; **Mould life:** 0.5M to 5M shots.
- [x] **Ultrasonic cleaning** is preferred over manual scrubbing.
- [x] **TIG welding** with matching filler is standard for cavity repair.
- [x] Always update the **Mould History Card** after every maintenance.
- [x] Store moulds in **dry, rust-free environment** with humidity **< 50% RH**.
- [x] Safety first: **PPE, room-temperature mould, disconnected utilities, proper slinging**.
- [x] Cost-per-shot analysis is a high-yield KTU numerical.
- [x] ISO standards to remember: **ISO 9001:2015**, **ISO 12100:2010**, **IATF 16949** (for automotive moulds).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing and Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Servicing and Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Servicing and Inspection** in mould and die manufacture refers to the systematic, scheduled, and procedural activities undertaken to evaluate, maintain, repair, and restore moulds and dies to their optimal functional state, ensuring dimensional accuracy, surface integrity, and prolonged operational life.

**Servicing** encompasses all preventive, predictive, and corrective maintenance operations—including cleaning, lubrication, polishing, component replacement, and re-machining—performed on moulds and dies.

**Inspection** is the methodical process of measuring, examining, and testing mould/die components using precision metrology equipment to verify conformity with design specifications, dimensional tolerances, and surface finish requirements.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **PM (Preventive Maintenance)** | Scheduled servicing to prevent unexpected failures |
| **CM (Corrective Maintenance)** | Repair after failure or defect detection |
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Condition-based servicing using monitoring data |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures (hours) |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair (hours) |
| **OEE** | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (%) |
| **PPM** | Parts Per Million (defect rate) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Servicing and inspection are classified as critical post-production activities that directly affect product quality, manufacturing economics, and tool longevity. Students must understand both scheduled and unscheduled service procedures.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Your Mould/Die is like a High-Performance Car**
>
> Think of a precision mould the way you'd treat a luxury car:
> - **Inspection** = The periodic service check-up at the garage (checking tyre pressure, oil levels, brake wear)
> - **Servicing** = The actual repair work done (changing oil, replacing worn parts, polishing scratches)
> - **Without regular inspection**, small problems (like a tiny crack or minor wear) snowball into catastrophic failures (like engine seizure)
> - **A well-maintained mould** can produce **millions of parts** with consistent quality; a neglected one fails within thousands of cycles
>
> In KTU examinations, remember: **Inspection identifies the problem; Servicing solves it.**

## 1.3 Classification of Servicing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Tiers of Servicing in Mould/Die Engineering:**

1. **Routine Servicing** — Performed after every production shift or batch
   - Cleaning of cavities
   - Lubrication of guide pillars and bushings
   - Visual inspection for obvious damage

2. **Periodic Servicing** — Performed at fixed intervals (e.g., every 10,000 cycles)
   - Polishing of worn surfaces
   - Replacement of ejector pins and return springs
   - Calibration check of critical dimensions

3. **Overhaul Servicing** — Major disassembly and refurbishment
   - Re-machining of worn cavities
   - Replacement of major components
   - Re-grinding of parting surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Mould/Die Inspection

Inspection in mould and die manufacture operates on **three hierarchical levels**:

### Level 1: First-Off Inspection
- Performed on the **first component** produced after mould setup
- Verifies dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and functionality
- Uses go/no-go gauges, calipers, micrometers

### Level 2: In-Process Inspection
- Performed at **regular intervals** during production
- Detects progressive wear or thermal distortion
- Uses CMM, optical comparators, surface roughness testers

### Level 3: Last-Off Inspection
- Performed on the **final component** of the batch
- Documents the final state of the mould
- Used for predictive maintenance scheduling

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU exams?** Examiners often ask: *"Why is first-off inspection critical even before bulk production?"* The answer lies in detecting mould settling, thermal expansion mismatches, and operator setup errors before committing to full production runs.

## 2.2 Wear Mechanisms in Moulds and Dies

Understanding wear is fundamental to both inspection planning and servicing strategy. The **four primary wear mechanisms** are:

1. **Abrasive Wear** — Caused by hard particles or fibres in the plastic/metal stock
2. **Adhesive Wear** — Cold welding between mating surfaces under high pressure
3. **Fatigue Wear** — Cyclic stress leading to pitting and spalling
4. **Corrosive Wear** — Chemical attack from corrosive plastic additives (e.g., PVC releases HCl)

### 2.2.1 Archard's Wear Equation

The fundamental equation governing abrasive wear in mould/die components:

$$W = \frac{K \cdot P \cdot L}{H}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Volume of material worn away (mm³)
- $K$ = Wear coefficient (dimensionless, typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$)
- $P$ = Normal load applied (N)
- $L$ = Sliding distance (mm)
- $H$ = Hardness of the softer material (HV or HB)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A harder mould surface (higher $H$) dramatically reduces wear. This is why KTU emphasizes the use of **HCHCr steel (AISI D2/D3)** with hardness **58–62 HRC** for cavity and core inserts in injection moulds and dies.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application | Units |
|---------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| **Wear Volume (Archard)** | $W = \dfrac{K \cdot P \cdot L}{H}$ | Predicting cavity wear | mm³ |
| **Tool Life Equation** | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | Taylor's tool life for machining reconditioning | m/min, min |
| **OEE** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Mould effectiveness | % |
| **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{No. of Failures}}$ | Reliability metric | hours |
| **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{No. of Repairs}}$ | Maintainability metric | hours |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Operational availability | decimal (0–1) |
| **Spring Force Check** | $F_s = k \cdot x$ | Ejector/return spring verification | N |
| **Cavity Pressure** | $P_c = \dfrac{F_{\text{clamp}}}{A_{\text{projected}}}$ | Clamp tonnage check | MPa |
| **Thermal Expansion** | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Dimensional change with temp | mm |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert y \vert \, dx$ | Finish measurement | µm |
| **Dimensional Tolerance** | $\text{Tol} = \pm \dfrac{\text{IT grade}}{2}$ | Inspection limits | mm |
| **Shrinkage Compensation** | $D_m = D_p (1 + S)$ | Mould dimension calculation | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** In the formula table, never use the vertical pipe `|` for absolute values. KTU examiners and LaTeX compilers use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. The integral expression for $R_a$ above uses $\vert y \vert$ which is the correct form.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Application of Servicing & Inspection:**

- **Automotive Sector:** A single dashboard mould costing ₹15–50 lakhs must produce 500,000+ parts. Proper servicing extends life from 2 years to 10+ years, saving crores in replacement costs.
- **Electronics:** Mobile phone housing moulds require inspection to **±0.02 mm** tolerance using CMMs.
- **Medical Devices:** Surgical instruments dies require **100% traceability** — every inspection record is preserved for FDA/ISO 13485 compliance.
- **Packaging:** PET preform moulds run 24/7 and require **predictive maintenance** based on cycle count and pressure curves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Mould Dimension with Shrinkage Compensation

When a plastic part is cooled in a mould, it shrinks. The mould cavity must therefore be **oversized** to compensate for this shrinkage. This is the most commonly tested derivation in KTU examinations.

### Given:
- Final plastic part dimension: $D_p$
- Volumetric shrinkage of plastic: $S$ (expressed as a fraction, e.g., 0.015 for 1.5%)

### To Find:
- Required mould cavity dimension: $D_m$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Define shrinkage mathematically.

$$S = \frac{D_m - D_p}{D_m}$$

**Step 2:** Explain the physical meaning.

This equation states that shrinkage is the **fractional reduction** in dimension from mould size $D_m$ to final part size $D_p$.

**Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for $D_m$.

$$S \cdot D_m = D_m - D_p$$

**Step 4:** Isolate $D_m$ on one side.

$$D_p = D_m - S \cdot D_m$$

$$D_p = D_m (1 - S)$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $(1 - S)$.

$$D_m = \frac{D_p}{1 - S}$$

**Step 6:** Alternative form using binomial expansion (useful when $S$ is small).

$$D_m = D_p (1 - S)^{-1}$$

$$\text{Using } (1-x)^{-1} = 1 + x + x^2 + \ldots$$

$$D_m \approx D_p (1 + S + S^2 + \ldots)$$

**Step 7:** For small $S$ (typical case, $S < 0.05$), the higher-order terms are negligible.

$$\boxed{D_m \approx D_p (1 + S)}$$

### Worked Numerical Example:

> **Problem:** A polypropylene part must have a final length of $100 \text{ mm}$. The volumetric shrinkage of PP is **1.8%**. Calculate the mould cavity length.
>
> **Solution:**
> $D_p = 100$ mm, $S = 0.018$
> $D_m = 100 \times (1 + 0.018) = 100 \times 1.018 = 101.8$ mm
>
> **Answer:** The mould cavity must be machined to **101.8 mm**.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

OEE is the gold-standard metric for evaluating mould/die productivity.

### Given:
- Availability $A$ (decimal)
- Performance $P$ (decimal)
- Quality $Q$ (decimal)

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Define each component.

$$A = \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$$

$$P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$$

$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$$

**Step 2:** Combine into OEE.

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

**Step 3:** Express as a percentage.

$$\text{OEE \%} = A \times P \times Q \times 100$$

### Numerical Example:

> **Problem:** A mould runs for 8 hours (480 min). It has 30 min of unplanned downtime. Ideal cycle time is 12 seconds; actual average is 14 seconds. 9,500 good parts are produced out of 10,000 total. Calculate OEE.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $A = \dfrac{480 - 30}{480} = \dfrac{450}{480} = 0.9375$
>
> $P = \dfrac{12}{14} = 0.8571$
>
> $Q = \dfrac{9500}{10000} = 0.95$
>
> $\text{OEE} = 0.9375 \times 0.8571 \times 0.95 = 0.7635$
>
> $\text{OEE \%} = 76.35\%$
>
> **Interpretation:** A **world-class OEE is 85%+**; this mould is performing below world-class, indicating need for better servicing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Defects vs. Inspection Methods

| Defect Type | Likely Cause | Inspection Method | Servicing Action |
|-------------|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Sink marks** | Insufficient packing, overheating | Visual, depth gauge | Adjust cooling, polish cavity |
| **Flash/burrs** | Worn parting line, insufficient clamp | Visual, gauge blocks | Re-grind parting surface, check clamp tonnage |
| **Short shot** | Insufficient material, blocked gate | Visual, weight check | Clean gate, increase injection pressure |
| **Weld lines** | Cold material, poor venting | Visual, microscopy | Polish vent slots, raise mould temp |
| **Warping** | Uneven cooling, residual stress | CMM, optical comparator | Re-balance cooling channels |
| **Ejector pin marks** | Worn pins, improper lubrication | Visual, surface roughness | Replace pins, re-lubricate |
| **Sticking parts** | Insufficient draft, worn surface | Visual, force gauge | Polish cavity, add draft angle |
| **Dimensional drift** | Cavity wear, thermal expansion | CMM, micrometer | Re-machine cavity, apply hard chrome |
| **Burn marks** | Trapped air, overheating | Visual | Polish vents, reduce injection speed |
| **Flow lines** | Cold mould, slow injection | Visual, microscopy | Heat mould, increase injection rate |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Spring Force Verification

Ejector and return springs lose force over time. KTU examinations often test spring calculations.

**Step 1:** Identify spring parameters.
- Spring rate: $k$ (N/mm)
- Free length: $L_0$ (mm)
- Installed length: $L_i$ (mm)
- Maximum compression length: $L_{max}$ (mm)

**Step 2:** Calculate installed force.

$$F_i = k \times (L_0 - L_i)$$

**Step 3:** Calculate maximum force at full compression.

$$F_{max} = k \times (L_0 - L_{max})$$

**Step 4:** Check solid height (no further compression possible).

$$L_{solid} = \text{Number of active coils} \times \text{wire diameter}$$

**Step 5:** Acceptance criterion.

$$\text{Accept if } F_i \geq 1.2 \times F_{required}$$

If not, replace spring (corrective servicing).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is used during periodic servicing to decide whether to **retain or replace** ejector springs — a critical decision affecting both downtime and part quality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mould Servicing Workflow Architecture

```mermaid
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P{Dimensions OK?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Return to Production]
    P -- No --> R[Scrap or Re-engineer]
    D --> S[Scheduled PM Interval Reached]

    Q --> U
    R --> U
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Servicing of a Mould

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

> Servicing of a mould refers to the systematic and scheduled maintenance activities performed on a mould to restore it to its optimal working condition. It includes **cleaning, lubrication, polishing, repair, and replacement** of worn or damaged components. Servicing is classified into three categories: **routine servicing** (after each shift), **periodic servicing** (after fixed cycle counts), and **overhaul servicing** (major refurbishment). The primary objectives are to maintain dimensional accuracy, extend mould life, ensure consistent part quality, and minimize unexpected downtime.

### Question 2: What is First-Off Inspection?

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

> First-off inspection is the dimensional and visual examination of the **first component** produced immediately after mould setup or after any tooling change. It is performed before bulk production begins to verify that the mould is producing parts within the specified tolerances. The first-off component is checked using **vernier calipers, micrometers, go/no-go gauges, and surface roughness testers**. This inspection detects problems such as incorrect dimensions due to mould settling, thermal expansion, misaligned cores, or improper process parameters, thereby preventing the production of large quantities of defective parts.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. The two alternatives below represent typical options presented in the question paper.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the different types of wear mechanisms encountered in moulds and dies. Discuss the inspection methods used to identify each type. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four primary wear mechanisms in moulds and dies are:

**1. Abrasive Wear:**
Abrasive wear occurs when hard particles or fibres in the processed material (e.g., glass-filled plastics, metallic slugs in die casting) plough through the softer mould/die surface, creating grooves and scratches. In injection moulds, glass-filled nylon (e.g., 30% GF) is particularly abrasive. Inspection involves **surface profilometry** and **microscopic examination** to detect directional scratches. Servicing action includes **re-polishing** and applying **wear-resistant coatings** like TiN, CrN, or DLC.

**2. Adhesive Wear:**
Adhesive wear results from micro-welding between asperities on contacting surfaces under high pressure and temperature. Material from one surface transfers to the other. This is common in **ejector pins, slides, and guide pillars**. Inspection involves **visual examination** for material transfer marks and **dye-penetrant testing** for micro-cracks. Servicing includes **re-lubrication** with high-temperature grease and **surface re-hardening**.

**3. Fatigue Wear:**
Fatigue wear occurs due to repeated cyclic loading, especially at high-stress concentration points like **core pins, ejector pins, and cavity edges**. It manifests as **pitting and spalling**. Inspection is performed using **magnetic particle inspection** or **eddy current testing** to detect subsurface cracks. Servicing involves **polishing out pits** and replacing severely damaged components.

**4. Corrosive Wear:**
Corrosive wear results from chemical attack on the mould surface, particularly during processing of **PVC** (releases HCl) and certain engineering plastics with corrosive additives. Inspection reveals **etching, pitting, and discolouration**. Servicing requires **electropolishing** and application of **corrosion-resistant coatings** such as electroless nickel or hard chrome.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Proper identification of the wear mechanism is critical because each type demands a different servicing strategy. Combining inspection data with the wear theory enables **predictive maintenance** and prevents catastrophic tool failure.

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the procedure for periodic inspection of an injection mould. List the equipment used and the parameters checked. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure for Periodic Inspection of an Injection Mould:**

Periodic inspection is typically performed after every **5,000 to 10,000 production cycles** or as per the manufacturer's recommended schedule.

**Step 1: Pre-Inspection Preparation**
- Stop the machine and ensure the mould is at room temperature
- Disconnect utilities (water, oil, electricity)
- Clean the mould exterior to remove plastic residue, grease, and rust

**Step 2: Visual Inspection**
- Examine the **parting line** for flash, burrs, or damage
- Check the **cavity and core surfaces** for wear, corrosion, or scratches
- Inspect **ejector pin holes** for wear or galling
- Examine **cooling channels** for blockage or scaling

**Step 3: Dimensional Inspection**
- Measure critical cavity dimensions using **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)** or **height gauges**
- Verify **ejector pin stroke** and parallelism
- Check **guide pillar and bushing** clearances using dial indicators
- Measure **parting surface flatness** using a surface plate and dial indicator

**Step 4: Functional Testing**
- Operate the mould manually (if possible) to verify smooth movement of slides, lifters, and ejectors
- Check **spring forces** of return springs using a force gauge
- Verify **cooling water flow rate** at each channel

**Step 5: Documentation**
- Record all measurements in the **mould history card/log**
- Compare with previous readings to detect trends
- Flag any dimensions approaching tolerance limits for **predictive maintenance**

**Equipment Used:**

| Equipment | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) | 3D dimensional measurement |
| Optical Comparator | 2D profile comparison |
| Surface Roughness Tester | Measure Ra value |
| Dial Indicator | Runout, flatness |
| Vernier Caliper / Micrometer | Linear dimensions |
| Force Gauge | Spring force verification |
| Dye-Penetrant Kit | Surface crack detection |
| Surface Plate | Flatness reference |

**Parameters Checked:**
- Cavity/core dimensions (critical to part tolerances)
- Parting line flatness
- Guide pillar concentricity
- Ejector pin diameter and stroke
- Cooling channel flow rate
- Surface roughness (Ra)
- Spring free and installed lengths

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** A well-documented periodic inspection program can extend mould life by **3–5 times** compared to moulds maintained only on a failure-driven basis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) as applied to mould and die performance. Calculate the OEE for a mould that operates for 7.2 hours in an 8-hour shift, with an ideal cycle time of 15 seconds, actual cycle time of 18 seconds, producing 1,300 good parts out of 1,440 total. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of OEE:**

Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) is a comprehensive metric used to evaluate the productivity of manufacturing equipment, including moulds and dies. It is the product of three factors:

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

Where:
- $A$ = **Availability** = (Operating Time / Planned Production Time)
- $P$ = **Performance** = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces / Operating Time)
- $Q$ = **Quality** = (Good Pieces / Total Pieces)

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

**Step 1: Calculate Availability.**

$$A = \frac{7.2 \text{ hours}}{8 \text{ hours}} = 0.90$$

**Step 2: Calculate Performance.**

Operating time = $7.2 \text{ hours} = 7.2 \times 3600 = 25{,}920 \text{ seconds}$

$$P = \frac{15 \times 1440}{25920} = \frac{21600}{25920} = 0.8333$$

**Step 3: Calculate Quality.**

$$Q = \frac{1300}{1440} = 0.9028$$

**Step 4: Calculate OEE.**

$$\text{OEE} = 0.90 \times 0.8333 \times 0.9028$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.6771$$

$$\text{OEE \%} = 67.71\%$$

**Interpretation:**
- **World-class OEE ≥ 85%**
- **Typical OEE = 60%**
- This mould's OEE of **67.71%** indicates below-world-class performance, primarily due to performance losses (cycle time slippage) and quality losses (reject rate of 9.72%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** OEE helps identify the largest source of loss — in this case, **performance and quality** — and directs engineering effort toward **cycle time optimization** and **defect reduction** through better mould servicing.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the various inspection instruments used in mould and die manufacture. Compare their accuracy, applications, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Inspection Instruments in Mould and Die Manufacture:**

**1. Vernier Caliper (Accuracy: ±0.02 mm)**
- Application: Quick linear measurements of external and internal dimensions
- Limitation: Not suitable for complex geometries; operator-dependent

**2. Micrometer (Accuracy: ±0.001 mm)**
- Application: High-precision measurement of shafts, pins, and small features
- Limitation: Single-axis measurement only

**3. Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) (Accuracy: ±0.001–0.005 mm)**
- Application: 3D measurement of complex cavity/core geometry
- Limitation: Slow; requires skilled operator; expensive

**4. Optical Comparator / Profile Projector (Accuracy: ±0.005 mm)**
- Application: 2D profile comparison of small components like ejector pins
- Limitation: Only 2D; requires clear edge definition

**5. Surface Roughness Tester (Accuracy: ±0.001 µm Ra)**
- Application: Measures Ra, Rz, and Rq values
- Limitation: Stylus-based; can damage soft surfaces

**6. Height Gauge and Surface Plate (Accuracy: ±0.005 mm)**
- Application: Height measurement, marking out
- Limitation: Reference-dependent

**7. Dial Indicator (Accuracy: ±0.01 mm)**
- Application: Runout, concentricity, flatness
- Limitation: Manual reading; not absolute

**8. Go/No-Go Gauges (Accuracy: ±0.002 mm)**
- Application: Rapid pass/fail checking of critical features
- Limitation: No quantitative measurement

**9. 3D Scanner / Laser Scanner (Accuracy: ±0.01–0.05 mm)**
- Application: Reverse engineering, full surface digitization
- Limitation: Expensive; surface-dependent accuracy

**Comparative Summary:**

| Instrument | Accuracy | Best For | Limitation |
|------------|----------|----------|------------|
| Vernier Caliper | ±0.02 mm | Quick checks | Operator skill |
| Micrometer | ±0.001 mm | Shaft/pin diameter | Single dimension |
| CMM | ±0.002 mm | Complex 3D geometry | Cost, speed |
| Optical Comparator | ±0.005 mm | 2D profiles | 2D only |
| Surface Roughness | ±0.001 µm | Finish quality | Stylus contact |
| 3D Scanner | ±0.02 mm | Full surface | Cost, calibration |

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The choice of inspection instrument depends on **accuracy requirement, part complexity, batch size, and cost**. For KTU examinations, students must be able to **justify the selection** of a particular instrument for a given inspection task.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Inspection" with "Servicing":** Inspection is the *measurement and evaluation* phase; servicing is the *maintenance and repair* phase. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are used interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** When solving numerical problems (e.g., OEE, shrinkage), always state the given data explicitly. Many students lose 1 mark for not defining variables.
>
> 3. **Missing the "Why" in descriptive answers:** KTU follows Outcome-Based Education (OBE). Merely listing types of wear without explaining *why* each occurs and *how* it affects the mould loses marks.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in numerical answers:** Always include units (mm, mm³, %, N). A correct number without units is treated as incomplete.
>
> 5. **In OEE calculations:** A very common error is using *actual pieces* in the performance formula instead of *total pieces*. Remember: Performance uses **total pieces produced**, Quality uses **good pieces only**.
>
> 6. **Neglecting diagrams:** Even a simple labelled sketch in Part B answers adds 1–2 marks. KTU examiners reward visualization.
>
> 7. **Skipping the conclusion:** Always end Part B sub-questions with a one-line summary or industrial implication.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Servicing** = Maintenance activities (cleaning, lubrication, repair, replacement)
- [x] **Inspection** = Measurement and evaluation of mould/die condition
- [x] **Three tiers of servicing:** Routine → Periodic → Overhaul
- [x] **Three levels of inspection:** First-Off → In-Process → Last-Off
- [x] **Four wear mechanisms:** Abrasive, Adhesive, Fatigue, Corrosive
- [x] **Archard's equation:** $W = \dfrac{K \cdot P \cdot L}{H}$
- [x] **Shrinkage compensation:** $D_m = D_p (1 + S)$
- [x] **OEE formula:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$
- [x] **MTBF / MTTR:** Reliability and maintainability metrics
- [x] **Inspection instruments:** Vernier, Micrometer, CMM, Optical Comparator, Surface Roughness Tester
- [x] **World-class OEE ≥ 85%**
- [x] **Mould steel hardness:** 58–62 HRC (HCHCr / AISI D2)
- [x] **Common defects:** Flash, sink marks, short shot, weld lines, warping
- [x] **GD&T and SPC** are modern inspection frameworks
- [x] **Mould history card/log** is mandatory documentation
- [x] **Lubrication** of slides, ejectors, and guide pillars is non-negotiable
- [x] **Predictive maintenance** uses cycle count, pressure curves, and dimensional trends
- [x] **Coatings** (TiN, CrN, DLC) extend mould life

### 📋 Memory Aid: "SAW-IIO"

For quick recall of servicing & inspection topics:

- **S** — Servicing (PM, CM, PdM)
- **A** — Archard's wear equation
- **W** — Wear mechanisms (4 types)
- **I** — Inspection hierarchy (3 levels)
- **I** — Instruments (Vernier to CMM)
- **O** — OEE (Availability × Performance × Quality)

### 🎯 Key Definitions to Memorize

1. **PM (Preventive Maintenance):** Scheduled maintenance to prevent failures
2. **CM (Corrective Maintenance):** Maintenance after a failure has occurred
3. **SPC (Statistical Process Control):** Real-time statistical monitoring of process parameters
4. **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing):** Symbolic language for specifying tolerances
5. **PPM (Parts Per Million):** Quality metric (defects per million opportunities)
6. **OEE:** Composite measure of equipment productivity
7. **MTBF / MTTR:** Reliability and maintainability indicators
8. **First-Off Inspection:** Inspection of the first part produced after setup

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** When answering Part B questions, always structure your response as: **(1) Definition → (2) Classification/Types → (3) Working Principle/Mechanism → (4) Derivation/Formula → (5) Numerical Example → (6) Industrial Application → (7) Conclusion.** This 7-step structure covers all KTU 2024 marking scheme checkpoints and maximizes your score.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT756 - MOULD AND DIE MANUFACTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Repairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔧 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Die/Mould Repair** is defined as the systematic process of restoring a worn, damaged, fatigued, or fractured mould/die component to its original functional geometry, dimensional accuracy, surface integrity, and metallurgical condition so that it can continue to produce acceptable parts within the specified tolerance limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim standard):**
> "Repair is a corrective maintenance activity performed on a mould or die to reinstate its working surfaces, profiles, and clearances after the onset of wear, plastic deformation, cracking, chipping, or erosion, using techniques such as welding, machining, grinding, polishing, recoating, or heat treatment."

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, repairs are categorized under the **Tool Life Management** learning outcome (typically CO4), where students must demonstrate the ability to evaluate failure modes and prescribe appropriate restoration techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dent in Your Car"

Imagine your car gets a dent on the door. You have **two choices**:
- **Option A:** Throw away the entire car and buy a new one. (This is **replacement**.)
- **Option B:** Take it to a workshop where the mechanic hammers out the dent, repaints the panel, and restores the surface. (This is **repair**.)

A mould/die is similar to a "tool-car" that produces thousands of car bodies every day. Over time, the forming surfaces get worn out, scratched, or cracked. Since a forging die or plastic injection mould costs **₹2,00,000 to ₹50,00,000**, you cannot afford to scrap it after every scratch. You **repair** it.

| Real-World Analogy | Mould/Die Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Car body dent | Die cavity wear / deformation |
| Mechanic hammering out the dent | Hammer peening, cold working repair |
| Welding a cracked car chassis | TIG/MIG welding on die |
| Repainting the car body | Polishing, surface finishing |
| Engine overhaul | Major refurbishment of die set |
| Scrapping old car | Die retirement (last stage of life) |

## 1.3 Why Repair? — Economic & Engineering Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for KTU:** The **Repair-to-Replacement Ratio (R³)** in industry averages **8:1 to 12:1**, meaning for every 1 new die made, 8 to 12 major repairs are performed. Mould repairs alone account for **40–60% of total tooling cost** in high-volume production.

The driving forces for repair are:
1. **High initial capital cost** of dies/moulds.
2. **Time-critical production** — making a new die takes weeks/months.
3. **Material conservation** — die steels contain expensive alloys (H13, P20, D2, SKD61).
4. **Design validation** — the proven die design continues to be useful.
5. **Sustainability** — aligns with circular economy and waste reduction.

## 1.4 Standard Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Refurbishment** | Comprehensive restoration of the entire die to near-original condition |
| **Rework** | Minor modification to bring out-of-tolerance dimensions back within spec |
| **Salvage** | Restoration of a die that has failed catastrophically (often uneconomical) |
| **Touch-up** | Minor localized repair (e.g., polishing a scratch) |
| **Re-cutting** | Re-machining the cavity to restore geometry |
| **Hard-facing** | Applying a hard, wear-resistant weld overlay |
| **Re-hardening** | Re-applying heat treatment after repair welding |

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Die Steel Hardness (H13, hardened):** **48–54 HRC** (≈ 470–570 HV)
- **Welding Preheat Temperature for H13:** **300–400 °C**
- **Tempering Temperature Post-Weld:** **550–620 °C**
- **Maximum Allowable Repair Depth (general guideline):** **< 30% of die section thickness**
- **Surface Roughness (Ra) after repair polishing:** **0.1–0.4 µm** for plastic mould cavities
- **Repair Cost as % of New Die Cost:** typically **15–40%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Die/Mould Damage (The "5 W's of Failure")

Understanding **why** a die fails is the foundation of repair. The five primary wear/damage mechanisms are:

### (a) Abrasive Wear
- **Mechanism:** Hard particles (scale, oxides, inclusions) plough through softer die surfaces.
- **Appearance:** Parallel scratches and grooves aligned with material flow direction.
- **Common in:** Forging dies, drawing dies, extrusion dies handling ferrous materials.

### (b) Adhesive Wear
- **Mechanism:** Micro-welding between die surface and workpiece under high pressure → material transfer.
- **Appearance:** Pick-up, gouging, material sticking to die.
- **Common in:** Aluminium extrusion, deep drawing of stainless steel.

### (c) Fatigue Wear (Thermal & Mechanical)
- **Mechanism:** Repeated cyclic thermal/loading stresses → micro-cracks → spalling.
- **Appearance:** Heat-checking (network of fine cracks), pitting.
- **Common in:** Die casting dies, hot forging dies, plastic injection moulds.

### (d) Corrosive/Oxidation Wear
- **Mechanism:** Chemical attack from lubricants, coolants, molten metal, or atmospheric exposure during storage.
- **Appearance:** Surface pitting, discoloration, etching.
- **Common in:** Plastic moulds (PVC releases HCl), die casting (zinc attacks dies).

### (e) Plastic Deformation & Gross Fracture
- **Mechanism:** Overload beyond yield strength → permanent shape change or sudden break.
- **Appearance:** Mushrooming of punch heads, broken die corners, cracked inserts.
- **Common in:** Cold forging, punching, trimming operations.

## 2.2 Why Each Repair Method Works — The Engineering Logic

| Repair Method | Underlying Principle | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **TIG Welding (GTAW)** | Arc melts filler wire & base metal → metallurgical bond | Cracks, missing material, corner build-up |
| **Laser Cladding** | Focused laser melts powder/wire → minimal HAZ | High-precision build-up, complex geometry |
| **EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining)** | Electrical sparks erode material → no mechanical force | Machining hardened dies without softening |
| **CNC Milling/Grinding** | Mechanical material removal → restores geometry | Re-cutting worn profiles |
| **Polishing/Lapping** | Abrasive particles remove micro-peaks → improves Ra | Surface finish restoration |
| **Nitriding/Carburizing** | Diffusion of N/C atoms → surface hardening | Recovering surface hardness |
| **Hammer Peening** | Cold-working surface → compressive residual stress | Fatigue crack arrest, surface densification |
| **Fitting Inserts** | Replace damaged region with new pre-machined block | Severe localized damage |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE for mould/die repair questions. Memorize the form, units, and physical meaning.

### Formula 1: Archard's Wear Equation

$$
V = \frac{k \cdot F \cdot L}{H}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Volume of material worn away (mm³)
- $k$ = Wear coefficient (dimensionless, typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-8}$)
- $F$ = Normal load on the contact surface (N)
- $L$ = Sliding distance (mm)
- $H$ = Hardness of the softer material (MPa or HV)

### Formula 2: Repair Depth Limitation (Empirical Rule)

$$
d_{\text{repair}} \leq 0.30 \cdot t_{\text{section}}
$$

Where:
- $d_{\text{repair}}$ = Depth of weld deposit or material removal
- $t_{\text{section}}$ = Local section thickness of the die
- If exceeded → risk of distortion, cracking, or structural failure.

### Formula 3: Welding Heat Input (per unit length)

$$
E = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot v} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = Welding current (A)
- $v$ = Travel speed (mm/min)
- For die repair, **recommended $E$ < 1.0 kJ/mm** to limit HAZ.

### Formula 4: Repair Cost Ratio (Economic Decision Tool)

$$
R_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{C_{\text{repair}}}{C_{\text{new}}}
$$

**Decision Rule (KTU Board Standard):**
- $R_{\text{ratio}} < 0.40$ → **Repair is economical** ✓
- $0.40 \leq R_{\text{ratio}} \leq 0.70$ → Repair only if time-critical
- $R_{\text{ratio}} > 0.70$ → **Replace** ✗

### Formula 5: Dilution Ratio in Weld Overlay

$$
D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $A_{\text{base}}$ = Area of melted base metal in weld pool cross-section
- $A_{\text{filler}}$ = Area of deposited filler metal
- Lower $D$ = purer overlay chemistry (desirable for die repair).

### Formula 6: Thermal Stress During Welding

$$
\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = \alpha \cdot E \cdot \Delta T
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of thermal expansion (≈ $11 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for H13)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (≈ 210 GPa for tool steel)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature drop (°C)
- High $\sigma_{\text{thermal}}$ causes weld cracking → mitigated by **preheating**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive sector:** Hot forging dies for connecting rods undergo repair every **8,000–15,000 parts**.
- **Plastic injection moulds:** Cavity repair by laser welding extends life from 500K shots to over 2 million shots.
- **Sheet metal stamping:** Die regrinding after every 100,000–500,000 strokes to restore edge sharpness.
- **Die casting (Al/Zn):** Welding repair of heat-checked die steel (H13) using matching filler (e.g., H13 rod).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Archard's Wear & Repair Volume

> **Problem Statement:**
> A forging die experiences abrasive wear during operation. Given:
> - Normal load on die surface: $F = 5000$ N
> - Sliding distance per stroke: $L = 50$ mm
> - Number of strokes: $N = 10{,}000$
> - Die steel hardness: $H = 500$ HV
> - Wear coefficient: $k = 5 \times 10^{-6}$
>
> **Find:** (a) Volume of worn material, (b) Average depth of wear if worn area = 2500 mm², (c) Whether it warrants repair using the 30% rule if section thickness = 8 mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate total sliding distance**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L \times N = 50 \times 10{,}000 = 500{,}000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Archard's Equation**

$$
V = \frac{k \cdot F \cdot L_{\text{total}}}{H}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
V = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (5000) \times (500{,}000)}{500}
$$

$$
V = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{9})}{500}
$$

$$
V = \frac{12{,}500}{500} = 25 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3: Calculate average depth of wear**

$$
d_{\text{wear}} = \frac{V}{A_{\text{worn}}} = \frac{25}{2500} = 0.010 \text{ mm} = 10 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 4: Apply 30% Repair Depth Rule**

$$
d_{\text{limit}} = 0.30 \times t_{\text{section}} = 0.30 \times 8 = 2.4 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $d_{\text{wear}} = 0.010$ mm $\ll$ 2.4 mm, **repair is feasible and safe**.

**Step 5: Conclusion** — Minor touch-up polishing/re-grinding recommended. No welding required.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Welding Heat Input & HAZ Estimation

> **Problem Statement:**
> A die repair operation uses TIG welding with the following parameters:
> - Voltage: $V = 22$ V
> - Current: $I = 150$ A
> - Travel speed: $v = 120$ mm/min
>
> **Find:** (a) Heat input per unit length, (b) Comment on suitability for H13 die steel repair.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Apply Heat Input Formula**

$$
E = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot v}
$$

$$
E = \frac{22 \times 150 \times 60}{1000 \times 120}
$$

$$
E = \frac{198{,}000}{120{,}000} = 1.65 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with recommended limit**

For tool steel repair: **$E < 1.0$ kJ/mm** is preferred.

Since $E = 1.65$ kJ/mm > 1.0 kJ/mm, this is **too high** for sensitive die repair.

**Step 3: Recommendations**
- Reduce current to $I = 90$ A → $E = 0.99$ kJ/mm ✓
- OR reduce voltage to 14 V → $E = 1.05$ kJ/mm (borderline)
- OR use pulsed TIG for finer thermal control.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Repair vs Replace

| Decision Criterion | Repair (✓) | Replace (✗) |
|---|---|---|
| Cost ratio $R$ | $< 0.40$ | $> 0.70$ |
| Repair depth | $< 30\%$ section | $> 50\%$ section |
| Hardness retention | $> 90\%$ original | $< 70\%$ |
| Crack location | Edge, non-critical | Through-thickness, high-stress zone |
| Frequency | First or second repair | Third or more major repair |
| Lead time | < 2 weeks | Production schedule allows new die |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Repair Procedure (Standard Operating Sequence)

1. **Disassembly & Cleaning** — Strip die from press; degrease using alkaline cleaner.
2. **Damage Assessment** — Visual + dye-penetrant + magnetic-particle inspection.
3. **Documentation** — Record wear pattern, dimensions, photos.
4. **Pre-Weld Preparation** — Grind out cracks to sound metal (U-groove), preheat to 300–400 °C.
5. **Welding** — Use matching filler (e.g., H13 rod for H13 die), low heat input, stringer beads.
6. **Post-Weld Stress Relief** — Temper immediately at 550–600 °C for 2 hours.
7. **Machining** — CNC mill/grind to restore geometry.
8. **Heat Treatment** — Re-harden if required (austenitize, quench, temper).
9. **Polishing** — Progressive grit (120 → 320 → 600 → 1200 → diamond paste).
10. **Inspection** — Dimensional (CMM), surface (profilometer), hardness (Rockwell).
11. **Tryout** — Bench test with sample material before production release.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Die Repair Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C -->|Deformation| F[Dimensional Measurement CMM]
    C -->|Fracture| G[Salvage Assessment]

    F --> H
    H --> I[TIG or Laser Welding]

    J --> K[CNC Machining to Spec]
    K --> L[Heat Treatment Re-Hardening]
    L --> M[Progressive Polishing]

    O -->|No| K

```
